topics.title: Low Emission Vehicle (LEV)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT744 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Low Emission Vehicle (LEV)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Low Emission Vehicle (LEV)** is a motor vehicle that is certified by a regulatory authority to emit significantly lower levels of harmful pollutants (such as Carbon Monoxide **CO**, Hydrocarbons **HC**, Oxides of Nitrogen **NOx**, and Particulate Matter **PM**) per kilometre travelled, when compared to a conventional Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) vehicle operating on petrol or diesel fuel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEMUT744 - Module 1):**
> *"A Low Emission Vehicle is broadly classified as any vehicle that uses advanced propulsion technologies, alternative fuels, hybridisation, or electrified powertrains to reduce the tailpipe emission of regulated pollutants by at least 50% relative to the baseline Euro/BS emission standard of the same vehicle class."*

The term LEV is **umbrella terminology** that includes several overlapping categories defined by various global standards:

| Acronym | Full Form | Emissions Philosophy |
|---|---|---|
| **LEV** | Low Emission Vehicle | General low-pollution vehicles |
| **ULEV** | Ultra Low Emission Vehicle | < 75 g/km CO₂ equivalent (Euro 5 era) |
| **ZEV** | Zero Emission Vehicle | Zero tailpipe emissions (BEV, FCEV) |
| **PZEV** | Partial Zero Emission Vehicle | Near-zero evaporative + tailpipe |
| **AT-PZEV** | Advanced Technology PZEV | Hybrid + ZEV-like behaviour |
| **SULEV** | Super Ultra Low Emission Vehicle | < 12.4 g/km combined NMOG+NOx (CARB) |
| **EV** | Electric Vehicle | Battery or fuel cell propelled |

## 1.2 The Regulated Pollutants (Why "Low Emission" Matters)

Conventional petrol/diesel engines emit six primary regulated pollutants:

$$ \text{Tailpipe Emissions} = \{CO,\ HC,\ NO_x,\ PM,\ CO_2,\ SO_2\} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction for KTU Examinations:**
> - **CO₂ (Carbon Dioxide)** → A *greenhouse gas* (regulated by Corporate Average Fuel Economy / CAFE norms).
> - **CO, HC, NOx, PM** → *Local air-quality pollutants* (regulated by Euro / BS norms).
> A vehicle may have **zero tailpipe emissions** (BEV) but still have an **upstream CO₂ footprint** from power generation.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Dirty Chai vs Filtered Water Analogy"

> [!TIP]
> **Imagine your daily commute as preparing your morning chai (tea):**
>
> 🫖 **The Conventional ICE (Petrol/Diesel):** Burning wood/coal in an open chulha — you get heat and your tea, but the kitchen fills with smoke (CO, PM, NOx). The neighbours complain, the walls blacken, and your eyes sting.
>
> ⚡ **The Low Emission Vehicle (HEV/PHEV/BEV):** An induction cooktop or electric kettle. The same tea is made, the kitchen stays clean, neighbours are happy, and energy is delivered precisely where needed with minimal waste.
>
> The "smoke" leaving your vehicle's tailpipe is what LEV technology aims to *eliminate or drastically reduce*. Just as an induction cooktop uses electromagnetic energy transfer (no combustion), a BEV uses electrochemical energy transfer (no tailpipe combustion).

## 1.4 Governing Emission Standards (Indian & Global Context)

The KTU syllabus specifically links LEVs to compliance with emission regulations. The current Indian context is the **Bharat Stage VI (BS-VI)** norms, equivalent to Euro 6.

| Standard | Implementation (India) | Key Tightening |
|---|---|---|
| India 2000 (Euro 1) | 2000 | Baseline |
| Bharat Stage II (Euro 2) | 2001 | Catalytic converter mandate |
| Bharat Stage III (Euro 3) | 2005 | On-Board Diagnostics (OBD-I) |
| Bharat Stage IV (Euro 4) | 2010 | Reduced NOx, PM by 50% |
| **Bharat Stage VI (Euro 6)** | **2020** (Leapfrogged from BS-IV) | **PM ↓ 82.5%, NOx ↓ 68%** vs BS-IV |
| Euro 7 / BS-VII | Proposed 2025+ | Real Driving Emissions (RDE) focus |

**Physical constants relevant to LEV analysis (memorise these for KTU):**

- Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (Gasoline): **AFRₛ = 14.7 : 1**
- Stoichiometric AFR (Diesel): **AFRₛ = 14.5 : 1**
- Stoichiometric AFR (CNG/Methane): **AFRₛ = 17.2 : 1**
- Lower Heating Value of Petrol: **LHV ≈ 44 MJ/kg**
- Density of Petrol: **ρ ≈ 0.75 kg/L**
- Density of Diesel: **ρ ≈ 0.85 kg/L**
- Energy density of Li-ion Battery: **≈ 0.6–0.9 MJ/kg** (vs petrol's 44 MJ/kg)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Classification of Low Emission Vehicles (KTU Module 1 Core)

LEVs are classified based on the **degree of electrification** and the **energy source** used for propulsion. This is the most frequently asked concept in KTU examinations.

### Tier 1: Conventional ICE with After-Treatment (Mild LEV)

These are standard petrol/diesel vehicles retrofitted with advanced emission control hardware.

- **Three-Way Catalytic Converter (TWC)** for petrol engines
- **Diesel Oxidation Catalyst (DOC)**
- **Diesel Particulate Filter (DPF)**
- **Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR)** with AdBlue injection
- **Exhaust Gas Recirculation (EGR)**

### Tier 2: Alternative Fuel Vehicles (AFV) — True LEVs

| Vehicle Type | Fuel | Tailpipe CO₂ Reduction | Indian Adoption |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CNG Vehicle** | Compressed Natural Gas | ~20–25% vs petrol | High (Delhi, Mumbai public transport) |
| **LPG Vehicle** | Liquefied Petroleum Gas | ~10–15% | Moderate (autorickshaws) |
| **Biodiesel Vehicle (B20)** | 20% bio + 80% diesel | ~15% (well-to-wheel) | Pilot stage |
| **Ethanol Vehicle (E20, E85)** | 20–85% ethanol blend | ~15–60% | India targeting **E20 by 2025–26** |
| **Hydrogen ICE Vehicle** | H₂ combustion | Zero CO₂ (water vapour only) | R&D stage |

### Tier 3: Hybrid Electric Vehicles (HEV) — Advanced LEV

A hybrid uses **two or more distinct energy sources**, typically an ICE + electric motor + battery.

**Three Sub-Architectures (memorise for 7-mark questions):**

1. **Micro/Mild Hybrid (12V or 48V):** Start-stop system + brake energy regeneration. No pure electric drive.
2. **Full Hybrid (HEV) — Toyota Prius architecture:** ICE + battery + motor. Can drive on electricity alone at low speeds (typically < 2 km). **Cannot be externally charged.**
3. **Plug-in Hybrid (PHEV):** Larger battery (8–20 kWh). Externally chargeable. Electric-only range 30–80 km. Example: Toyota Prius Prime, MG Hector Plus.

### Tier 4: Zero Emission Vehicles (ZEV)

- **Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV):** Powered solely by grid-charged batteries. Examples: Tata Nexon EV, MG ZS EV, Tesla Model 3.
- **Fuel Cell Electric Vehicle (FCEV):** Powered by a **Proton Exchange Membrane (PEM) fuel cell** using compressed hydrogen. By-product is pure water. Examples: Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table for last-minute revision. Every formula below has appeared in KTU 2024 Scheme questions.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Unit | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Tailpipe CO₂ from fuel** | $m_{CO_2} = m_{fuel} \times \alpha_C \times \frac{44}{12}$ | kg | Emission inventory |
| 2 | **Specific CO₂ emission per km** | $E_{CO_2} = \frac{m_{CO_2}}{d}$ | g/km | Euro/BS compliance |
| 3 | **Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC)** | $\text{BSFC} = \frac{\dot{m}_{fuel}}{P_{brake}} \times 3600$ | g/kWh | Engine efficiency indicator |
| 4 | **Brake Thermal Efficiency (η_bt)** | $\eta_{bt} = \frac{P_{brake}}{\dot{m}_{fuel} \times \text{LHV}} \times 100$ | % | LEV ICE benchmark |
| 5 | **Engine displacement CO₂ factor** | $E_{CO_2,urban} = a \cdot V_d + b$ | g/km | Empirical (e.g., Toyota formula) |
| 6 | **Fuel-cell efficiency (Hydrogen)** | $\eta_{FC} = \frac{V_{cell}}{1.482} \times 100$ | % | FCEV analysis |
| 7 | **Battery State of Charge (SoC)** | $\text{SoC} = \frac{Q_{remaining}}{Q_{rated}} \times 100$ | % | BEV/HEV energy management |
| 8 | **Regenerative braking energy** | $E_{regen} = \frac{1}{2} m (v_1^2 - v_2^2) \cdot \eta_{regen}$ | J | HEV range extension |
| 9 | **Vehicle kinetic energy** | $E_k = \frac{1}{2} m v^2$ | J | Energy regeneration analysis |
| 10 | **Equivalence ratio (combustion)** | $\phi = \frac{(A/F)_{actual}}{(A/F)_{stoich}}$ | dimensionless | Emission control lever |

Where:
- $m_{CO_2}$ = mass of CO₂ emitted
- $m_{fuel}$ = mass of fuel burnt
- $\alpha_C$ = carbon mass fraction of fuel (≈ 0.85 for petrol, 0.87 for diesel)
- $V_d$ = engine displacement volume (L)
- $V_{cell}$ = fuel cell operating voltage (V)
- $Q_{remaining}, Q_{rated}$ = remaining and rated battery capacity (Ah)
- $v_1, v_2$ = vehicle velocities before/after braking (m/s)
- $\eta_{regen}$ = regen efficiency (typically 0.3–0.7)

## 2.3 Why Hybridization Reduces Emissions — The "Why" Behind LEV

The "How" of every LEV strategy reduces to **three physical levers**:

1. **Lever 1 — Smaller, Optimised Engine:** A full hybrid can use a 1.5 L Atkinson-cycle engine instead of a 2.5 L Otto engine, since the electric motor supplements peak loads. Smaller engine = lower fuel burn = lower CO₂.

2. **Lever 2 — Operating Point Optimisation:** Conventional ICEs are most efficient (~38%) at one specific RPM-load point. Hybrids use the **motor to absorb torque transients** and keep the ICE near its sweet spot. **Penalty avoided** = excess fuel burn.

3. **Lever 3 — Energy Recovery (Regenerative Braking):** In cities, ~30–50% of fuel energy is lost to brake heat. The electric motor acts as a generator, converting this to stored battery energy.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world utility:** These three levers collectively allow a Toyota Prius (1.8 L HEV) to deliver petrol-engine-class torque with **~50% lower fuel consumption** in urban duty cycles — directly translating to **LEV certification** under Indian and global norms.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Tailpipe CO₂ Mass from Fuel Consumption

**Problem Statement:** A passenger car consumes 5.0 L of petrol over a 60 km urban drive. Calculate the specific CO₂ emission in g/km, given: density of petrol = 0.75 kg/L, carbon mass fraction = 0.85, and the molecular weight ratio of CO₂ to C is 44/12.

### Step 1: Convert fuel volume to mass

$$ m_{fuel} = V_{fuel} \times \rho_{petrol} = 5.0 \text{ L} \times 0.75 \text{ kg/L} = 3.75 \text{ kg} $$

### Step 2: Calculate the mass of carbon in the fuel

$$ m_C = m_{fuel} \times \alpha_C = 3.75 \text{ kg} \times 0.85 = 3.1875 \text{ kg} $$

**Conversion logic:** Petrol is a hydrocarbon mixture (C₈H₁₈ average). Of every kg of petrol, 0.85 kg is combustible carbon; the rest is hydrogen, sulphur, and inert additives.

### Step 3: Apply stoichiometric combustion

Each carbon atom oxidises to one CO₂ molecule:

$$ C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 $$

$$ m_{CO_2} = m_C \times \frac{M_{CO_2}}{M_C} = m_C \times \frac{44}{12} $$

Substituting:

$$ m_{CO_2} = 3.1875 \times \frac{44}{12} = 3.1875 \times 3.6667 $$

$$ m_{CO_2} = 11.6875 \text{ kg} $$

### Step 4: Calculate specific emission (g/km)

$$ E_{CO_2} = \frac{m_{CO_2}}{d} = \frac{11.6875 \text{ kg} \times 1000 \text{ g/kg}}{60 \text{ km}} $$

$$ E_{CO_2} = \frac{11687.5}{60} = 194.79 \text{ g/km} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result: 194.79 g/km** — This is typical of a non-LEV petrol sedan (BS-VI norm is 130 g/km for sub-1.5 L engines). The car fails LEV/BS-VI compliance. **Conversion to a hybrid or CNG would reduce this by 25–50%.**

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio for a Generic Hydrocarbon

**Derivation Goal:** Show that for a generic hydrocarbon fuel CₓHᵧ, the stoichiometric AFR depends only on x and y.

### Step 1: Write the complete combustion equation

$$ C_x H_y + \left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right) O_2 \rightarrow x CO_2 + \frac{y}{2} H_2 O $$

### Step 2: Compute moles of O₂ required per mole of fuel

$$ n_{O_2} = x + \frac{y}{4} $$

### Step 3: Account for nitrogen in air (air is 21% O₂ + 79% N₂ by volume)

$$ n_{air} = 4.762 \times \left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right) $$

**Conversion logic:** The factor 4.762 = 1/0.21, i.e., 1 mole of O₂ is accompanied by 3.762 moles of N₂ in air.

### Step 4: Mass-based AFR using molecular weights (Air = 28.97 g/mol, C = 12, H = 1)

$$ (A/F)_{stoich} = \frac{n_{air} \times 28.97}{x \times 12 + y \times 1} $$

### Step 5: Verify for petrol (C₈H₁₈, octane)

$$ (A/F)_{stoich} = \frac{4.762 \times (8 + 18/4) \times 28.97}{8 \times 12 + 18 \times 1} $$

$$ = \frac{4.762 \times 12.5 \times 28.97}{96 + 18} = \frac{1724.5}{114} $$

$$ (A/F)_{stoich} = 15.13 \approx 14.7 \text{ (empirical)} $$

The small discrepancy arises because petrol is a *mixture* of isooctane, toluene, xylenes, etc. The KTU reference value is **14.7 : 1**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — BSFC and Brake Thermal Efficiency Linkage

**Problem:** An engine develops 50 kW at the crankshaft while consuming 12 kg/hr of petrol. Find BSFC and η_bt. (LHV of petrol = 44 MJ/kg)

### Step 1: Convert fuel flow to kg/s

$$ \dot{m}_{fuel} = \frac{12 \text{ kg/hr}}{3600 \text{ s/hr}} = 0.003333 \text{ kg/s} $$

### Step 2: Calculate chemical input power

$$ \dot{Q}_{in} = \dot{m}_{fuel} \times \text{LHV} = 0.003333 \times 44{,}000{,}000 = 146{,}666 \text{ W} = 146.67 \text{ kW} $$

### Step 3: Brake Thermal Efficiency

$$ \eta_{bt} = \frac{P_{brake}}{\dot{Q}_{in}} \times 100 = \frac{50}{146.67} \times 100 $$

$$ \eta_{bt} = 34.09\% $$

### Step 4: BSFC

$$ \text{BSFC} = \frac{\dot{m}_{fuel} \times 3600}{P_{brake}} = \frac{12 \text{ kg/hr}}{50 \text{ kW}} = 0.240 \text{ kg/kWh} $$

$$ \text{BSFC} = 240 \text{ g/kWh} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Benchmark:** A modern LEV-grade Atkinson-cycle engine achieves BSFC ≈ 200 g/kWh, η_bt ≈ 38–41%. Anything above 250 g/kWh in the KTU answer sheet will be marked down as "non-LEV grade engine".

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Regenerative Braking Energy Recovery (HEV)

**Problem:** A 1500 kg HEV decelerates from 60 km/h to 20 km/h. Find the energy recoverable if η_regen = 0.6.

### Step 1: Convert speeds to m/s

$$ v_1 = 60 \text{ km/h} = \frac{60 \times 1000}{3600} = 16.667 \text{ m/s} $$

$$ v_2 = 20 \text{ km/h} = \frac{20 \times 1000}{3600} = 5.556 \text{ m/s} $$

### Step 2: Kinetic energy difference

$$ \Delta E_k = \frac{1}{2} m (v_1^2 - v_2^2) $$

$$ = \frac{1}{2} \times 1500 \times (16.667^2 - 5.556^2) $$

$$ = 750 \times (277.78 - 30.86) = 750 \times 246.92 $$

$$ \Delta E_k = 185{,}190 \text{ J} = 185.19 \text{ kJ} $$

### Step 3: Apply regen efficiency

$$ E_{regen} = \Delta E_k \times \eta_{regen} = 185.19 \times 0.6 = 111.11 \text{ kJ} $$

### Step 4: Convert to kWh for battery storage (HEV battery handles kW-scale transients)

$$ E_{regen} = \frac{111.11}{3.6 \times 10^6} = 3.086 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kWh} \approx 30.9 \text{ Wh} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A 30.9 Wh recovery in a single deceleration event may seem small, but accumulated over a typical city drive with 200+ such events, regen can extend EV range by 8–15% — a key reason HEVs qualify as LEVs.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Master Classification of Low Emission Vehicles

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[LOW EMISSION VEHICLE LEV] --> B[CONVENTIONAL ICE WITH AFTER TREATMENT]
    A --> C[ALTERNATIVE FUEL VEHICLES]
    A --> D[HYBRID ELECTRIC VEHICLES]
    A --> E[ZERO EMISSION VEHICLES]

    B --> B1[BS6 Petrol TWC]
    B --> B2[BS6 Diesel DOC DPF SCR]
    B --> B3[EGR Cooled System]

    C --> C1[CNG Vehicles]
    C --> C2[LPG Vehicles]
    C --> C3[Ethanol E20 E85]
    C --> C4[Biodiesel B20 B100]
    C --> C5[Hydrogen ICE]

    D --> D1[Mild Hybrid 12V 48V]
    D --> D2[Full Hybrid HEV]
    D --> D3[Plug in Hybrid PHEV]

    E --> E1[Battery Electric BEV]
    E --> E2[Fuel Cell FCEV]

    B1 --> F1[Tailpipe CO HC NOx reduced 50 to 80 percent]
    C1 --> F2[CO2 reduced 20 to 25 percent]
    D2 --> F3[CO2 reduced 30 to 50 percent]
    D3 --> F4[CO2 reduced 50 to 70 percent]
    E1 --> F5[Zero tailpipe emissions ZEV]
    E2 --> F6[Zero tailpipe emissions water vapour only]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Architecture of a Full Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[FUEL TANK Petrol] --> B[ICE ENGINE Atkinson Cycle]
    B --> C[POWER SPLIT PLANETARY GEAR]
    D[HV BATTERY Lithium Ion] --> E[MOTOR GENERATOR MG1]
    D --> F[MOTOR GENERATOR MG2]
    E --> C
    F --> G[FINAL DRIVE DIFFERENTIAL]
    C --> G
    G --> H[WHEELS]
    B --> I[EXHAUST TWC THREE WAY CATALYST]
    I --> J[CLEAN TAILPIPE EMISSION]
    D <--> K[INVERTER DC to AC]
    K <--> E
    K <--> F
    L[BRAKE PEDAL] --> M[REGEN CONTROLLER]
    M --> F
    M --> D
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Emission Formation and After-Treatment Chain

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[FUEL AIR MIXTURE] --> B[COMBUSTION CHAMBER]
    B --> C[RAW EXHAUST GAS]
    C --> D[CO Carbon Monoxide]
    C --> E[HC Unburnt Hydrocarbons]
    C --> F[NOx Oxides of Nitrogen]
    C --> G[PM Particulate Matter]
    D --> H[TWC DOC]
    E --> H
    F --> I[SCR EGR]
    G --> J[DPF]
    H --> K[CATALYTIC CONVERSION CO2 H2O N2]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[LEV COMPLIANT CLEAN EMISSION]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Fuel Cell Electric Vehicle (FCEV) Power Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[H2 TANK 700 bar] --> B[REGULATOR]
    B --> C[PEM FUEL CELL STACK]
    D[AMBIENT AIR] --> E[AIR COMPRESSOR]
    E --> C
    C --> F[DC DC CONVERTER]
    F --> G[HV BATTERY BUFFER]
    G --> H[INVERTER]
    H --> I[TRACTION MOTOR]
    I --> J[WHEELS]
    C --> K[COOLANT RADIATOR WATER BYPRODUCT]
    C --> L[PURE WATER VAPOUR EXHAUST]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Diagram Interpretation Tip:** When asked to "draw the architecture of an FCEV" in the exam, examiners award full marks only if you show: H₂ storage → pressure regulator → fuel cell stack → DC-DC converter → battery buffer → inverter → traction motor → wheels. The **water-vapour exhaust** is the unique ZEV identifier.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]
**Define a Low Emission Vehicle (LEV). List any four categories of LEVs with one example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 for definition + 2 for list):**

A Low Emission Vehicle (LEV) is a motor vehicle that emits significantly lower levels of regulated pollutants (CO, HC, NOx, PM) and/or greenhouse gases (CO₂) per kilometre compared to a baseline conventional ICE vehicle, as certified under emission norms such as BS-VI or Euro 6.

**Four categories:**

1. **Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV)** — Example: Tata Nexon EV (zero tailpipe emissions)
2. **Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV)** — Example: Toyota Camry Hybrid (CO₂ ~100 g/km)
3. **Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicle (PHEV)** — Example: Volvo XC90 T8
4. **Compressed Natural Gas (CNG) Vehicle** — Example: Maruti Suzuki Eeco CNG

> [!TIP]
> **Mark split:** Definition = 1 mark | Four categories with examples = 2 marks (½ mark each). Skipping examples forfeits 1 mark.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Internal Assessment]
**What is Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC)? State its unit and the typical BSFC value for a modern LEV-grade petrol engine.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** BSFC is the mass of fuel consumed per unit of brake power output per unit time. It is a direct inverse indicator of engine thermal efficiency — lower BSFC = better efficiency.

**Unit (0.5 mark):** g/kWh (grams per kilowatt-hour)

**Typical value (1 mark):** A modern LEV-grade Atkinson-cycle petrol engine achieves BSFC in the range of **200–220 g/kWh** at its best operating point. Conventional Otto-cycle engines are 250–280 g/kWh.

> [!NOTE]
> Examiners often deduct a mark if students write BSFC in "kg/kWh" or "litres/kWh". Always use **g/kWh**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A. [14 Marks — KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]
#### (a) [7 Marks] Explain the working principle of a Full Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV) with the help of a power-flow block diagram. Discuss how it qualifies as an LEV.

#### (b) [7 Marks] A 1200 kg HEV decelerates from 80 km/h to a standstill, recovering 60% of its kinetic energy. The regen energy is stored in a 1.5 kWh battery initially at 40% SoC. Calculate (i) recovered energy in kWh, (ii) final SoC, and (iii) the equivalent reduction in tailpipe CO₂ if the ICE was running 100% of the time (assume fuel-to-CO₂ conversion of 2.31 kg CO₂ per kWh of petrol).

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks:**

A full HEV (e.g., Toyota Hybrid Synergy Drive) combines an **Atkinson-cycle ICE**, a **planetary gearset** as a power-split device, **two motor-generators (MG1 and MG2)**, and a **high-voltage battery** (~1.5 kWh).

**Working principle (4 marks):**
- At low speeds/light loads, MG2 alone drives the wheels — ICE is off (zero fuel, zero emission).
- At cruising speeds, ICE drives the wheels directly through the planetary gear; MG1 acts as a generator to charge the battery.
- At acceleration, both ICE and MG2 combine to deliver peak torque.
- During braking, MG2 becomes a generator, recovering kinetic energy (regenerative braking).
- The planetary gear acts as a continuously variable transmission (CVT), keeping the ICE at its most efficient operating point.

**LEV qualification (3 marks):**
- Fuel consumption drops 30–50% in urban duty cycles (Indian ARAI test).
- CO₂ emissions fall from ~180 g/km (conventional) to ~90–100 g/km → qualifies under BS-VI and CARB SULEV norms.
- Tailpipe CO, HC, NOx are reduced proportionally due to cleaner combustion at the optimised ICE operating point.
- The 3-way catalyst operates at higher steady-state temperatures → higher conversion efficiency.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall (KTU Valuation):** Students often *forget to mention the planetary gearset's role* as a power-split device and write only "ICE + Motor + Battery". This loses 1.5 marks. Always mention: **planetary gear = power split = continuous variable ratio**.

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks:**

**(i) Recovered energy in kWh (3 marks):**

$$ v_1 = 80 \text{ km/h} = \frac{80 \times 1000}{3600} = 22.222 \text{ m/s} $$

$$ \Delta E_k = \frac{1}{2} m v_1^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 1200 \times (22.222)^2 $$

$$ = 600 \times 493.83 = 296{,}296 \text{ J} $$

$$ = \frac{296{,}296}{3.6 \times 10^6} = 0.0823 \text{ kWh} $$

$$ E_{regen} = 0.0823 \times 0.60 = 0.0494 \text{ kWh} $$

**(ii) Final SoC (2 marks):**

$$ \Delta \text{SoC} = \frac{E_{regen}}{Q_{rated}} \times 100 = \frac{0.0494}{1.5} \times 100 = 3.29\% $$

$$ \text{SoC}_{final} = 40\% + 3.29\% = 43.29\% $$

**(iii) Equivalent CO₂ reduction (2 marks):**

$$ m_{CO_2, saved} = E_{regen} \times 2.31 = 0.0494 \times 2.31 = 0.114 \text{ kg} $$

$$ = 114 \text{ g of CO}_2 \text{ avoided per braking event} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Valuation Tip:** Step (i) needs both unit conversions (km/h → m/s, J → kWh) shown explicitly. Examiners deduct 1 mark per missing conversion. Step (iii) is the "application" component — many students stop at (ii) and lose 2 marks.

---

### Question B. [14 Marks — KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted]
#### (a) [7 Marks] Discuss the role of a Three-Way Catalytic Converter (TWC) in reducing tailpipe emissions from a petrol engine. How does it enable BS-VI compliance?

#### (b) [7 Marks] A 1.2 L three-cylinder petrol engine runs at 3000 RPM with 80% volumetric efficiency. The measured BSFC is 240 g/kWh and brake torque is 90 Nm. Calculate (i) the brake power, (ii) the fuel mass flow rate, and (iii) the specific CO₂ emission in g/km if the test cycle covers 12 km in 18 minutes.

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Role of TWC (4 marks):**
- The TWC simultaneously oxidises **CO → CO₂** and **HC → CO₂ + H₂O**, and reduces **NOx → N₂**.
- It contains two monolith brick substrates: the first promotes oxidation (platinum/palladium), the second promotes reduction (rhodium).
- The catalyst operates only above **light-off temperature (~250 °C)** and at the **stoichiometric air-fuel ratio (λ = 1 ± 0.01)**. A closed-loop lambda sensor maintains this narrow window.

**BS-VI compliance (3 marks):**
- BS-VI mandates **PM limit of 4.5 mg/km** (for petrol) and NOx of 60 mg/km. The TWC removes > 95% of CO, HC, and NOx when the lambda is precisely controlled.
- Modern TWC units are paired with **GPF (Gasoline Particulate Filter)** to capture the ultrafine PM emitted by direct-injection petrol engines.
- On-Board Diagnostics (OBD-II) continuously monitors catalyst efficiency and triggers a Malfunction Indicator Lamp (MIL) if conversion efficiency drops below threshold.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Writing "catalyst removes 100% emissions" is wrong. The correct phrasing is "**> 95% conversion efficiency** under stoichiometric operation." Examiners mark this strictly.

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks:**

**(i) Brake Power (2 marks):**

$$ P_{brake} = \frac{2 \pi N T}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 3000 \times 90}{60} $$

$$ = \frac{2 \times 3.1416 \times 3000 \times 90}{60} = \frac{1{,}696{,}460}{60} = 28{,}274.33 \text{ W} $$

$$ P_{brake} = 28.27 \text{ kW} $$

**(ii) Fuel mass flow rate (2.5 marks):**

$$ \dot{m}_{fuel} = \frac{\text{BSFC} \times P_{brake}}{3600 \times 1000} $$

$$ = \frac{240 \text{ g/kWh} \times 28.27 \text{ kW}}{3600 \text{ s}} $$

**Conversion logic:** BSFC is in g/kWh, multiply by power (kW) to get g/h, then divide by 3600 to get g/s.

$$ = \frac{6784.8 \text{ g/h}}{3600 \text{ s/h}} = 1.8847 \text{ g/s} $$

$$ \dot{m}_{fuel} = 0.0018847 \text{ kg/s} = 6.785 \text{ kg/hr} $$

**(iii) Specific CO₂ emission (2.5 marks):**

First, total fuel consumed in 18 minutes:

$$ m_{fuel,total} = \dot{m}_{fuel} \times t = 1.8847 \text{ g/s} \times (18 \times 60) \text{ s} $$

$$ = 1.8847 \times 1080 = 2035.5 \text{ g} = 2.0355 \text{ kg} $$

CO₂ produced:

$$ m_{CO_2} = m_{fuel} \times \alpha_C \times \frac{44}{12} = 2.0355 \times 0.85 \times 3.6667 $$

$$ = 6.343 \text{ kg} = 6343 \text{ g} $$

Specific CO₂:

$$ E_{CO_2} = \frac{6343 \text{ g}}{12 \text{ km}} = 528.6 \text{ g/km} $$

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** The value 528.6 g/km looks unrealistically high because the engine is operating at full torque continuously. In a real test cycle, average load is ~30%. KTU examiners expect students to **comment on the duty cycle assumption** (1 mark) — many skip this and lose marks. **Always state assumptions explicitly.**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Reasons for Marks Deduction in LEV Questions:**
>
> 1. **Conflating "Zero Emission" with "Zero CO₂ footprint":** A BEV has zero *tailpipe* emissions but a *generation-dependent* upstream CO₂ footprint. Use precise language: "**zero tailpipe emissions**" — not "no emissions at all."
>
> 2. **Ignoring Units:** BSFC must be in **g/kWh**, CO₂ in **g/km**, power in **kW**. Mismatched units = 0.5–1 mark deduction per step.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the Planetary Gearset in HEV diagrams:** This is the most common omission in HEV architecture questions. Worth 1.5–2 marks.
>
> 4. **Confusing AFR with equivalence ratio (φ):** AFR = 14.7 means stoichiometric; AFR < 14.7 means rich; AFR > 14.7 means lean. Equivalence ratio is the **inverse ratio** — students mix these up and lose 1 mark.
>
> 5. **Skipping the Assumption Statement:** In numerical problems, always state the duty cycle (urban/highway), ambient conditions, and fuel type assumption. KTU 2024 scheme follows strict outcome-based evaluation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist for LEV (KTU PEMUT744 Module 1):**

- ✅ **LEV = umbrella term** covering ZEV, HEV, PHEV, BEV, FCEV, CNG, LPG, E20, Biodiesel.
- ✅ **Six regulated pollutants:** CO, HC, NOx, PM, CO₂, SO₂. CO₂ is a GHG, the others are local air pollutants.
- ✅ **BS-VI (India, 2020)** = Euro 6 equivalent. PM ↓ 82.5% and NOx ↓ 68% vs BS-IV.
- ✅ **Stoichiometric AFR:** Petrol = **14.7:1**, Diesel = **14.5:1**, CNG = **17.2:1**.
- ✅ **BSFC of LEV engine ≈ 200–220 g/kWh**, brake thermal efficiency **≥ 35%**.
- ✅ **Three levers of LEV emission reduction:** (1) Engine downsizing, (2) Operating-point optimisation, (3) Regenerative braking.
- ✅ **HEV architecture:** ICE + Battery + MG1 + MG2 + **Planetary gearset (power-split CVT)**.
- ✅ **PHEV vs HEV:** PHEV has a larger battery (8–20 kWh) and is **externally chargeable**; HEV is not.
- ✅ **BEV = Zero tailpipe emissions**, but has upstream grid-emission footprint.
- ✅ **FCEV exhaust is pure water vapour** — the only true zero-emission transport.
- ✅ **CO₂ emission formula:** $E_{CO_2} = \frac{m_{fuel} \times \alpha_C \times 44/12}{d}$ → result in g/km.
- ✅ **Regenerative braking energy:** $E_{regen} = \frac{1}{2} m (v_1^2 - v_2^2) \times \eta_{regen}$ — always use SI units (m/s).
- ✅ **Fuel-cell efficiency:** $\eta_{FC} = \frac{V_{cell}}{1.482} \times 100$ (the 1.482 V is the thermodynamic HHV limit).
- ✅ **Equivalence ratio φ = 1** → stoichiometric. **φ > 1** → rich (high CO, HC). **φ < 1** → lean (high NOx).
- ✅ **LHV of petrol = 44 MJ/kg**; density = 0.75 kg/L. Memorise for derivations.
- ✅ **Key exam phrasing:** Always say "**zero tailpipe emissions**" for BEV/FCEV, not "zero emissions."

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 1 Notes — Low Emission Vehicle (LEV).** Cross-reference with Module 1 sub-topics: EV Architecture, Hybrid Configurations, Emission Control Technologies, and Alternative Fuels for comprehensive KTU 2024 preparation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transitional Low Emission Vehicle (TLEV)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT744 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Transitional Low Emission Vehicle (TLEV) ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Transitional Low Emission Vehicle (TLEV):** As defined by the **California Air Resources Board (CARB)** under the Low Emission Vehicle (LEV) program, a TLEV is a vehicle that produces **75%** fewer emissions than the average 1990 model-year vehicle at 50,000 miles and **25%** fewer emissions at 100,000 miles. It represents the *first tier* in a tiered regulatory framework introduced to progressively reduce criteria pollutants and ozone-forming compounds from light-duty passenger cars and light-duty trucks. The TLEV category was officially introduced under **CARB's 1990 LEV regulation** and began phasing into production from **1994 onwards**. It served as a *bridge* between the older Tier-0 (conventional) emission standards and the more stringent **LEV, ULEV, and SULEV** categories that followed. > [!NOTE] > **Key Regulatory Body:** California Air Resources Board (CARB) — the benchmark agency whose standards are adopted by several US states and influence global emission norms (including India's BS-VI and proposed BS-VII). --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!TIP] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Student Grading System":** > Imagine a teacher moving a class from a strict "Pass/Fail" system to a graded system (A+, A, B, C, D). The teacher introduces a **"D grade"** category first as a *transitional step* — better than failing, but not yet excellent. **TLEV is the automotive equivalent of that "D grade."** It is *better* than a traditional dirty car, but it is *not* the cleanest. It was the first rung on the ladder toward cleaner air. - A **conventional 1990 car** = "Failing student" (high emissions) - A **TLEV** = "Just-passing student" (modest improvement) - A **LEV / ULEV** = "Average / Good student" - A **SULEV** = "Topper" (extremely low emissions) - A **ZEV (Electric vehicle)** = "Perfect score" (zero tailpipe emissions) ### 1.3 Core Performance Metrics of a TLEV > [!IMPORTANT] > **Primary Pollutants Regulated in a TLEV:** > 1. **NMOG (Non-Methane Organic Gases)** — measured jointly with **NOx** > 2. **CO (Carbon Monoxide)** > 3. **PM (Particulate Matter)** for diesel variants > 4. **HCHO (Formaldehyde)** > 5. **Evaporative Emissions** (fuel system losses) ### 1.4 Standard TLEV Emission Limits (50,000 mi / 5 yrs) | Pollutant | Symbol | TLEV Limit | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | Non-Methane Organic Gases + NOx | NMOG + NOx | **0.125** | g/mi | | Carbon Monoxide | CO | **3.4** | g/mi | | Formaldehyde | HCHO | **15** | mg/mi | | Pollutant | Symbol | TLEV Limit (100,000 mi) | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | NMOG + NOx | NMOG + NOx | **0.156** | g/mi | | Carbon Monoxide | CO | **4.2** | g/mi | | Formaldehyde | HCHO | **18** | mg/mi | > [!NOTE] > **Useful Life Periods:** Two durability periods are regulated — (a) **Intermediate Useful Life (IUL) = 5 yrs / 50,000 mi**, and (b) **Full Useful Life (FUL) = 11 yrs / 120,000 mi** (post-2004). TLEV limits are easier to meet at IUL and slightly relaxed at FUL. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Why was TLEV Created? — The Regulatory Logic The clean-air crisis in California during the 1980s forced CARB to act. However, **auto-manufacturers were not technologically ready** to leap directly from 1990-tier standards to ULEV/ZEV levels. Hence, CARB designed a *graduated* compliance system: $$\text{Conventional} \rightarrow \text{TLEV} \rightarrow \text{LEV} \rightarrow \text{ULEV} \rightarrow \text{SULEV} \rightarrow \text{ZEV}$$ This **step-function reduction** allowed OEMs to: - Reuse **proven** emission control technology. - Phase in R&D investments. - Maintain vehicle affordability for the average consumer. ### 2.2 Technology Suite Inside a TLEV A TLEV relies on **enhancement of conventional powertrain technology** rather than radical redesign. The key enabling hardware is: 1. **Three-Way Catalytic Converter (TWC)** with improved washcoat formulations (higher PGM loading). 2. **Closed-Loop Electronic Fuel Injection (EFI)** with adaptive lambda control ($\lambda = 1.000 \pm 0.01$). 3. **Optimized Combustion Chamber Geometry** to minimize crevice volumes and quench areas. 4. **Improved Evaporative Emission Controls** (activated carbon canisters with greater capacity). 5. **Exhaust Gas Recirculation (EGR)** — typically **5% to 10%** of exhaust mass flow. 6. **On-Board Diagnostics (OBD-I)** for early failure detection. ### 2.3 The Combined Pollutant Standard — NMOG + NOx > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why combine NMOG and NOx?** Both pollutants are **ozone precursors**. California, being a hot, sunny, photochemically-active region, suffers severe smog. Summing them gives a more **regulatory-relevant** metric than regulating each in isolation. The combined standard for a TLEV at **Intermediate Useful Life (IUL)** is: $$(\text{NMOG} + \text{NO}_x)_{\text{TLEV, IUL}} \leq 0.125 \; \text{g/mi}$$ For comparison, the *typical* 1990 baseline car emitted roughly **0.50 g/mi** of NMOG + NOx — a TLEV therefore achieves about a **75% reduction**. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | NMOG + NOx Combined Limit (TLEV, IUL) | $\text{NMOG} + \text{NO}_x \le 0.125$ | g/mi | 5-yr compliance cap | | NMOG + NOx Combined Limit (TLEV, FUL) | $\text{NMOG} + \text{NO}_x \le 0.156$ | g/mi | 11-yr compliance cap | | CO Limit (TLEV, IUL) | $\text{CO} \le 3.4$ | g/mi | 5-yr compliance cap | | HCHO Limit (TLEV, IUL) | $\text{HCHO} \le 15$ | mg/mi | Formaldehyde cap | | Emission Reduction vs. 1990 Baseline | $\eta_{\text{red}} = \dfrac{E_{1990} - E_{\text{TLEV}}}{E_{1990}} \times 100\%$ | % | Performance metric | | Stoichiometric AFR (gasoline) | $\text{AFR}_{\text{stoich}} = 14.7 : 1$ | ratio | Combustion reference | | Lambda Factor | $\lambda = \dfrac{\text{AFR}_{\text{actual}}}{\text{AFR}_{\text{stoich}}}$ | dimensionless | Mixture strength | | EGR Rate | $r_{\text{EGR}} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{\text{EGR}}}{\dot{m}_{\text{exhaust}}} \times 100\%$ | % | Recirculation fraction | | Weighted Fleet Average NMOG + NOx | $\bar{E}_{\text{fleet}} = \dfrac{\sum_i (n_i \cdot E_i)}{\sum_i n_i}$ | g/mi | Manufacturer average | > [!NOTE] > **Crucial Exam Tip:** Always quote the **NMOG + NOx combined** limit when asked about TLEV emission caps. Do NOT separate them unless the question specifically asks for individual species. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **OEM Compliance Strategy:** Manufacturers earn "LEV credits" for producing TLEVs. These credits can offset production of dirtier vehicles, giving flexibility in the model mix. - **Basis for Export Markets:** Indian BS-IV/BS-VI standards, Euro-3/Euro-4 norms, and Chinese National III/IV standards were all conceptually derived from CARB's tiered LEV framework. - **Diagnostic Benchmarking:** TLEV compliance tests (FTP-75, US06, SC03) form the procedural backbone for modern OBD-II fault threshold calibrations. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Percentage Emission Reduction of a TLEV versus a 1990 Baseline **Given:** - Baseline 1990 average passenger car: $E_{1990} = 0.50 \; \text{g/mi}$ of NMOG + NOx. - TLEV compliance cap (IUL): $E_{\text{TLEV}} = 0.125 \; \text{g/mi}$ of NMOG + NOx. **Find:** Percentage reduction $\eta_{\text{red}}$. **Step 1 — Write the general reduction formula.** $$ \begin{aligned} \eta_{\text{red}} &= \dfrac{E_{1990} - E_{\text{TLEV}}}{E_{1990}} \times 100\% \\ \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values.** $$ \begin{aligned} \eta_{\text{red}} &= \dfrac{0.50 - 0.125}{0.50} \times 100\% \\ &= \dfrac{0.375}{0.50} \times 100\% \\ &= 0.75 \times 100\% \\ \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Final result.** $$ \boxed{\eta_{\text{red}} = 75\%} $$ This confirms the CARB regulatory definition: a TLEV produces **75% fewer** smog-forming emissions than the average 1990 vehicle at the 50,000-mile mark. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Weighted Fleet-Average NMOG + NOx Compliance **Scenario:** A manufacturer produces 100,000 cars in a model year. Of these: - 30,000 are **TLEVs** with $E_1 = 0.125 \; \text{g/mi}$. - 50,000 are **LEVs** with $E_2 = 0.090 \; \text{g/mi}$. - 20,000 are **ULEVs** with $E_3 = 0.055 \; \text{g/mi}$. **Find:** The fleet-weighted average emission $\bar{E}_{\text{fleet}}$. **Step 1 — Apply the weighted mean formula.** $$ \begin{aligned} \bar{E}_{\text{fleet}} &= \dfrac{\sum_i (n_i \cdot E_i)}{\sum_i n_i} \\ \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Compute the numerator.** $$ \begin{aligned} \sum (n_i \cdot E_i) &= (30{,}000 \times 0.125) + (50{,}000 \times 0.090) + (20{,}000 \times 0.055) \\ &= 3750 + 4500 + 1100 \\ &= 9350 \;\text{g/mi}\cdot\text{vehicles} \\ \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Compute the denominator (total fleet).** $$ \begin{aligned} \sum n_i &= 30{,}000 + 50{,}000 + 20{,}000 = 100{,}000 \; \text{vehicles} \\ \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Final weighted average.** $$ \begin{aligned} \bar{E}_{\text{fleet}} &= \dfrac{9350}{100{,}000} \\ &= 0.0935 \; \text{g/mi} \\ \end{aligned} $$ $$ \boxed{\bar{E}_{\text{fleet}} = 0.0935 \; \text{g/mi}} $$ **Interpretation:** This fleet average of $0.0935 \; \text{g/mi}$ falls **between the TLEV and LEV limits**, satisfying the manufacturer's regulatory target if the corporate fleet average is mandated to be, e.g., $\le 0.100 \; \text{g/mi}$ (typical for an OEM's Phase-In LEV program). --- ### 3.3 Derivation: Required EGR Mass Flow for a TLEV-Compliant Engine **Given:** Engine displacement $V_d = 2.0 \; \text{L}$, Volumetric efficiency $\eta_v = 0.90$, Engine speed $N = 2500 \; \text{rpm}$, 4-stroke engine, intake air density $\rho_a = 1.20 \; \text{kg/m}^3$, target EGR rate $r_{\text{EGR}} = 8\%$. **Find:** EGR mass flow $\dot{m}_{\text{EGR}}$. **Step 1 — Air mass flow rate entering the engine.** $$ \begin{aligned} \dot{m}_{\text{air}} &= \dfrac{V_d \cdot \eta_v \cdot N \cdot \rho_a}{2 \times 60} \\ &= \dfrac{0.002 \cdot 0.90 \cdot 2500 \cdot 1.20}{120} \\ &= \dfrac{5.40}{120} \\ &= 0.045 \; \text{kg/s} \\ \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Assuming exhaust mass flow $\approx$ intake air mass flow for a stoichiometric gasoline engine:** $$ \begin{aligned} \dot{m}_{\text{exhaust}} &\approx \dot{m}_{\text{air}} = 0.045 \; \text{kg/s} \\ \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Apply the EGR rate formula.** $$ \begin{aligned} \dot{m}_{\text{EGR}} &= r_{\text{EGR}} \cdot \dot{m}_{\text{exhaust}} \\ &= 0.08 \cdot 0.045 \\ \end{aligned} $$ $$ \boxed{\dot{m}_{\text{EGR}} = 0.0036 \; \text{kg/s} = 3.6 \; \text{g/s}} $$ This small but precisely metered EGR flow is sufficient to suppress peak combustion temperatures and hence knock and NOx — a key enabler of TLEV compliance. ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 CARB LEV Tier Hierarchy (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[1990 Baseline Car
Tier-0 Conventional] --> B[Transitional LEV
TLEV - 1994 to 2003] B --> C[Low Emission Vehicle
LEV] C --> D[Ultra Low Emission Vehicle
ULEV] D --> E[Super Ultra Low Emission Vehicle
SULEV] E --> F[Zero Emission Vehicle
ZEV - BEV / FCEV] A1[NMOG+NOx approx 0.50 g/mi] -.-> A B1[NMOG+NOx less than 0.125 g/mi] -.-> B C1[NMOG+NOx less than 0.090 g/mi] -.-> C D1[NMOG+NOx less than 0.055 g/mi] -.-> D E1[NMOG+NOx less than 0.025 g/mi] -.-> E F1[NMOG+NOx equals 0 g/mi] -.-> F A --> G[Technology Bridge:
Improved TWC + EFI + EGR] G --> B B --> H[Advanced TWC
Multi-stage catalysts] H --> C C --> I[Close-Coupled Catalyst
+ Secondary Air Injection] I --> D D --> J[PZEV Hardware
GPF + Optimized Cold-Start] J --> E E --> K[Electric Powertrain
No Combustion] K --> F ``` ### 4.2 TLEV Powertrain Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR FT[Fuel Tank] --> CK[Carbon Canister
Evap Control] FT --> FRL[Fuel Rail] FRL --> INJ[Multi-Point EFI Injectors] INJ --> ENG[4-Stroke SI Engine
Optimized Combustion] AIR[Filtered Air] --> MAF[Mass Air Flow Sensor] MAF --> TB[Throttle Body] TB --> ENG ENG --> EGR[EGR Cooler and Valve] EGR --> ENG ENG --> EXG[Exhaust Manifold] EXG --> TWC[Three-Way Catalytic Converter
PGM Rich Washcoat] TWC --> O2[Post-Catalyst O2 Sensor] O2 --> ECU[Engine Control Unit] ECU --> INJ ECU --> TB ECU --> EGR O2 --> TAIL[Tailpipe] TWC --> TAIL ECU --> OBD[OBD-I Diagnostic Port] ``` ### 4.3 Emission Compliance Decision Flow (For Exam Recall) ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Start: Vehicle Tested on FTP-75 Cycle] --> S2[Measure NMOG, NOx, CO, HCHO] S2 --> S3{All Pollutants under TLEV cap?} S3 -- Yes --> S4[Certify as TLEV] S3 -- No --> S5{Under LEV cap?} S5 -- Yes --> S6[Certify as LEV] S5 -- No --> S7{Re-engineer and Re-test} S7 --> S2 S4 --> S8[Generate ZEP Credits
for Manufacturer Fleet] S6 --> S8 ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2019]** *Define Transitional Low Emission Vehicle (TLEV) and state the regulatory body that introduced this classification.* > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > A TLEV is a light-duty vehicle that produces **75% fewer** non-methane organic gas (NMOG) + NOx emissions than the average 1990 model-year vehicle, at the 50,000-mile useful life benchmark. It was introduced by the **California Air Resources Board (CARB)** in 1990 as the first tier of the Low Emission Vehicle (LEV) program. The combined NMOG + NOx cap for a TLEV is **0.125 g/mi** at intermediate useful life. *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for CARB, 1 mark for cap value.)* **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2020]** *List the major technology upgrades that enable a vehicle to meet TLEV standards.* > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > 1. **Improved Three-Way Catalytic Converter (TWC)** with higher platinum-group-metal (PGM) loading. *(1 mark)* > 2. **Closed-loop Electronic Fuel Injection (EFI)** with stoichiometric lambda control. *(1 mark)* > 3. **Exhaust Gas Recirculation (EGR)** plus enhanced evaporative emission control via larger carbon canisters. *(1 mark)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Do NOT confuse TLEV with Euro-1 or BS-II standards.** TLEV is a CARB (US-California) classification. Euro-1/BS-II are *separate* European/Indian regimes. > 2. Always quote the **NMOG + NOx combined** value (e.g., 0.125 g/mi) — students who quote only "NOx" or "HC" lose 2–3 marks. > 3. Do not write "TLEV reduces emissions by 75%" without specifying **the baseline year (1990) and useful life (50,000 mi)**. Examiners deduct marks for missing qualifiers. > 4. The technology discussion is *not* optional — a 14-mark answer without technology details will cap at 8–9 marks maximum. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2021]** **(a)** Explain in detail the **regulatory framework** of the California Air Resources Board (CARB) Low Emission Vehicle (LEV) program, with emphasis on the position of TLEV within the tiered structure. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Discuss the **exhaust and evaporative emission control technologies** used in a TLEV, and explain how each technology contributes to meeting TLEV standards. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **(a) — Regulatory Framework of CARB's LEV Program (7 marks):** 1. **Origin and Need:** The LEV program was promulgated by CARB in **1990** as a response to severe smog in the Los Angeles basin. It came into production effect from **model year 1994**. *(1 mark)* 2. **Tiered Architecture:** CARB categorized vehicles into five ascending cleanliness tiers: - **TLEV** (Transitional Low Emission Vehicle) — entry tier. - **LEV** — primary tier. - **ULEV** (Ultra Low Emission Vehicle). - **SULEV** (Super Ultra Low Emission Vehicle). - **ZEV** (Zero Emission Vehicle) — mandates for electrics/hydrogen. *(1 mark)* 3. **Compliance Metrics:** Regulated pollutants are **NMOG + NOx (combined)**, **CO**, **HCHO**, **PM**, and **evaporative emissions**. The combined NMOG + NOx cap for TLEV is **0.125 g/mi** at 50,000 mi. *(1 mark)* 4. **Two Useful Life Periods:** IUL = 5 yrs / 50,000 mi and FUL = 11 yrs / 120,000 mi. Limits are tighter at IUL and slightly relaxed at FUL. *(1 mark)* 5. **Fleet-Average Compliance:** Manufacturers must meet a corporate fleet average of NMOG + NOx, weighted by production volume, encouraging the production of cleaner-than-TLEV vehicles. *(1 mark)* 6. **Credit Trading:** Production of cleaner-than-required vehicles earns "LEV credits" usable to offset dirtier models, providing regulatory flexibility. *(1 mark)* 7. **Test Cycles:** Compliance uses the **FTP-75**, **US06**, and **SC03** chassis dynamometer cycles. *(1 mark)* **(b) — Emission Control Technologies in a TLEV (7 marks):** 1. **Three-Way Catalytic Converter (TWC) (2 marks):** - Reduces CO, HC, and NOx simultaneously when operated at stoichiometry ($\lambda = 1.000$). - **TLEV-specific upgrade:** Higher PGM (Pt/Pd/Rh) loading (often **2–3×** the conventional 1990 converter) and improved washcoat with higher surface area. - Cold-light-off time reduced to < 30 s via close-coupled placement. 2. **Electronic Fuel Injection (EFI) with Closed-Loop Lambda Control (2 marks):** - Maintains $\lambda = 1.000 \pm 0.01$ using a heated zirconia oxygen sensor in the exhaust stream. - Eliminates carburetor's mixture maldistribution — a key reason carbureted cars could not meet TLEV. 3. **Exhaust Gas Recirculation (EGR) (1.5 marks):** - Routes **5–10%** of exhaust mass back into the intake manifold. - Lowers peak combustion temperature by **~50–80 K**, suppressing thermal NOx formation. - Formula: $\dot{m}_{\text{EGR}} = r_{\text{EGR}} \cdot \dot{m}_{\text{exhaust}}$. 4. **Evaporative Emission Control (1 mark):** - **Activated carbon canister** captures fuel vapors from the tank; purged into the intake during decel. - Larger canisters (typically **2–3 L**) versus the 1990 baseline (1 L). 5. **On-Board Diagnostics (OBD-I) (0.5 marks):** - Detects misfires, catalyst degradation, and O2 sensor failure to maintain compliance throughout the vehicle's life. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2022]** **(a)** With the help of a flowchart, illustrate the **tiered classification** of vehicles under CARB's LEV program. Discuss the role of TLEV in this hierarchy. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A manufacturer's fleet consists of 40,000 TLEVs, 35,000 LEVs, 15,000 ULEVs, and 10,000 SULEVs. Their respective NMOG + NOx emissions are 0.125, 0.090, 0.055, and 0.025 g/mi. Calculate the **fleet-weighted average emission** and determine if the manufacturer meets a regulatory fleet average of **0.090 g/mi**. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **(a) — Tiered Classification with TLEV's Role (7 marks):** The student is expected to draw (or describe) the flowchart from **Section 4.1** of these notes and explain it. Key points to cover: 1. The hierarchy follows: $$\text{Baseline 1990} \rightarrow \text{TLEV} \rightarrow \text{LEV} \rightarrow \text{ULEV} \rightarrow \text{SULEV} \rightarrow \text{ZEV}$$ *(2 marks for the hierarchy itself)* 2. **TLEV's Role (5 marks):** - **Transitional Bridge:** TLEV acted as the *entry-level* cleaner category, allowing manufacturers to enter the LEV program with minimum R&D disruption. *(1 mark)* - **Compliance Baseline:** It established the **0.125 g/mi** NMOG + NOx floor above which no vehicle could be sold in CARB-regulated states. *(1 mark)* - **Credit Generator:** Each TLEV produced earns a small set of emission credits, with progressively higher credits for LEV/ULEV/SULEV/ZEV. *(1 mark)* - **Technology Demonstrator:** TLEV validated the use of advanced TWC + EFI + EGR for mass production. *(1 mark)* - **Phased Phase-out:** TLEV was effectively retired after MY 2003 in California once virtually all OEMs could comfortably meet the stricter LEV cap. *(1 mark)* **(b) — Fleet-Weighted Average Calculation (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Write the formula.** $$ \begin{aligned} \bar{E}_{\text{fleet}} &= \dfrac{\sum (n_i \cdot E_i)}{\sum n_i} \\ \end{aligned} $$ *(1 mark for writing the formula)* **Step 2 — Substitute values.** $$ \begin{aligned} \bar{E}_{\text{fleet}} &= \dfrac{(40{,}000)(0.125) + (35{,}000)(0.090) + (15{,}000)(0.055) + (10{,}000)(0.025)}{40{,}000 + 35{,}000 + 15{,}000 + 10{,}000} \\ \end{aligned} $$ *(1 mark for correct substitution)* **Step 3 — Compute the numerator term-by-term.** $$ \begin{aligned} 40{,}000 \times 0.125 &= 5000 \\ 35{,}000 \times 0.090 &= 3150 \\ 15{,}000 \times 0.055 &= 825 \\ 10{,}000 \times 0.025 &= 250 \\ \text{Sum} &= 5000 + 3150 + 825 + 250 = 9225 \\ \end{aligned} $$ *(2 marks for correct arithmetic)* **Step 4 — Compute the denominator.** $$ \begin{aligned} \sum n_i &= 100{,}000 \\ \end{aligned} $$ *(1 mark)* **Step 5 — Final calculation.** $$ \begin{aligned} \bar{E}_{\text{fleet}} &= \dfrac{9225}{100{,}000} = 0.09225 \; \text{g/mi} \\ \end{aligned} $$ *(1 mark)* **Step 6 — Compare with regulation.** $$ \boxed{0.09225 \; \text{g/mi} > 0.090 \; \text{g/mi}} $$ *(1 mark for correct comparison and final remark.)* **Conclusion:** The manufacturer's fleet does **NOT meet** the regulatory fleet average of 0.090 g/mi, falling short by **0.00225 g/mi**. The OEM must either increase the share of ULEVs/SULEVs or purchase emission credits from competitors. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:** - **TLEV = Transitional Low Emission Vehicle**, defined by **CARB** in 1990, effective MY 1994. - **TLEV NMOG + NOx cap = 0.125 g/mi** (IUL, 50,000 mi) and **0.156 g/mi** (FUL). - **CO cap = 3.4 g/mi** (IUL); **HCHO cap = 15 mg/mi** (IUL). - TLEV = **75% reduction** in NMOG + NOx vs. 1990 baseline. - Hierarchy: **Baseline → TLEV → LEV → ULEV → SULEV → ZEV**. - Key tech: **TWC (↑PGM), closed-loop EFI (λ=1), EGR (5–10%), carbon canister, OBD-I**. - TLEV is **NOT** Euro-1, NOT BS-II — it is a CARB tier. - TLEV combined standard uses **NMOG + NOx** (summed, not separate). - Fleet-average compliance uses the **weighted-mean** formula. - TLEV was effectively **retired after MY 2003** in California. - TLEV testing uses **FTP-75, US06, SC03** chassis cycles. - Lambda for stoichiometric gasoline combustion: **λ = 1.000**, AFR = **14.7 : 1**. - EGR mass flow formula: $\dot{m}_{\text{EGR}} = r_{\text{EGR}} \cdot \dot{m}_{\text{exhaust}}$. - Always quote the **useful-life period (IUL vs FUL)** alongside emission numbers. - A 14-mark answer without explicit technology discussion **loses 5–6 marks** — be thorough.

topics.title: Ultra Low Emission Vehicle (ULEV)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT744 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Ultra Low Emission Vehicle (ULEV)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
An **Ultra Low Emission Vehicle (ULEV)** is a motor vehicle certified to emit pollution levels **at least 50% lower than the average new vehicle of its model year**, as defined under the **California Air Resources Board (CARB)** Low Emission Vehicle (LEV) standards. ULEV classification is part of a tiered emission taxonomy that progressively tightens tailpipe output for criteria pollutants — Non-Methane Organic Gases (NMOG), Carbon Monoxide (CO), Oxides of Nitrogen (NOx), and Particulate Matter (PM).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, ULEV is studied as a foundational benchmark for **post-Euro 4 era emission compliance** and as a stepping stone toward PZEV (Partial Zero Emission Vehicle), SULEV (Super Ultra Low Emission Vehicle), and ZEV (Zero Emission Vehicle) classifications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** ULEV forms the *gateway standard* for modern automotive emission control. All hybrid powertrains, advanced after-treatment systems, and direct-injection gasoline engines are benchmarked against ULEV criteria before progressing to SULEV/PZEV certifications.

### 1.2 Tiered Emission Classification (CARB LEV Family)

| Tier | Acronym | Meaning | Relative Stringency |
|---|---|---|---|
| LEV | Low Emission Vehicle | Baseline (100% of average) | Standard |
| **ULEV** | **Ultra Low Emission Vehicle** | **50% cleaner** | **Stricter** |
| SULEV | Super Ultra Low Emission Vehicle | 90% cleaner | Very strict |
| PZEV | Partial Zero Emission Vehicle | Zero evaporative + SULEV tailpipe | Near-zero |
| ZEV | Zero Emission Vehicle | No tailpipe emissions | 100% zero |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cleaner Engine" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Filtered Water Pitcher:**
> Imagine a household water filter that removes 50% of impurities (ULEV), versus an advanced RO purifier that removes 90% (SULEV), versus no impurities at all (ZEV). The *engine block* is the water source, and the *after-treatment chain* (catalyst + EGR + filter) is the filtration cartridge. ULEV represents the first "premium" cartridge stage — clean enough to meet modern urban air-quality norms, but not the absolute benchmark.

For a B.Tech student: think of ULEV as the *industry-acceptable sweet spot* — a balance between **emission cleanliness** and **cost/feasibility** of mass production.

### 1.4 Key Standard Metrics (Bold Constants)

- **NMOG (Non-Methane Organic Gases):** **0.040 g/mi** (ULEV, 50k miles)
- **CO (Carbon Monoxide):** **1.7 g/mi**
- **NOx (Oxides of Nitrogen):** **0.2 g/mi**
- **PM (Particulate Matter):** **0.01 g/mi**
- **Useful life:** **120,000 miles / 15 years**
- **Compliance fuel:** **California Phase 2 gasoline (≤ 30 ppm sulfur)**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Pollutants Addressed by ULEV

ULEV is engineered to minimize four criteria pollutants and one greenhouse gas:

1. **CO (Carbon Monoxide):** Product of *incomplete combustion* due to local rich mixtures (λ < 1). Toxic; binds with haemoglobin (~240× affinity vs O₂).
2. **NOx (Nitric Oxide + Nitrogen Dioxide):** Formed at *high in-cylinder temperatures* (T > 1800 K) via the Zeldovich mechanism.
3. **NMOG (Non-Methane Organic Gases):** Unburnt + partially oxidised hydrocarbons (HC) from wall-quenching, crevice volumes, oil films.
4. **PM (Particulate Matter):** Especially relevant for direct-injection engines; sub-micron carbonaceous soot.
5. **CO₂ (Greenhouse gas):** Reduced via improved fuel efficiency (not a regulated criterion pollutant for ULEV tier, but monitored).

### 2.2 ULEV Enabling Technologies

To meet the **50% cleaner** threshold, the engine and after-treatment system must be redesigned across multiple subsystems:

- **Three-Way Catalytic Converter (TWC)** with high noble-metal loading (Pt, Pd, Rh) — the workhorse after-treatment.
- **Closed-Loop Electronic Fuel Injection (EFI)** with narrow ±1% lambda control.
- **Variable Valve Timing (VVT)** and **Variable Valve Lift (VVL)** to optimize residual gas fraction and pumping loss.
- **Exhaust Gas Recirculation (EGR)** — internal (EIVC, LIVC) and external low-pressure EGR — to suppress NOx.
- **Direct Injection (GDI)** for gasoline engines with stratified charge operation at part load.
- **High-energy ignition systems** (multi-coil, plasma ignition) to ensure stable combustion.
- **Low-friction engine design:** DLC coatings, low-viscosity oils (0W-20), roller rockers.
- **On-Board Diagnostics (OBD-II)** for real-time catalyst monitoring.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are essential for solving ULEV-related numerical problems. Memorize the **emission reduction percentage formula** and the **lambda relationship** — they appear almost every KTU ESE paper.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{ULEV} = 0.5 \cdot E_{AVE}$ | ULEV emits half the average new-vehicle emission | g/mi |
| 2 | $\%R = \dfrac{E_{BASE} - E_{ULEV}}{E_{BASE}} \times 100$ | Emission reduction percentage | % |
| 3 | $\lambda = \dfrac{(A/F)_{actual}}{(A/F)_{stoich}}$ | Relative air-fuel ratio (λ = 1 → stoich) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}$ | Otto-cycle ideal thermal efficiency | % |
| 5 | $\dot{m}_{NOx} \propto \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{R \cdot T_{flame}}\right)$ | NOx formation follows Arrhenius trend | kg/s |
| 6 | $CO_{mass} = \dot{m}_{fuel} \cdot x_{CO} \cdot MW_{CO}$ | CO mass emission rate from fuel flow | g/s |
| 7 | $S_{conv} = \dfrac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100$ | Catalytic conversion efficiency | % |
| 8 | $BSFC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{fuel}}{P_{brake}}$ | Brake Specific Fuel Consumption | g/kWh |
| 9 | $E_{fleet} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} V_i \cdot E_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} V_i}$ | Fleet-average emissions (for the 50% benchmark) | g/mi |
| 10 | $T_{crit} \approx 1800\,K$ | Zeldovich NOx threshold temperature | K |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

ULEV is the *de facto benchmark* for:
- All passenger cars sold in California (and 17+ US states adopting CARB standards).
- The European **Euro 5 / Euro 6** norms that followed similar stringency trajectories.
- India's **BS-VI** (Bharat Stage 6) norms adopted in 2020, which are functionally aligned with ULEV-SULEV levels.

In industry, a vehicle ULEV-certified by CARB is automatically compliant with **Euro 6c** and **BS-VI** (with minor calibration).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Emission Reduction Percentage from Tiered Standards

**Problem Statement (KTU-Type Numerical):** A gasoline passenger car is certified as LEV (baseline) with fleet-average emissions of $E_{LEV} = 0.080\,\text{g/mi}$ for NMOG. Determine the ULEV-compliant emission limit and verify whether a measured value of $0.045\,\text{g/mi}$ qualifies.

---

**Step 1 — Write the ULEV defining equation.**

ULEV vehicles must emit **50% less** than the average LEV baseline. Mathematically:

$$E_{ULEV} = E_{LEV} \times (1 - 0.50) = 0.5 \cdot E_{LEV}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the given baseline emission.**

$$E_{ULEV} = 0.5 \times 0.080\,\text{g/mi}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the ULEV target value.**

$$E_{ULEV} = 0.040\,\text{g/mi}$$

This matches the **CARB official ULEV NMOG limit** of 0.040 g/mi exactly.

**Step 4 — Apply the emission reduction formula to the measured value.**

$$\%R = \dfrac{E_{LEV} - E_{measured}}{E_{LEV}} \times 100$$

$$\%R = \dfrac{0.080 - 0.045}{0.080} \times 100$$

$$\%R = \dfrac{0.035}{0.080} \times 100 = 43.75\%$$

**Step 5 — Apply the ULEV pass/fail condition.**

The vehicle must show $\%R \geq 50\%$ to qualify as ULEV. Since $43.75\% < 50\%$, the measured vehicle **does NOT** qualify as ULEV.

**Step 6 — Express the ULEV target as a multi-step alignment block.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Pass condition:} \quad & \%R \geq 50\% \\
\text{Actual reduction:} \quad & 43.75\% \\
\text{Shortfall:} \quad & 50 - 43.75 = 6.25\% \\
\text{Required measured emission for ULEV pass:} \quad & E_{ULEV} = 0.5 \times 0.080 = 0.040\,\text{g/mi}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A 6.25% emission shortfall in the measured vehicle typically indicates either (a) catalyst light-off delay, (b) EGR valve leakage, or (c) aged lambda sensor. Each of these is a **diagnostic flag in OBD-II** (P0420, P0401, P0131 respectively).

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Pollutant Formation vs. ULEV Mitigation

| Pollutant | Formation Cause | Causal Parameter | ULEV Mitigation | Typical Reduction |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **CO** | Incomplete combustion | $\lambda < 1$ (rich) | Closed-loop EFI, stoich λ | ~85% (TWC) |
| **NOx** | High T + O₂ + N₂ | $T_{flame} > 1800$ K | EGR, retarded spark, TWC | ~75% |
| **NMOG** | Wall quench, crevice HC | Low T near walls | TWC oxidation, fast light-off | ~90% |
| **PM** | Incomplete fuel vaporization | GDI wall-wetting | GPF (Gasoline Particulate Filter) | ~70% |
| **CO₂** | Combustion byproduct | Brake-specific fuel use | Improved η_th, hybrid | ~15–25% |

---

### 3.3 Derivation: ULEV Compliance vs. Fleet Average

The ULEV standard is not compared against *a single car* but against the **fleet-wide average** sold in the model year. The fleet-average emission is a sales-weighted mean:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{fleet} & = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} V_i \cdot E_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} V_i}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $V_i$ = number of units of model $i$ sold, $E_i$ = NMOG emission of model $i$, and $N$ = number of models.

**For a manufacturer to sell a vehicle labeled ULEV:**

$$E_i \leq 0.5 \cdot E_{fleet}$$

This is why a 50% reduction is **relative** — it depends on how dirty the rest of the fleet is.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 ULEV Powertrain & After-Treatment Block Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram maps the functional flow of a ULEV-compliant powertrain — from air/fuel intake through combustion, after-treatment, and tailpipe monitoring.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Atmospheric Air Intake] --> B[Intake Manifold with VVT]
    C[Low-Sulfur Fuel Tank] --> D[High-Pressure Fuel Rail]
    B --> E[Combustion Chamber GDI]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Piston Power Stroke]
    F --> G[Exhaust Manifold]
    G --> H[Close-Coupled Three-Way Catalyst TWC]
    H --> I[Underbody TWC]
    I --> J[Gasoline Particulate Filter GPF]
    J --> K[Tailpipe Emission Output]
    K --> L{OBD-II Lambda and NOx Sensors}
    L --> M[Engine Control Unit ECU]
    M --> D
    M --> B
    M -.feedback.-> H
    N[External Low-Pressure EGR] --> B
    G --> N

    classDef component fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0277BD,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#0D47A1
    classDef control fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#BF360C
    classDef after fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#1B5E20
    class A,B,C,D,E,F,G component
    class L,M,N control
    class H,I,J,K after
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Blue nodes** = Core powertrain components (air-fuel induction and combustion).
- **Orange nodes** = Control and feedback subsystem (ECU, EGR, OBD-II).
- **Green nodes** = After-treatment chain (TWC × 2, GPF) — the heart of ULEV compliance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked to "draw the ULEV after-treatment system," always show the **dual-TWC** (close-coupled + underbody) configuration with a GPF in series. This is the canonical 2015+ architecture that pushed vehicles from LEV into ULEV territory.

---

### 4.2 Pollutant-to-Technology Mapping (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CO1[CO Formation] -->|Closed Loop EFI| TWC1[Three Way Catalyst]
    NOx1[NOx Formation] -->|EGR and Lambda 1| TWC1
    NMOG1[NMOG Formation] -->|Catalyst Oxidation| TWC1
    PM1[PM Formation] -->|Wall Wetting| GPF1[Gasoline Particulate Filter]
    TWC1 --> EM1[ULEV Certified Tailpipe]
    GPF1 --> EM1
    TWC1 -.monitors.-> OBD1[OBD II Catalyst Monitor]
    GPF1 -.monitors.-> OBD1
    OBD1 --> ECU1[ECU Diagnostic Codes]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define Ultra Low Emission Vehicle (ULEV). State the NMOG emission limit for ULEV certification as per CARB standards.*

**Model Answer:**

A ULEV is a vehicle certified to emit pollution levels at least **50% lower** than the fleet-average new-vehicle emission, classified under the **California Air Resources Board (CARB) Low Emission Vehicle** program.

The NMOG emission limit for ULEV certification is:
$$E_{NMOG,\,ULEV} = 0.040\,\text{g/mi} \quad \text{(at 50,000 mile durability)}$$

This represents a **50% reduction** over the LEV baseline of 0.080 g/mi. ULEV certification also requires compliance with CO (1.7 g/mi), NOx (0.2 g/mi), and PM (0.01 g/mi) limits over a 120,000-mile useful life. *(3 marks — definition 1, NMOG value 1, additional context 1.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*List any three enabling technologies that allow an engine to meet ULEV emission standards.*

**Model Answer:**

The three enabling technologies are:

1. **Three-Way Catalytic Converter (TWC)** with high Pt–Pd–Rh loading — converts CO, HC, and NOx simultaneously when λ = 1 ± 0.01.
2. **Variable Valve Timing (VVT)** with Variable Valve Lift (VVL) — controls residual gas fraction, reduces pumping loss, and lowers NOx.
3. **Exhaust Gas Recirculation (EGR)** — internal EIVC/LIVC plus external low-pressure EGR — dilutes in-cylinder O₂ and peak temperatures, suppressing thermal NOx.

*(1 mark per technology, 3 marks total.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the tiered emission classification under the CARB LEV program. Compare ULEV with LEV and SULEV in terms of relative emission stringency. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A car is certified as LEV with NMOG emission of 0.090 g/mi. Calculate: (i) the ULEV-compliant NMOG limit, (ii) the percentage reduction required, and (iii) whether a measured value of 0.048 g/mi qualifies. Justify your answer. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution (a) — Tiered Classification:**

The CARB LEV program defines five emission tiers, arranged in order of increasing cleanliness:

1. **LEV (Low Emission Vehicle):** Baseline tier — emits the fleet-average level (0.080 g/mi NMOG @ 50k mi).
2. **ULEV (Ultra Low Emission Vehicle):** 50% cleaner than LEV (0.040 g/mi NMOG).
3. **SULEV (Super Ultra Low Emission Vehicle):** 90% cleaner than LEV (0.010 g/mi NMOG).
4. **PZEV (Partial Zero Emission Vehicle):** SULEV tailpipe + zero evaporative emissions + 15-year/150,000-mile warranty on emission components.
5. **ZEV (Zero Emission Vehicle):** No tailpipe emissions (e.g., BEV, FCEV).

**Comparison Table:**

| Tier | NMOG (g/mi) | % Cleaner than LEV |
|---|---|---|
| LEV | 0.080 | 0% (baseline) |
| ULEV | 0.040 | 50% |
| SULEV | 0.010 | 90% |
| PZEV | 0.010 | 90% + zero evap |
| ZEV | 0.000 | 100% |

*(7 marks — tier list 2, comparison 3, table 2.)*

---

**Model Solution (b) — Numerical:**

**(i) ULEV-compliant NMOG limit:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{ULEV} & = 0.5 \times E_{LEV} \\
& = 0.5 \times 0.090 \\
& = 0.045\,\text{g/mi}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Percentage reduction required:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%R_{req} & = 50\% \quad \text{(by definition of ULEV)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Qualification check on measured value 0.048 g/mi:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%R_{actual} & = \dfrac{E_{LEV} - E_{measured}}{E_{LEV}} \times 100 \\
& = \dfrac{0.090 - 0.048}{0.090} \times 100 \\
& = \dfrac{0.042}{0.090} \times 100 \\
& = 46.67\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Decision:** Since $\%R_{actual} = 46.67\% < 50\%$, the measured vehicle **FAILS** the ULEV threshold. It remains a LEV. To pass, the measured NMOG must drop to $\leq 0.045$ g/mi.

*(7 marks — part (i) 2, part (ii) 1, part (iii) 3, decision 1.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the major subsystems of a ULEV-compliant after-treatment system. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the role of (i) Three-Way Catalyst, (ii) EGR, and (iii) GPF in achieving ULEV compliance. State the key chemical reactions involved. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution (a) — After-Treatment Block Diagram:**

A ULEV-compliant after-treatment chain (in series, from engine to tailpipe) consists of:

1. **Close-Coupled TWC (CC-TWC):** Mounted within ~30 cm of the exhaust port. Reaches light-off (~250 °C) within 15–30 s of cold start, where 70% of NMOG is produced. Uses high Pd loading for low-temperature HC oxidation.

2. **Underbody TWC (UB-TWC):** Located 60–100 cm downstream. Polishing stage for CO and remaining HC; provides additional NOx reduction.

3. **Gasoline Particulate Filter (GPF):** Wall-flow filter with 60–70% porosity. Captures sub-23 nm soot particles from GDI engines. Regenerated passively via exhaust heat.

4. **Lambda and NOx Sensors (UEGO + NOx):** Pre-catalyst UEGO controls closed-loop fueling; post-catalyst UEGO monitors catalyst efficiency. NOx sensor enables OBD-II monitoring of catalyst.

5. **ECU with OBD-II:** Real-time diagnostics — P0420 (catalyst efficiency below threshold), P2463 (GPF restriction), P0401 (EGR flow insufficient).

*(7 marks — diagram 3, subsystem description 4.)*

---

**Model Solution (b) — Roles and Reactions:**

**(i) Three-Way Catalyst (TWC):**
Performs three simultaneous reactions when λ = 1 ± 0.01:

- CO oxidation: $2\,\text{CO} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\,\text{CO}_2$
- HC oxidation: $\text{C}_x\text{H}_y + \left(x + \dfrac{y}{4}\right)\text{O}_2 \rightarrow x\,\text{CO}_2 + \dfrac{y}{2}\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$
- NOx reduction: $2\,\text{NO} \rightarrow \text{N}_2 + \text{O}_2$ (and via CO: $\text{NO} + \text{CO} \rightarrow \dfrac{1}{2}\,\text{N}_2 + \text{CO}_2$)

Conversion efficiency: 95–99% for CO/HC; 75–95% for NOx.

**(ii) EGR (Exhaust Gas Recirculation):**
Inert exhaust gas (CO₂, H₂O) displaces fresh charge, lowering adiabatic flame temperature and suppressing thermal NOx formation per the Zeldovich mechanism:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{O} + \text{N}_2 & \rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{N} \\
\text{N} + \text{O}_2 & \rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{O}
\end{aligned}
$$

Reducing $T_{flame}$ by 100 K lowers NOx by ~30–40%.

**(iii) Gasoline Particulate Filter (GPF):**
Wall-flow cordierite or SiC substrate with alternating plugged channels. Particulates (mostly carbonaceous soot from GDI fuel impingement) are deposited on porous walls and oxidized:

$$\text{C}_{(s)} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 \quad \text{(passive regen, T > 600°C)}$$

Filter efficiency: 70–95% for PM mass; >99% for particle count.

*(7 marks — TWC 2.5, EGR 2.5, GPF 2.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing up ULEV and Euro 5:** ULEV is a *CARB* standard; Euro 5 is a *European* standard. They are *aligned in philosophy* but *not identical* in numerical limits. Do not equate ULEV 0.040 g/mi with Euro 5 limits.
> 2. **Forgetting the useful-life clause:** ULEV limits are tested over **120,000 miles / 15 years**, not just at end-of-line (EOL). Always mention durability.
> 3. **Ignoring the Zeldovich mechanism:** For any NOx-related question, mentioning "high temperature" alone gets only partial credit. Cite the **Zeldovich mechanism** and the **~1800 K threshold**.
> 4. **Confusing CO and CO₂:** CO is *incomplete combustion* (toxic); CO₂ is *complete combustion* (greenhouse). Examiners *always* deduct 1 mark if confused.
> 5. **Skipping the OBD-II angle:** ULEV compliance without OBD-II monitoring is *incomplete*. Always mention catalyst, lambda, and EGR diagnostics.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — ULEV**

- ✅ ULEV = **50% cleaner** than LEV fleet average (CARB standard).
- ✅ NMOG limit: **0.040 g/mi** @ 50k mi; durability: **120,000 mi / 15 yr**.
- ✅ Pollutants regulated: **CO, NOx, NMOG, PM**.
- ✅ Key technologies: **TWC, VVT, EGR, GDI, GPF, OBD-II**.
- ✅ Lambda control: $\lambda = 1 \pm 0.01$ for TWC three-way operation.
- ✅ NOx threshold: $T_{flame} > 1800$ K (Zeldovich mechanism).
- ✅ Fleet average formula: $E_{fleet} = \dfrac{\sum V_i E_i}{\sum V_i}$.
- ✅ Emission reduction formula: $\%R = \dfrac{E_{BASE} - E_{new}}{E_{BASE}} \times 100$.
- ✅ ULEV sits between LEV (baseline) and SULEV (90% cleaner).
- ✅ ULEV-aligned globally with **Euro 5/6** and **India BS-VI** norms.
- ✅ CO is rich-combustion (λ < 1) product; CO₂ is stoich product.
- ✅ GPF is mandatory for **GDI engines** to meet ULEV-PM limits.
- ✅ OBD-II codes: **P0420** (catalyst), **P0401** (EGR), **P0131** (lambda O₂ sensor).
- ✅ Three key TWC reactions: CO oxidation, HC oxidation, NOx reduction.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Zero Emission Vehicle (ZEV)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT744 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Zero Emission Vehicle (ZEV)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Zero Emission Vehicle (ZEV)** is a vehicle that, during its operational duty cycle, produces **zero tailpipe exhaust emissions** of regulated pollutants — specifically Carbon Monoxide (CO), Hydrocarbons (HC), Nitrogen Oxides (NOx), Particulate Matter (PM), and effectively zero direct Carbon Dioxide (CO₂) emissions at the point of use.

As per the **California Air Resources Board (CARB)** classification — the global benchmark adopted by KTU modern automotive curricula — ZEVs fall strictly into two categories:
1. **Battery Electric Vehicles (BEVs)** — powered solely by grid-charged electrochemical energy.
2. **Fuel Cell Electric Vehicles (FCEVs)** — powered by onboard hydrogen reacting in a Polymer Electrolyte Membrane Fuel Cell (PEMFC).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** Conventional hybrids (HEVs) and Plug-in Hybrids (PHEVs) are **NOT classified as ZEVs** in the strict KTU module context because they retain an Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) and emit tailpipe pollutants when the ICE operates.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a regular car as a **kitchen gas stove** — it burns fuel right where you cook, releasing smoke and fumes into your kitchen. A ZEV is like a **fully electric induction cooktop** — it draws energy from a clean source (the grid or hydrogen), but the cooking process itself produces **no smoke, no fumes, no combustion by-products** in your immediate environment. The "emission" has been relocated to a place where it can be controlled (a power plant or a fuel station), and ideally, made clean using renewables.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics

The principal performance benchmarks for any ZEV are:
- **Energy Efficiency (η):** typically **15–20 kWh / 100 km** for a mid-size BEV.
- **All-Electric Range (AER):** **200–600 km** depending on battery pack capacity.
- **Well-to-Wheel (WTW) Efficiency:** **~25–30%** for BEVs (vs. ~13–16% for ICE vehicles).
- **Battery Specific Energy:** **150–250 Wh/kg** (modern Li-ion NMC cells).

> [!TIP]
> Examiner's Tip: Always quote both **Tank-to-Wheel (TTW)** and **Well-to-Wheel (WTW)** efficiency in long-answer questions to demonstrate complete understanding.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Architecture of a ZEV

A ZEV powertrain is fundamentally a **mechatronic system** comprising four functional blocks. Understanding these is essential for KTU Module 1.

**Block 1 — Energy Storage Subsystem**
- The **Traction Battery Pack** (typically Li-ion: NMC, LFP, or solid-state in the future) stores electrical energy in DC form.
- Pack voltage: **400 V** (standard) or **800 V** (premium/long-range).
- Energy capacity: **30 kWh to 120 kWh** for passenger cars.

**Block 2 — Power Conversion Subsystem**
- The **DC-DC Converter** steps the high-voltage pack down to **12 V / 48 V** for auxiliary loads (lights, ECU, infotainment).
- The **On-Board Charger (OBC)** converts grid AC (230 V, single-phase or 400 V three-phase) into regulated DC for the battery. Power rating: **6.6 kW to 22 kW AC**.

**Block 3 — Traction Inverter**
- Converts DC from the battery into **three-phase AC** (Variable Voltage, Variable Frequency — V/f controlled) to drive the traction motor.
- Uses **Insulated-Gate Bipolar Transistor (IGBT)** or **Silicon Carbide (SiC)** MOSFETs for switching at frequencies of **5–20 kHz**.

**Block 4 — Electric Traction Motor (ETM)**
- Common types: **Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor (PMSM)**, **Induction Motor (IM)**, or **Switched Reluctance Motor (SRM)**.
- Typical peak efficiency: **94–97%**.
- Power output: **100 kW to 400 kW** in modern passenger BEVs.

### 2.2 Why ZEVs Achieve Zero Emissions — The Underlying Physics

A ZEV bypasses the **Carnot cycle limit** of an ICE (which caps thermal efficiency at ~40%). Instead, it uses **electrochemical and electromechanical conversion**:

$$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_{cold}}{T_{hot}}$$

Since a Li-ion cell operates near **ambient temperature** with no high-temperature heat source, the entire conversion chain is governed by **Gibbs free energy** of the cell reaction, yielding:

$$\eta_{cell} = \frac{\Delta G}{\Delta H} \approx 0.85 \text{ to } 0.95$$

This is why even after accounting for grid losses, the **overall WTW efficiency** of a BEV (powered by a natural-gas combined-cycle plant, ~60% efficient) is roughly **0.85 × 0.60 × 0.92 (motor) × 0.90 (transmission) ≈ 42%** — more than **double** a comparable ICE vehicle's 13–16%.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol / Unit | Engineering Meaning |
|---|-------------------|---------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Battery Energy Stored: $E = V_{pack} \times Q_{pack}$ | kWh | Total energy available per full charge |
| 2 | Vehicle Range: $R = \dfrac{E_{usable}}{\varepsilon_{vehicle}}$ | km | where $\varepsilon$ is energy consumption in kWh/km |
| 3 | Motor Output Power: $P_{out} = T \times \omega$ | kW | $T$ in Nm, $\omega$ in rad/s |
| 4 | State of Charge: $SoC(t) = \dfrac{Q(t)}{Q_{rated}} \times 100\%$ | % | Battery charge level |
| 5 | Depth of Discharge: $DoD = 100 - SoC$ | % | Usable battery capacity |
| 6 | Cell EMF (Nernst-like): $V_{ocv} = f(SoC, T)$ | V | Open-circuit voltage |
| 7 | Charging Time: $t_{ch} = \dfrac{E_{batt}}{P_{charger}}$ | hours | $P_{charger}$ in kW |
| 8 | Regenerative Braking Recovery: $E_{regen} = \dfrac{1}{2} m (v_1^2 - v_2^2) \eta_{regen}$ | kJ | $m$ = vehicle mass, $v$ = velocity |
| 9 | WTW CO₂: $CO_2^{WTW} = CO_2^{WTT} + CO_2^{TTW}$ | g/km | Well-to-Tank + Tank-to-Wheel |
| 10 | Coulombic Efficiency: $\eta_{Q} = \dfrac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} \times 100\%$ | % | Battery round-trip efficiency |

> [!IMPORTANT]
