> **KTU Examiner Note:** Always include the unit of every variable in your answer. Marks are deducted for ambiguous notation (e.g., writing "E" without kWh).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

ZEV technology is the cornerstone of:
- **Urban Public Transport:** Electric buses (e-Buses) deployed by KSRTC, BEST, and DTC in India under the FAME-II scheme.
- **Last-Mile Delivery:** Companies like Amazon India and Flipkart are electrifying their fleets using Tata Ace EVs and Mahindra eSupro.
- **Two-Wheeler Electrification:** Hero Electric, Ola Electric, and Ather lead India's high-volume ZEV segment.
- **Grid Integration:** Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G) — using parked EV batteries as distributed energy storage.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Estimating the All-Electric Range of a ZEV

This is a classic KTU numerical problem. We derive the range equation from fundamental physics.

**Given:**
- Battery pack voltage: $V_{pack} = 400$ V
- Pack capacity: $Q_{pack} = 100$ Ah
- Vehicle curb mass: $m = 1500$ kg
- Drag coefficient: $C_d = 0.29$
- Frontal area: $A_f = 2.2$ m²
- Rolling resistance coefficient: $C_{rr} = 0.012$
- Drivetrain efficiency: $\eta_{drivetrain} = 0.90$
- Constant cruise speed: $v = 90$ km/h = $25$ m/s
- Air density: $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³

**Required:** All-Electric Range $R$ in km.

**Step 1 — Compute Usable Battery Energy**
$$E_{batt} = V_{pack} \times Q_{pack} = 400 \times 100 = 40{,}000 \text{ Wh} = 40 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Usable Depth-of-Discharge**
Standard automotive Li-ion batteries are not discharged below 10% SoC (to protect cycle life).

$$E_{usable} = E_{batt} \times DoD = 40 \times 0.90 = 36 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Total Resistive Forces at Cruise**

There are three resistive forces acting on the vehicle:

**3(a) Rolling Resistance Force:**
$$F_{roll} = C_{rr} \times m \times g = 0.012 \times 1500 \times 9.81 = 176.58 \text{ N}$$

**3(b) Aerodynamic Drag Force:**
$$F_{drag} = \frac{1}{2} \times \rho \times C_d \times A_f \times v^2$$
$$F_{drag} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.29 \times 2.2 \times 25^2$$
$$F_{drag} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.29 \times 2.2 \times 625$$
$$F_{drag} = 244.22 \text{ N}$$

**3(c) Climbing Force (level road, slope = 0):**
$$F_{climb} = m \times g \times \sin(\theta) = 0 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Total Tractive Force**
$$F_{total} = F_{roll} + F_{drag} + F_{climb} = 176.58 + 244.22 + 0 = 420.80 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5 — Power Required at the Wheels**
$$P_{wheel} = F_{total} \times v = 420.80 \times 25 = 10{,}520 \text{ W} = 10.52 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 6 — Power Drawn from the Battery (after drivetrain losses)**
$$P_{batt} = \frac{P_{wheel}}{\eta_{drivetrain}} = \frac{10.52}{0.90} = 11.69 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 7 — Cruise Time on a Full Battery**
$$t = \frac{E_{usable}}{P_{batt}} = \frac{36}{11.69} = 3.08 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 8 — All-Electric Range**
$$R = v \times t = 25 \text{ m/s} \times 3.08 \text{ h} \times 3600 \text{ s/h} \times \frac{1 \text{ km}}{1000 \text{ m}}$$

Performing the arithmetic step-by-step:
$$R = 25 \times 3.08 \times 3.6 = 277.2 \text{ km}$$

**Answer:** The estimated all-electric range at constant 90 km/h cruise is **≈ 277 km**.

### 3.2 Derivation: WTW CO₂ Emission of a BEV vs ICE Vehicle

This is a frequently asked **Part B (14-mark)** analytical question.

**Given:**
- ICE vehicle: Fuel economy = 18 km/L, Petrol density = 0.745 kg/L, Carbon content = 86%, Petrol TTW CO₂ = 2.31 kg CO₂/L
- BEV: Energy consumption = 0.15 kWh/km, Grid emission factor = 0.7 kg CO₂/kWh (India CEA average)

**Step 1 — ICE Vehicle TTW CO₂ (per km)**
$$CO_2^{TTW,\,ICE} = \frac{2.31}{18} = 0.1283 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{km}$$

**Step 2 — ICE Vehicle WTT CO₂ (per km)**
WTT (extraction + refining) is typically **~20% of TTW**:
$$CO_2^{WTT,\,ICE} = 0.20 \times 0.1283 = 0.0257 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{km}$$

**Step 3 — ICE Vehicle WTW CO₂ (per km)**
$$CO_2^{WTW,\,ICE} = 0.1283 + 0.0257 = 0.1540 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{km}$$

**Step 4 — BEV TTW CO₂ (per km)**
A BEV emits **zero** tailpipe CO₂:
$$CO_2^{TTW,\,BEV} = 0 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{km}$$

**Step 5 — BEV WTT CO₂ (per km)** (i.e., grid generation emissions)
$$CO_2^{WTT,\,BEV} = 0.15 \times 0.7 = 0.1050 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{km}$$

**Step 6 — BEV WTW CO₂ (per km)**
$$CO_2^{WTW,\,BEV} = 0 + 0.1050 = 0.1050 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{km}$$

**Step 7 — Comparative Reduction**
$$\Delta CO_2 = 0.1540 - 0.1050 = 0.049 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{km}$$
$$\text{Reduction \%} = \frac{0.049}{0.1540} \times 100 \approx 31.8\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** The 31.8% reduction uses India's current (2024) coal-heavy grid. If the grid shifts to **50% renewables** (India's 2030 target), the grid emission factor drops to ~0.35 kg CO₂/kWh, and the reduction jumps to **~64%** — making BEVs decisively cleaner.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: ZEV vs ICE

| Parameter | ZEV (BEV) | Conventional ICE |
|-----------|-----------|------------------|
| Energy Source | Grid Electricity | Petrol / Diesel |
| Energy Conversion | Electrochemical + Electromechanical | Thermodynamic (Carnot) |
| Peak Efficiency | **85–95%** (motor + battery) | **30–40%** (engine) |
| WTW Efficiency | **25–30%** | **13–16%** |
| Tailpipe CO₂ | **0 g/km** | **120–250 g/km** |
| Tailpipe NOx | **0 g/km** | **0.05–0.5 g/km** |
| Moving Parts | ~**20** | ~**2000+** |
| Maintenance Cost | **Low** (no oil, fewer brakes) | **High** |
| Refuel Time | 30 min (DC fast) – 8 h (AC) | **3 minutes** |
| Upfront Cost (India 2024) | ₹12 L – ₹25 L | ₹6 L – ₹20 L |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: ZEV Powertrain Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    BAT[TRACTION BATTERY PACK 400V DC] --> INV[TRAVERTED INVERTER VVVF]
    BAT --> DCDC[DC-DC CONVERTER]
    BAT --> OBC[ON-BOARD CHARGER AC-DC]
    GRID[AC GRID 230V 50HZ] --> OBC
    INV --> MTR[TRACTION MOTOR PMSM 150KW]
    MTR --> GBX[SINGLE SPEED GEARBOX]
    GBX --> WHLS[DRIVE WHEELS]
    DCDC --> AUX[12V AUXILIARY LOADS ECU LIGHTS]
    MTR -. REGEN BRAKING .-> INV
    BMS[BATTERY MANAGEMENT SYSTEM] -. MONITORS .-> BAT
    BMS -. COMMUNICATION CAN BUS .-> INV
    ECU[VEHICLE CONTROL UNIT VCU] --> INV
    ECU --> MTR
```

### 4.2 Functional Flow Diagram: ZEV Operating Modes

```mermaid
graph TD
    DRV[DRIVER DEMAND] --> VCU[VEHICLE CONTROL UNIT]
    VCU --> MODE{MODE SELECTOR}
    MODE -->|FORWARD| TRAC[TRACTION MODE]
    MODE -->|BRAKE PEDAL| REGEN[REGENERATIVE BRAKING]
    MODE -->|STATIONARY| IDLE[IDLE / CHARGING]
    TRAC --> INV[TRAVERTER INVERTER ON]
    INV --> MOT[MOTOR DRIVES WHEELS]
    REGEN --> INV2[INVERTER RECTIFIES AC TO DC]
    INV2 --> BAT[BATTERY RECHARGED]
    IDLE --> OBC[ON-BOARD CHARGER ACTIVE]
    OBC --> BAT2[BATTERY CHARGED FROM GRID]
```

### 4.3 Classification Tree: Types of Zero and Low-Emission Vehicles

```mermaid
graph TD
    ZEV[ZERO EMISSION VEHICLE ZEV] --> BEV[BATTERY ELECTRIC VEHICLE BEV]
    ZEV --> FCEV[FUEL CELL ELECTRIC VEHICLE FCEV]
    BEV --> BAT1[LI-ION NMC LFP SOLID STATE]
    FCEV --> PEM[PEMFC POLYMER ELECTROLYTE MEMBRANE FCEV]
    PEM --> H2[H2 STORAGE 700 BAR TANK]
    BEV --> CHG[CHARGING AC LEVEL 1 2 AND DC FAST]
    FCEV --> STN[H2 REFUELING STATION 5 MIN]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Diagram Interpretation Aid:** The arrows indicate **power flow** (solid lines) and **signal/control flow** (dotted lines). The Battery Management System (BMS) sends monitoring signals to the inverter and battery via the **CAN bus** at a baud rate of 500 kbps (CAN 2.0B).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define a Zero Emission Vehicle (ZEV). Name the two main categories of ZEVs recognized by CARB.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** A ZEV is a vehicle that emits **no tailpipe pollutants** — no CO, HC, NOx, PM, or direct CO₂ — during its operational duty cycle.
- **Categories (2 marks):**
  - **(i) Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV):** Stores electrical energy in an onboard traction battery and uses an electric motor for propulsion. Recharged by external electricity source.
  - **(ii) Fuel Cell Electric Vehicle (FCEV):** Generates electricity onboard via the electrochemical reaction of compressed hydrogen with oxygen in a PEM fuel cell, producing only water as the by-product.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
State any **three** advantages of a ZEV over a conventional ICE vehicle.

**Model Answer (3 marks, 1 mark each):**
- **(i) Zero tailpipe emissions:** Eliminates urban air pollution (CO, NOx, PM) at the point of use.
- **(ii) Higher energy efficiency:** ~85–95% drivetrain efficiency vs. 30–40% for ICE, leading to lower operating cost per km.
- **(iii) Lower maintenance cost:** Fewer moving parts (~20 vs. ~2000), no oil changes, regenerative braking extends brake-pad life by up to 2×.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the major components of a Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV) powertrain and state the function of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A BEV has a 60 kWh Li-ion battery pack with 90% usable Depth of Discharge. The vehicle, weighing 1600 kg with $C_d = 0.30$, $A_f = 2.3$ m², $C_{rr} = 0.011$, is driven at a constant speed of 80 km/h on a level road. Assuming drivetrain efficiency of 92% and air density of 1.225 kg/m³, compute the all-electric range. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer to Q.A(a):**

**Block Diagram Description (3 marks):**
The BEV powertrain consists of the following interconnected subsystems:

1. **Traction Battery Pack (1 mark):** Stores DC electrical energy at 400 V nominal; built from series-parallel Li-ion cells with a Battery Management System (BMS) for cell balancing, thermal management, and SoC monitoring.

2. **On-Board Charger (OBC) (1 mark):** Converts single-phase/three-phase AC from the grid into regulated DC for battery charging. Typical rating: 6.6 kW AC (Type-2 connector in India).

3. **DC-DC Converter (1 mark):** Steps down the 400 V traction voltage to 12 V to power auxiliary loads (headlamps, ECU, infotainment, ABS pump).

4. **Traction Inverter (1 mark):** Converts DC from the battery into 3-phase AC (Variable Voltage, Variable Frequency) using IGBT/SiC switches at 5–20 kHz.

5. **Traction Motor (1 mark):** Typically PMSM, with peak efficiency 94–97%, delivers torque to the wheels via a single-speed reduction gearbox.

6. **Vehicle Control Unit (VCU) + BMS (1 mark):** The VCU is the master controller coordinating torque demand, regenerative braking, and thermal management via CAN bus communication.

**Block Diagram (already shown in Section 4.1 — refer).**

---

**Model Answer to Q.A(b):**

**Given:**
- $E_{batt} = 60$ kWh, $DoD = 0.90$ → $E_{usable} = 60 \times 0.90 = 54$ kWh
- $m = 1600$ kg, $C_d = 0.30$, $A_f = 2.3$ m², $C_{rr} = 0.011$
- $v = 80$ km/h $= 22.22$ m/s
- $\eta = 0.92$, $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $\theta = 0$

**Step 1 — Rolling Resistance (1 mark):**
$$F_{roll} = C_{rr} \times m \times g = 0.011 \times 1600 \times 9.81 = 172.66 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Aerodynamic Drag (1 mark):**
$$F_{drag} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.30 \times 2.3 \times 22.22^2$$
$$= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.30 \times 2.3 \times 493.73 = 208.64 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Total Force and Wheel Power (2 marks):**
$$F_{total} = 172.66 + 208.64 = 381.30 \text{ N}$$
$$P_{wheel} = F_{total} \times v = 381.30 \times 22.22 = 8{,}471.5 \text{ W} = 8.47 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4 — Battery Power and Time (1 mark):**
$$P_{batt} = \frac{8.47}{0.92} = 9.21 \text{ kW}$$
$$t = \frac{54}{9.21} = 5.86 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 5 — Range (1 mark):**
$$R = 22.22 \times 5.86 \times 3.6 = 468.5 \text{ km}$$

**Answer:** The all-electric range is **≈ 468.5 km**.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Compare the Well-to-Wheel (WTW) efficiency of a ZEV (BEV) with that of a conventional petrol ICE vehicle. Use standard values of grid emission factor and fuel properties. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the role of the Battery Management System (BMS) and the Vehicle Control Unit (VCU) in a ZEV. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer to Q.B(a):**

**Step 1 — ICE Vehicle Chain (2 marks):**
- Well-to-Tank (WTT) of petrol: includes crude extraction, refining, transportation. Average efficiency ≈ **80–85%** (i.e., 15–20% energy lost as upstream CO₂).
- Tank-to-Wheel (TTW) of a petrol ICE: governed by **Carnot limit** and friction; practical efficiency ≈ **25–30%** (modern BS-VI engines).
- **WTW efficiency of ICE = 0.82 × 0.28 ≈ 0.230 or 23%**.

**Step 2 — BEV Chain (2 marks):**
- WTT of grid electricity: Thermal power plant efficiency ≈ **35–40%**; transmission/distribution losses ≈ **5%**; so grid delivery efficiency ≈ **0.38 × 0.95 ≈ 0.36**.
- TTW of BEV: Battery round-trip ≈ **90%** × motor & inverter ≈ **92%** = **0.90 × 0.92 = 0.828**.
- **WTW efficiency of BEV = 0.36 × 0.828 ≈ 0.298 or 30%**.

**Step 3 — Comparison (2 marks):**
- BEV WTW efficiency (**~30%**) is **~30% higher** than ICE WTW (~23%).
- As the grid transitions to renewables (solar, wind, hydro), BEV WTW efficiency approaches **50–60%**, while ICE remains stuck at ~23%.
- CO₂ emissions: BEV ≈ 100 g CO₂/km vs. ICE ≈ 150 g CO₂/km (current Indian grid, 2024).

**Step 4 — Conclusion (1 mark):** Even with today's coal-heavy grid, BEVs are cleaner and more efficient than ICE vehicles, with the advantage growing every year as the grid decarbonizes.

---

**Model Answer to Q.B(b):**

**Role of BMS (3.5 marks):**
- **Cell Monitoring:** Measures voltage, current, and temperature of each cell using a distributed daisy-chained architecture.
- **State Estimation:** Computes State of Charge (SoC), State of Health (SoH), and State of Power (SoP) using Kalman-filter or coulomb-counting algorithms.
- **Cell Balancing:** Equalizes cell voltages passively (resistor bleed) or actively (DC-DC shuttling) to prevent capacity drift.
- **Thermal Management:** Controls liquid cooling/heating loops to maintain cells between **15°C and 35°C** for optimal cycle life.
- **Safety:** Triggers contactor disconnection during over-voltage, under-voltage, over-temperature, or short-circuit events (ISO 26262 ASIL-C/D compliant).

**Role of VCU (3.5 marks):**
- **Torque Coordination:** Receives accelerator pedal signal, computes torque demand, and dispatches commands to the traction inverter.
- **Mode Arbitration:** Manages transitions between traction, regenerative braking, and idle modes.
- **Energy Optimization:** Implements eco-driving algorithms that minimize energy consumption using route slope and traffic data.
- **Communication Gateway:** Master node on the CAN bus, interfacing with BMS, OBC, ABS/ESC, instrument cluster, and telematics unit.
- **Diagnostics:** Runs OBD-II compliant fault detection (DTC storage) and supports OTA (Over-the-Air) firmware updates.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> - ❌ Confusing **TTW** with **WTW** — examiners award marks for specifying the *scope* of efficiency.
> - ❌ Writing "electric vehicle is 100% efficient" — this is **scientifically wrong**; you must show the chain of losses.
> - ❌ Omitting units in numerical answers (e.g., writing "60" instead of "60 kWh").
> - ❌ Forgetting to multiply by **3.6** to convert m/s × h → km.
> - ❌ Drawing block diagrams without **arrows showing power flow direction** — loses 1 mark in 7-mark questions.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **ZEV** = zero tailpipe emissions. BEV and FCEV are the only two true ZEVs.
- A BEV drivetrain comprises: **Battery + OBC + DC-DC + Inverter + Motor + VCU/BMS**.
- **WTW efficiency** is the most important comparison metric: BEV (~30%) > ICE (~23%) using current Indian grid.
- Key formulae to memorize: $E = V \times Q$, $R = E_{usable} / \varepsilon$, $F_{drag} = 0.5 \rho C_d A_f v^2$.
- Li-ion traction batteries operate at **400 V / 800 V** with **150–250 Wh/kg** specific energy.
- **Regenerative braking** can recover **10–30%** of the propulsion energy in urban drive cycles.
- India's grid emission factor (CEA 2023–24) ≈ **0.7 kg CO₂/kWh**; this is the number to use in numericals unless otherwise stated.
- **Bharat Stage (BS-VI)** norms apply to ICE vehicles; ZEVs are exempt from tailpipe norms since they have no tailpipe.
- FAME-II (Faster Adoption and Manufacturing of Electric Vehicles) India scheme provides ₹10,000–₹1.5 L subsidy on ZEV purchase.
- **Common exam trap:** HEVs and PHEVs are **not** ZEVs in the strict sense.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Components: Inverters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT744 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Components: Inverters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Automotive Power Inverter** is a static power electronic converter that uses semiconductor switching devices (IGBTs/MOSFETs) to convert **DC (Direct Current)** electrical energy from the vehicle's high-voltage traction battery into **variable-frequency, variable-magnitude AC (Alternating Current)** energy required to drive a three-phase AC traction motor. In modern hybrid (HEV), plug-in hybrid (PHEV), battery electric (BEV), and fuel-cell electric (FCEV) vehicles, the inverter is a mission-critical component of the powertrain, and is often integrated with a bidirectional DC-DC converter into a single **Power Electronics Unit (PEU)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For PEMUT744, the inverter is studied as a *basic component* of the modern electric powertrain, alongside the motor, battery, and DC-DC converter. Focus is on the **topology (VSI)**, **switching principle (PWM/SVPWM)**, and its **role in motoring and regenerative braking modes**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **"Translator of Languages" Analogy:**
> Imagine the battery as a person who speaks only one "language" — a steady, flat DC voltage. The AC induction or permanent-magnet traction motor is a person who only understands "AC language" — a smoothly oscillating voltage. The **inverter is the interpreter** sitting between them in the powertrain, translating DC into the precise AC waveform the motor needs. If the driver presses the accelerator, the interpreter speaks *faster* (higher frequency) and *louder* (higher amplitude). If the driver lifts off the accelerator, the interpreter reverses direction — converting the motor's AC back into DC to recharge the battery (regenerative braking).

### 1.3 Role of the Inverter in an EV Powertrain

| Powertrain Stage | Energy Form | Component Handling It |
|---|---|---|
| Battery Pack | High-Voltage DC (typically **400 V** or **800 V**) | Battery Management System |
| Inverter Input | DC Link (smoothed by capacitor) | Inverter (DC → AC) |
| Inverter Output | 3-phase variable AC | Traction Motor |
| Mechanical Output | Rotary mechanical motion | Wheels (via transmission) |
| Regenerative Mode | AC → DC (reverse power flow) | Inverter in rectification mode |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Automotive Metric:** A typical EV traction inverter operates at a **DC link voltage of 400 V**, a **switching frequency of 5–20 kHz**, an output fundamental frequency of **0–1200 Hz**, and a peak efficiency of **95–98%**. The modern benchmark automotive inverter is the **Toyota Prius Gen-4** inverter, which uses Silicon Carbide (SiC) power devices.

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Inverters Used in Automobiles

Automotive traction inverters are almost exclusively **Voltage Source Inverters (VSI)**. Current Source Inverters (CSI) are rarely used in production EVs.

- **Voltage Source Inverter (VSI):** DC link is a stiff voltage source (battery + large DC-link capacitor). Output is a PWM voltage waveform.
- **Current Source Inverter (CSI):** DC link has a large inductor to maintain constant current. Output is a current waveform. (Largely obsolete in automotive traction.)

### 2.2 Two-Level VSI Topology — The Standard Automotive Inverter

A three-phase two-level VSI consists of **three legs (half-bridges)**, one per phase (U, V, W). Each leg contains **two switches with anti-parallel diodes**.

- **Upper switch** (S₁, S₃, S₅) and **lower switch** (S₂, S₄, S₆) are complementary.
- When upper switch is ON, the phase is tied to **+V_DC**.
- When lower switch is ON, the phase is tied to **0 V** (DC-link negative rail).
- The two states produce a **two-level** output: +V_DC or 0.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Two-Level"?** Because the line-to-neutral phase voltage can take only **two discrete values** (+V_DC/2 and −V_DC/2) at any instant. This is the workhorse topology used in nearly all production EVs through the 2010s.

### 2.3 Modulation Strategy — How AC is Synthesized from DC

Pure switching between +V_DC and 0 yields a crude square wave rich in harmonics. To approximate a clean sine wave, automotive inverters use **Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)**:

- **Sinusoidal PWM (SPWM):** A reference sine wave is compared with a high-frequency triangular carrier. The intersection points determine switching instants.
- **Space Vector PWM (SVPWM):** Industry standard for traction drives. Treats the three phases as a single rotating space vector in the α-β plane and uses the **eight switching states** (six active + two zero) to synthesize a reference vector with higher DC-bus utilization (≈ **15.47% higher** than SPWM).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** SVPWM is the modulation scheme of choice in nearly every modern EV inverter (Tesla Model 3, Hyundai E-GMP, BYD e-Platform 3.0) because it produces less harmonic distortion and delivers a higher line-to-line RMS voltage for the same DC bus.

### 2.4 Operating Quadrants — Why the Inverter is Bidirectional

| Quadrant | Motor Action | Power Flow | Inverter Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| Q1 | Forward motoring | Battery → Motor | Rectifier-inverted (DC → AC) |
| Q2 | Forward braking | Motor → Battery | Rectifier (AC → DC), regen |
| Q3 | Reverse motoring | Battery → Motor | Reversed phase sequence |
| Q4 | Reverse braking | Motor → Battery | Rectifier, regen |

> [!NOTE]
> Bidirectional power flow is what enables **regenerative braking** — the kinetic energy of the moving vehicle drives the motor as a generator, and the inverter rectifies the resulting AC back into DC to charge the battery.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| $$V_{LL,RMS} = m_a \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2\sqrt{2}} \cdot V_{DC}$$ | Line-to-line RMS output of SPWM VSI | $m_a$ is modulation index, $0 \le m_a \le 1$ |
| $$V_{LL,RMS} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \frac{V_{DC}}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{V_{DC}}{\sqrt{2}}$$ | Maximum line-to-line RMS in linear SPWM | Reached at $m_a = 1$ |
| $$V_{LL,RMS,SVPWM} = \frac{V_{DC}}{\sqrt{2}}$$ | Max line-to-line RMS with SVPWM (linear region) | Same magnitude, better harmonic profile |
| $$V_{ph} = m_a \cdot \frac{V_{DC}}{2}$$ | Per-phase output voltage (fundamental) | Depends on $m_a$ |
| $$P_{out} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{LL,RMS} \cdot I_{LL,RMS} \cdot \cos\phi$$ | Three-phase output power | Standard 3-φ relation |
| $$f_{sw} = \frac{1}{T_{sw}}$$ | Switching frequency definition | 5–20 kHz typical for EV |
| $$\eta_{inv} = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{DC,in}} = 1 - \frac{P_{loss}}{P_{in}}$$ | Inverter efficiency | Target 95–98% in EV |
| $$N_s = \frac{V_{DC}}{4 \cdot f_{sw} \cdot L_s \cdot \Delta I_{max}}$$ | Turns ratio (for coupled inductors, if used) | $L_s$ leakage inductance |
| $$T_{em} = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{P}{2} \cdot \vert \Psi_{PM} \vert \cdot I_q \cdot \eta_{inv}$$ | Approx electromagnetic torque (PMSM, ideal) | $P$ pole pairs, $I_q$ q-axis current |
| $$THD = \frac{\sqrt{\sum_{n=2}^{\infty} V_n^2}}{V_1} \times 100\%$$ | Total Harmonic Distortion (quality metric) | Lower is better; <5% target |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Formula Pitfall:** In markdown tables, the absolute value of a quantity is written as $\vert x \vert$ — never with vertical bars, which break the table column delimiter.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Tesla Model 3 Inverter:** Uses **SiC MOSFETs**, switching at ~10–20 kHz, 320 kW peak per unit, 97% efficiency.
- **Toyota Prius Inverter:** First mass-produced automotive inverter (1997), uses IGBTs at ~5 kHz.
- **Hyundai E-GMP 800 V platform:** Inverter must handle 800 V DC-link, requires 1200 V-rated SiC devices.
- **FCEVs (Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo):** Fuel cell produces DC; inverter inverts it to AC for the traction motor.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Sinusoidal PWM Modulation Index

The **modulation index** $m_a$ is the single most important quantity that describes "how strongly" the inverter is being driven. Let us derive it from first principles.

**Step 1 — Define the modulating and carrier signals.**

The reference (modulating) signal is a sine wave:

$$v_{ref}(t) = V_{ref,m} \sin(\omega t)$$

The high-frequency carrier is a triangular wave of amplitude $V_{tri}$ and frequency $f_{sw}$:

$$v_{tri}(t) \text{ is a triangle wave with peak } V_{tri}$$

**Step 2 — Define the modulation index.**

The modulation index is the ratio of reference peak to carrier peak:

$$m_a = \frac{V_{ref,m}}{V_{tri}}$$

For the inverter to remain in the **linear modulation region** (no overmodulation, no pulse dropping):

$$0 \le m_a \le 1$$

**Step 3 — Relate $m_a$ to the fundamental output voltage.**

The fundamental component of the PWM phase voltage (line-to-neutral) is:

$$V_{ph,1} = m_a \cdot \frac{V_{DC}}{2}$$

This result comes from double Fourier analysis of the PWM waveform. The factor of 1/2 arises because each phase leg can output either $+V_{DC}/2$ or $-V_{DC}/2$ about the mid-point of the DC bus.

**Step 4 — Derive the line-to-line RMS voltage.**

The line-to-line voltage is the difference of two phase voltages 120° apart:

$$V_{LL,1}(t) = V_{U,1}(t) - V_{V,1}(t)$$

For balanced sinusoidal modulation, the line-to-line fundamental RMS is:

$$V_{LL,RMS} = \sqrt{3} \cdot \frac{V_{ph,1}}{\sqrt{2}} = \sqrt{3} \cdot \frac{m_a \cdot V_{DC}}{2\sqrt{2}}$$

**Step 5 — Simplify.**

$$V_{LL,RMS} = m_a \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2\sqrt{2}} \cdot V_{DC} \approx 0.612 \cdot m_a \cdot V_{DC}$$

**Step 6 — Maximum achievable value (linear region).**

At $m_a = 1$:

$$V_{LL,RMS,max} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2\sqrt{2}} \cdot V_{DC} = \frac{V_{DC}}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707 \cdot V_{DC}$$

**Step 7 — SVPWM achieves 15.47% more voltage.**

In SVPWM, the linear-region maximum reaches:

$$V_{LL,RMS,SVPWM,max} = \frac{V_{DC}}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{3}{2}} = \frac{V_{DC}}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} = \frac{V_{DC}}{\sqrt{2}}$$

In six-step mode (square wave, $m_a \to \infty$), the maximum achievable is:

$$V_{LL,RMS,6-step} = \frac{2\sqrt{2}}{\pi} \cdot V_{DC} \approx 0.9 \cdot V_{DC}$$

**Step 8 — Worked Numerical Example.**

Given: $V_{DC} = 400$ V (typical EV), $m_a = 0.85$ (linear SPWM).

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{ph,1} &= m_a \cdot \frac{V_{DC}}{2} = 0.85 \cdot \frac{400}{2} = 170 \text{ V (peak)} \\
V_{ph,1,RMS} &= \frac{170}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 120.2 \text{ V} \\
V_{LL,RMS} &= 0.612 \cdot 0.85 \cdot 400 \approx 208.1 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the voltage applied to the stator of the traction motor in an EV with a 400 V battery pack under partial throttle.

### 3.2 Derivation of Switching & Conduction Losses

**Step 1 — Switching loss per transition (IGBT).**

$$E_{sw} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot V_{DC} \cdot I_{load} \cdot (t_{on} + t_{off})$$

**Step 2 — Total switching loss per device.**

$$P_{sw} = E_{sw} \cdot f_{sw} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot V_{DC} \cdot I_{load} \cdot (t_{on} + t_{off}) \cdot f_{sw}$$

**Step 3 — Conduction loss per device.**

$$P_{cond} = V_{CE,sat} \cdot I_{load,avg} + r_{CE} \cdot I_{load,RMS}^2$$

**Step 4 — Total inverter loss (6 switches).**

$$P_{loss,total} = 6 \cdot (P_{sw} + P_{cond}) + P_{aux}$$

The auxiliary losses $P_{aux}$ include gate-drive power, control PCB consumption, and coolant pump overhead.

**Step 5 — Efficiency.**

$$\eta_{inv} = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{out} + P_{loss,total}}$$

For a 150 kW EV inverter with $V_{DC} = 400$ V, $f_{sw} = 10$ kHz, and SiC MOSFETs:

$$\eta_{inv} \approx 97\% \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_{loss,total} \approx 4.5 \text{ kW}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** That 4.5 kW of heat is why EV inverters are **liquid-cooled**, not air-cooled. The Toyota Prius inverter is integrated into the transmission housing and shares engine coolant.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Inverter Technologies

| Parameter | IGBT-VSI (Standard) | SiC-MOSFET VSI (Modern) | GaN VSI (Emerging) | CSI (Obsolete) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Voltage Rating | 600–1200 V | 600–1700 V | 100–650 V | 600–1200 V |
| Switching Frequency | 5–10 kHz | 20–50 kHz | 50–200 kHz | 5–10 kHz |
| Efficiency | 94–96% | 97–99% | 98–99% | 92–95% |
| Cost | Low | Medium | High | High |
| Automotive Use | 1997–2018 | 2018–present | R&D stage | None |
| Thermal Management | Liquid | Liquid (compact) | Air/PCB | Complex |
| Example Vehicle | Toyota Prius Gen-3 | Tesla Model 3, Hyundai E-GMP | Concept EVs | N/A |

```mermaid
flowchart TB
```

```mermaid
    HEX[Hexagon with six active vectors V1 to V6 and two zero vectors V0 V7]:::hex
    S1[Sector 1 IGBT pattern 100]:::sec
    S2[Sector 2 IGBT pattern 110]:::sec
    S3[Sector 3 IGBT pattern 010]:::sec
    S4[Sector 4 IGBT pattern 011]:::sec
    S5[Sector 5 IGBT pattern 001]:::sec
    S6[Sector 6 IGBT pattern 101]:::sec

    OUT[Three Phase PWM Pulses to Inverter Legs]:::out

    REF --> HEX
    HEX --> S1
    HEX --> S2

    HEX --> S4
    HEX --> S5

    classDef ref fill:#003366,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef hex fill:#660033,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef sec fill:#336600,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef pwm fill:#663300,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef out fill:#333,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The reference vector $V_{ref}$ rotates at the desired motor electrical frequency. It is decomposed into the two nearest active vectors and one zero vector. Their dwell times $T_1$, $T_2$, $T_0$ (per switching period $T_{sw}$) are:

$$
T_1 = m_a \cdot T_{sw} \cdot \sin(60° - \theta), \quad T_2 = m_a \cdot T_{sw} \cdot \sin(\theta), \quad T_0 = T_{sw} - T_1 - T_2
$$

where $\theta$ is the angle of $V_{ref}$ within the current sector.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** *Define an automotive power inverter. State its function in an electric vehicle powertrain.*

**Model Answer:**
An automotive power inverter is a static power-electronic converter that uses semiconductor switching devices such as **IGBTs** or **SiC MOSFETs** to convert the high-voltage DC supply from the traction battery into **variable-frequency, variable-magnitude three-phase AC** suitable for driving the AC traction motor. Its primary function in an EV powertrain is to enable **controlled motoring** (DC → AC) and **regenerative braking** (AC → DC), forming the energy-conversion heart of the vehicle's drive system. In production EVs it is integrated into the **Power Electronics Unit (PEU)** alongside the DC-DC converter and the on-board charger.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** *Differentiate between a Voltage Source Inverter (VSI) and a Current Source Inverter (CSI) in the context of EV traction.*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Voltage Source Inverter (VSI) | Current Source Inverter (CSI) |
|---|---|---|
| DC-link element | Large **capacitor** (stiff voltage) | Large **inductor** (stiff current) |
| Output waveform | PWM **voltage** | PWM **current** |
| Switching device | IGBT with anti-parallel diode | Reverse-blocking IGBT / thyristor |
| Topology complexity | Simpler | More complex |
| Automotive use | **Universal standard** | Not used in modern EVs |
| Bidirectional power | Easy (anti-parallel diodes) | Difficult |
| Efficiency | 95–98% | 92–95% |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Almost every production EV (Tesla, Hyundai, BYD, Toyota, Tata) uses a **two-level VSI** with SVPWM modulation. CSIs are reserved for niche high-power industrial drives.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]** *(a)* With a neat power-circuit diagram, explain the working of a **three-phase two-level Voltage Source Inverter** used in electric vehicles. *(b)* Derive the expression for the line-to-line RMS output voltage of a sinusoidal-PWM VSI in terms of the modulation index $m_a$ and the DC-link voltage $V_{DC}$.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

The three-phase two-level VSI is built from **three legs** of two complementary switches each (S₁/S₂ for phase U, S₃/S₄ for phase V, S₅/S₆ for phase W). Each switch has an **anti-parallel diode** that allows reverse current flow — essential for **regenerative braking** when the motor acts as a generator.

- When S₁ is ON, phase U is connected to **+V_DC**.
- When S₂ is ON, phase U is connected to the **negative rail (0 V)**.
- The output line-to-neutral voltage $V_{U0}$ is either **+V_DC/2** or **−V_DC/2** — hence the term *two-level*.

The output is a PWM waveform, and by varying the **pulse widths** in a sinusoidal pattern, the low-frequency content of the phase voltage approximates a sine wave. A large **DC-link capacitor** stiffens the DC bus against current ripple caused by the inductive load.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks** (Derivation reproduced from §3.1 above, condensed)

$$\begin{aligned}
m_a &= \frac{V_{ref,m}}{V_{tri}}, \quad 0 \le m_a \le 1 \\
V_{ph,1} &= m_a \cdot \frac{V_{DC}}{2} \\
V_{LL,1,RMS} &= \sqrt{3} \cdot \frac{V_{ph,1}}{\sqrt{2}} \\
V_{LL,RMS} &= m_a \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2\sqrt{2}} \cdot V_{DC} \\
V_{LL,RMS,max}\big\vert_{m_a=1} &= \frac{V_{DC}}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707\,V_{DC}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #1:** Many students write $V_{LL,RMS} = m_a \cdot V_{DC}$ — wrong. The constant $\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.612$ must be present.
>
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #2:** Forgetting to state the *linear region* constraint $m_a \le 1$. Beyond $m_a = 1$ the inverter enters overmodulation/six-step mode and the linear formula no longer holds.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]** *(a)* Explain the principle of **Sinusoidal PWM (SPWM)** and **Space Vector PWM (SVPWM)** for a three-phase VSI, with reference to their use in EV traction. *(b)* A 400 V DC-link EV inverter uses SVPWM with $m_a = 0.9$. Compute the fundamental RMS line-to-line output voltage and the peak phase voltage. Compare the result with a 6-step (square-wave) inverter operating on the same DC bus.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Sinusoidal PWM (SPWM):** A sinusoidal reference wave $v_{ref}(t) = V_{ref,m}\sin(\omega t)$ is compared with a high-frequency triangular carrier wave $v_{tri}$ of amplitude $V_{tri}$ at frequency $f_{sw}$. The comparator output is high when $v_{ref} > v_{tri}$ and low otherwise. This produces PWM pulses whose **average value over one switching period** tracks the sine wave. The reference/cutput ratio $m_a = V_{ref,m}/V_{tri}$ controls the fundamental amplitude.

**Space Vector PWM (SVPWM):** The three phase voltages are represented as a single rotating space vector in the α-β plane:

$$\vec{V}_{ref} = \frac{2}{3}\left( V_U + V_V \cdot e^{j120°} + V_W \cdot e^{j240°} \right)$$

There are **eight possible switching states** of a two-level VSI: six active vectors (V₁ to V₆) and two zero vectors (V₀, V₇). The hexagon formed by the six active vectors is divided into six sectors. For any reference vector $\vec{V}_{ref}$ in a given sector, the inverter dwells in the two adjacent active vectors and the zero vector for times $T_1$, $T_2$, $T_0$ such that $T_1 + T_2 + T_0 = T_{sw}$.

**SVPWM advantages in EV traction:**
- ~**15.47% higher** DC-bus utilization than SPWM in the linear region.
- Lower total harmonic distortion (THD) in phase current.
- Well-suited for digital DSP implementation — the standard in modern ECUs.
- Smoother torque ripple in the traction motor.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks** (Numerical Computation)

**Given:** $V_{DC} = 400$ V, $m_a = 0.9$ (SVPWM, linear region).

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{ph,peak} &= m_a \cdot \frac{V_{DC}}{2} = 0.9 \cdot \frac{400}{2} = 180 \text{ V} \\
V_{ph,RMS} &= \frac{180}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 127.3 \text{ V} \\
V_{LL,RMS} &= \sqrt{3} \cdot \frac{V_{ph,peak}}{\sqrt{2}} = \sqrt{3} \cdot 127.3 \approx 220.4 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}$$

**For 6-step (square wave) inverter on the same bus:**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{LL,RMS,6-step} &= \frac{2\sqrt{2}}{\pi} \cdot V_{DC} = \frac{2\sqrt{2}}{\pi} \cdot 400 \approx 360.1 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}$$

**Comparison:**

| Quantity | SVPWM ($m_a=0.9$) | 6-Step | Ratio |
|---|---|---|---|
| $V_{LL,RMS}$ | 220.4 V | 360.1 V | 0.61 |
| $V_{ph,peak}$ | 180 V | 326.5 V | 0.55 |

**Inference:** The 6-step inverter delivers ~63% more fundamental voltage but produces very high harmonic content (THD ≈ 31%), which causes large torque ripple, additional copper/iron losses, and acoustic noise. SVPWM is therefore preferred in EVs even at the cost of lower fundamental voltage, because the smooth waveform preserves motor life and ride quality.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #3:** When asked to *compare*, many students write only numerical answers without a concluding engineering statement. Always add a one-line inference explaining **why** SVPWM is preferred for traction.
>
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #4:** Using $\sqrt{3} \cdot V_{DC}/2$ instead of the correct $V_{DC}/\sqrt{2}$ for the maximum SVPWM line-to-line RMS. Memorize the factor $\frac{2\sqrt{2}}{\pi} \approx 0.9$ for square-wave mode.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- An **automotive inverter** converts HV battery DC to variable-frequency, variable-voltage 3-phase AC for the traction motor — and reverses the flow for regenerative braking.
- Standard topology: **three-phase, two-level Voltage Source Inverter (VSI)** with 6 IGBTs (or SiC MOSFETs) and 6 anti-parallel diodes.
- **DC link** is stiffened by a large capacitor; voltage ratings: **400 V** (legacy) or **800 V** (modern premium EVs).
- **SPWM** compares a sine reference with a triangle carrier; **SVPWM** uses a space-vector hexagon and gives **15.47% higher** DC-bus utilization.
- Maximum line-to-line RMS in linear SPWM: $V_{LL,RMS} = \frac{V_{DC}}{\sqrt{2}}$; in 6-step: $V_{LL,RMS} = \frac{2\sqrt{2}}{\pi} V_{DC} \approx 0.9 V_{DC}$.
- Inverter must operate in **all four quadrants** (forward/reverse, motoring/regen) — hence the term "bidirectional".
- **Switching frequency**: 5–20 kHz for IGBT, 20–50 kHz for SiC; switching losses are the dominant loss mechanism at high speeds.
- **Efficiency** target: 95–98%; the residual loss is rejected as heat via a **liquid-cooling jacket** integrated with the motor housing.
- Modern benchmarks: **SiC MOSFETs** (Tesla Model 3, Hyundai E-GMP), integrated PEU, 800 V architecture.
- Always state the **modulation region** ($0 \le m_a \le 1$ for linear) when writing PWM formulas in the exam.
- The inverter is the single most failure-prone and most-engineered power component in an EV — its design balances **efficiency, EMI, cost, thermal mass, and packaging**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Examination Tip:** When a question says "explain the working of an inverter in an EV", you MUST include: (1) the two-level VSI circuit, (2) the bidirectional motoring/regen flow, (3) the modulation strategy (SPWM or SVPWM), and (4) at least one real automotive example (e.g., Toyota Prius or Tesla Model 3). A generic answer without these four elements is graded as **incomplete** by KTU examiners.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Battery Packs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT744 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Battery Packs # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Battery Pack (Traction Battery Pack):** A high-voltage, rechargeable electrochemical energy storage assembly, typically composed of multiple Lithium-ion cells arranged in series–parallel configurations, interconnected with a Battery Management System (BMS), thermal management hardware, and protective enclosures, designed to deliver propulsion energy to the electric drivetrain of Battery Electric Vehicles (BEVs), Hybrid Electric Vehicles (HEVs), and Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicles (PHEVs). According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEMUT744)** syllabus framework, a battery pack is treated as the **central energy buffer** of any modern electrified powertrain. The pack is *not* a single monolithic unit but a hierarchical system in which individual **cells** are grouped into **modules**, which in turn are integrated into a **pack** governed by a Battery Management System. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (PEMUT744 – Module 1):** Students must be able to distinguish between cell, module, and pack-level architectures, compute pack electrical parameters (voltage, capacity, energy), describe cell formats (cylindrical, prismatic, pouch), and explain the role of the BMS in operational safety. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – Plain English Intuition Imagine a **large water tank supplying a city** through a network of pipes: - The **water tank** itself is the *battery pack* (total energy stored). - The tank is built from many **smaller water containers** stacked and connected — these are the *cells*. - These smaller containers are bundled into **transport crates** for easier handling — these are the *modules*. - A **smart supervisor** watches the water level, pressure, and temperature in every crate, opening or closing valves as needed — this is the *Battery Management System (BMS)*. - Finally, a **cooling jacket** wrapped around the tank prevents overheating — this is the *thermal management system*. So, a battery pack is simply a **carefully orchestrated collection of small electrochemical containers** that, when supervised and cooled correctly, behaves like a single, powerful, rechargeable "fuel tank" for an electric vehicle. ## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Standard Units) > [!IMPORTANT] > Every battery pack is specified by these fundamental parameters (use these exact units in KTU examinations): > - **Nominal Voltage (V):** typically **350 V – 800 V** for modern EVs. > - **Pack Energy Capacity (kWh):** typically **30 kWh – 120 kWh**. > - **Specific Energy (Wh/kg):** typically **150 – 250 Wh/kg** for Li-ion packs. > - **C-Rate (C):** charge/discharge rate relative to rated capacity. > - **State of Charge (SoC):** expressed in **% (0 – 100%)**. > - **State of Health (SoH):** expressed in **% (100% = new)**. > - **Cycle Life:** typically **1000 – 3000 cycles** to 80% SoH. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Cell → Module → Pack Hierarchy The battery pack follows a strict three-tier physical and electrical hierarchy: - **Cell (Electrochemical Unit):** The smallest individually sealed electrochemical device. Single nominal voltage (e.g., 3.6 V – 3.7 V for Li-ion). Capacity ranges from 2 Ah (pouch) to 100 Ah (prismatic EV cells). - **Module (Mechanical + Electrical Sub-Assembly):** A group of cells (typically 10–12 cells) hard-mounted into a frame with local monitoring electronics, busbars, and sometimes a dedicated cooling plate. - **Pack (Full HV System):** The complete assembly containing multiple modules, the master BMS, contactors, HV cabling, current/ voltage sensors, fuses, and the outer enclosure. ## 2.2 Cell Formats Used in Automotive Packs There are **three dominant cell formats** in the EV industry: - **Cylindrical Cells:** Examples — 18650 (18 mm × 65 mm), 21700 (21 mm × 70 mm), and 4680 (46 mm × 80 mm, Tesla). Robust mechanically, good thermal path along the cylindrical surface. Used in **Tesla, Rivian, Lucid**. - **Prismatic Cells:** Hard aluminium or steel rectangular can. Higher packing efficiency than cylindrical. Used by **BYD, BMW, Volkswagen (in some platforms)**. - **Pouch Cells:** Laminated polymer-aluminium foil. Highest energy density but requires strong external mechanical compression. Used by **GM Ultium, Hyundai E-GMP, LG Energy Solution**. ## 2.3 Series–Parallel (S–P) Configurations The most critical conceptual step in pack design is understanding how cells are wired: - **Series connection (Ns):** Increases **voltage** by adding cell voltages. Capacity (Ah) remains the same. - **Parallel connection (Np):** Increases **capacity (Ah)** by adding cell capacities. Voltage remains the same. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** To build a *400 V architecture*, you typically need ~96 to 100 cells in series. To build an *800 V architecture* (Porsche Taycan, Hyundai E-GMP, Kia EV6), you need ~192 to 200 cells in series. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Remarks | |---|-----------|---------|-------|---------| | 1 | Pack Nominal Voltage | $V_{pack} = N_s \cdot V_{cell}$ | V (Volts) | $N_s$ = cells in series, $V_{cell}$ = nominal cell voltage | | 2 | Pack Capacity (Ah) | $C_{pack} = N_p \cdot C_{cell}$ | Ah | $N_p$ = parallel strings, $C_{cell}$ = cell capacity | | 3 | Pack Energy | $E_{pack} = V_{pack} \cdot C_{pack} \cdot \dfrac{1}{1000}$ | kWh | Convert Wh → kWh by /1000 | | 4 | Pack Energy (Alternative) | $E_{pack} = N_s \cdot N_p \cdot V_{cell} \cdot C_{cell} / 1000$ | kWh | Direct S–P form | | 5 | C-Rate | $C = \dfrac{I}{C_{rated}}$ | dimensionless | $I$ = charge/discharge current (A) | | 6 | Continuous Power | $P_{pack} = V_{pack} \cdot I_{max}$ | W (or kW) | $I_{max}$ = allowable discharge current | | 7 | EV Range (Estimation) | $R = \dfrac{E_{pack}}{E_{cons}}$ | km | $E_{cons}$ = vehicle consumption in kWh/km | | 8 | Internal Heat Generation | $Q = I^{2} \cdot R_{int}$ | W | $R_{int}$ = pack internal resistance | | 9 | Energy Density (Gravimetric) | $\rho_{g} = \dfrac{E_{pack}}{m_{pack}}$ | Wh/kg | Mass-based specific energy | | 10 | Energy Density (Volumetric) | $\rho_{v} = \dfrac{E_{pack}}{V_{pack}}$ | Wh/L | Volume-based specific energy | > [!IMPORTANT] > **LaTeX Safety Note:** Absolute values and "such that" vertical bars are written as `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent breaking the markdown table syntax (per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 protocol). ## 2.5 Real-World Utility of Pack Design in Automotive Engineering A modern EV's range, charging speed, performance, and safety **all depend on pack engineering**. Series count determines the **voltage class** (400 V vs 800 V), which affects motor efficiency and fast-charging cable current. Parallel count determines **pack capacity** and therefore the **usable kWh** available to drive the vehicle. Engineers balance these against weight, volume, cost, and thermal limits — making battery pack design one of the **highest-leverage engineering decisions** in EV product development. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked-Out Problem 1: Pack Voltage, Capacity & Energy **Problem Statement (KTU Style):** A battery pack consists of **96 cells in series**, with **2 parallel strings**. Each cell has a nominal voltage of **3.7 V** and a capacity of **50 Ah**. Compute the (a) pack voltage, (b) pack capacity, and (c) pack energy in kWh. **Given Data:** $$ \begin{aligned} N_s &= 96 \text{ cells (series)} \\ N_p &= 2 \text{ strings (parallel)} \\ V_{cell} &= 3.7 \text{ V} \\ C_{cell} &= 50 \text{ Ah} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 1 — Compute Pack Voltage:** $$ \begin{aligned} V_{pack} &= N_s \cdot V_{cell} \\ &= 96 \cdot 3.7 \text{ V} \\ &= 355.2 \text{ V} \end{aligned} $$ > Textual logic: Each cell contributes 3.7 V; with 96 cells in series, voltages add directly because the same current passes through all of them. **Step 2 — Compute Pack Capacity:** $$ \begin{aligned} C_{pack} &= N_p \cdot C_{cell} \\ &= 2 \cdot 50 \text{ Ah} \\ &= 100 \text{ Ah} \end{aligned} $$ > Textual logic: Each parallel string provides 50 Ah; with 2 identical strings in parallel, the total deliverable charge is the sum of the strings. **Step 3 — Compute Pack Energy:** $$ \begin{aligned} E_{pack} &= V_{pack} \cdot C_{pack} \cdot \dfrac{1}{1000} \\ &= 355.2 \cdot 100 \cdot \dfrac{1}{1000} \\ &= 35.52 \text{ kWh} \end{aligned} $$ > Textual logic: Energy in Wh is volts × amp-hours. Dividing by 1000 converts Wh to kWh, the standard unit for EV traction batteries. **Final Answer:** $V_{pack} = 355.2 \text{ V}$, $C_{pack} = 100 \text{ Ah}$, $E_{pack} = 35.52 \text{ kWh}$. --- ## 3.2 Worked-Out Problem 2: EV Range Estimation from Pack Energy **Problem Statement:** A vehicle has a pack energy of **40 kWh** and a real-world consumption of **0.16 kWh/km**. Compute the **estimated driving range** in km. **Given Data:** $$ \begin{aligned} E_{pack} &= 40 \text{ kWh} \\ E_{cons} &= 0.16 \text{ kWh/km} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 1 — Apply Range Formula:** $$ \begin{aligned} R &= \dfrac{E_{pack}}{E_{cons}} \\ &= \dfrac{40}{0.16} \\ &= 250 \text{ km} \end{aligned} $$ > Textual logic: Total stored energy divided by per-kilometre demand gives the maximum distance the vehicle can travel before the pack is fully discharged. **Final Answer:** $R = 250 \text{ km}$. --- ## 3.3 Worked-Out Problem 3: C-Rate & Continuous Power **Problem Statement:** A pack of nominal voltage **400 V** and rated capacity **100 Ah** delivers a continuous current of **2C**. Compute (a) the actual current in Amps and (b) the continuous power in kW. **Given Data:** $$ \begin{aligned} V_{pack} &= 400 \text{ V} \\ C_{rated} &= 100 \text{ Ah} \\ C_{rate} &= 2 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 1 — Compute Actual Current:** $$ \begin{aligned} I &= C_{rate} \cdot C_{rated} \\ &= 2 \cdot 100 \\ &= 200 \text{ A} \end{aligned} $$ > Textual logic: A 2C discharge means the pack is fully discharged in 30 minutes; current = rate × capacity. **Step 2 — Compute Continuous Power:** $$ \begin{aligned} P &= V_{pack} \cdot I \\ &= 400 \cdot 200 \\ &= 80{,}000 \text{ W} \\ &= 80 \text{ kW} \end{aligned} $$ > Textual logic: Power is the product of terminal voltage and current; dividing by 1000 converts W to kW. **Final Answer:** $I = 200 \text{ A}$, $P = 80 \text{ kW}$. --- ## 3.4 Worked-Out Problem 4: Heat Generation in Pack (Thermal Management Justification) **Problem Statement:** A pack has an internal resistance of **0.05 Ω** and is discharging at a constant current of **200 A**. Compute the heat generated internally in **Watts**. **Given Data:** $$ \begin{aligned} R_{int} &= 0.05 \text{ \Omega} \\ I &= 200 \text{ A} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 1 — Apply Joule's Heating Law:** $$ \begin{aligned} Q &= I^{2} \cdot R_{int} \\ &= (200)^{2} \cdot 0.05 \\ &= 40{,}000 \cdot 0.05 \\ &= 2{,}000 \text{ W} \end{aligned} $$ > Textual logic: Every Ampere of current passing through the pack's internal resistance dissipates power as heat. At 200 A, even a small resistance (50 milliohms) produces 2 kW of waste heat — this is **why liquid cooling is mandatory** in modern EV packs. **Final Answer:** $Q = 2{,}000 \text{ W} = 2 \text{ kW}$. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Battery Pack Functional Architecture (Mermaid Flowchart) The following Mermaid block diagrams the **functional architecture of a modern EV battery pack** — the cell-to-module-to-pack hierarchy, BMS supervisory loops, and thermal management interaction. (All node IDs are alphanumeric; no reserved keywords are used.) ```mermaid flowchart TD A1[Cell Level
3.6 to 3.7 V Nominal Li-ion] A2[Cell Format 1
Cylindrical 18650 21700 4680] A3[Cell Format 2
Prismatic Aluminium Can] A4[Cell Format 3
Pouch Laminated Foil] B1[Module Level
10 to 12 Cells Series] B2[Module Hardware
Busbars Cooling Plate Cell Holders] B3[Slave BMS
Local Voltage Temperature Sensing] C1[Pack Level
High Voltage Assembly 400 V or 800 V] C2[Master BMS
SoC SoH Estimation Cell Balancing] C3[Thermal Management
Liquid Cooling Loop] C4[Pack Enclosure
IP67 Sealed Steel or Aluminium] C5[Contactors and Fuses
HVIL Precharge Circuit] D1[Vehicle Inverter and Motor] D2[Onboard Charger OBC] D3[DC Fast Charge Port CCS Type 2] A1 --> A2 A1 --> A3 A1 --> A4 A2 --> B1 A3 --> B1 A4 --> B1 B1 --> B2 B2 --> B3 B3 --> C1 C1 --> C2 C1 --> C3 C1 --> C4 C1 --> C5 C2 --> D1 C5 --> D2 C5 --> D3 C2 -.Controls.-> C3 C2 -.Protects.-> C1 ``` ## 4.2 Series-Parallel Pack Wiring Logic (Mermaid Block Diagram) The next block clarifies how individual cells are electrically combined into a pack using series and parallel connections. ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[Cell S1
3.7 V 50 Ah] S2[Cell S2
3.7 V 50 Ah] S3[Cell S3
3.7 V 50 Ah] S4[Cell S4
3.7 V 50 Ah] P1[Cell P1
3.7 V 50 Ah] P2[Cell P2
3.7 V 50 Ah] P3[Cell P3
3.7 V 50 Ah] P4[Cell P4
3.7 V 50 Ah] S1 --- S2 --- S3 --- S4 P1 --- P2 --- P3 --- P4 S4 -.Series String.-> PK P4 -.Parallel String.-> PK PK[Pack Output
14.8 V 100 Ah 1.48 kWh] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reading the diagram:** The top string (S1–S4) provides $4 \cdot 3.7 = 14.8 \text{ V}$ at 50 Ah. The bottom string (P1–P4) also produces 14.8 V at 50 Ah. Wiring the two strings **in parallel** doubles the capacity to 100 Ah while keeping voltage at 14.8 V. This is the *exact* logic that scales up to 400 V / 800 V EV packs. ## 4.3 BMS Supervisory Loop (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD V[Sense: Cell Voltages] T[Sense: Cell Temperatures] I[Sense: Pack Current Shunt] EST[SoC and SoH Estimator] BAL[Active or Passive Cell Balancer] PROT[Safety Protection Logic] RLY[Main Contactor Driver] COOL[Coolant Pump and Chiller Command] V --> EST I --> EST T --> EST T --> PROT V --> PROT EST --> BAL EST --> PROT PROT --> RLY T --> COOL EST --> COOL ``` > [!NOTE] > **Mermaid Compilation Safeguard Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (`S1`, `B3`, `EST`, `RLY`, etc.), no reserved words used as standalone node names, and labels are clean uppercase text without markdown bold/italic tags inside double-quoted labels. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > KTU Part A questions expect a concise, definition-level answer (3–5 lines). No derivations. ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme] **Define a battery pack. List the three main cell formats used in modern EV battery packs.** (3 Marks) **Model Answer:** A *battery pack* is a high-voltage, rechargeable electrochemical energy storage assembly that supplies traction power to an electric vehicle's drivetrain. It is built from individual cells interconnected in series and parallel, integrated with a Battery Management System (BMS), thermal management hardware, and a protective enclosure. The three main cell formats are: - **Cylindrical cells** (e.g., 18650, 21700, 4680) - **Prismatic cells** (hard-cased rectangular) - **Pouch cells** (laminated polymer-aluminium foil) --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme] **What is C-rate? A pack is rated 75 Ah. Calculate the current drawn when discharged at 0.5C and 2C.** (3 Marks) **Model Answer:** **C-rate** is the ratio of the charge/discharge current to the rated capacity of the cell/pack. It indicates how fast a battery is charged or discharged relative to its nominal capacity. For $C_{rated} = 75$ Ah: $$ \begin{aligned} I_{0.5C} &= 0.5 \cdot 75 = 37.5 \text{ A} \\ I_{2C} &= 2 \cdot 75 = 150 \text{ A} \end{aligned} $$ --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU Part B questions offer *internal choice* (either OR) within a module. Two completely independent 14-mark question sets are provided below — a student would answer either **Question A** *or* **Question B**. --- ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme] **A. (a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **cell → module → pack hierarchy** of an EV traction battery. List the functions of the Battery Management System (BMS). (7 Marks) **Model Answer:** The hierarchy is structured as: 1. **Cell:** Smallest sealed electrochemical unit (nominal 3.6–3.7 V for Li-ion). Available in cylindrical, prismatic, and pouch formats. 2. **Module:** A group of 10–12 cells mechanically clamped together, fitted with local busbars, a slave BMS for voltage/temperature sensing, and a cooling interface. 3. **Pack:** The complete high-voltage assembly containing multiple modules, a master BMS, contactors, fuses, HV cabling, the thermal management loop, and the outer IP67 sealed enclosure. **Functions of the BMS:** - **Cell Monitoring:** Measures individual cell voltages and temperatures continuously. - **State Estimation:** Computes State of Charge (SoC) and State of Health (SoH). - **Cell Balancing:** Equalises charge levels across cells (passive or active). - **Safety Protection:** Disconnects pack via contactors during over-voltage, under-voltage, over-current, over-temperature, or short-circuit events. - **Thermal Management Control:** Commands coolant pumps and chillers to keep the pack within the optimal 15 °C – 35 °C window. - **Communication:** Interfaces with the Vehicle Control Unit (VCU) via **CAN bus**. --- **A. (b)** A battery pack has **100 cells in series**, with **3 parallel strings**. Each cell has a nominal voltage of **3.6 V** and a capacity of **60 Ah**. Compute the (i) pack voltage, (ii) pack capacity, and (iii) pack energy in kWh. (7 Marks) **Model Answer:** **Given:** $N_s = 100$, $N_p = 3$, $V_{cell} = 3.6$ V, $C_{cell} = 60$ Ah. **(i) Pack Voltage:** $$ \begin{aligned} V_{pack} &= N_s \cdot V_{cell} \\ &= 100 \cdot 3.6 = 360 \text{ V} \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Pack Capacity:** $$ \begin{aligned} C_{pack} &= N_p \cdot C_{cell} \\ &= 3 \cdot 60 = 180 \text{ Ah} \end{aligned} $$ **(iii) Pack Energy:** $$ \begin{aligned} E_{pack} &= \dfrac{V_{pack} \cdot C_{pack}}{1000} \\ &= \dfrac{360 \cdot 180}{1000} \\ &= 64.8 \text{ kWh} \end{aligned} $$ **Final Answer:** $V_{pack} = 360 \text{ V}$, $C_{pack} = 180 \text{ Ah}$, $E_{pack} = 64.8 \text{ kWh}$. --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme] **B. (a)** Explain the **three cell formats** (cylindrical, prismatic, pouch) with their advantages, disadvantages, and one vehicle example for each. (7 Marks) **Model Answer:** **1. Cylindrical Cells (e.g., 18650, 21700, 4680):** - *Construction:* Electrodes wound into a cylindrical jellyroll and sealed in a metal can. - *Advantages:* Mechanically robust, excellent thermal path along the cylindrical wall, mature manufacturing, well-characterised safety behaviour. - *Disadvantages:* Lower packing efficiency (hexagonal stacking leaves gaps), more weld points per kWh. - *Example Use:* Tesla Model 3 / Model Y use **21700 cells**; Tesla Cybertruck uses **4680 cells**. **2. Prismatic Cells:** - *Construction:* Rectangular aluminium or steel hard case enclosing stacked electrode layers. - *Advantages:* Higher packing efficiency than cylindrical, fewer cells needed for a given pack, robust housing. - *Disadvantages:* Heavier than pouch, can swell if internal pressure builds. - *Example Use:* **BYD Blade** cells, **BMW i3 (2014–2022)**, **Volkswagen MEB platform**. **3. Pouch Cells:** - *Construction:* Electrodes laminated in a soft polymer-aluminium foil pouch. - *Advantages:* Highest gravimetric and volumetric energy density, flexible form factor. - *Disadvantages:* Requires strong external compression to prevent swelling, more sensitive to puncture and thermal runaway propagation. - *Example Use:* **GM Ultium**, **Hyundai E-GMP (Ioniq 5, EV6)**, **Ford Mustang Mach-E**. --- **B. (b)** A 400 V, 50 kWh EV pack discharges at an average current of **125 A**. The pack's internal resistance is **0.08 Ω**. Compute (i) the **internal heat generated** in Watts, and (ii) the **C-rate** of the pack if its rated capacity is **125 Ah**. (7 Marks) **Model Answer:** **Given:** $I = 125$ A, $R_{int} = 0.08$ Ω, $E_{pack} = 50$ kWh, $C_{rated} = 125$ Ah, $V_{pack} = 400$ V. **(i) Internal Heat Generation:** $$ \begin{aligned} Q &= I^{2} \cdot R_{int} \\ &= (125)^{2} \cdot 0.08 \\ &= 15{,}625 \cdot 0.08 \\ &= 1{,}250 \text{ W} \end{aligned} $$ > This is equivalent to **1.25 kW of continuous waste heat** that the cooling system must evacuate. **(ii) C-Rate:** $$ \begin{aligned} C_{rate} &= \dfrac{I}{C_{rated}} \\ &= \dfrac{125}{125} \\ &= 1\text{C} \end{aligned} $$ > A 1C discharge means the pack would be fully depleted in **1 hour** if the current were maintained constant. **Final Answer:** $Q = 1{,}250 \text{ W}$, $C_{rate} = 1\text{C}$. --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Valuation:** > 1. **Forgetting the /1000 conversion** when reporting pack energy in kWh. Always write: $E = \dfrac{V \cdot C}{1000}$ to get kWh. Reporting **35,520 Wh** instead of **35.52 kWh** is a frequent error. > 2. **Mixing up series and parallel effects:** Series adds **voltage**; parallel adds **capacity (Ah)**. Students often write "series adds capacity" — this is a 1-mark direct cut. > 3. **Unit confusion:** Writing $V_{pack}$ in Ah or $C_{pack}$ in V is treated as a *conceptual error* by examiners and attracts 0.5–1 mark deduction. > 4. **Omitting the BMS** in a long answer on pack architecture: The BMS is a **mandatory sub-topic** under the KTU 2024 PEMUT744 syllabus and must be explained. > 5. **Skipping the heat generation step** in a numerical: Examiners explicitly look for the $I^{2} \cdot R_{int}$ step in any thermal-context question. Always show it. --- ## 5.4 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-density rapid-revision checklist — read this 5 minutes before the exam:** - ✅ A **battery pack** = series–parallel assembly of cells + modules + BMS + thermal management + enclosure. - ✅ **Hierarchy:** Cell → Module → Pack → Vehicle HV bus. - ✅ **Three cell formats:** Cylindrical, Prismatic, Pouch. Each has distinct mechanical, thermal, and energy-density trade-offs. - ✅ **Series adds voltage; Parallel adds capacity.** - ✅ **Core formulas (memorise these in order):** - $V_{pack} = N_s \cdot V_{cell}$ - $C_{pack} = N_p \cdot C_{cell}$ - $E_{pack} = \dfrac{V_{pack} \cdot C_{pack}}{1000}$ in kWh - $C_{rate} = I / C_{rated}$ - $Q = I^{2} \cdot R_{int}$ - $R = E_{pack} / E_{cons}$ - ✅ **Modern EV architectures:** 400 V (most EVs) vs 800 V (Porsche Taycan, Hyundai E-GMP, Kia EV6). 800 V enables faster charging and thinner HV cables. - ✅ **BMS functions** (must know all 6): Monitoring, SoC/SoH estimation, balancing, protection, thermal control, CAN communication. - ✅ **Optimal pack operating temperature:** 15 °C – 35 °C. Below 0 °C, charging risks lithium plating; above 45 °C, lifetime degrades rapidly. - ✅ **Typical energy density:** 150–250 Wh/kg for modern Li-ion packs; Tesla 4680 cells target ~300 Wh/kg at pack level. - ✅ **Heat generation** scales with the *square* of current — a doubled current generates 4× the heat. This is why pack cooling system design is current-dependent, not energy-dependent. - ✅ **KTU keyword mappings for full marks:** Use exact syllabus terms — *traction battery*, *state of charge (SoC)*, *state of health (SoH)*, *cell balancing*, *HVIL (High Voltage Interlock Loop)*, *IP67 enclosure*, *thermal runaway*.

topics.title: Battery Management Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT744 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Battery Management Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Battery Management System (BMS):** An electronic control system that manages a rechargeable battery (cell or battery pack) by monitoring its state, calculating secondary data, reporting that data, protecting the battery, controlling its environment, and balancing/equalizing its state-of-charge (SOC) to ensure safe, efficient, and reliable operation throughout its service life.

In the context of **Modern Automotive Technologies** (KTU 2024 Scheme), the BMS is the **central nervous system of any electrified powertrain** — whether in a Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV), Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV), Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicle (PHEV), or Fuel Cell Electric Vehicle (FCEV). It is classified as a **critical safety-critical embedded system** because a malfunction can lead to thermal runaway, fire, or explosion.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Smart Bank Vault Manager" Analogy**

Imagine a bank vault containing thousands of safe deposit boxes, each holding valuable but volatile gold bars. A vault manager must:

1. **Continuously check** the temperature, weight, and integrity of every box.
2. **Report** the current inventory and any anomalies to headquarters.
3. **Redistribute** gold from overflowing boxes to underfilled ones so they all last equally long.
4. **Lock down** the entire vault if any box overheats or is tampered with.
5. **Log** every transaction for auditing.

The **BMS is exactly this vault manager**, but for electrochemical cells instead of gold bars. It ensures no single cell is "overworked," no cell runs too hot, and every cell reaches the end of its life at the same time — maximizing pack longevity, range, and safety.

### 1.3 Key Performance Metrics & Standard Reference Values

The following metrics are universally used across the EV industry and form the **core vocabulary** of BMS:

| Metric | Standard Symbol | Typical Range | Importance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Nominal Cell Voltage | $V_{nom}$ | **3.6 V – 3.7 V** (NMC) | Defines pack architecture |
| Pack Nominal Voltage | $V_{pack}$ | **350 V – 800 V** | Affects motor and charging design |
| State of Charge | $SOC$ | **0 % – 100 %** | "Fuel gauge" reading |
| State of Health | $SOH$ | **80 % – 100 %** (new) | End-of-life when SOH ≈ 80 % |
| C-Rate (Discharge) | $C$ | **0.5 C – 3 C** (typical EV) | Current delivery capability |
| Operating Temperature | $T_{op}$ | **−20 °C to +60 °C** | Thermal window for safe operation |
| Cycle Life | $N_{cycle}$ | **1000 – 3000 cycles** | Total charge-discharge cycles |

### 1.4 Why a BMS is Mandatory in Modern EVs

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Single-Cell vs Pack Reality**
> A single Li-ion cell delivers only **~3.7 V** and **~2.5 Ah**. To power a 400 V traction motor drawing 200 kW, the pack needs **~108 cells in series** and many parallel strings. Without a BMS, individual cells drift apart in voltage, causing:
> - **Overcharging** → electrolyte decomposition, gas venting, fire
> - **Over-discharging** → copper dissolution, internal short circuit
> - **Capacity imbalance** → premature pack failure
> - **Thermal runaway** → catastrophic cell-to-cell propagation

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Architecture of a BMS

A modern automotive BMS follows a **three-tier hierarchical architecture** that mirrors the physical layout of the battery pack:

**Tier 1 — Cell Monitoring Unit (CMU) / Slave BMS**
- Mounted directly on **each cell module** (typically 10–16 cells).
- Performs analog measurements: cell voltage, module temperature, current shunt voltage.
- Communicates via **isolated serial bus** (typically daisy-chained SPI or isolated UART).

**Tier 2 — Battery Control Unit (BCU) / Master BMS**
- Aggregates data from all CMUs.
- Runs **State Estimation Algorithms** (SOC, SOH, SOP).
- Executes **Cell Balancing Algorithms**.
- Communicates with Vehicle Control Unit (VCU) via **CAN bus** (typically **CAN 2.0B at 500 kbps**).

**Tier 3 — Pack Disconnect Unit (PDU) / High-Voltage Junction Box**
- Contains **contactors, pre-charge relay, current sensor (Hall-effect), and HV fuse**.
- Executes emergency disconnect commanded by BCU during fault conditions.

### 2.2 Core Functional Block Analysis

The BMS performs six critical functions. Each must be analyzed for its operational principle:

**(a) Cell Voltage Monitoring**
- High-precision **16-bit ADC** sampling at **≥ 100 Hz**.
- Measurement accuracy requirement: **± 5 mV per cell** (industry standard for automotive AEC-Q100 qualification).

**(b) Current Sensing**
- **Hall-effect sensor** for bidirectional current measurement.
- Typical full-scale range: **± 400 A** with **± 0.5 %** accuracy.
- Used for Coulomb counting (integration of current over time).

**(c) Temperature Monitoring**
- **NTC thermistors** (10 kΩ at 25 °C, β = 3950) embedded between cells.
- Minimum of **2 sensors per module**; automotive systems use **4–8 per module** for safety margin.

**(d) State Estimation**
- **SOC (State of Charge):** Remaining capacity as a percentage of rated capacity.
- **SOH (State of Health):** Ratio of current maximum capacity to rated capacity.
- **SOP (State of Power):** Maximum charge/discharge power deliverable for next 10 s and 10 min windows.

**(e) Cell Balancing (Equalization)**
- Prevents capacity drift between series-connected cells.
- Two principal methods: **Passive (resistive dissipation)** and **Active (inductive/capacitive transfer)**.

**(f) Safety Protection & Fault Diagnostics**
- Detects: over-voltage, under-voltage, over-current, short circuit, over-temperature, insulation breakdown.
- Complies with **ISO 26262 ASIL-C or ASIL-D** functional safety requirements.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Equations for KTU 2024 ESE — Memorize these for full marks in numericals.**

| # | Formula | Description | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $SOC(t) = SOC_0 + \dfrac{1}{C_{rated}} \displaystyle\int_{0}^{t} \eta \cdot I(\tau) \, d\tau$ | Coulomb Counting (Ampere-hour integration) | Dimensionless (%) |
| 2 | $C_{rate} = \dfrac{I_{discharge}}{C_{rated}}$ | C-rate definition | Dimensionless (1/h) |
| 3 | $E_{pack} = V_{pack} \times C_{pack}$ | Stored pack energy | Wh or kWh |
| 4 | $V_{oc} = f(SOC, T)$ | Open-circuit voltage as function of SOC and temperature | V |
| 5 | $V_{terminal} = V_{oc} - I \cdot R_{internal}$ | Terminal voltage under load | V |
| 6 | $SOH = \dfrac{C_{max,current}}{C_{rated}} \times 100\%$ | State of Health (capacity-based) | % |
| 7 | $P_{balance} = \dfrac{(V_{cell,max} - V_{cell,min})^2}{R_{balance}}$ | Passive balancing power dissipation | W |
| 8 | $\eta_{transfer} = \dfrac{E_{out}}{E_{in}} = 1 - \dfrac{R_{wire} \cdot I^2}{V \cdot I}$ | Active balancing efficiency | Dimensionless |
| 9 | $Q_{heat} = I^2 \cdot R_{internal} \cdot t$ | Joule heating in cell (for thermal model) | J |
| 10 | $P_{max} = V_{oc} \cdot I_{max} - I_{max}^2 \cdot R_{int}$ | Maximum deliverable power (SOP) | W |
| 11 | $V_{pack} = n_s \cdot V_{cell} + n_p \cdot$ parallel redundancy | Pack voltage from series/parallel cells | V |
| 12 | $C_{pack} = n_p \cdot C_{cell}$ | Pack capacity from parallel strings | Ah |

**Boundary Conditions for KTU Numerical Problems:**

- **SOC must lie in** $\vert SOC \vert \leq 100\%$
- **C-rate sign convention:** Discharge = positive, Charge = negative
- **Coulombic efficiency** $\eta$ typically **0.98 – 0.99** for Li-ion
- **End-of-life criterion:** EV batteries are retired when **SOH ≤ 80 %**

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Range Prediction:** SOC drives the dashboard "km remaining" estimate.
- **Regenerative Braking Control:** VCU uses SOP to limit regen current so cells are not over-charged.
- **Second-Life Batteries:** A retired EV pack (SOH 70–80 %) is repurposed for **grid energy storage** — a $30 billion projected market by 2030.
- **Fast-Charging Infrastructure:** Modern 350 kW DC fast chargers require BMS handshaking via **ISO 15118 / DIN 70121** protocol to negotiate safe current limits.
- **V2G (Vehicle-to-Grid):** The BMS becomes the gatekeeper for bidirectional power flow, a key feature of smart-grid integration.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Coulomb Counting for SOC Estimation

**Coulomb counting** is the foundational SOC estimation technique. Below is the complete, step-by-step mathematical derivation suitable for a 14-mark KTU ESE answer.

**Starting Point — Definition of Charge Delivered:**

The total charge $Q$ delivered by a battery from time $0$ to time $t$ is defined as the time-integral of the discharge current:

$$
Q(t) = \int_{0}^{t} I(\tau) \, d\tau
$$

**Step 1 — Apply Coulombic Efficiency**

In real cells, charge lost to side reactions (electrolyte decomposition, SEI growth) means that not every coulomb of charge input becomes stored charge. This is captured by the **Coulombic efficiency** $\eta$:

$$
Q_{stored}(t) = \eta \cdot \int_{0}^{t} I(\tau) \, d\tau
$$

**Step 2 — Define Normalized SOC**

The State of Charge is the *remaining* charge expressed as a fraction of the *rated* (nominal) capacity $C_{rated}$. Starting from an initial SOC value $SOC_0$:

$$
SOC(t) = SOC_0 + \frac{Q_{stored}(t)}{C_{rated}}
$$

**Step 3 — Combine into Master Equation**

Substituting Step 1 into Step 2:

$$
\boxed{\,SOC(t) = SOC_0 + \frac{\eta}{C_{rated}} \int_{0}^{t} I(\tau) \, d\tau\,}
$$

**Step 4 — Discrete-Time Implementation (for embedded firmware)**

In a real BMS microcontroller, the continuous integral is replaced by a discrete-time summation sampled every $\Delta t$ seconds:

$$
SOC[k] = SOC[k-1] + \frac{\eta \cdot I[k] \cdot \Delta t}{C_{rated}}
$$

where $k$ is the sample index. This is implemented in **fixed-point arithmetic** on a 32-bit MCU for deterministic real-time performance.

**Step 5 — Error Accumulation Analysis**

The fundamental weakness of Coulomb counting is **drift**. If the current sensor has a small offset error $\Delta I$, the SOC error grows linearly with time:

$$
\Delta SOC_{error}(t) = \frac{\Delta I \cdot t}{C_{rated}}
$$

For a **0.1 A** offset on a **50 Ah** pack, after **100 hours** of driving:

$$
\Delta SOC_{error} = \frac{0.1 \times 100}{50} = 20\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** This is why **modern automotive BMS systems use Kalman Filters or Neural Networks** to fuse Coulomb counting with voltage-based SOC estimates — a standard 14-mark KTU question topic.

### 3.2 Derivation: Pack Energy and Range Calculation

**Given Data (typical KTU numerical):**
- Pack: 96 cells in series, 2 parallel strings
- Cell nominal voltage: $V_{cell} = 3.7$ V
- Cell capacity: $C_{cell} = 50$ Ah
- Vehicle consumption: $E_{cons} = 15$ kWh / 100 km

**Step 1 — Calculate Pack Voltage**

$$
V_{pack} = n_s \times V_{cell} = 96 \times 3.7 = 355.2 \, \text{V}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Pack Capacity**

$$
C_{pack} = n_p \times C_{cell} = 2 \times 50 = 100 \, \text{Ah}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Pack Energy**

$$
E_{pack} = V_{pack} \times C_{pack} = 355.2 \times 100 = 35{,}520 \, \text{Wh} = 35.52 \, \text{kWh}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Usable SOC Window**

EVs typically only use **SOC 10 % to 90 %** to protect cell longevity:

$$
E_{usable} = 0.80 \times E_{pack} = 0.80 \times 35.52 = 28.42 \, \text{kWh}
$$

**Step 5 — Calculate Vehicle Range**

$$
R = \frac{E_{usable}}{E_{cons}} \times 100 = \frac{28.42}{15} \times 100 = 189.5 \, \text{km}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Cell Balancing Method Comparison

| Parameter | Passive Balancing | Active Balancing (Inductive) | Active Balancing (Capacitive) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Principle** | Dissipate excess energy as heat via resistor | Transfer energy via switched inductor | Transfer energy via switched capacitor |
| **Efficiency** | **0 %** (energy wasted) | **80 % – 90 %** | **70 % – 85 %** |
| **Current Capability** | Low (≤ 1 A) | High (up to 10 A) | Medium (2–5 A) |
| **Thermal Load** | High — requires heatsink | Low | Low |
| **Complexity** | Simple, cheap | Complex (MOSFETs, inductor, control) | Moderate (many capacitors) |
| **Cost** | **Low (preferred for low-cost EVs)** | High | Medium |
| **Use Case** | Nissan Leaf, older EVs | Tesla Model S, Lucid Air | Research / niche applications |
| **Heat Generated** | $Q = I^2 R \cdot t$ J | Negligible | Negligible |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 BMS Functional Architecture (Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the complete interaction between BMS sub-modules, the battery pack, and the rest of the vehicle. This is the **single most important diagram** for a 14-mark KTU ESE question on BMS architecture.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Battery Pack: 96s2p Li-ion] --> B[Cell Monitoring Unit CMU]
    A --> C[Thermal Sensors NTC Array]
    A --> D[Current Sensor Hall Effect]
    
    B --> E[Battery Control Unit BCU]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    
    E --> F[SOC Estimator]
    E --> G[SOH Estimator]
    E --> H[SOP Calculator]
    E --> I[Cell Balancing Controller]
    E --> J[Safety and Fault Handler]
    E --> K[Thermal Management Controller]
    
    F --> L[CAN Bus Interface 500 kbps]
    G --> L
    H --> L
    J --> L
    
    L --> M[Vehicle Control Unit VCU]
    L --> N[Instrument Cluster SOC Display]
    L --> O[Onboard Charger OBC]
    L --> P[DC Fast Charger External]
    
    I --> Q[Cell Balancing Circuitry]
    Q --> A
    
    J --> R[Pack Disconnect Unit PDU]
    R --> S[Main Contactor HV Relay]
    R --> T[Pre-charge Relay]
    R --> U[Pyrotechnic Fuse]
    
    K --> V[Coolant Pump and Chiller]
    V --> A
```

### 4.2 Cell Balancing Algorithm Flow (Active Inductive Method)

This diagram illustrates the closed-loop control flow used in active balancing — a high-yield topic for KTU viva and short-answer questions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Start Balancing Cycle] --> S2[Read All Cell Voltages]
    S2 --> S3{Identify Max and Min Voltage Cells}
    S3 --> S4[Delta V greater than Threshold?]
    S4 --> S5[No: Skip Balancing]
    S4 --> S6[Yes: Activate MOSFET Switching]
    S6 --> S7[Store Energy in Inductor]
    S7 --> S8[Switch Polarity via Diode]
    S8 --> S9[Transfer Energy to Weak Cell]
    S9 --> S10[Re-measure Cell Voltages]
    S10 --> S11{Balanced?}
    S11 --> S12[Yes: Stop]
    S11 --> S6[No: Repeat Cycle]
```

### 4.3 BMS Software Stack (Layered Model)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L5[Application Layer: SOC SOH SOP Algorithms] --> L4
    L4[Middleware Layer: CAN Stack UDS Diagnostics ISO 14229] --> L3
    L3[RTOS Layer: FreeRTOS or AUTOSAR OS Task Scheduling] --> L2
    L2[Hardware Abstraction Layer HAL: ADC SPI GPIO Drivers] --> L1
    L1[Physical Layer: MCU Pins Cell Sensors Contactors]
```

### 4.4 Safety Fault Tree (Top-Down Analysis)

A fault tree is essential for ISO 26262 compliance. The **top event** is "Thermal Runaway of Battery Pack":

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    TOP[Battery Pack Thermal Runaway] --> A1[Cell Overcharge]
    TOP --> A2[Cell Internal Short Circuit]
    TOP --> A3[Cell External Short Circuit]
    TOP --> A4[Cell Overheating]
    TOP --> A5[Mechanical Crush or Penetration]
    
    A1 --> B1[BMS Failure to Detect]
    A1 --> B2[Charger Malfunction]
    
    A2 --> B3[Separator Failure]
    A2 --> B4[Manufacturing Defect]
    A2 --> B5[Dendrite Growth After Cycling]
    
    A4 --> B6[Cooling System Failure]
```
