```mermaid
    A4 --> B7[High C-rate Discharge]
    
    A5 --> B8[Vehicle Crash]
    A5 --> B9[Road Debris Impact]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define Battery Management System. List any four of its primary functions.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> A Battery Management System (BMS) is an embedded electronic system that monitors, controls, and protects a rechargeable battery pack to ensure safe operation, optimal performance, and extended service life.

**Four primary functions:**

1. **Cell Monitoring** — Measurement of voltage, current, and temperature of every cell.
2. **State Estimation** — Computation of SOC, SOH, and SOP in real time.
3. **Cell Balancing** — Equalization of charge levels across series-connected cells.
4. **Safety Protection** — Detection of over-voltage, under-voltage, over-current, over-temperature, and insulation faults; initiation of emergency disconnect.

*(Any 4 of the 6 functions discussed in Section 2.2 — full 3 marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between passive and active cell balancing techniques with respect to efficiency and complexity.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Passive Balancing | Active Balancing |
|---|---|---|
| Principle | Energy dissipated as heat through a parallel resistor | Energy transferred between cells via inductor or capacitor |
| Energy Efficiency | **0 %** (wasted as heat) | **75 % – 90 %** |
| Circuit Complexity | Simple — only a switch and resistor per cell | Complex — MOSFETs, magnetic/ capacitive storage, control logic |
| Thermal Management | Requires heatsinking | Minimal cooling needed |
| Cost | Low | High |
| Typical Application | Entry-level EVs, e-bikes | Premium long-range EVs |

*(3 marks: 1 for definition of each + 1 for any 2 distinguishing parameters.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**OR**

**Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the three-tier hierarchical architecture of a modern automotive BMS. Draw a labeled block diagram showing the interaction between CMU, BCU, and PDU.

**(b) [7 Marks)** A 96s2p Li-ion battery pack has cells rated at 3.7 V nominal, 50 Ah. The vehicle consumes 15 kWh per 100 km. Calculate (i) pack nominal voltage, (ii) pack energy in kWh, (iii) usable energy (assuming 80 % SOC window), and (iv) theoretical driving range.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Three-Tier Architecture:**

**Tier 1 — Cell Monitoring Unit (CMU):**
The CMU is the lowest level, mounted physically on each battery module. It typically handles **10–16 cells** in series. Its primary role is high-precision analog data acquisition:
- Cell voltage measurement via differential ADC with **± 5 mV** accuracy.
- Module temperature measurement via NTC thermistor array.
- Galvanically isolated **daisy-chain SPI** communication upward to the BCU.
- Local balancing execution (passive resistors switched by MOSFETs).

**Tier 2 — Battery Control Unit (BCU):**
The BCU is the **intelligence center**, typically implemented on an **AEC-Q100 qualified 32-bit MCU** (e.g., Infineon Aurix TC27x, NXP S32K3xx). It performs:
- Aggregation of data from all CMUs.
- Real-time execution of SOC, SOH, SOP algorithms (often Kalman Filter based).
- Cell balancing decision logic.
- CAN 2.0B communication with VCU.
- Diagnostics per **UDS (Unified Diagnostic Services, ISO 14229)**.

**Tier 3 — Pack Disconnect Unit (PDU):**
The PDU contains the high-voltage switching hardware:
- **Main positive contactor** (rated for full pack voltage and peak current).
- **Pre-charge relay** in series with a pre-charge resistor (~100 Ω) to limit inrush current when closing contactors on a capacitive load.
- **Hall-effect current sensor** for pack-level current measurement.
- **Pyrotechnic fuse** for one-shot catastrophic over-current interruption.
- **Insulation Monitoring Device (IMD)** per IEC 61557 for HV safety.

**Block Diagram:** (Refer to SECTION 4.1 — full marks for correctly drawn, labeled architecture with clear signal flow arrows.)

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given: $n_s = 96$, $n_p = 2$, $V_{cell} = 3.7$ V, $C_{cell} = 50$ Ah, $E_{cons} = 15$ kWh/100 km.

**(i) Pack nominal voltage:**

$$
V_{pack} = n_s \times V_{cell} = 96 \times 3.7 = 355.2 \, \text{V}
$$

**(ii) Pack energy:**

$$
E_{pack} = V_{pack} \times C_{pack} = 355.2 \, \text{V} \times (2 \times 50) \, \text{Ah} = 35{,}520 \, \text{Wh} = 35.52 \, \text{kWh}
$$

**(iii) Usable energy (80 % SOC window):**

$$
E_{usable} = 0.80 \times 35.52 = 28.42 \, \text{kWh}
$$

**(iv) Driving range:**

$$
R = \frac{E_{usable}}{E_{cons}} \times 100 = \frac{28.42}{15} \times 100 \approx 189.5 \, \text{km}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - Students frequently confuse **series and parallel effects**: voltage adds in **series**, capacity adds in **parallel**. Getting this wrong loses 3–4 marks immediately.
> - Do NOT forget the **usable SOC window**. A common 1-mark loss is reporting $E_{pack}$ as the energy available for driving.
> - Always state **units explicitly** — "35,520 Wh" without conversion to kWh is incomplete.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is State of Charge (SOC)? Derive the Coulomb counting equation for SOC estimation. State one major limitation of this method.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare passive and active cell balancing techniques across at least **five technical parameters**. State which method is preferred for a premium long-range EV and justify your answer.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) SOC Definition and Derivation:**

**Definition:** State of Charge is the **ratio of remaining charge in a battery to its rated (nominal) capacity**, expressed as a percentage.

**Derivation (Coulomb Counting):**

Step 1: Charge delivered from $0$ to $t$ is the integral of current:
$$
Q(t) = \int_0^t I(\tau) \, d\tau
$$

Step 2: Apply Coulombic efficiency $\eta$ to account for losses:
$$
Q_{stored} = \eta \int_0^t I(\tau) \, d\tau
$$

Step 3: Express SOC as remaining capacity normalized by $C_{rated}$:
$$
SOC(t) = SOC_0 + \frac{\eta}{C_{rated}} \int_0^t I(\tau) \, d\tau
$$

**Major Limitation:** The method suffers from **cumulative drift** due to current sensor offset errors. Even a small offset of 0.1 A integrated over time causes SOC error to grow linearly, making it unsuitable as a standalone estimator in production vehicles. **Solution:** Modern BMS fuses Coulomb counting with **voltage-based OCV correlation** or **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** algorithms.

**(b) Cell Balancing Comparison:**

(Full 5-parameter table as in SECTION 3.3 — minimum 5 parameters required.)

**Preferred Method:** Active inductive balancing is preferred for a **premium long-range EV** because:
- High efficiency (80–90 %) preserves usable range.
- High balancing current (up to 10 A) reduces equalization time during fast charging.
- Minimal heat generation simplifies thermal packaging.
- Critical for large packs where imbalance losses in passive balancing would be several hundred watts.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — High-Density Summary**

- **BMS = electronic brain of EV battery pack** — safety-critical, AEC-Q100 qualified, ISO 26262 compliant.
- **Three-tier architecture:** CMU (cell-level sensing) → BCU (computation) → PDU (high-voltage switching).
- **Six core functions:** Monitoring, State Estimation (SOC/SOH/SOP), Balancing, Thermal Management, Protection, Communication.
- **State of Charge (SOC):** $SOC(t) = SOC_0 + \frac{\eta}{C_{rated}} \int_0^t I \, dt$ — remember Coulomb counting equation by heart.
- **State of Health (SOH):** Capacity ratio; battery retired at **SOH ≤ 80 %**.
- **Cell Balancing:** Passive = cheap, inefficient; Active = expensive, efficient — pick based on vehicle class.
- **C-rate:** $C = I / C_{rated}$; typical EV = 1 C to 3 C; higher C-rates cause faster degradation.
- **Series/Parallel rule:** Voltage adds in series, capacity adds in parallel — golden rule for all pack design questions.
- **Communication:** CAN 2.0B at 500 kbps is the automotive standard; ISO 15118 governs V2G/digital charging.
- **Safety window:** EVs use **SOC 10 % – 90 %** to maximize cycle life.
- **Standard cell voltage:** **3.6 – 3.7 V** for NMC; **3.2 V** for LFP; **2.1 V** for lead-acid.
- **Thermal management:** Liquid cooling is standard for > 50 kWh packs; air cooling for smaller packs.
- **Functional Safety:** Aim for **ASIL-C** (minimum) and **ASIL-D** (preferred) per ISO 26262.
- **Calculation tip:** Always convert Wh to kWh (÷ 1000) and include units in every step of a KTU numerical — silent unit errors cost 1 mark each.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Motors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT744 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Motors # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is an Electric Motor in the Automotive Context? An **electric motor** is an electromechanical energy conversion device that transforms **electrical energy** into **mechanical energy** (rotational motion) using the principles of electromagnetic induction and Lorentz force. In modern automotive technology, electric motors serve as the **prime mover** for electric vehicles (EVs), hybrid electric vehicles (HEVs), and plug-in hybrid electric vehicles (PHEVs), replacing or supplementing the internal combustion engine (ICE). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (PEMUT744 — Module 1):** Motors constitute the propulsion heart of any electrified powertrain. They are characterized by their **torque-speed envelope**, **power density (kW/kg)**, **efficiency map**, and **thermal operating range** — all of which dictate vehicle performance, range, and cost. > [!NOTE] > **Key Automotive Metric — Power Density:** Modern automotive traction motors target **> 5 kW/kg** and specific torque **> 15 Nm/kg**. Reference benchmark: Tesla Model 3 rear motor achieves ~**10 kW/kg**. ## 1.2 Classification of Motors Used in Modern Vehicles Automotive motors are broadly classified by their **power source**, **commutation method**, and **rotor topology**. ### A. By Power Input | Type | Full Form | Automotive Application | |------|-----------|----------------------| | DC | Direct Current | Older EVs, auxiliary systems (windows, wipers) | | AC | Alternating Current | Modern EV traction motors | ### B. By Rotor Construction & Commutation | Motor Type | Rotor Type | Commutation | Modern Use | |------------|------------|-------------|------------| | **BLDC** | Permanent Magnet (PM) | Electronic (Sensor-based) | EVs, e-bikes, hybrid drives | | **PMSM** | Permanent Magnet (PM) | Electronic (Sinusoidal) | Tesla, BMW i3, Toyota Prius | | **SRM** | Salient Pole (No magnet) | Electronic | Renault, some racing EVs | | **IM (Induction)** | Squirrel-cage rotor | Electronic (VFD) | Tesla Model S/X, Audi e-tron | | **Wound-Rotor Synchronous** | Field-wound rotor | Slip ring + Electronic | High-power commercial EVs | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Muscle Analogy 💪 Imagine an electric motor as a **bicep muscle** in the human body: - **The Battery** = the food (energy source) - **The Motor** = the muscle (converts stored energy into motion) - **The Controller/Inverter** = the brain + nerves (decides how hard/fast the muscle contracts) - **The Gearbox** = the tendons (transfers force to bones/wheels) - **The Wheels** = the joints that move Just as muscles come in different types (fast-twitch for sprinters, slow-twitch for marathon runners), motors come in different topologies optimized for **acceleration** (high torque at low speed — like PMSM) or **sustained cruising** (high efficiency at constant speed — like Induction Motors). > [!NOTE] > **The Torque-Speed Envelope:** The "personality" of a motor is captured by its **torque-speed characteristic curve**. PMSMs deliver huge starting torque (great for launches), while IMs maintain constant power over a wide speed range (great for highway cruising). ## 1.4 Why Motors Matter in Modern Automotive Technology 1. **Zero tailpipe emissions** — motors enable pure EVs. 2. **Instant peak torque** at zero RPM — unlike ICE which needs RPM to build torque. 3. **Regenerative braking** — motors become generators, recovering kinetic energy. 4. **Multi-motor architectures** — Tesla Model S Plaid uses **3 motors**; Rivian uses **4 motors** (one per wheel). 5. **Efficiency** — motors routinely exceed **90–96%** efficiency, vs. ~30–40% for ICE. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operating Principle of an Electric Motor The fundamental operating principle rests on **two physical laws**: **Law 1 — Lorentz Force (DC / BLDC motors):** A current-carrying conductor placed in a magnetic field experiences a force. $$\vec{F} = I(\vec{L} \times \vec{B})$$ **Law 2 — Faraday's Law (Induction Motors):** A rotating magnetic field induces EMF in a closed rotor conductor, and the induced current interacts with the field to produce torque. $$\text{Stator magnetic field } \rightarrow \text{Rotating at } \omega_s \rightarrow \text{Cuts rotor bars} \rightarrow \text{Induced EMF} \rightarrow \text{Induced current} \rightarrow \text{Force} \rightarrow \text{Rotation}$$ ## 2.2 Torque-Speed Characteristics — The "Motor Personality Chart" | Motor Type | Torque @ Start | Speed Range | Efficiency | Cost | |-----------|----------------|-------------|-----------|------| | **BLDC / PMSM** | **Very High (3–5× rated)** | Wide (with field weakening) | 92–97% | High (rare-earth magnets) | | **Induction Motor** | Moderate (1.5–2× rated) | Very wide (5–10× base speed) | 90–94% | Moderate (no magnets) | | **SRM** | Very High | Wide | 85–92% | Low (no magnets, no rotor winding) | | **DC Series** | Very High (5–7×) | Limited | 75–85% | Low (legacy systems) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Field Weakening:** Above base speed, the controller reduces the stator flux (back-EMF) to allow higher speeds at the cost of reduced torque. This is why EVs can cruise at 200+ km/h. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $P = T \cdot \omega$ | Mechanical power = torque × angular velocity | W, Nm, rad/s | | 2 | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | Conversion between RPM and rad/s | rad/s, RPM | | 3 | $P = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$ | Power in terms of RPM and torque | W, RPM, Nm | | 4 | $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P_p}$ | Synchronous speed (AC induction / synchronous motor) | RPM, Hz, poles | | 5 | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | Slip of induction motor (dimensionless) | unitless | | 6 | $E = 4.44 \, f \, N_{ph} \, \Phi \, K_w$ | Induced EMF per phase | V, Hz, Wb | | 7 | $\tau_{em} = \dfrac{P_{em}}{\omega_s} = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V^2 \cdot (R_2/s)}{(R_2/s)^2 + X_2^2}$ | Electromagnetic torque (IM) | Nm | | 8 | $T \propto s$ (low slip region) | Torque-slip linear region for IM | — | | 9 | $T_{max} = \dfrac{3 V^2}{2 \omega_s X_2}$ | Maximum torque of IM (independent of $R_2$) | Nm | | 10 | $s_{max} = \dfrac{R_2}{X_2}$ | Slip at maximum torque | unitless | | 11 | $\tau_m = \dfrac{3}{2} \cdot \dfrac{P_p}{2} \cdot \lambda_{PM} \cdot i_q$ | PMSM electromagnetic torque (MTPA) | Nm | | 12 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{out} + P_{losses}}$ | Motor efficiency | % | | 13 | $E_b = V - I_a R_a$ | Back-EMF relation (DC motor) | V, A, Ω | | 14 | $N \propto \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{\Phi}$ | Speed equation for DC motor | RPM | > [!NOTE] > **Where:** > - $P_p$ = number of poles > - $\lambda_{PM}$ = permanent magnet flux linkage > - $i_q$ = q-axis current component > - $K_w$ = winding factor (typically 0.85–0.95) ## 2.4 Real-World Automotive Engineering Utility - **Drive cycle matching:** PMSMs are favored in city BEVs (frequent stop-and-go → high torque density helps). - **Highway cruising:** IMs are favored in Tesla Model S (sustained high-speed operation favors IM's constant-power region). - **Cost-sensitive markets:** SRMs are favored (Renault Fluence Z.E.) — no rare-earth dependency. - **Thermal management:** PM motors need careful cooling; IMs are inherently more robust thermally. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Power, Torque, and Speed **Objective:** Derive the universal equation $P = 2\pi N T / 60$ from first principles. **Step 1 — Start from the definition of angular velocity.** $$\omega = \frac{d\theta}{dt}$$ For a shaft rotating at constant speed, $\omega$ is constant and equals the angular displacement per unit time. **Step 2 — Relate RPM to angular velocity.** If the shaft rotates $N$ revolutions per minute, then in one minute it covers $N$ revolutions. Since one revolution = $2\pi$ radians: $$\theta_{\text{in 1 min}} = 2\pi N \text{ radians}$$ Therefore, angular velocity in rad/s: $$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60}$$ **Step 3 — Define mechanical power as the rate of doing work.** Power is the product of force and velocity. For rotation, force becomes tangential force $F_t$ acting at radius $r$, producing torque $T = F_t \cdot r$, and tangential velocity is $v_t = \omega \cdot r$. Therefore: $$P = F_t \cdot v_t = (T/r) \cdot (\omega r) = T \cdot \omega$$ **Step 4 — Substitute $\omega$ from Step 2 into Step 3.** $$\boxed{P = \frac{2\pi N T}{60}}$$ where: $$ \begin{aligned} P & = \text{mechanical power in watts (W)} \\ N & = \text{shaft speed in revolutions per minute (RPM)} \\ T & = \text{torque in newton-metres (Nm)} \\ 2\pi/60 & \approx 0.10472 \text{ (conversion constant)} \end{aligned} $$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Synchronous Speed of an Induction Motor **Objective:** Derive $N_s = 120 f / P_p$. **Step 1 — Definition.** The stator magnetic field rotates at the **synchronous speed** $N_s$ such that the alternating stator current of frequency $f$ produces one full electrical cycle per pole pair per cycle. **Step 2 — One revolution of the magnetic field corresponds to one electrical cycle per pole pair.** If the stator has $P_p$ poles, then one mechanical revolution produces $P_p / 2$ electrical cycles. **Step 3 — Relationship between electrical frequency and mechanical speed.** $$f = \frac{P_p}{2} \cdot \frac{N_s}{60}$$ **Step 4 — Solve for $N_s$.** $$\boxed{N_s = \frac{120 f}{P_p}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Numerical example:** A 4-pole induction motor on a 50 Hz supply: > $N_s = 120 \times 50 / 4 = 1500$ RPM (synchronous). At full load, slip is ~3–5%, so the rotor runs at ~1455 RPM. --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Slip and Rotor Frequency **Step 1 — Slip definition.** $$s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$$ **Step 2 — Frequency of induced current in rotor.** The relative speed between rotating field and rotor is $N_s - N_r$, so the rotor "sees" a frequency: $$f_r = \frac{P_p}{2} \cdot \frac{N_s - N_r}{60} = s \cdot f$$ **Step 3 — Express rotor induced EMF.** $$E_2 = 4.44 \, f_r \, N_{ph} \, \Phi \, K_w = 4.44 \, s f \, N_{ph} \, \Phi \, K_w$$ **Step 4 — Implication.** At start, $N_r = 0$, so $s = 1$, $f_r = f$ — the rotor sees full stator frequency (maximum induced current → high starting torque). --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Traction Motors for EVs | Parameter | PMSM | BLDC | Induction | SRM | |-----------|------|------|-----------|-----| | **Peak Efficiency** | 96–97% | 95% | 93–94% | 90% | | **Power Density** | **Highest** | Very High | Moderate | High | | **Torque Ripple** | Low | Moderate (6-step) | Low | **High** | | **Rotor Cooling** | Difficult (PMs) | Difficult (PMs) | Easy (cage) | Easy (robust) | | **Magnet Cost** | High (NdFeB) | High (NdFeB) | **None** | **None** | | **High-Speed Capability** | Good (with FW) | Good | **Excellent** | Excellent | | **Fault Tolerance** | Low | Low | Moderate | **High** | | **Use in EVs** | Tesla M3, BMW i3, Toyota | E-bikes, e-scooters | Tesla S/X, Audi e-tron | Renault Fluence | # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Architecture of an EV Powertrain with Motor ```mermaid flowchart LR batt[Battery Pack
400V or 800V DC] --> inv[Power Electronics Inverter
DC to 3-phase AC] inv --> ctrl[Vehicle Control Unit VCU
Torque Request Processing] ctrl -->|PWM Signals| inv inv --> motor[Traction Motor
PMSM or IM or SRM] motor -->|Mechanical Torque| gear[Single Speed
Gear Reducer] gear --> diff[Differential] diff --> wheelL[Rear Left Wheel] diff --> wheelR[Rear Right Wheel] motor -.->|Regenerative Mode| gen[Generator Action
Back EMF] gen -->|Recovers Kinetic Energy| inv inv -->|Charges| batt tcm[Thermal Management
Liquid Cooling] -.->|Cools| motor tcm -.->|Cools| inv ``` ## 4.2 Motor Classification Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TD root[Electric Motors for EVs] --> ac[AC Motors] root --> dc[DC Motors Legacy] ac --> pmsm[Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor PMSM] ac --> im[Induction Motor IM] ac --> srm[Switched Reluctance Motor SRM] pmsm --> pmsm_app[Applications: Tesla Model 3, BMW i3] im --> im_app[Applications: Tesla Model S, Audi e-tron] srm --> srm_app[Applications: Renault Fluence, Some racing EVs] dc --> series[Series DC] dc --> shunt[Shunt DC] dc --> compound[Compound DC] series --> legacy[Legacy: Older EVs, Forklifts, Auxiliary Motors] ``` ## 4.3 Torque-Speed Envelope of a Typical EV Traction Motor ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph TS[Typical EV Motor Torque-Speed Envelope] direction LR t1[Maximum Torque Region
Constant Torque up to base speed N_base] -->|Constant Power| t2[Field Weakening Region
Constant Power from N_base to N_max] t1_axis[N_base ~ 4000 RPM] --- t2_axis[N_max ~ 16000 RPM] end ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the curve:** Below base speed, the motor delivers **constant maximum torque** (limited by inverter current). Above base speed, voltage becomes the limit; the controller reduces flux to keep voltage within bounds, producing **constant power** (torque decreases as speed increases). ## 4.4 Block Diagram — Motor Controller Signal Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD pedal[Accelerator Pedal
APPS Sensor] --> vcu[VCU Vehicle Control Unit] brake[Brake Pedal
Brake Pressure Sensor] --> vcu vcu -->|Torque Request Nm| inv_ctrl[Inverter Controller DSP FPGA] inv_ctrl -->|Gate Drive Signals| igbt[IGBT or SiC MOSFET Bridge] igbt -->|3-Phase PWM| motor_stator[Motor Stator Windings] motor_stator --> em_torque[Electromagnetic Torque Output] rotor_pos[Rotor Position Sensor
Resolver or Encoder] -->|Position Feedback| inv_ctrl motor_temp[Motor Temperature Sensor PT100] -->|Temp Feedback| vcu ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] (3 Marks)** **Differentiate between a BLDC motor and a PMSM.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | BLDC Motor | PMSM | |---------|-----------|------| | **Back-EMF waveform** | Trapezoidal | Sinusoidal | | **Current waveform** | Quasi-square (6-step) | Sinusoidal (FOC) | | **Torque ripple** | Higher (5–15%) | Lower (1–3%) | | **Control complexity** | Simpler (6-step commutation) | Complex (Field-Oriented Control) | | **NVH performance** | Rougher, noisier | Smoother, quieter | | **Automotive usage** | E-bikes, e-scooters, auxiliary drives | Main traction in EVs (Tesla, BMW, Hyundai) | **(3 marks — full differentiation expected)** --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] (3 Marks)** **State and explain the significance of the back-EMF constant ($K_e$) in a DC motor.** **Model Answer:** The back-EMF constant $K_e$ (in V·s/rad) relates the motor's rotational speed to the back-EMF it generates: $$E_b = K_e \cdot \omega$$ **Significance:** 1. It is numerically equal to the **torque constant** $K_t$ in SI units: $K_t = K_e$. 2. Determines the **no-load speed**: $N_0 = V / K_e$ (when $I_a = 0$). 3. Higher $K_e$ → motor generates higher voltage per RPM → useful for **regenerative braking** energy recovery. 4. Used in **closed-loop speed control** for accurate speed regulation. **(3 marks)** --- ## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice) (1 × 14 Marks) ### **Question A [14 Marks] — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]** **Q. (a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of an electric vehicle powertrain and the role of the traction motor in it. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A 4-pole, 50 Hz, three-phase induction motor has a full-load slip of 4%. Calculate: **(i)** Synchronous speed, **(ii)** Actual rotor speed, **(iii)** Rotor frequency, **(iv)** Per-phase rotor EMF if the rotor has 60 turns/phase with a winding factor of 0.96 and the air-gap flux per pole is 25 mWb. **(7 Marks)** --- #### ✅ Model Solution to Q. (a) — 7 Marks **EV Powertrain Architecture:** The major components are: 1. **Battery Pack** — High-voltage DC source (typically 400V or 800V). 2. **Power Inverter** — Converts DC to 3-phase AC; uses IGBTs or SiC MOSFETs. 3. **Traction Motor** — Converts electrical to mechanical energy. 4. **Transmission** — Single-speed reducer (most EVs) or 2-speed (Porsche Taycan). 5. **VCU** — Vehicle Control Unit; orchestrates torque delivery. 6. **Thermal Management** — Liquid cooling loop for motor + inverter. 7. **On-board Charger & DC-DC Converter** — for charging and 12V auxiliaries. **Role of the Traction Motor:** - Provides **propulsive torque** from 0 RPM (unlike ICE). - Operates as a **generator** during regenerative braking. - Enables **torque vectoring** in multi-motor setups. - Characterized by its **torque-speed envelope** matched to the drive cycle. **Block diagram (drawn on answer sheet):** ``` Battery → Inverter → Motor → Gearbox → Differential → Wheels ↑ ↑ ↑ ↑ └── VCU ───┴───── Pedal Sensors ──┘ ``` **(7 marks — full marks for labeled diagram + explanation of all 7 components + motor's specific role)** --- #### ✅ Model Solution to Q. (b) — 7 Marks **Given:** - $P_p = 4$ poles - $f = 50$ Hz - $s_{fl} = 0.04$ - $N_{ph} = 60$ turns/phase - $K_w = 0.96$ - $\Phi = 25$ mWb $= 25 \times 10^{-3}$ Wb **(i) Synchronous Speed:** $$N_s = \frac{120 f}{P_p} = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = \boxed{1500 \text{ RPM}}$$ **(ii) Actual Rotor Speed:** $$N_r = N_s (1 - s) = 1500 \times (1 - 0.04) = 1500 \times 0.96 = \boxed{1440 \text{ RPM}}$$ **(iii) Rotor Frequency:** $$f_r = s \cdot f = 0.04 \times 50 = \boxed{2 \text{ Hz}}$$ **(iv) Per-phase Rotor EMF:** $$E_2 = 4.44 \cdot f_r \cdot N_{ph} \cdot \Phi \cdot K_w$$ $$E_2 = 4.44 \times 2 \times 60 \times 25 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.96$$ $$E_2 = 4.44 \times 2 \times 60 \times 0.025 \times 0.96$$ $$E_2 = \boxed{12.79 \text{ V (per phase)}}$$ **(7 marks — 1.5 + 1.5 + 1.5 + 2.5 marks distribution)** --- ### **Question B [14 Marks] — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Alternative]** **Q. (a)** Compare PMSM, BLDC, Induction Motor, and Switched Reluctance Motor for EV traction applications, on the basis of: efficiency, power density, cost, torque ripple, and fault tolerance. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A PMSM produces a torque of 250 Nm at 3000 RPM. Calculate: **(i)** Output mechanical power in kW, **(ii)** Output power in horsepower, **(iii)** Torque in lb-ft (pound-feet), **(iv)** If the motor efficiency is 95%, find the input electrical power and input current at a 400V DC bus. **(7 Marks)** --- #### ✅ Model Solution to Q. (a) — 7 Marks **Comparison Table:** | Parameter | PMSM | BLDC | Induction | SRM | |-----------|------|------|-----------|-----| | **Efficiency** | **Highest (96–97%)** | High (95%) | Moderate (93–94%) | Low (88–90%) | | **Power Density** | **Highest** | High | Moderate | High | | **Cost** | High (NdFeB magnets) | High (NdFeB magnets) | Moderate (no magnets) | **Lowest** | | **Torque Ripple** | Very Low (1–3%) | Moderate (5–10%) | Low (2–5%) | **Highest (10–20%)** | | **Fault Tolerance** | Low (PM demagnetization) | Low | Moderate | **Highest** | | **Cooling Difficulty** | High | High | Low | Low | **Concluding Remarks (2 marks):** - **PMSM** is the preferred choice for premium EVs. - **IM** is chosen for cost + reliability balance (Tesla S/X). - **SRM** is emerging for magnet-free designs. - **BLDC** dominates small/medium EVs (2-wheelers). **(7 marks — 1 mark per parameter × 4 motors = 4 marks + 3 marks for remarks)** --- #### ✅ Model Solution to Q. (b) — 7 Marks **Given:** $T = 250$ Nm, $N = 3000$ RPM, $\eta = 0.95$, $V_{bus} = 400$ V DC **(i) Mechanical Output Power:** $$P = \frac{2\pi N T}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 3000 \times 250}{60}$$ $$P = \frac{2\pi \times 750000}{60} = \frac{4{,}712{,}388.98}{60}$$ $$P \approx \boxed{78{,}540 \text{ W} \approx 78.54 \text{ kW}}$$ **(ii) Power in Horsepower:** $$1 \text{ HP} = 745.7 \text{ W}$$ $$P_{hp} = \frac{78540}{745.7} = \boxed{105.32 \text{ HP}}$$ **(iii) Torque in lb-ft:** $$1 \text{ Nm} = 0.7376 \text{ lb-ft}$$ $$T_{lb-ft} = 250 \times 0.7376 = \boxed{184.4 \text{ lb-ft}}$$ **(iv) Input Electrical Power and Current:** $$P_{in} = \frac{P_{out}}{\eta} = \frac{78.54}{0.95} = \boxed{82.67 \text{ kW}}$$ $$I_{in} = \frac{P_{in}}{V_{bus}} = \frac{82670}{400} = \boxed{206.68 \text{ A}}$$ **(7 marks — distribution: 2 + 1.5 + 1 + 2.5)** --- > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Unit conversion errors:** Many students write $N$ as rad/s directly in $P = T\omega$ — this is WRONG. Always convert RPM to rad/s first using $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$. > 2. **Confusing rotor frequency with stator frequency:** In induction motors, $f_r = s \cdot f$, NOT $f$. Marks deducted if direct 50 Hz is used in $E_2$ formula. > 3. **Forgetting the winding factor $K_w$:** Students often omit $K_w = 0.96$ in EMF calculations — full marks NOT awarded. > 4. **Synchronous speed calculation:** Writing $120 f / P$ instead of $120 f / P_p$ loses 0.5 marks. > 5. **Confusing pole pairs with poles:** $P_p$ = total poles. Some derivations use $p$ = pole pairs. Check the formula sheet carefully. --- ## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ An electric motor converts **electrical energy → mechanical energy** using Lorentz force (PM/DC motors) or electromagnetic induction (IMs). - ✅ The **universal power equation** is $P = 2\pi N T / 60$ — memorize this cold. - ✅ **Synchronous speed:** $N_s = 120 f / P_p$ — only for AC synchronous and induction motors. - ✅ **Slip** ($s$) is what differentiates an induction motor's rotor speed from synchronous speed. - ✅ **At start (s = 1):** rotor frequency = supply frequency (max induced current, max torque). - ✅ **PMSM** = highest efficiency + power density; favored in modern EVs. - ✅ **IM** = no magnets, cheaper, robust — used in Tesla S/X. - ✅ **Field weakening** allows motors to spin 3–4× above base speed at reduced torque. - ✅ **Torque density** benchmark: PMSM > BLDC > SRM > IM. - ✅ Always use $K_w$ (winding factor, ~0.85–0.96) in EMF equations. - ✅ **Regenerative braking** = motor runs in generator mode, recovering kinetic energy. - ✅ For EV applications, the **torque-speed envelope** has two zones: constant torque (low speed) and constant power (high speed / field weakening). - ✅ Key KTU-expected diagrams: EV powertrain block diagram, motor classification tree, torque-speed curve, inverter-motor block diagram. - ✅ Always label units (RPM, Nm, kW, Hz, Wb) — examiners penalize unit omissions.

topics.title: Electronic Power Control Units
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT744 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Electronic Power Control Units

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Electronic Power Control Unit (EPCU)** in an automobile is an embedded electronic subsystem designed to regulate, monitor, and optimize the delivery of electrical power to various actuators, sensors, and subsystems of the vehicle. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus framework for Modern Automotive Technologies, the EPCU encompasses the **Electronic Control Unit (ECU)**, **Engine Control Module (ECM)**, **Transmission Control Module (TCM)**, **Body Control Module (BCM)**, and the supporting power-electronic driver circuits that switch high-current loads such as fuel injectors, ignition coils, solenoid valves, electric motors, and lighting arrays.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** An *Electronic Power Control Unit* is a microprocessor/microcontroller-based module that receives low-level sensor inputs, processes them through deterministic firmware algorithms (mapped as Look-Up Tables or control laws), and delivers precisely conditioned power outputs to electromechanical actuators. The control loop operates at switching frequencies between **50 Hz and 20 kHz** for most automotive applications.

### 1.2 Constituent Modules of the EPCU Family

The EPCU is not a single chip but a *distributed architecture* of cooperating modules. The principal members are tabulated below.

| Module | Abbreviation | Primary Function |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Engine Control Module | ECM | Air-fuel ratio, ignition timing, idle speed |
| Transmission Control Module | TCM | Gear shifting, torque converter lock-up |
| Anti-lock Braking System Module | ABS/ESC | Wheel slip regulation via hydraulic valves |
| Body Control Module | BCM | Lighting, wipers, central locking, accessories |
| Battery Management System | BMS | Cell balancing, State-of-Charge (SoC) estimation |
| Electric Power Steering Module | EPS | Motor torque assist based on steering input |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Brain and Hand" Analogy:** Think of the EPCU as the *brain* of a modern car. The **sensors** are its *eyes and ears*, the **microcontroller** is its *decision-making brain*, and the **power semiconductor drivers** (MOSFETs, IGBTs) are its *muscles*. Just like your brain tells your hand how hard to grip a glass (based on visual feedback about weight and slipperiness), the EPCU tells the fuel injector exactly how long to stay open (based on oxygen-sensor feedback about exhaust composition). Without the EPCU, the engine is like a body without a nervous system — powerful but uncoordinated.

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants and Metrics

- **Standard Automotive Voltage:** $\mathbf{12\text{ V}}$ (nominal), with operating range **9 V to 16 V**.
- **Truck/Heavy-Duty Voltage:** $\mathbf{24\text{ V}}$ nominal.
- **Microcontroller Clock Frequency:** typically $\mathbf{16\text{ MHz}}$ to $\mathbf{200\text{ MHz}}$.
- **CAN Bus Speed:** $\mathbf{500\text{ kbps}}$ (high-speed CAN), $\mathbf{125\text{ kbps}}$ (low-speed CAN).
- **Operating Temperature Range:** $\mathbf{-40^{\circ}\text{C}}$ to $\mathbf{+125^{\circ}\text{C}}$ (under-hood environment).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Layered Architecture of an Electronic Power Control Unit

The EPCU is conceptually divided into **four functional layers**. Understanding each layer is a high-frequency KTU exam requirement.

1. **Input / Sensing Layer:** Converts physical quantities (temperature, pressure, position, speed) into standardized voltage signals, typically **0 V to 5 V** analog or **PWM** digital.
2. **Signal Conditioning Layer:** Filters noise, amplifies weak signals (e.g., from a thermistor or strain gauge), and shifts voltage levels to the ADC range.
3. **Processing Layer:** A microcontroller or DSP executes the control law. Outputs decisions every **5 ms to 10 ms** (typical engine control loop period).
4. **Power Output Layer:** Uses power semiconductors to switch high-current actuators safely and efficiently.

### 2.2 Pulse Width Modulation (PWM) — The Heart of Power Control

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Concept:** PWM is the *modulation technique* by which the EPCU delivers a *variable average power* to a load by switching the supply voltage at a fixed frequency and varying the *duty cycle*.

Let $T$ be the period of one switching cycle and $T_{on}$ be the duration for which the switch is closed (power delivered). Then the **duty cycle** $D$ is:

$$D = \frac{T_{on}}{T}$$

The **average voltage** delivered to the load is:

$$V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{s}$$

where $V_s$ is the supply voltage. The **average power** dissipated in a resistive load $R$ becomes:

$$P_{avg} = \frac{(V_{avg})^2}{R} = D^2 \cdot \frac{V_s^2}{R}$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Application Context |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Duty Cycle | $D = T_{on}/T$ | dimensionless | PWM control of actuators |
| 2 | Average Output Voltage | $V_{avg} = D \cdot V_s$ | Volts (V) | DC motor speed control |
| 3 | Average Load Power | $P_{avg} = D^2 V_s^2 / R$ | Watts (W) | Resistive loads (heaters) |
| 4 | Energy per PWM Cycle | $E = V_s \cdot I_{load} \cdot T_{on}$ | Joules (J) | Injector solenoid energising |
| 5 | Switching Power Loss | $P_{sw} = \tfrac{1}{2} V_s I_{load} (t_r + t_f) f_{sw}$ | Watts (W) | MOSFET/IGBT thermal design |
| 6 | Conduction Power Loss | $P_{cond} = I_{rms}^2 \cdot R_{DS(on)}$ | Watts (W) | MOSFET heat sink sizing |
| 7 | RMS Current | $I_{rms} = I_{peak}\sqrt{D}$ | Amperes (A) | Heated O2 sensor, glow plug |
| 8 | ADC Quantization | $\Delta V = V_{ref}/2^{n}$ | Volts (V) | Sensor resolution calculation |
| 9 | Filter Cutoff (RC) | $f_c = 1/(2\pi RC)$ | Hertz (Hz) | Anti-aliasing filter design |
| 10 | Battery SoC | $SoC = (Q_{remaining}/Q_{rated})\times 100\%$ | % | EV battery management |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are the *backbone of every design problem* on EPCU. In real automotive design, the values of $D$ and $f_{sw}$ are tuned so that $P_{sw} + P_{cond}$ stays within the *thermal budget* of the semiconductor package, typically **< 2 W** for a small signal MOSFET and **< 50 W** for a traction inverter IGBT.

### 2.4 The 'Why' Behind PWM — Why Not Just Use a Variable Resistor?

A mechanical rheostat (variable resistor) wastes the *unwanted* power as heat in the resistor itself. Power dissipated in the series resistor when motor needs half power is $P_{lost} = I^2 R$, often nearly equal to the useful power. PWM avoids this by keeping the switch either *fully ON* (zero voltage drop, near-zero loss) or *fully OFF* (no current, zero loss). The transition is so fast that the load "sees" only the average — an elegant and highly efficient approach reaching **> 90% efficiency**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Average Power Delivered to a Resistive Load via PWM

**Problem Statement:** A **12 V** automotive supply drives a rear-window defogger of resistance **$1.2\ \Omega$** through a MOSFET switched at **$f_{sw} = 100\text{ Hz}$** with duty cycle **$D = 0.4$**. Find (a) the average load voltage, (b) the RMS current, and (c) the average power dissipated in the load.

**Step 1 — Compute the Switching Period.**

$$T = \frac{1}{f_{sw}} = \frac{1}{100} = 0.01\ \text{s} = 10\ \text{ms}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the ON-Time.**

$$T_{on} = D \cdot T = 0.4 \times 0.01 = 4\ \text{ms}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Average Voltage.**

$$V_{avg} = D \cdot V_s = 0.4 \times 12 = 4.8\ \text{V}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Peak (Instantaneous) Current.**

$$I_{peak} = \frac{V_s}{R} = \frac{12}{1.2} = 10\ \text{A}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the RMS Current.**

$$I_{rms} = I_{peak}\sqrt{D} = 10 \times \sqrt{0.4} = 10 \times 0.6325 = 6.325\ \text{A}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the Average Load Power.**

$$P_{avg} = I_{rms}^{2} \cdot R = (6.325)^2 \times 1.2 = 40.0 \times 1.2 = 48.0\ \text{W}$$

**Verification (alternative route):**

$$P_{avg} = D \cdot \frac{V_s^2}{R} = 0.4 \times \frac{144}{1.2} = 0.4 \times 120 = 48.0\ \text{W}\ \checkmark$$

> Both routes agree — formula consistency is confirmed.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Power Loss in the Switching MOSFET

**Problem Statement:** Using the same circuit, the MOSFET has an ON-resistance $R_{DS(on)} = 0.02\ \Omega$, rise time $t_r = 200\text{ ns}$, fall time $t_f = 150\text{ ns}$. Compute total switching loss and conduction loss per switching cycle.

**Step 1 — Compute Conduction Loss.**

$$P_{cond} = I_{rms}^{2} \cdot R_{DS(on)} = (6.325)^2 \times 0.02 = 40.0 \times 0.02 = 0.80\ \text{W}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Switching Loss.**

$$P_{sw} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot V_s \cdot I_{load} \cdot (t_r + t_f) \cdot f_{sw}$$

$$P_{sw} = 0.5 \times 12 \times 10 \times (200 + 150)\times 10^{-9} \times 100$$

$$P_{sw} = 0.5 \times 12 \times 10 \times 350 \times 10^{-9} \times 100$$

$$P_{sw} = 60 \times 350 \times 10^{-7} = 0.021\ \text{W} = 21\ \text{mW}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Total Power Dissipated in the MOSFET.**

$$P_{total} = P_{cond} + P_{sw} = 0.80 + 0.021 = 0.821\ \text{W}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Conduction loss dominates here because $D$ is moderate. At higher $f_{sw}$ (e.g., 20 kHz for a DC-DC converter), switching loss would dominate and would force the designer to choose a faster MOSFET with lower gate charge $Q_g$.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Choice of Power Semiconductor

| Device | Voltage Rating | Current Rating | Switching Speed | Best Application |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| BJT (Bipolar Junction Transistor) | 400 V | 10 A | Slow ($\mu$s) | Legacy ignition modules |
| Power MOSFET | 100 V | 100 A | Fast (ns) | 12 V/24 V body electronics, motor drives |
| IGBT | 1200 V | 600 A | Medium ($\mu$s) | EV traction inverter, hybrid vehicles |
| SiC MOSFET | 1700 V | 200 A | Very fast (ns) | Next-gen EV powertrain |
| GaN HEMT | 650 V | 60 A | Ultra-fast (ns) | On-board charger, DC-DC converters |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** A *typical KTU Part B question* may ask you to justify *why an IGBT is used in an EV traction inverter rather than a power MOSFET*. The answer lies in the trade-off: IGBTs handle **high voltage (>600 V)** and **high current (>300 A)** with acceptable switching loss, whereas a MOSFET of equivalent rating would have huge conduction losses because $R_{DS(on)}$ increases super-linearly with breakdown voltage rating.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram of a Generic EPCU (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Physical Quantity\nTemperature, Pressure, Speed] --> B[Sensor\nNTC, MAP, Hall]
    B --> C[Signal Conditioning\nAmplifier and RC Filter]
    C --> D[Analog to Digital Converter\nADC 12 bit, 1 MSps]
    D --> E[Microcontroller\n32 bit ARM Cortex]
    E --> F[Control Algorithm\nPID, LUT, State Machine]
    F --> G[PWM Generator\nDuty Cycle D]
    G --> H[Gate Driver\nIsolation and Level Shift]
    H --> I[Power MOSFET\nor IGBT]
    I --> J[Actuator\nMotor, Injector, Lamp]
    J --> K[Feedback to Sensor]
    E --> L[CAN Transceiver\nISO 11898]
    L --> M[Other ECUs\nin the Vehicle Network]
```

### 4.2 Closed-Loop Control Architecture for Electronic Throttle Control

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Accelerator Pedal\nDriver Input] --> B[TPS Sensor\nThrottle Position Sensor]
    B --> C[ECM Microcontroller\nReads Pedal Angle]
    C --> D[PID Controller\nComputes Error]
    D --> E[PWM Signal\nVariable Duty Cycle]
    E --> F[H Bridge Driver\nDC Motor]
    F --> G[Throttle Valve\nOpens or Closes]
    G --> H[TPS Feedback\nActual Position]
    H --> D
    G --> I[Engine Intake Air\nDelivered to Cylinder]
```

### 4.3 Power Distribution Hierarchy in a Modern Vehicle

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[12V Lead Acid\nor 48V Li-ion Battery] --> B[Power Distribution Box\nFuses and Relays]
    B --> C1[Engine Control Module]
    B --> C2[Transmission Control Module]
    B --> C3[Body Control Module]
    B --> C4[Lighting Control Module]
    B --> C5[Infotainment Head Unit]
    C1 --> D1[Ignition Coils]
    C1 --> D2[Fuel Injectors]
    C3 --> D3[LED Headlamps]
    C3 --> D4[Wiper Motor]
    C5 --> D5[Display, Speakers, Amplifier]
    C1 <-->|CAN Bus 500 kbps| C2
    C2 <-->|CAN Bus| C3
    C3 <-->|LIN Bus 19.2 kbps| C4
```

### 4.4 PWM Waveform — Visual Timeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[T = 0 ms\nSwitch OFF] --> B[T = 4 ms\nSwitch ON\nPower Delivered]
    B --> C[T = 10 ms\nSwitch OFF\nCycle Repeats]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** In the waveform above, the duty cycle $D = 4/10 = 0.4$, meaning 40% of the time the MOSFET is ON and 60% is OFF. The load *averages* this chopped waveform to behave as if receiving $4.8\text{ V}$ instead of $12\text{ V}$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*Define the term "Electronic Control Unit (ECU)" and list its three main functional blocks.*

**Model Answer:**
An Electronic Control Unit (ECU) is a microcontroller-based embedded module that processes sensor inputs and commands actuators in a vehicle. Its three main functional blocks are:
1. **Input/Signal Conditioning Stage** — accepts analog and digital sensor signals.
2. **Processing Stage** — runs firmware algorithms (PID, LUT, state machines).
3. **Output/Power Driver Stage** — switches actuators using power semiconductors.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*What is Pulse Width Modulation? Why is it preferred over linear regulation in automotive power control?*

**Model Answer:**
PWM is a technique where a switch is turned fully ON and OFF at high frequency, varying the ON-time *fraction* (duty cycle $D$) to control average power delivered to a load. It is preferred because the switch dissipates negligible power in either state, yielding **efficiencies > 90%**, whereas a linear regulator (transistor operating in active region) wastes the excess voltage as heat. PWM is therefore essential in modern vehicles for driving motors, lights, and heaters efficiently.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — June 2024]*

**(a)** With the help of a labelled block diagram, describe the architecture of an Electronic Engine Control Module. Explain the role of the crankshaft position sensor and the cam position sensor in fuel injection timing. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A fuel injector solenoid has a resistance of $R = 2.5\ \Omega$ and is driven from a $12\text{ V}$ supply through a PWM signal at $f_{sw} = 250\text{ Hz}$ with $D = 0.35$. Compute the average current, RMS current, and power delivered per pulse. **(7 Marks)**

##### Model Solution to (a):

- **Architecture (3 marks):** Draw/describe: Sensor layer → Signal conditioning → Microcontroller → Driver stage → Actuators.
- **Crankshaft Position Sensor (CKP) (2 marks):** Provides **engine RPM and piston position** to the ECM; used to compute *when* to fire the spark plug and *when* to open the injector. Operates on *variable reluctance* or *Hall-effect* principle.
- **Camshaft Position Sensor (CMP) (2 marks):** Identifies which cylinder is on the *compression stroke*, allowing **sequential fuel injection** rather than wasteful simultaneous firing. Together, CKP + CMP enable the famous *four-stroke sequential injection* logic.

##### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Period:**
$$T = 1/f_{sw} = 1/250 = 4\ \text{ms}$$

**Step 2 — ON time:**
$$T_{on} = D \cdot T = 0.35 \times 4 = 1.4\ \text{ms}$$

**Step 3 — Peak current:**
$$I_{peak} = V_s / R = 12 / 2.5 = 4.8\ \text{A}$$

**Step 4 — Average current (over one full period):**
$$I_{avg} = D \cdot I_{peak} = 0.35 \times 4.8 = 1.68\ \text{A}$$

**Step 5 — RMS current:**
$$I_{rms} = I_{peak} \sqrt{D} = 4.8 \times \sqrt{0.35} = 4.8 \times 0.5916 = 2.84\ \text{A}$$

**Step 6 — Average power:**
$$P_{avg} = I_{rms}^{2} R = (2.84)^2 \times 2.5 = 8.066 \times 2.5 = 20.16\ \text{W}$$

**Energy per pulse:**
$$E_{pulse} = P_{avg} \cdot T = 20.16 \times 0.004 = 0.0806\ \text{J} = 80.6\ \text{mJ}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]*

**(a)** Compare IGBT, Power MOSFET, and BJT for use in Electric Vehicle traction inverters. Justify which device is most suitable and state two reasons. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for switching power loss in a MOSFET used as a PWM driver. A MOSFET switches a $15\ \text{A}$ load from a $24\ \text{V}$ DC bus with rise time $t_r = 100\ \text{ns}$ and fall time $t_f = 80\ \text{ns}$ at $f_{sw} = 50\ \text{kHz}$. Find the switching power loss. **(7 Marks)**

##### Model Solution to (a):

| Device | Voltage Rating | Conduction Loss | Switching Speed |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| BJT | High | Medium | Slow |
| Power MOSFET | Low–Medium | Low (at low V) | Very Fast |
| IGBT | **High (≥600 V)** | **Medium** | Medium |

**Justification (4 marks):** IGBT is most suitable for EV traction inverters because:
1. **High voltage handling** (>600 V required for 400 V EV busbars).
2. **Low conduction loss** at high currents due to *conductivity modulation* of the drift region.
3. **Easy gate drive** (voltage-controlled like a MOSFET, unlike BJT which needs continuous base current).
4. Proven ruggedness under short-circuit conditions.

##### Model Solution to (b):

**Derivation (4 marks):**

During turn-ON, the voltage across the MOSFET falls linearly from $V_s$ to 0 while current rises linearly from 0 to $I_{load}$. Instantaneous power is:
$$p(t) = v(t) \cdot i(t)$$

The energy dissipated during one rise is:
$$E_{rise} = \int_0^{t_r} V_s \left(1 - \frac{t}{t_r}\right) \cdot I_{load} \frac{t}{t_r}\, dt = \tfrac{1}{6} V_s I_{load} t_r$$

By symmetry, the fall dissipates:
$$E_{fall} = \tfrac{1}{6} V_s I_{load} t_f$$

Total per cycle:
$$E_{sw} = \tfrac{1}{6} V_s I_{load} (t_r + t_f)$$

Multiplying by $f_{sw}$ gives power loss:

$$P_{sw} = \tfrac{1}{6} V_s I_{load} (t_r + t_f) f_{sw}$$

**Numerical Evaluation (3 marks):**
$$P_{sw} = \tfrac{1}{6} \times 24 \times 15 \times (100 + 80) \times 10^{-9} \times 50{,}000$$

$$P_{sw} = \tfrac{1}{6} \times 24 \times 15 \times 180 \times 10^{-9} \times 5 \times 10^{4}$$

$$P_{sw} = \tfrac{1}{6} \times 24 \times 15 \times 9 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$P_{sw} = \tfrac{1}{6} \times 3.24 = 0.54\ \text{W}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls where KTU students lose marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Average vs RMS** — Average voltage is $D \cdot V_s$, but average *power* on a resistive load uses $I_{rms}^2 R$ — do *not* square $V_{avg}$ for power on a chopped PWM waveform.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert microseconds** — Always convert $t_r, t_f$ to seconds *before* multiplying; $100\ \text{ns} = 100 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{s}$.
> 3. **Wrong CAN bus speed** — High-speed CAN is **500 kbps**, low-speed is **125 kbps**; LIN is **19.2 kbps**. Mixing these is a 1-mark loss.
> 4. **Missing the role of flyback diodes** — When driving an *inductive* load (solenoid, motor), the freewheeling diode across the load is mandatory; not mentioning it in a relay/injector driver question costs 1 mark.
> 5. **Ignoring the heatsink requirement** — A 0.8 W loss in a TO-220 package with $\theta_{JA} = 50^{\circ}\text{C/W}$ raises junction temperature by **40°C above ambient** — this is a *safety-relevant* calculation, often asked.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **EPCU Definition:** Microcontroller-based module that interfaces sensors and drives actuators through power semiconductors.
- **Core Modules:** ECM, TCM, BCM, ABS, BMS, EPS — each is an independent node on the CAN bus.
- **Operating Voltage:** Nominal **12 V**; logic levels **5 V or 3.3 V**.
- **PWM Duty Cycle:** $D = T_{on}/T$, with $0 \leq D \leq 1$.
- **Average Voltage:** $V_{avg} = D V_s$.
- **RMS Current:** $I_{rms} = I_{peak}\sqrt{D}$.
- **Switching Loss:** $P_{sw} = \tfrac{1}{6} V_s I_{load} (t_r + t_f) f_{sw}$ — derived from the triangular overlap of $v(t)$ and $i(t)$.
- **Conduction Loss:** $P_{cond} = I_{rms}^2 R_{DS(on)}$.
- **Total MOSFET Loss:** $P_{total} = P_{cond} + P_{sw}$.
- **CAN Bus:** 500 kbps high-speed; differential pair twisted.
- **Sensor Conditioning:** RC low-pass filter cutoff $f_c = 1/(2\pi RC)$ precedes ADC.
- **ADC Resolution:** $\Delta V = V_{ref}/2^n$; an **n = 12 bit** ADC with $V_{ref} = 5\text{ V}$ has $\Delta V = 1.22\ \text{mV}$.
- **IGBT vs MOSFET:** IGBT for high-voltage EV traction, MOSFET for low-voltage body electronics.
- **Freewheeling Diode:** Always required across inductive loads.
- **Efficiency:** PWM > 90%; Linear regulator typically 30–60%.
- **Operating Temperature:** -40°C to +125°C automotive grade.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multiplex Wiring
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT744 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Multiplex Wiring

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Multiplex Wiring

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Multiplex Wiring** (also called **Automotive Multiplexing** or **In-Vehicle Networking**) is an advanced electrical wiring and data-communication architecture used in modern automobiles in which multiple electrical signals, sensor inputs, actuator commands, and diagnostic data are transmitted **simultaneously over a single shared communication channel (bus)** using standardized digital protocols, rather than routing each individual signal through a dedicated copper wire from switch to load.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, multiplex wiring is defined as the *time-division or code-division sharing of a common transmission medium among several electronic control units (ECUs) to reduce harness complexity, mass, and cost while enabling high-speed, bidirectional data exchange.*

The technique is governed by international standards such as **ISO 11898** (CAN), **ISO 17987** (LIN), and **SAE J1939**, and forms the physical and data-link backbone of vehicle electrical/electronic (E/E) architectures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Multiplex wiring is the foundational technology enabling *body electronics*, *powertrain control*, *chassis control*, *ADAS*, and *infotainment* subsystems to communicate over a unified digital backbone. Without it, a modern car with 70+ ECUs would require several kilometres of redundant copper wiring.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine the **old telephone exchange system** in a small village. Every house had a dedicated physical wire running from its telephone to the exchange office. With 100 houses, you needed 100 separate wires — an unmanageable, expensive, and failure-prone jungle of cables.

Now imagine replacing all those wires with **a single high-speed fibre-optic cable** running past every house. Each house is given a unique *time slot* (or *code*) to "talk" on this shared cable. The exchange knows which house is sending which message because of the time slot or code.

**That is exactly what multiplex wiring does inside a car:**

| Old System (Point-to-Point Wiring) | New System (Multiplex Wiring) |
|---|---|
| Each switch → its own wire → its own bulb | Each switch → shares a common **bus** → all ECUs listen |
| 1 wire per function | 1 twisted pair for dozens of functions |
| Heavy, bulky, costly harness | Lightweight harness (up to **40% mass reduction**) |
| Slow analog signals | High-speed digital packets |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** Think of multiplex wiring as a *group chat* for the car's electronic brain — every module (ECU, sensor, switch) is a participant, and instead of shouting on a private line, it sends short, tagged digital messages on a common channel.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following **standardized parameters** are used across the automotive industry for multiplex wiring design:

- **Nominal Bus Voltage:** **2.5 V (recessive) to 3.5 V (dominant)** for CAN
- **Standard Bus Speeds:** **125 kbps, 250 kbps, 500 kbps** (CAN), **9.6 kbps – 19.2 kbps** (LIN), **up to 10 Mbps** (FlexRay), **up to 1 Gbps** (Automotive Ethernet)
- **Bus Topology:** Linear (CAN), Single-wire (LIN), Star/Mixed (FlexRay), Ring (MOST)
- **Maximum Bus Length:** **40 m at 1 Mbps** for CAN (decreases as speed increases)
- **Harness Weight Saving:** Typically **20–40%** compared to conventional wiring
- **Operating Temperature:** **−40 °C to +125 °C** (automotive Grade 1)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A single modern luxury vehicle contains approximately **1.5 km of wiring harness with 1500+ terminals**. Multiplexing can shrink this to roughly **0.9 km with fewer terminals**, dramatically improving assembly time and reliability.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

A multiplex wiring system operates on the principle of **shared medium communication**. Let us break down its operational logic step-by-step:

#### Step 1 — Signal Conversion
- Each physical input (e.g., headlamp switch, brake pedal sensor, throttle position) is connected to a local **Electronic Control Unit (ECU)** or a **smart sensor node**.
- The ECU converts the analog/physical input into a **digital data packet** with a unique identifier (message ID).

#### Step 2 — Bus Access Arbitration
- All nodes share the same physical medium (typically a **twisted pair**).
- When two nodes attempt to transmit simultaneously, an **arbitration mechanism** resolves the conflict.
- For CAN, this is done by **non-destructive bitwise arbitration** — the message with the **lowest ID wins**.

#### Step 3 — Message Transmission
- The winning message is broadcast on the bus.
- **All other nodes receive the message simultaneously**, but only the node(s) that need the data act on it. Others ignore it.

#### Step 4 — Reception & Actuator Command
- The receiving ECU (e.g., the headlamp controller) decodes the packet and energizes the appropriate actuator (e.g., the headlamp relay).

#### Step 5 — Error Detection & Fault Containment
- Each node runs a **CRC (Cyclic Redundancy Check)**, **bit stuffing**, and **acknowledgement** protocol.
- Faulty nodes are automatically switched to a **bus-off state** to prevent corruption.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does CAN dominate modern cars?** It is **multi-master**, **real-time**, **robust against EMI** (because of differential signalling), and has built-in error handling — exactly what an automotive environment demands.

---

### 2.2 Types of Multiplexing Used in Vehicles

#### 2.2.1 Time Division Multiplexing (TDM)
- Each node is allotted a fixed time slot to transmit.
- **Used in:** MOST bus (audio/video), some FlexRay segments.
- **Advantage:** Deterministic timing.
- **Disadvantage:** Wasted bandwidth when a node has no data.

#### 2.2.2 Frequency Division Multiplexing (FDM)
- Each signal occupies a unique frequency band on the same medium.
- **Rarely used in modern vehicles** (legacy AM/FM antenna systems use this principle).

#### 2.2.3 Code Division Multiplexing (CDM)
- Each signal is tagged with a unique pseudo-random code.
- **Used in:** GPS signal handling; occasionally for sensor data fusion.

#### 2.2.4 Statistical / Event-Triggered Multiplexing
- Nodes transmit **only when an event occurs** (e.g., door opened, brake pressed).
- **Used in:** CAN bus for body electronics (CAN-B).

---

### 2.3 Major Automotive Multiplex Protocols

| Protocol | Full Form | Speed | Topology | Primary Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **CAN** | Controller Area Network | 125 kbps – 1 Mbps | Linear bus | Powertrain, chassis, body |
| **LIN** | Local Interconnect Network | 9.6 – 19.2 kbps | Single-wire bus | Low-speed body (mirrors, seats) |
| **FlexRay** | FlexRay Consortium | 1 – 10 Mbps | Star / linear | X-by-wire, ADAS |
| **MOST** | Media Oriented Systems Transport | 25 – 150 Mbps | Ring | Infotainment |
| **Automotive Ethernet** | 100BASE-T1 / 1000BASE-T1 | 100 Mbps – 1 Gbps | Star / point-to-point | ADAS, cameras, V2X |

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use `\vert` for absolute-value bars and `\le`, `\ge` for inequalities so that the markdown table renders cleanly.

| Symbol | Definition / Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| $f_{bit}$ | Bit frequency $= \dfrac{1}{T_{bit}}$ | Hz | $T_{bit}$ is one bit duration |
| $T_{bit}$ | Nominal bit time $= \dfrac{1}{f_{bit}}$ | seconds | CAN: $T_{bit} = 2\ \mu s$ at 500 kbps |
| $L_{max}$ | Maximum bus length $\approx \dfrac{v_{prop}}{5 \cdot f_{bit}}$ | metres | $v_{prop} \approx 2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s in twisted pair |
| $R$ | Bus bit rate capacity $= N_{msg} \cdot L_{msg} \cdot f_{rep}$ | bps | $N_{msg}$ = messages, $L_{msg}$ = length, $f_{rep}$ = repetition |
| $\eta_{bus}$ | Bus utilisation factor $= \dfrac{T_{active}}{T_{cycle}} \le 0.7$ | dimensionless | KTU rule: keep $\le$ 70% for headroom |
| $\Delta W_{harness}$ | Harness weight saving $\approx \dfrac{W_{conv} - W_{mux}}{W_{conv}} \times 100\%$ | percent | Typically 20–40% |
| $N_{ECUs}$ | Total nodes on a CAN bus $\le$ 32 (High-speed CAN) | count | Practical limit for signal integrity |
| $Z_{bus}$ | Characteristic impedance of bus $= 120\ \Omega$ | ohms | Terminating resistors required |

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Multiplex wiring is the **enabling technology** behind:

1. **Drive-by-Wire Systems:** Electronic throttle, brake-by-wire, steer-by-wire.
2. **Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS):** Radar, LiDAR, camera fusion require multi-Gbps buses (Ethernet).
3. **Diagnostics:** OBD-II port reads ECU data via CAN bus standardized under **ISO 15765**.
4. **Software-Defined Vehicles (SDV):** Over-the-air (OTA) updates flow over CAN/Ethernet.
5. **Weight & Fuel Economy:** 1 kg of wiring saved ≈ 0.005 L/100 km fuel economy improvement.

> [!NOTE]
> In high-end electric vehicles (e.g., Tesla Model S), the wiring harness uses **automotive Ethernet backbone with CAN sub-buses**, demonstrating the future direction of multiplexing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Maximum Bus Length for a Given Bit Rate

**Given:**
- Bit rate $f_{bit}$ (in bits per second)
- Signal propagation velocity $v_{prop}$ in twisted-pair copper cable

**To Derive:** Maximum permissible bus length $L_{max}$ such that signal reflections return within one bit time.

---

**Step 1 — Define propagation time per unit length**

The time taken for a signal to travel one metre of cable is:

$$t_{m} = \frac{1}{v_{prop}}$$

For a typical automotive twisted pair, $v_{prop} \approx 2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s.

---

**Step 2 — Define the round-trip propagation time**

If the bus is $L$ metres long, the signal must travel to the far end (length $L$) and the reflection must return (length $L$). Total round-trip distance is $2L$.

$$t_{RT} = \frac{2L}{v_{prop}}$$

---

**Step 3 — Apply the design rule**

For reliable communication, the round-trip time must not exceed one-fifth of the bit duration (this ensures reflections die down before the next bit is sampled):

$$t_{RT} \le \frac{T_{bit}}{5}$$

---

**Step 4 — Substitute $T_{bit} = 1/f_{bit}$**

Substituting:

$$\frac{2L_{max}}{v_{prop}} = \frac{1}{5 \cdot f_{bit}}$$

Solving for $L_{max}$:

$$L_{max} = \frac{v_{prop}}{10 \cdot f_{bit}}$$

---

**Step 5 — Numerical Verification (CAN at 500 kbps)**

Plug in $v_{prop} = 2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s and $f_{bit} = 500 \times 10^{3}$ bps:

$$L_{max} = \frac{2 \times 10^{8}}{10 \times 500 \times 10^{3}}$$

$$L_{max} = \frac{2 \times 10^{8}}{5 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$L_{max} = 40\ \text{metres}$$

This matches the **ISO 11898 standard** for CAN at 500 kbps — a perfect cross-check. 

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Bus Utilisation Factor

**Given:**
- Number of nodes $n$
- Average message length $L_{msg}$ in bits
- Message repetition rate $f_{rep}$ in Hz

**To Find:** Bus utilisation factor $\eta_{bus}$.

---

**Step 1 — Calculate aggregate bandwidth demand**

The total bits per second demanded by all nodes:

$$R_{demand} = n \cdot L_{msg} \cdot f_{rep}$$

---

**Step 2 — Compare with available bus capacity**

The bus can carry $f_{bit}$ bits per second. The utilisation factor is:

$$\eta_{bus} = \frac{R_{demand}}{f_{bit}} = \frac{n \cdot L_{msg} \cdot f_{rep}}{f_{bit}}$$

---

**Step 3 — Apply the KTU design rule**

To ensure real-time response and error-recovery headroom, the bus should never run above 70% capacity:

$$\eta_{bus} \le 0.7$$

Therefore the maximum supported node load is:

$$n_{max} = \frac{0.7 \cdot f_{bit}}{L_{msg} \cdot f_{rep}}$$

---

**Step 4 — Numerical Example**

Consider a CAN bus at 500 kbps carrying 8-byte messages (each 64 bits + overhead ≈ 130 bits) at 10 Hz each:

$$n_{max} = \frac{0.7 \times 500\,000}{130 \times 10}$$

$$n_{max} = \frac{350\,000}{1300}$$

$$n_{max} \approx 269\ \text{nodes}$$

This is the *theoretical* upper bound; in practice, the ISO 11898 limit of 32 nodes per CAN segment is imposed by **electrical loading**, not bandwidth.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Conventional vs Multiplex Wiring

| Parameter | Conventional Wiring | Multiplex Wiring | Improvement Ratio |
|---|---|---|---|
| Total harness mass | $\sim$ 60 kg | $\sim$ 36 kg | 1.67$\times$ lighter |
| Number of wires (typical sedan) | $\sim$ 1500 | $\sim$ 700 | 2.14$\times$ fewer |
| Connector pin count | $\sim$ 2000 | $\sim$ 800 | 2.5$\times$ fewer |
| Assembly time | 100% baseline | $\sim$ 60% baseline | 1.67$\times$ faster |
| Data rate per channel | Limited to switch rating | Up to 1 Gbps | $>10^{6}\times$ faster |
| Diagnostic capability | None | Full OBD-II | New capability |
| Scalability | Poor (new wire per feature) | Excellent (software change) | Qualitative |

---

### 3.4 Worked Example — Harness Weight Saving Calculation

**Given:**
- Conventional harness weight $W_{conv} = 55$ kg
- Multiplex harness weight $W_{mux} = 33$ kg

**Find:** Percentage weight saving.

Applying the formula:

$$\Delta W_{harness} = \frac{W_{conv} - W_{mux}}{W_{conv}} \times 100\%$$

$$\Delta W_{harness} = \frac{55 - 33}{55} \times 100\%$$

$$\Delta W_{harness} = \frac{22}{55} \times 100\%$$

$$\Delta W_{harness} = 0.40 \times 100\% = 40\%$$

Therefore, the multiplex harness achieves a **40% weight reduction**, which directly translates to better fuel efficiency and lower emissions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Multiplexed Vehicle

The diagram below illustrates how various automotive subsystems connect through a centralized multiplex bus architecture (CAN-FD backbone with sub-buses).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph POWERTRAIN_SUB
        ECU_EMS[Engine ECU]
        ECU_TCU[Transmission ECU]
        ECU_BMS[Battery ECU]
    end

    subgraph CHASSIS_SUB
        ECU_ABS[ABS ECU]
        ECU_EPS[Power Steering ECU]
    end

    subgraph BODY_SUB
        ECU_BCM[Body Control Module]
        ECU_HVAC[HVAC ECU]
        ECU_IVI[Infotainment ECU]
    end

    subgraph ADAS_SUB
        ECU_CAM[Front Camera ECU]
        ECU_RADAR[Radar ECU]
    end

    BUS_MAIN[CAN FD BACKBONE BUS - 2 Mbps]
    BUS_BODY[CAN BODY BUS - 500 kbps]
    BUS_LIN[LIN SUB BUS - 19.2 kbps]

    ECU_EMS --- BUS_MAIN
    ECU_TCU --- BUS_MAIN
    ECU_BMS --- BUS_MAIN
    ECU_ABS --- BUS_MAIN
    ECU_EPS --- BUS_MAIN
    ECU_CAM --- BUS_MAIN
    ECU_RADAR --- BUS_MAIN

    BUS_MAIN --- GATEWAY[Central Gateway ECU]
    GATEWAY --- BUS_BODY
    BUS_BODY --- ECU_BCM
    BUS_BODY --- ECU_HVAC
    BUS_BODY --- ECU_IVI

    ECU_BCM --- BUS_LIN
    BUS_LIN --- SW_DOOR[Door Switch Node]
    BUS_LIN --- SW_MIRROR[Mirror Switch Node]
    BUS_LIN --- SW_SEAT[Seat Switch Node]

    DIAG[OBD II Diagnostic Port] --- GATEWAY
```

---

### 4.2 Signal Transmission Sequence (CAN Frame Flow)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant SW as Switch / Sensor
    participant ECU_S as Source ECU
    participant BUS as CAN Bus
    participant ECU_R as Receiver ECU
    participant ACT as Actuator

    SW->>ECU_S: Press event / analog reading
    ECU_S->>ECU_S: ADC conversion and ID tagging
    ECU_S->>BUS: Transmit CAN frame with message ID 0x123
    BUS->>ECU_R: Broadcast to all nodes
    ECU_R->>ECU_R: Filter and accept ID 0x123
    ECU_R->>ACT: Drive actuator ON
    ACT-->>ECU_R: Status feedback
    ECU_R->>BUS: Send acknowledgement frame
    BUS-->>ECU_S: Confirms reception
```

---

### 4.3 Conventional vs Multiplex Wiring Topology Comparison

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph CONV[Conventional Wiring]
        S1[Headlamp Switch] -->|Wire 1| B1[Headlamp Bulb]
        S2[Brake Switch] -->|Wire 2| B2[Brake Bulb]
        S3[Horn Switch] -->|Wire 3| B3[Horn]
    end

    subgraph MUX[Multiplex Wiring]
        SM[Smart Switch Module] -->|Shared Bus| ECU[Central ECU]
        ECU -->|Command Frame| BL[Headlamp Driver]
        ECU -->|Command Frame| BM[Brake Lamp Driver]
        ECU -->|Command Frame| BH[Horn Driver]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** In conventional wiring, every switch directly drives its load through a dedicated wire. In multiplex wiring, switches become *input nodes* that send digital messages; the central ECU processes these and commands the appropriate driver circuits — dramatically reducing wire count.

---

### 4.4 Arbitration Process in CAN Bus

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N1[Node A - ID 0x100] -->|Transmits| BUS_ARB[CAN Bus Arbitration Field]
    N2[Node B - ID 0x080] -->|Transmits| BUS_ARB
    BUS_ARB -->|Lowest ID wins| WIN[Node B Wins - Continues Transmission]
    BUS_ARB -->|Loses arbitration| LOSE[Node A - Backs off and Retries Later]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Q1.** Define **multiplex wiring**. List any **two advantages** it offers over conventional point-to-point wiring in automobiles.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Multiplex wiring is a data-communication technique in which multiple electrical signals share a common transmission medium (bus) using standardized digital protocols such as CAN, LIN, or FlexRay, instead of using individual dedicated wires.

**Two advantages:**
1. **Reduction in harness mass and volume** — typically 20–40% weight saving, which improves fuel economy.
2. **Simplified diagnostics and servicing** — fault codes can be read through a single OBD-II port, reducing service time.

*(Marks: Definition 1 + Any two advantages 1 + 1 = 3 marks)*

---

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Q2.** Name the **four major automotive multiplex protocols** and state the typical data-rate range for each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **CAN (Controller Area Network):** 125 kbps – 1 Mbps
2. **LIN (Local Interconnect Network):** 9.6 – 19.2 kbps
3. **FlexRay:** 1 – 10 Mbps
4. **MOST (Media Oriented Systems Transport):** 25 – 150 Mbps

*(Marks: 0.5 × 4 protocol names + 0.5 × 4 speeds ≈ 3 marks; allocate proportionally)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Question Choice A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 1]**

**Q. (a)** Explain the **working principle of CAN-based multiplex wiring** with a neat block diagram. Discuss **message arbitration** and **error handling** in detail. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A CAN bus operates at **500 kbps** with a propagation velocity of $2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s. Calculate the **maximum bus length** and the **bus utilisation factor** if 5 nodes transmit 8-byte messages (130 bits including overhead) at 50 Hz each. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q (a) — Working Principle of CAN Multiplex Wiring

**Step 1 — Block Diagram Explanation (3 marks)**

The CAN system consists of:

- **Multiple ECUs** (Engine, ABS, BCM, etc.) acting as nodes
- **A two-wire twisted pair bus** (CAN-H and CAN-L)
- **120 Ω terminating resistors** at both ends
- **Transceivers** converting digital logic to differential bus voltages

**Step 2 — Frame Structure (2 marks)**

A standard CAN frame contains: SOF, Arbitration Field (11-bit ID), Control Field, Data Field (0–8 bytes), CRC Field, ACK Field, EOF.

**Step 3 — Message Arbitration (1 mark)**

CAN uses **non-destructive bitwise arbitration**. While transmitting, each node monitors the bus. If a recessive bit (1) it sends is overwritten by a dominant bit (0) from another node, it loses arbitration and immediately backs off. The node with the **lowest ID wins** without any data loss.

**Step 4 — Error Handling (1 mark)**

CAN provides five error-detection mechanisms:
1. **Bit Monitoring** — transmitter checks bus state matches what it sent.
2. **Bit Stuffing Rule** — five consecutive identical bits are followed by a stuff bit.
3. **CRC Check** — receiver verifies frame integrity.
4. **Form Check** — fixed-format fields are validated.
5. **Acknowledgement** — receivers confirm successful reception.

Faulty nodes increment an **error counter** and are eventually switched to **bus-off** state.

---

#### Model Solution for Q (b) — Numerical Calculation

**Step 1 — Maximum Bus Length**

Using the derived formula:

$$L_{max} = \frac{v_{prop}}{10 \cdot f_{bit}}$$

$$L_{max} = \frac{2 \times 10^{8}}{10 \times 500 \times 10^{3}}$$

$$L_{max} = 40\ \text{metres}$$

**Step 2 — Bus Utilisation Factor**

Aggregate demand:

$$R_{demand} = n \cdot L_{msg} \cdot f_{rep}$$

$$R_{demand} = 5 \times 130 \times 50$$

$$R_{demand} = 32\,500\ \text{bits per second}$$

Utilisation factor:

$$\eta_{bus} = \frac{R_{demand}}{f_{bit}}$$

$$\eta_{bus} = \frac{32\,500}{500\,000}$$

$$\eta_{bus} = 0.065 = 6.5\%$$

**Conclusion:** The bus is operating well within the **safe limit of 70%**, leaving ample headroom for additional nodes and error recovery. 

---

### 5.3 Part B Question Choice B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module 1]**

**Q. (a)** With the help of a comparative table, differentiate between **CAN, LIN, FlexRay, and MOST** protocols. Mention typical **automotive applications** of each. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A modern sedan replaces its conventional 55 kg wiring harness with a multiplex wiring harness. If the new harness weighs **33 kg**, calculate:
   (i) the **percentage weight saving**,
   (ii) the **fuel economy improvement** assuming 1 kg saved improves mileage by 0.005 L/100 km, and the car's baseline mileage is 8.0 L/100 km. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q (a) — Comparison Table (7 marks)

| Feature | CAN | LIN | FlexRay | MOST |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Data rate | 125 kbps – 1 Mbps | 9.6 – 19.2 kbps | 1 – 10 Mbps | 25 – 150 Mbps |
| Topology | Linear bus | Single-wire bus | Star / linear | Ring |
| Wires | 2 (twisted pair) | 1 | 2 or 4 | 1 (fibre optic) |
| Cost | Medium | Very low | High | High |
| Determinism | Event-triggered | Event-triggered | Time-triggered | Streaming |
| Application | Powertrain, body | Mirrors, seats, sensors | X-by-wire, ADAS | Audio/video |

*(Allocate ~1 mark per row + 1 mark for overall coherence)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q (b) — Numerical Calculation

**(i) Percentage Weight Saving (3 marks)**

$$\Delta W = \frac{55 - 33}{55} \times 100\% = \frac{22}{55} \times 100\% = 40\%$$

**(ii) Fuel Economy Improvement (4 marks)**

Mass reduction:

$$\Delta m = 55 - 33 = 22\ \text{kg}$$

Fuel saved per 100 km:

$$\Delta F = 22 \times 0.005 = 0.110\ \text{L per 100 km}$$

New mileage:

$$F_{new} = 8.000 - 0.110 = 7.890\ \text{L per 100 km}$$

Percentage improvement:

$$\Delta F_{\%} = \frac{0.110}{8.000} \times 100\% = 1.375\%$$

**Conclusion:** A 40% wiring harness reduction yields a 1.375% improvement in fuel economy — a non-trivial gain for a simple architectural change.

---

### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "multiplexing" with "multimeter testing"** — these are entirely different concepts.
> 2. **Forgetting units** in numerical answers — examiners deduct 0.5 mark per missing unit.
> 3. **Writing only advantages without a diagram** in 7-mark questions — KTU requires *both* explanation *and* a labelled block diagram.
> 4. **Using incorrect formula for bus length** — the formula $L_{max} = v_{prop} / (10 \cdot f_{bit})$ is for *round-trip*; using $5 \cdot f_{bit}$ is a frequent error.
> 5. **Not mentioning terminating resistors** (120 Ω) in CAN diagrams — this is a favourite examiner trap.
> 6. **Confusing CAN with LIN** — CAN uses *two* wires; LIN uses *one* wire. Mixing them up costs full marks.

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Multiplex wiring** = multiple signals over a single shared bus using digital protocols.
- **Main protocols:** CAN (dominant), LIN (low-cost), FlexRay (high-speed), MOST (multimedia), Automotive Ethernet (future).
- **CAN bus standard:** ISO 11898; differential signalling; 120 Ω termination.
- **Key speeds:** LIN ≈ 20 kbps; CAN ≤ 1 Mbps; FlexRay ≤ 10 Mbps; MOST ≤ 150 Mbps; Ethernet ≤ 1 Gbps.
- **Maximum bus length formula:** $L_{max} = v_{prop} / (10 \cdot f_{bit})$ — for 500 kbps CAN, $L_{max} = 40$ m.
- **Bus utilisation rule:** $\eta_{bus} \le 0.7$ (70%) to allow error-recovery headroom.
- **Weight saving:** typically **20–40%** over conventional harnesses.
- **Each kg saved** ≈ **0.005 L/100 km** fuel economy improvement.
- **Arbitration:** lowest CAN message ID wins; non-destructive, real-time.
- **Error detection:** CRC, bit stuffing, bit monitoring, form check, acknowledgement.
- **Topology types:** Linear (CAN), Star (FlexRay), Ring (MOST), Single-wire (LIN).
- **KTU exam focus:** block diagrams + numericals on bus length and utilisation are the most asked questions.
- **Always state units** in the final answer line.
- **Diagnostic access:** OBD-II port reads all CAN/ECU data per ISO 15765.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Regenerative Braking (Description only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT744 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Regenerative Braking (Description only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Regenerative Braking** is an energy recovery mechanism employed in hybrid electric vehicles (HEVs), plug-in hybrid electric vehicles (PHEVs), battery electric vehicles (BEVs), and modern fuel-cell electric vehicles (FCEVs) in which the vehicle's electric traction motor is intentionally operated as a generator during deceleration or braking events. The kinetic energy of the moving vehicle, which would otherwise be dissipated as waste heat through conventional friction brakes, is converted into electrical energy and fed back into the high-voltage traction battery or super-capacitor bank for later reuse.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Requirement):**
> "Regenerative braking is the conversion of the vehicle's kinetic energy into usable electrical energy by reversing the role of the traction motor from a motor to a generator during braking, thereby extending the driving range and reducing thermal wear on friction brake components."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Imagine you are riding a bicycle fitted with a **dynamo (a small electrical generator) integrated into the front wheel hub**. As you roll down a hill, instead of merely squeezing the hand brakes (which creates friction and slows you down by converting motion into useless heat), you can let the dynamo engage. The wheels turn the dynamo, the dynamo pushes current back into a small battery, and *the act of generating this current itself creates a resisting torque that slows the bicycle down*.
