You are essentially **borrowing speed to make electricity**, and then spending that electricity later when you pedal up the next hill.

A regenerative braking system in an electric car works on exactly this principle, but on a much larger and more controllable scale:

- The electric motor that drove the wheels is **reversed** to act as a generator.
- The driver's accelerator pedal release or brake pedal press signals the **Vehicle Control Unit (VCU)** to engage regen mode.
- The mechanical resistance created by generating electricity is what **slows the vehicle**.
- The electricity produced is **stored** instead of wasted.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

The following key performance indicators govern regenerative braking design and are mandatory for KTU numerical questions:

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Energy Recovery Efficiency | $\eta_{regen}$ | **0.6 – 0.8 (60–80 %)** | Fraction of kinetic energy recovered |
| Maximum Regen Braking Force | $F_{regen}$ | **0.3 – 0.4 g** | Deceleration limit without ABS interference |
| Traction Battery Voltage | $V_{batt}$ | **400 V / 800 V** | Modern EV architectures |
| State of Charge (SOC) Operating Window | $SOC$ | **20 % – 80 %** | Avoids battery degradation |
| Motor-Generator Peak Power | $P_{mg}$ | **80 – 250 kW** | Determines regen ceiling |

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants to Remember:**
> - Acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.81 \, m/s^{2}$
> - Gravitational acceleration (used in road-load calculations): $g \approx 9.81 \, m/s^{2}$
> - Conversion: $1 \, kWh = 3.6 \times 10^{6} \, J$

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — The Five Sequential Stages

Regenerative braking does not occur in a single action; it is a coordinated sequence executed by the vehicle's powertrain control architecture.

### Stage 1 — Driver Intention Detection
The accelerator pedal position sensor (APPS) and brake pedal position sensor (BPPS) continuously stream signals to the **Vehicle Control Unit (VCU)**. The VCU interprets the *rate of pedal release* and *brake pedal travel* as a deceleration request.

### Stage 2 — Mode Arbitration (Blended Braking Decision)
Modern vehicles rarely use 100 % regenerative braking because of regulatory safety requirements (the brake pedal must always feel "mechanical"). The VCU therefore performs **blended braking arbitration**, allocating braking torque between the electric regen system and the hydraulic friction system. A typical split for a mid-speed stop is **60–70 % regen** and **30–40 % friction**.

### Stage 3 — Motor Torque Reversal
The traction motor (usually a Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor — PMSM, or an Induction Motor — IM) is commanded by the **Motor Control Unit (MCU)** to operate in the **generator quadrant** of its torque-speed map. The MCU sends a negative torque request to the inverter.

### Stage 4 — Electromechanical Energy Conversion
Spinning rotor magnets induce an EMF in the stator windings (Faraday's Law). This induced EMF, after three-phase rectification by the inverter, becomes DC electrical power. The very act of current flowing against this induced EMF produces a **counter-torque** on the rotor that resists rotation — this counter-torque is what decelerates the vehicle.

### Stage 5 — Energy Storage & SOC Management
The DC power is fed into the high-voltage bus. The **Battery Management System (BMS)** continuously monitors State of Charge (SOC), cell temperature, and C-rate. If SOC exceeds the safe upper threshold (typically 80–85 %), regenerative braking is gracefully ramped down and friction braking takes over to prevent battery damage.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Stage

| Stage | Core "Why" | Engineering Justification |
|---|---|---|
| 1 — Detection | Driver wants to slow down | Translates human intent into electrical signals |
| 2 — Arbitration | Regen alone cannot perform emergency stops | Ensures regulatory compliance and safety |
| 3 — Torque Reversal | Motor = Generator by symmetry of construction | Avoids additional hardware cost |
| 4 — Conversion | Faraday's Law of Induction | Fundamental physics — no escape from this law |
| 5 — Storage | Energy must not be wasted or damage battery | Maximises range, preserves battery longevity |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Vehicle Kinetic Energy | $KE = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}$ | $m$ = mass (kg), $v$ = velocity (m/s), $KE$ in Joules | Total energy available for recovery |
| 2 | Recoverable Energy | $E_{regen} = \eta_{regen} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} m (v_{i}^{2} - v_{f}^{2})$ | $\eta_{regen}$ = dimensionless | Energy actually stored in battery |
| 3 | Instantaneous Regen Power | $P_{regen}(t) = F_{regen}(t) \cdot v(t)$ | $F$ in N, $v$ in m/s, $P$ in W | Peak power design for inverter |
| 4 | Regen Deceleration | $a_{regen} = \dfrac{F_{regen}}{m} = \dfrac{T_{mg} \cdot \eta_{tr}}{m \cdot r_{w}}$ | $T_{mg}$ = motor torque (Nm), $\eta_{tr}$ = transmission eff., $r_{w}$ = wheel radius (m) | Braking feel tuning |
| 5 | Motor Counter-EMF | $E = K_{e} \cdot \omega_{m}$ | $K_{e}$ = EMF constant (V·s/rad), $\omega_{m}$ = motor angular speed (rad/s) | Voltage at battery terminals |
| 6 | Wheel Angular Speed | $\omega_{w} = \dfrac{v}{r_{w}}$ | $v$ = vehicle speed, $r_{w}$ = wheel radius | Couples vehicle motion to motor shaft |
| 7 | Battery Charging Power | $P_{batt} = V_{bus} \cdot I_{chg} = \eta_{inv} \cdot T_{mg} \cdot \omega_{m}$ | $\eta_{inv}$ = inverter efficiency | Energy accounting |
| 8 | Equivalent Braking Force | $F_{eq} = F_{regen} + F_{friction}$ | Both in Newtons | Braking system sizing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pipe Symbol Rule Reminder:** All absolute-value notations have been deliberately rendered as $\vert x \vert$ (using `\vert`) inside the table above. **Never use the bare keyboard pipe `|` for absolute value** in KTU answer scripts — it breaks the markdown table and may cause grading system parsing errors in the digital valuation portal.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of Regenerative Braking

Regenerative braking is **not merely a fuel-economy gimmick**. Its engineering utility extends across four critical domains:

1. **Range Extension:** A typical EV traveling in stop-and-go urban traffic recovers **15–25 %** of its expended energy, directly translating to additional kilometres of range.
2. **Brake Pad Longevity:** Because friction brakes are used far less frequently, brake pads on EVs can last **200,000 km or more**, versus 40,000–60,000 km on an internal combustion vehicle.
3. **Thermal Management:** Reduced friction-brake usage means reduced brake fade on long downhill descents — critical for mountainous terrain.
4. **Energy Efficiency Standards Compliance:** Global regulations (CAFE in the USA, WLTP in Europe, and the Bharat Stage Corporate Average Fuel Economy norms in India) credit manufacturers for regenerative braking in efficiency certifications.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Recoverable Energy During a Braking Event from $v_i$ to $v_f$

We derive the closed-form expression for the total electrical energy recovered when a vehicle decelerates from an initial speed $v_i$ to a final speed $v_f$ under regenerative braking only.

### Step 1: Define the Instantaneous Kinetic Energy

The instantaneous kinetic energy of the vehicle at any moment in time is given by Newton's classical formula:

$$KE(t) = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot m \cdot v(t)^{2}$$

**Logic:** Kinetic energy is proportional to mass and the square of velocity. Heavier vehicles and faster vehicles store disproportionately more energy in their motion.

### Step 2: Express the Change in Kinetic Energy

The total mechanical energy available for conversion is the difference between initial and final kinetic energy:

$$\Delta KE = KE_{i} - KE_{f} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot m \cdot v_{i}^{2} - \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot m \cdot v_{f}^{2}$$

**Logic:** Energy is conserved in the overall vehicle system; whatever kinetic energy is "lost" by the vehicle must appear somewhere — either as electrical energy in the battery or as heat in the friction brakes.

### Step 3: Apply the Regenerative Efficiency Factor

Not all of the kinetic energy reduction is recoverable. Losses occur in the motor (copper + iron), in the power electronics (inverter switching + conduction), and in the battery (internal resistance + coulombic inefficiency). We aggregate these into a single regenerative efficiency factor $\eta_{regen}$:

$$E_{regen} = \eta_{regen} \cdot \Delta KE$$

### Step 4: Substitute and Simplify

Substituting Step 2 into Step 3 yields the final closed-form expression:

$$E_{regen} = \eta_{regen} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot m \cdot \bigl( v_{i}^{2} - v_{f}^{2} \bigr)$$

**Logic:** The energy stored in the battery at the end of the braking event is proportional to the mass of the vehicle, the square of the speed change, and the system efficiency. This is the *master formula* for every regenerative-braking numerical problem on KTU exams.

## 3.2 Derivation II — Peak Regen Power as a Function of Deceleration

We now derive the instantaneous electrical power being generated at any moment during a braking event.

### Step 1: State Newton's Second Law for the Vehicle

The braking force acting on the vehicle produces a deceleration $a$ according to:

$$F_{regen} = m \cdot a_{regen}$$

**Logic:** A heavier vehicle requires more braking force to achieve the same deceleration as a lighter vehicle.

### Step 2: Identify the Mechanical Power Being Extracted

Mechanical power is force times velocity. Because the regen system is extracting kinetic energy from the moving mass, the mechanical power at the wheel contact patch is:

$$P_{mech}(t) = F_{regen}(t) \cdot v(t)$$

**Logic:** At low speeds, even a large braking force extracts little power; at high speeds, even modest forces extract huge power. Power is a *rate* — it scales with both force and speed.

### Step 3: Apply System Efficiency to Obtain Electrical Power

Only a fraction of this mechanical power reaches the battery as usable electrical power:

$$P_{regen}(t) = \eta_{regen} \cdot F_{regen}(t) \cdot v(t)$$

### Step 4: Express in Terms of Deceleration

Substituting $F_{regen} = m \cdot a_{regen}$:

$$\boxed{P_{regen}(t) = \eta_{regen} \cdot m \cdot a_{regen} \cdot v(t)}$$

**Logic:** This compact equation allows engineers to size the inverter and battery C-rate from a simple drive-cycle deceleration profile.

## 3.3 Derivation III — Motor Counter-Torque as the Active Braking Agent

We now show mathematically why the vehicle actually slows down — i.e., derive the braking torque at the wheel from the motor's electromagnetic behaviour.

### Step 1: Relate Motor Torque to Current

For a Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor, electromagnetic torque is:

$$T_{mg} = \tfrac{3}{2} \cdot p \cdot \Psi_{m} \cdot I_{q}$$

where $p$ = number of pole pairs, $\Psi_{m}$ = permanent-magnet flux linkage, $I_{q}$ = quadrature-axis current.

**Logic:** Torque is directly proportional to the q-axis current in the d-q rotating reference frame.

### Step 2: Relate Battery Current to Motor Current via the Inverter

The three-phase motor currents are reconstructed by the inverter from the DC bus current. In the generator (regen) mode, the DC current flows *out of* the inverter into the battery:

$$I_{batt} = \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \cdot \eta_{inv} \cdot m_{a} \cdot I_{q}$$

**Logic:** The factor $\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$ emerges from the Park-Clarke transformation used in field-oriented control; $m_{a}$ is the modulation index.

### Step 3: Reflect Motor Torque to the Wheel Through the Transmission

The transmission multiplies torque and divides speed by the gear ratio $N_{g}$:

$$T_{wheel} = N_{g} \cdot \eta_{tr} \cdot T_{mg}$$

### Step 4: Convert Wheel Torque to Braking Force

The braking force at the road-tire interface is:

$$F_{regen} = \dfrac{T_{wheel}}{r_{w}} = \dfrac{N_{g} \cdot \eta_{tr} \cdot T_{mg}}{r_{w}}$$

**Logic:** A smaller wheel radius and a larger gear ratio both increase the achievable braking force for the same motor torque — a key design trade-off in sports EVs.

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Exam Pattern)

**Problem:** A 1500 kg electric vehicle decelerates from **80 km/h to 20 km/h** under regenerative braking alone. If the regenerative efficiency is **0.70**, calculate:
(a) The total recoverable energy in kWh.
(b) The peak instantaneous regen power if the deceleration is constant at **0.25 g**.

### Solution:

#### Part (a) — Recoverable Energy

Convert speeds to SI units:

$$v_{i} = 80 \, km/h = 80 \cdot \tfrac{1000}{3600} = 22.22 \, m/s$$

$$v_{f} = 20 \, km/h = 20 \cdot \tfrac{1000}{3600} = 5.56 \, m/s$$

Apply the master formula derived in Section 3.1:

$$E_{regen} = 0.70 \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 1500 \cdot (22.22^{2} - 5.56^{2})$$

$$E_{regen} = 0.70 \cdot 750 \cdot (493.83 - 30.91)$$

$$E_{regen} = 0.70 \cdot 750 \cdot 462.92$$

$$E_{regen} = 243033 \, J$$

Convert to kWh:

$$E_{regen} = \dfrac{243033}{3.6 \times 10^{6}} \approx 0.0675 \, kWh$$

#### Part (b) — Peak Instantaneous Power

At the **beginning** of braking, $v$ is maximum and equals $22.22 \, m/s$. Compute braking force:

$$F_{regen} = m \cdot a = 1500 \cdot (0.25 \cdot 9.81) = 1500 \cdot 2.4525 = 3678.75 \, N$$

Apply the power equation from Section 3.2:

$$P_{regen} = 0.70 \cdot 3678.75 \cdot 22.22 \approx 57228 \, W \approx 57.2 \, kW$$

**Answer:** **(a) ≈ 0.0675 kWh (243 kJ), (b) ≈ 57.2 kW.**

---

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Regenerative Braking — Functional Architecture Flow

The following block-level diagram traces the signal and energy flow through every major subsystem involved in a regenerative braking event. Because physical drawings of an automotive powertrain cannot be rendered in plain text, the **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** below maps every interaction with engineering precision.

```mermaid
graph TD
    drvA[Driver Intent Input]
    drvB[Accelerator and Brake Pedal Sensors]
    drvC[Vehicle Control Unit VCU]
    drvD[Brake Mode Arbitration Logic]
    drvE[Motor Control Unit MCU]
    drvF[Power Inverter DC to AC and AC to DC]
    drvG[Traction Motor operating as Generator]
    drvH[Wheels and Tyres]
    drvI[High Voltage Battery Pack]
    drvJ[Battery Management System BMS]
    drvK[SOC and Thermal Monitoring]
    drvL[Hydraulic Friction Brake System]

    drvA --> drvB
    drvB --> drvC
    drvC --> drvD
    drvD --> drvE
    drvE --> drvF
    drvF --> drvG
    drvG --> drvH
    drvH --> drvG
    drvG --> drvF
    drvF --> drvI
    drvI --> drvJ
    drvJ --> drvK
    drvK --> drvD
    drvD --> drvL
```

**Diagram Interpretation Guide:**

| Block ID | Subsystem | Role in Regen Braking |
|---|---|---|
| drvA | Driver | Provides deceleration request via pedal inputs |
| drvB | Pedal Sensors | Convert pedal position into electrical signals |
| drvC | VCU | Master decision-maker for the entire vehicle |
| drvD | Arbitration Logic | Splits braking demand between regen and friction |
| drvE | MCU | Commands motor torque and direction |
| drvF | Power Inverter | Bidirectional AC/DC conversion |
| drvG | Traction Motor / Generator | The energy conversion device |
| drvH | Wheels / Tyres | Mechanical interface with the road |
| drvI | HV Battery | Stores recovered electrical energy |
| drvJ | BMS | Protects battery from over-charge and overheating |
| drvK | SOC Monitor | Closes the feedback loop to drvD |
| drvL | Friction Brakes | Provides supplementary braking force |

## 4.2 Energy Conversion Chain — Sankey-Style Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    eA[Vehicle Kinetic Energy]
    eB[Mechanical Power at Wheels]
    eC[Electromagnetic Power in Motor]
    eD[Electrical Power at Inverter Output]
    eE[DC Power at Battery Terminals]
    eF[Chemical Energy Stored in Battery]
    eG[Waste Heat in Friction Brakes]

    eA --> eB
    eB --> eC
    eC --> eD
    eD --> eE
    eE --> eF
    eB --> eG
```

**Energy Path Explanation:**

- **eA → eB:** The vehicle's bulk kinetic energy is delivered as mechanical power at the tyre-road contact patch.
- **eB → eC:** This mechanical power is transmitted through the drivetrain into the motor's rotor.
- **eC → eD:** The spinning rotor (acting as a generator) converts mechanical power into three-phase AC electrical power.
- **eD → eE:** The inverter rectifies the AC into DC suitable for the battery.
- **eE → eF:** The battery's electrochemistry stores this DC power as chemical potential energy.
- **eB → eG:** Any portion of braking force not handled by regen is dissipated as heat in the friction brakes — this is the *lost* branch.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Visual Aid Tip:** When asked to draw a regenerative braking block diagram in the exam, always label *both* the signal flow (control arrows) and the energy flow (power arrows). Examiners award extra marks for explicitly showing **bidirectional** arrows between the motor and inverter, since this is the key feature distinguishing regen from conventional motoring.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Define regenerative braking. Mention any two advantages of using regenerative braking in modern electric vehicles.**

**Model Answer:**
Regenerative braking is an energy recovery mechanism in which the electric traction motor of an EV is operated as a generator during deceleration or braking, thereby converting the vehicle's kinetic energy into electrical energy that is fed back into the traction battery for later use.

**Two Advantages:**
1. **Improved Energy Efficiency / Extended Range:** Recovered kinetic energy extends the vehicle's driving range by 15–25 % in typical urban driving cycles.
2. **Reduced Brake Wear:** Friction brake components (pads, discs, drums) experience significantly less wear, leading to lower maintenance costs and longer service intervals.

*(Alternative valid advantages: reduced thermal load, lower emissions in ICE hybrids, compliance with CAFE/WLTP efficiency norms.)*

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Why is regenerative braking alone insufficient for emergency braking in EVs? What supplementary system is employed?**

**Model Answer:**
Regenerative braking is insufficient for emergency braking for three primary reasons:

1. **Power Limitation:** The maximum regen power is bounded by the motor's rated power and the battery's safe charging C-rate. During emergency braking, the required deceleration (≈ 1 g) demands much more power than the regen system can deliver.
2. **Speed Limitations:** At very low speeds (below ~5–8 km/h), the induced EMF in the motor becomes too low to push current into the battery, so regen braking fades out near a complete stop.
3. **Battery SOC Saturation:** If the battery is near full charge, the BMS prohibits further charging, disabling regen braking entirely.

**Supplementary System:** A **hydraulic (or electro-mechanical) friction brake system** is used in parallel, with the VCU performing *blended braking arbitration* to combine both systems seamlessly.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: (a)** Explain the operating principle of regenerative braking in a battery electric vehicle with the help of a functional block diagram. Describe the role of the VCU, inverter, traction motor, and BMS in this process. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 1800 kg EV decelerates uniformly from 90 km/h to a complete stop under regenerative braking. If the average regenerative efficiency is 0.72, calculate:
  (i) The total energy recovered in kWh.
  (ii) The range extension in km, assuming the vehicle consumes 150 Wh/km on average. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Operating Principle:**

The regenerative braking process in a BEV operates in five coordinated stages:

1. **Driver Intent Capture:** When the driver releases the accelerator or presses the brake, the pedal sensors send position signals to the **Vehicle Control Unit (VCU)**.

2. **Brake Mode Arbitration:** The VCU evaluates the deceleration request, current **State of Charge (SOC)**, vehicle speed, and battery temperature. It then computes a torque-split ratio between the regenerative and friction brake systems.

3. **Motor Command Dispatch:** The VCU sends a **negative torque request** to the **Motor Control Unit (MCU)**, which commands the **Power Inverter** to operate in regenerative mode.

4. **Electromechanical Conversion:** The traction motor — now functioning as a generator — converts the rotational mechanical energy of the drivetrain into three-phase AC electrical energy via **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**.

5. **Energy Storage and Protection:** The inverter rectifies this AC into DC and feeds it into the **High-Voltage Battery Pack**. The **Battery Management System (BMS)** monitors cell voltages, temperature, and SOC, throttling or disabling regen if any parameter exceeds safe limits.

**Block Diagram:**
*(Refer to Section 4.1, Diagram 1, for the complete functional architecture flow.)*

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Energy Recovered:**

Convert speed: $v_i = 90 \, km/h = 25 \, m/s$, $v_f = 0 \, m/s$.

Apply the master formula:

$$E_{regen} = \eta_{regen} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot m \cdot v_{i}^{2}$$

$$E_{regen} = 0.72 \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 1800 \cdot (25)^{2}$$

$$E_{regen} = 0.72 \cdot 900 \cdot 625$$

$$E_{regen} = 405000 \, J$$

Convert to kWh:

$$E_{regen} = \dfrac{405000}{3.6 \times 10^{6}} = 0.1125 \, kWh = 112.5 \, Wh$$

**(ii) Range Extension:**

$$\text{Range Extension} = \dfrac{E_{regen}}{\text{Energy Consumption per km}} = \dfrac{112.5 \, Wh}{150 \, Wh/km} = 0.75 \, km$$

**Answer: (i) 0.1125 kWh (112.5 Wh), (ii) 0.75 km.**

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: (a)** Discuss the factors that limit the maximum regenerative braking force achievable in an electric vehicle. How is *blended braking* implemented to overcome these limitations? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of the master regenerative-braking energy equation, compare the recoverable energy for two cases:
  - Case 1: A 1200 kg vehicle braking from 60 km/h to 20 km/h.
  - Case 2: A 1500 kg vehicle braking from 60 km/h to 20 km/h.
  Assume $\eta_{regen} = 0.75$ for both. What inference do you draw about the dominant factor in regenerative energy recovery? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Limiting Factors & Blended Braking:**

**Factors Limiting Maximum Regenerative Braking Force:**

1. **Motor Thermal Limit:** The motor's continuous and peak torque ratings are bounded by its thermal dissipation capacity. Exceeding these limits causes permanent demagnetisation of permanent magnets or insulation failure.

2. **Battery C-Rate Limit:** Lithium-ion batteries can only accept charge at a limited C-rate (typically 1C–3C continuous). Exceeding this accelerates degradation and may cause lithium plating.

3. **Battery SOC Headroom:** When SOC exceeds ~80–85 %, the BMS restricts further charging, reducing available regen torque.

4. **Tyre-Road Friction Limit:** The maximum deceleration transferable to the road is bounded by the friction coefficient $\mu$. If regen torque exceeds the friction circle, wheel lock-up occurs, triggering ABS intervention.

5. **Speed-Dependent EMF:** At low speeds, induced EMF in the motor drops below the battery voltage, making regen impossible near a stop.

6. **Drive Cycle / Smoothness:** Sudden high regen requests can destabilise the vehicle, so the VCU smoothly ramps torque to maintain ride comfort.

**Blended Braking Implementation:**

*Blended braking* is a control strategy in which the VCU continuously distributes the total driver-requested braking torque between the regenerative system and the hydraulic friction system. The implementation follows these rules:

- **Low-to-Moderate Braking:** Up to ~0.3 g, the VCU uses **100 % regenerative braking**, provided SOC and temperature permit.
- **Moderate-to-Heavy Braking:** From ~0.3 g to ~0.6 g, the VCU allocates a fixed regen ceiling and supplements with friction brakes.
- **Emergency / Heavy Braking:** Above ~0.6 g, regen is held at its maximum and friction brakes supply the remainder.
- **Low-Speed Cutoff:** Below ~8 km/h, regen is faded out and friction brakes take over completely to bring the vehicle to a halt.

The transition between regen and friction is calibrated to be **pedal-feel transparent** — the driver cannot distinguish the source of braking force.

**Part (b) — Numerical Comparison:**

**Case 1: m = 1200 kg**

Convert: $v_i = 60 \, km/h = 16.67 \, m/s$, $v_f = 20 \, km/h = 5.56 \, m/s$.

$$E_{regen,1} = 0.75 \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 1200 \cdot (16.67^{2} - 5.56^{2})$$

$$E_{regen,1} = 0.75 \cdot 600 \cdot (277.89 - 30.91)$$

$$E_{regen,1} = 0.75 \cdot 600 \cdot 246.98$$

$$E_{regen,1} = 111141 \, J \approx 111.1 \, kJ$$

**Case 2: m = 1500 kg**

$$E_{regen,2} = 0.75 \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 1500 \cdot (16.67^{2} - 5.56^{2})$$

$$E_{regen,2} = 0.75 \cdot 750 \cdot (277.89 - 30.91)$$

$$E_{regen,2} = 0.75 \cdot 750 \cdot 246.98$$

$$E_{regen,2} = 138926 \, J \approx 138.9 \, kJ$$

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Case 1 | Case 2 | Observation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Vehicle Mass | 1200 kg | 1500 kg | Case 2 is 25 % heavier |
| Speed Change | 60 → 20 km/h | 60 → 20 km/h | Identical |
| $\eta_{regen}$ | 0.75 | 0.75 | Identical |
| Recovered Energy | 111.1 kJ | 138.9 kJ | Case 2 recovers 25 % more |

**Inference:**

Since both vehicles undergo the *same* speed change, the difference in recovered energy is **directly proportional to vehicle mass** (E ∝ m). Therefore, **vehicle mass is the dominant factor** when the speed change is held constant. However, in real-world driving, speed change (i.e., the square of velocity) typically dominates because vehicles vary speed far more than they vary mass. The kinetic energy formula $KE = \tfrac{1}{2}mv^{2}$ shows that *velocity contributes quadratically*, while *mass contributes linearly*.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Students frequently forget to convert km/h to m/s, resulting in answers off by a factor of $(3.6)^{2} \approx 13$. Always convert velocity to SI units before substituting.
> 2. **Forgetting the Efficiency Factor:** Many students write $\tfrac{1}{2}mv^{2}$ directly as "recoverable energy" without multiplying by $\eta_{regen}$. Examiners deduct **2 marks** for this omission.
> 3. **Joules vs kWh Confusion:** Energy values are typically huge in Joules. Always convert to kWh at the end using $\div (3.6 \times 10^{6})$.
> 4. **Mixing Up Battery and Capacitor Behaviour:** Regen braking works because batteries can absorb charge; super-capacitors (used in some FCEVs) can absorb charge even faster and over a wider SOC range — do not confuse the two.
> 5. **Ignoring the Low-Speed Cutoff:** A common conceptual error is assuming regen works at zero speed. It does not — induced EMF vanishes when $\omega = 0$.
> 6. **Single-Line Block Diagrams:** Examiners expect at least **five labelled blocks** with arrows showing both signal and energy flow. Single-box diagrams lose up to **3 marks**.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Core Definition:** Regenerative braking converts vehicle kinetic energy into electrical energy using the traction motor in generator mode.
- **Five Operating Stages:** Driver detection → Mode arbitration → Motor torque reversal → Electromechanical conversion → Battery storage with SOC management.
- **Master Formula:** $E_{regen} = \eta_{regen} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot m \cdot (v_{i}^{2} - v_{f}^{2})$.
- **Power Formula:** $P_{regen}(t) = \eta_{regen} \cdot m \cdot a_{regen} \cdot v(t)$.
- **Typical Efficiency:** $\eta_{regen}$ ranges from **0.6 to 0.8** (60–80 %).
- **SOC Operating Window:** Regen is active only between roughly **20 % and 80 %** SOC.
- **Blended Braking:** The VCU arbitrates between regen and friction brakes; regen dominates up to ~0.3 g deceleration.
- **Low-Speed Cutoff:** Below ~5–8 km/h, regen fades and friction brakes take over.
- **Key Subsystems:** VCU (decision-maker), MCU (motor controller), Inverter (AC/DC bidirectional converter), Traction Motor/Generator, HV Battery, BMS.
- **Advantages:** Range extension (15–25 %), reduced brake wear, lower thermal load, regulatory compliance.
- **Limitations:** Motor thermal limit, battery C-rate limit, SOC headroom, tyre-road friction, low-speed EMF cutoff.
- **Conversions to Memorise:** $1 \, kWh = 3.6 \times 10^{6} \, J$, $1 \, km/h = \tfrac{5}{18} \, m/s$, $g = 9.81 \, m/s^{2}$.
- **Faraday's Law Connection:** Regenerative braking is a direct practical application of Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction — always cite this in theoretical answers.
- **Bidirectional Inverter:** The inverter must be capable of bidirectional power flow; this is what distinguishes an EV powertrain inverter from a conventional motor drive.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hybrid Electric Vehicles: Types – Series and Parallel Hybrid Layouts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT744 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Hybrid Electric Vehicles: Types – Series and Parallel Hybrid Layouts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV) – Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition**
> A **Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV)** is a vehicle that combines two or more distinct energy sources and/or power converters — typically an **Internal Combustion Engine (ICE)** together with one or more **electric machines (EM)** and an **energy storage system (ESS)** — to propel the vehicle. The combination is controlled by an **Energy Management Strategy (EMS)** such that the strengths of each power source compensate for the weaknesses of the other, achieving superior fuel economy, lower emissions, and improved drivability compared to a conventional ICE-only vehicle.

According to the **KTU 2024 PEMUT744 syllabus**, HEVs are broadly classified based on the **topology of power flow** between the ICE, the electric machine, and the wheels. The two foundational architectures covered in Module 1 are:

1. **Series Hybrid Electric Vehicle (SHEV)**
2. **Parallel Hybrid Electric Vehicle (PHEV)** — *Note: KTU Module 1 deals with the mild/full parallel type; plug-in variants are covered in Module 2.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plain-English Analogy (Series Hybrid):**
> Imagine you are on a bicycle and a friend is **sitting on a trailer** turning a generator to charge a small battery. The battery alone powers an electric motor that actually drives the rear wheel. The petrol engine *never* touches the wheel directly — it is a **mobile power plant on wheels** whose only job is to feed electricity to the motor. → This is the **Series Hybrid**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plain-English Analogy (Parallel Hybrid):**
> Now imagine you and a friend are **both pushing the same cart from behind**, side by side. You can push alone, your friend can push alone, or you can both push together. Your legs (the ICE) and your friend's arms (the electric motor) are **two independent paths of force** that mechanically merge at the cart's axle. → This is the **Parallel Hybrid**.

### 1.3 Key Physical Quantities and Standard Metrics

The following are the **standard engineering metrics** (as per **SAE J1711** and **ISO 23274**) that govern HEV performance evaluation under the KTU 2024 scheme:

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Battery State of Charge | **SOC** | **30 % – 80 %** (operational window) |
| Specific Energy of Battery | – | **100 – 250 Wh/kg** (Li-ion) |
| ICE Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{th}$ | **30 % – 40 %** (Otto), up to **42 %** (Diesel) |
| Electric Machine Efficiency | $\eta_{em}$ | **90 % – 95 %** |
| Regenerative Braking Recovery | – | **10 % – 30 %** of total braking energy |
| Combined System Fuel Economy Gain | – | **20 % – 40 %** over conventional |

> [!NOTE]
> The **operational SOC window** of **30 %–80 %** is deliberately chosen to maximise battery cycle life. KTU examiners frequently test this window as a "definition-type" 3-mark question.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Series Hybrid Electric Vehicle (SHEV) — Architecture & Operation

In a **Series Hybrid**, the **mechanical path between the ICE and the wheels is broken**. Power flows in a single electrical series:

$$\text{ICE} \rightarrow \text{Generator} \rightarrow \text{Battery (DC Bus)} \rightarrow \text{Traction Motor} \rightarrow \text{Wheels}$$

**Operational Logic — Step by Step:**

- **Step 1 — Generator-Set Operation:** The ICE is mechanically coupled *only* to a generator (or integrated Starter-Generator, **ISG**). It operates at a single fixed point on the Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (**BSFC**) map — the point of **minimum fuel consumption** for a given power output.
- **Step 2 — Power Split at DC Bus:** The generated electrical power is routed to the **DC bus**, where it either:
  (a) charges the battery, or
  (b) directly feeds the traction inverter, or
  (c) splits between both.
- **Step 3 — Traction Motor Drive:** A single high-power traction motor (typically **3-phase AC induction** or **PMSM**) drives the wheels through a fixed-ratio transmission.
- **Step 4 — Regenerative Braking:** The traction motor acts as a generator, converting kinetic energy back into electrical energy stored in the battery.
- **Step 5 — Engine-Off Capability:** The ICE can be switched off entirely during idle, low-load cruise, or reverse, with the battery alone propelling the vehicle (this defines a **full hybrid** as per KTU classification).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Because the ICE in a Series Hybrid is **mechanically decoupled from the wheels**, it can run *always* at its **Best Operating Point (BOP)**. This is why SHEVs are often preferred for **city buses, delivery trucks, and refuse vehicles** (e.g., the **Leyland OLYMPUS**, **Hino S'elega Hybrid**).

### 2.2 Parallel Hybrid Electric Vehicle (PHEV) — Architecture & Operation

In a **Parallel Hybrid**, **two independent mechanical paths** deliver torque to the wheels, and these paths are merged through a **mechanical coupling** (clutch, planetary gearset, or torque coupler):

$$\text{ICE} \rightarrow \text{Clutch} \rightarrow \bigotimes \leftarrow \text{EM} \rightarrow \text{Transmission} \rightarrow \text{Wheels}$$

**Operational Logic — Step by Step:**

- **Step 1 — Single-Source Mode (ICE-only):** For sustained high-speed cruising on highways, the clutch engages and the EM is decoupled. The vehicle behaves as a conventional ICE car.
- **Step 2 — Single-Source Mode (EV-only):** For low-speed city driving, the ICE is decoupled (clutch open) and the EM alone propels the vehicle. Range is limited by battery SOC.
- **Step 3 — Parallel Boost Mode:** Both ICE and EM deliver torque simultaneously to the wheels. This is used during **acceleration and hill-climbing** to overcome the peak-power demand that a downsized ICE cannot meet alone.
- **Step 4 — Power-Split / Load-Leveling:** The EM acts as a generator driven by the ICE, storing excess ICE output in the battery when the road load is below engine BOP.
- **Step 5 — Regenerative Braking:** Same concept as SHEV — kinetic energy recovered via the EM.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The **planetary gearset** (used in Toyota Hybrid System – **THS**) is *technically* a **Power-Split Hybrid** (a sub-class of parallel). The KTU 2024 syllabus treats THS as a separate advanced topology covered in Module 2, but examiners often use it as a **3-mark comparison question** in Module 1.

### 2.3 Energy Flow Equations — Universal HEV Power Balance

For any hybrid topology, the **power demand at the wheels** $P_{wheels}$ must equal the sum of all power sources minus all losses:

$$P_{wheels} = P_{ICE} \cdot \eta_{ICE} + P_{EM} \cdot \eta_{EM} \pm P_{batt}$$

Where the sign convention is:
- **$(+)$** for **discharge** (battery supplying power),
- **$(-)$** for **charge** (battery absorbing power, e.g., regen braking or generator-set mode).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM CHEAT SHEET — Module 1: Series & Parallel Hybrid Layouts**
> Memorise the symbols, units, and boundary conditions. All questions on this module are solvable with this table.

| # | Quantity / Formula | Expression | Units | Boundary / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Wheel Power Demand | $P_{w} = F_{tr} \cdot v$ | **W** | $F_{tr}$ = tractive force, $v$ = velocity |
| 2 | Tractive Force | $F_{tr} = m \cdot a + m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta + F_{rr} + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_{d} A v^{2}$ | **N** | 4 components: inertia, grade, rolling, aero |
| 3 | ICE BSFC | $\text{BSFC} = \dot{m}_{f} / P_{ICE}$ | **g/kWh** | **Minimum BSFC** = Best Operating Point |
| 4 | Series Power Flow | $P_{wheels} = \eta_{gen} \cdot \eta_{inv} \cdot \eta_{em} \cdot P_{ICE}$ | **W** | **Decoupled path** — ICE never touches wheels |
| 5 | Parallel Torque Merge | $T_{wheels} = (T_{ICE} \cdot \eta_{tr}) + (T_{EM} \cdot \eta_{em}) \cdot i_{g}$ | **N·m** | Torques add at coupling, then through gear $i_{g}$ |
| 6 | Battery SOC Dynamics | $\text{SOC}(t) = \text{SOC}(0) + \dfrac{1}{Q_{bat}} \int_{0}^{t} I_{bat} \, d\tau$ | **dimensionless (%)** | $Q_{bat}$ = battery capacity in **Ah** |
| 7 | Regen Braking Limit | $E_{regen} = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^{2} \cdot \eta_{regen} \cdot (1 - \phi)$ | **J** | $\phi$ = friction-brake fraction; $\eta_{regen} \leq 0.3$ |
| 8 | Equivalent Fuel Energy | $E_{fuel} = \dot{m}_{f} \cdot \text{LHV}$ | **J/s = W** | LHV of petrol = **44 MJ/kg** |
| 9 | HEV Efficiency | $\eta_{HEV} = \dfrac{P_{wheels}}{P_{fuel} + P_{elec,grid}}$ | **dimensionless** | For charge-sustaining (CS) mode |
| 10 | SOC Sustaining Constraint | $\Delta \text{SOC}_{cycle} \approx 0$ | **%** | Definition of **CS-HEV** per KTU Module 1 |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Examiner Trap:** Students often confuse $\eta_{gen}$ (generator) with $\eta_{em}$ (electric machine in motor mode). In SHEV, the ICE drives the **generator**, so the chain is $\eta_{ICE} \rightarrow \eta_{gen} \rightarrow \eta_{inv} \rightarrow \eta_{em}$. Missing the **inverter efficiency** $\eta_{inv} \approx 0.95$ is a guaranteed **2-mark loss** in numerical problems.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Topology | Best-Suited Applications | Real Production Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Series Hybrid** | City buses, garbage trucks, delivery vans, rail-hybrids | Nissan e-Power (e.g., **Nissan Kicks e-Power**), BMW i3 REx |
| **Parallel Hybrid** | Sedans, SUVs, light commercial vehicles | Honda Civic Hybrid (IMA), Hyundai Ioniq Hybrid |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Tractive Force Demand Equation

The **tractive force** $F_{tr}$ required at the tyre–road interface is the foundation of all HEV sizing problems. We derive it from **Newton's Second Law** applied to a vehicle of mass $m$ moving on a road with slope angle $\theta$:

**Step 1 — Free-Body Diagram Identification.**
Four resistive forces oppose motion:

1. **Inertial force** $F_{i} = m \cdot a$
2. **Grade force** $F_{g} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta$
3. **Rolling resistance force** $F_{rr} = m \cdot g \cdot C_{rr} \cdot \cos\theta$
4. **Aerodynamic drag force** $F_{d} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho \cdot C_{d} \cdot A \cdot v^{2}$

**Step 2 — Summation of Forces.**
Applying $\sum F = m \cdot a$ along the direction of motion:

$$F_{tr} - F_{g} - F_{rr} - F_{d} = m \cdot a$$

**Step 3 — Rearranging for $F_{tr}$.**

$$F_{tr} = m \cdot a + m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta + m \cdot g \cdot C_{rr} \cdot \cos\theta + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho \cdot C_{d} \cdot A \cdot v^{2}$$

This is the **canonical KTU Module 1 tractive force equation**. For level road ($\theta = 0$) and steady cruise ($a = 0$):

$$F_{tr} = m \cdot g \cdot C_{rr} + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho \cdot C_{d} \cdot A \cdot v^{2}$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Series Hybrid Wheel-Pheel Power Chain

We now derive the **end-to-end power efficiency** of a Series Hybrid from the ICE shaft to the wheel hub.

**Step 1 — ICE Output Power at Crank.**
Let $P_{ICE}$ be the brake power developed at the ICE crankshaft. The fuel chemical energy converted to mechanical power:

$$P_{mech,ICE} = P_{ICE} \cdot \eta_{mech,ICE}$$

**Step 2 — Generator Conversion.**
The ICE shaft drives the generator. Electrical power produced at generator terminals:

$$P_{gen,elec} = P_{ICE} \cdot \eta_{mech,ICE} \cdot \eta_{gen}$$

**Step 3 — DC Bus / Battery Routing.**
If the electrical power is routed *through* the battery (charge-discharge cycle), apply **round-trip efficiency** $\eta_{batt,rt} = \eta_{ch} \cdot \eta_{dis}$:

$$P_{dc,bus} = P_{gen,elec} \cdot \eta_{batt,rt}$$

If routed *directly* to the inverter (bypass mode), $\eta_{batt,rt} = 1$.

**Step 4 — Inverter + Traction Motor.**
The DC bus feeds an inverter that drives the traction motor:

$$P_{motor,mech} = P_{dc,bus} \cdot \eta_{inv} \cdot \eta_{em}$$

**Step 5 — Final Transmission to Wheels.**
Final mechanical power at the wheel contact patch:

$$P_{wheels} = P_{motor,mech} \cdot \eta_{final\,drive}$$

**Step 6 — Combining All Stages.**

$$\boxed{P_{wheels} = P_{ICE} \cdot \eta_{mech,ICE} \cdot \eta_{gen} \cdot \eta_{batt,rt} \cdot \eta_{inv} \cdot \eta_{em} \cdot \eta_{final\,drive}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numeric Check (KTU-Style Problem):** Take $P_{ICE} = 50$ kW, and use the **typical efficiencies** $\eta_{mech,ICE} = 0.35$, $\eta_{gen} = 0.92$, $\eta_{batt,rt} = 0.90$, $\eta_{inv} = 0.95$, $\eta_{em} = 0.93$, $\eta_{fd} = 0.95$. The wheel power is:
>
> $$P_{wheels} = 50 \times 0.35 \times 0.92 \times 0.90 \times 0.95 \times 0.93 \times 0.95 \approx 11.7 \text{ kW}$$
>
> This **4× drop** in delivered power is *exactly why* the KTU syllabus emphasises that the ICE in a Series Hybrid must be **sized 3×–4× larger** than the peak wheel power demand to compensate for the multi-stage conversion losses.

### 3.3 Derivation: Parallel Hybrid Torque-Merge Equation

In a Parallel Hybrid, the ICE and EM are mechanically coupled. At the coupling point, **torques add** (not powers):

**Step 1 — At the Coupling.**
$$T_{coupling} = T_{ICE} \cdot \eta_{tr,coup} + T_{EM} \cdot \eta_{em,coup}$$

**Step 2 — Through Transmission Gear $i_g$.**

$$T_{shaft} = (T_{ICE} \cdot \eta_{tr,coup} + T_{EM} \cdot \eta_{em,coup}) \cdot i_g$$

**Step 3 — Through Final Drive $i_0$.**

$$\boxed{T_{wheels} = (T_{ICE} \cdot \eta_{tr,coup} + T_{EM} \cdot \eta_{em,coup}) \cdot i_g \cdot i_0}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight (KTU Favourite):** Unlike Series, the Parallel architecture incurs **only one electrical conversion** (EM side). This is why the ICE in a Parallel Hybrid can be **downsized by only 20 %–30 %**, while the Series Hybrid requires the ICE to be **oversized** to overcome chain losses.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Series vs Parallel

| Parameter | **Series Hybrid (SHEV)** | **Parallel Hybrid (PHEV)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical link to wheels** | **None** (fully decoupled) | **Yes** (clutch or coupler) |
| **ICE operating point** | **Fixed** at Best Operating Point (BOP) | **Variable**, follows load |
| **Number of electric machines** | 1 (Traction) + 1 (Generator) = **2** | **1** (Traction/Regen) |
| **Transmission complexity** | **Simple** (single-speed reducer) | **Complex** (multi-speed gearbox + clutch) |
| **ICE sizing for peak demand** | **3×–4× wheel power** | **1×–1.3× wheel power** |
| **EV-only mode range** | **Longer** (battery dominant) | **Shorter** (battery assist) |
| **Overall efficiency at low load** | **Higher** (ICE-off possible) | **Lower** (ICE must idle or drag) |
| **Overall efficiency at high load** | **Lower** (chain losses) | **Higher** (direct path) |
| **Component cost** | **Higher** (2 EMs, large battery) | **Lower** (1 EM, small battery) |
| **Best application** | **City / urban cycle** | **Highway / mixed cycle** |
| **Emission at constant cruise** | **Lowest** | Moderate |
| **Cold-start emissions** | Reduced (engine runs at BOP) | Higher (engine on for traction) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Series Hybrid Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[FUEL TANK] --> B[ICE INTERNAL COMBUSTION ENGINE]
    B --> C[GENERATOR G2]
    C --> D[DC BUS WITH RECTIFIER]
    D --> E[BATTERY PACK ESS]
    D --> F[TRACTION INVERTER]
    F --> G[TRACTION MOTOR M1]
    G --> H[FINAL DRIVE FIXED RATIO]
    H --> I[DRIVING WHEELS]
    G -. regenerative braking .-> D
    J[BRAKE PEDAL] --> G
    K[ACCEL PEDAL] --> F
    L[EMS ENERGY MANAGEMENT CONTROLLER] --> B
    L --> F
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Note the **single unidirectional power flow** (left-to-right) and the **regenerative feedback arrow** from the traction motor back to the DC bus. The **ICE has no direct mechanical link to the wheels** — this is the defining visual signature of a Series Hybrid.

### 4.2 Parallel Hybrid Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[FUEL TANK] --> B[ICE INTERNAL COMBUSTION ENGINE]
    B --> C[CLUTCH C1]
    C --> D[TORQUE COUPLER PLANETARY OR SIMPLE]
    E[BATTERY PACK ESS] --> F[INVERTER]
    F --> G[ELECTRIC MACHINE M1]
    G --> D
    D --> H[MULTI SPEED TRANSMISSION]
    H --> I[FINAL DRIVE]
    I --> J[DRIVING WHEELS]
    G -. regenerative braking .-> E
    K[ACCEL PEDAL] --> L[EMS CONTROLLER]
    L --> B
    L --> G
    M[BRAKE PEDAL] --> J
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Observe the **two parallel mechanical paths** (ICE path on top, EM path on bottom) **merging at the Torque Coupler**. The EMS controller commands *both* the ICE throttle and the EM torque independently, which is the operational essence of the **Parallel Hybrid**.

### 4.3 Series vs Parallel — Side-by-Side Topological Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SH["SERIES HYBRID TOPOLOGY"]
        s1[ICE] --> s2[GEN]
        s2 --> s3[DC BUS]
        s3 --> s4[INV]
        s4 --> s5[TRACTION EM]
        s5 --> s6[WHEELS]
    end
    subgraph PH["PARALLEL HYBRID TOPOLOGY"]
        p1[ICE] --> p2[CLUTCH]
        p2 --> p3[COUPLER]
        p4[INV] --> p5[EM]
        p5 --> p3
        p3 --> p6[GEARBOX]
        p6 --> p7[WHEELS]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Series** is a **series electrical chain** with **one** traction motor. **Parallel** is a **parallel mechanical chain** with **two** torque sources merging at a single coupling. This single contrast is the **single most important Module 1 takeaway** for KTU 2024.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> The following question bank is calibrated to the **KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Evaluation) pattern**: Part A carries 3 marks each (short answer), Part B carries 14 marks with **internal choice** (typically sub-parts of 7 marks each).

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a Series Hybrid Electric Vehicle. List two of its main advantages over a Parallel Hybrid.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Series Hybrid Electric Vehicle (SHEV) consists of an **ICE mechanically coupled only to a generator**, which charges a **DC bus / battery** that in turn powers a **single traction motor** driving the wheels. The ICE has **no mechanical link** to the wheels. The power flow chain is: **ICE → Generator → Battery → Inverter → Traction Motor → Wheels**.

**Two Advantages over Parallel Hybrid:**

1. The ICE can operate **always at its Best Operating Point (BOP)**, giving superior urban-cycle fuel economy.
2. **Simpler transmission** — only a single-speed reducer is needed, as the traction motor handles all speed variation.

*(Diagram: 1 mark; Chain explanation: 1 mark; Two advantages: 1 mark = 3 marks total)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 1]**
*Differentiate between Series and Parallel Hybrid Electric Vehicles on the basis of (i) mechanical coupling, (ii) number of electric machines, and (iii) typical city-cycle fuel economy.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

| Criterion | Series Hybrid | Parallel Hybrid |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Mechanical Coupling | **No direct ICE-to-wheel link** (fully decoupled) | **Direct ICE-to-wheel link** through clutch |
| (ii) No. of Electric Machines | **2** (one generator + one traction motor) | **1** (traction/regen only) |
| (iii) City-Cycle Fuel Economy | **Superior** (engine-off + BOP operation) | **Good** but lower than Series in stop-go traffic |

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

**Q3. Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main]**

**(a)** With the help of a power-flow block diagram, explain the working principle of a **Parallel Hybrid Electric Vehicle**. List the **four operating modes** of a Parallel Hybrid. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Parallel Hybrid passenger car has the following data: **Vehicle mass $m = 1400$ kg**, **rolling resistance coefficient $C_{rr} = 0.012$**, **aerodynamic drag coefficient $C_d = 0.30$**, **frontal area $A = 2.2$ m²**, **air density $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m³**, **road gradient $\theta = 5°$**, **vehicle speed $v = 60$ km/h**, and **acceleration $a = 0.5$ m/s²**.
Compute the **total tractive force demand** and the **required wheel power**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — 7 marks:**

In a Parallel Hybrid, **two independent power sources — an ICE and an Electric Machine (EM)** — are mechanically coupled to the driveshaft, typically via a **clutch and a torque coupler** (which may be a simple gear train, a planetary gearset, or an electronically controlled clutch-pack).

**Four Operating Modes:**

1. **ICE-Only Mode:** The clutch is engaged; the EM is idle. Used for steady highway cruise.
2. **EV-Only Mode:** The clutch is open; the ICE is off. The EM alone propels the vehicle. Used for low-speed city driving.
3. **Parallel Boost Mode:** Both ICE and EM deliver torque simultaneously. Used for maximum acceleration and hill-climbing.
4. **Regenerative Braking Mode:** The EM acts as a generator; the ICE is decoupled. Kinetic energy is recovered to the battery.

*(Block diagram: 2 marks; Working principle: 2 marks; Four modes with examples: 3 marks)*

**(b) — 7 marks:**

**Given Data:**
$m = 1400$ kg, $C_{rr} = 0.012$, $C_d = 0.30$, $A = 2.2$ m², $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m³, $\theta = 5°$, $v = 60$ km/h, $a = 0.5$ m/s², $g = 9.81$ m/s².

**Step 1 — Unit Conversions.**
$v = 60 \text{ km/h} = \dfrac{60 \times 1000}{3600} = 16.667$ m/s.
$\theta = 5° \Rightarrow \sin\theta = 0.0872$, $\cos\theta = 0.9962$.

**Step 2 — Compute Each Force Component.**

Inertial force: $F_{i} = 1400 \times 0.5 = 700$ N

Grade force: $F_{g} = 1400 \times 9.81 \times 0.0872 = 1197.5$ N

Rolling resistance: $F_{rr} = 1400 \times 9.81 \times 0.012 \times 0.9962 = 164.0$ N

Aerodynamic drag: $F_{d} = 0.5 \times 1.2 \times 0.30 \times 2.2 \times (16.667)^{2}$
$$F_{d} = 0.5 \times 1.2 \times 0.30 \times 2.2 \times 277.8 = 110.0 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Total Tractive Force.**

$$F_{tr} = F_{i} + F_{g} + F_{rr} + F_{d}$$
$$F_{tr} = 700 + 1197.5 + 164.0 + 110.0 = 2171.5 \text{ N} \approx 2.17 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4 — Required Wheel Power.**

$$P_{w} = F_{tr} \cdot v = 2171.5 \times 16.667 = 36190 \text{ W} \approx 36.2 \text{ kW}$$

**Final Answer:** $F_{tr} \approx 2.17$ kN and $P_{w} \approx 36.2$ kW.

*(Force components: 3 marks; Summation: 1 mark; Power calculation: 2 marks; Units and answer: 1 mark = 7 marks total)*

---

**Q3. Question B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Main]**

**(a)** Explain with a block diagram the working of a **Series Hybrid Electric Vehicle**. Derive the **end-to-end power efficiency chain** from the ICE to the wheels. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An SHEV has an ICE rated at **$P_{ICE} = 60$ kW** with $\eta_{mech,ICE} = 0.38$. The generator has $\eta_{gen} = 0.93$, the battery round-trip efficiency is $\eta_{batt,rt} = 0.88$, the inverter has $\eta_{inv} = 0.96$, the traction motor has $\eta_{em} = 0.94$, and the final drive has $\eta_{fd} = 0.95$. Compute the **net power available at the wheels** and the **overall system efficiency**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — 7 marks:**

In a Series Hybrid, the **ICE is mechanically decoupled from the wheels**. The ICE drives a **generator (G)**, whose electrical output feeds a **DC bus**. The DC bus charges a **battery (ESS)** and simultaneously supplies an **inverter** that drives a **traction motor (M)**. The traction motor, in turn, drives the wheels through a **single-speed reducer and final drive**. Regenerative braking is enabled by using the traction motor as a generator during deceleration.

**End-to-end power chain:**

$$P_{wheels} = P_{ICE} \cdot \eta_{mech,ICE} \cdot \eta_{gen} \cdot \eta_{batt,rt} \cdot \eta_{inv} \cdot \eta_{em} \cdot \eta_{fd}$$

*(Block diagram: 2 marks; Working explanation: 2 marks; Derivation: 3 marks)*

**(b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Apply the chain equation.**

$$P_{wheels} = 60 \times 0.38 \times 0.93 \times 0.88 \times 0.96 \times 0.94 \times 0.95$$

**Step 2 — Compute the cumulative product.**

$\eta_{cum} = 0.38 \times 0.93 \times 0.88 \times 0.96 \times 0.94 \times 0.95$

Intermediate multiplications:
$0.38 \times 0.93 = 0.3534$
$0.3534 \times 0.88 = 0.3110$
$0.3110 \times 0.96 = 0.2986$
$0.2986 \times 0.94 = 0.2807$
$0.2807 \times 0.95 = 0.2667$

**Step 3 — Wheel Power.**

$$P_{wheels} = 60 \times 0.2667 = 16.0 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4 — Overall System Efficiency.**

$$\eta_{overall} = \dfrac{P_{wheels}}{P_{ICE}} = \dfrac{16.0}{60} = 0.2667 \approx 26.7\%$$

**Final Answer:** $P_{wheels} = 16.0$ kW and $\eta_{overall} = 26.7\%$.

*(Formula setup: 2 marks; Step-by-step multiplication: 3 marks; Final power: 1 mark; Efficiency: 1 mark = 7 marks total)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Series and Parallel diagrams** (–2 marks): If you draw a Series topology but call it Parallel, you lose 2 marks even if the rest of the answer is correct. Always start with the **power-flow arrow direction**.
> 2. **Forgetting the battery round-trip efficiency** in Series chain (–1 mark): $\eta_{batt,rt} \neq 1$ unless the problem states "bypass mode". The default KTU assumption is **charge-discharge mode**.
> 3. **Unit conversion errors** in tractive-force problems (–1 mark): Forgetting to convert **km/h to m/s** in the power equation $P = F \cdot v$ is the single most common numerical mistake.
> 4. **Ignoring $\cos\theta$ factor** in rolling resistance when $\theta \neq 0$ (–1 mark): $F_{rr} = m g C_{rr} \cos\theta$, **not** $m g C_{rr}$. For $\theta = 5°$, the error is only 0.4 %, but examiners still deduct the mark.
> 5. **Not stating the SOC operational window** (–1 mark): If the question asks "why is the SOC maintained between 30 % and 80 %?", stating only "to extend life" is insufficient — also mention **avoiding deep discharge (cell damage)** and **avoiding full charge (lithium plating)**.

---

### 5.1 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 1 Rapid-Revision Bullets — Series & Parallel Hybrid Layouts**
>
> - **HEV Definition:** A vehicle with **two or more energy sources**, typically an **ICE + Electric Machine + Battery**, governed by an **Energy Management Strategy (EMS)**.
> - **Series Hybrid:** ICE is **mechanically decoupled** from the wheels. Power flows in a **single electrical series** — ICE → Generator → Battery → Inverter → Traction Motor → Wheels.
> - **Parallel Hybrid:** ICE and EM are **mechanically coupled** to the driveshaft; **torques add** at the coupler.
> - **Two electric machines in Series** (Generator + Traction), **one in Parallel** (combined traction/regen).
> - **Series ICE** runs at **fixed Best Operating Point (BOP)** → best for **urban city cycle**.
> - **Parallel ICE** runs **variably with load** → best for **highway + mixed cycle**.
> - **Series chain efficiency** is much lower at peak power → ICE must be **oversized by 3×–4×** to compensate.
> - **Parallel** can use a **downsized ICE (×1 to ×1.3)** with EM for boost.
> - **Canonical tractive force equation:** $F_{tr} = m a + m g \sin\theta + m g C_{rr} \cos\theta + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^{2}$.
> - **Wheel power:** $P_{w} = F_{tr} \cdot v$ (note **v in m/s**, not km/h).
> - **SOC operational window:** **30 % – 80 %** (charge-sustaining mode for CS-HEV).
> - **Regenerative braking** recovers **10 % – 30 %** of total braking energy; rest is wasted as heat in friction brakes.
> - **LHV of petrol** = **44 MJ/kg**; **LHV of diesel** ≈ **43 MJ/kg**.
> - **Real-world examples — Series:** Nissan e-Power, BMW i3 REx. **Parallel:** Honda Civic IMA, Hyundai Ioniq.
> - **KERS** (Kinetic Energy Recovery System) is the **F1 racing ancestor** of modern HEV regenerative braking — frequently asked as a 2-mark contextual question.
> - **THS (Toyota Hybrid System)** uses a **planetary power-split** device and is *not* a pure series or parallel — KTU keeps it in Module 2 but references it in Module 1 comparisons.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Comparison of Power Systems and Control Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT744 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Comparison of Power Systems and Control Systems ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In the context of **Modern Automotive Technologies**, every vehicle — whether a conventional Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) vehicle, a Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV), a Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV), or a Fuel Cell Vehicle (FCV) — is architected around the **seamless integration of two functionally distinct engineering domains**: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Power System (Energy System):** The subsystem of an automotive platform responsible for the **generation, storage, conditioning, conversion, and transmission of energy** from a primary source to the vehicle's traction driveline. In modern vehicles, this includes the engine/motor, fuel tank/battery pack, power electronics converters (inverters, DC-DC converters), and the mechanical transmission. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Control System (Information/Command System):** The subsystem responsible for **sensing, computing, deciding, and actuating** to ensure that the power system operates at its desired performance point. It comprises sensors, Electronic Control Units (ECUs), control algorithms (often implemented in software), communication buses (CAN, LIN, FlexRay, Automotive Ethernet), and actuators. A modern automobile is therefore a **cyber-physical system (CPS)** — a tight coupling where the *physical* power plant and the *cyber* control hierarchy operate in a closed loop to deliver torque, speed, and energy efficiency to the wheels. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Body > [!NOTE] > **Think of a car as a human body running a marathon:** > - The **Power System** is the **muscular and cardiovascular system** — the heart (engine/motor), lungs (intake), blood (fuel/electricity), and muscles (transmission, driveshaft). It actually *does the work* of moving the body forward. > - The **Control System** is the **nervous system and brain** — the eyes (sensors), spinal cord (CAN bus / wiring harness), and brain (ECU). It does *not produce energy itself*; it **decides how much energy each muscle should deliver, when, and at what rate**, by sending tiny low-power electrical signals. A powerlifter can have enormous muscles (a powerful engine), but without a nervous system telling those muscles *how* and *when* to contract, the body cannot walk, let alone run. Conversely, a brilliant brain (sophisticated ECU) cannot move the body without the muscular system (power train). **Both must work together — and their characteristics (bandwidth, power level, time constant) are fundamentally different.** --- ### 1.3 Core Distinction at a Glance | Aspect | Power System | Control System | |---|---|---| | **Primary Function** | Energy delivery | Energy *regulation* | | **Physical Quantity** | kW, kWh, Nm (high magnitudes) | Volts, bits, °C (low magnitudes) | | **Typical Bandwidth** | **0.1 Hz – 10 Hz** (slow) | **10 Hz – 10 kHz** (fast) | | **Dominant Physics** | Thermodynamics, EM, mechanics | Signal processing, logic, feedback | | **Failure Consequence** | Catastrophic (stall, fire) | Erratic behavior (jerks, misfire) | | **Example Component** | 150 kW traction motor | 32-bit 100 MHz ECU | --- ### 1.4 Why the Comparison Matters in Modern Automotive Tech The automotive industry has been on a **40-year transition curve**: from purely mechanical vehicles (1900s) → mechatronic vehicles (1980s) → by-wire vehicles (2010s) → software-defined vehicles (2024+). In every step, the *ratio* of control complexity to power complexity has **inverted**. Today, premium vehicles contain **>100 ECUs**, ~150 MB of software, and over **5 km of wiring**, while the *physical* power components have actually become *simpler* (e.g., BEVs have ~20 moving parts vs. ~2000 in an ICE). Understanding this divergence is the **central pillar of Module 1**. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Decomposition of an Automotive Power System The **power path** in any modern vehicle follows a canonical chain: $$\text{Source} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Converter} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Transmitter} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Receiver (Wheels)}$$ **Step-by-step logic:** - **Step 1 — Source (Energy Reservoir):** Stores potential energy. In ICE → gasoline/diesel (chemical, ~**44 MJ/kg**); in BEV → Li-ion pack (electrochemical, ~**0.25–0.30 kWh/kg** or **~900 kJ/kg**). - **Step 2 — Converter (Prime Mover):** Transforms stored energy into mechanical/electrical form. ICE → crankshaft rotation; BEV → battery → inverter → 3-phase AC motor. - **Step 3 — Transmission:** Mechanical gearbox, differential, driveshaft — adapts torque/speed to wheel requirements. - **Step 4 — Load:** Wheels + vehicle inertia + aerodynamic drag + rolling resistance. > The **governing equation** for any power system is conservation of energy, expressed in the rate form (Power balance): > > $$P_{\text{source}} = P_{\text{load}} + P_{\text{losses}} + \frac{dE_{\text{stored}}}{dt}$$ --- ### 2.2 Decomposition of an Automotive Control System The **control path** follows a parallel canonical chain (the *classical feedback loop*): $$\text{Reference} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Controller} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Actuator} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Plant} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Output}$$ $$\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\uparrow$$ $$\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\text{Sensor} \;\longleftarrow\; \text{Plant}$$ **Step-by-step logic:** - **Step 1 — Reference:** Driver demand (e.g., accelerator pedal position → torque request). - **Step 2 — Controller (ECU):** Compares reference to measured feedback; computes control signal via an algorithm (PID, Model Predictive Control, State Machine). - **Step 3 — Actuator:** Converts low-power control signal into high-power physical action (throttle valve, fuel injector, motor inverter IGBT gate). - **Step 4 — Plant:** The *physical* power system being controlled. - **Step 5 — Sensor:** Measures output (crankshaft position, motor speed, current, temperature) and feeds back. The mathematical heart of any control system is the **Transfer Function** in the Laplace domain: $$G(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{U(s)}$$ where $U(s)$ is the control input and $Y(s)$ is the measured output. --- ### 2.3 The Fundamental Asymmetry — Bandwidth and Power Gap The most important engineering insight of this module is the **power-bandwidth asymmetry**: > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Power System operates at HIGH POWER and LOW BANDWIDTH.** > **The Control System operates at LOW POWER and HIGH BANDWIDTH.** This asymmetry is *exploited* in every modern vehicle: the ECU's ~**5 W** microcontroller commands a **150 kW** traction motor, achieving a **power amplification ratio of 30,000:1** through the inverter and motor. This is the engineering miracle of automotive electronics. --- ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Module 1) | # | Concept | Formula | Units | Boundary / Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | A typical ICE engine produces **80–200 kW** peak — illustrating the *peak-to-cruise ratio* (~10:1) that justifies transmission design and control strategy. --- ### 2.5 Real-World Utility Across Engineering Fields | Field | Application of Power–Control Comparison | |---|---| | **EV Powertrain Design** | Sizing motor (power) vs. inverter/ECU (control) for cost/performance trade-off | | **Autonomous Driving** | High-level path planner (control) sends torque/steering requests to low-level power actuators | | **Hybrid Energy Management** | Rule-based or optimization-based controller decides ICE vs. electric split (a control-of-power problem) | | **Functional Safety (ISO 26262)** | Control system failure modes (ASIL-D) vs. power system failure modes require *different* diagnostic approaches | | **Vehicle Dynamics (ABS/ESC)** | Hydraulic brake modulator (power) commanded 50+ times/sec by control algorithm | ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: From Driver Pedal to Wheel Torque — A Closed-Loop Walkthrough We will derive the **steady-state and dynamic relationship** between accelerator pedal position (control input) and resulting wheel torque (power output), explicitly separating the *control* sub-system from the *power* sub-system. **Given data (representative BEV):** - Vehicle mass: $m = 1500$ kg - Wheel radius: $r = 0.33$ m - Gear ratio (single-speed): $G = 9.0$ - Motor torque constant: $K_t = 1.6$ Nm/A - Motor back-EMF constant: $K_e = 1.6$ V·s/rad - Battery voltage: $V_{\text{bat}} = 400$ V - Motor resistance + inductance: $R_a = 0.05$ $\Omega$, $L_a = 0.5$ mH - Pedal-to-torque-request gain: $K_{\text{pedal}} = 250$ Nm/unit - PI controller: $K_p = 0.4$, $K_i = 20$ s$^{-1}$ **Step 1 — Map the pedal to a torque request (Control side).** The accelerator pedal is a sensor with characteristic curve: $$\tau_{\text{req}}^{*} = K_{\text{pedal}} \cdot \theta_{\text{pedal}}$$ For a 60% pedal position: $$\tau_{\text{req}}^{*} = 250 \cdot 0.60 = 150 \text{ Nm}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the error signal (Control side).** A shaft encoder on the motor measures actual motor speed $\omega_m$. The torque-tracking error is: $$e(t) = \tau_{\text{req}}^{*}(t) - \tau_{\text{actual}}(t)$$ But the ECU typically controls *current* (proportional to torque): let $\tau_{\text{actual}} = K_t \cdot i_a$. So: $$e(t) = 150 - 1.6 \cdot i_a(t)$$ **Step 3 — Apply the PI controller (Control side).** The voltage command to the inverter is: $$v_{\text{cmd}}(t) = K_p \cdot e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\sigma)\, d\sigma$$ Substituting: $$v_{\text{cmd}}(t) = 0.4\,(150 - 1.6\,i_a) + 20 \int_0^t (150 - 1.6\,i_a)\, d\sigma$$ **Step 4 — Model the motor electrical dynamics (Power side, plant).** Kirchhoff's voltage law on the armature circuit: $$v_{\text{cmd}}(t) = R_a \cdot i_a(t) + L_a \frac{di_a}{dt} + K_e \cdot \omega_m(t)$$ Rearranging: $$L_a \frac{di_a}{dt} = v_{\text{cmd}}(t) - R_a \cdot i_a(t) - K_e \cdot \omega_m(t)$$ **Step 5 — Model the motor mechanical dynamics (Power side, plant).** Newton's 2nd law for the rotor inertia $J_m \approx 0.05$ kg·m²: $$J_m \frac{d\omega_m}{dt} = K_t \cdot i_a(t) - B_m \cdot \omega_m(t) - \frac{\tau_{\text{load,ref}}}{G}$$ where $\tau_{\text{load,ref}} = F_{\text{veh}} \cdot r$ is the wheel load torque reflected back through gear ratio $G$. **Step 6 — Combine to form the transfer function of the plant (Power side, complete).** Taking the Laplace transform of Step 4 and Step 5 (assuming zero initial conditions): $$ \begin{aligned} V_{\text{cmd}}(s) &= (R_a + L_a s)\, I_a(s) + K_e\, \Omega_m(s) \\ (J_m s + B_m)\, \Omega_m(s) &= K_t\, I_a(s) - \frac{T_{\text{load}}(s)}{G} \end{aligned} $$ Solving the second equation for $I_a(s)$: $$I_a(s) = \frac{1}{K_t}\left[(J_m s + B_m)\, \Omega_m(s) + \frac{T_{\text{load}}(s)}{G}\right]$$ Substitute into the first equation: $$V_{\text{cmd}}(s) = (R_a + L_a s) \cdot \frac{1}{K_t}\left[(J_m s + B_m)\, \Omega_m(s) + \frac{T_{\text{load}}(s)}{G}\right] + K_e\, \Omega_m(s)$$ Grouping $\Omega_m(s)$ terms: $$ \begin{aligned} V_{\text{cmd}}(s) &= \Omega_m(s)\left[\frac{(R_a + L_a s)(J_m s + B_m)}{K_t} + K_e\right] + \frac{(R_a + L_a s)}{K_t\, G}\, T_{\text{load}}(s) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 7 — Identify the plant transfer function (Power side, the "what the control system sees").** The plant transfer function from voltage to speed is: $$G_p(s) = \frac{\Omega_m(s)}{V_{\text{cmd}}(s)}\bigg\vert_{T_{\text{load}}=0} = \frac{K_t}{L_a J_m\, s^2 + (L_a B_m + R_a J_m)\, s + (R_a B_m + K_e K_t)}$$ **Step 8 — Numerically evaluate the plant coefficients (Plug in values).** - $L_a J_m = 0.0005 \cdot 0.05 = 2.5 \times 10^{-5}$ - $L_a B_m + R_a J_m \approx 0.0005 \cdot 0.001 + 0.05 \cdot 0.05 \approx 2.5 \times 10^{-3}$ (assuming $B_m \approx 0.001$) - $R_a B_m + K_e K_t = 0.05 \cdot 0.001 + 1.6 \cdot 1.6 = 0.00005 + 2.56 \approx 2.56$ So the simplified plant is: $$G_p(s) = \frac{1.6}{2.5 \times 10^{-5}\, s^2 + 2.5 \times 10^{-3}\, s + 2.56}$$ **Step 9 — Form the closed-loop transfer function with the PI controller (Control side closing the loop).** The forward path is $G_c(s) \cdot G_p(s)$ where $G_c(s) = K_p + \dfrac{K_i}{s} = \dfrac{0.4 s + 20}{s}$. For unity feedback ($H(s) = 1$): $$T(s) = \frac{G_c(s)\, G_p(s)}{1 + G_c(s)\, G_p(s)}$$ This is the **end-to-end relationship** from pedal (control) to motor speed (power output) — and is the canonical KTU 2024 model for a "drive-by-wire" powertrain. **Step 10 — Physical interpretation (the "why" and "so what").** - The **plant $G_p(s)$** is *second-order, stable, low-bandwidth* — this is the **power system**. - The **controller $G_c(s)$** adds a **pole at origin (integrator)** and a **zero at $-K_i/K_p = -50$ rad/s** — this is the **control system**. - The combined closed loop tracks a pedal step within **~50 ms** (bandwidth ≈ 20 Hz), whereas the open-loop motor alone has a bandwidth of only **~3 Hz**. The control system *increased the system bandwidth by ~7×*. This is the entire justification for the control system. --- ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Key Differences Summarized for Exam Use | Dimension | Power System | Control System | |---|---|---| | **Energy Magnitude** | 10 – 300 kW (high) | 0.001 – 10 W (low) | | **Signal Type** | Continuous, analog (high power) | Discrete, digital (logic levels 0/3.3/5 V) | | **Time Constant** | **0.1 – 10 s** | **0.1 – 10 ms** | | **Bandwidth** | 0.1 – 10 Hz | 10 Hz – 10 kHz | | **Dominant Equation** | $P = Fv$, $P = T\omega$ | $\dot{x} = Ax + Bu$, $y = Cx + Du$ | | **Failure Mode** | Overcurrent, overheating, wear | Bit-flip, sensor dropout, software bug | | **Cooling Need** | Liquid/air cooling (kW heat) | Passive (chip self-heating) | | **Design Constraint** | Thermal limit, weight, cost | Latency, determinism, memory | | **Test Metric** | Efficiency %, range km | Step-response settling time, overshoot % | | **Example ECU Clock** | N/A (mechanical frequency) | 100 – 500 MHz | | **Regulatory Standard** | ECE R85 (net power), ISO 12405 (battery) | ISO 26262 (functional safety) | ## 4. The **red curve** (control) takes **~50 milliseconds** — a fast, oscillatory (but stable) response governed by the closed-loop pole placement. The control response is **~100× faster** than the power response. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]** *State any three fundamental differences between an automotive power system and an automotive control system. Justify each difference with one example component.* **Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per difference):** 1. **Power Level:** Power systems handle high energy (kW to MW range, e.g., a 150 kW traction motor), while control systems handle low-energy signals (mW to W range, e.g., a 5 V PWM signal from an ECU). *(1 mark)* 2. **Time Response / Bandwidth:** Power systems have slow dynamics (time constants of 0.1–10 s, e.g., engine warm-up), while control systems have fast dynamics (1–10 ms time constants, e.g., anti-lock braking loop operating at 50 Hz). *(1 mark)* 3. **Design Objective:** Power systems are designed for *efficiency and durability* (e.g., minimizing $P_{\text{loss}}$ in an inverter), while control systems are designed for *stability, accuracy, and latency* (e.g., PID tuning for a cruise control loop). *(1 mark)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *With a neat block diagram, explain the role of an Electronic Control Unit (ECU) in closing the feedback loop between a driver command and a power actuator in a modern vehicle.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The ECU sits in the **forward path** of the closed-loop control system. It performs three roles: *(1 mark for block diagram, 1 mark for each role)* - **Sensory Interface:** Receives inputs from pedal position sensor, motor speed encoder, current sensor, and temperature sensor. - **Computational Core:** Runs a control algorithm (PID, MPC, or lookup-table based) that computes the actuator command, typically at a loop rate of 1–10 kHz. - **Actuator Driver Interface:** Sends low-power commands (PWM or CAN messages) to power actuators like the inverter, throttle body, or brake modulator. > The ECU itself consumes only **~5–50 W** but commands power systems delivering **>100 kW** — a power amplification ratio exceeding **10,000:1**. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - Students commonly **conflate "power" with "energy"** in definitions — these are *rate* vs. *quantity*; specifying units (W vs. J) is mandatory. > - In comparison answers, examiners award marks only for **explicit contrasting pairs** ("Power is X *whereas* Control is Y"). A one-sided answer loses 30–40% marks. > - Numerical answers **must show units at every step** and final numerical substitution. A formula without a numerical answer scores ≤ 50%. > - Block diagrams drawn in the exam **must have arrowheads** on feedback paths; unlabelled arrows are penalized. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Power System Sizing with Control Implications* > **[KTU University Exam – Sample Module 1, ESE Pattern]** **(a)** A battery-electric vehicle of mass **$m = 1600$ kg** is to cruise at a constant speed of **$v = 90$ km/h** on a flat road. Compute the **total power demand at the wheels** and the **required motor shaft power**, assuming the drivetrain efficiency is **$\eta_d = 0.90$**. Use the following data: rolling resistance coefficient $\mu_{rr} = 0.012$, aerodynamic drag coefficient $C_d = 0.30$, frontal area $A = 2.2$ m², air density $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m³, gravitational acceleration $g = 9.81$ m/s². *(7 marks)* **(b)** Explain how the *control system* of this BEV (specifically the Vehicle Control Unit, VCU) must be designed to maintain this cruising speed despite varying road gradient. Draw the closed-loop block diagram showing at least **one feedback path** and identify the **plant, controller, sensor, and disturbance**. *(7 marks)* --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Part (a) — 7 marks:** **Step 1 — Convert units:** $v = 90$ km/h $= \dfrac{90}{3.6} = 25$ m/s. *(0.5 mark)* **Step 2 — Compute rolling resistance force:** $$F_{\text{roll}} = \mu_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g = 0.012 \cdot 1600 \cdot 9.81 = 188.4 \text{ N}$$ *(1 mark)* **Step 3 — Compute aerodynamic drag force:** $$F_{\text{aero}} = \frac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 1.2 \cdot 0.30 \cdot 2.2 \cdot (25)^2$$ $$F_{\text{aero}} = 0.5 \cdot 1.2 \cdot 0.30 \cdot 2.2 \cdot 625 = 247.5 \text{ N}$$ *(1.5 marks — 1 mark for substitution, 0.5 for answer)* **Step 4 — On a flat road, $\theta = 0$, so grade and acceleration forces are zero.** *(0.5 mark)* **Step 5 — Total tractive force:** $$F_{\text{total}} = F_{\text{roll}} + F_{\text{aero}} = 188.4 + 247.5 = 435.9 \text{ N}$$ *(0.5 mark)* **Step 6 — Power demand at the wheels:** $$P_{\text{wheel}} = F_{\text{total}} \cdot v = 435.9 \cdot 25 = 10{,}897.5 \text{ W} \approx 10.9 \text{ kW}$$ *(1.5 marks)* **Step 7 — Required motor shaft power (accounting for drivetrain loss):** $$P_{\text{motor}} = \frac{P_{\text{wheel}}}{\eta_d} = \frac{10{,}897.5}{0.90} \approx 12{,}108 \text{ W} \approx 12.1 \text{ kW}$$ *(1.5 marks)* **Final Answer (boxed for clarity):** $P_{\text{wheel}} \approx \mathbf{10.9 \text{ kW}}$, $P_{\text{motor}} \approx \mathbf{12.1 \text{ kW}}$. --- **(b) Part (b) — 7 marks:** **Conceptual Answer:** The VCU must implement a **closed-loop speed regulator**. The elements are: *(1 mark for block diagram, rest for explanation)* - **Reference:** Set speed $v_{\text{ref}} = 25$ m/s (from cruise control button or driver). - **Controller:** A PI controller in the VCU, with $K_p \approx 0.5$, $K_i \approx 0.2$ s$^{-1}$ (typical values). - **Actuator:** Traction motor inverter (commands motor torque). - **Plant:** Vehicle longitudinal dynamics $\left(m \frac{dv}{dt} = F_{\text{tractive}} - F_{\text{load}}\right)$. - **Sensor:** Wheel-speed sensor (or GPS-based speed). - **Disturbance:** Road grade $\theta(t)$, which adds an unmeasured load torque. **Block diagram (drawn in exam):** ``` v_ref →(+)→[PI Controller]→τ_cmd→[Inverter + Motor]→F_trac→[1/(ms+b)]→v (−)↑ [Wheel Speed Sensor] ←————————————— v ``` Where $b$ is a damping coefficient combining rolling and aerodynamic losses. The grade $\theta$ enters as a **disturbance** added to $F_{\text{trac}}$ in the plant block. *(2 marks for diagram with correct labels and feedback arrow)* **Controller design rationale:** Because the plant is a first-order integrator with a load disturbance, a PI controller is sufficient — the **integrator action** guarantees zero steady-state speed error even under a constant grade. The bandwidth of the closed loop is set to **~0.5–1 Hz**, well below the actuator bandwidth but above the typical grade variation frequency (~0.1 Hz). *(2 marks)* **Comparison highlight:** Note that the *power system* (motor, battery, transmission) must be physically sized for **peak** demand (acceleration, hill climb, ~100 kW), while the *control system* is sized for *dynamic accuracy* at low power. *(1 mark)* --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Bandwidth and Stability Comparison* > **[KTU University Exam – Internal Choice for Module 1]** **(a)** Define **bandwidth** and **time constant** for a first-order power plant with transfer function $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K}{\tau s + 1}$.
