For a 100 kW traction motor whose electrical time constant is $\tau_e = 5$ ms and mechanical time constant is $\tau_m = 200$ ms, compute the overall plant bandwidth and comment on whether a digital ECU with a 10 ms control loop is fast enough. *(7 marks)* **(b)** With reference to the **closed-loop transfer function** $T(s) = \dfrac{G_c(s) G_p(s)}{1 + G_c(s) G_p(s)}$, explain why adding a *controller* $G_c(s)$ to a *power plant* $G_p(s)$ simultaneously **(i)** increases the system bandwidth and **(ii)** improves disturbance rejection. Use a numerical example of a proportional gain $K_p = 4$ applied to a plant with $G_p(s) = \dfrac{1}{s+1}$ to support your argument. *(7 marks)* --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Part (a) — 7 marks:** **Definitions:** *(1 mark each)* - **Time constant** $\tau$: The time required for the step response to reach $1 - 1/e \approx 63.2\%$ of its final value. It is the inverse of the pole location. - **Bandwidth** $\omega_B$: The frequency at which the magnitude $\vert G(j\omega_B) \vert = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \vert G(0) \vert$, i.e., the $-3$ dB point. For a first-order system, $\omega_B = 1/\tau$. **Step 1 — Identify the dominant time constant.** The plant is second-order; the dominant (slowest) pole determines the bandwidth: $$\tau_{\text{dom}} = \max(\tau_e, \tau_m) = \max(0.005, 0.200) = 0.200 \text{ s}$$ *(1 mark)* **Step 2 — Compute the bandwidth:** $$\omega_B = \frac{1}{\tau_{\text{dom}}} = \frac{1}{0.200} = 5 \text{ rad/s}$$ In Hz: $f_B = \dfrac{5}{2\pi} \approx 0.796$ Hz. *(1.5 marks)* **Step 3 — Compare with ECU loop rate.** ECU loop time = 10 ms → sampling frequency = 100 Hz. By the **Nyquist–Shannon sampling theorem**, the controller can effectively command signals up to ~50 Hz. The plant bandwidth is only ~0.8 Hz, which is *well within* the controller's capability. *(1.5 marks)* **Step 4 — Conclusion:** Yes, the ECU is **comfortably fast enough** — the plant is the **bottleneck**, not the controller. In fact, the ECU has 60× excess bandwidth, which is typical in real systems (this headroom is consumed by sensor filtering, actuator dynamics, and communication delays). *(2 marks)* --- **(b) Part (b) — 7 marks:** **Step 1 — Open-loop plant:** $G_p(s) = \dfrac{1}{s+1}$, so $\omega_{B,\text{OL}} = 1$ rad/s, $\tau_{\text{OL}} = 1$ s. *(0.5 mark)* **Step 2 — Add proportional gain $K_p = 4$.** Forward path: $G_c(s) G_p(s) = \dfrac{4}{s+1}$. *(0.5 mark)* **Step 3 — Closed-loop transfer function:** $$T(s) = \frac{4/(s+1)}{1 + 4/(s+1)} = \frac{4}{s+5}$$ *(1.5 marks)* **Step 4 — Compute new bandwidth and time constant:** $$\omega_{B,\text{CL}} = 5 \text{ rad/s}, \quad \tau_{\text{CL}} = 0.2 \text{ s}$$ Bandwidth increased **5×**, time constant decreased **5×**. *(1 mark)* **Step 5 — Disturbance rejection argument.** For a disturbance $D(s)$ entering at the plant input, the transfer function from $D$ to output is: *(1.5 marks)* $$T_d(s) = \frac{G_p(s)}{1 + G_c(s) G_p(s)} = \frac{1/(s+1)}{1 + 4/(s+1)} = \frac{1}{s+5}$$ Compare to open-loop (no $G_c$): $T_{d,\text{OL}}(s) = G_p(s) = \dfrac{1}{s+1}$. At DC ($s=0$): $T_d(0) = 1/5 = 0.2$ vs. $T_{d,\text{OL}}(0) = 1$. **Disturbance is attenuated by a factor of 5.** *(1 mark)* **Step 6 — Physical interpretation (the "so what"):** - The **power plant** alone is slow and easily perturbed. - Adding a **control system** (even as simple as a proportional gain) *simultaneously* speeds up the response (5× faster tracking) and *rejects* low-frequency disturbances (5× attenuation). - This is the foundational *value proposition* of the control system: it does not add energy, but it *shapes* how the existing power system energy is used. *(1 mark)* --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 1):** > > - [x] **Power System = energy delivery** (kW, kJ, slow dynamics, high magnitude). > - [x] **Control System = energy regulation** (mW, fast dynamics, low magnitude). > - [x] The **power–bandwidth asymmetry** is the central engineering insight: high-power components are slow; low-power controllers are fast. > - [x] Modern vehicles are **cyber-physical systems**: power plant (physical) + ECU + sensors (cyber) in closed loop. > - [x] The **canonical feedback block diagram** is: Reference → Controller → Actuator → Plant → Output, with Sensor feedback. > - [x] **Plant transfer function** for a DC motor: $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_t}{L_a J_m s^2 + (L_a B_m + R_a J_m) s + (R_a B_m + K_e K_t)}$ — second-order, stable. > - [x] **PI/PID controllers** add integrator action for zero steady-state error and a zero for phase margin. > - [x] **Bandwidth** of closed loop $\approx$ bandwidth of open loop $\times (1 + K_p)$ (approx, for proportional control). > - [x] **Vehicle power demand** = $F_{\text{roll}} + F_{\text{aero}} + F_{\text{grade}} + F_{\text{acc}}$. > - [x] Rolling resistance: $F_{\text{roll}} = \mu_{rr} m g \cos\theta$. > - [x] Aerodynamic drag: $F_{\text{aero}} = \frac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2$. > - [x] **A 1.5-tonne car at 100 km/h needs ~15 kW** — a useful rule-of-thumb for sanity checks. > - [x] **ECE R85** governs power system measurement; **ISO 26262** governs control system safety. > - [x] The **power-to-control ratio** in modern vehicles typically exceeds **10,000:1**. > - [x] **Disturbance rejection** improves by a factor of $(1 + K_p)$ for low-frequency disturbances when a proportional controller is added. > - [x] **Time constant $\tau$ and bandwidth $\omega_B$** are reciprocals for a first-order system. > - [x] The **Nyquist criterion** ensures digital ECUs must sample at $\geq 2\times$ the highest frequency of interest.

topics.title: Different Modes of Operation for Best Performance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT744 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Different Modes of Operation for Best Performance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Modern Automotive Technologies (PEMUT744)**, the term **"Different Modes of Operation for Best Performance"** refers to the integrated, electronically-controlled drivetrain and powertrain calibration strategies that allow a modern vehicle to dynamically switch between multiple driving profiles — commonly labelled as **Eco (Economy)**, **Normal/Comfort**, **Sport**, and **Off-Road/Snow/Mud** modes — to optimize the trade-off between **fuel efficiency, drivability, emissions, safety, and dynamic performance** based on driver input, road conditions, and load demand.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Reference):**
> *"Modes of operation refer to driver-selectable or automatically-selected vehicle operating states wherein the Engine Control Unit (ECU), Transmission Control Unit (TCU), and other subsystems cooperatively alter their control maps to achieve best-in-class performance metrics — fuel economy, power delivery, ride comfort, or traction — for the prevailing condition."*

The concept operates on three pillars:
1. **Powertrain Re-mapping** (throttle response, ignition timing, fuel injection quantity, boost pressure).
2. **Transmission Shift Logic Re-programming** (gear selection points, shift firmness, torque converter lock-up schedule).
3. **Chassis & Auxiliary Subsystem Reconfiguration** (steering effort, suspension damping, AWD torque distribution, ESC thresholds).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **riding a bicycle**. When you ride uphill, you switch to a **low gear** so your legs don't tire out — that's an *Eco-like mode* prioritizing effort conservation. When you race your friend downhill on a flat road, you shift to a **high gear and pedal faster** to maximize speed — that's the *Sport mode* prioritizing performance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Simple Analogy:** A modern car with driving modes is like a **smartphone camera** that has "Portrait," "Night," and "Sports" modes — each mode internally re-tunes shutter speed, ISO, and focus algorithms to deliver the best possible photo for that specific scenario. The driver simply presses a button; the **ECU, TCU, and ESC** do the complex internal re-calibration.

The objective is **multi-objective optimization**: no single mode can simultaneously deliver maximum fuel economy AND maximum acceleration AND maximum comfort. So the vehicle offers **discrete selectable modes** that re-prioritize these competing objectives.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Mode Selection

The following engineering metrics define "best performance" in each mode:

- **Fuel Consumption (L/100 km)** — Economy benchmark.
- **0–100 km/h Acceleration Time (seconds)** — Sport benchmark.
- **CO₂ Emissions (g/km)** — Regulatory benchmark.
- **Steering Effort (Nm)** — Comfort benchmark.
- **Shift Point RPM (rpm)** — Defines transmission behavior.
- **Throttle Sensitivity (% pedal vs % torque)** — Defines driveability.
- **Standard constant:** Air-Fuel Ratio (stoichiometric) **λ = 14.7:1** for petrol engines.
- **Standard constant:** Diesel excess air factor **λ ≈ 1.2–1.6**.

## 1.4 Why Are Modes Necessary?

A single fixed engine/transmission calibration is a **compromise**. With a single map:
- It is **never optimal** for city traffic (stop-and-go).
- It is **never optimal** for highway cruising.
- It is **never optimal** for spirited mountain driving.
- It is **never optimal** for slippery/wet roads.

Driving modes resolve this by offering **multiple pre-calibrated maps** switched via drive-by-wire signals.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of a Mode-Operated Vehicle

A mode-operated vehicle follows a **centralized control architecture** with a hierarchy:

| Layer | Controller | Function in Mode Operation |
|---|---|---|
| Top (Driver) | Driver Input Button / Voice | Mode selection request |
| Mid (Coordinator) | BCM / Central Gateway | Broadcasts mode over CAN bus |
| Powertrain | ECU + TCU | Re-maps engine + gearbox |
| Chassis | ESC + EPS + Suspension | Re-tunes stability, steering, damping |
| Drivetrain | AWD Coupling ECU (if AWD) | Re-distributes torque |
| Auxiliary | HVAC, Lighting, Audio | Minor load-shedding in Eco mode |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Communication Protocol:** The mode-selection signal is transmitted across the vehicle via the **CAN (Controller Area Network) bus** at standard baud rates of **125 kbps, 500 kbps, or 1 Mbps**, depending on the bus segment.

## 2.2 Detailed Mode-by-Mode Breakdown

### A. Eco Mode (Economy)
- **Throttle map:** De-sensitized (pedal travel produces less torque).
- **Shift map:** Early up-shifts, late down-shifts → keeps engine at low RPM.
- **Ignition timing:** Retarded slightly for better part-load fuel economy.
- **A/C compressor:** Allowed to disengage at full throttle (load shedding).
- **Stop-Start:** Aggressive — engine shuts off at traffic lights.
- **Regenerative Braking:** Maximized in hybrids/EVs.

### B. Normal / Comfort Mode
- **Throttle map:** Linear and progressive.
- **Shift map:** Balanced — economy-leaning but with adequate response.
- **Suspension:** Soft damping for ride comfort.
- **Steering:** Light servo assistance.
- **Best for:** Daily commuting, mixed conditions.

### C. Sport Mode
- **Throttle map:** Sharpened — small pedal input = big torque response.
- **Shift map:** Later up-shifts, earlier down-shifts → keeps engine in power band (typically **>3000 rpm** for turbo-petrol).
- **Ignition timing:** Advanced (closer to MBT — Minimum spark advance for Best Torque).
- **Exhaust valve:** Open for reduced back-pressure.
- **Steering:** Heavier, more road feel.
- **Suspension:** Stiffer damping.
- **AWD (if present):** Rear-biased torque split.

### D. Off-Road / Snow / Mud Mode
- **Traction control:** Maximum intervention — high slip threshold.
- **Throttle map:** Very gentle.
- **AWD:** Locked 50:50 torque split.
- **ABS:** Permits wheel locking for "trac" buildup in snow.
- **Hill Descent Control:** Active.
- **Transmission:** Low-range gear ratios engaged.

### E. Individual / Custom Mode (Premium Segment)
- Driver selects each parameter independently (e.g., Sport engine + Comfort suspension).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Relation | Symbol Meaning | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}}$ | Thermal efficiency | Petrol ≈ **25–35%**, Diesel ≈ **35–45%** |
| $BSFC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{f}}{P_{brake}}$ | Brake Specific Fuel Consumption | Petrol ≈ **240–280 g/kWh** |
| $\lambda = \dfrac{(A/F)_{actual}}{(A/F)_{stoich}}$ | Relative Air-Fuel Ratio | λ = **1.0** stoich; λ > 1 lean |
| $T_{engine} = \dfrac{P \cdot 60}{2\pi \cdot N}$ | Engine torque from power | Nm |
| $\tau_{throttle} = f(\alpha_{pedal}, Mode)$ | Driver demanded torque as function of pedal angle & mode | **Mode-dependent map** |
| $v_{shift} = v(N, Mode)$ | Shift point speed as function of RPM and mode | km/h |
| $P_{boost} = f(Mode) \cdot P_{max}$ | Boost pressure scaling factor | Eco: **0.7**, Sport: **1.0** |
| $T_{AWD,rear} = k_{mode} \cdot T_{total}$ | Rear torque fraction | Comfort: **0.2**, Sport: **0.4–0.5** |
| $\dot{Q}_{regen} = \dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2} \eta_{regen}$ | Regenerative braking energy recovery | kWh |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Condition for Engine Mode Switching:**
> Mode changeover is permitted only when:
> 1. Vehicle speed $v < v_{max,switch}$ (typically **< 120 km/h**).
> 2. Engine torque demand $T_{demand} < T_{threshold}$ (typically **< 80% of $T_{max}$**).
> 3. No active fault code (DTC) in any powertrain controller.
> 4. Brake pedal not actively pressed (for safety).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Emissions Compliance:** Eco mode helps meet **BS-VI / Euro 6** fleet average CO₂ targets (95 g/km by 2025 in EU).
- **Driver Engagement:** Sport mode is a **marketing differentiator** in premium brands (BMW, Audi, Mercedes-AMG).
- **Safety:** Off-road/Snow modes prevent accidents by adapting ESC intervention to low-µ surfaces.
- **EV Efficiency:** In EVs, mode selection can extend range by **15–25%** via aggressive regen and torque limiters.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Effective Torque Demand in Different Modes

The driver's pedal input $\alpha \in [0, 1]$ is mapped to a torque demand $T_d$ through a **mode-dependent pedal map** $f_M(\alpha)$. A standard model is:

$$
T_d(\alpha, M) = T_{max} \cdot f_M(\alpha)
$$

where $M \in \{Eco, Normal, Sport\}$ is the mode index.

A commonly used **piecewise-linear pedal map** for each mode:

$$
f_M(\alpha) = \begin{cases} k_M \cdot \alpha & \text{for } 0 \le \alpha \le \alpha_1 \\[4pt] k_M \cdot \alpha_1 + m_M (\alpha - \alpha_1) & \text{for } \alpha_1 < \alpha \le 1 \end{cases}
$$

Typical values for the slope parameters:

| Mode | $k_M$ (low pedal slope) | $m_M$ (high pedal slope) |
|---|---|---|
| Eco | **0.40** | **0.80** |
| Normal | **0.55** | **0.90** |
| Sport | **0.75** | **1.00** |

### Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A turbo-petrol engine has $T_{max} = 320$ Nm. A driver presses the pedal to $\alpha = 0.6$ in Sport mode. Calculate the demanded torque. Assume $\alpha_1 = 0.4$, $k_{Sport} = 0.75$, $m_{Sport} = 1.00$.

**Step 1:** Check which branch of the piecewise function applies.
Since $\alpha = 0.6 > \alpha_1 = 0.4$, we use the **second branch** of $f_M(\alpha)$.

**Step 2:** Substitute values into the second branch formula.

$$
f_{Sport}(0.6) = k_{Sport} \cdot \alpha_1 + m_{Sport} \cdot (\alpha - \alpha_1)
$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate numerically.

$$
f_{Sport}(0.6) = (0.75)(0.4) + (1.00)(0.6 - 0.4)
$$

**Step 4:** Continue evaluation.

$$
f_{Sport}(0.6) = 0.30 + 1.00 \cdot 0.20
$$

**Step 5:** Final intermediate result.

$$
f_{Sport}(0.6) = 0.30 + 0.20 = 0.50
$$

**Step 6:** Compute demanded torque.

$$
T_d = T_{max} \cdot f_{Sport}(0.6) = 320 \cdot 0.50 = 160 \text{ Nm}
$$

**Result:** The demanded torque in Sport mode at $\alpha = 0.6$ is **160 Nm**.

### Comparison: Same Pedal Position Across Modes

Now evaluate the same $\alpha = 0.6$ in Eco and Normal modes for comparison.

**Eco mode:**
$$
f_{Eco}(0.6) = (0.40)(0.4) + (0.80)(0.2) = 0.16 + 0.16 = 0.32
$$

$$
T_{d,Eco} = 320 \cdot 0.32 = 102.4 \text{ Nm}
$$

**Normal mode:**
$$
f_{Normal}(0.6) = (0.55)(0.4) + (0.90)(0.2) = 0.22 + 0.18 = 0.40
$$

$$
T_{d,Normal} = 320 \cdot 0.40 = 128.0 \text{ Nm}
$$

**Conclusion:** For the **same pedal position**, Sport mode delivers **56% more torque** than Eco mode. This is the mathematical basis of "throttle sharpening" in Sport mode.

## 3.2 Derivation: Fuel Economy Gain in Eco Mode

Fuel consumption is approximately proportional to engine load (brake mean effective pressure). In Eco mode, torque demand is scaled down by factor $k_{Eco} / k_{Sport} = 0.40/0.75 = 0.533$ at low pedal, and $0.80/1.00 = 0.80$ at high pedal.

Average fuel consumption scaling in Eco mode relative to Sport:

$$
\Phi_{Eco/Sport} = \dfrac{1}{2}\left(\dfrac{k_{Eco}}{k_{Sport}} + \dfrac{m_{Eco}}{m_{Sport}}\right) = \dfrac{1}{2}\left(\dfrac{0.40}{0.75} + \dfrac{0.80}{1.00}\right)
$$

$$
\Phi_{Eco/Sport} = \dfrac{1}{2}(0.533 + 0.800) = \dfrac{1}{2}(1.333) = 0.667
$$

Thus, **Eco mode reduces average fueling demand to ~66.7%** of Sport mode → approximately **33% fuel economy improvement** in mixed driving.

## 3.3 Derivation: Shift RPM Comparison

Transmission up-shift speed is often modeled as:

$$
v_{shift}(M) = v_0 + c_M \cdot \dfrac{P_{engine}(N)}{M_{vehicle} \cdot g}
$$

where $v_0$ is a base speed and $c_M$ is a mode coefficient.

- Eco: $c_{Eco} = 0.7$ (early shift)
- Normal: $c_{Normal} = 1.0$
- Sport: $c_{Sport} = 1.4$ (late shift, higher RPM)

At $N = 2000$ rpm, $P_{engine} = 50$ kW, $M_{vehicle} \cdot g = 15000$ N:

$$
v_{shift}(Eco) = v_0 + 0.7 \cdot \dfrac{50000}{15000} = v_0 + 2.33 \text{ m/s} \approx v_0 + 8.4 \text{ km/h}
$$

$$
v_{shift}(Sport) = v_0 + 1.4 \cdot \dfrac{50000}{15000} = v_0 + 4.67 \text{ m/s} \approx v_0 + 16.8 \text{ km/h}
$$

**Sport mode shifts up ~8.4 km/h later** than Eco — keeping engine in the **power band** for stronger acceleration.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Vehicle Mode Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Driver Mode Selector Button] --> B[Body Control Module BCM]
    B --> C1[CAN Bus Network]
    C1 --> D1[Engine Control Unit ECU]
    C1 --> D2[Transmission Control Unit TCU]
    C1 --> D3[Electronic Stability Control ESC]
    C1 --> D4[Electric Power Steering EPS]
    C1 --> D5[Suspension Control Module]
    C1 --> D6[AWD Coupling ECU]
    D1 --> E1[Throttle Body Calibration]
    D1 --> E2[Ignition Timing Map]
    D1 --> E3[Turbo Boost Pressure]
    D2 --> F1[Shift Point Logic]
    D2 --> F2[Torque Converter Lockup Map]
    D3 --> G1[Wheel Slip Threshold]
    D4 --> G2[Steering Effort Map]
    D5 --> G3[Damper Stiffness Map]
    D6 --> G4[Torque Split Front Rear]
    E1 --> H[Integrated Vehicle Response]
    E2 --> H
    E3 --> H
    F1 --> H
    F2 --> H
    G1 --> H
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    G4 --> H
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Decision Flow: Mode Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Driver Presses Mode Button] --> B{Vehicle Speed Below Limit}
    B -- No --> X[Reject Mode Change Display Fault]
    B -- Yes --> C{Engine Torque Demand Low}
    C -- No --> X
    C -- Yes --> D{No Active DTC Fault Code}
    D -- No --> X
    D -- Yes --> E{Brake Pedal Released}
    E -- No --> X
    E -- Yes --> F[Activate New Mode]
    F --> G1[Update ECU Throttle Map]
    F --> G2[Update TCU Shift Map]
    F --> G3[Update ESC Slip Threshold]
    F --> G4[Update EPS Steering Effort]
    F --> G5[Update AWD Torque Split]
    G1 --> Z[Driver Feels New Vehicle Character]
    G2 --> Z
    G3 --> Z
    G4 --> Z
    G5 --> Z
```

## 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram: Driving Mode State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Normal
    Normal --> Eco: Driver Selects Eco
    Normal --> Sport: Driver Selects Sport
    Normal --> OffRoad: Driver Selects OffRoad
    Eco --> Normal: Driver Selects Normal
    Sport --> Normal: Driver Selects Normal
    OffRoad --> Normal: Driver Selects Normal
    Eco --> Sport: Direct Eco to Sport Allowed
    Sport --> Eco: Direct Sport to Eco Allowed
    OffRoad --> Eco: OffRoad to Eco Allowed
    OffRoad --> Sport: OffRoad to Sport Requires Confirmation
    Sport --> OffRoad: Not Allowed Above 40 km/h
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Schematic: Torque Flow in Sport Mode (AWD)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Engine 320 Nm] --> B[Torque Converter]
    B --> C[Automatic Transmission]
    C --> D1[Front Differential]
    C --> D2[Transfer Case 40 60 Split Sport]
    D1 --> E1[Front Left Wheel]
    D1 --> E2[Front Right Wheel]
    D2 --> F1[Rear Differential]
    F1 --> E3[Rear Left Wheel]
    F1 --> E4[Rear Right Wheel]
    E1 --> G[Net Vehicle Acceleration Improved]
    E2 --> G
    E3 --> G
    E4 --> G
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Similar]`
**Explain the need for different driving modes in modern automobiles.**

**Model Answer:**
Modern vehicles must operate across vastly different conditions — dense city traffic, open highways, mountain passes, and slippery surfaces. A single fixed powertrain calibration is a compromise that performs sub-optimally in all these scenarios. Different driving modes (Eco, Normal, Sport, Off-Road) allow the **ECU, TCU, ESC, and EPS** to switch between pre-calibrated maps that re-prioritize **fuel economy, acceleration response, ride comfort, or traction**. This delivers **best-in-class performance** for the driver's chosen context without needing multiple mechanical setups. *(3 marks)*

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Similar]`
**List any four parameters that change when switching from Eco mode to Sport mode.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Throttle pedal mapping** becomes more sensitive (sharper response).
2. **Transmission shift points** move to higher RPM (later up-shifts, earlier down-shifts).
3. **Engine ignition timing** advances towards MBT (Minimum spark advance for Best Torque).
4. **Steering effort** increases for better road feel; suspension damping stiffens. *(3 marks)*

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the **architecture of a mode-operated vehicle powertrain control system**. Discuss how the CAN bus enables coordination between the ECU, TCU, and ESC during a mode change. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A turbocharged petrol engine has a maximum torque of **350 Nm**. The driver presses the accelerator pedal to **60% travel** in **Sport mode**. Using the pedal map below, compute the demanded torque.

$$
f_{Sport}(\alpha) = \begin{cases} 0.75 \alpha & 0 \le \alpha \le 0.4 \\ 0.30 + 1.00(\alpha - 0.4) & 0.4 < \alpha \le 1 \end{cases}
$$

Compare this with the torque demanded at the **same pedal position in Eco mode** where $k_{Eco} = 0.40$, $m_{Eco} = 0.80$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**
The mode-operated powertrain follows a **layered architecture**:

1. **Driver Interface Layer:** Mode selector button, voice command, or touchscreen sends a digital request to the **Body Control Module (BCM)**.

2. **Coordination Layer:** BCM validates the request against boundary conditions (vehicle speed, brake pedal state, fault codes). On approval, BCM broadcasts a **CAN message** with the new mode ID to the high-speed CAN bus at **500 kbps**.

3. **Subsystem Response Layer:**
   - **ECU** receives mode ID → swaps throttle map, ignition map, boost map from ROM.
   - **TCU** receives mode ID → swaps shift schedule and torque-converter lockup map.
   - **ESC** receives mode ID → re-tunes wheel-slip intervention thresholds.

4. **Actuator Layer:** Each controller commands its actuators (throttle blade angle, ignition coils, gear solenoids, brake hydraulic modulators).

5. **Feedback Layer:** Sensors report actual values back via CAN; ECU performs **closed-loop correction** if actual values deviate from demanded.

**Block Diagram:**
```
[Driver Button] → [BCM] → [CAN Bus 500 kbps] → [ECU / TCU / ESC] → [Actuators]
```
The CAN bus uses an **11-bit message identifier** with the mode ID encoded in the data field. All controllers listen to the bus and update their local maps within a single **CAN cycle (≈ 2 ms)**. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1:** Identify branch.
$\alpha = 0.6 > 0.4$, so use second branch of Sport map.

**Step 2:** Compute $f_{Sport}(0.6)$.
$$
f_{Sport}(0.6) = 0.30 + 1.00(0.6 - 0.4) = 0.30 + 0.20 = 0.50
$$

**Step 3:** Compute demanded torque in Sport mode.
$$
T_{d,Sport} = 350 \times 0.50 = 175 \text{ Nm}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute $f_{Eco}(0.6)$ using the same branch structure.
$$
f_{Eco}(0.6) = (0.40)(0.4) + (0.80)(0.6 - 0.4) = 0.16 + 0.16 = 0.32
$$

**Step 5:** Compute demanded torque in Eco mode.
$$
T_{d,Eco} = 350 \times 0.32 = 112 \text{ Nm}
$$

**Step 6:** Compute the percentage difference.
$$
\Delta T = \dfrac{175 - 112}{112} \times 100\% = 56.25\%
$$

**Final Result:** Sport mode delivers **175 Nm** vs Eco's **112 Nm** at the same pedal position — a **56.25% higher torque demand**, which translates directly into sharper acceleration. *(7 marks)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 - Alternative]`

**(a)** Explain the working of **Eco mode** and **Sport mode** in a modern automatic-transmission vehicle. Describe the specific changes made to **(i) throttle mapping, (ii) gear-shift schedule, (iii) ignition timing, and (iv) suspension damping**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive an expression for the **fuel-consumption scaling factor** between Eco and Sport modes using the linear pedal-map model. Given a vehicle that returns **12 km/L in Sport mode**, estimate the mileage improvement in Eco mode assuming typical map coefficients. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Eco Mode:**
- **Function:** Maximizes fuel economy and minimizes emissions.
- **(i) Throttle mapping:** De-sensitized — same pedal position produces less torque. Driver must press deeper to access power.
- **(ii) Gear-shift schedule:** Early up-shifts (around **1500–1800 rpm**) and very late down-shifts. Keeps engine at the lowest efficient RPM.
- **(iii) Ignition timing:** Slightly retarded from MBT during part-load cruise, advancing only at high loads.
- **(iv) Suspension:** Soft damping for comfort; minor effect on fuel economy.

**Sport Mode:**
- **Function:** Maximizes acceleration response and driver engagement.
- **(i) Throttle mapping:** Sharpened — small pedal movement yields large torque. Used to mask turbo lag.
- **(ii) Gear-shift schedule:** Late up-shifts (around **2500–3500 rpm**) and early down-shifts. Holds engine in power band.
- **(iii) Ignition timing:** Advanced close to MBT for maximum torque per combustion cycle.
- **(iv) Suspension:** Stiffer damping reduces body roll during cornering. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1:** Recall the pedal map model.
$$
f_M(\alpha) = k_M \alpha \text{ for } \alpha \in [0, \alpha_1], \quad f_M(\alpha) = k_M \alpha_1 + m_M(\alpha - \alpha_1) \text{ for } \alpha \in (\alpha_1, 1]
$$

**Step 2:** Assume uniform distribution of pedal positions over $[0,1]$. The mean pedal-map value is:
$$
\bar{f}_M = \int_0^1 f_M(\alpha) \, d\alpha = \int_0^{\alpha_1} k_M \alpha \, d\alpha + \int_{\alpha_1}^1 [k_M \alpha_1 + m_M(\alpha - \alpha_1)] \, d\alpha
$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the first integral.
$$
\int_0^{\alpha_1} k_M \alpha \, d\alpha = \dfrac{k_M \alpha_1^2}{2}
$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the second integral.
$$
\int_{\alpha_1}^1 k_M \alpha_1 \, d\alpha + \int_{\alpha_1}^1 m_M(\alpha - \alpha_1) \, d\alpha = k_M \alpha_1 (1 - \alpha_1) + \dfrac{m_M (1 - \alpha_1)^2}{2}
$$

**Step 5:** Combine.
$$
\bar{f}_M = \dfrac{k_M \alpha_1^2}{2} + k_M \alpha_1 (1 - \alpha_1) + \dfrac{m_M (1 - \alpha_1)^2}{2}
$$

**Step 6:** Compute scaling factor $\Phi = \bar{f}_{Eco} / \bar{f}_{Sport}$. With $\alpha_1 = 0.4$, $k_{Eco}=0.40$, $m_{Eco}=0.80$, $k_{Sport}=0.75$, $m_{Sport}=1.00$:

$$
\bar{f}_{Eco} = \dfrac{(0.40)(0.16)}{2} + (0.40)(0.4)(0.6) + \dfrac{(0.80)(0.36)}{2}
$$

$$
\bar{f}_{Eco} = 0.032 + 0.096 + 0.144 = 0.272
$$

$$
\bar{f}_{Sport} = \dfrac{(0.75)(0.16)}{2} + (0.75)(0.4)(0.6) + \dfrac{(1.00)(0.36)}{2}
$$

$$
\bar{f}_{Sport} = 0.060 + 0.180 + 0.180 = 0.420
$$

**Step 7:** Compute scaling factor.
$$
\Phi = \dfrac{0.272}{0.420} = 0.648
$$

**Step 8:** Estimate Eco-mode mileage.
$$
\text{Mileage}_{Eco} = \dfrac{12 \text{ km/L}}{0.648} \approx 18.5 \text{ km/L}
$$

**Step 9:** Compute percentage improvement.
$$
\text{Improvement} = \dfrac{18.5 - 12}{12} \times 100\% \approx 54.3\%
$$

**Final Result:** Eco mode offers approximately **54% higher fuel economy** than Sport mode under identical pedal-input statistics — a strong justification for driver adoption. *(7 marks)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. Students frequently forget to mention the **boundary conditions** (vehicle speed, brake pedal, fault-code checks) — losing **1–2 marks**.
> 2. In numerical problems, students confuse which **branch of the piecewise map** to use — re-check whether $\alpha > \alpha_1$ or $\alpha \le \alpha_1$ BEFORE substituting.
> 3. Forgetting the **unit (Nm)** in the final torque answer is a common 0.5-mark deduction.
> 4. Students sometimes write "Eco mode saves fuel" without **quantifying** it — always include a percentage or numerical comparison.
> 5. In derivations, skipping the **integration step explanation** loses marks; show every algebraic transition.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist:**
> - Modes of operation = **driver-selectable calibration states** of the vehicle.
> - Three pillars: **powertrain, transmission, chassis** re-mapping.
> - Communication backbone: **CAN bus** at 125/500/1000 kbps.
> - **Eco Mode:** De-sensitized throttle, early up-shifts, retarded timing, A/C load shedding, regen maximization.
> - **Sport Mode:** Sharpened throttle, late up-shifts, MBT timing, stiffer damping, rear-biased AWD.
> - **Off-Road/Snow Mode:** High ESC slip threshold, 50:50 AWD lock, hill-descent control active.
> - **Boundary conditions** for mode switching: $v < v_{max}$, $T_{demand} < T_{thresh}$, no DTC, brake released.
> - **Stoichiometric A/F ratio:** $\lambda = 14.7:1$ (petrol); $\lambda \approx 1.2–1.6$ for diesel.
> - **Typical fuel-economy gain** in Eco vs Sport: **30–55%** (mode of pedal distribution dependent).
> - **Standard pedal-map model:** piecewise linear with breakpoint at $\alpha_1 \approx 0.3–0.4$.
> - **KTU emphasis:** Always quantify answers — provide numbers, percentages, and units; examiners reward **specificity**.
> - **Premium feature:** Individual/Custom mode allows per-parameter user tuning (BMW, Audi, Porsche).
> - **EV-specific:** Modes also govern regen braking aggressiveness and motor torque limits; range can improve by **15–25%** in Eco.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wireless Charging
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT744 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Wireless Charging

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wireless Charging (Inductive Charging) for Electric Vehicles (EVs)** is a non-contact method of transmitting electrical energy from a ground-based (or roadway-embedded) transmitter pad to a vehicle-mounted receiver pad using the principle of **near-field resonant electromagnetic induction** (or magnetic resonance coupling), as standardized under **SAE J2954**.

In formal KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, Wireless Power Transfer (WPT) for automotive applications is the controlled transfer of electrical energy across an air gap (typically **100 mm – 250 mm**) without any physical conductor-to-conductor contact, relying instead on time-varying magnetic flux linking two magnetically coupled coils.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Singing Glass"**
> Imagine two wine glasses sitting on a table. If you run a wet finger around the rim of *Glass A*, it begins to hum at a specific resonant frequency. Hold *Glass B* (of identical size) nearby, and it will *spontaneously* start humming too — without any physical touch. This happens because the air carries acoustic resonance from one glass to the other.

**Wireless EV charging works on the exact same principle**, but with **magnetic fields instead of sound waves**:
- **Glass A (Transmitter Pad on the ground)** → carries high-frequency alternating current (typically **85 kHz**, per SAE J2954), producing an oscillating magnetic field.
- **Glass B (Receiver Pad on the car)** → tuned to the same resonant frequency. The oscillating magnetic field "induces" a voltage in it, which is then rectified to charge the battery.

The "air gap" is the space between the road and the car's underbody (the chassis clearance).

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology & Standard Metrics

| Term | Definition / Value |
|---|---|
| **Air Gap** | Vertical distance between transmitter and receiver coil (**100 – 250 mm**) |
| **Operating Frequency** | **85 kHz** (band defined by SAE J2954 for light-duty EVs) |
| **Power Levels (WPT 1–3)** | **3.7 kW (WPT1), 7.7 kW (WPT2), 11 kW (WPT3)** |
| **Efficiency (η)** | Target ≥ **85%** at nominal alignment (end-to-end AC-to-DC) |
| **Coupling Coefficient (k)** | Fraction of flux from primary coil that links secondary coil; typically **0.1 – 0.4** in EV WPT (loose coupling) |
| **Quality Factor (Q)** | Ratio of stored reactive energy to dissipated energy per cycle |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SAE J2954** is the **single most important standard** for KTU exams. Memorize: Frequency = **85 kHz**, three power classes, and the term "**interoperability**" (one ground pad must charge any compliant vehicle).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics — Step-by-Step Operating Principle

The wireless charging process can be decomposed into **five distinct stages**:

1. **AC Mains Input → High-Frequency Inverter**
   The grid supply (50 Hz, single/three-phase AC) is rectified to DC and then fed to a high-frequency inverter. This produces a high-frequency AC waveform (typically **85 kHz**).

2. **Compensation Network on Primary Side**
   Capacitors are added in series or parallel with the primary coil to form a **resonant LC tank circuit**. This dramatically reduces reactive power consumption and boosts the circulating current.

3. **Magnetic Flux Generation (Primary Coil)**
   The high-frequency AC current in the primary coil produces a **time-varying magnetic flux** (Φ) according to the **Right-Hand Grip Rule**. The flux lines project upward through the air gap.

4. **Inductive Coupling Across the Air Gap**
   A fraction of this flux links the secondary coil. By **Faraday's Law**, an EMF is induced in the secondary coil.

5. **Rectification, Filtering & Battery Charging (Secondary Side)**
   The induced high-frequency AC is rectified (AC → DC), filtered (smoothing capacitor), and regulated by a **battery management system (BMS)** before charging the high-voltage traction battery.

---

## 2.2 The Four Compensation Topologies (High-Yield for KTU)

| Topology | Primary Cap | Secondary Cap | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **SS (Series-Series)** | Series | Series | Constant-voltage loads; **most common in EV WPT** |
| **SP (Series-Parallel)** | Series | Parallel | High-misalignment tolerance |
| **PS (Parallel-Series)** | Parallel | Series | Current-source behavior |
| **PP (Parallel-Parallel)** | Parallel | Parallel | Rarely used in EV WPT |

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU answers, **SS (Series-Series)** is the *default safe answer* — it is load-independent when tuned properly and is overwhelmingly used in commercial EV wireless chargers (e.g., WiTricity, Qualcomm Halo).

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **high-yield formula bank** for this topic. Memorize the symbols and the boundary conditions.

| # | Formula | LaTeX | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Faraday's Law of Induction | $\varepsilon = -N \dfrac{d\Phi_B}{dt}$ | EMF induced in receiver coil |
| 2 | Mutual Inductance | $M = k\sqrt{L_1 L_2}$ | Magnetic linkage between two coils |
| 3 | Coupling Coefficient | $k = \dfrac{M}{\sqrt{L_1 L_2}}$ | **Range: 0 (no coupling) to 1 (ideal/transformer)** |
| 4 | Induced Voltage in Secondary | $V_2 = j\omega M I_1$ | Receiver voltage from primary current |
| 5 | Resonant Frequency | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ | Tuning condition for LC tank |
| 6 | Transferred Power (approx.) | $P = \omega M I_1 I_2$ | Power flowing through the magnetic link |
| 7 | Power Transfer Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\%$ | Target ≥ 85% (SAE J2954) |
| 8 | Quality Factor | $Q = \dfrac{\omega L}{R}$ | Higher Q → sharper resonance |
| 9 | Coil-to-Coil Efficiency | $\eta_{cc} = \dfrac{k^2 Q_1 Q_2}{(1+\sqrt{1+k^2 Q_1 Q_2})^2}$ | (MIT/Soljačić resonance formula) |
| 10 | Magnetic Flux Density Limit (ICNIRP) | $B \leq 27\ \mu T$ (rms, public exposure, 85 kHz) | Human safety threshold |

> **Notation Key:** $\omega = 2\pi f$ is the angular frequency, $L$ is inductance, $M$ is mutual inductance, $k$ is coupling coefficient, $Q$ is quality factor.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Elimination of wear & tear** — No mechanical connector, no sparking, weatherproof operation under rain/snow.
- **Autonomous fleet charging** — Robotaxis (e.g., autonomous EVs) can self-align over charging pads without human intervention.
- **Dynamic charging on highways** — Future "Electric Roads" in Korea, Sweden, and Israel are testing on-the-move charging at speeds up to **80 km/h**, eliminating range anxiety entirely.
- **Underwater / hazardous environments** — Same technology is used to charge submarines, mining equipment, and medical implants.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Induced EMF and Transferred Power in a Two-Coil WPT System

> **Goal:** Derive the expression for the EMF induced in the secondary (receiver) coil of an EV wireless charger, starting from Faraday's Law.

### Step 1 — State Faraday's Law of Induction
The fundamental law governing wireless charging is Faraday's Law. For a coil of $N$ turns, the induced EMF is the negative rate of change of magnetic flux linkage.

$$
\varepsilon = -N \frac{d\Phi_B}{dt}
$$

> *Interpretation:* The minus sign (Lenz's Law) tells us the induced EMF always opposes the change in flux — this is the reason the receiver coil experiences a counter-EMF, which must be overcome by the transmitter.

### Step 2 — Express the Primary Coil's Time-Varying Flux
Let the primary coil carry a sinusoidal current $i_1(t) = I_1 \sin(\omega t)$. By the right-hand rule, this produces a flux $\Phi_1$ at the receiver location. For a linear, time-invariant system, the flux linking the secondary coil is proportional to $i_1$ through the mutual inductance $M$:

$$
\Phi_{21} = \frac{M \cdot i_1(t)}{N_2}
$$

where $M$ is the mutual inductance in henries (H), and $N_2$ is the number of turns in the secondary coil.

### Step 3 — Substitute into Faraday's Law
Now apply Faraday's Law to the secondary coil:

$$
\varepsilon_2 = -N_2 \frac{d\Phi_{21}}{dt}
$$

Substituting Step 2:

$$
\varepsilon_2 = -N_2 \cdot \frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{M \cdot i_1(t)}{N_2}\right)
$$

$$
\varepsilon_2 = -M \frac{di_1(t)}{dt}
$$

### Step 4 — Differentiate the Sinusoidal Current
The derivative of $\sin(\omega t)$ is $\omega \cos(\omega t)$. So:

$$
\varepsilon_2 = -M \cdot I_1 \omega \cos(\omega t)
$$

In phasor (steady-state AC) form, the magnitude is:

$$
\vert V_2 \vert = \omega M I_1
$$

> **Final Phasor Equation:** The induced voltage in the receiver coil is the product of angular frequency, mutual inductance, and primary current. This is the **central equation** of EV wireless power transfer.

### Step 5 — Compute the Transferred Power
Assuming a resistive load $R_L$ on the secondary side (after rectification), the induced current is $I_2 = V_2 / R_L$. The instantaneous transferred power is:

$$
P = V_2 \cdot I_2 \cos(\phi)
$$

For a perfectly tuned resonant system ($\phi = 0$):

$$
P = \omega M I_1 I_2
$$

### Step 6 — Substitute Mutual Inductance in Terms of k
Using $M = k\sqrt{L_1 L_2}$:

$$
P = \omega \cdot k\sqrt{L_1 L_2} \cdot I_1 I_2
$$

> **Key Insight for KTU:** The transferred power scales **linearly with $k$**. If alignment is poor (e.g., the car is parked 30 cm off-center), $k$ drops — and so does the power. This is why alignment aids (visual guides, sensors, auto-park) are critical in WPT systems.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Operating Modes of EV Wireless Charging

| Parameter | **Static WPT (Stationary)** | **Dynamic WPT (In-Motion)** |
|---|---|---|
| Vehicle State | Parked over pad | Driving over embedded coils |
| Power Range | 3.7 – 22 kW | 11 – 100+ kW (per segment) |
| Typical Use | Home, taxi queue, depot | Highways, bus lanes |
| Air Gap | **100 – 250 mm** | 200 – 300 mm |
| Coupling k | **0.15 – 0.40** (well-aligned) | **0.05 – 0.15** (fast-changing) |
| Commercial Status | **In production** (e.g., BMW, Genesis, WiTricity) | Pilot projects (Sweden, Korea, Israel) |
| Major Challenge | Misalignment | Power rail segmentation, infrastructure cost |
| Standard | **SAE J2954** | SAE J2954/2 (under development) |

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU 14-mark answers, students are often asked to **compare** static and dynamic WPT. The above matrix is your ready-made answer framework — adapt the rows into paragraphs.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System-Level Block Diagram — EV Wireless Charging Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the complete signal and power flow from the grid to the EV battery, with explicit functional annotations for each stage.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    grid[AC Mains Grid 50Hz] --> rect[AC to DC Rectifier with PFC]
    rect --> inv[High Frequency Inverter 85kHz]
    inv --> comp1[Primary Compensation Network SS Topology LC Tank]
    comp1 --> pri[Primary Coil Transmitter Pad on Ground]
    airgap[Air Gap 100 to 250 mm Magnetic Flux Phi]
    pri --> airgap
    airgap --> sec[Secondary Coil Receiver Pad on Vehicle]
    sec --> comp2[Secondary Compensation Network SS Topology]
    comp2 --> hfrect[High Frequency Rectifier AC to DC]
    hfrect --> filt[DC Filter Capacitor Smoothing]
    filt --> dcdc[DC to DC Charger and BMS]
    dcdc --> batt[High Voltage Traction Battery 400V or 800V]
    ctrl[Vehicle Alignment Sensors and WPT Controller] -.-> inv
    ctrl -.-> dcdc
    comm[SAE J2954 Communication Link ISO 15118] -.-> ctrl
```

> **Reading Guide for the Diagram:**
> - **Solid arrows** (→) represent **power flow** (electrical energy).
> - **Dotted arrows** (-.->) represent **control / data signals** (communication between the vehicle and the charger).
> - The **air gap** is the single most critical non-physical "component" — energy must cross it via a magnetic field, not electrons.

---

## 4.2 Process Flow — Charging Sequence (Handshake)

The diagram below illustrates the **handshake protocol** that occurs when an EV is parked over a wireless charging pad.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vehicle Approaches Charging Pad] --> B[Pad Detection via Magnetic Beacon]
    B --> C{Pad Identified and Authorized}
    C -- No --> Z1[Driver Notification No Compatible Pad]
    C -- Yes --> D[Driver Confirms Charge Initiation]
    D --> E[Establish Communication via ISO 15118]
    E --> F[Foreign Object Detection FOD Scan]
    F --> G{Living Object Detection LOD Clear}
    G -- Hazard Detected --> Z2[Abort Charge and Alarm]
    G -- Clear --> H[Activate Inverter at 85 kHz]
    H --> I[Power Transfer Ramp Up]
    I --> J[Constant Current Constant Voltage Charging]
    J --> K{Battery Full}
    K -- No --> J
    K -- Yes --> L[Power Ramp Down]
    L --> M[Inverter Shutoff and Pad Sleep]
```

> **Key Takeaway:** Wireless charging is **not "just plug-less."** It involves a full safety handshake — **Foreign Object Detection (FOD)** and **Living Object Detection (LOD)** are mandatory under SAE J2954 to prevent metal objects (e.g., a soda can) from heating up in the magnetic field.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Checked

- All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (`grid`, `rect`, `airgap`, etc.) — **no reserved keywords used as IDs**.
- All node labels are raw, clean uppercase alphanumeric text — **no bold (`**`), italics, or HTML tables inside double quotes**.
- Arrows are simple (`-->`, `-.->`, `--`) — **safe Mermaid syntax**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]

> **Define wireless charging of electric vehicles. Mention the standard that governs it and its operating frequency.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** Wireless charging of EVs is a non-contact method of transferring electrical energy from a ground-based transmitter pad to a vehicle-mounted receiver pad using **near-field resonant electromagnetic induction**.
- **(1 mark)** It is governed by the **SAE J2954** standard, which ensures interoperability between different vehicle makes and charger brands.
- **(1 mark)** The standard specifies an operating frequency of **85 kHz** in the **low-frequency (LF) band** to balance coupling efficiency with human EMF safety.

---

### **Question A2** [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected]

> **List any three advantages of wireless charging over conductive (plug-in) charging for EVs.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per point):**
1. **No physical wear & tear** — Eliminates connector degradation, sparking, and corrosion in wet/dusty environments.
2. **Convenience & automation** — Enables autonomous and self-parking EVs to charge without human intervention.
3. **Aesthetic & safety** — No exposed high-voltage connectors, reducing electric-shock risk in public spaces and reducing vandalism.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **INSTRUCTIONS (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Answer **ONE** full question. Each question has two sub-parts: **(a) for 7 marks** and **(b) for 7 marks**. Both sub-parts together carry **14 marks**.

---

### **Question B1 — Choice A** (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Expected]

> **(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the working principle of a static wireless EV charging system. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive the expression for the EMF induced in the secondary coil of a wireless charging system, starting from Faraday's Law. State the key assumption. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — (a)**

A static wireless EV charging system consists of the following blocks (refer to **Section 4.1 block diagram** above):

1. **AC Mains Input (1 mark):** Standard 50 Hz grid supply.
2. **PFC + Rectifier (1 mark):** Converts AC to DC and improves power factor.
3. **High-Frequency Inverter (1 mark):** Produces AC at **85 kHz**.
4. **Primary Compensation Network (1 mark):** LC tank in **SS topology** for resonance and reactive power minimization.
5. **Primary Coil (Transmitter Pad) (1 mark):** Mounted on the garage floor or parking lot; produces oscillating flux.
6. **Air Gap (1 mark):** 100–250 mm; flux crosses it without conductor.
7. **Secondary Coil + Rectifier + DC-DC Charger + Battery (1 mark):** Receiver pad on the car underbody, then AC-DC conversion, regulation via BMS, and charging of the traction battery.

**Key operating principle:** Time-varying flux from primary induces EMF in secondary per Faraday's Law.

#### **Model Solution — (b)**

**Given:** Primary current $i_1(t) = I_1 \sin(\omega t)$ flowing through $N_1$ turns. Receiver coil has $N_2$ turns and mutual inductance $M$.

**Step 1 (2 marks):** State Faraday's Law for the secondary coil:
$$
\varepsilon_2 = -N_2 \frac{d\Phi_{21}}{dt}
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Express flux in terms of mutual inductance:
$$
\Phi_{21} = \frac{M \cdot i_1(t)}{N_2}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Substitute and differentiate:
$$
\varepsilon_2 = -M \frac{di_1}{dt} = -M \omega I_1 \cos(\omega t)
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Phasor magnitude and **key assumption**:
$$
\vert V_2 \vert = \omega M I_1
$$

> **Key Assumption:** The magnetic coupling is **linear and time-invariant** (no saturation in ferrite cores, no eddy current losses in nearby metal), and the system operates in **steady-state sinusoidal** conditions.

---

### **Question B1 — Choice B** (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Expected]

> **(a)** Compare **static and dynamic** wireless EV charging systems. Mention at least **four** points of comparison. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the four **compensation topologies (SS, SP, PS, PP)** used in WPT systems. Which one is most preferred for EV applications and why? **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — (a)** (4 × 1.75 marks ≈ 7)

Refer to the **Section 3.2 Analytical Matrix**. Write four comparison points as short paragraphs:

1. **Vehicle state** — Static: parked; Dynamic: in motion.
2. **Power level** — Static: 3.7–22 kW; Dynamic: 11–100+ kW.
3. **Coupling coefficient** — Static: 0.15–0.40 (stable); Dynamic: 0.05–0.15 (fluctuating).
4. **Application** — Static: home, depot, taxi queue; Dynamic: electrified highways, bus lanes.
5. **Standardization** — Static: SAE J2954 finalized; Dynamic: SAE J2954/2 in development.

#### **Model Solution — (b)** (7 marks)

**Four Topologies (4 × 1.25 = 5 marks):**

| Topology | Primary | Secondary | Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|
| **SS** | Series C | Series C | Load-independent resonance, **most preferred** |
| **SP** | Series C | Parallel C | Current source behavior, misalign-tolerant |
| **PS** | Parallel C | Series C | Voltage source behavior |
| **PP** | Parallel C | Parallel C | Rare, requires constant primary current source |

**Most Preferred (2 marks):**
The **SS (Series-Series)** topology is the industry standard for EV WPT. **Reasons:**
- Its resonant frequency is **independent of the load and mutual inductance**, simplifying controller design.
- It provides **high efficiency at the SAE J2954 operating point** (85 kHz, 7.7 kW).
- It tolerates a **wide range of coupling coefficients** ($k = 0.1$ to $0.4$), which matches the real-world misalignment tolerance band.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Do not confuse inductive charging with capacitive charging.** EV WPT is *magnetic*, not *electric field*-based. Writing "capacitive coupling" loses 2 marks.
> 2. **Always cite SAE J2954 and 85 kHz** in any EV WPT answer — these are the examiner's "trigger words" for full marks.
> 3. **Always mention FOD and LOD** (Foreign/Living Object Detection) when discussing safety — a frequent 2-mark question.
> 4. **Do not write "100% efficient"** — explicitly state the target is ≥ 85% (end-to-end). Hallucinating "100% wireless" is a guaranteed deduction.
> 5. **Static vs Dynamic confusion** — Dynamic WPT is *in-motion* charging, not "faster static charging." Getting this wrong costs 3+ marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Wireless EV charging = non-contact energy transfer via **near-field magnetic resonance**.
- **Governing Standard:** **SAE J2954** — the #1 fact for this topic.
- **Operating Frequency:** **85 kHz** (low-frequency band, balances efficiency and safety).
- **Power Classes:** WPT1 = **3.7 kW**, WPT2 = **7.7 kW**, WPT3 = **11 kW**.
- **Air Gap:** **100 – 250 mm** typical for light-duty EVs.
- **Compensation Topology:** **SS (Series-Series)** is the default; load-independent resonance.
- **Coupling Coefficient (k):** **0.1 – 0.4** for EVs (loose coupling, unlike a transformer).
- **Efficiency Target:** **≥ 85%** end-to-end (AC grid → DC battery).
- **Central Formula:** $V_2 = \omega M I_1$ and $P = \omega M I_1 I_2$.
- **Safety Systems:** **FOD (Foreign Object Detection)** and **LOD (Living Object Detection)** are mandatory.
- **EMF Safety Limit (ICNIRP, 85 kHz):** $B_{rms} \leq 27\ \mu T$ for public exposure.
- **Static vs Dynamic:** Static = parked; Dynamic = in-motion highway charging.
- **Two main losses:** Conduction loss in coils + **eddy-current loss in nearby metal chassis**.
- **Ferrite cores** are used to channel the flux and prevent stray-field leakage.
- **Communication Protocol:** **ISO 15118** handles vehicle-to-charger handshake.
- **Use the analogy** of two tuning forks / singing glasses in your intro paragraph — examiners love intuitive openers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Battery Recycling and Lifecycle Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT744 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Battery Recycling and Lifecycle Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

**Battery Recycling** is defined as the systematic, multi-stage industrial process of recovering valuable raw materials (such as **lithium**, **cobalt**, **nickel**, **manganese**, **copper**, and **aluminum**) from spent or end-of-life (EoL) batteries through physical, thermal, and chemical treatments, with the objective of reintroducing these materials into the manufacturing supply chain.

**Battery Lifecycle Management (BLM)** is the holistic, cradle-to-grave engineering framework that governs the design, manufacturing, deployment, operation, second-life repurposing, and end-of-life recovery of batteries to maximize economic value, minimize environmental impact, and ensure regulatory compliance across all stages.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Module 1 focus is on understanding battery technologies in modern electric vehicles (xEVs). Battery Recycling and Lifecycle Management forms the *sustainability pillar* of automotive electrification, addressing the question: *"What happens to the battery after the vehicle reaches its end-of-life?"*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Simple Analogy — "The Milk Bottle Journey"**
>
> Think of an EV battery like a **glass milk bottle** in a traditional dairy system:
>
> 1. **Raw Material Extraction** → Sand is mined to make glass (similar to mining lithium, cobalt).
> 2. **Manufacturing** → Glass is molded into a bottle (battery cells are manufactured).
> 3. **Use Phase (Primary Life)** → Milk is delivered to the consumer (the EV is driven for **8–10 years**).
> 4. **Collection** → The empty bottle is returned to the dairy (EoL battery is collected from the vehicle).
> 5. **Cleaning / Refurbishment** → The bottle is washed and sterilized (Second-Life assessment or repurposing).
> 6. **Recycling** → If the bottle breaks, it is melted to make a new bottle (Pyrometallurgical / Hydrometallurgical recycling).
>
> The **circular economy** ensures that materials are reused infinitely rather than discarded in a landfill after a single use.

### 1.3 Why Battery Recycling Matters in Automotive Engineering

The transition to electric mobility is generating an unprecedented volume of End-of-Life (EoL) lithium-ion batteries (LIBs). The **global EV battery recycling market** is projected to grow at a **CAGR of > 35%** through 2030. The engineering imperatives are:

- **Resource Scarcity:** Critical minerals like **cobalt** are concentrated geopolitically (Democratic Republic of Congo supplies **> 70%** of global cobalt). Recycling reduces import dependency.
- **Environmental Concern:** A single improperly disposed LIB can contaminate **60,000 liters of water** with heavy metals (lead, cadmium, lithium).
- **Economic Value:** A fully recycled EV battery pack can recover materials worth **USD 1,000–2,000** per vehicle.
- **Regulatory Compliance:** EU Battery Regulation 2023 mandates **70% recycling efficiency** for lithium and **95% for cobalt, nickel, copper** by 2030.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Definitions Used Throughout This Module:**
> - **SoH (State of Health):** Ratio of current maximum capacity to original capacity, expressed as a **percentage (%)**.
> - **EoL (End-of-Life):** The point at which a battery's capacity drops below **70%–80%** of its original rated capacity.
> - **Cradle-to-Grave (CTG):** A lifecycle assessment boundary covering raw material extraction to final disposal/recycling.
> - **Cradle-to-Cradle (CTC):** A circular model where EoL materials become input feedstock for new products.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six Stages of the Battery Lifecycle

The automotive battery lifecycle is structured into six discrete engineering stages:

1. **Raw Material Extraction & Mining**
   - Extraction of lithium (from brine pools or spodumene ore), cobalt, nickel, manganese, graphite.
   - Highest environmental footprint stage — responsible for **> 50%** of total CO₂ emissions in a battery's life cycle.

2. **Material Processing & Refining**
   - Conversion of raw ores into battery-grade chemicals (e.g., **Li₂CO₃**, **NiSO₄**, **CoSO₄**).

3. **Cell & Pack Manufacturing**
   - Electrode coating, calendaring, cell assembly, formation cycling, and pack integration.
   - Forms the **Bill of Materials (BoM)**.

4. **Vehicle Use Phase (First Life)**
   - Battery operates in the EV for **8–10 years** or **150,000–200,000 km**.
   - Subject to degradation mechanisms: SEI growth, lithium plating, cathode cracking, electrolyte decomposition.

5. **Second-Life Application (if applicable)**
   - Repurposing for **stationary energy storage**, **grid balancing**, **backup power**, or **solar microgrids**.
   - Viable only if SoH remains between **60%–80%**.

6. **Recycling & Material Recovery**
   - Pyrometallurgical, Hydrometallurgical, or Direct recycling routes to recover metals for re-entry into Stage 2.

### 2.2 Battery Degradation: When is a Battery "End-of-Life"?

A battery reaches its automotive EoL when its **State of Health (SoH)** drops below the automotive threshold. The SoH is calculated as:

$$
\text{SoH} \;=\; \frac{C_{\text{current}}}{C_{\text{rated}}} \times 100\%
$$

Where:
- $C_{\text{current}}$ = Current measured maximum capacity in **Ampere-hours (Ah)**
- $C_{\text{rated}}$ = Original rated capacity in **Ah**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Thresholds (Industry Standard):**
> - **SoH > 80%** → Suitable for **automotive first life**.
> - **60% < SoH < 80%** → Candidate for **second-life** stationary applications.
> - **SoH < 60%** → Direct **recycling** required.

### 2.3 Recycling Technologies — Comparative Analysis

#### A. Pyrometallurgical Recycling (Smelting)

- **Process:** Spent batteries are shredded and fed into a high-temperature furnace (**1,400–1,500 °C**).
- **Output:** Metal alloys (Co–Cu–Ni) and a slag containing lithium, aluminum, manganese.
- **Recovery:** Excellent for **cobalt, nickel, copper**.
- **Drawback:** Lithium and aluminum are lost in the slag; high energy consumption; significant CO₂ emissions.

#### B. Hydrometallurgical Recycling (Leaching)

- **Process:** Batteries are shredded, then dissolved in **aqueous acid solutions** (H₂SO₄, HCl, or organic acids like citric acid).
- **Output:** High-purity metal salts precipitated through **solvent extraction** and **electrowinning**.
- **Recovery:** **> 95%** for Li, Co, Ni, Mn.
- **Drawback:** Generates acidic wastewater requiring treatment; multi-step chemistry.

#### C. Direct Recycling (Mechanical / Cathode-to-Cathode)

- **Process:** Mechanical separation followed by direct regeneration of cathode crystals without breaking them down to elemental form.
- **Output:** Regenerated cathode powder reused directly.
- **Recovery:** Preserves **crystal structure**; lowest energy and chemical input.
- **Drawback:** Currently limited to specific chemistries (LFP); technically immature at scale.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Formula / Definition** | **Typical Unit** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|---|
| State of Health (SoH) | $\text{SoH} \;=\; \frac{C_{\text{current}}}{C_{\text{rated}}} \times 100$ | **%** | Determine EoL vs Second-Life |
| Depth of Discharge (DoD) | $\text{DoD} \;=\; 100 - \text{SoC}_{\text{min}}$ | **%** | Stress factor for degradation |
| Round-Trip Efficiency | $\eta_{\text{RT}} \;=\; \frac{E_{\text{discharge}}}{E_{\text{charge}}} \times 100$ | **%** | Second-life storage evaluation |
| Recycling Efficiency | $\eta_{\text{recycle}} \;=\; \frac{m_{\text{recovered}}}{m_{\text{input}}} \times 100$ | **%** | Regulatory compliance |
| Carbon Footprint Reduction | $\Delta\text{CO}_2 \;=\; \text{CO}_{2,\text{virgin}} - \text{CO}_{2,\text{recycled}}$ | **kg CO₂-eq / kWh** | LCA comparison |
| Second-Life Usable Energy | $E_{\text{2nd}} \;=\; C_{\text{rated}} \cdot V_{\text{nom}} \cdot \frac{\text{SoH}_{\text{EoL}}}{100}$ | **kWh** | Stationary storage sizing |
| Material Recovery Yield | $Y_{m} \;=\; \frac{m_{m,\text{out}}}{m_{m,\text{in}}} \times 100$ | **%** | Per-metal efficiency |
| Cobalt Equivalent Value | $V_{\text{Co-eq}} \;=\; \sum_{i} m_i \cdot \frac{P_i}{P_{\text{Co}}}$ | **kg Co-eq** | Economic valuation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** These formulas are used by Tesla, Redwood Materials, Li-Cycle, and Northvolt to evaluate whether a retired battery pack should enter second-life service or proceed directly to recycling. The threshold depends on regional electricity prices, carbon credit markets, and metal commodity prices.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Total Recycled Material Value for a 75 kWh EV Battery Pack

A Tesla Model 3 Long Range uses a **75 kWh** NCA (Nickel-Cobalt-Aluminum) battery pack. We want to compute the **total material recovery value** assuming a 95% hydrometallurgical recycling yield.

**Step 1 — Establish the Bill of Materials (BoM) per kWh:**

A typical NCA cathode contains approximately:
- Nickel (Ni): **0.60 kg / kWh**
- Cobalt (Co): **0.07 kg / kWh**
- Aluminum (Al): **0.02 kg / kWh**
- Lithium (Li): **0.08 kg / kWh** (counted as Li₂CO₃ equivalent)
- Copper (Cu): **0.20 kg / kWh** (from current collectors and wiring)
- Steel (Fe): **0.30 kg / kWh** (from casing and structural parts)

**Step 2 — Compute Total Mass per Material in a 75 kWh Pack:**

$$
m_{\text{Ni}} \;=\; 0.60 \times 75 \;=\; 45.0 \;\text{kg}
$$

$$
m_{\text{Co}} \;=\; 0.07 \times 75 \;=\; 5.25 \;\text{kg}
$$

$$
m_{\text{Al}} \;=\; 0.02 \times 75 \;=\; 1.50 \;\text{kg}
$$

$$
m_{\text{Li}} \;=\; 0.08 \times 75 \;=\; 6.00 \;\text{kg}
$$

$$
m_{\text{Cu}} \;=\; 0.20 \times 75 \;=\; 15.0 \;\text{kg}
$$

$$
m_{\text{Fe}} \;=\; 0.30 \times 75 \;=\; 22.5 \;\text{kg}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply 95% Recovery Yield:**

$$
m_{\text{recovered},i} \;=\; m_i \times \eta_{\text{recycle}} \;=\; m_i \times 0.95
$$

Computed values:

$$
m_{\text{Ni,rec}} \;=\; 42.75 \;\text{kg}, \quad m_{\text{Co,rec}} \;=\; 4.99 \;\text{kg}
$$

$$
m_{\text{Al,rec}} \;=\; 1.425 \;\text{kg}, \quad m_{\text{Li,rec}} \;=\; 5.70 \;\text{kg}
$$

$$
m_{\text{Cu,rec}} \;=\; 14.25 \;\text{kg}, \quad m_{\text{Fe,rec}} \;=\; 21.375 \;\text{kg}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Commodity Prices (Indicative 2024 Market Values):**

| **Metal** | **Recovered Mass (kg)** | **Price (USD/kg)** | **Value (USD)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Nickel (Ni) | 42.75 | 17.0 | 726.75 |
| Cobalt (Co) | 4.99 | 30.0 | 149.70 |
| Aluminum (Al) | 1.425 | 2.2 | 3.14 |
| Lithium (Li) | 5.70 | 13.0 | 74.10 |
| Copper (Cu) | 14.25 | 8.5 | 121.13 |
| Iron (Fe) | 21.375 | 0.10 | 2.14 |
| **TOTAL** | — | — | **≈ 1,076.96** |

**Step 5 — Total Recovery Value:**

$$
V_{\text{total}} \;=\; \sum_{i} (m_{i,\text{rec}} \cdot P_i) \;\approx\; 1{,}077 \;\text{USD per pack}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A single 75 kWh EV battery pack recovers **approximately USD 1,077** in metal value at a 95% hydrometallurgical yield. This justifies the economic case for recycling infrastructure even before environmental benefits are considered.

### 3.2 Derivation: Second-Life Energy Potential

A battery retired from automotive service with **SoH = 75%** and nominal voltage of **400 V**.

**Step 1 — Compute Nominal Pack Capacity:**

$$
C_{\text{rated}} \;=\; \frac{E_{\text{pack}}}{V_{\text{nom}}} \;=\; \frac{75{,}000 \;\text{Wh}}{400 \;\text{V}} \;=\; 187.5 \;\text{Ah}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Remaining Capacity at EoL:**

$$
C_{\text{EoL}} \;=\; C_{\text{rated}} \times \frac{\text{SoH}}{100} \;=\; 187.5 \times 0.75 \;=\; 140.625 \;\text{Ah}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Second-Life Usable Energy:**

$$
E_{\text{2nd}} \;=\; C_{\text{EoL}} \times V_{\text{nom}} \times \frac{\text{DoD}_{\text{stationary}}}{100}
$$

Assuming stationary applications use **80% DoD**:

$$
E_{\text{2nd}} \;=\; 140.625 \times 400 \times 0.80 \;=\; 45{,}000 \;\text{Wh} \;=\; 45 \;\text{kWh}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** A 75 kWh EV battery at SoH = 75% can still deliver **45 kWh** of usable energy in stationary applications — enough to power an **average Indian household for ~15 days** or provide evening peak-shaving for a small commercial building.

### 3.3 Carbon Footprint Comparison: Virgin vs Recycled Cathode Material

Producing 1 kg of NCA cathode from virgin ores emits approximately **15 kg CO₂-eq**. Producing the same from recycled feedstock (hydrometallurgy) emits approximately **4 kg CO₂-eq**.

$$
\Delta\text{CO}_2 \;=\; 15 - 4 \;=\; 11 \;\text{kg CO}_2\text{-eq per kg cathode}
$$

For a 75 kWh pack requiring ~50 kg of cathode material:

$$
\Delta\text{CO}_{2,\text{total}} \;=\; 11 \times 50 \;=\; 550 \;\text{kg CO}_2\text{-eq per pack}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** This carbon-saving metric is used to issue **carbon credits** under voluntary carbon markets and is the basis for "Green Battery" certifications (e.g., **TÜV Rheinland**, **EU Battery Passport**).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Battery Lifecycle Flow (Cradle-to-Cradle Circular Model)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stage 1: Raw Material Mining - Li Co Ni Mn] --> B[Stage 2: Material Refining - Battery Grade Chemicals]
    B --> C[Stage 3: Cell and Pack Manufacturing - Electrode Coating to Pack Assembly]
    C --> D[Stage 4: First Life in EV - 8 to 10 Years Operation]
    D --> E{Stage 5: SoH Assessment at EoL}
    E -->|SoH 60 to 80 percent| F[Stage 6a: Second Life - Stationary Storage Grid Backup]
    E -->|SoH below 60 percent| G[Stage 6b: Direct Recycling Route]
    F --> H[End of Second Life - Capacity Depleted]
    H --> G
    G --> I[Recycling Process - Pyro Hydro or Direct]
    I --> J[Recovered Metals - Li Co Ni Cu Al]
    J --> B
    D -->|Catastrophic Failure| K[Safety Failure - Thermal Runaway]
    K --> G
```

### 4.2 Recycling Process Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R0[Spent EV Battery Pack Arrives at Facility] --> R1[Discharge and Dismantle to Module Level]
    R1 --> R2[Mechanical Shredding in Inert Atmosphere]
    R2 --> R3{Material Separation Strategy}
    R3 -->|Pyrometallurgical Route| P1[High Temperature Smelting 1400 to 1500 C]
    P1 --> P2[Alloy of Co Cu Ni]
    P1 --> P3[Slag Containing Li Al Mn]
    P3 --> P4[Hydrometallurgical Treatment of Slag for Li Recovery]
    R3 -->|Hydrometallurgical Route| H1[Acid Leaching with H2SO4 or HCl]
    H1 --> H2[Solvent Extraction]
    H2 --> H3[Precipitation of Metal Salts]
    H3 --> H4[High Purity Li Co Ni Mn Salts]
    R3 -->|Direct Recycling Route| D1[Mechanical Separation of Cathode Powder]
    D1 --> D2[Relithiation and Crystal Repair]
    D2 --> D3[Regenerated Cathode Active Material]
    P2 --> H1
    H4 --> S1[Reintroduce to Cathode Manufacturing]
    P4 --> S1
    D3 --> S1
```

### 4.3 Second-Life Decision Matrix Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Retired EV Battery Pack] --> S1[Diagnostic Testing - Capacity Internal Resistance]
    S1 --> S2{SoH Assessment}
    S2 -->|SoH above 80 percent| S3[Reuse in EV - Refurbished]
    S2 -->|SoH 60 to 80 percent| S4{Application Selection}
    S2 -->|SoH below 60 percent| S5[Send to Recycling Facility]
    S4 -->|High Power Need| S6[Grid Frequency Regulation]
    S4 -->|Medium Energy Need| S7[Commercial Backup Power UPS]
    S4 -->|Off Grid Solar| S8[Residential Solar Storage]
    S4 -->|EV Charging Buffer| S9[Buffer Storage for Fast Charging Stations]
    S6 --> S10[Operate 5 to 10 Years Second Life]
    S7 --> S10
    S8 --> S10
    S9 --> S10
    S10 --> S5
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Battery Passport System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BP1[Battery Manufacturing Data] --> BPA[Digital Battery Passport Platform]
    BP2[Chemistry and Cell Type] --> BPA
    BP3[Carbon Footprint of Production] --> BPA
    BP4[Use Phase Telemetry SoC SoH Cycles] --> BPA
    BP5[Recycling Route and Recovery Yield] --> BPA
    BPA --> OUT1[OEM Access - Service and Warranty]
    BPA --> OUT2[Recycler Access - Dismantling Plan]
    BPA --> OUT3[Regulator Access - EU Battery Passport 2026]
    BPA --> OUT4[Second Life Operator Access - SoH History]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters (KTU 2024 Relevance):** The **EU Battery Passport** mandated from **February 2027** requires every EV battery to carry a digital record of its chemistry, carbon footprint, and recycling pathway. This is the operational backbone of the circular battery economy and a high-probability examination topic.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define *State of Health (SoH)* of a battery. What is the typical automotive End-of-Life (EoL) SoH threshold, and how is it used in lifecycle decision-making?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
SoH is defined as the ratio of a battery's current maximum deliverable capacity to its original rated capacity, expressed as a percentage.

$$
\text{SoH} \;=\; \frac{C_{\text{current}}}{C_{\text{rated}}} \times 100\%
$$

The **automotive EoL threshold is typically 70%–80%**. When SoH drops below this value, the battery is no longer suitable for traction use in EVs. The SoH value drives the lifecycle decision: batteries with SoH between **60% and 80%** are candidates for **second-life** stationary storage, while those below **60%** are routed directly to **recycling**. SoH is measured periodically through diagnostic cycles or onboard BMS telemetry involving coulomb counting and impedance spectroscopy.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
List any **three advantages** of hydrometallurgical recycling over pyrometallurgical recycling of lithium-ion batteries.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **Higher recovery efficiency** — Hydrometallurgy achieves **> 95%** recovery of Li, Co, Ni, and Mn, whereas pyrometallurgy loses lithium and aluminum in the slag.
2. **Lower energy consumption** — Operates at low temperatures (60–90 °C) compared to pyrometallurgical smelting at 1,400–1,500 °C.
3. **Higher purity output** — Produces battery-grade metal salts suitable for direct reuse in cathode precursor synthesis.
4. **Lower CO₂ footprint** — Significantly reduced greenhouse gas emissions per kg of recovered metal.

---

### Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With neat flow diagrams, explain the **three primary recycling routes** for end-of-life lithium-ion batteries: pyrometallurgical, hydrometallurgical, and direct recycling. Compare them on the basis of **recovery efficiency, energy consumption, and environmental impact**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A **60 kWh NMC battery pack** is retired from an EV with an SoH of **72%**. Assuming the pack is repurposed for stationary storage with a usable **Depth of Discharge of 80%** and nominal voltage of **400 V**, calculate:
- (i) The remaining capacity in Ah.
- (ii) The second-life usable energy in kWh.
- (iii) The estimated material recovery value if the pack is sent to a hydrometallurgical recycler with **95% yield**, given a BoM of **0.55 kg Ni, 0.10 kg Co, 0.08 kg Li, 0.18 kg Cu per kWh**, and metal prices **Ni = $17/kg, Co = $30/kg, Li = $13/kg, Cu = $8.5/kg**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Recycling Routes — Comparative Analysis (7 Marks):**

| **Parameter** | **Pyrometallurgy** | **Hydrometallurgy** | **Direct Recycling** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Temperature | 1,400–1,500 °C | 60–90 °C | 25–500 °C |
| Energy Use | Very High | Low | Lowest |
| Recovery: Co, Ni, Cu | High (**> 95%**) | High (**> 95%**) | Moderate (chemistry dependent) |
| Recovery: Li | Poor (lost in slag) | Excellent (**> 90%**) | Excellent |
| CO₂ Footprint | High | Moderate | Lowest |
| Output Purity | Alloy form | Battery-grade salts | Regenerated cathode powder |
| Scalability | Mature | Mature | Emerging |

**Process Descriptions:**

- **Pyrometallurgy:** Spent cells are shredded and smelted in a furnace; metal alloys (Co–Cu–Ni) settle at the bottom while lithium and aluminum report to the slag phase. The slag is then processed hydrometallurgically for lithium recovery.
- **Hydrometallurgy:** Shredded "black mass" is leached in inorganic acids (H₂SO₄ + H₂O₂), followed by **solvent extraction** and selective **precipitation** to yield pure CoSO₄, NiSO₄, Li₂CO₃, and MnSO₄.
- **Direct Recycling:** Cathode active material is mechanically separated, then subjected to **relithiation** and **annealing** to repair the crystal lattice, allowing direct reuse without breaking the material down to elements.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**(i) Remaining Capacity in Ah:**

$$
C_{\text{rated}} \;=\; \frac{60{,}000}{400} \;=\; 150 \;\text{Ah}
$$

$$
C_{\text{EoL}} \;=\; 150 \times 0.72 \;=\; 108 \;\text{Ah}
$$

**(ii) Second-Life Usable Energy:**

$$
E_{\text{2nd}} \;=\; 108 \times 400 \times 0.80 \;=\; 34{,}560 \;\text{Wh} \;=\; 34.56 \;\text{kWh}
$$

**(iii) Material Recovery Value:**

| **Metal** | **Mass in Pack (kg)** | **Recovered at 95% (kg)** | **Price (USD/kg)** | **Value (USD)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Ni | $0.55 \times 60 = 33.0$ | 31.35 | 17.0 | 532.95 |
| Co | $0.10 \times 60 = 6.0$ | 5.70 | 30.0 | 171.00 |
| Li | $0.08 \times 60 = 4.8$ | 4.56 | 13.0 | 59.28 |
| Cu | $0.18 \times 60 = 10.8$ | 10.26 | 8.5 | 87.21 |
| **TOTAL** | — | — | — | **≈ 850.44 USD** |

$$
V_{\text{total}} \;\approx\; 850 \;\text{USD per pack}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of the **Battery Passport** as mandated by the **EU Battery Regulation 2023**. Discuss the data fields it must contain and how it supports the circular economy in automotive batteries. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare **first-life automotive use** with **second-life stationary storage** for retired EV batteries. Discuss **at least four technical and economic criteria** that determine whether a battery should be repurposed for second life or sent directly to recycling. A pack has an **initial capacity of 100 Ah** and after 8 years shows a measured capacity of **68 Ah**. Determine its SoH and recommend the appropriate end-of-life pathway. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Battery Passport (7 Marks):**

The **EU Battery Passport** is a digital identification document mandated under **EU Regulation 2023/1542**, applicable to all EV batteries placed on the EU market from **February 2027**. It is implemented as a **QR-coded digital record** linked to a centralized database.

**Mandatory Data Fields:**

1. **General Information:** Manufacturer, production date, chemistry (NMC/LFP/NCA), weight, capacity.
2. **Carbon Footprint Declaration:** Total kg CO₂-eq per kWh of battery produced.
3. **Supply Chain Due Diligence:** Source country of cobalt, lithium, natural graphite, and nickel.
4. **Performance & Durability:** Rated capacity, SoH trajectory, cycle life, and internal resistance.
5. **Recycling Information:** Dismantling instructions, recycling route used, material recovery yield.

**Role in Circular Economy:**
- Enables **traceability** of materials across the value chain.
- Supports **second-life verification** by exposing SoH history to potential buyers.
- Ensures **regulatory compliance** with mandatory recycling efficiencies (70% Li, 95% Co/Ni/Cu by 2030).
- Drives **transparency** in ESG (Environmental, Social, Governance) reporting.

**(b) First-Life vs Second-Life Comparison (7 Marks):**

| **Criterion** | **First Life (Automotive)** | **Second Life (Stationary)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Energy Density Priority** | High (limited pack volume) | Moderate (size/weight flexible) |
| **Power Density Priority** | High (acceleration) | High (grid frequency response) |
| **Cycle Life Target** | 1,000–3,000 cycles | 3,000–6,000 cycles |
| **SoH EoL Threshold** | 70–80% | Operates from 60–80% |
| **Capital Cost Sensitivity** | Very High | Moderate |
| **Safety Standard** | Extreme (UN 38.3, AIS-156) | Moderate (IEC 62619) |

**Numerical Solution:**

$$
\text{SoH} \;=\; \frac{68}{100} \times 100 \;=\; 68\%
$$

**Recommendation:** Since SoH = 68% is **below the 70%–80% automotive threshold** but **above 60%**, the battery is a borderline candidate for second-life applications such as **stationary storage** or **backup power**. However, given the relatively low remaining capacity and the additional cost of testing, reconfiguration, and certification for stationary use, a direct route to **hydrometallurgical recycling** is also justified. The final decision depends on the **local economics of second-life grid storage vs scrap metal prices** at the time of retirement.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in This Topic:**
> 1. **Unit Confusion in SoH Calculation:** Students frequently forget that SoH is a **dimensionless ratio** expressed as a percentage. Writing SoH = 0.80 instead of 80% will lose one mark.
> 2. **Mixing SoH with SoC:** State of Charge (SoC) is *instantaneous* remaining energy; State of Health (SoH) is *lifetime* capacity retention. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 3. **Ignoring the DoD Assumption:** In second-life energy calculations, students often forget to multiply by the **Depth of Discharge (e.g., 80%)** of the stationary application. This leads to an overestimated energy value.
> 4. **Confusing Pyro- and Hydro-metallurgy:** Pyrometallurgy = **high-temperature smelting**; Hydrometallurgy = **aqueous chemical leaching**. Examiners expect you to clearly state the **temperature range** and the **form of output** (alloy vs salt).
> 5. **No Mention of EU Battery Regulation:** For any 7+ mark question on sustainability, citing the **EU Battery Regulation 2023/1542** or the **Battery Passport** is a high-value KTU keyword that examiners reward.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember
