- **Battery Lifecycle = 6 Stages:** Mining → Refining → Manufacturing → First Life → Second Life → Recycling.
- **SoH Formula:** $\text{SoH} = \frac{C_{\text{current}}}{C_{\text{rated}}} \times 100\%$ — units in **%**, not fraction.
- **Decision Thresholds:** SoH > 80% (EV use); 60% < SoH < 80% (Second Life); SoH < 60% (Recycle).
- **Three Recycling Routes:** Pyrometallurgy (high T, alloy output), Hydrometallurgy (acid leach, salt output, > 95% recovery), Direct Recycling (cathode-to-cathode, lowest emissions).
- **EU Battery Passport 2027** is mandatory for all EV batteries sold in the EU — a high-probability KTU question.
- **Economic Value:** A 75 kWh EV pack recovers approximately **USD 1,000–1,100** in metals at 95% yield.
- **Carbon Savings:** Recycled cathode production saves **~11 kg CO₂-eq per kg** compared to virgin mining.
- **Second-Life Energy Formula:** $E_{\text{2nd}} = C_{\text{rated}} \times V_{\text{nom}} \times \frac{\text{SoH}}{100} \times \text{DoD}_{\text{stationary}}$.
- **Critical Minerals Concentration:** Cobalt — DRC (70%+); Lithium — Australia, Chile, China; Nickel — Indonesia, Philippines.
- **Regulatory Target by 2030:** **70% Li, 95% Co, Ni, Cu** recycling efficiency (EU Regulation 2023/1542).
- **Always cite UN 38.3 and AIS-156** (India) safety standards when discussing battery EoL in any KTU answer.
- **Circular Economy > Linear Economy:** The shift from cradle-to-grave to cradle-to-cradle is the central engineering philosophy of modern battery sustainability.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stratified Charge Engines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT744 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Stratified Charge Engines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stratified Charge Engine (SCE):** A stratified charge engine is an internal combustion engine (typically a spark-ignition or compression-ignition variant) in which the air–fuel mixture is **deliberately non-uniform** within the combustion chamber. A **rich (stoichiometric or near-stoichiometric) mixture** is concentrated in a localized zone around the spark plug (or around the auto-ignition kernel), while the **remainder of the chamber** is filled with a **lean mixture or pure air**. This deliberate spatial variation of the local equivalence ratio $\phi(x, y, z, t)$ is termed **charge stratification**.

**Equivalence Ratio Definition (Recap):**

$$\phi = \frac{(F/A)_{\text{actual}}}{(F/A)_{\text{stoichiometric}}}$$

- $\phi = 1$ → Stoichiometric
- $\phi < 1$ → Lean mixture
- $\phi > 1$ → Rich mixture
- In a stratified engine, $\phi$ varies from point to point inside the cylinder.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – PEMUT744, Module 2):** The student must be able to distinguish between **Homogeneous Charge Spark Ignition (HCSI)**, **Stratified Charge Spark Ignition (SCSI)**, and **Homogeneous Charge Compression Ignition (HCCI)** engines, with emphasis on the working principle, advantages, limitations, and modern applications (e.g., GDI – Gasoline Direct Injection).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world Analogy — The Campfire Trick:**
> Imagine you are lighting a campfire in a large, windy field. Instead of soaking **every log** in kerosene (which would be wasteful, smoky, and dangerous), you pour a small amount of kerosene **only on the topmost log** where the match is struck. The kerosene-soaked log catches fire instantly, and the flame then spreads to the drier logs below. The fire is *stratified* — concentrated fuel at the ignition point, leaner fuel elsewhere.
>
> A stratified charge engine does the **same thing in miniature**: it squirts fuel **only near the spark plug at the moment of ignition**, instead of premixing fuel with all the air in the cylinder long before combustion. This saves fuel, allows the engine to run on much **leaner overall mixtures** (better part-load economy), and even permits **multi-fuel operation** (diesel, kerosene, alcohol, etc.).

**Standard Reference Metrics Used in This Topic:**

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value in SCE |
|---|---|---|
| Overall (bulk) air–fuel ratio | $A/F_{\text{overall}}$ | **18:1 to 25:1 (very lean)** |
| Local A/F near spark plug | $A/F_{\text{local}}$ | **12:1 to 14:1 (rich/stoichiometric)** |
| Stratification ratio | $SR$ | **3:1 to 6:1** |
| Combustion chamber pressure rise | $\frac{dP}{d\theta}$ | **0.2 – 0.4 MPa/deg** (controlled) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" Behind Stratification — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — The Premixed Homogeneous Engine Problem**
In a conventional **PFI (Port Fuel Injection) SI engine**, fuel is mixed with air in the intake manifold *long* before entering the cylinder. The mixture is therefore spatially uniform (homogeneous) with a single global $\phi \approx 1$. At part load, this forces the engine to *throttle* the intake air, causing **pumping losses** (reduced volumetric efficiency $\eta_v$).

**Step 2 — The Pumping Loss Penalty**
Throttling reduces the manifold absolute pressure $P_{\text{man}}$ below atmospheric. The work spent pulling air through the throttle at part load is wasted:

$$W_{\text{pump loss}} = \int \left( P_{\text{atm}} - P_{\text{man}} \right) \, dV_{\text{displaced}}$$

A stratified engine avoids this by running un-throttled with **excess air** and very lean overall mixture.

**Step 3 — Localized Rich Zone for Ignition**
Lean mixtures are hard to ignite (higher activation energy, slower flame kernel growth). Therefore, the engine **stratifies** the charge so that the spark plug is bathed in a small, locally rich (or stoichiometric) pocket. The flame kernel forms reliably, then propagates outward into the surrounding lean mixture.

**Step 4 — The Result**
The combustion is **fast and complete** because the kernel is rich, yet the **bulk mixture is lean**, which:
- Improves **part-load fuel economy** by 10–25 %.
- Lowers **unburnt HC and CO** (lean burn = excess O₂ for oxidizing CO → CO₂).
- Raises **NOx** (high in-cylinder temperature due to lean burn + flame propagation).
- Allows **multi-fuel capability** because the rich kernel ignites reliably even with low-cetane/poor-vaporization fuels.

## 2.2 Operational Phases of a Stratified Charge Engine (Cycle)

A typical **direct-injection stratified-charge SI engine** follows these four phases per cycle:

1. **Intake Stroke:** Only **air** (no fuel) is inducted. No throttling.
2. **Compression Stroke:** Air is compressed to a high pressure (compression ratio $r$ typically **12:1 to 14:1**).
3. **Injection + Ignition (near TDC):** A high-pressure injector sprays fuel *directly* into the cylinder close to Top Dead Center, forming the rich kernel around the spark plug. The spark fires, igniting the kernel.
4. **Combustion + Exhaust:** Flame propagates from the rich kernel outward into the lean bulk mixture. Combustion products are expelled.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they cover 90 % of numerical/derivations asked in KTU ESE for this topic.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\phi = \dfrac{(F/A)_{\text{act}}}{(F/A)_{\text{stoich}}}$ | Equivalence ratio (local or global) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\eta_{v} = \dfrac{m_{\text{actual}}}{m_{\text{atmospheric}}} = \dfrac{V_d \cdot \rho_a \cdot \eta_{v}}{m_a}$ | Volumetric efficiency (stratified engines run ≈ 1.0 un-throttled) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\text{BSFC} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{P_b} \times 3600$ | Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (lower for stratified) | g/kWh |
| 4 | $\eta_{\text{th}} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}} \cdot \dfrac{Q_B}{Q_{\text{in}}}$ | Thermal efficiency depends on compression ratio $r$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | $SR = \dfrac{(A/F)_{\text{lean bulk}}}{(A/F)_{\text{rich zone}}}$ | Stratification Ratio — key parameter | dimensionless |
| 6 | $P_{\text{inj}} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f \cdot v_{\text{inj}}}{C_d A_{\text{nozzle}}}$ | High-pressure injection pressure required (GDI typical: **100–350 bar**) | bar |
| 7 | $\text{NOx} \propto \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{R T_{\text{flame}}}\right)$ | Zeldovich mechanism — NOx rises exponentially with flame temperature | ppm |

> **Pitfall Avoidance Note:** When writing tables in your KTU answer sheet, never use the vertical bar `|` inside math expressions (e.g., for absolute value). Use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Stratified charge concepts are foundational to:
- **GDI (Gasoline Direct Injection)** — Bosch, Continental, Delphi systems (10–15 % fuel economy gain).
- **HCCI (Homogeneous Charge Compression Ignition)** — used in diesels and research prototypes.
- **Divided Chamber / Pre-chamber Engines** — Honda CVCC, Texaco TCCS.
- **Rotary (Wankel) Stratified Engines** — Mazda RENESIS.
- **Aerospace & Military Multi-fuel Engines** — run on JP-8, kerosene, gasoline, ethanol.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Stratification Ratio and Its Effect on Flame Propagation

**Given:**
- Local rich-zone air–fuel ratio: $(A/F)_{\text{rich}}$
- Bulk lean-mixture air–fuel ratio: $(A/F)_{\text{lean}}$
- Laminar flame speed (Metghalchi–Keck empirical correlation):

$$S_L = S_{L,0} \left( \dfrac{T_u}{T_{\text{ref}}} \right)^{\alpha} \left( \dfrac{P_u}{P_{\text{ref}}} \right)^{\beta} \left( 1 - 2.1 \, y_{\text{res}} \right)$$

where $y_{\text{res}}$ is the residual gas fraction, $\alpha \approx 2.18 - 0.8(\phi - 1)$, $\beta \approx -0.16 + 0.22(\phi - 1)$.

**Derivation Objective:** Show how the *stratification ratio* modifies the effective flame speed.

**Step 1 — Express local equivalence ratios.**
Using $\phi = 1 / (\lambda)$ where $\lambda$ is the relative air–fuel ratio (excess air factor), the rich zone has:

$$\phi_{\text{rich}} = \dfrac{(F/A)_{\text{act, rich}}}{(F/A)_{\text{stoich}}}$$

and the lean bulk has:

$$\phi_{\text{lean}} = \dfrac{(F/A)_{\text{act, lean}}}{(F/A)_{\text{stoich}}} < 1$$

**Step 2 — Define the Stratification Ratio.**

$$\boxed{SR = \dfrac{\lambda_{\text{lean}}}{\lambda_{\text{rich}}} = \dfrac{(A/F)_{\text{lean}}}{(A/F)_{\text{rich}}}}$$

Numerically, if the rich zone is $\phi_{\text{rich}} = 1$ (stoichiometric) at $A/F = 14.7$ and the lean bulk is at $A/F = 40$, then $SR = 40/14.7 \approx 2.72$.

**Step 3 — Relate SR to combustion efficiency.**

The combustion efficiency for a stratified charge can be modeled as a **mass-weighted average** of the local combustion efficiencies in each zone:

$$\eta_{\text{comb}} = f_{\text{rich}} \cdot \eta_{\text{comb, rich}} + (1 - f_{\text{rich}}) \cdot \eta_{\text{comb, lean}}$$

where $f_{\text{rich}}$ is the mass fraction of fuel in the rich zone (typically **0.05 – 0.15** for optimum SCE).

**Step 4 — Lean-burn limit (extinction limit).**
The lean bulk has a *flammability limit*. If the bulk mixture is too lean, the flame will not propagate and the engine misfires. The lean extinction limit is approximately:

$$\phi_{\text{lean, min}} \approx 0.45 \;\; \text{(for methane-air at 1 atm)}$$

For gasoline-air, $\phi_{\text{lean, min}} \approx 0.55$ to $0.6$.

**Step 5 — Implication for KTU numerical problems.**
If a question gives a global (bulk) $A/F$ of **22:1** and the rich zone is stoichiometric ($A/F = 14.7$), the student must compute $SR = 22/14.7 \approx 1.50$. This is a **typical KTU 3-mark or 7-mark sub-question**.

## 3.2 Derivation II — Fuel Economy Improvement of SCE Over Homogeneous Engine

**Given:**
- Homogeneous PFI engine BSFC at part load: $\text{BSFC}_{\text{HSI}} = 380$ g/kWh at 25 % load.
- Stratified charge engine BSFC at same load: $\text{BSFC}_{\text{SCE}} = 290$ g/kWh.
- Brake power: $P_b = 50$ kW.
- Test duration: $t = 1$ h.

**Step 1 — Fuel consumed by HSI engine.**

$$m_{f,\text{HSI}} = \text{BSFC}_{\text{HSI}} \times P_b \times t = 380 \times 10^{-6} \times 50 \times 3600$$

$$m_{f,\text{HSI}} = 380 \times 50 \times 3.6 \times 10^{-3} = 68.4 \;\text{kg}$$

Wait — let's correct units. BSFC is in **g/kWh**, so:

$$m_{f,\text{HSI}} = 380 \;\dfrac{\text{g}}{\text{kWh}} \times 50\;\text{kW} \times 1\;\text{h} = 19{,}000\;\text{g} = 19.0\;\text{kg}$$

**Step 2 — Fuel consumed by SCE.**

$$m_{f,\text{SCE}} = 290 \times 50 \times 1 = 14{,}500\;\text{g} = 14.5\;\text{kg}$$

**Step 3 — Percentage fuel saving.**

$$\%\text{Saving} = \dfrac{m_{f,\text{HSI}} - m_{f,\text{SCE}}}{m_{f,\text{HSI}}} \times 100$$

$$\%\text{Saving} = \dfrac{19.0 - 14.5}{19.0} \times 100 = 23.68\;\text{\%}$$

$$\boxed{\%\text{Saving} \approx 23.7\;\text{\%}}$$

**Step 4 — Engineering interpretation.**
A ~24 % fuel saving at 25 % part load is **typical** for stratified charge operation and is the *primary* reason automotive manufacturers adopted GDI technology from 2000 onwards. **Always show unit cancellation** in your KTU answer sheets — this is one of the most common marks lost by students.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU High-Yield)

| Parameter | Homogeneous PFI (HSI) | Stratified Charge (SCE / GDI) | HCCI |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mixture preparation | Intake manifold | In-cylinder (direct inj.) | Intake + late inj. |
| Spark plug required? | **Yes** | **Yes** | **No** (auto-ignition) |
| Throttling at part load | **Yes** (pumping loss) | **No / minimal** | **No** |
| Overall $\phi$ at part load | ≈ 1.0 | 0.5 – 0.7 (lean) | 0.3 – 0.5 (very lean) |
| BSFC improvement | baseline | **10 – 25 % lower** | **25 – 35 % lower** |
| NOx emissions | Moderate | **High** (high T) | Moderate |
| HC / CO emissions | Moderate-High | **Low** | **Low** |
| Compression ratio | 8:1 – 11:1 | 11:1 – 14:1 | 14:1 – 18:1 |
| Cold-start performance | Good | Poor (wall wetting) | Very poor |
| Multi-fuel capability | Limited | **Good** | Excellent |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Schematic — Stratified Charge Combustion Chamber (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagram below maps the **functional architecture** of a stratified charge engine cylinder, including charge zones, injection timing, and flame propagation logic. It uses only alphanumeric node IDs and clean uppercase labels (no markdown formatting inside node text).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[INTAKE STROKE PURE AIR ONLY] --> A2[COMPRESSION STROKE HIGH CR RATIO 12 TO 14]
    A2 --> A3[END OF COMPRESSION NEAR TDC]
    A3 --> B1[HIGH PRESSURE INJECTOR FIRES 100 TO 350 BAR]
    B1 --> B2[FUEL SPRAY FORMS RICH KERNEL AROUND SPARK PLUG]
    B2 --> C1[SPARK PLUG IGNITES RICH KERNEL PHI APPROX 1]
    C1 --> C2[FLAME PROPAGATES OUTWARD INTO LEAN BULK]
    C2 --> C3[COMBUSTION OF LEAN ZONE A F APPROX 22 TO 30]
    C3 --> D1[POWER STROKE HIGH PRESSURE ON PISTON]
    D1 --> D2[EXHAUST STROKE BURNT GASES EJECTED]
    D2 --> E1[POST CYCLE CHECK NOx HIGH HC LOW BSFC LOW]

    subgraph Zone_Map
        Z1[ZONE 1 SPARK PLUG RICH STOICHIOMETRIC]
        Z2[ZONE 2 INTERMEDIATE TRANSITION]
        Z3[ZONE 3 BULK CHAMBER LEAN EXCESS AIR]
    end
    C1 -.ignites.-> Z1
    C2 -.travels.-> Z2
    C2 -.travels.-> Z3
```

## 4.2 Schematic — Comparison of Mixture Preparation Methods

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PFI[HOMOGENEOUS PFI FUEL IN INTAKE MANIFOLD] --> PFI1[UNIFORM MIXTURE IN CYLINDER]
    PFI1 --> PFI2[SPARK IGNITES HOMOGENEOUS CHARGE]

    GDI[STRATIFIED GDI FUEL DIRECT IN CYLINDER] --> GDI1[NON UNIFORM MIXTURE]
    GDI1 --> GDI2[RICH KERNEL AT SPARK PLUG]
    GDI1 --> GDI3[LEAN BULK IN CHAMBER]
    GDI2 --> GDI4[FLAME FROM KERNEL INTO BULK]
    GDI3 --> GDI4

    HCCI[HCCI HOMOGENEOUS COMPRESSION IGNITION] --> HCCI1[LEAN HOMOGENEOUS MIXTURE]
    HCCI1 --> HCCI2[AUTO IGNITION AT MULTIPLE SITES NO SPARK]
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Typical Divided-Chamber Stratified Engine (Pre-Chamber Type)

> Adapted from the **Texaco Controlled Combustion System (TCCS)** and **Honda CVCC** — the pre-chamber holds a rich mixture; the main chamber holds lean mixture.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    MAIN[MAIN CHAMBER LEAN MIXTURE A F APPROX 25 TO 1]
    PRE[PRE CHAMBER RICH MIXTURE A F APPROX 12 TO 1]
    ORIFICE[ORIFICE CONNECTING PRE CHAMBER TO MAIN CHAMBER]
    SPARK[SPARK PLUG LOCATED IN PRE CHAMBER]
    INJ[FUEL INJECTOR PRE CHAMBER SIDE]

    INJ --> PRE
    SPARK --> PRE
    PRE --> ORIFICE
    ORIFICE --> MAIN
    MAIN --> COMB[COMBUSTION IN BOTH CHAMBERS]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define stratified charge engine. How does it differ from a homogeneous charge engine in terms of mixture preparation?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** A stratified charge engine is one in which the air–fuel mixture inside the combustion chamber is **not uniform**; a **rich mixture (A/F ≈ 12:1 to 14.7:1)** is concentrated near the spark plug while the **bulk of the chamber has a very lean mixture (A/F ≈ 20:1 to 30:1)**.
- **Mixture preparation difference (2 marks):** In a **homogeneous charge (PFI) engine**, fuel and air are mixed in the **intake manifold** long before entering the cylinder, producing a spatially uniform charge. In a **stratified charge (GDI / pre-chamber) engine**, fuel is **injected directly into the cylinder near TDC** (or into a pre-chamber), so the equivalence ratio $\phi$ varies spatially with a *rich* zone at the ignition point and a *lean* zone elsewhere.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List any four advantages and two disadvantages of stratified charge engines.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Advantages (2 marks — any four):**
1. Improved part-load fuel economy (10 – 25 % lower BSFC) due to elimination of throttling losses.
2. Lower unburnt HC and CO emissions (excess O₂ in lean zones oxidizes CO → CO₂).
3. Multi-fuel capability (can run on low-cetane fuels, alcohols, kerosene).
4. Higher compression ratio possible (12:1 – 14:1) → higher thermal efficiency.
5. Lower CO₂ (greenhouse gas) per km driven.

**Disadvantages (1 mark — any two):**
1. Higher NOx emissions (high flame temperature in lean burn).
2. Complexity and cost of high-pressure injection system (100 – 350 bar).
3. Cold-start difficulty (fuel wall-wetting in GDI).
4. Particulate (soot) formation in overly rich kernel regions.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A — 14 Marks** [KTU ESE Model – Module 2 Choice Pattern]

**(a)** With the help of a neat schematic, explain the working principle of a **direct-injection stratified charge spark-ignition (DISI / GDI) engine**. Discuss the role of the rich kernel and the lean bulk zone. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive an expression for the **fuel saving percentage** of a stratified charge engine compared to a homogeneous charge engine operating at 30 % part load. Given: $\text{BSFC}_{\text{HSI}} = 370$ g/kWh, $\text{BSFC}_{\text{SCE}} = 280$ g/kWh, brake power = 60 kW. Comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Working Principle of DISI / GDI Engine (7 marks):**

1. **Intake stroke (1 mark):** Only **pure air** (no fuel) is drawn into the cylinder. There is **no throttle plate restriction**; the throttle is fully or nearly fully open, eliminating pumping losses.

2. **Compression stroke (1 mark):** Air is compressed to a high pressure (typical compression ratio **12:1 to 14:1**). The in-cylinder pressure and temperature near TDC are sufficient for auto-ignition resistance (knock resistance).

3. **Injection (2 marks):** Near TDC, a **high-pressure (100 – 350 bar) solenoid or piezo injector** sprays fuel *directly* into the combustion chamber. The fuel spray is directed so that the **rich, vaporizing cone** of fuel is concentrated around the **spark plug** (typically centrally mounted).

4. **Ignition + Combustion (2 marks):** The **spark plug fires** within milliseconds, igniting the **rich kernel** (local $A/F \approx 12:1$ to $14.7:1$, i.e., $\phi \approx 1$). The flame kernel grows rapidly and **propagates outward** into the surrounding **lean bulk mixture** (local $A/F \approx 22:1$ to $30:1$, i.e., $\phi \approx 0.5$).

5. **Power + exhaust (1 mark):** Combustion of the lean bulk completes the energy release, the high-pressure gases drive the piston down on the power stroke, and exhaust gases are expelled.

**Schematic (required for full marks):** Refer to **Section 4.1** of these notes for the Mermaid block-level architecture.

**Role of rich kernel:** Guarantees reliable flame kernel formation and rapid early flame development (lean mixtures have low laminar flame speed $S_L$ and are difficult to ignite).

**Role of lean bulk:** Allows the engine to operate with **excess air** overall, eliminating throttling losses and improving part-load fuel economy.

---

**(b) Derivation of Fuel Saving Percentage (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Set up the BSFC formula.**

$$\text{BSFC} = \dfrac{m_f}{P_b \cdot t}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Calculate fuel consumed by HSI engine.**

$$m_{f,\text{HSI}} = \text{BSFC}_{\text{HSI}} \times P_b \times t = 370\;\dfrac{\text{g}}{\text{kWh}} \times 60\;\text{kW} \times 1\;\text{h}$$

$$m_{f,\text{HSI}} = 22{,}200\;\text{g} = 22.2\;\text{kg}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Calculate fuel consumed by SCE.**

$$m_{f,\text{SCE}} = 280\;\dfrac{\text{g}}{\text{kWh}} \times 60\;\text{kW} \times 1\;\text{h}$$

$$m_{f,\text{SCE}} = 16{,}800\;\text{g} = 16.8\;\text{kg}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Apply the percentage-saving formula.**

$$\%\text{Saving} = \dfrac{m_{f,\text{HSI}} - m_{f,\text{SCE}}}{m_{f,\text{HSI}}} \times 100$$

$$\%\text{Saving} = \dfrac{22.2 - 16.8}{22.2} \times 100 = 24.32\;\text{\%}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Comment.**
A **~24 % fuel saving** at 30 % part load is a substantial improvement and is **typical of stratified charge operation** at low-to-medium loads. This saving comes primarily from **eliminated throttling (pumping) losses** and from the engine's ability to run on much leaner overall mixtures while still maintaining reliable ignition via the rich kernel.

$$\boxed{\%\text{Fuel Saving} \approx 24.3\;\text{\%}}$$

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** [KTU ESE Model]

**(a)** Explain the **Texaco Controlled Combustion System (TCCS)** with a labelled diagram. How does the pre-chamber create a stratified charge? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **stratified charge spark ignition (SCSI)** and **homogeneous charge compression ignition (HCCI)** engines with respect to: (i) ignition source, (ii) overall equivalence ratio, (iii) NOx emissions, (iv) cold-start behavior, (v) compression ratio, (vi) throttling, (vii) main advantage. **(7 marks — 1 mark each)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Texaco Controlled Combustion System (TCCS) (7 marks):**

1. **Architecture (2 marks):** The TCCS uses a **divided (two) combustion chamber**:
   - A small **pre-chamber** (auxiliary chamber) connected to the main chamber by a narrow **orifice** or throat.
   - A large **main chamber** above the piston where the bulk of the air resides.

2. **Mixture formation (2 marks):** Fuel is **injected into the pre-chamber** (or into the main chamber through a dual-injection system). The **pre-chamber is rich** (locally stoichiometric, $A/F \approx 14.7:1$); the **main chamber is lean** ($A/F \approx 22:1$ to $30:1$). This spatial difference is the *stratification*.

3. **Combustion sequence (2 marks):** A **spark plug located inside the pre-chamber** ignites the rich mixture. The burning rich gases **jet through the orifice** into the main chamber at high velocity, **entraining the lean air-fuel mixture** and **igniting it by torch effect**. The flame front spreads throughout the main chamber, completing combustion.

4. **Key benefit (1 mark):** The torch-flame from the pre-chamber is very effective at igniting the *lean* main-chamber mixture — much more so than a spark alone in a lean bulk would be. This allows very lean overall operation with stable combustion.

**Refer to Section 4.3** for the corresponding Mermaid architecture.

---

**(b) Comparison Table (7 marks):**

| # | Parameter | SCSI (Stratified SI) | HCCI (Homogeneous CI) |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) | Ignition source | **Spark plug** | **Auto-ignition** (no spark) |
| (ii) | Overall $\phi$ | 0.5 – 0.7 (lean) | 0.3 – 0.5 (very lean) |
| (iii) | NOx | **High** (high local T) | Moderate (lower peak T) |
| (iv) | Cold-start | Poor (wall wetting) | **Very poor** (cold charge) |
| (v) | Compression ratio | 11:1 – 14:1 | **14:1 – 18:1** |
| (vi) | Throttling | No / minimal | No |
| (vii) | Main advantage | Multi-fuel + part-load economy | Highest thermal efficiency |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas in Stratified Charge Engine Questions (Verified by KTU Board Examiners):**
> 1. **Confusing "homogeneous" with "uniform overall A/F" globally in SCE** — the *overall* A/F in SCE is lean, but the *local* A/F near the spark is rich. Examiners deduct 1 – 2 marks if this distinction is missing.
> 2. **Forgetting to write units in numerical answers** — KTU answer scripts lose ~0.5 to 1 mark per sub-question when SI units (g/kWh, kg, %, MPa) are omitted.
> 3. **Skipping the schematic in long answers** — Part B (a) questions that demand "neat schematic" *always* carry 1 – 2 marks for the diagram. Even a labelled block diagram earns partial credit.
> 4. **Writing `|` inside markdown tables in your answer sheet** — this breaks the table layout. Use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX-style answers.
> 5. **Not showing step-by-step calculation in derivations** — examiners award step marks. Even if the final answer is wrong, partial credit is given for correct intermediate algebra. **Always show all steps.**
> 6. **Confusing TCCS (pre-chamber) with GDI (direct injection)** — they are *both* stratified but operate on different principles. TCCS uses a divided chamber + spark; GDI sprays fuel directly into a single chamber.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stratified Charge Engines — Key Takeaways for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- ✅ **Definition:** Non-uniform A/F inside the cylinder — rich kernel near spark plug, lean bulk elsewhere.
- ✅ **Equivalence ratio** $\phi$ varies spatially (key distinction from homogeneous engines).
- ✅ **Stratification Ratio (SR)** = (A/F of lean bulk) / (A/F of rich zone) — typical **2:1 to 6:1**.
- ✅ **Main benefit:** **10 – 25 % lower BSFC at part load** due to eliminated throttling losses.
- ✅ **Operating sequence:** Intake (air only) → Compression → Late direct injection → Spark → Combustion.
- ✅ **TCCS / CVCC** = pre-chamber rich + main chamber lean, connected by orifice (torch ignition).
- ✅ **GDI** = high-pressure (100 – 350 bar) direct injection into single chamber, spark centrally located.
- ✅ **Compression ratio** for SCE: typically **12:1 to 14:1** (higher than PFI for knock resistance).
- ✅ **NOx is HIGHER** in stratified engines (Zeldovich mechanism — high flame temperature).
- ✅ **HC and CO are LOWER** in stratified engines (excess O₂ in lean zones oxidizes them).
- ✅ **Multi-fuel capability** is a major advantage — can run on gasoline, ethanol, methanol, kerosene, JP-8.
- ✅ **Cold-start** is a weakness (wall wetting of fuel spray in GDI).
- ✅ **HCCI** = no spark, auto-ignites lean homogeneous mixture via high compression ratio (14:1 – 18:1).
- ✅ **Key formula:** $\%\text{Saving} = \dfrac{m_{f,\text{HSI}} - m_{f,\text{SCE}}}{m_{f,\text{HSI}}} \times 100$.
- ✅ **Key formula:** $\text{BSFC} = \dfrac{m_f}{P_b \cdot t}$ in g/kWh.
- ✅ **Always write units, show steps, and draw a labelled schematic** in KTU 14-mark answers.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reactivity Controlled Compression Ignition (RCCI) Engines
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT744 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Reactivity Controlled Compression Ignition (RCCI) Engines

---

# Reactivity Controlled Compression Ignition (RCCI) Engines

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reactivity Controlled Compression Ignition (RCCI)** is an advanced **Low-Temperature Combustion (LTC)** strategy in which two or more fuels with widely different auto-ignition reactivities are strategically introduced into the combustion chamber to create a *spatially and temporally stratified reactivity field*. The resulting auto-ignition front is governed by in-cylinder **mixing-controlled kinetics** rather than by a spark (SI) or a pure diffusion flame (conventional CI).

RCCI is widely classified as a *Dual-Fuel / Multi-Mode Advanced Combustion* technique under the umbrella of **Kalghatgi-style gasoline compression ignition (GCI)** research and is endorsed in modern KTU syllabus literature as the most commercially viable bridge between homogeneous HCCI and stratified PCCI.

**Key design pillars:**
- **Reactivity gradient** is engineered *in situ* — not by spark timing.
- Combustion is **premixed-dominant** for the bulk charge and **mixing-assisted** for ignition control.
- The classical Knocking boundary is *intentionally crossed* but in a controlled, low-rate manner.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Two-Chef Kitchen"

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine a kitchen with two chefs cooking one dish together.
> - **Chef A (Low Reactivity Fuel — e.g., gasoline):** preps the *slow-cooking base* — it enters early (port injection) and gets uniformly distributed throughout the cylinder.
> - **Chef B (High Reactivity Fuel — e.g., diesel):** adds a *fast-searing spice* right at the top of the compression stroke (direct injection).
>
> As the piston rises, the *mixture* transitions from "mostly bland + small spicy pockets" to "uniformly seasoned." Ignition therefore begins *locally* where the spice is dense and propagates *gradually* into the bland base — giving a long, controlled, evenly-timed cook instead of an explosive burn.

This is exactly what happens inside an RCCI cylinder: instead of an instantaneous explosion (like in SI) or a spray-controlled flame (like in diesel), we get a **smooth, reactivity-graded auto-ignition wave**.

### 1.3 Quantitative Metrics of RCCI Operation

| Metric | Typical Range | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Premixed Burn Fraction** | **70–95 %** | Quantifies how much fuel burns in premixed mode |
| **CA10–CA90** (combustion duration) | 8°–25° crank angle | Mass burn duration |
| **CA50** (50 % MFB crank angle) | **5°–15° aTDC** | Combustion phasing |
| **Indicated Thermal Efficiency** | **42–48 %** | Near-attainment of Diesel-like efficiency |
| **NOx (raw)** | **< 50 ppm** | Comparable to HCCI |
| **PM (raw)** | **< 0.01 g/kWh** | Comparable to SI gasoline |
| **PRF Number** of high-react. fuel | **0–40** | e.g., n-heptane, diesel |
| **PRF Number** of low-react. fuel | **80–100** | e.g., gasoline, iso-octane |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operating Principle — Step by Step

RCCI operates as a **four-stage stratified combustion process**:

1. **Stage 1 — Port-Injection (Early):** A *low-reactivity* fuel (gasoline, PRF ≈ 90) is injected through the intake port during the **intake stroke**. It mixes *homogeneously* with air and forms a low-reactivity bulk mixture. The effective cetane number of the bulk is artificially suppressed.
2. **Stage 2 — Direct Injection (Late, Near TDC):** A *high-reactivity* fuel (diesel or n-heptane, PRF ≈ 0–30) is injected *directly* into the cylinder at **50°–10° BTDC** via a common-rail injector. This creates *localized reactivity pockets* with very high cetane number.
3. **Stage 3 — Reactivity-Triggered Auto-ignition:** As compression raises the bulk gas temperature, the *high-reactivity pockets* ignite first because their local reactivity exceeds the critical threshold. This ignition acts as the "soft spark" of RCCI.
4. **Stage 4 — Propagation Wave:** The reaction front then *propagates* through the surrounding low-reactivity charge. Because this charge has a *designed delay*, the burn rate becomes mixing-controlled and highly tunable by:
   - Injection timing (SOI)
   - Injection pressure
   - EGR %
   - Premixed fuel reactivity (RON octane number)
   - Compression ratio

### 2.2 Why RCCI Works — The Chemistry Behind It

The **two-fuel reactivity concept** exploits the fundamental property that ignition delay varies *exponentially* with reactivity:

$$\tau_{ig} \propto \frac{1}{CN} \cdot \exp\left(\frac{E_a}{R T}\right) \cdot [O_2]^{-n}$$

Where $CN$ is the cetane number (a proxy for reactivity), $E_a$ is the global activation energy, and $T$ is the unburned mixture temperature.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" behind RCCI's success:** In conventional diesel, the *entire* charge has high reactivity → fires almost simultaneously with a sharp pressure rise rate (PRR > 10 bar/°CA → engine knock / NVH issues). In HCCI, the *entire* charge has the *same* low reactivity → ignition occurs almost simultaneously when the global temperature crosses the threshold (again, high PRR). **RCCI alone engineers a smooth gradient so that different zones ignite at different crank angles** — distributing the heat release rate over a wider window.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following markdown table contains the **entire suite of equations** you need for RCCI-related numerical and analytical questions. Master them.

| # | Equation / Parameter | Expression | Meaning / Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reactivity Stratification Index | $RSI = \dfrac{CN_{HI} - CN_{LO}}{CN_{HI} + CN_{LO}}$ | Quantifies the *spread* of reactivity; typically $\mathbf{0.4 \leq RSI \leq 0.85}$ for stable RCCI |
| 2 | Cetane–Octane Equivalence | $CN \approx 60 - 0.5 \cdot RON$ | Empirical link between reactivity scales |
| 3 | PRF Blend Number | $RON_{blend} = x_{iso} \cdot 100 + x_{n-h} \cdot 0$ | Used to *synthesize* desired reactivity |
| 4 | Ignition Delay (Arrhenius) | $\tau_{ig} = A \cdot P^{-n} \exp(E_a / RT)$ | Governing kinetic equation |
| 5 | Mass Fraction Burned | $x_b = 1 - \exp\left[-a \left(\dfrac{\theta - \theta_0}{\Delta\theta}\right)^m\right]$ (Wiebe) | Describes RCCI burn profile (typically $m \approx 2$, $a \approx 5$) |
| 6 | CA50 Phasing | $\theta_{50} = f(SOI, EGR, RON, \varepsilon, N)$ | The single most important tuning parameter |
| 7 | PRR Limit | $\left(\dfrac{dP}{d\theta}\right)_{max} \leq 5 \text{ bar/°CA}$ | NVH constraint |
| 8 | Indicated Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{it} = \dfrac{W_i}{m_f \cdot Q_{LHV}}$ | Reaches **0.45–0.48** in well-tuned RCCI |
| 9 | Premixed Energy Fraction | $f_{pre} = \dfrac{m_{f,port}}{m_{f,total}}$ | Typically **0.7–0.95** |
| 10 | Equivalence Ratio (bulk) | $\phi_b = \dfrac{F/A}{(F/A)_{stoich}}$ | Held **0.30–0.55** (lean) |
| 11 | Dilution Limit | $x_{EGR} \leq 50\%$ | Beyond this, misfire and HC rise sharply |
| 12 | Combustion Efficiency | $\eta_c = 1 - \dfrac{\dot{m}_{CO} + \dot{m}_{HC}}{\dot{m}_{f,total}}$ | Critical at low loads (< 0.5) |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Heavy-duty diesel engines** (Caterpillar, MAN) — prototype dual-fuel RCCI achieves **> 46 % brake thermal efficiency** with near-zero PM and 70 % NOx reduction.
- **Light-duty passenger cars** — RCCI concepts underpin research engines that meet Euro VII / EPA Tier 4 without SCR or DPF.
- **Marine / locomotive prime movers** — dual-fuel RCCI (diesel + natural gas) is in pre-commercial trials by **Wärtsilä** and **MAN Energy Solutions**.
- **Sustainable fuels** — RCCI is the *most flexible* combustion system for **CO₂-neutral fuels** (ammonia, methanol, H₂) because fuel reactivity can be *dialed in* by blending.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Wiebe Function for RCCI Combustion Profile

The **Wiebe function** is the standard KTU-level analytical tool for characterising RCCI mass-burn rate. We derive its foundational form.

**Starting Assumption:** Mass burn rate is proportional to the unburned mass, raised to a power $n$, modulated by an exponential timescale.

$$\frac{dx_b}{d\theta} = \frac{a}{\Delta\theta} \cdot \left(\dfrac{\theta - \theta_0}{\Delta\theta}\right)^{m+1} \cdot \exp\left[-a\left(\dfrac{\theta - \theta_0}{\Delta\theta}\right)^{m+1}\right]$$

where $\theta_0$ is the start of combustion, $\Delta\theta$ is the total burn duration, $a$ is the *Wiebe efficiency parameter* (typically **$a = 5$** → covers **99.33 %** of mass), and $m$ is the *shape factor* (**$m = 2$** for RCCI, **$m = 3$** for SI).

**Step 1 — Integration for Cumulative Mass Burned Fraction:**

$$x_b(\theta) = \int_{\theta_0}^{\theta} \frac{dx_b}{d\theta} \, d\theta$$

**Step 2 — Substitution:** Let $u = a\left(\dfrac{\theta - \theta_0}{\Delta\theta}\right)^{m+1}$. Then $du = \dfrac{a(m+1)}{\Delta\theta}\left(\dfrac{\theta - \theta_0}{\Delta\theta}\right)^{m} d\theta$.

**Step 3 — Evaluate the Integral:**

$$\int_{0}^{u} e^{-u'} \, du' = 1 - e^{-u}$$

**Step 4 — Back-Substitute:**

$$\boxed{\; x_b(\theta) = 1 - \exp\!\left[-a\left(\dfrac{\theta - \theta_0}{\Delta\theta}\right)^{m+1}\right] \;}$$

This is the **master Wiebe equation** used in every RCCI numerical problem.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: RCCI Combustion Phasing (CA50) — Analytical Matrix

The location of 50 % mass burned (**CA50**) is the *single most important tunable parameter* in RCCI. We derive it from the Wiebe function.

**Step 1 — Set $x_b = 0.5$ in the Wiebe equation:**

$$0.5 = 1 - \exp\!\left[-a\left(\dfrac{\theta_{50} - \theta_0}{\Delta\theta}\right)^{m+1}\right]$$

**Step 2 — Simplify:**

$$\exp\!\left[-a\left(\dfrac{\theta_{50} - \theta_0}{\Delta\theta}\right)^{m+1}\right] = 0.5$$

**Step 3 — Take natural logarithm of both sides:**

$$-a\left(\dfrac{\theta_{50} - \theta_0}{\Delta\theta}\right)^{m+1} = \ln(0.5) = -\ln 2$$

**Step 4 — Solve for CA50:**

$$\left(\dfrac{\theta_{50} - \theta_0}{\Delta\theta}\right)^{m+1} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{a}$$

**Step 5 — Final Closed Form:**

$$\boxed{\; \theta_{50} = \theta_0 + \Delta\theta \cdot \left(\dfrac{\ln 2}{a}\right)^{\tfrac{1}{m+1}} \;}$$

**Step 6 — Plug in RCCI Typical Values** ($\theta_0 = -10°$ aTDC, $\Delta\theta = 25°$CA, $a = 5$, $m = 2$):

$$\theta_{50} = -10 + 25 \cdot \left(\dfrac{0.693}{5}\right)^{1/3}$$

$$\theta_{50} = -10 + 25 \cdot (0.1386)^{0.3333}$$

$$\theta_{50} = -10 + 25 \cdot 0.5184$$

$$\boxed{\; \theta_{50} \approx +2.96° \text{ aTDC} \;}$$

This matches the empirically-observed optimum **CA50 of +3° to +8° aTDC** for maximum indicated efficiency in RCCI engines.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Reactivity Stratification Index (RSI)

The **RSI** quantifies *how reactive-stratified* the cylinder charge is.

**Definition:**

$$RSI = \dfrac{CN_{HI} - CN_{LO}}{CN_{HI} + CN_{LO}}$$

**Step 1 — Apply for diesel/gasoline pair** ($CN_{HI} = 55$ for diesel, $CN_{LO} = 15$ for gasoline — converted using $CN \approx 60 - 0.5 RON$, so $RON_{gasoline} = 90 \Rightarrow CN = 15$):

$$RSI = \dfrac{55 - 15}{55 + 15} = \dfrac{40}{70} = 0.571$$

**Step 2 — Interpretation:** $RSI = 0.571$ falls in the *stable RCCI window* ($0.4 < RSI < 0.85$).

**Step 3 — Boundary Cases:**
- $RSI \rightarrow 0$ → both fuels equally reactive → collapses to HCCI (no stratification).
- $RSI \rightarrow 1$ → maximum gradient → collapses to partially premixed CI (high PRR).

**Step 4 — Limiting Reactivity Ratio:** Often also reported as $RR = CN_{HI} / CN_{LO} = 55 / 15 \approx 3.67$. Industry rule of thumb: **$2 \leq RR \leq 6$** is desirable.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical — RCCI Premixed Energy

**Problem (KTU Pattern):** A 2.0 L, single-cylinder RCCI engine uses port-injected gasoline (RON 92) and direct-injected diesel (CN 52). The port-injected mass per cycle is **$m_{p} = 14.0$ mg**, and the direct-injected mass per cycle is **$m_{d} = 4.0$ mg**. Calculate (a) RSI, (b) Reactivity Ratio, (c) Premixed Energy Fraction $f_{pre}$.

**(a) Compute CN of gasoline:**

$$CN_{gas} = 60 - 0.5 \cdot 92 = 60 - 46 = 14$$

**(b) RSI Calculation:**

$$RSI = \dfrac{52 - 14}{52 + 14} = \dfrac{38}{66} = 0.576$$

**(c) Reactivity Ratio:**

$$RR = \dfrac{52}{14} = 3.71$$

**(d) Premixed Energy Fraction** (assuming LHV of both fuels are similar so energy ≈ mass):

$$f_{pre} = \dfrac{m_p}{m_p + m_d} = \dfrac{14.0}{14.0 + 4.0} = \dfrac{14.0}{18.0} = 0.778$$

**Conclusion:** All three parameters fall in the optimal RCCI window — the engine will operate in *stable, low-emission* RCCI mode.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — RCCI Functional Architecture

The following mermaid block diagram represents the **functional architecture** of an RCCI engine control loop, showing how the dual-fuel reactivity stratification is *engineered and maintained*.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Engine Speed and Load Demand] --> B[Engine Control Unit ECU]
    B --> C{Strategy Selection}
    C -->|Low Load| D[RCCI Mode - High EGR]
    C -->|Medium Load| E[RCCI Mode - Moderate EGR]
    C -->|High Load| F[Conventional CI Backup]
    D --> G[Port Injector Command]
    D --> H[Common Rail DI Command]
    E --> G
    E --> H
    F --> I[DI Diesel Only]
    G --> J[In-Cylinder Reactivity Field]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Pressure Sensor]
    K --> L[CA50 Estimator]
    L --> M{Phi - CA50 Error}
    M -->|Too Early| N[Retard SOI of DI Fuel]
    M -->|Too Late| O[Advance SOI of DI Fuel]
    N --> B
    O --> B
    M --> P[Combustion Output - Power NOx PM]
    P --> Q[Aftertreatment - SCR DPF TWC Optional]

    classDef ctrl fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef act fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef sen fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    class A,B,C,D,E,F,M,N,O ctrl
    class G,H,I,J act
    class K,L sen
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Process Diagram — Crank-Angle Timeline of RCCI Combustion

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Intake Stroke - Port Injection of Low Reactivity Fuel]
    P2[Compression Stroke 1 - Bulk Homogenization]
    P3[Compression Stroke 2 - Direct Injection of High Reactivity Fuel near TDC]
    P4[Auto Ignition of High Reactivity Pockets]
    P5[Premixed Reactivity Front Propagation]
    P6[Tail Burning of Residual Mixture]
    P7[Power Stroke Expansion]

    P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5 --> P6 --> P7

    classDef stage fill:#9467bd,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    class P1,P2,P3,P4,P5,P6,P7 stage
```

### 4.3 RCCI Cylinder Schematic — Conceptual Stratification Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CC[Combustion Chamber at 20 deg BTDC]
        direction TB
        R1[Zone A - Low Reactivity - Mostly Gasoline - High Ignition Delay]
        R2[Zone B - Medium Reactivity - Mixed Vapour]
        R3[Zone C - High Reactivity - Diesel Spray Core - Lowest Ignition Delay]
    end
    R3 -.Ignition Starts.-> R2
    R2 -.Flame Propagation.-> R1

    classDef low fill:#7fb3d5,stroke:#000
    classDef med fill:#f4d03f,stroke:#000
    classDef high fill:#cb4335,stroke:#fff
    class R1 low
    class R2 med
    class R3 high
```

### 4.4 Comparative Schematic — RCCI vs HCCI vs PCCI vs Conventional CI

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph X[RCCI - This Module]
        X1[Two fuels: port low CN + DI high CN]
        X2[React. gradient engineered in situ]
        X3[Best controllability]
    end
    subgraph Y[HCCI]
        Y1[One fuel - premixed]
        Y2[No gradient - all ignites together]
        Y3[Limited load range]
    end
    subgraph Z[PCCI]
        Z1[One fuel DI very early]
        Z2[Mild gradient via mixing]
        Z3[Compromise between HCCI and CI]
    end
    subgraph W[Conventional CI Diesel]
        W1[Diesel only DI]
        W2[Diffusion flame]
        W3[High NOx and PM]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Pattern]**

#### **Q1. Define Reactivity Controlled Compression Ignition (RCCI). List any three of its major advantages over conventional diesel combustion.**
*(3 Marks — CO2, Remembering/Understanding level)*

**Model Answer:**
RCCI is a **low-temperature dual-fuel combustion strategy** in which a low-reactivity fuel (e.g., gasoline) is introduced through the **port** to create a homogeneous, low-cetane bulk charge, while a high-reactivity fuel (e.g., diesel) is **directly injected** near TDC to create localized ignition kernels. Combustion proceeds as a controlled reactivity-graded auto-ignition front rather than via spark or pure diffusion flame.

**Three major advantages over conventional CI:**
1. **NOx reduction up to 90 %** due to low peak in-cylinder temperatures (typically **< 1800 K**).
2. **Near-zero particulate matter (PM)** because of extensive premixing of fuel with air before combustion.
3. **High indicated thermal efficiency** (**42–48 %**) comparable to conventional diesel but with gasoline-like emissions.
4. (Bonus) Flexibility to use **CO₂-neutral alternative fuels** (methanol, ethanol, ammonia).

---

#### **Q2. What is the Reactivity Stratification Index (RSI)? State the stability window for stable RCCI operation.**
*(3 Marks — CO2, Understanding level)*

**Model Answer:**
The **Reactivity Stratification Index (RSI)** is a non-dimensional parameter that quantifies the *difference in reactivity* between the high-cetane and low-cetane fuel fractions inside the cylinder.

$$RSI = \dfrac{CN_{HI} - CN_{LO}}{CN_{HI} + CN_{LO}}$$

**Stability window:** RCCI operation is empirically stable and controllable in the range:

$$0.40 \leq RSI \leq 0.85$$

Below 0.40, the gradient is too weak and combustion resembles HCCI (high PRR). Above 0.85, the gradient is too steep and the burn resembles partially premixed CI (high PM and noise).

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Module Choice Pattern — Both alternatives carry EQUAL marks; examiners value CLEAN DIAGRAMS and DERIVATIONS.**

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**(a) With the help of a labelled block diagram, explain the operational principle of an RCCI engine. Discuss how the reactivity gradient is established and controlled. (7 Marks)**

**(b) Derive the Wiebe function for the cumulative mass-burned fraction in RCCI combustion. Using typical RCCI parameters, compute the CA50 location. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Operational Principle (7 marks):**
- Introduce RCCI as dual-fuel LTC strategy (**1 mark**).
- State port fuel = low reactivity (RON 85–95), direct fuel = high reactivity (CN 40–55) (**1 mark**).
- Explain four stages with crank-angle notation:
  - Stage 1: Port injection during intake stroke → homogeneous bulk (**1 mark**).
  - Stage 2: DI of high-reactivity fuel 50°–10° BTDC → reactive pockets (**1 mark**).
  - Stage 3: Auto-ignition of pockets when local reactivity × temperature > threshold (**1 mark**).
  - Stage 4: Reactivity wave propagates into low-reactivity bulk (**1 mark**).
- Discuss controls: SOI timing, EGR, compression ratio, port-fuel fraction (**1 mark**).

**(b) Wiebe Derivation & CA50 Calculation (7 marks):**
- Write Wiebe differential form (**1 mark**).
- Substitute $u = a \cdot ((\theta - \theta_0)/\Delta\theta)^{m+1}$ (**1 mark**).
- Integrate to obtain $x_b(\theta) = 1 - e^{-u}$ (**1 mark**).
- Show CA50 derivation: set $x_b = 0.5$, solve for $\theta_{50}$ (**1 mark**).
- Substitute $\theta_0 = -10°$, $\Delta\theta = 25°$, $a = 5$, $m = 2$ (**1 mark**).
- Final numerical answer: $\theta_{50} \approx +3°$ aTDC (**1 mark**).
- Note: full derivation provided in Section 3.1 and 3.2 of these notes.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern — Alternative Choice]**

**(a) Explain with neat sketches the differences between HCCI, PCCI, and RCCI combustion modes. Comment on the advantages and challenges of RCCI. (7 Marks)**

**(b) An RCCI engine uses port-injected PRF 90 (RON = 90) and direct-injected diesel (CN = 50). The mass of port fuel per cycle is 16 mg and DI fuel is 5 mg. Calculate (i) CN of port fuel, (ii) Reactivity Stratification Index, (iii) Reactivity Ratio, and (iv) Premixed Energy Fraction. Comment on whether the engine will operate in stable RCCI mode. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) HCCI vs PCCI vs RCCI (7 marks):**
- **HCCI (Homogeneous Charge CI):** Single fuel, fully premixed charge, *uniform* reactivity → ignition occurs nearly simultaneously when bulk temperature exceeds threshold. **Issue:** High PRR, narrow operating window, combustion-phasing control extremely difficult (**2 marks**).
- **PCCI (Premixed Charge CI):** Single fuel DI'd very early (e.g., 60°–80° BTDC) to create partially premixed charge. Provides *mild* reactivity gradient. **Issue:** Still has hot zones — high HC and CO emissions (**2 marks**).
- **RCCI:** Two fuels with very different reactivities. Reactivity is *engineered* to vary smoothly throughout the chamber. **Advantages:** Wide load range, near-zero NOx and PM, fuel-flexible. **Challenges:** Complex controls, HC/CO at low load, fuel tank integration. (**3 marks**).

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Cetane Number of port fuel** (PRF 90 ⇒ RON = 90):

$$CN_{port} = 60 - 0.5 \cdot 90 = 60 - 45 = \mathbf{15}$$

**(ii) Reactivity Stratification Index:**

$$RSI = \dfrac{50 - 15}{50 + 15} = \dfrac{35}{65} = \mathbf{0.538}$$

**(iii) Reactivity Ratio:**

$$RR = \dfrac{50}{15} = \mathbf{3.33}$$

**(iv) Premixed Energy Fraction** (assuming equal LHV):

$$f_{pre} = \dfrac{16}{16 + 5} = \dfrac{16}{21} = \mathbf{0.762}$$

**Stability Assessment:**
- RSI = 0.538 falls inside the stable RCCI window (0.40–0.85) ✓
- RR = 3.33 falls in the desirable range (2–6) ✓
- $f_{pre} = 0.762$ corresponds to high premixed energy fraction ✓

**Conclusion:** All three parameters lie within the empirically established RCCI stability envelope. The engine will operate in **stable, low-emission RCCI mode**.

*(Award breakdown: 1 mark each for steps i–iii, 2 marks for step iv, 2 marks for stability comment and conclusion.)*

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks on RCCI Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing HCCI with RCCI** — many students write HCCI principles when RCCI is asked. RCCI's defining feature is the **reactivity gradient** controlled by *two* fuels. (Lose up to 3 marks.)
> 2. **Forgetting to convert RON to CN** in numerical problems. Always show $CN = 60 - 0.5 \cdot RON$. (Lose 1 mark.)
> 3. **Skipping the four operational stages** in (a)-part answers. Examiners allocate 1 mark per stage.
> 4. **Not stating the stability windows** (RSI window 0.40–0.85; RR window 2–6). These are *board-favourite* numerical boundaries.
> 5. **Ignoring units** — always state crank angles in °CA, mass in mg, energy fraction as a number between 0 and 1.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** RCCI = dual-fuel LTC mode with in-cylinder reactivity stratification.
- **Fuel pair:** Low-CN fuel via port + High-CN fuel via DI.
- **Reactivity Stratification Index (RSI):** stable window = **0.40–0.85**.
- **Reactivity Ratio (RR):** desirable range = **2–6**.
- **Wiebe function** governs RCCI burn profile: typical $a = 5$, $m = 2$.
- **CA50 optimal target:** **+3° to +8° aTDC** for peak $\eta_{it}$.
- **Premixed Energy Fraction** should be **70–95 %** for stable operation.
- **Pressure Rise Rate (PRR)** must stay below **5 bar/°CA** to avoid knock/NVH.
- **EGR limit:** $\leq$ **50 %** before misfire and HC climb sharply.
- **NOx** in raw exhaust: **< 50 ppm**; **PM** < **0.01 g/kWh**.
- **Indicated thermal efficiency:** **42–48 %**.
- **Major tuning knobs:** SOI, EGR, port-fuel fraction, compression ratio, fuel reactivity (PRF number).
- **Major challenges:** Combustion-phasing control, low-load HC/CO, cold-start, fuel-system complexity, on-board reformer for reactivity control.
- **Real-world relevance:** Caterpillar, MAN, Wärtsilä, Honda RCCI prototype engines; key enabling technology for **sustainable CO₂-neutral fuels**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydrogen Engines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT744 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Hydrogen Engines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Hydrogen Internal Combustion Engine (H2-ICE)** is a reciprocating or rotary heat engine that derives its motive power from the controlled combustion (or, in some advanced variants, near-isothermal expansion) of hydrogen gas (H₂) with an oxidizer—typically air or, in rare cases, pure oxygen—inside a working cylinder. From the perspective of the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *Modern Automotive Technologies*, a hydrogen engine is classified as a **carbon-free or near-zero-carbon prime mover**, in which the primary fuel molecule contains no carbon-hydrogen (C–H) bonds and therefore yields no carbon-based emissions such as CO, CO₂, unburnt hydrocarbons (HC), or soot/particulate matter (PM) during ideal combustion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot**
> A Hydrogen Engine is an ICE modified to operate on H₂ as the primary fuel, distinguished by its very high auto-ignition temperature, wide flammability limits, high flame speed, and near-zero carbon emissions. The fuel may be supplied via Port Fuel Injection (PFI), Direct Injection (DI), or as a hydrogen-air mixture.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Think of a conventional petrol engine as a **wood-burning campfire**: the fuel (gasoline) contains carbon, so when it burns, you inevitably get smoke (carbon emissions), soot (particulate matter), and a relatively slow, uneven flame front.

A hydrogen engine, in contrast, is like a **clean, fast-burning natural gas stove**—but even cleaner. Hydrogen is the simplest and lightest element in the universe, and its combustion product is fundamentally just **water vapor (H₂O)**. Imagine pouring a thimble of water into a chamber and igniting it with a spark—the resulting expansion pushes a piston with remarkable speed and minimal polluting residue.

> **Real-World Analogy:** If gasoline is a "slow-cooking charcoal grill" (sooty, slow flame, lots of residue), then hydrogen is a "Bunsen burner on steroids" (instantaneous flame propagation, near-zero residue, but harder to control because the flame wants to spread *everywhere*).

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following baseline properties of hydrogen are essential for KTU numerical problems and must be memorized:

| Property | Value | Units |
|---|---|---|
| Molecular weight of H₂ | **2.016** | g/mol |
| Density of gaseous H₂ at STP | **0.0899** | kg/m³ |
| Lower Heating Value (LHV) | **119.96** | MJ/kg |
| Higher Heating Value (HHV) | **141.86** | MJ/kg |
| Auto-ignition temperature | **≈ 858** | K (≈ 585 °C) |
| Flammability limits in air (volumetric) | **4% to 75%** | — |
| Flammability limits in oxygen | **4% to 94%** | — |
| Minimum ignition energy | **0.02** | mJ |
| Stoichiometric air-fuel ratio (by mass) | **34.3 : 1** | — |
| Laminar flame speed (in air) | **≈ 2.5 to 3.5** | m/s |
| Diffusion coefficient of H₂ in air | **≈ 0.61 × 10⁻⁴** | m²/s |
| Flame temperature (stoichiometric, adiabatic) | **≈ 2400** | K |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:** Hydrogen has the **lowest minimum ignition energy** of any common fuel (0.02 mJ vs. gasoline's ~0.24 mJ)—roughly **12 times easier to ignite**. This is why hydrogen engines are highly susceptible to pre-ignition and backfire, a point frequently tested in Part A questions.

---

## 1.4 Historical Context & KTU Relevance

Hydrogen as an automotive fuel was first experimentally demonstrated by **Francois Isaac de Rivaz in 1807**, who built a hydrogen-fueled internal combustion engine. Modern research intensified after the **1970s oil crises**, and today, manufacturers like **BMW (Hydrogen 7 series), Toyota, Honda, and Ford** have demonstrated hydrogen-powered ICE vehicles. Under the KTU 2024 NEP-aligned syllabus for *Modern Automotive Technologies*, this topic sits at the intersection of:
- **Alternative fuels** (Module 2)
- **Emission control** (CO₂ reduction mandates)
- **Sustainable mobility** (NEP 2020 environmental sustainability goals)

---

## 1.5 Why Hydrogen Engines Matter — The "Why" Behind the Topic

The engineering rationale for studying hydrogen engines stems from three converging global pressures:

1. **Decarbonization Mandates:** The Paris Agreement and India's Panchamrit targets (500 GW non-fossil capacity by 2030, net-zero by 2070) demand transport-sector decarbonization.
2. **Energy Security:** Hydrogen can be produced domestically from water electrolysis, reducing petroleum import dependency.
3. **Ultra-Low Emissions:** Unlike battery-electric vehicles (BEVs), hydrogen ICEs can use existing ICE manufacturing infrastructure—lowering the capital barrier to clean transport.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (CO Mapping):** This topic directly addresses **CO2 (Understand alternative powertrain concepts)**, **CO3 (Analyze fuel properties and combustion behavior)**, and **CO5 (Evaluate emission and efficiency trade-offs)** of PEMUT744.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Operational Principle — The Heart of a Hydrogen Engine

A hydrogen engine operates on essentially the same **Otto cycle (4-stroke)** or **modified Atkinson/Miller cycle** as a gasoline engine, but with three critical modifications:

### Logic Step 1: Intake (Modified)
- In **Port Fuel Injection (PFI)** hydrogen engines, gaseous H₂ is injected into the intake port just upstream of the intake valve.
- In **Direct Injection (DI)** variants, H₂ is injected directly into the cylinder at high pressure (typically 100–350 bar).
- The charge may be homogeneous (premixed) or stratified (late-injection DI).

### Logic Step 2: Compression (Critical)
- The air-hydrogen (or pure hydrogen for oxygen-breathing engines) mixture is compressed to a high ratio.
- **Critical constraint:** Because hydrogen has such a high auto-ignition temperature (~858 K) and very high flame speed, *abnormal combustion* phenomena (knock, backfire, pre-ignition) become the dominant design challenge.

### Logic Step 3: Combustion
- Ignition occurs via a spark plug.
- The combustion reaction is:

$$
\text{2H}_2 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{2H}_2\text{O} + \text{Energy}
$$

- For air as oxidizer:

$$
\text{2H}_2 + (\text{O}_2 + 3.76\text{N}_2) \rightarrow \text{2H}_2\text{O} + 3.76\text{N}_2
$$

### Logic Step 4: Expansion & Exhaust
- Hot gases expand against the piston, producing work.
- Exhaust contains mainly **H₂O (water vapor)**, excess N₂, and small amounts of **NOx** (the only regulated pollutant of concern).
- On cold starts, water vapor may condense visibly from the tailpipe—an unmistakable signature of a hydrogen vehicle.

## 2.2 Classification of Hydrogen Engines

### A. Based on Fuel Induction Method

| Type | Description | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Premixed (PFI)** | H₂ + air mixed upstream of cylinder | Simple, smooth combustion | High backfire risk, lower power density |
| **Direct Injection (DI)** | H₂ injected directly into cylinder | Higher power, eliminates backfire | Complex injectors, very high pressures needed |
| **Cryogenic Liquid H₂** | Liquid H₂ stored at 20 K | High energy density | Boil-off losses, cryogenic tank cost |
| **Hydride-stored** | H₂ released from metal hydride | Safe, ambient storage | Heavy tanks, slow release |

### B. Based on Aspiration

- **Naturally Aspirated (NA)**
- **Turbocharged / Supercharged**

### C. Based on Cycle

- **4-Stroke Otto**
- **2-Stroke**
- **Homogeneous Charge Compression Ignition (HCCI)** — uses H₂'s high octane rating
- **Reactivity Controlled Compression Ignition (RCCI)**

## 2.3 Thermodynamic Cycle Analysis — The "How" of Power Generation

The ideal Otto cycle efficiency is governed by:

$$
\eta_{\text{Otto}} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}
$$

where:
- $r$ = compression ratio
- $\gamma$ = ratio of specific heats (≈ 1.4 for air; for hydrogen-air mixtures $\gamma \approx 1.35$–$1.40$)

### The CR Paradox of Hydrogen Engines

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Key Trade-Off:**
> Hydrogen's **high octane rating (RON > 130)** permits *very high compression ratios* (theoretically up to **22:1**) — which would dramatically improve thermal efficiency. **HOWEVER**, abnormal combustion (knock and backfire) practically limits real-world CR to **12:1 to 14:1** for PFI engines. DI systems can push CR higher by injecting fuel late.

## 2.4 Abnormal Combustion Phenomena — The Engineer's Nemesis

### 2.4.1 Pre-Ignition
Endothermic auto-ignition of H₂ before the spark, often due to hot-spot deposits or residual heat. Mitigation: **excess air (lean burn)**, hot-spot elimination, oil-cooling.

### 2.4.2 Backfire (Most Common Failure Mode)
Combustion occurring in the intake manifold during the intake stroke. Caused by residual hot gases igniting premixed H₂-air. Mitigation:
- **DI injection** (eliminates the problem entirely)
- **Lean operation**
- **Effective exhaust gas recirculation (EGR)**

### 2.4.3 Knocking
Uncontrolled, rapid auto-ignition of end-gas. With hydrogen's low minimum ignition energy, knock is a persistent issue.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Relationship | Variables | Typical KTU Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\eta_{\text{Otto}} = 1 - r^{1-\gamma}$ | $r$ = CR; $\gamma$ = specific heat ratio | Compute theoretical efficiency |
| 2 | $\text{AFR}_{\text{stoich}} = 34.3$ | Mass basis | Stoichiometric mixture calculation |
| 3 | $Q_{\text{LHV,H}_2} = 119.96$ MJ/kg | LHV | Energy content per kg |
| 4 | $\phi_{\text{equiv}} = (F/A)_{\text{actual}} / (F/A)_{\text{stoich}}$ | Equivalence ratio | Lean burn analysis |
| 5 | $\dot{m}_{\text{H}_2} = \frac{P \cdot \text{BSFC}}{Q_{\text{LHV}}}$ | Mass flow rate of H₂ | Fuel consumption problem |
| 6 | $\text{BSEC} = \frac{\dot{m}_{\text{fuel}} \cdot Q_{\text{LHV}}}{P_{\text{brake}}}$ | Brake Specific Energy Consumption | Compare efficiency vs. gasoline |
| 7 | $\text{NO}_x \propto \exp(-E_a / RT_{\text{adiabatic}})$ | Zeldovich mechanism | Predict NOx formation |
| 8 | $V_{\text{H}_2 \text{ per km}} = \frac{\text{Energy/km}}{Q_{\text{LHV}} \cdot \rho_{\text{H}_2}}$ | Volumetric fuel rate | Compare tank size to gasoline |
| 9 | $T_{\text{adiabatic flame}} = T_0 + \frac{Q_{\text{LHV}}}{m_{\text{mix}} \cdot c_{p,\text{mix}}}$ | Adiabatic flame temperature | Lean burn limit determination |
| 10 | $\text{Volumetric efficiency } \eta_v = \frac{\dot{m}_{\text{actual}}}{\dot{m}_{\text{theoretical}}}$ | VE | Performance evaluation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Tip:** KTU frequently asks to compare BSEC (Brake Specific Energy Consumption) of hydrogen vs. gasoline. Hydrogen typically shows BSEC ≈ 1.4×–1.6× that of gasoline due to displacement of air by H₂, but the *thermodynamic efficiency* is often higher.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Hydrogen engines serve as a **transitional technology**:
- **Maritime propulsion** (e.g., Wärtsilä engines)
- **Heavy-duty trucking** (MAN, Scania prototypes)
- **Stationary power generation**
- **Racing vehicles** (BMW, Toyota Le Mans prototypes)

The core utility is that hydrogen ICEs reuse existing ICE manufacturing infrastructure—an enormous economic advantage over BEVs for certain duty cycles.

---

## 2.7 Lean Burn Strategy — The Cornerstone of H₂ Engine Operation

Lean burn (excess air, $\phi < 1$) is the single most important strategy for hydrogen engines:

**Why lean burn?**
1. **Reduces peak flame temperature** → suppresses NOx
2. **Improves fuel economy** (denser air charge)
3. **Suppresses knock and backfire**
4. **Permits higher compression ratios**

**The Lean Limit:** Beyond a certain excess air ratio (typically $\lambda > 2.5$), combustion becomes unstable due to slower flame propagation. KTU commonly tests this limit.

## 2.8 NOx Formation — The Sole Regulated Pollutant

Despite zero CO₂ emissions, hydrogen engines produce **NOx** via the **extended Zeldovich mechanism**:

$$
\text{O} + \text{N}_2 \rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{N}
$$

$$
\text{N} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{O}
$$

Mitigation strategies:
- **EGR (Exhaust Gas Recirculation)**
- **Lean-burn operation**
- **Three-way catalytic converters** (modified formulations)
- **Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR)** with urea injection

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical Air-Fuel Ratio for Hydrogen

We begin with the balanced stoichiometric combustion equation for hydrogen in air:

$$
\text{2H}_2 + (\text{O}_2 + 3.76\text{N}_2) \rightarrow \text{2H}_2\text{O} + 3.76\text{N}_2
$$

**Step 1 — Identify molecular weights:**

$$
M_{\text{H}_2} = 2 \times 1.008 = 2.016 \text{ kg/kmol}
$$

$$
M_{\text{O}_2} = 32 \text{ kg/kmol}
$$

$$
M_{\text{N}_2} = 28 \text{ kg/kmol}
$$

**Step 2 — Mass of fuel (H₂) per kmol of H₂:**

$$
m_{\text{fuel}} = 2 \times 2.016 = 4.032 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 3 — Mass of oxidizer (air) required:**

Mass of O₂ per kmol of H₂ burned = 1 kmol = 32 kg.

Mass of N₂ accompanying O₂ = $3.76 \times 28 = 105.28$ kg.

Mass of air = mass of O₂ + mass of N₂:

$$
m_{\text{air}} = 32 + 105.28 = 137.28 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 4 — Stoichiometric AFR (mass basis):**

$$
\text{AFR}_{\text{stoich}} = \frac{m_{\text{air}}}{m_{\text{fuel}}} = \frac{137.28}{4.032}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{AFR}_{\text{stoich}} \approx 34.05 \text{ (rounded to } 34.3 \text{ in practice)}}
$$

**Interpretation:** For every 1 kg of H₂ burned, ~34 kg of air is needed stoichiometrically—far higher than gasoline (~14.7:1).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Energy Density Comparison (H₂ vs Gasoline)

**Step 1 — Energy per kg:**

$$
Q_{\text{H}_2} = 119.96 \text{ MJ/kg}
$$

$$
Q_{\text{gasoline}} \approx 44 \text{ MJ/kg}
$$

**Step 2 — Energy per unit volume (gaseous H₂ at 200 bar storage):**

Using ideal gas approximation: $V = nRT/P$.

At 200 bar and 300 K, density of compressed gaseous H₂ is approximately:

$$
\rho_{\text{H}_2, 200\text{bar}} \approx 15 \text{ kg/m}^3
$$

Energy density by volume:

$$
E_{\text{H}_2,\text{vol}} = 119.96 \times 15 = 1799.4 \text{ MJ/m}^3
$$

For gasoline:

$$
\rho_{\text{gasoline}} \approx 750 \text{ kg/m}^3
$$

$$
E_{\text{gasoline,vol}} = 44 \times 750 = 33{,}000 \text{ MJ/m}^3
$$

**Step 3 — Volume ratio:**

$$
\frac{V_{\text{H}_2}}{V_{\text{gasoline}}} = \frac{33{,}000}{1799.4} \approx 18.34
$$

**Conclusion:** Compressed gaseous H₂ at 200 bar requires **~18× more volume** than gasoline for equivalent energy. This is why liquid hydrogen or 700-bar storage is essential for automotive applications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Insight:** A common question type is to compute the mass of H₂ required to cover a given driving distance, given the BSFC (or BSEC) of the engine.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Equivalence Ratio and Lean Burn Limit Analysis

**Definition of equivalence ratio:**

$$
\phi = \frac{(F/A)_{\text{actual}}}{(F/A)_{\text{stoich}}}
$$

**For lean burn:** $\phi < 1$, equivalently $\lambda > 1$ where $\lambda = 1/\phi$.

**Example KTU-style problem:** A hydrogen engine operates at $\lambda = 2.0$. Compute the actual mass of air per kg of fuel.

**Step 1 — Compute stoichiometric AFR:**

From §3.1: $\text{AFR}_{\text{stoich}} = 34.05$

**Step 2 — Apply $\lambda$:**

$$
\text{AFR}_{\text{actual}} = \lambda \times \text{AFR}_{\text{stoich}} = 2.0 \times 34.05 = 68.1
$$

**Step 3 — Per 1 kg H₂:**

$$
m_{\text{air}} = 68.1 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 4 — Mass of mixture entering cylinder per kg fuel:**

$$
m_{\text{mix}} = m_{\text{fuel}} + m_{\text{air}} = 1 + 68.1 = 69.1 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 5 — Mass fraction of fuel in mixture:**

$$
y_{\text{fuel}} = \frac{1}{69.1} \approx 0.01448 \text{ or } 1.448\%
$$

**Interpretation:** Even in lean operation, hydrogen occupies a small mass fraction but a *very large volumetric fraction* (because of its low density), displacing significant air.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Adiabatic Flame Temperature for Lean H₂-Air Mixture

For a lean mixture at equivalence ratio $\phi$, energy balance gives:

$$
T_{\text{ad}} = T_0 + \frac{\phi \cdot Y_{\text{fuel}} \cdot Q_{\text{LHV}}}{c_{p,\text{mix}}}
$$

where $Y_{\text{fuel}}$ is the fuel mass fraction.

**Worked example:** Find $T_{\text{ad}}$ at $\phi = 0.5$ ($T_0 = 300$ K, assume $c_{p,\text{mix}} = 1.05$ kJ/kg·K).

**Step 1 — Compute $Y_{\text{fuel}}$ at $\phi = 0.5$:**

Actual AFR = $\phi \times \text{AFR}_{\text{stoich}} = 0.5 \times 34.05 = 17.025$

$$
Y_{\text{fuel}} = \frac{1}{1 + 17.025} = \frac{1}{18.025} \approx 0.0555
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$
T_{\text{ad}} = 300 + \frac{0.5 \times 0.0555 \times 119{,}960}{1.05 \times 1000}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerator evaluation:**

$$
\text{Numerator} = 0.5 \times 0.0555 \times 119{,}960 = 3328.89
$$

**Step 4 — Denominator evaluation:**

$$
\text{Denominator} = 1.05 \times 1000 = 1050
$$

**Step 5 — Final result:**

$$
T_{\text{ad}} = 300 + \frac{3328.89}{1050} = 300 + 3.17 \approx 303.17 \text{ K? (ERROR!)}
$$

**Correction:** We forgot that $c_{p,\text{mix}}$ is per kg and $Q_{\text{LHV}}$ is in kJ/kg. Let me redo with consistent units:

$Q_{\text{LHV}} = 119.96$ MJ/kg = 119,960 kJ/kg.

$\phi \cdot Y_{\text{fuel}} \cdot Q_{\text{LHV}} = 0.5 \times 0.0555 \times 119{,}960 = 3328.89$ kJ/kg of mixture.

Then:

$$
\Delta T = \frac{3328.89 \text{ kJ/kg}}{1.05 \text{ kJ/kg·K}} \approx 3170 \text{ K}
$$

So:

$$
T_{\text{ad}} = 300 + 3170 = 3470 \text{ K}
$$

This exceeds the standard stoichiometric value because at very lean conditions with a fixed reference, the calculation requires including excess air's heating capacity more carefully. **For stoichiometric ($\phi = 1$):** $T_{\text{ad}} \approx 2400$ K.

**Revised calculation using $c_p$ averaged for products ($\text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{N}_2$):**

For lean $\phi = 0.5$, $c_{p,\text{products}} \approx 1.40$ kJ/kg·K (includes excess N₂ and H₂O vapor).

Per kg of mixture, products carry $Q = 3328.89$ kJ.

$$
T_{\text{ad}} = 300 + \frac{3328.89}{1.40} \approx 300 + 2378 = 2678 \text{ K}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key takeaway:** Higher excess air ratio lowers peak flame temperature, which is why lean burn suppresses NOx.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: NOx Formation (Zeldovich Mechanism)

The extended Zeldovich equations:

$$
\text{(1) } \text{O} + \text{N}_2 \rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{N}
$$

$$
\text{(2) } \text{N} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{O}
$$

$$
\text{(3) } \text{N} + \text{OH} \rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{H}
$$

The thermal (Zeldovich) NOx formation rate follows:

$$
\frac{d[\text{NO}]}{dt} = k_1 [\text{O}][\text{N}_2] + k_2 [\text{N}][\text{O}_2]
$$

where $k_1$ and $k_2$ follow Arrhenius form:

$$
k = A \exp\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)
$$

**Conclusion:** Because NOx formation is exponential in $T$, every 100 K reduction in peak temperature reduces NOx by roughly **a factor of 2 to 3**.

For KTU: simply note that lean burn ($T_{\text{ad}} \downarrow$) reduces NOx proportionally; this is the central design lever.

---

## 3.6 Comprehensive Numerical Worked Example (KTU Pattern)

> **Problem:** A hydrogen-fueled 4-stroke spark-ignition engine has a displacement of 1.5 L, operates at 3000 rpm with a volumetric efficiency of 85%, and uses a lean mixture with $\lambda = 1.8$. Compute:
> (a) Volumetric flow rate of air at intake
> (b) Mass flow rate of H₂
> (c) Brake power output if brake thermal efficiency is 32%

**Given:**
- $V_d = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m³
- $N = 3000$ rpm = 50 rev/s
- $\eta_v = 0.85$
- $\lambda = 1.8$
- $\text{AFR}_{\text{stoich}} = 34.05$
- $\eta_{\text{bt}} = 0.32$
- $\rho_{\text{air}} = 1.2$ kg/m³ (standard atmospheric)

### (a) Volumetric flow rate of air

For a 4-stroke engine, intake events occur once per 2 revolutions:

$$
n_{\text{intake}} = \frac{N}{2} = 25 \text{ intake events/s}
$$

Volumetric flow rate:

$$
\dot{V}_{\text{air}} = V_d \times n_{\text{intake}} \times \eta_v
$$

$$
\dot{V}_{\text{air}} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 25 \times 0.85
$$

$$
\boxed{\dot{V}_{\text{air}} = 0.031875 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}}
$$

### (b) Mass flow rate of H₂

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{air}} = \dot{V}_{\text{air}} \times \rho_{\text{air}} = 0.031875 \times 1.2 = 0.03825 \text{ kg/s}
$$

At $\lambda = 1.8$:

$$
\text{AFR}_{\text{actual}} = 1.8 \times 34.05 = 61.29
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{H}_2} = \frac{\dot{m}_{\text{air}}}{\text{AFR}_{\text{actual}}} = \frac{0.03825}{61.29}
$$

$$
\boxed{\dot{m}_{\text{H}_2} = 6.24 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/s}}
$$

### (c) Brake power output

Energy input rate:

$$
\dot{Q}_{\text{in}} = \dot{m}_{\text{H}_2} \times Q_{\text{LHV}} = 6.24 \times 10^{-4} \times 119.96 \times 10^6
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_{\text{in}} = 6.24 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.1996 \times 10^8 = 7.486 \times 10^4 \text{ W} = 74.86 \text{ kW}
$$

Brake power:

$$
\dot{W}_b = \eta_{\text{bt}} \times \dot{Q}_{\text{in}} = 0.32 \times 74.86
$$

$$
\boxed{\dot{W}_b \approx 23.96 \text{ kW} \;(\approx 32.6 \text{ HP})}
$$

**Verification of reasonableness:** A 1.5 L engine producing ~24 kW at 3000 rpm with lean burn is plausible—typical of a small automotive hydrogen engine.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS
