# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Q: List any three properties of hydrogen that distinguish it as an automotive fuel from gasoline.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> 1. **Carbon-free fuel:** Hydrogen contains no carbon atoms in its molecular structure; therefore, combustion yields no CO, CO₂, HC, or particulate matter (PM) emissions — only water vapor and NOx as the sole regulated pollutant (1 mark).
>
> 2. **Wider flammability limits:** Hydrogen is flammable over 4%–75% volumetric concentration in air, compared to gasoline's narrow 1%–7.6%. This permits **lean burn operation** ($\lambda$ up to ~2.5), improving efficiency and suppressing knock (1 mark).
>
> 3. **Higher flame speed and lower ignition energy:** Hydrogen's minimum ignition energy (0.02 mJ) is approximately 12 times lower than gasoline's (0.24 mJ), enabling rapid flame propagation. However, this also causes abnormal combustion like backfire and knock (1 mark).

---

### Question A2: `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Q: Define the term "backfire" as it relates to hydrogen engines. Mention one strategy to mitigate it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition:** Backfire is the abnormal combustion phenomenon in hydrogen engines where the air-hydrogen mixture ignites in the **intake manifold** during the intake stroke, producing a loud explosion that travels backward through the intake system (2 marks).
>
> **Cause:** Residual hot exhaust gases or hot spots ignite the premixed H₂-air charge prematurely.
>
> **Mitigation Strategy:** Use **Direct Injection (DI)** of hydrogen into the cylinder, eliminating any premixed charge in the intake manifold. Alternative: lean-burn operation and effective EGR (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### PART B — QUESTION A: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Q: (a)** Explain with suitable sketches the working of a **Port Fuel Injection (PFI) hydrogen engine**. Discuss its advantages and limitations over a conventional gasoline engine. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hydrogen engine operates at $\lambda = 2.2$ with a brake thermal efficiency of 35%. If the engine consumes 0.5 kg of H₂ per hour, compute the **brake power output** and the **mass flow rate of air** supplied to the engine. **(7 marks)**

---

#### PART B — QUESTION A — Model Solution

### Part (a) — Working of PFI Hydrogen Engine (7 marks)

**Working Principle:**
A Port Fuel Injection hydrogen engine is a modified 4-stroke SI engine where hydrogen gas is injected into the intake port just upstream of the intake valve. The working cycle proceeds as follows:

1. **Intake Stroke:** The intake valve opens; the piston descends, creating a low-pressure region. Gaseous H₂ is metered by the ECU-controlled solenoid injector into the port, where it mixes with incoming air to form a homogeneous premixed charge. (1 mark)

2. **Compression Stroke:** Both valves are closed; the premixed H₂-air charge is compressed to approximately **1/12 to 1/14 of its original volume** (compression ratio limited to ~12:1 due to abnormal combustion risk). (1 mark)

3. **Power Stroke:** At TDC, the spark plug fires. Because hydrogen has very low minimum ignition energy (0.02 mJ) and high flame speed (2.5–3.5 m/s), combustion is nearly instantaneous. The expansion of hot gases (primarily H₂O vapor and N₂) drives the piston downward. (1 mark)

4. **Exhaust Stroke:** The exhaust valve opens; products of combustion (mostly H₂O and N₂ with traces of NOx) are expelled. Water vapor may condense visibly from the tailpipe on cold days — a signature feature of H₂ vehicles. (1 mark)

**Schematic (sketch description):**
A standard 4-cylinder arrangement with: H₂ tank → pressure regulator → filter → ECU-controlled injector in each intake port → intake manifold → cylinder with spark plug and piston → exhaust manifold → three-way catalytic converter → tailpipe.

**Advantages over gasoline engine:** (2 marks)
- Zero CO, CO₂, HC emissions (carbon-free)
- Higher thermal efficiency due to lean burn and high octane rating
- Wide flammability limits enable stable lean operation
- No fuel washing of cylinder walls (clean combustion)
- Reduced engine oil contamination

**Limitations:** (1 mark)
- High backfire risk in intake manifold
- Limited compression ratio (12–14:1) due to knock
- Low energy density by volume → large storage tanks
- Hydrogen embrittlement of metal components

---

### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Excess air ratio $\lambda = 2.2$
- Brake thermal efficiency $\eta_{\text{bt}} = 0.35$
- $\dot{m}_{\text{H}_2} = 0.5$ kg/hr

**Step 1:** Convert mass flow rate to SI units:

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{H}_2} = \frac{0.5}{3600} = 1.389 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute energy input rate:

$$
\dot{Q}_{\text{in}} = \dot{m}_{\text{H}_2} \times Q_{\text{LHV,H}_2}
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_{\text{in}} = 1.389 \times 10^{-4} \times 119.96 \times 10^6
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_{\text{in}} = 1.666 \times 10^4 \text{ W} = 16.66 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute brake power:

$$
\dot{W}_b = \eta_{\text{bt}} \times \dot{Q}_{\text{in}} = 0.35 \times 16.66
$$

$$
\boxed{\dot{W}_b = 5.83 \text{ kW}}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute actual AFR at $\lambda = 2.2$:

$$
\text{AFR}_{\text{actual}} = \lambda \times \text{AFR}_{\text{stoich}} = 2.2 \times 34.05 = 74.91
$$

**Step 5:** Compute mass flow rate of air:

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{air}} = \dot{m}_{\text{H}_2} \times \text{AFR}_{\text{actual}}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{air}} = 1.389 \times 10^{-4} \times 74.91
$$

$$
\boxed{\dot{m}_{\text{air}} = 1.04 \times 10^{-2} \text{ kg/s} = 0.0104 \text{ kg/s}}
$$

**Verification:** Mass flow rate of air = 0.0104 × 3600 = 37.44 kg/hr, which is reasonable for a small engine.

**Final Answers:**
- Brake power = **5.83 kW (≈ 7.93 HP)**
- Air mass flow rate = **0.0104 kg/s (37.44 kg/hr)**

---

### PART B — QUESTION B: `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Q: (a)** Discuss the various **abnormal combustion phenomena** encountered in hydrogen engines. Explain the strategies used to mitigate each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke hydrogen engine has a total displacement of 2.0 L and runs at 2500 rpm. The volumetric efficiency is 80% and the engine operates with $\phi = 0.6$. Calculate the **indicated power** assuming indicated thermal efficiency is 33%. Use $\rho_{\text{air}} = 1.2$ kg/m³. **(7 marks)**

---

#### PART B — QUESTION B — Model Solution

### Part (a) — Abnormal Combustion Phenomena (7 marks)

Hydrogen engines are particularly susceptible to abnormal combustion due to hydrogen's unique properties — high flame speed, low ignition energy, and wide flammability range. The main phenomena are:

**1. Backfire (2 marks)**
- **Definition:** Premature ignition of the H₂-air mixture in the intake manifold during the intake stroke.
- **Cause:** Residual hot exhaust gases or hot-spot deposits in the intake manifold ignite the premixed charge.
- **Mitigation:** Use of **Direct Injection (DI)** instead of port injection, which eliminates premixing in the manifold. Also: lean operation ($\lambda > 1.5$), effective EGR, and water injection to cool intake charge.

**2. Pre-Ignition (2 marks)**
- **Definition:** Endothermic auto-ignition of the H₂-air mixture **before** the spark event, due to hot spots in the combustion chamber.
- **Cause:** Overheated spark plug electrodes, glowing carbon deposits, or hot valve surfaces initiate combustion.
- **Mitigation:** Use of cooler-running spark plugs (iridium), maintaining lean mixtures, oil cooling of pistons, and use of low-heat-rejection combustion chamber coatings.

**3. Knocking (Knock) (2 marks)**
- **Definition:** Uncontrolled, rapid auto-ignition of the end-gas region, producing high-frequency pressure oscillations.
- **Cause:** Hydrogen's very low minimum ignition energy makes end-gas auto-ignition easier; combined with high flame speed, this produces intense knocking.
- **Mitigation:** Limit compression ratio to ~12:1 (PFI) or ~14:1 (DI), retard spark timing, use EGR, and operate lean.

**4. Other related issues (1 mark):**
- **Flame quenching** at extremely lean limits (beyond $\lambda = 2.5$)
- **NOx formation** due to high adiabatic flame temperature (~2400 K)

---

### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $V_d = 2.0 \times 10^{-3}$ m³
- $N = 2500$ rpm = 41.67 rev/s
- $\eta_v = 0.80$
- $\phi = 0.6$
- $\eta_{\text{it}} = 0.33$ (indicated thermal efficiency)
- $\rho_{\text{air}} = 1.2$ kg/m³
- $\text{AFR}_{\text{stoich}} = 34.05$

**Step 1:** Volumetric flow rate of air (4-stroke, 1 intake per 2 revolutions):

$$
\dot{V}_{\text{air}} = V_d \times \frac{N}{2} \times \eta_v
$$

$$
\dot{V}_{\text{air}} = 2.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 20.83 \times 0.80 = 0.03333 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 2:** Mass flow rate of air:

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{air}} = \dot{V}_{\text{air}} \times \rho_{\text{air}} = 0.03333 \times 1.2 = 0.040 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute actual AFR for $\phi = 0.6$:

$$
\lambda = \frac{1}{\phi} = \frac{1}{0.6} = 1.667
$$

$$
\text{AFR}_{\text{actual}} = 1.667 \times 34.05 = 56.74
$$

**Step 4:** Mass flow rate of H₂:

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{H}_2} = \frac{\dot{m}_{\text{air}}}{\text{AFR}_{\text{actual}}} = \frac{0.040}{56.74} = 7.05 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 5:** Energy input rate:

$$
\dot{Q}_{\text{in}} = \dot{m}_{\text{H}_2} \times Q_{\text{LHV}} = 7.05 \times 10^{-4} \times 119.96 \times 10^6
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_{\text{in}} = 84{,}571 \text{ W} = 84.57 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 6:** Indicated power:

$$
\dot{W}_i = \eta_{\text{it}} \times \dot{Q}_{\text{in}} = 0.33 \times 84.57
$$

$$
\boxed{\dot{W}_i = 27.91 \text{ kW} \;(\approx 37.96 \text{ HP})}
$$

**Verification:** For a 2.0 L, 4-cylinder engine at 2500 rpm with lean burn, 27.9 kW indicated power is reasonable. The power output is reduced due to the lean operation ($\phi = 0.6$).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Losing Mistakes (KTU Examiner Observations):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the "intake events per second" factor:** For a 4-stroke engine, intake events = $N/2$. Students often mistakenly use $N$ (full RPM), inflating the result by 2×. **Always write out the "4-stroke = N/2" conversion explicitly.**
>
> 2. **Confusing equivalence ratio $\phi$ with excess air ratio $\lambda$:** $\lambda = 1/\phi$. Students mix these up regularly. $\phi < 1$ → lean; $\phi > 1$ → rich. **Always state both for clarity.**
>
> 3. **Wrong stoichiometric AFR:** The KTU syllabus uses 34.3 (or 34.05 in derivation form). Do not use gasoline's 14.7. **Memorize 34.05:1.**
>
> 4. **Forgetting water vapor in exhaust:** When asked "what are the emissions of a H₂ engine?", students must say: **H₂O (water vapor), N₂, O₂ (excess air), and small NOx**. Missing NOx costs marks.
>
> 5. **Mis-stating LHV:** Use **119.96 MJ/kg** (some texts round to 120). Do not use HHV (141.86) unless the question explicitly says higher heating value.
>
> 6. **Skipping units in numerical answers:** Always end with explicit **SI units (kg/s, kW, m³/s)** — losing 0.5 marks for unit omission is a common tragedy.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

### Core Concepts
- ✅ Hydrogen engine = carbon-free ICE using H₂ as fuel; emits H₂O and NOx
- ✅ Combustion reaction: $2H_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2H_2O$
- ✅ Stoichiometric AFR = **34.05:1** (mass basis)
- ✅ LHV of H₂ = **119.96 MJ/kg**
- ✅ Auto-ignition temperature ≈ **858 K** (very high → low knock tendency in theory)
- ✅ Minimum ignition energy = **0.02 mJ** (very low → high abnormal combustion risk in practice)
- ✅ Flammability range = **4–75% in air** (very wide → enables lean burn)
- ✅ Flame speed = **2.5–3.5 m/s** (high → fast combustion, short burn duration)

### Engine Types
- ✅ **PFI (Port Fuel Injection):** simple, but prone to backfire; CR limited to 12–14:1
- ✅ **DI (Direct Injection):** eliminates backfire; permits higher CR (up to ~16:1); preferred modern approach
- ✅ **HCCI (Homogeneous Charge Compression Ignition):** uses H₂'s high octane; advanced research stage

### Abnormal Combustion — Three Villains
- 🔥 **Backfire:** in intake manifold → mitigated by DI
- 🔥 **Pre-ignition:** before spark → mitigated by lean burn, oil cooling
- 🔥 **Knock:** end-gas auto-ignition → mitigated by EGR, lower CR, retarded timing

### Key Strategies
- ✅ **Lean burn** is the cornerstone: improves efficiency, reduces NOx, suppresses knock
- ✅ **Lean limit:** $\lambda \approx 2.5$ — beyond this, combustion becomes unstable
- ✅ **EGR (Exhaust Gas Recirculation):** dilutes charge, lowers peak temperature
- ✅ **DI** is the most effective single technology for hydrogen engines

### NOx — The Sole Pollutant
- ✅ Formed via **extended Zeldovich mechanism**
- ✅ Strongly temperature-dependent (exponential in $T$)
- ✅ Mitigated by lean burn, EGR, SCR, three-way catalysts

### Advantages vs. Challenges
| Advantages | Challenges |
|---|---|
| Zero CO₂ emissions | Low volumetric energy density |
| Wide flammability range | Backfire and abnormal combustion |
| High flame speed | Hydrogen embrittlement |
| Compatible with existing ICE infra | High-pressure storage required |
| High theoretical efficiency | NOx formation requires after-treatment |

### Energy Density Facts
- ✅ H₂ gas at 200 bar: ~18× volume of gasoline for same energy
- ✅ Liquid H₂ at 20 K: ~4× volume of gasoline for same energy
- ✅ H₂ LHV (per kg): **~2.7× gasoline**

### Numerical Problem Solving Recipe
1. Identify cycle type (4-stroke → divide by 2 for intake events)
2. Convert all quantities to SI
3. Compute $\dot{V}_{\text{air}} = V_d \times (N/2) \times \eta_v$
4. Convert to $\dot{m}_{\text{air}}$ using density
5. Compute $\dot{m}_{\text{H}_2}$ using AFR (= $\lambda \times 34.05$)
6. Compute $\dot{Q}_{\text{in}} = \dot{m}_{\text{H}_2} \times Q_{\text{LHV}}$
7. Compute power = $\eta \times \dot{Q}_{\text{in}}$

---

> - **LPG Engines:** Comparable volumetric efficiency challenges

topics.title: Flex Fuel Vehicles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT744 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Flex Fuel Vehicles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

A **Flex Fuel Vehicle (FFV)** is a vehicle equipped with an internal combustion engine (typically spark-ignition) that is engineered to operate seamlessly on any volumetric blend of gasoline and ethanol — ranging from pure gasoline (**E0**, 0% ethanol) to **E85** (85% ethanol + 15% gasoline) — and in many modern systems up to **E100** (hydrous/neat ethanol). The powertrain contains a **fuel composition sensor**, ethanol-compatible fuel system components, and a recalibrated **Engine Control Unit (ECU)** that dynamically adjusts fuel injection quantity, ignition timing, and cold-start strategy based on the real-time ethanol fraction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** FFVs are *not* bi-fuel vehicles. A bi-fuel vehicle uses **two separate fuel tanks** (e.g., CNG + petrol) and runs on *one* fuel at a time. An FFV uses **one tank** and a *mixture* of fuels, with the engine computer continuously adapting to the blend.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Designation System:**
> - **E0** = Pure gasoline
> - **E10** = 10% ethanol + 90% gasoline (common pump grade worldwide)
> - **E85** = 85% ethanol + 15% gasoline (standard FFV fuel)
> - **E100** = Neat ethanol (Brazil pre-2003 fleet standard)

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine a **smartphone with a variable-wattage charger**. The charger doesn't care whether the wall socket gives 110 V or 240 V — it *detects* the input and *adjusts* its internal circuit to deliver the correct charging current. 

An **FFV works the same way**: the fuel composition sensor acts like the "voltage detector," the ECU acts like the "smart circuit," and the injectors + spark plugs act like the "current regulators." Whether you pour E10 or E85 into the same tank, the engine automatically recalibrates itself in milliseconds and keeps running smoothly.

## 1.3 Why Ethanol? The Engineering Motivation

| Property | Gasoline (E0) | Ethanol (E100) | Engineering Implication |
|----------|---------------|----------------|--------------------------|
| Octane Number (RON) | ~92 | ~108–110 | Higher knock resistance → higher compression ratio possible |
| Latent Heat of Vaporization | **~350 kJ/kg** | **~840 kJ/kg** | Better charge cooling, but harder cold starts |
| Lower Heating Value | **~43.5 MJ/kg** | **~26.9 MJ/kg** | ~30% more fuel volume needed for same energy |
| Stoichiometric A/F | **14.7 : 1** | **9.0 : 1** | ECU must inject ~63% more fuel mass on E100 |
| Hygroscopicity | Negligible | High | Material compatibility (corrosion) issue |
| Vapor Pressure (Reid) | 60–80 kPa | ~15 kPa | Harder cold-start vaporization |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constants (Box these for the exam):**
> - Stoichiometric A/F of gasoline: **14.7 : 1**
> - Stoichiometric A/F of pure ethanol: **9.0 : 1**
> - LHV of gasoline: **43.5 MJ/kg**
> - LHV of ethanol: **26.9 MJ/kg**
> - Density of gasoline: **0.74 kg/L**
> - Density of ethanol: **0.789 kg/L**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of a Flex Fuel Powertrain

The FFV system differs from a conventional gasoline vehicle in **four** critical subsystems:

### 2.1.1 Fuel System Modifications
- **Stainless steel fuel lines and fittings** — ethanol is hygroscopic and mildly corrosive to plain carbon steel and certain aluminum alloys.
- **Ethanol-compatible elastomer seals** (Viton / FKM / PTFE-based) replacing standard nitrile rubber (NBR) hoses and O-rings.
- **Higher-capacity fuel pump and injector** — must deliver up to ~63% more fuel mass when running on E100 to maintain stoichiometry.

### 2.1.2 Fuel Composition Sensor
A dedicated sensor mounted in the fuel rail that measures the **dielectric constant / permittivity** of the incoming liquid fuel.
- Gasoline permittivity: **~2.0–2.2**
- Ethanol permittivity: **~24.3**

Because the difference is enormous, the sensor produces a near-linear voltage signal proportional to ethanol volume fraction (Φ). This signal is fed to the ECU as the **FFS (Fuel Flex Sensor)** input.

### 2.1.3 ECU Re-Calibration
The ECU contains a **three-dimensional calibration map** indexed by (Engine RPM, Engine Load, Ethanol Fraction). Real-time outputs:
1. **Injector pulse width** — lengthened for high-ethanol blends.
2. **Ignition timing** — advanced (retard) based on ethanol's higher octane.
3. **Cold-start enrichment** — additional fuel + spark retard during warm-up.
4. **Closed-loop lambda control** — the wide-band O2 sensor still controls the *stoich* point; ECU just translates lambda target into the correct injector pulse.

### 2.1.4 Cold-Start Hardware
Ethanol's high latent heat of vaporization + low vapor pressure = poor cold-start atomization. FFV cold-start systems typically include one or more of:
- **Auxiliary cold-start injector** (in intake manifold)
- **Grid heater** in the intake air stream
- **Spark timing retard** during cranking to increase exhaust heat
- **Block heater** (customer accessory in cold climates)

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Subsystem

| Subsystem | Physical Reason | Why the Modification is Needed |
|-----------|-----------------|---------------------------------|
| FFS sensor | Ethanol's ε = 24.3 >> Gasoline's ε ≈ 2 | ECU must *know* the blend to meter correct fuel |
| Bigger injectors | Ethanol needs 1/0.61 = 1.63× the mass | Stock injectors cannot deliver E100 stoich mass |
| Ethanol-resistant seals | Ethanol swells/destroys NBR | Prevent leaks, emissions, fire hazard |
| SS fuel lines | Ethanol + water → galvanic corrosion | Prevent rust, fuel system failure |
| Cold-start aids | Ethanol BP = 78 °C; hard to vaporize at –10 °C | Enable cold cranking below 0 °C |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam-Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Always use these formulas with the correct units. Examiners deduct marks for unit inconsistency.**

### Formula 1: Stoichiometric A/F Ratio of a Fuel Blend

$$AFR_{blend} = \frac{1}{\sum_{i} \left( \frac{massFraction_i}{AFR_i} \right)}$$

### Formula 2: Mass Fraction of Component in E85 (Volumetric)

$$m_{eth} = V_{eth} \cdot \rho_{eth} = 0.85 \cdot 0.789 = 0.6707 \text{ kg/L}$$

$$m_{gas} = V_{gas} \cdot \rho_{gas} = 0.15 \cdot 0.74 = 0.111 \text{ kg/L}$$

$$m_{total} = 0.7817 \text{ kg/L}$$

### Formula 3: Energy Content of a Blend per Litre

$$E_{blend} = \sum_{i} (V_i \cdot \rho_i \cdot LHV_i)$$

### Formula 4: Fuel Economy Penalty (%)

$$\eta_{penalty}(\%) = \left(1 - \frac{E_{blend}}{E_{gasoline}}\right) \times 100$$

### Formula 5: Required Injector Scaling Factor (E100 vs E0)

$$k_{inj} = \frac{AFR_{gas}}{AFR_{eth}} = \frac{14.7}{9.0} = 1.633$$

### Formula 6: Compression Ratio Knock-Limited Octane Approximation

$$CR_{max} \approx 1 + \left(\frac{RON - 100}{15}\right) \quad \text{(empirical)}$$

### Formula 7: Cold-Start Enrichment Factor (Mass-based)

$$f_{cs} = 1 + \alpha \cdot \frac{LHV_{gas}}{LHV_{blend}} \cdot \frac{T_{vap,blend}}{T_{vap,gas}}$$

where α is a calibration constant and T_vap are latent heats of vaporization.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Use:** These formulas are used by OEMs (e.g., Ford's Super Duty FFV, GM's EcoFlex, Toyota's flex-fuel Corolla in Brazil) to certify EPA fuel economy, to size injectors, and to comply with **Tier-3 emission norms** where ethanol's higher latent heat reduces cold-start HC emissions.

## 2.4 Engine Knock and Ethanol — The Underlying Physics

Knocking is the uncontrolled auto-ignition of the end-gas ahead of the flame front. Knock resistance is quantified by the **Octane Number (ON)**. Ethanol's exceptionally high ON (≈ **108 RON, 92 MON** for E100) arises from:

1. **High latent heat** → cools the charge → reduces end-gas temperature.
2. **High heat of vaporization of droplets** → reduces local auto-ignition tendency.
3. **Oxygenated molecule (C₂H₅OH)** → cleaner, cooler combustion.

Practical consequence: A naturally aspirated FFV can operate at **CR = 12:1 to 14:1** on E85 versus **CR = 10:1** on gasoline, recovering much of the energy-density penalty through thermal efficiency gains.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1 — Stoichiometric A/F Ratio of E85 (Volume Basis)

**Problem:** Determine the stoichiometric air-to-fuel mass ratio of **E85** (85 % ethanol + 15 % gasoline by volume).

**Given data:**

| Parameter | Ethanol | Gasoline |
|-----------|---------|----------|
| Density (kg/L) | 0.789 | 0.740 |
| Stoich A/F | 9.0 | 14.7 |
| LHV (MJ/kg) | 26.9 | 43.5 |

### Step 1 — Take 1 Litre of E85 as the basis

$$V_{eth} = 0.85 \text{ L}, \quad V_{gas} = 0.15 \text{ L}$$

### Step 2 — Convert each volume to mass

$$m_{eth} = V_{eth} \cdot \rho_{eth} = 0.85 \text{ L} \times 0.789 \text{ kg/L} = 0.6707 \text{ kg}$$

$$m_{gas} = V_{gas} \cdot \rho_{gas} = 0.15 \text{ L} \times 0.740 \text{ kg/L} = 0.1110 \text{ kg}$$

### Step 3 — Sum the masses

$$m_{fuel} = m_{eth} + m_{gas} = 0.6707 + 0.1110 = 0.7817 \text{ kg}$$

### Step 4 — Compute mass fractions

$$w_{eth} = \frac{0.6707}{0.7817} = 0.8580$$

$$w_{gas} = \frac{0.1110}{0.7817} = 0.1420$$

### Step 5 — Compute the air required for each component (using individual A/F ratios)

For an air mass of $m_{air,i}$ needed to burn 1 kg of component $i$:

$$m_{air,eth} = w_{eth} \times 9.0 = 0.8580 \times 9.0 = 7.722 \text{ kg}$$

$$m_{air,gas} = w_{gas} \times 14.7 = 0.1420 \times 14.7 = 2.087 \text{ kg}$$

### Step 6 — Sum and divide by total fuel mass

$$m_{air,total} = 7.722 + 2.087 = 9.809 \text{ kg air}$$

$$\boxed{AFR_{E85} = \frac{m_{air,total}}{m_{fuel}} = \frac{9.809}{0.7817} \approx 9.81 : 1}$$

> **This matches the empirical industry value of ~9.8 : 1 used by Ford, GM, and Bosch calibrations for E85.**

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2 — Fuel Economy Penalty of E85 vs Gasoline

**Problem:** A car delivers **15 km/L on E0**. Estimate its fuel economy on E85 (assuming same engine efficiency).

### Step 1 — Energy per litre of gasoline

$$E_{gas} = \rho_{gas} \cdot LHV_{gas} = 0.740 \text{ kg/L} \times 43.5 \text{ MJ/kg} = 32.19 \text{ MJ/L}$$

### Step 2 — Energy per litre of E85 (sum of component energies)

$$E_{E85} = (V_{eth} \cdot \rho_{eth} \cdot LHV_{eth}) + (V_{gas} \cdot \rho_{gas} \cdot LHV_{gas})$$

$$E_{E85} = (0.85 \times 0.789 \times 26.9) + (0.15 \times 0.740 \times 43.5)$$

$$E_{E85} = 18.041 + 4.829 = 22.87 \text{ MJ/L}$$

### Step 3 — Energy ratio

$$\frac{E_{E85}}{E_{gas}} = \frac{22.87}{32.19} = 0.7104$$

### Step 4 — Apply to fuel economy

$$\text{FE}_{E85} = 15 \text{ km/L} \times 0.7104 = 10.66 \text{ km/L}$$

### Step 5 — Percentage drop

$$\Delta \text{FE} = \frac{15 - 10.66}{15} \times 100 = 28.9\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** E85 gives ~29 % lower fuel economy by volume. In practice, the real-world drop is **20–25 %** because E85's higher octane permits a more advanced ignition map, partially recovering efficiency.

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation 3 — Injector Pulse-Width Correction

**Problem:** ECU receives a base pulse width $t_{b}$ for gasoline stoichiometry. Compute the corrected pulse width $t_{c}$ for E100 (pure ethanol), assuming injectors flow rate is constant and only mass-based metering differs.

### Step 1 — Recall stoichiometric air-fuel ratios

$$AFR_{gas} = 14.7, \quad AFR_{eth} = 9.0$$

### Step 2 — Fuel mass ratio (more ethanol required per unit air)

$$k = \frac{AFR_{gas}}{AFR_{eth}} = \frac{14.7}{9.0} = 1.6333$$

### Step 3 — Corrected pulse width

$$t_{c} = t_{b} \cdot k = 1.6333 \cdot t_{b}$$

> **Conclusion:** ECU must keep the injector open ~63 % longer on E100. This is why FFV injectors are either **larger-flow** units or operated at **higher fuel rail pressure** (up to 4 bar → 10–20 bar in direct-injection FFV systems).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix (Examiner's Quick Lookup)

| Design Decision | E0 (Gasoline) | E85 | E100 | Justification |
|-----------------|---------------|-----|------|----------------|
| Stoich A/F | 14.7 : 1 | ~9.8 : 1 | 9.0 : 1 | Oxygen in ethanol reduces air demand |
| CR (NA engine) | 10 : 1 | 12 : 1 | 14 : 1 | Higher octane enables higher CR |
| Ignition Timing | Baseline | +4° to +6° advanced | +6° to +10° advanced | Detonation margin from RON 108 |
| Injector Pulse | Baseline | × 1.5 | × 1.63 | Mass metering |
| Cold-Start Aid | None | Grid heater | Grid heater + cold inj. | Latent heat of vaporization |
| Fuel Line Material | Carbon steel OK | Stainless required | Stainless required | Ethanol hygroscopicity + corrosion |
| Seal Material | NBR | Viton/FKM | Viton/FFKM | Ethanol attacks nitrile |
| λ (Lambda) Target | 1.00 | 1.00 | 1.00 | Three-way catalyst still wants stoich |
| Expected FE Penalty | 0 % | ~25–29 % | ~39 % | Energy density difference |
| Cold-Start Limit | –30 °C | –15 °C | –5 °C | Vapor pressure limitation |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Flex Fuel Powertrain Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[FUEL TANK - E0 to E100 Compatible] --> B[High Pressure Electric Fuel Pump]
    B --> C[Fuel Rail Manifold]
    C --> D[FFS - Fuel Flex Sensor]
    D --> E[ECU - Engine Control Unit]
    E -->|Inj Pulse Width| F[Multi-Port Injectors x4 or x6]
    E -->|Ignition Timing Advance| G[Ignition Coils and Spark Plugs]
    F --> H[Intake Manifold]
    H --> I[Engine Cylinders - 4 Stroke SI]
    I --> J[Three Way Catalytic Converter TWC]
    J --> K[Lambda Oxygen Sensor - Wideband]
    K -->|Closed Loop Feedback| E
    E -->|Start Enrichment| L[Auxiliary Cold Start Injector]
    E -->|Grid Heat Relay| M[Intake Air Grid Heater]
    L --> H
    M --> H
    E -.FFS Signal Processing.-> D
```

## 4.2 Signal-Flow Diagram — ECU Closed-Loop Adaptation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[FFS Sensor] --> S2[Ethanol Fraction Phi]
    S2 --> M1[3D Map Lookup RPM Load Phi]
    M1 --> O1[Injector Pulse Width tw]
    M1 --> O2[Ignition Timing Advance DeltaTheta]
    M1 --> O3[Cold Start Enrichment Factor fcs]
    O1 --> F1[Combustion in Cylinder]
    F1 --> F2[Exhaust Gas]
    F2 --> S3[Wideband O2 Sensor]
    S3 --> S4[Lambda Value]
    S4 --> S5[PI Controller in ECU]
    S5 --> M1
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Fuel Composition Sensor Operating Principle

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Capacitive Plates in Fuel Flow] --> P2[Dielectric Constant Measurement]
    P2 --> P3[Oscillator Circuit - Frequency Modulated]
    P3 --> P4[Signal Conditioning Amplifier]
    P4 --> P5[Analog to Digital Converter]
    P5 --> P6[ECU Input Pin - 0 to 5 V]
    P6 --> P7[Lookup Table Conversion]
    P7 --> P8[Phi - Ethanol Volume Fraction 0 to 1]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Note (Block Diagram vs. Physical Drawing):** Per protocol, when a topic requires complex physical geometry (e.g., a fuel injector cross-section), the system architecture block diagram above conveys equivalent functional understanding for KTU 14-mark questions, which predominantly test system-level reasoning rather than geometric part-drawing.

## 4.4 Process Flow — Cold-Start Sequence on E85

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Q0[Ignition Key ON] --> Q1[ECU Reads FFS - Phi 0.85 Detected]
    Q1 --> Q2[Look Up Cold-Start Map]
    Q2 --> Q3[Activate Grid Heater 8s]
    Q3 --> Q4[Open Auxiliary Cold Injector 3s]
    Q4 --> Q5[Crank Engine]
    Q5 --> Q6{Engine Started?}
    Q6 -- No --> Q7[Extend Enrichment - Max 15s]
    Q7 --> Q5
    Q6 -- Yes --> Q8[Lambda Closed-Loop Active]
    Q8 --> Q9[Normal Operation - FFV Mode]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define a Flex Fuel Vehicle. Differentiate it from a Bi-Fuel vehicle.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024 Pattern] (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

A **Flex Fuel Vehicle (FFV)** is a vehicle whose internal combustion engine is designed to operate on any volumetric blend of two fuels — typically gasoline and ethanol — within a specified range (commonly **E0 to E85**), using a **single fuel tank** and a **single fuel delivery system**. An on-board **fuel composition sensor** continuously informs the ECU, which adjusts injection duration and ignition timing in real time.

| Aspect | Flex Fuel Vehicle (FFV) | Bi-Fuel Vehicle |
|--------|------------------------|-----------------|
| Fuel Tanks | **One** common tank | **Two** separate tanks |
| Operating Mode | Mixture of fuels in one tank | Only one fuel at a time (driver-selectable) |
| Example | Ford F-150 FFV running E85 | Maruti Suzuki Wagon-R running CNG + Petrol |
| Sensor | Fuel Composition Sensor (FFS) | Fuel Selector Switch / Solenoid valves |
| ECU | One ECU, multi-fuel map | Two separate maps, switched by selector |

---

### **Q2. Why does an FFV require a fuel composition sensor? Explain briefly.** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024] (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

Ethanol (ε ≈ 24.3) and gasoline (ε ≈ 2.0) have a **~12× difference in dielectric permittivity**. A **fuel composition sensor (FFS)** placed in the fuel rail measures this dielectric property and outputs a **voltage proportional to the ethanol volume fraction (Φ)** in the blend. The ECU uses this signal to:

1. **Correct injector pulse width** — E85 needs ~50 % more fuel mass than E0.
2. **Advance ignition timing** — E85's RON 105–108 allows more advance without knock.
3. **Schedule cold-start enrichment** — higher ethanol content requires more enrichment.

Without the FFS, the ECU would be blind to the blend and could not maintain λ = 1.00 stoichiometry, causing misfires, poor drivability, and catalyst damage.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q A.(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the **major subsystems** of a Flex Fuel Vehicle and how the engine ECU manages multi-fuel operation. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A Flex Fuel Vehicle contains the following major subsystems, illustrated in the block diagram of Section 4.1:

**(i) Modified Fuel Delivery System** — The fuel tank, electric pump, fuel rail, and injectors are upgraded for ethanol compatibility. **Stainless steel lines** and **Viton seals** prevent corrosion. Injector flow is sized for up to **63 % greater mass flow** to meet the stoichiometric requirement of E100.

**(ii) Fuel Composition Sensor (FFS)** — A capacitive sensor in the fuel rail measures the blend's permittivity and outputs 0–5 V corresponding to Φ = 0 (gasoline) to Φ = 1 (ethanol). This is the **single most important input** for ECU multi-fuel operation.

**(iii) Engine Control Unit (ECU)** — The ECU executes a 3-D calibration map indexed by (RPM, Load, Φ). It produces three primary outputs:
- **Injector pulse width** scaled by Φ.
- **Ignition timing** advanced as Φ increases.
- **Cold-start enrichment** via auxiliary injector / grid heater.

**(iv) Three-Way Catalyst (TWC) with Wideband O₂ Sensor** — Maintains λ = 1.00 regardless of blend. The O₂ sensor closes the loop; the ECU translates λ-target into the *correct* pulse width for the *current* Φ.

**(v) Cold-Start Hardware** — Grid heater + auxiliary injector to vaporize the high-latent-heat ethanol-rich mixture.

**ECU Management Strategy:** The ECU runs a **closed-loop dual-input control** — FFS sets the *base map* lookup, while the wideband O₂ sensor performs a *feedback trim*. The two signals combine via a PI controller to deliver stoichiometric combustion across the entire Φ range in real time.

---

**Q A.(b)** A vehicle runs on **E85** (85 % ethanol, 15 % gasoline by volume). Given the data below, calculate: **(i)** the stoichiometric A/F ratio of E85, **(ii)** the energy content per litre of E85, and **(iii)** the percentage drop in fuel economy if the vehicle delivers 16 km/L on pure gasoline. **(7 Marks)**

| Parameter | Ethanol (C₂H₅OH) | Gasoline |
|-----------|------------------|----------|
| Density (kg/L) | 0.789 | 0.740 |
| Stoich A/F (mass) | 9.0 | 14.7 |
| LHV (MJ/kg) | 26.9 | 43.5 |

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Stoichiometric A/F of E85**

Mass of ethanol in 1 L: $0.85 \times 0.789 = 0.6707$ kg  
Mass of gasoline in 1 L: $0.15 \times 0.740 = 0.1110$ kg  
Total fuel mass: $0.6707 + 0.1110 = 0.7817$ kg  

Air for ethanol: $0.6707 \times 9.0 = 6.036$ kg  
Air for gasoline: $0.1110 \times 14.7 = 1.632$ kg  
Total air: $6.036 + 1.632 = 7.668$ kg  

$$\boxed{AFR_{E85} = \frac{7.668}{0.7817} = 9.81 : 1}$$

**(ii) Energy content per litre of E85**

$$E_{E85} = (0.85)(0.789)(26.9) + (0.15)(0.740)(43.5)$$

$$E_{E85} = 18.04 + 4.83 = 22.87 \text{ MJ/L}$$

**(iii) Fuel-economy drop**

Energy of pure gasoline per litre: $(0.740)(43.5) = 32.19$ MJ/L  

$$\text{FE}_{E85} = 16 \times \frac{22.87}{32.19} = 16 \times 0.7104 = 11.37 \text{ km/L}$$

$$\text{Drop} = \frac{16 - 11.37}{16} \times 100 = 28.9\%$$

> **Answer: AFR = 9.81:1, Energy = 22.87 MJ/L, FE drop ≈ 29 %**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative**

**Q B.(a)** Discuss the **properties of ethanol** that make it suitable as a gasoline extender in FFVs, and the **material-compatibility and cold-start challenges** that arise from those very properties. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Favourable Properties of Ethanol:**

1. **High Octane Number (≈ 108 RON, 92 MON):** Ethanol's knock resistance allows a higher compression ratio (12–14:1 vs 10:1 for gasoline), yielding higher thermal efficiency and partially offsetting the energy-density deficit.

2. **Oxygenated Molecule (C₂H₅OH):** Contains ~35 % oxygen by mass. This enables **leaner combustion** and reduces CO and unburnt HC emissions. Promotes more complete combustion of fuel-rich zones.

3. **High Latent Heat of Vaporization (840 kJ/kg):** Cools the intake charge → higher volumetric efficiency → reduced tendency for knock → allows advanced ignition timing.

4. **Renewable Bio-Source:** Sugarcane (Brazil), corn (USA), molasses, and cellulosic biomass. Closes the CO₂ cycle → lower well-to-wheel CO₂ footprint by **30–60 %** depending on feedstock.

5. **Non-Toxic and Biodegradable:** Spills do not contaminate soil/groundwater like petroleum.

6. **High Flame Speed (~3.4 m/s vs ~2.3 m/s for gasoline):** Allows lean-burn operation and better thermal efficiency.

**Challenges Stemming from the Same Properties:**

1. **Hygroscopicity** (water affinity): Ethanol absorbs water from the atmosphere. Water + ethanol + galvanic metals → **corrosion** of fuel lines, tanks, carburettors, and aluminum parts. Solution: **Stainless steel lines, ethanol-rated elastomer seals (Viton, FFKM)**.

2. **High Latent Heat of Vaporization** → difficult cold-start. At –5 °C, ethanol does not vaporize sufficiently to form a combustible mixture. Solution: **grid heater, auxiliary cold-start injector, advanced spark timing during cranking**.

3. **Low Energy Density (LHV = 26.9 MJ/kg vs 43.5 for gasoline):** ~39 % less energy per kg. Result: **larger fuel tank or shorter range**. Solution: **High-CR engine, advanced timing, hybrid drive** to recover efficiency.

4. **Solvent Action:** Ethanol dissolves sludge, varnish, and old fuel deposits, which can clog fuel filters at first fill-up. Solution: **fuel filter replacement** when switching a non-FFV to E85.

5. **Material Attacks:** Nitrile rubber (NBR) hoses, magnesium/aluminium alloys, zinc plating — all degrade. Solution: **material substitution programme** during FFV manufacture.

---

**Q B.(b)** Explain in detail the **role of the Engine Control Unit (ECU)** in an FFV. How does it use inputs from the **Fuel Composition Sensor** and **Oxygen Sensor** to deliver optimal combustion across the entire ethanol-blend range? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The ECU is the **brain** of the FFV. Its role can be broken into five functions:

**1. Fuel-Composition Tracking:** The ECU reads the FFS voltage and converts it via a lookup table to **Φ (ethanol volume fraction, 0 to 1)**. This is updated every **10–100 ms** depending on the calibration.

**2. Base Map Selection:** The ECU accesses a 3-D map: **Pulse Width = f(RPM, Load, Φ)**. For each operating point, the correct stoichiometric pulse width is pre-tabulated. Higher Φ → longer pulse width (e.g., 1.5× at E85, 1.63× at E100).

**3. Ignition Timing Control:** A second map **Spark Advance = g(RPM, Load, Φ)** advances timing as Φ rises (because higher ethanol = higher knock margin). Typical advance: +4° at E85, +6–10° at E100.

**4. Cold-Start Strategy:** During cranking, the ECU enters a *cranking subroutine* that:
- Activates the **grid heater** (8–15 s).
- Energizes the **auxiliary cold-start injector**.
- Retards spark by 5–10° to dump unburnt energy into exhaust → faster catalyst light-off.

**5. Closed-Loop Lambda Trim:** Once the engine starts and the O₂ sensor reaches operating temperature (~300 °C, after ~30 s), the ECU enters closed-loop mode. The wideband O₂ sensor returns a **lambda value** (1.00 = stoich). The ECU's **PI controller** multiplies the base pulse width by a **short-term fuel trim (STFT)** factor ±10 % and a **long-term fuel trim (LTFT)** factor ±25 % to correct for:
- FFS sensor drift,
- Fuel temperature variation,
- Altitude / barometric pressure changes,
- Engine aging.

**End Result:** The ECU continuously reconciles the *predictive* (FFS) and *corrective* (O₂) signals into a final injector pulse width that holds λ = 1.000 ± 0.005 across the **entire** Φ range, ensuring the three-way catalyst can simultaneously remove CO, HC, and NOₓ — meeting **BS-VI / Euro-VI / Tier-3** emission norms.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE MARKS in FFV questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing A/F ratio units** — Examiners expect *mass-based* A/F. Writing "A/F = 9.8" without specifying mass ratio loses **1 mark**.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert volume fractions to mass fractions** in blend calculations. Pure E85 volume → mass is a mandatory step; skipping it costs **2 marks**.
> 3. **Writing E85 has the same A/F as gasoline** — this is a **very common error** that examiners actively hunt for. Memorize **14.7 vs 9.81**.
> 4. **Not stating units** in energy-content calculations. "32 MJ" without "/L" or "/kg" loses **0.5–1 mark**.
> 5. **Omitting the role of the O₂ sensor** in the ECU section — many students write only the FFS, forgetting that *closed-loop* lambda control is what guarantees emissions compliance.
> 6. **Spelling/terminology errors** — "Flex Fuel" written as "flexible fuel," "FFS" as "FSS," "stoichiometric" as "stochiometric" — examiners mark these as half-errors.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Top 15 High-Yield Bullet Points for Last-Minute Revision:**

- **FFV = one tank, one fuel system, multi-fuel blend**; bi-fuel = two tanks, one fuel at a time.
- **Operating window:** typically **E0 to E85**; some markets support up to **E100** (Brazil).
- **Stoich A/F of pure ethanol = 9.0:1**; **gasoline = 14.7:1**; **E85 ≈ 9.81:1**.
- **LHV of ethanol = 26.9 MJ/kg**; **gasoline = 43.5 MJ/kg** → **~29 % energy penalty** at E85.
- **Octane of ethanol ≈ 108 RON** → allows **CR up to 14:1** (vs 10:1 on gasoline).
- **Latent heat of vaporization of ethanol = 840 kJ/kg** (gasoline = 350) → **cold-start difficulty** below 0 °C.
- **Fuel composition sensor** exploits the dielectric permittivity difference: ε_ethanol ≈ 24.3, ε_gasoline ≈ 2.0.
- **Ethanol is hygroscopic** → requires **stainless steel lines + Viton/FFKM seals** (NBR is incompatible).
- **ECU strategy:** 3-D map indexed by (RPM, Load, Φ) + closed-loop lambda trim from wideband O₂ sensor.
- **Injector pulse-width scaling factor E100/E0 = 14.7/9.0 = 1.633**.
- **Cold-start aids:** grid heater, auxiliary injector, spark retard during cranking, block heater (accessory).
- **Brazil's FFV fleet is the largest in the world** (~80 % of new cars); USA second largest (Ford, GM, Chrysler).
- **Environmental benefit:** ~30–60 % lower well-to-wheel CO₂ for bio-ethanol vs gasoline (feedstock dependent).
- **Tier-3 / BS-VI compliance:** possible only because the three-way catalyst can run stoich; FFVs hold **λ = 1.00 ± 0.005**.
- **Examiner's favourite numbers:** memorize **14.7, 9.0, 9.81, 26.9, 43.5, 0.789, 0.74, 108, 840, 350** — at least 70 % of numerical questions in KTU ESE on this topic use these.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT744 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Biofuels and Synthetic Fuels

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Biofuels — Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Biofuels** are liquid, gaseous, or solid fuels derived from **recently living biological organic matter (biomass)** — as opposed to fossil fuels, which are derived from long-term geological transformation of ancient biomass. They are considered **carbon-neutral** because the CO₂ released during combustion is recaptured by the next generation of crops/plants grown to replace them, completing a closed carbon cycle.

Biofuels are broadly classified into four generations based on feedstock and technology readiness:

| Generation | Feedstock | Examples | Engine Compatibility |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1st Gen** | Sugar, starch, vegetable oils, animal fats | Ethanol from sugarcane, biodiesel from soybean | Direct blends (E10, B20) |
| **2nd Gen** | Lignocellulosic biomass, agricultural residues | Cellulosic ethanol, BTL diesel | Requires advanced engines |
| **3rd Gen** | Algae | Algal biodiesel, algal biogas | Drop-in fuels |
| **4th Gen** | Genetically engineered organisms, solar-to-fuel | Engineered biofuels, electrofuels | Emerging technology |

### 1.2 Synthetic Fuels — Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Synthetic Fuels (Synfuels / e-Fuels)** are manufactured fuels produced from **non-petroleum carbon sources** (coal, natural gas, biomass, or captured CO₂ + green H₂) through chemical synthesis processes such as **Fischer-Tropsch (FT)** synthesis, **Methanol-to-Gasoline (MTG)**, or **Sabatier reactions**. They are chemically identical or near-identical to petroleum-derived fuels, allowing **drop-in compatibility** with existing engines and refueling infrastructure.

The three primary routes to synthetic fuels are denoted by feedstock origin:

- **CTL (Coal-To-Liquid):** Coal gasification → Syngas → FT/MTG synthesis
- **GTL (Gas-To-Liquid):** Natural gas reforming → Syngas → FT synthesis (Sasol, Shell Pearl)
- **BTL (Biomass-To-Liquid):** Biomass gasification → Syngas → FT/MTG synthesis (renewable route)

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — The Coffee Refill Analogy:**
> Think of fossil fuels as a **single large coffee cup poured long ago and kept warm on a hot plate** (finite, depleting, original-carbon-locked). Biofuels are like **brewing fresh coffee every morning from new coffee beans** — the plant pulls CO₂ from the air to grow the bean, you brew it (extract energy), drink it (combust), and the next morning new plants have already reabsorbed that CO₂. Synthetic fuels are like **synthesizing artificial coffee flavor from water + CO₂ + electricity** — you don't need beans at all, you chemically construct the fuel molecule from scratch using renewable electricity and captured carbon. Both biofuel and synthetic routes offer **circular/renewable carbon cycles**, in contrast to the one-way depletion of petroleum.

---

### 1.4 Why These Fuels Matter in Automotive Engineering

The automotive industry's transition toward net-zero emissions hinges on three pillars:
1. **Electrification (BEVs)** — mature but constrained by battery raw materials & grid capacity.
2. **Hydrogen (FCEVs)** — efficient but infrastructure-heavy.
3. **Biofuels & Synthetic Fuels** — uniquely valuable because they **leverage existing ICE powertrains, refueling networks, and supply chains**, making them a pragmatic transitional solution, especially for heavy-duty trucking, aviation, and marine sectors where battery energy density is insufficient.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PEMUT744):** Module 2 explicitly tests students on the **production chemistry, physicochemical properties, blending ratios, and engine application limitations** of ethanol, biodiesel, biogas, methanol, butanol, hydrogen, and Fischer-Tropsch synthetic diesel.

## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Ethanol (C₂H₅OH) — The Fermentation-Derived Spark-Ignition Fuel

#### 2.1.1 Production Chemistry (Sugar → Ethanol)

Ethanol is produced by **anaerobic fermentation** of sugars (sucrose, glucose) by yeast (*Saccharomyces cerevisiae*):

$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \xrightarrow{\text{Zymase enzyme}} 2\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 2\,\text{CO}_2$$

For cellulosic feedstocks, hydrolysis (acidic or enzymatic) is required first to break cellulose into glucose, then fermentation follows.

#### 2.1.2 Physicochemical Properties vs. Gasoline

| Property | Gasoline | Ethanol | Significance for SI Engines |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Density (kg/m³)** | 720–780 | **789** | Affects fuel mass metering |
| **Octane Number (RON)** | 88–98 | **108–110** | Excellent knock resistance |
| **Lower Heating Value (MJ/kg)** | 43–44 | **26.9** | ~35 % energy deficit |
| **Latent Heat of Vaporization (kJ/kg)** | 305 | **904** | Charge cooling → higher volumetric efficiency |
| **Stoichiometric A/F Ratio** | 14.7 | **9.0** | Leaner combustion mixture |
| **Flame Speed (m/s)** | 0.4 | **0.6** | Faster flame propagation |
| **Water Solubility** | Negligible | **Miscible** | Corrosion & phase-separation risk |

#### 2.1.3 Blending & Engine Application

- **E10** (10 % ethanol + 90 % gasoline) — OEM-approved, no engine modification.
- **E20–E85** — Requires **flex-fuel vehicle (FFV)** systems: corrosion-resistant fuel lines, larger injectors (60–80 % flow increase), alcohol-compatible elastomers, heated fuel rails, and an alcohol-content sensor.

### 2.2 Biodiesel (FAME) — The Compression-Ignition Substitute

#### 2.2.1 Production via Transesterification

Biodiesel is **Fatty Acid Methyl Ester (FAME)**, produced by reacting vegetable oil / animal fat with methanol in the presence of a catalyst (NaOH or KOH):

$$\text{Triglyceride} + 3\,\text{CH}_3\text{OH} \xrightarrow{\text{NaOH}} 3\,\text{FAME} + \text{Glycerol}$$

Stoichiometrically, **3 moles of methanol per mole of triglyceride** are required, but in practice a **6:1 molar ratio (methanol:oil)** is used to drive the reversible reaction forward.

#### 2.2.2 Physicochemical Properties vs. Diesel

| Property | Petro-Diesel | Biodiesel (FAME) | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Density (kg/m³)** | 820–860 | **880** | Higher mass per stroke |
| **Viscosity at 40 °C (mm²/s)** | 2–4 | **4–6** | Injection & atomization penalty |
| **Cetane Number** | 45–55 | **50–65** | Better cold-start |
| **LHV (MJ/kg)** | 42–45 | **37–39** | ~8 % energy deficit |
| **Flash Point (°C)** | 55–65 | **>130** | Safer storage |
| **Oxygen Content (%)** | 0 | **10–11** | Cleaner combustion, lower CO/PM |
| **Iodine Value** | — | Varies with feedstock | Predicts oxidative stability |

#### 2.2.3 Engine Application Notes

- **B20 (20 % biodiesel + 80 % diesel)** is the most common fleet blend — preserves engine hardware.
- **B100 (pure biodiesel)** requires replacement of natural-rubber seals with Viton/FKM, heated fuel lines in cold climates, and periodic fuel-filter changes due to higher solvency (cleans accumulated deposits).

### 2.3 Biogas — The Anaerobic-Digestion CI Substitute

Biogas is produced by microbial **anaerobic digestion** of organic waste in oxygen-free digesters. The overall biochemistry is a four-stage cascade:

$$\text{Organics} \rightarrow \text{Hydrolysis} \rightarrow \text{Acidogenesis} \rightarrow \text{Acetogenesis} \rightarrow \text{Methanogenesis} \rightarrow \text{CH}_4 + \text{CO}_2$$

**Composition:** typically **55–70 % CH₄, 30–45 % CO₂, trace H₂S, NH₃, water vapor**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dual-Fuel Engine Operation:** Biogas (with its high CO₂ dilution and low flame speed) cannot auto-ignite reliably in a CI engine. Therefore, biogas is **pilot-ignited** by a small diesel injection (~10–20 % of full load), while the bulk of combustion energy comes from biogas. The diesel substitution ratio can reach **80–90 %**.

### 2.4 Methanol & Butanol — Alcohol Family Alternatives

- **Methanol (CH₃OH):** Produced from syngas (CO + 2H₂ → CH₃OH). High octane (~114 RON), but highly corrosive, toxic, and hygroscopic. Used in M85 blends and high-performance racing (e.g., methanol dragsters).
- **Butanol (C₄H₉OH):** Produced via *Clostridium acetobutylicum* fermentation (ABE process). Energy density closer to gasoline (~29 MJ/kg vs. ethanol's 26.9), lower water affinity, can be transported via existing pipelines. Can be used up to **100 % (B100) in modified engines**.

### 2.5 Hydrogen — The Zero-Carbon Combustion Fuel

- **Lower Heating Value:** 120 MJ/kg (highest of any fuel by mass), but extremely low density → must be stored compressed (350–700 bar), liquefied (-253 °C), or in metal hydrides.
- **Auto-ignition temperature:** ~570 °C (very high) → difficult to use in CI engines without pilot injection.
- **Two pathways in automotive:** (i) **Hydrogen Internal Combustion Engine (H₂ICE)** with port/injector delivery; (ii) **Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell (PEMFC)** generating electricity for an EV drivetrain.

### 2.6 Synthetic Fuels — Fischer-Tropsch Diesel & MTG

#### 2.6.1 Fischer-Tropsch Synthesis

The **syngas (CO + H₂)** produced from coal/biomass/natural-gas gasification is converted over an iron or cobalt catalyst:

$$n\,\text{CO} + (2n+1)\,\text{H}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Fe or Co catalyst, 200–350 °C}} \text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+2} + n\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

The product is a mixture of **straight-chain paraffins (wax to gasoline range)** which is then cracked/hydrocracked to yield the required diesel/gasoline/kerosene fractions.

#### 2.6.2 Methanol-to-Gasoline (MTG)

$$\text{CO} + 2\,\text{H}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Cu/ZnO/Al}_2\text{O}_3} \text{CH}_3\text{OH} \xrightarrow{\text{Zeolite ZSM-5, 370 °C}} \text{Hydrocarbons (C}_5\text{–C}_{10}\text{)}$$

The **Mobil MTG process (New Zealand, 1985–1995)** proved the technology at industrial scale.

---

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table consolidates every formula and conversion a KTU PEMUT744 student must memorize for Module 2 problems.**

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula / Relation | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Energy Density Ratio** | $\eta_{\text{ED}} = \dfrac{LHV_{\text{biofuel}}}{LHV_{\text{petro}}}$ | dimensionless | E.g., ethanol = 26.9/43.5 = 0.62 |
| 2 | **Biodiesel Stoichiometric Ratio** | $n_{\text{MeOH}} : n_{\text{Oil}} = 3:1$ (ideal), **6:1** (practice) | mol/mol | Excess methanol drives equilibrium |
| 3 | **Transesterification Yield** | $Y = \dfrac{m_{\text{FAME}}}{m_{\text{oil}}} \times 100$ | % | Typical 90–98 % |
| 4 | **Biogas Methane Number** | $\text{MN} = f(\text{CH}_4\%,\text{CO}_2\%,\text{H}_2\text{S}\%)$ | dimensionless | Higher MN → more knock-resistant |
| 5 | **Air-Fuel Ratio Comparison** | $(A/F)_{\text{eth}} = 9.0$, $(A/F)_{\text{gasoline}} = 14.7$ | — | Alcohols need richer mixtures |
| 6 | **Cetane Number Reduction Formula** | $\Delta CN \approx -0.2 \times (\% \text{biodiesel blend})$ | — | Approximate heuristic |
| 7 | **H₂ Mass Energy Density** | $LHV_{H_2} = 120\,\text{MJ/kg}$ | MJ/kg | Volumetric at 700 bar ≈ 4.7 MJ/L |
| 8 | **FT Stoichiometric Coefficient** | $n\,\text{CO} + (2n+1)\,\text{H}_2$ | — | Defines H₂:CO usage ratio |
| 9 | **CO₂ Neutrality Mass Balance** | $m_{\text{CO}_2,\text{comb}} = \dfrac{44}{12} \times m_{\text{C,fuel}}$ | kg | Compare with photosynthetic uptake |
| 10 | **Blending Energy Penalty** | $LHV_{\text{blend}} = X_A \cdot LHV_A + (1-X_A) \cdot LHV_B$ | MJ/kg | Linear (ideal) mixing rule |

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Significance:**
> - **E20 petrol rollout** is mandated in India under the **Ethanol Blended Petrol (EBP) Programme** (Ministry of Petroleum, target E20 by 2025–26).
> - **Biodiesel B20** powers city bus fleets in Bengaluru, Delhi, and São Paulo.
> - **Shell Pearl GTL** (Qatar) produces **140,000 barrels/day** of FT synthetic diesel.
> - **Porsche, Audi, Bosch** are industrializing **e-fuels (synthetic hydrocarbons from green H₂ + captured CO₂)** to enable carbon-neutral ICE vehicles post-2035 EU ban.

## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Numerical Problem — Ethanol Energy Penalty & Air-Fuel Ratio Recalculation

> **Problem (KTU Sample):** A flex-fuel vehicle operates on E85 (85 % ethanol by volume, 15 % gasoline by volume). Calculate: (a) the energy density of the blend per kg, (b) the stoichiometric air-fuel ratio of the blend, and (c) the mass of blend required to deliver the same energy as 1 kg of pure gasoline.

**Given Data:**
- Density of ethanol, $\rho_E = 789 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Density of gasoline, $\rho_G = 750 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- LHV of ethanol, $LHV_E = 26.9 \text{ MJ/kg}$
- LHV of gasoline, $LHV_G = 43.5 \text{ MJ/kg}$
- Stoichiometric A/F of ethanol = 9.0
- Stoichiometric A/F of gasoline = 14.7

---

#### Step 1: Mass Fraction of Each Component

Take **1 m³ (1000 L)** of E85 blend as the basis.

**Volume of ethanol** = 850 L = 0.850 m³
**Volume of gasoline** = 150 L = 0.150 m³

$$m_E = \rho_E \times V_E = 789 \times 0.850 = 670.65 \text{ kg}$$

$$m_G = \rho_G \times V_G = 750 \times 0.150 = 112.50 \text{ kg}$$

$$m_{\text{blend}} = 670.65 + 112.50 = 783.15 \text{ kg}$$

**Mass fractions:**

$$w_E = \frac{670.65}{783.15} = 0.8563$$

$$w_G = \frac{112.50}{783.15} = 0.1437$$

---

#### Step 2: Energy Density of the Blend

The blend LHV (per kg) is the **mass-weighted average**:

$$LHV_{\text{blend}} = w_E \cdot LHV_E + w_G \cdot LHV_G$$

$$LHV_{\text{blend}} = (0.8563 \times 26.9) + (0.1437 \times 43.5)$$

$$LHV_{\text{blend}} = 23.034 + 6.251$$

$$\boxed{LHV_{\text{blend}} = 29.29 \text{ MJ/kg}}$$

---

#### Step 3: Stoichiometric A/F Ratio of the Blend

The A/F ratio of a multi-component blend (on a mass basis) is given by:

$$\left(\frac{A}{F}\right)_{\text{blend}} = \frac{1}{\dfrac{w_E}{(A/F)_E} + \dfrac{w_G}{(A/F)_G}}$$

This is the **harmonic-mass-mean** form, derived from mass conservation of oxygen demand.

$$\frac{1}{(A/F)_{\text{blend}}} = \frac{w_E}{(A/F)_E} + \frac{w_G}{(A/F)_G}$$

$$\frac{1}{(A/F)_{\text{blend}}} = \frac{0.8563}{9.0} + \frac{0.1437}{14.7}$$

$$\frac{1}{(A/F)_{\text{blend}}} = 0.09514 + 0.00978 = 0.10492$$

$$\boxed{(A/F)_{\text{blend}} = 9.53}$$

This means the engine's ECU must enrich the mixture (reduce A/F from 14.7 → 9.53) when switching to E85.

---

#### Step 4: Mass of Blend Equivalent to 1 kg of Gasoline (Energy Equivalence)

Energy delivered by 1 kg of pure gasoline:

$$E_{\text{1 kg gasoline}} = 1 \times 43.5 = 43.5 \text{ MJ}$$

Mass of E85 blend delivering the same energy:

$$m_{\text{required}} = \frac{43.5}{29.29} = 1.485 \text{ kg}$$

$$\boxed{m_{\text{required}} = 1.485 \text{ kg of E85} \equiv 1 \text{ kg of gasoline (energy-wise)}}$$

**Conclusion:** The driver must consume **48.5 % more mass (and volume)** of E85 to match gasoline's energy output — explaining why fuel economy drops by ~25–35 % in FFVs on E85.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem — Biodiesel Mass Balance from Transesterification

> **Problem (KTU Sample):** 1000 kg of soybean oil (assume pure triolein, $M = 885.4 \text{ g/mol}$) is transesterified with methanol using a 6:1 molar ratio. Calculate: (a) the mass of methanol required, (b) the mass of FAME (methyl oleate) produced, and (c) the mass of glycerol co-product.

**Molar mass data:**
- Triolein: $M_{\text{oil}} = 885.4 \text{ g/mol}$
- Methanol: $M_{\text{MeOH}} = 32.04 \text{ g/mol}$
- Methyl oleate (FAME): $M_{\text{FAME}} = 296.5 \text{ g/mol}$
- Glycerol: $M_{\text{gly}} = 92.1 \text{ g/mol}$

---

**Step (a) — Moles of oil:**

$$n_{\text{oil}} = \frac{1000 \times 10^3 \text{ g}}{885.4 \text{ g/mol}} = 1129.4 \text{ mol}$$

**Moles of methanol required (6:1):**

$$n_{\text{MeOH}} = 6 \times 1129.4 = 6776.4 \text{ mol}$$

**Mass of methanol:**

$$m_{\text{MeOH}} = 6776.4 \times 32.04 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{m_{\text{MeOH}} = 217.12 \text{ kg}}$$

---

**Step (b) — Moles and mass of FAME:**

From stoichiometry, 1 mol triolein → 3 mol FAME.

$$n_{\text{FAME}} = 3 \times 1129.4 = 3388.2 \text{ mol}$$

$$m_{\text{FAME}} = 3388.2 \times 296.5 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{m_{\text{FAME}} = 1004.6 \text{ kg}}$$

---

**Step (c) — Mass of glycerol:**

$$n_{\text{gly}} = 1129.4 \text{ mol (1:1 with oil)}$$

$$m_{\text{gly}} = 1129.4 \times 92.1 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{m_{\text{gly}} = 104.0 \text{ kg}}$$

**Verification (mass balance):**

$$m_{\text{in}} = m_{\text{oil}} + m_{\text{MeOH}} = 1000 + 217.12 = 1217.12 \text{ kg}$$

$$m_{\text{out}} = m_{\text{FAME}} + m_{\text{gly}} = 1004.6 + 104.0 = 1108.6 \text{ kg}$$

$$\text{Mass loss} \approx 108.5 \text{ kg} \;(\approx 9\%)$$

This mass loss corresponds to the **unreacted excess methanol + water + soap by-products**, which are separated and the methanol is recovered by distillation for recycling. KTU examiners expect students to mention this recovery step for full marks.

---

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**[A1] [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Q: Define "first-generation" and "second-generation" biofuels with one example each.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **First-generation biofuels** are produced from edible sugar/starch crops (e.g., sugarcane, corn) and vegetable oils (e.g., soybean oil) using well-established technologies like fermentation and transesterification. **Example: Ethanol from sugarcane.**
> - **Second-generation biofuels** are produced from non-edible lignocellulosic biomass (e.g., agricultural residues, wood waste, switchgrass) and require advanced pre-treatment processes such as enzymatic hydrolysis or thermochemical gasification. **Example: Cellulosic ethanol from rice straw.**

---

**[A2] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q: List three advantages and two disadvantages of biodiesel over petro-diesel.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **Advantages:** (i) Renewable and biodegradable; (ii) Higher cetane number (50–65 vs. 45–55) → smoother combustion; (iii) Oxygen content (~11 %) reduces CO and particulate matter emissions; (iv) Higher flash point (>130 °C) → safer storage/transport.
> - **Disadvantages:** (i) Higher viscosity (4–6 vs. 2–4 mm²/s) → poor atomization and injector deposits; (ii) Higher NOₓ emissions due to oxygen presence and elevated combustion temperatures; (iii) Higher cloud/pour point → cold-flow limitations.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Module 2 Biofuels:**
> Students **commonly lose marks** in the following ways:
> 1. Writing only the production reaction **without mentioning the catalysts, temperature, and time** required (e.g., NaOH catalyst at 60 °C for transesterification).
> 2. Forgetting to state that **biogas cannot auto-ignite** in a CI engine and hence requires **pilot diesel injection** in dual-fuel mode.
> 3. Confusing **Fischer-Tropsch synthesis** with **cracking** (FT synthesizes hydrocarbons from syngas; cracking breaks long chains into shorter ones).
> 4. Skipping the **volumetric vs. gravimetric energy density** distinction when comparing hydrogen with other fuels.
> 5. Failing to mention **material compatibility** (natural rubber → Viton, aluminum → stainless) when discussing high-percentage alcohol blends.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2]**

**(a)** With neat sketches and balanced chemical equations, explain the **production of biodiesel by transesterification**. Discuss the role of the catalyst, the effect of methanol-to-oil molar ratio, and the typical product yields. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **physicochemical properties of biodiesel and petro-diesel** in a tabular form. Discuss the engine modifications required to operate a CI engine on B100 (pure biodiesel). **(7 marks)**

> **Model Solution for Question A:**
>
> **(a) Transesterification of Vegetable Oil to Biodiesel (7 marks):**
> Biodiesel, chemically known as **Fatty Acid Methyl Ester (FAME)**, is produced when a **triglyceride** reacts with **methanol** in the presence of an **alkaline catalyst (typically NaOH or KOH at 0.5–1 % w/w of oil)** at a temperature of **60–65 °C** for **60–120 minutes** with continuous stirring. The reaction is reversible and yields a **two-phase product**: a heavier **glycerol layer** (bottom) and a lighter **FAME layer** (top), which are separated by gravity in a settling tank.
>
> **Balanced Equation:**
>
> Triglyceride + 3 CH₃OH ⇌ 3 R-COOCH₃ (FAME) + Glycerol
>
> **Role of Catalyst:** NaOH/KOH acts as a homogeneous base catalyst by generating **methoxide ion (CH₃O⁻)**, which is the actual nucleophile attacking the carbonyl carbon of the triglyceride. Catalysts drastically lower the activation energy, allowing the reaction to proceed at low temperatures.
>
> **Effect of Molar Ratio:** The stoichiometric ratio is 3:1 (methanol:oil), but a **6:1 excess** is used industrially because (i) the reaction is reversible and Le Chatelier's principle favors excess methanol, and (ii) methanol is immiscible with oil, so excess methanol improves mass transfer.
>
> **Yields:** Typically 90–98 % FAME; ~10 % glycerol as a co-product (recovered and sold to pharmaceutical industries).
>
> **(b) Property Comparison and Engine Modifications (7 marks):**
> *(Refer to Section 2.2.2 table — students should reproduce with all 7 properties.)*
>
> **Key property differences and engine modifications for B100:**
> - **Higher viscosity** → requires **heated fuel lines** and **indirect injection** with swirl assist for adequate atomization.
> - **Material compatibility** → replace **natural-rubber/NBR seals and hoses** with **Viton (FKM) or PTFE**, because biodiesel's solvency attacks and swells natural rubber.
> - **Higher cloud/pour point** → install **fuel heaters** and **winterization blends** for cold climates.
> - **Solvent effect (cleaning deposits)** → expect **clogged fuel filters** shortly after switching from diesel; pre-emptively replace filters at 1000 km.
> - **NO₡ emissions increase** → may require **EGR recalibration** or **selective catalytic reduction (SCR)**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2]**

**(a)** Describe the **Fischer-Tropsch (FT) process** for producing synthetic diesel from coal/biomass. Include a process flow diagram, the role of the catalyst, and the key operating conditions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **engine performance and emission characteristics** when a CI engine is fuelled with **E85 (ethanol blend)** in a dual-injection spark-assisted configuration. Compare the results with baseline gasoline operation. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Solution for Question B:**
>
> **(a) Fischer-Tropsch Process (7 marks):**
> The FT process converts a mixture of **carbon monoxide and hydrogen (syngas)** into liquid hydrocarbons using a **heterogeneous catalyst** at elevated temperature and pressure.
>
> **Feedstock-to-Syngas Stage:** Coal (CTL) or biomass (BTL) is fed into a **gasifier** operating at 800–1200 °C with limited oxygen, producing **raw syngas (CO + H₂)** plus CO₂, H₂S, NH₃, and particulates.
>
> **Syngas Cleaning:** Acid-gas removal (Rectisol or Selexol process) eliminates H₂S and CO₂; the cleaned syngas has an **H₂:CO molar ratio ≈ 2.0–2.2**, ideal for FT.
>
> **FT Reactor:** Syngas is passed over an **iron (Fe) or cobalt (Co) catalyst** at **200–350 °C** and **20–40 bar** in either a **slurry-bed reactor** or **fixed-fluidized-bed reactor**.
>
> **Overall Reaction:** n CO + (2n+1) H₂ → CₙH₂ₙ₊₂ + n H₂O
>
> The product distribution follows the **Anderson-Schulz-Flory (ASF) distribution**, yielding a mixture of **straight-chain paraffins ranging from C₁ to C₆₀+**. The wax fraction (heavy FT product) is then subjected to **hydrocracking** in a downstream reactor to yield the desired **diesel (C₁₂–C₂₀), kerosene, and naphtha fractions**.
>
> **Advantages of FT diesel:** Essentially zero sulfur, near-zero aromatics, very high cetane number (~70–75), and full drop-in compatibility with existing diesel engines and infrastructure.
>
> **(b) E85 in Spark-Assisted CI Engine (7 marks):**
> *(Note: Conventional CI engines cannot operate on E85 due to low cetane number. A dual-injection or spark-assisted CI engine must be considered, OR the question accepts E85 in a flex-fuel SI engine — both interpretations should be covered.)*
>
> **Performance characteristics of E85 vs. gasoline (SI baseline):**
> - **Thermal efficiency:** ~5–10 % higher due to higher octane number (108 RON), enabling **higher compression ratios (12:1 vs. 10:1)** and charge cooling from ethanol's high latent heat (904 kJ/kg).
> - **Brake specific fuel consumption (BSFC):** Increases by **30–40 %** on a mass basis due to ethanol's lower LHV (26.9 vs. 43.5 MJ/kg). On a volumetric basis the increase is less (~20–25 %).
> - **Power output:** Slightly lower (5–10 %) per unit fuel volume due to stoichiometric A/F ratio difference.
>
> **Emission characteristics:**
> - **CO emissions:** Reduced by 20–50 % due to fuel-bound oxygen promoting more complete combustion.
> - **HC emissions:** May increase due to lower flame temperatures in crevice regions and wall quenching; significantly reduced if an oxidation catalyst is used.
> - **NOₓ emissions:** Typically reduced by 10–20 % because of the high latent heat reducing peak flame temperature.
> - **PM emissions:** Negligible (ethanol is non-sooting).
> - **Aldehydes (acetaldehyde, formaldehyde):** Increased due to partial oxidation of ethanol; must be controlled with catalysts.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 60-Second KTU Module 2 Revision Checklist — Biofuels and Synthetic Fuels:**

- [ ] **Biofuel generations:** 1G (sugar/starch/oil) → 2G (cellulose) → 3G (algae) → 4G (synthetic biology / e-fuels).
- [ ] **Ethanol:** $LHV = 26.9 \text{ MJ/kg}$, $RON = 108$, stoichiometric A/F = 9.0, **miscible in water** (corrosion risk).
- [ ] **Biodiesel (FAME):** $\text{Triglyceride} + 3\text{MeOH} \xrightarrow{\text{NaOH}} 3\text{FAME} + \text{Glycerol}$; 6:1 MeOH:oil ratio used in practice.
- [ ] **Biodiesel properties:** Density 880 kg/m³, viscosity 4–6 mm²/s, cetane 50–65, oxygen 11 %.
- [ ] **Biogas:** $\text{CH}_4$ 55–70 %, $\text{CO}_2$ 30–45 %; needs **pilot diesel ignition** in CI engines (dual-fuel).
- [ ] **Methanol:** $LHV = 20.1 \text{ MJ/kg}$, $RON = 114$, toxic, corrosive; made from syngas $\text{CO} + 2\text{H}_2$.
- [ ] **Butanol:** Higher energy density (29.2 MJ/kg), lower water affinity than ethanol; ABE fermentation.
- [ ] **Hydrogen:** $LHV = 120 \text{ MJ/kg}$ (best by mass), but $LHV = 4.7 \text{ MJ/L}$ at 700 bar (very poor by volume); **NO carbon emissions** at tailpipe.
- [ ] **Fischer-Tropsch:** $n\text{CO} + (2n+1)\text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+2} + n\text{H}_2\text{O}$ over Fe/Co catalyst at 200–350 °C, 20–40 bar.
- [ ] **CTL / GTL / BTL:** Coal / Gas / Biomass to Liquid — all converge on **syngas → FT** pathway.
- [ ] **MTG (Methanol-to-Gasoline):** Syngas → Methanol → Zeolite ZSM-5 catalyst → Gasoline-range hydrocarbons.
- [ ] **Engine modifications for high-percentage alcohol blends:** Viton seals, stainless fuel lines, enlarged injectors, alcohol-content sensor (FFV), heated fuel rail.
- [ ] **Biodiesel cold-flow issue:** Higher cloud/pour point → fuel heater required in winter.
- [ ] **Always state catalyst, temperature, and residence time** in transesterification / FT / fermentation reactions.
- [ ] **Drop-in compatibility:** FT-diesel > Biodiesel > Ethanol blends (in terms of needing fewer engine modifications).
- [ ] **KTU Mark-loser trap:** Confusing "Cetane Number" (diesel knock resistance, higher = better) with "Octane Number" (SI knock resistance, higher = better).
- [ ] **Mass-balance verification** is expected in all numerical problems; methanol recovery (recycling) must be mentioned in transesterification problems.

---

> **END OF MODULE 2 NOTES — BIOFUELS AND SYNTHETIC FUELS**
> *Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020) Outcome-Based Education framework for PEMUT744.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vehicle Operation and Engine Control: Application of Sensors, Actuators, and Microprocessors for Optimal Fuel Economy and Performance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT744 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Vehicle Operation and Engine Control: Application of Sensors, Actuators, and Microprocessors for Optimal Fuel Economy and Performance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Engine Management System (EMS)** — also called the **Engine Control Unit (ECU)** or **Electronic Control Module (ECM)** — is an integrated electronic system that uses **microprocessors**, real-time **sensors**, and precision **actuators** to monitor and continuously regulate critical engine operating parameters (air-fuel mixture, ignition timing, idle speed, exhaust gas recirculation, and emission levels) to deliver **optimal fuel economy**, **maximum drivability**, and **lowest regulated exhaust emissions**.

The architecture is a classic **closed-loop, multi-variable, real-time feedback control system** governed by embedded control algorithms (typically based on **PID control**, **lookup tables / 3D maps**, and **adaptive learning strategies**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Box:** Engine control = **SENSE → DECIDE → ACT** loop executed thousands of times per second by a 32-bit / 64-bit automotive-grade microcontroller (e.g., Infineon AURIX, NXP S32K, Renesas RH850) operating on the **CAN bus (ISO 11898)** communication protocol at bit rates up to **1 Mbit/s**.

## 1.2 The Three Pillars of Engine Control

| Pillar | Function | Vehicle "Analogue" |
|--------|----------|--------------------|
| **Sensors** | Measure physical/chemical state (T, P, λ, position, speed) | Human **5 senses** (eyes, ears, skin) |
| **Microprocessor (ECU)** | Computes desired outputs using control algorithms | Human **brain** |
| **Actuators** | Convert electrical commands into mechanical/chemical action | Human **muscles & glands** |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Body

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine driving a car *without* an ECU. You would have to manually push the accelerator, inject fuel with a syringe, set spark timing with a stopwatch, and watch the tailpipe with a mirror — all while driving! The **ECU does this 100 times every second**, faster and more accurately than any human, by constantly **listening (sensors) → thinking (microprocessor) → acting (actuators)**. Just as your body uses nerves, brain, and muscles to keep you alive, the car uses sensors, ECU, and actuators to keep the engine running cleanly and efficiently.

## 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (Industry-Standard Constants)

- **Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (Petrol/Gasoline):** **A/F = 14.7 : 1** (by mass)
- **Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (Diesel):** **A/F ≈ 14.5 : 1**
- **Calorific Value of Gasoline:** **~ 44 MJ/kg**
- **Calorific Value of Diesel:** **~ 43 MJ/kg**
- **Engine Cranking Speed:** **80 – 200 rpm**
- **Idle Speed (typical):** **700 – 900 rpm**
- **Lambda (λ) = 1** ⇒ Stoichiometric; **λ < 1** ⇒ Rich; **λ > 1** ⇒ Lean
- **Closed-loop control bandwidth:** **10 – 100 Hz** (lambda control loop)

## 1.5 Why Engine Control Matters — The KTU CO Alignment

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This topic directly maps to **CO2 (Module 2):** *"Describe the operation of sensors, actuators, and microprocessors used in modern automotive engine management for fuel economy and emission control."* Mastery of sensor types, ECU signal processing, and actuator response curves is **non-negotiable** for KTU university exam success.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Sense → Decide → Act Operational Loop

The ECU executes a deterministic control loop, typically within **5 – 10 ms** per iteration:

### Step 1 — SENSE (Input Acquisition)
The ECU reads **analog and digital signals** from sensors via its **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** at sample rates between **1 kHz – 100 kHz**.

- **Mass Air Flow (MAF) Sensor** → measures intake air mass (g/s)
- **Manifold Absolute Pressure (MAP) Sensor** → measures intake manifold pressure (kPa)
- **Throttle Position Sensor (TPS)** → measures throttle plate angle (0° – 90°)
- **Crankshaft Position Sensor (CKP)** → measures engine RPM and piston position
- **Camshaft Position Sensor (CMP)** → identifies intake/exhaust stroke
- **Coolant Temperature Sensor (CTS / ECT)** → measures engine temperature (°C)
- **Intake Air Temperature (IAT) Sensor** → measures incoming air temperature
- **Oxygen / Lambda Sensor (O₂ / UEGO)** → measures exhaust O₂ content
- **Knock Sensor (KS)** → detects engine knock via piezoelectric vibration
- **Vehicle Speed Sensor (VSS)** → measures wheel/vehicle speed

### Step 2 — DECIDE (Computation)
The microprocessor executes:

1. **Air-charge calculation** (Speed-Density or Alpha-N method)
2. **Base fuel pulse-width lookup** from 3D map indexed by **RPM × Load**
3. **Closed-loop correction** using the **lambda (λ) feedback signal**
4. **Ignition timing advance calculation** (with knock retard)
5. **Idle speed control** (PI controller)
6. **Diagnostic checks** (OBD-II readiness, fault storage)

### Step 3 — ACT (Output Command)
ECU drives actuators via **PWM (Pulse Width Modulation)** or **H-bridge drivers**:

- **Fuel Injector** → electromagnetic solenoid, opening time **1 – 10 ms**
- **Ignition Coil (IGBT-switched)** → primary current ~ **7 A**, secondary voltage **20 – 40 kV**
- **Electronic Throttle Body (ETB)** → DC motor with dual TPS feedback
- **Idle Air Control (IAC) Valve** → stepper motor / PWM valve
- **Exhaust Gas Recirculation (EGR) Valve** → vacuum or solenoid actuated
- **Variable Valve Timing (VVT) Solenoid** → hydraulic oil control valve
- **Evaporative Emission (EVAP) Purge Valve** → solenoid
- **Turbocharger Wastegate Actuator** → pneumatic / electric

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MEMORIZE THIS TABLE** — these are the 12 most-tested formulas for KTU Module 2 university exams.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Theoretical air mass per cycle (4-stroke) | $m_{air} = \rho_{air} \cdot V_d \cdot \eta_{vol} / (N/2)$ | kg/s | $\rho_{air} \approx 1.2\ \text{kg/m}^3$ at STP |
| 2 | Brake Specific Fuel Consumption | $\text{BSFC} = \dot{m}_f / P_b$ | g/kWh | Lower is better; petrol $\approx 250$ – 350 |
| 3 | Brake Power | $P_b = (2 \pi \cdot N \cdot T) / 60$ | W (or kW) | $N$ in rpm, $T$ in N·m |
| 4 | Brake Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{b,th} = P_b / (\dot{m}_f \cdot \text{CV})$ | dimensionless | CV = calorific value |
| 5 | Volumetric Efficiency | $\eta_{vol} = m_{air,actual} / m_{air,theoretical}$ | dimensionless | Typical: 80 – 95% |
| 6 | Lambda (Excess Air Factor) | $\lambda = (\text{A/F})_{actual} / (\text{A/F})_{stoich}$ | dimensionless | $\lambda = 1$ stoichiometric |
| 7 | Injector Pulse Width | $T_{inj} = (m_{fuel,req} / \dot{m}_{inj}) \cdot 1000$ | ms | $\dot{m}_{inj}$ in g/s |
| 8 | Indicated Mean Effective Pressure | $\text{IMEP} = W_{net} / V_d$ | Pa or bar | $V_d$ in m³ |
| 9 | Engine Air Mass Flow (Speed-Density) | $m_{air} = \eta_{vol} \cdot V_d \cdot N \cdot P_{man} / (2 \cdot R \cdot T_{man})$ | kg/s | Speed-Density equation |
| 10 | Fuel Mass per Cycle | $m_f = m_{air} / (\text{AFR} \cdot \lambda)$ | kg | Per cylinder, per cycle |
| 11 | Otto Cycle Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{th} = 1 - (1/r^{\gamma - 1})$ | dimensionless | $r$ = compression ratio, $\gamma = 1.4$ |
| 12 | BSFC → Brake Efficiency | $\eta_b = 3600 / (\text{BSFC} \cdot \text{CV})$ | dimensionless | BSFC in g/kWh, CV in kJ/kg |

> **Note on Lambda and Emissions:** Three-way catalytic converter (TWC) achieves > 98% conversion efficiency ONLY when **$\lambda$ stays within the narrow window of $0.99 \le \lambda \le 1.01$** — this is why the O₂ sensor and closed-loop control are mandatory, not optional.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Fuel Economy Improvement:** Modern ECUs improve BSFC by **15 – 30%** vs. carbureted engines.
- **Emission Compliance:** ECU control enables **BS-VI / Euro 6** compliance by holding $\lambda \approx 1$.
- **Drive-by-Wire:** Electronic throttle eliminates mechanical cable, enables **Traction Control (TCS)**, **Electronic Stability Control (ESC)**, and **Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC)**.
