\text{mAP} = \frac{1}{C} \sum_{c=1}^{C} \text{AP}_c
$$

**Why COCO mAP is more rigorous (2 marks):**
- **PASCAL VOC mAP** uses a single loose IoU threshold of **0.5**, so a poorly-localized box with IoU = 0.51 receives full credit.
- **COCO mAP@0.5:0.95** averages AP across **10 IoU thresholds** (0.50, 0.55, 0.60, …, 0.95), requiring the detector to produce **precisely localized** boxes at strict thresholds (0.75, 0.90).
- This forces the model to be both **accurate** (right class) and **precise** (tight box), better reflecting real-world autonomous-driving safety requirements.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the end-to-end pipeline of a deep-learning-based object recognition system used in modern ADAS. Label and describe the function of each block. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the Intersection over Union (IoU) metric for two axis-aligned bounding boxes. A predicted box has coordinates $(10, 20, 110, 220)$ and a ground-truth box has $(30, 40, 120, 200)$. Compute the IoU and interpret whether the prediction is acceptable under the PASCAL VOC criterion. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Q.A:**

**(a) End-to-End ADAS Object Recognition Pipeline (7 marks):**

The pipeline consists of **six functional blocks** executed sequentially:

1. **Sensor Acquisition Block:** Captures synchronized multi-modal data (8 cameras, 5 radars, 1 LiDAR). PTP timestamps with sub-microsecond jitter ensure cross-modal alignment. *(1 mark)*

2. **Pre-Processing Block:** Performs intrinsic/extrinsic calibration, distortion correction (using the Brown–Conrady model with $k_1, k_2, p_1, p_2$ coefficients), denoising, and exposure normalization (CLAHE). *(1 mark)*

3. **Feature Extraction Backbone:** A deep CNN (e.g., CSPDarknet-53 in YOLOv8) or Vision Transformer (Swin-T) extracts hierarchical features. Early layers capture low-level edges; deeper layers capture semantic concepts (wheels, faces). *(1.5 marks)*

4. **Detection/Classification/Regression Heads:** Three parallel branches predict class probabilities (softmax over $C$ classes), 2D bounding boxes (4-D regression), and objectness scores. *(1.5 marks)*

5. **Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS) Block:** Eliminates redundant overlapping detections using an IoU threshold (typically 0.5), keeping the highest-confidence box. *(1 mark)*

6. **Multi-Object Tracking Block:** A Kalman Filter or SORT/DeepSORT maintains temporal identity and smooths noisy detections across frames. *(1 mark)*

**Block Diagram:** Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.2.

---

**(b) IoU Derivation & Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Derivation (3 marks):** As derived in Section 3.1,

$$
\text{IoU} = \frac{A_{int}}{A_{p} + A_{gt} - A_{int}}
$$

where $A_{int} = \max(0, x_{i2} - x_{i1}) \times \max(0, y_{i2} - y_{i1})$, with $x_{i1} = \max(x_{p1}, x_{g1})$, etc.

**Numerical Computation (4 marks):**

Predicted box: $B_p = (10, 20, 110, 220)$.
Ground-truth box: $B_{gt} = (30, 40, 120, 200)$.

**Step 1 — Intersection coordinates:**

$$
x_{i1} = \max(10, 30) = 30, \quad y_{i1} = \max(20, 40) = 40
$$

$$
x_{i2} = \min(110, 120) = 110, \quad y_{i2} = \min(220, 200) = 200
$$

**Step 2 — Intersection area:**

$$
A_{int} = (110 - 30) \times (200 - 40) = 80 \times 160 = 12800
$$

**Step 3 — Areas of individual boxes:**

$$
A_{p} = (110 - 10) \times (220 - 20) = 100 \times 200 = 20000
$$

$$
A_{gt} = (120 - 30) \times (200 - 40) = 90 \times 160 = 14400
$$

**Step 4 — Union area:**

$$
A_{union} = 20000 + 14400 - 12800 = 21600
$$

**Step 5 — IoU:**

$$
\text{IoU} = \frac{12800}{21600} \approx 0.593
$$

**Interpretation (1 mark):**
- Under **PASCAL VOC** criterion (IoU $\geq$ 0.5), the prediction **is acceptable** ($\text{IoU} = 0.593 > 0.5$).
- Under **COCO strict** criterion (e.g., IoU $\geq$ 0.75), the prediction **fails** ($0.593 < 0.75$).
- **Conclusion:** The detector correctly identified the object and approximately localized it, but the box is **slack** — the regression head should be retrained for tighter localization.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative**

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of a YOLO-based single-shot object detector. Compare its advantages and disadvantages over region-proposal-based detectors (Faster R-CNN) for real-time automotive applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A forward-looking monocular camera with focal length $f = 1200$ px observes a passenger car of known real-world height $h_{real} = 1.5$ m. The car's image height is measured as $h_{image} = 100$ px. The host vehicle is moving at $v = 20$ m/s. **(i)** Compute the distance to the car. **(ii)** If the recognized car's bounding box grows by 5% in height over the next frame ($\Delta t = 33$ ms), compute the new distance and the time-to-collision (TTC). **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Q.B:**

**(a) YOLO Architecture & Comparison (7 marks):**

**YOLO Architecture (4 marks):**

YOLO ("You Only Look Once") reframes object detection as a **single regression problem** from image pixels to bounding-box coordinates and class probabilities, executed in one forward pass.

**Key components:**
- **Backbone (CSPDarknet-53 in YOLOv8):** Extracts hierarchical features via cross-stage partial connections, reducing gradient redundancy. *(1 mark)*
- **Neck (PANet/FPN):** Aggregates multi-scale features via top-down and bottom-up pathways, enabling detection at three scales: 80×80, 40×40, 20×20. *(1 mark)*
- **Detection Head:** At each scale, predicts $B$ anchor boxes with $(x, y, w, h, \text{objectness}, c_1, c_2, \dots, c_C)$ per anchor — a total of $3 \times (5 + C)$ outputs per spatial location. *(1 mark)*
- **Decoding + NMS:** Anchor-relative predictions are decoded to absolute image coordinates and NMS is applied. *(1 mark)*

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Aspect | YOLO (Single-Shot) | Faster R-CNN (Two-Stage) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Speed | **120+ FPS** (real-time) | 7 FPS (not real-time) |
| mAP | ~85% (COCO) | ~78% (COCO) |
| Architecture | One-stage, end-to-end | RPN + RoI Pooling + Head |
| Best For | Mass-production ADAS, AEB | Offline high-accuracy labeling |
| Hardware Cost | Can run on **Orin Nano** (low power) | Requires **multi-GPU** server |
| Small Object Detection | Weaker (improved in v8) | Stronger |
| Automotive Verdict | **Preferred** for production | Preferred for **validation/test** |

---

**(b) Monocular Distance & Time-to-Collision (7 marks):**

**Given:** $f = 1200$ px, $h_{real} = 1.5$ m, $h_{image} = 100$ px, $v = 20$ m/s, $\Delta t = 33$ ms.

**(i) Initial distance (2 marks):**

$$
d_1 = \frac{f \cdot h_{real}}{h_{image}} = \frac{1200 \times 1.5}{100} = 18 \text{ m}
$$

**(ii) New image height after 5% growth (1 mark):**

$$
h_{image,2} = 100 \times 1.05 = 105 \text{ px}
$$

**(iii) New distance (2 marks):**

$$
d_2 = \frac{1200 \times 1.5}{105} = \frac{1800}{105} \approx 17.143 \text{ m}
$$

**(iv) Time-to-Collision (TTC) (2 marks):**

The closing speed is:

$$
v_{rel} = \frac{d_1 - d_2}{\Delta t} = \frac{18 - 17.143}{0.033} = \frac{0.857}{0.033} \approx 25.97 \text{ m/s}
$$

The TTC is:

$$
\text{TTC} = \frac{d_2}{v_{rel}} = \frac{17.143}{25.97} \approx 0.66 \text{ s}
$$

**Interpretation:** A TTC of **0.66 seconds** is **below the 1.0 s safety threshold** for AEB activation (per Euro NCAP). The system must trigger **full emergency braking** immediately.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Most-Common Mark-Deduction Reasons:**
> 1. **Confusing Recognition with Detection:** Recognition = detection + classification + tracking. If a student writes only "detection," they lose **2 marks** on 14-mark questions.
> 2. **Forgetting Units:** Writing "IoU = 0.59" without context loses 0.5 marks. Always state IoU threshold and criterion (PASCAL/COCO).
> 3. **Skipping Negative Cases in IoU:** Students forget the $\max(0, \cdot)$ term. Always mention it explicitly.
> 4. **Using Horizontal Coordinates Only:** Many students only do $x$ for IoU. The 2D product must be shown.
> 5. **Forgetting Process Noise Covariance Q in Kalman:** The $Q$ term distinguishes a tracking filter from a static estimator. Missing $Q$ = **1.5 mark loss**.
> 6. **Mixing up f-stop with focal length:** Monocular depth derivation requires $f$ in pixels, not f-stops.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section and revise 30 minutes before the exam.**

- [ ] Object Recognition = **Detection + Classification + Localization + Tracking**.
- [ ] **Pipeline order:** Sensor → Pre-process → Feature Extract → Heads → NMS → Tracker → Output.
- [ ] **YOLO** = single-shot, real-time, ~120 FPS, best for production ADAS.
- [ ] **Faster R-CNN** = two-stage, accurate, slow — for validation only.
- [ ] **IoU = Intersection / Union**; threshold is 0.5 (PASCAL) or averaged 0.5:0.95 (COCO).
- [ ] **mAP** is computed per class then averaged across all $C$ classes.
- [ ] **Pinhole depth:** $d = f \cdot h_{real} / h_{image}$.
- [ ] **LiDAR depth:** $d = c \cdot \Delta t / 2$ where $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s.
- [ ] **Kalman filter** = **Predict** (with $\mathbf{F}$, $\mathbf{Q}$) + **Update** (with $\mathbf{H}$, $\mathbf{R}$, $\mathbf{K}$).
- [ ] **NMS** removes duplicate overlapping boxes; threshold typically 0.5.
- [ ] **Sensor Fusion** has three levels: Early, Deep, Late.
- [ ] **TTC** below **1.0 s** triggers AEB (Euro NCAP).
- [ ] **Real-time budget** for Level 3: $\leq 100$ ms; Level 4: $\leq 30$ ms.
- [ ] **mAP@0.5:0.95** is the modern rigorous benchmark; **COCO** is the default.
- [ ] Process noise covariance $\mathbf{Q}$ models unmodeled accelerations; **never** set it to zero.
- [ ] YOLO loss has $\lambda_{coord} = 5$, $\lambda_{noobj} = 0.5$ weighting factors.
- [ ] **Negative IoU** is impossible — intersection cannot be negative; use $\max(0, \cdot)$.
- [ ] Image coordinates follow $(x_{tl}, y_{tl}, x_{br}, y_{br})$ convention; top-left is origin.
- [ ] Tracking algorithms: **SORT** (linear), **DeepSORT** (with appearance features), **ByteTrack** (current SOTA).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Top 3 High-Yield Topics for ESE 2024:**
> 1. IoU derivation and numerical computation.
> 2. YOLO vs Faster R-CNN architectural comparison.
> 3. Kalman filter for 1D tracking with explicit $\mathbf{F}, \mathbf{Q}, \mathbf{H}, \mathbf{R}$ matrices.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Connected Vehicles and IoT: Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) Communication
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT744 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Connected Vehicles and IoT: Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) Communication ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) Communication** is a sophisticated, multi-node vehicular networking paradigm that enables the real-time bidirectional exchange of operational, safety, infotainment, and environmental data between a vehicle and any entity that may affect, or be affected by, the vehicle itself. The "X" in V2X is an umbrella variable that resolves into five primary communication archetypes: **Vehicle-to-Vehicle (V2V)**, **Vehicle-to-Infrastructure (V2I)**, **Vehicle-to-Pedestrian (V2P)**, **Vehicle-to-Network (V2N)**, and **Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G)**. V2X is formally classified by the **SAE International (Society of Automotive Engineers)** under the **SAE J3061** cybersecurity framework and the **SAE J2945** communication standards. It operates primarily in the **5.9 GHz Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITS)** band, governed globally by the **IEEE 802.11p** standard for Dedicated Short-Range Communications (DSRC) and the **3GPP Release 14/15/16 Cellular-V2X (C-V2X)** specification. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** V2X is a foundational pillar for **Cooperative Intelligent Transport Systems (C-ITS)** and is a mandatory pre-requisite concept for **ADAS Level 3+ autonomy** and **IoT-driven Smart City** frameworks. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a busy, unsignalised four-way intersection during heavy fog. You cannot see beyond 5 meters. Now imagine every car, every traffic light, every pedestrian's smartphone, and the road surface itself suddenly speaks a common language. The car in front of you whispers: *"I am braking hard in 30 meters."* The traffic light broadcasts: *"I will turn red in 4 seconds."* The pedestrian's phone screams: *"Jaywalker stepping onto the road at your 2 o'clock."* This is V2X — a **continuous, low-latency, 360-degree "conversation"** that effectively gives the vehicle "X-ray vision" beyond the line-of-sight limitations of cameras, radar, and LiDAR. It transforms a vehicle from a closed, mechanical-electrical island into a **node on a sprawling IoT mesh network**. ### 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Standardized by ETSI & 3GPP) | Metric | Value | Significance | |---|---|---| | **Operational Frequency Band** | **5.85–5.925 GHz** | Reserved globally for ITS safety messaging | | **Maximum Latency** | **< 100 ms** (safety-critical) / **< 20 ms** (collision-avoidance) | Lower than human reaction time (~250 ms) | | **Effective Communication Range** | **300 m** (DSRC) / **~1000 m** (C-V2X) | Covers intersection non-line-of-sight cases | | **Relative Vehicle Speed Tolerance** | **Up to 500 km/h** (relative) | Handles high-speed differential scenarios | | **Message Broadcast Frequency** | **10 Hz** (BSM typical) | 10 Basic Safety Messages per second | > [!NOTE] > **Fundamental Distinction:** Unlike traditional **IoT devices** (which typically transmit kilobytes per hour), a V2X-enabled vehicle can be considered a **high-velocity, mobile edge-IoT node** generating **MB to GB** of telemetry per hour from CAN bus, GNSS, radar, and inertial sensors. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The V2X Communication Taxonomy (Five Pillars) V2X is not a single protocol; it is an architecture. It is deconstructed as follows: * **V2V (Vehicle-to-Vehicle):** Peer-to-peer mesh networking. Vehicles broadcast **Basic Safety Messages (BSMs)** containing position, velocity, heading, and acceleration. Used for forward collision warning, blind spot detection, and cooperative adaptive cruise control. * **V2I (Vehicle-to-Infrastructure):** Communication with **Roadside Units (RSUs)** such as traffic lights, variable message signs, and toll gantries. Enables **GLOSA (Green Light Optimal Speed Advisory)** and intersection collision avoidance. * **V2P (Vehicle-to-Pedestrian):** Communication with vulnerable road users (VRUs) carrying smartphones or wearable IoT devices. Critical for urban safety. * **V2N (Vehicle-to-Network):** Long-range cellular communication with cloud servers (MEC — Multi-access Edge Computing). Used for HD map updates, software-over-the-air (SOTA), and fleet telematics. * **V2G (Vehicle-to-Grid):** Bidirectional energy flow between **EV battery packs** and the smart electrical grid. The vehicle acts as a distributed energy resource (DER). ### 2.2 Two Dominant Underlying Technologies The "radio pipe" beneath V2X is contested by two standards: **A. DSRC (Dedicated Short-Range Communications)** * Based on **IEEE 802.11p** (a vehicular-adapted variant of Wi-Fi). * Operates in the **5.9 GHz** band. * Uses a **CSMA/CA** (Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Avoidance) MAC layer. * **Ad-hoc, decentralized** — no cellular tower required. Latency is very low (~4 ms one-hop). * Backing: SAE J2735 (message set dictionary), SAE J2945 (performance requirements). **B. C-V2X (Cellular-V2X)** * Defined by **3GPP Release 14** (LTE-V2X) and **Release 16** (5G NR-V2X). * Operates in two modes: * **PC5 Interface (Sidelink):** Direct device-to-device, similar to DSRC, bypassing the cellular network. * **Uu Interface (Network):** Communicates via cellular base stations for wide-area V2N. * Leverages existing **4G/5G infrastructure**, offering superior range, reliability, and integration with edge-cloud AI. ### 2.3 The OSI Model Mapping for V2X | OSI Layer | DSRC Stack | C-V2X Stack | Function | |---|---|---|---| | **Application Layer** | SAE J2735 / J2945 | SAE J2735 / ETSI ITS-G5 | Safety, efficiency, infotainment apps | | **Facility Layer** | SAE J2735 | 3GPP V2X Service Layer | Message assembly, encoding | | **Network & Transport Layer** | WSMP (WAVE Short Message Protocol) / UDP / TCP | IP / TCP / UDP / Non-IP | Addressing & reliable delivery | | **Access Layer** | IEEE 802.11p (MAC + PHY) | LTE/5G NR Sidelink (PC5) | Radio resource allocation | | **Physical Layer** | 5.9 GHz OFDM | 5.9 GHz OFDM (sidelink) | Modulation, RF transmission | ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Concept | Formula / Parameter | Description & Units | |---|---|---| | **Shannon-Hartley Channel Capacity** | $C = B \cdot \log_{2}\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | $C$ in bits/s; $B$ = bandwidth (Hz); $S/N$ = signal-to-noise ratio (dimensionless). Sets the **theoretical upper bound** of V2X throughput. | | **Effective Range (Friis Free-Space Loss)** | $P_{r} = P_{t} \cdot G_{t} \cdot G_{r} \cdot \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^{2}$ | $P_r, P_t$ in Watts; $G$ = antenna gain; $\lambda$ in metres; $d$ = distance. Determines DSRC/C-V2X line-of-sight coverage. | | **End-to-End Latency Budget** | $T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{sense}} + T_{\text{process}} + T_{\text{tx}} + T_{\text{prop}} + T_{\text{queue}}$ | Sum of sensing, processing, transmission, propagation, and queuing delays. Must be **< 100 ms**. | | **Propagation Delay** | $T_{\text{prop}} = \dfrac{d}{c}$ | $d$ in metres; $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s (speed of light). | | **Packet Delivery Ratio (PDR)** | $\text{PDR} = \dfrac{N_{\text{received}}}{N_{\text{transmitted}}}$ | Reliability metric; safety apps require **PDR ≥ 90%** at 300 m. | | **Doppler Frequency Shift** | $f_{d} = \dfrac{v}{\lambda} \cdot \cos(\theta)$ | $v$ = relative velocity (m/s); $\theta$ = angle of arrival. Causes carrier frequency offset at high speeds. | | **Cooperative Awareness Message Rate** | $\text{BSM Rate} = \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{BSM}}}$ | Typically **10 Hz** ($T_{\text{BSM}} = 100$ ms) per SAE J2945/1. | | **Market Penetration Sensitivity** | $P_{\text{eff}}(t) = 1 - (1 - p)^{n(t)}$ | $p$ = per-vehicle V2X equipage; $n$ = neighbour count. Safety benefit scales **non-linearly**. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why These Formulas Matter in Engineering:** > * The **Shannon-Hartley** equation dictates why C-V2X with its wider bandwidth allocation can outperform DSRC in dense traffic. > * **Latency Budgeting** is the bedrock of safety-case certification under **ISO 26262 ASIL-D** classification. > * **PDR ≥ 90%** is the empirical threshold below which V2X-based collision avoidance algorithms lose statistical efficacy. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: V2X Maximum Achievable Throughput Using Shannon-Hartley Theorem The Shannon-Hartley theorem provides the **absolute theoretical maximum** data rate that a V2X channel can sustain without error, given a fixed bandwidth and signal-to-noise ratio. **Step 1 — Statement of the Theorem:** $$ C = B \cdot \log_{2}\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) $$ Where: * $C$ = channel capacity in **bits per second (bps)** * $B$ = channel bandwidth in **Hertz (Hz)** * $S$ = average signal power in **Watts (W)** * $N$ = average noise power in **Watts (W)** **Step 2 — Convert Signal-to-Noise Ratio to Decibels (for practical use):** $$ \left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_{\text{dB}} = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{S}{N}\right) $$ **Step 3 — Linear Conversion Back from dB:** $$ \frac{S}{N} = 10^{\left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_{\text{dB}} / 10} $$ **Step 4 — Substitution into Capacity Equation:** $$ C = B \cdot \log_{2}\left(1 + 10^{\left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_{\text{dB}} / 10}\right) $$ **Step 5 — Numerical Example (KTU-style problem):** Consider a C-V2X sidelink channel with: * $B = 20$ MHz (allocated 5G NR-V2X bandwidth) * $S/N = 15$ dB (typical urban vehicular SNR) First, convert 15 dB to linear scale: $$ \frac{S}{N} = 10^{15 / 10} = 10^{1.5} \approx 31.62 $$ Now substitute into the capacity equation: $$ C = 20 \times 10^{6} \cdot \log_{2}(1 + 31.62) $$ $$ C = 20 \times 10^{6} \cdot \log_{2}(32.62) $$ $$ C = 20 \times 10^{6} \cdot 5.027 $$ $$ C \approx 100.54 \text{ Mbps} $$ **Step 6 — Engineering Interpretation:** The achievable theoretical throughput is approximately **100.54 Mbps**. Real-world systems operate at **30–50% efficiency** due to pilot signals, guard bands, HARQ retransmissions, and protocol overhead, yielding a practical throughput of **~30–50 Mbps** — still ample for BSM, sensor sharing, and HD map chunks. --- ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Performance — DSRC vs. C-V2X (LTE) vs. NR-V2X (5G) | Performance Parameter | DSRC (IEEE 802.11p) | C-V2X (LTE Rel. 14) | NR-V2X (5G Rel. 16/17) | |---|---|---|---| | **Bandwidth** | 10 MHz (single channel) | Up to 20 MHz | Up to 100 MHz (FR1) | | **Peak Data Rate** | ~6 Mbps (practical) | ~50 Mbps | ~500 Mbps | | **One-Way Latency** | ~4 ms | ~20 ms | ~3 ms (URLLC mode) | | **Range** | ~300 m | ~500 m | ~1000 m | | **High-Speed Mobility Support** | Up to ~200 km/h | Up to ~250 km/h | Up to ~500 km/h | | **Network Dependency** | None (fully ad-hoc) | Optional (PC5 sidelink) | Optional (PC5 sidelink) | | **Synchronization Requirement** | Asynchronous | Network-sync (GNSS) | Network or GNSS-sync | | **Spectral Efficiency** | Low (CSMA contention) | High (scheduled) | Very high (flexible numerology) | | **Maturity / Deployment** | Mature (US pilot fleet) | Deployed (China, EU) | Emerging (2024+) | | **Best-Use Scenario** | Low-density V2V | Mixed V2V/V2I/V2N | Autonomous + IoT + HD mapping | ### 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation: V2X End-to-End Latency Budget A KTU-favourite question is: *"Calculate the total latency for a V2X Basic Safety Message transmitted between two vehicles separated by 250 metres."* **Given parameters:** * Sensing delay: $T_{\text{sense}} = 10$ ms (radar/CAN acquisition) * Processing delay: $T_{\text{process}} = 5$ ms (BSM encoding by V2X stack) * Transmission delay: $T_{\text{tx}} = 2$ ms (packet airtime for 200-byte BSM @ 6 Mbps) * Propagation delay: $T_{\text{prop}} = ?$ ms * Queuing delay: $T_{\text{queue}} = 1$ ms (MAC contention) **Step 1 — Compute Propagation Delay:** $$ T_{\text{prop}} = \frac{d}{c} = \frac{250 \text{ m}}{3 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}} $$ $$ T_{\text{prop}} \approx 8.33 \times 10^{-7} \text{ s} = 0.000833 \text{ ms} $$ **Step 2 — Apply the Latency Equation:** $$ T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{sense}} + T_{\text{process}} + T_{\text{tx}} + T_{\text{prop}} + T_{\text{queue}} $$ $$ T_{\text{total}} = 10 + 5 + 2 + 0.000833 + 1 $$ $$ T_{\text{total}} \approx 18.000833 \text{ ms} $$ **Step 3 — Verify Safety Compliance:** $$ T_{\text{total}} \approx 18 \text{ ms} \quad \ll \quad 100 \text{ ms (safety threshold)} $$ Therefore, the system **complies** with SAE J2945 latency requirements. **Step 4 — Engineering Takeaway:** Propagation delay is **negligible** in V2X because RF energy travels at the speed of light. The dominant latency contributors are **sensing, processing, and MAC-layer queuing** — which is why on-board ECU consolidation and high-speed hardware accelerators (e.g., NXP SAF5400, Qualcomm Snapdragon Auto) are critical. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 High-Level V2X Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Cloud_Plane ["Cloud & Edge Plane"] MEC["MEC Edge Server
AI Inference & HD Maps"] Cloud["Cloud Backend
Fleet Analytics, OTA"] end subgraph Network_Plane ["Network Plane"] RSU1["RSU - Traffic Light
GLOSA, SPaT"] RSU2["RSU - Toll Gantry
Payment & Enforcement"] BS["5G Base Station gNB
Uu Interface"] end subgraph Vehicle_Plane ["Vehicle On-Board Plane"] OBU["V2X On-Board Unit
SAE J2945 Stack"] ECU1["ADAS ECU
Radar + Camera Fusion"] ECU2["Telematics Control Unit
GNSS + 4G/5G Modem"] CAN["CAN-FD Bus
Powertrain & Chassis Data"] end subgraph VRU_Plane ["Vulnerable Road User Plane"] Phone["Pedestrian Smartphone
V2P App / BLE"] Wear["Cyclist Wearable
V2P Beacon"] end MEC <--> BS BS <--> ECU2 Cloud <--> MEC RSU1 <--> OBU RSU2 <--> OBU OBU <--> ECU1 ECU1 <--> CAN ECU2 <--> OBU Phone <--> OBU Wear <--> OBU ``` ### 4.2 V2X Communication Taxonomy (Five Communication Types) ```mermaid flowchart LR V["Central Vehicle
OBU + Sensors"]:::vehicle V2V["V2V
Vehicle-to-Vehicle
BSM Exchange"]:::commtype V2I["V2I
Vehicle-to-Infrastructure
RSU + SPaT"]:::commtype V2P["V2P
Vehicle-to-Pedestrian
VRU Alerts"]:::commtype V2N["V2N
Vehicle-to-Network
4G/5G + Cloud"]:::commtype V2G["V2G
Vehicle-to-Grid
EV Bidirectional"]:::commtype V --> V2V V --> V2I V --> V2P V --> V2N V --> V2G classDef vehicle fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 classDef commtype fill:#1E90FF,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF ``` ### 4.3 V2X Protocol Stack — DSRC vs. C-V2X Comparison ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph AppLayer ["Application Layer"] A1["SAE J2735
BSM, MAP, SPaT, PSM"] A2["ETSI ITS-G5
CAM, DENM"] end subgraph FacLayer ["Facility / Message Layer"] F1["SAE J2945 Encoding"] F2["ETSI Facility Layer"] end subgraph NetLayer ["Network / Transport Layer"] N1["WSMP / UDP / IP"] N2["IP / TCP / UDP / Non-IP Sidelink"] end subgraph AccLayer ["Access / MAC Layer"] M1["IEEE 802.11p
CSMA-CA, 5.9 GHz"] M2["LTE-V2X PC5
SPS / Sensing"] end subgraph PhyLayer ["Physical Layer"] P1["OFDM 10 MHz Channels"] P2["OFDM / SC-FDM Flexible Numerology"] end AppLayer --> FacLayer --> NetLayer --> AccLayer --> PhyLayer M1 -.DSRC.-> P1 M2 -.CV2X.-> P2 F1 -.DSRC.-> N1 F2 -.CV2X.-> N2 A1 -.DSRC.-> F1 A2 -.CV2X.-> F2 ``` ### 4.4 V2X Data Flow — From Sensor Detection to Network Broadcast ```mermaid flowchart TD S1["Step 1: GNSS Position Fix
Lat, Long, Alt, Heading"] S2["Step 2: CAN Bus Read
Speed, Yaw Rate, Steering Angle"] S3["Step 3: Sensor Fusion
Radar + Camera + LiDAR"] S4["Step 4: BSM Encoding
SAE J2735 Packet Assembly"] S5["Step 5: MAC Arbitration
CSMA-CA or SPS Allocation"] S6["Step 6: RF Transmission
5.9 GHz OFDM Burst"] S7["Step 7: Receiver BSM Decode
Remote OBU Validation"] S8["Step 8: Decision Engine
ADAS Warning or Autonomous Braking"] S1 --> S4 S2 --> S4 S3 --> S4 S4 --> S5 S5 --> S6 S6 --> S7 S7 --> S8 ``` ### 4.5 V2X Security Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB P["V2X Message Origin
Sender Vehicle"]:::sender Sign["Cryptographic Signing
ECDSA or PKI per IEEE 1609.2"]:::sec Cert["Certificate Authority
PKI Hierarchy (SCMS)"]:::sec Tx["Encrypted Transmission
5.9 GHz Sidelink"]:::tx Rx["Receiver OBU"]:::rx Ver["Certificate Verification
+ Plausibility Check"]:::sec Dec["Decision: Accept / Reject / Penalise Misbehavior Node"]:::sec P --> Sign Cert --> Sign Sign --> Tx Tx --> Rx Rx --> Ver Ver --> Dec classDef sender fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#FFF classDef sec fill:#9370DB,stroke:#000,color:#FFF classDef tx fill:#20B2AA,stroke:#000,color:#FFF classDef rx fill:#4682B4,stroke:#000,color:#FFF ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)** --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model Paper)]** Define V2X communication. List and briefly explain its five primary modes. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything) is a vehicular communication paradigm in which a vehicle exchanges data with surrounding entities to improve safety, efficiency, and infotainment services. The five modes are: 1. **V2V (Vehicle-to-Vehicle):** Direct peer communication for safety alerts. 2. **V2I (Vehicle-to-Infrastructure):** Communication with Roadside Units (RSUs). 3. **V2P (Vehicle-to-Pedestrian):** Communication with vulnerable road users. 4. **V2N (Vehicle-to-Network):** Long-range cellular cloud connectivity. 5. **V2G (Vehicle-to-Grid):** Bidirectional EV-to-grid energy exchange. *(1 mark for definition + 0.4 marks per mode = 3 marks)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Paper)]** Compare DSRC and C-V2X in terms of underlying standard, latency, and network dependency. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Parameter | DSRC | C-V2X | |---|---|---| | **Standard** | IEEE 802.11p | 3GPP Release 14/15/16 | | **Latency** | ~4 ms | ~20 ms (LTE) / ~3 ms (5G NR) | | **Network Dependency** | None (fully ad-hoc) | Optional via PC5 sidelink | | **Infrastructure** | Requires RSUs | Reuses cellular base stations | DSRC operates independently without a network, while C-V2X uses cellular infrastructure (eMTC/NR) but also supports direct sidelink communication. *(1 mark per row + 0.5 synthesis = 3 marks)* --- ### **PART B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)** --- ## **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **(a)** Explain in detail the V2X communication architecture with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the SAE J2735 and SAE J2945 standards. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A C-V2X sidelink channel has a bandwidth of 20 MHz and SNR of 12 dB. Calculate the theoretical maximum channel capacity using the Shannon-Hartley theorem. Comment on the practical throughput achievable in a real vehicular environment. **(7 Marks)** ### **Model Solution:** **(a) V2X Architecture & SAE Standards (7 Marks):** The V2X architecture is a layered, multi-tier system comprising: * **Vehicle On-Board Unit (OBU):** Hosts the V2X protocol stack, interfaces with CAN bus, GNSS, and ADAS sensors. * **Roadside Unit (RSU):** Fixed infrastructure that relays messages between vehicles and the cloud. * **Backend Network:** Cloud servers, MEC (Multi-access Edge Computing) nodes, and PKI certificate authorities. * **VRU Devices:** Smartphones, wearables for V2P. **Data Flow:** Sensors → CAN/FlexRay → OBU → Encoding (J2735) → MAC (802.11p/PC5) → PHY (5.9 GHz) → Air Interface → Remote OBU → Decoding → ADAS/Autonomous Stack. **Role of Standards:** * **SAE J2735:** Defines the **message set dictionary** (BSM, MAP, SPaT, PSM). It specifies the byte-level encoding of all V2X payloads. * **SAE J2945:** Defines **performance requirements** (latency, PDR, message frequency). For example, J2945/1 mandates BSM broadcast at 10 Hz. *Block diagram carries 3 marks; layered explanation 2 marks; standards role 2 marks.* **(b) Shannon-Hartley Throughput Calculation (7 Marks):** **Given:** $B = 20$ MHz, $S/N = 12$ dB **Step 1:** Convert SNR from dB to linear scale. $$ \frac{S}{N} = 10^{12/10} = 10^{1.2} \approx 15.85 $$ **Step 2:** Apply Shannon-Hartley theorem. $$ C = 20 \times 10^{6} \cdot \log_{2}(1 + 15.85) $$ $$ C = 20 \times 10^{6} \cdot \log_{2}(16.85) $$ $$ C = 20 \times 10^{6} \cdot 4.075 $$ $$ C \approx 81.5 \text{ Mbps} $$ **Step 3:** Practical interpretation. The theoretical maximum is **81.5 Mbps**, but practical throughput in real vehicular environments (with HARQ overhead, pilot signals, guard bands, MIMO inefficiency, and congestion at intersections) is typically **30–50% of theoretical**, yielding **~25–40 Mbps**. This is still sufficient for BSMs (200 B @ 10 Hz ≈ 16 kbps), sensor sharing, and HD map delta updates. *Formula substitution 3 marks; numerical evaluation 2 marks; practical comment 2 marks.* --- ## **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **(a)** What is C-V2X? Explain the PC5 sidelink and Uu interface modes. Discuss how 5G NR-V2X improves upon LTE-V2X. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Two vehicles are 250 m apart. The V2X safety stack has: sensing delay 12 ms, processing 8 ms, transmission 3 ms, queuing 2 ms. Calculate the total end-to-end latency and verify compliance with the SAE J2945 100 ms safety threshold. **(7 Marks)** ### **Model Solution:** **(a) C-V2X and 5G NR-V2X (7 Marks):** **C-V2X (Cellular-V2X)** is a 3GPP-defined communication standard (Rel. 14 onwards) that uses cellular infrastructure to enable V2X. **Two Operating Modes:** * **PC5 (Sidelink) Mode:** Direct device-to-device communication between vehicles, bypassing the cellular network. Operates in the **5.9 GHz ITS band**. Ideal for safety-critical low-latency messaging. * **Uu (Network) Mode:** Traditional uplink/downlink communication via the cellular base station (eNodeB/gNodeB). Suitable for non-time-critical V2N services (HD maps, OTA updates). **5G NR-V2X Improvements over LTE-V2X:** * **Lower Latency:** 3 ms (URLLC) vs 20 ms in LTE. * **Higher Bandwidth:** Up to 100 MHz (FR1) vs 20 MHz in LTE. * **Flexible Numerology:** Subcarrier spacing of 15/30/60/120 kHz adaptable to high mobility Doppler conditions. * **Sidelink Resource Allocation:** Mode 2 (autonomous sensing-based scheduling) avoids network dependency. * **Native support for platooning, sensor sharing, and coordinated cooperative driving.** *Definitions 2 marks; PC5/Uu 3 marks; 5G improvements 2 marks.* **(b) Latency Calculation (7 Marks):** **Given:** $d = 250$ m, $T_{\text{sense}} = 12$ ms, $T_{\text{process}} = 8$ ms, $T_{\text{tx}} = 3$ ms, $T_{\text{queue}} = 2$ ms **Step 1:** Compute propagation delay. $$ T_{\text{prop}} = \frac{250}{3 \times 10^{8}} \approx 0.000833 \text{ ms} \approx 0.001 \text{ ms (negligible)} $$ **Step 2:** Sum all components. $$ T_{\text{total}} = 12 + 8 + 3 + 0.001 + 2 = 25.001 \text{ ms} $$ **Step 3:** Compare to threshold. $$ T_{\text{total}} \approx 25 \text{ ms} \quad \ll \quad 100 \text{ ms (SAE J2945 limit)} $$ **Conclusion:** The system **complies** with SAE J2945/1 safety latency requirements, leaving a comfortable margin of **75 ms** for additional processing, retransmission, or congestion. *Each step 2 marks; verification + conclusion 1 mark.* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting propagation delay** in latency calculations — it is negligible but MUST appear in the equation for full marks. > 2. **Mixing up DSRC and C-V2X** frequency bands or confusing IEEE 802.11p with 802.11ac (Wi-Fi). > 3. **Not converting SNR from dB to linear** before applying Shannon-Hartley — this is the most common arithmetic error. > 4. **Omitting the practical efficiency factor** when discussing theoretical throughput — examiners expect a real-world commentary. > 5. **V2G confusion:** Many students wrongly classify V2G as only "charging." It is **bidirectional energy flow** with Vehicle-to-Home (V2H) and Vehicle-to-Load (V2L) sub-categories. > 6. **Missing SAE J3061** cybersecurity reference — it is mandatory for KTU 2024 NEP-aligned cybersecurity outcomes. --- ### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember * **V2X = V2V + V2I + V2P + V2N + V2G** — memorize all five. * **Two underlying technologies:** DSRC (IEEE 802.11p, ad-hoc) and C-V2X (3GPP, cellular). * **Operating band:** **5.85–5.925 GHz** (globally harmonized ITS spectrum). * **Message standards:** SAE **J2735** (encoding), SAE **J2945** (performance). * **BSM broadcast rate:** **10 Hz** per vehicle (J2945/1). * **Latency target:** **< 100 ms** general, **< 20 ms** for collision avoidance. * **Channel capacity:** Use **Shannon-Hartley** $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ — always convert SNR from dB. * **PDR threshold:** **≥ 90%** at 300 m for safety certification. * **5G NR-V2X advantages:** URLLC, wider bandwidth, higher mobility support, native sidelink. * **Security framework:** IEEE **1609.2** + SCMS PKI for message authenticity. * **Real-world analogy:** V2X is the vehicle's "omniscient sixth sense" beyond line-of-sight sensors. * **Standard alignment:** ETSI ITS-G5 (Europe), SAE J3061/J2945 (US/global), 3GPP (cellular). * **Application domains:** Cooperative Adaptive Cruise Control (C-ACC), Platooning, GLOSA, Intersection Safety, V2X-based Autonomous Driving (L4+). * **Market sensitivity:** Safety benefits scale **non-linearly** with penetration — $P_{\text{eff}} = 1 - (1-p)^n$.

topics.title: Telematics and Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT744 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Telematics and Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Automotive Telematics** is an interdisciplinary field that integrates **telecommunications**, **informatics**, **sensor technology**, and **vehicle electronics** to enable bidirectional data exchange between a vehicle and external systems (cloud servers, other vehicles, infrastructure, or mobile devices). The term itself is a linguistic portmanteau of **Telecommunications + Informatics**, formally introduced by the French engineer **Simon Nora** in 1976.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, automotive telematics encompasses the in-vehicle hardware (TCU — Telematics Control Unit), the wireless communication channels (cellular, satellite, DSRC, C-V2X), the back-end cloud infrastructure, and the software applications that consume vehicle-generated data for safety, convenience, commercial, and regulatory purposes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEMUT744 – Module 3):**
> Telematics is the *convergent layer* that connects the previously studied mechanical, electrical, and electronic subsystems (Modules 1 & 2) to the wider digital ecosystem. Examiners frequently pose questions that bridge telematics with ADAS, EV powertrains, and autonomous driving.

### Key Constituent Disciplines

| Discipline | Contribution to Telematics |
|---|---|
| **Telecommunications** | GSM / LTE / 5G / Satellite data links |
| **Informatics** | Cloud platforms, Big Data analytics, OTA software |
| **Sensing** | GPS, IMU, CAN-bus data, OBD-II diagnostics |
| **Vehicle Electronics** | ECU integration, gateway ECUs, in-vehicle networking |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Car as a Smartphone on Wheels

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Pump:** Imagine your car is a **smartphone with four wheels and a 1.5-tonne battery**. Just as a smartphone constantly transmits its location to apps like Uber or Google Maps, an instrumented vehicle *continuously broadcasts* its position, speed, engine health, driving behavior, and crash status to a remote server. The telematics control unit is the *modem + baseband processor* of this vehicular smartphone.

A simpler analogy: think of telematics as the **"WhatsApp of the automotive world"** — it allows your car to *send messages* (telemetry data: speed, fuel level, DTC codes) and *receive messages* (remote lock/unlock, software updates, emergency dispatch) over a wireless channel, with the OEM or fleet operator acting as the *contact list server*.

## 1.3 Why Telematics Matters — Engineering Motivation

- **Safety:** Automatic crash notification (eCall in EU, GESARA in India) reduces emergency response time by **~40%** in rural areas.
- **Commercial:** Fleet operators reduce fuel costs by **5–15%** through route optimization and driver-behavior analytics.
- **Regulatory:** Mandated by governments (e.g., AIS-140 in India for public transport vehicles since 2018).
- **Insurance:** Usage-Based Insurance (UBI) premiums vary by **±30%** based on telematics-scored driving behavior.
- **Autonomy:** Telematics forms the **connectivity backbone** required for Level 3+ autonomous vehicles (V2X communication).

## 1.4 Architectural Block Layers (Top-Level)

A telematics system has four canonical layers:

1. **Vehicle Layer** — Sensors, ECU, TCU, antennas
2. **Communication Layer** — Cellular (4G/5G), GNSS, DSRC/C-V2X, Wi-Fi, Bluetooth
3. **Cloud / Edge Layer** — Data ingestion, processing, ML inference
4. **Application Layer** — End-user services (mobile apps, dashboards, insurance, emergency)

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for the four layers:** **"V-C-C-A" → "Vehicle-Cloud-Communication-Apps"** (rearrange as "**V²C²A**" — the *square* of vehicle + cloud).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Telematics Control Unit (TCU) — Architectural Anatomy

The TCU is the **heart** of any automotive telematics system. It is a hardened, automotive-grade (AEC-Q100 qualified) embedded computer that typically contains:

| Subsystem | Function |
|---|---|
| **Microcontroller / SoC** | Runs real-time OS (AUTOSAR Classic/Adaptive, Linux, QNX) |
| **GNSS Receiver** | Multi-constellation (GPS, GLONASS, Galileo, BeiDou, IRNSS) |
| **Cellular Modem** | 4G LTE Cat-1/Cat-M1 or 5G NR Sub-6/mmWave |
| **CAN / LIN / Ethernet Transceivers** | Reads vehicle bus data (speed, RPM, DTCs) |
| **Accelerometer / Gyroscope (IMU)** | Crash detection (Δv threshold ~ 2 g) |
| **Non-Volatile Storage** | Buffers data during cellular dead zones |
| **Secure Element (eUICC)** | Hardware root-of-trust for OTA & authentication |
| **Backup Battery** | Operates ~ 10 minutes after main battery disconnection |

## 2.2 Data Flow Logic — Step-by-Step Operational Sequence

> [!NOTE]
> **The 7-Step Telematics Data Journey** (memorize this for KTU 14-mark answers):

1. **Acquisition:** TCU polls CAN bus at ~ 100 Hz for vehicle dynamics.
2. **Time-Stamping:** A GNSS-disciplined RTC stamps each frame.
3. **Geotagging:** GPS coordinates (lat, lon, alt) are appended.
4. **Compression & Encryption:** Payload is AES-128/256 encrypted, then zlib-compressed.
5. **Transport:** Sent over TCP/UDP via MQTT or HTTPs to the OEM cloud (MQTT is preferred for low-bandwidth telemetry).
6. **Ingestion & Processing:** Cloud Kafka queue → stream processor → time-series DB.
7. **Action / Visualization:** API serves data to fleet dashboards, mobile apps, or insurance engines.

## 2.3 Communication Protocol Stack

| Layer | Automotive Telematics Protocol |
|---|---|
| **Application** | MQTT, HTTP/REST, gRPC, SOME/IP |
| **Transport** | TCP (reliable), UDP (low-latency V2X) |
| **Network** | IPv4/IPv6 |
| **Data-Link** | LTE-Uu, 5G NR, IEEE 802.11p (DSRC), C-V2X (3GPP Rel. 14/16) |
| **Physical** | Licensed cellular spectrum (700 MHz – 6 GHz), 5.9 GHz ITS band |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **examination-ready**. Tables use `\vert` instead of the pipe character to avoid markdown syntax conflicts.

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbol Legend | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **GPS Pseudorange** | $\rho_i = c \cdot (t_r - t_s)$ | $\rho_i$ = pseudorange to satellite $i$, $c$ = speed of light ($3 \times 10^8$ m/s), $t_r$ = receive time, $t_s$ = transmit time | meters |
| 2 | **Trilateration Geometry** | $(x - x_i)^2 + (y - y_i)^2 + (z - z_i)^2 = \rho_i^2$ | $(x,y,z)$ = unknown vehicle position, $(x_i,y_i,z_i)$ = known satellite position | meters |
| 3 | **Shannon Channel Capacity** | $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ | $C$ = capacity (bps), $B$ = bandwidth (Hz), SNR = signal-to-noise ratio (linear) | bits/s |
| 4 | **Data Throughput** | $T = \dfrac{P \times 8}{t}$ | $T$ = throughput (bps), $P$ = packet payload (bytes), $t$ = transmission time (s) | bits/s |
| 5 | **Cellular Line-of-Sight Range** | $d \approx \sqrt{2 R_e h}$ | $d$ = max range (m), $R_e$ = Earth radius ($6.371 \times 10^6$ m), $h$ = antenna height (m) | meters |
| 6 | **Crash Detection Δv Threshold** | $\Delta v = \int_0^{\tau} a(t) \, dt$ | $\Delta v$ = velocity change (m/s), $a$ = deceleration (m/s²), $\tau$ = pulse duration | m/s |
| 7 | **Doppler Frequency Shift (V2X)** | $f_d = \dfrac{v \cdot f_0}{c} \cos\theta$ | $f_d$ = Doppler shift (Hz), $v$ = relative velocity, $f_0$ = carrier frequency, $\theta$ = angle of arrival | Hz |
| 8 | **Latency Budget (5G URLLC)** | $L_{total} = L_{proc} + L_{queue} + L_{tx} + L_{prop}$ | $L$ = latency component (ms), URLLC target $\leq 1$ ms | ms |
| 9 | **Fuel-Economy Telematics Index** | $FE = \dfrac{D}{V_f}$ | $FE$ = fuel economy (km/L), $D$ = distance (km), $V_f$ = volume of fuel (L) | km/L |
| 10 | **UBI Driving Score (weighted)** | $S = w_1 H + w_2 B + w_3 S + w_4 N$ | $H$ = harsh braking, $B$ = speeding, $S$ = smoothness, $N$ = night-driving, $\sum w_i = 1$ | dimensionless (0–100) |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **eCall / ERA-GLONASS:** EU mandates automatic emergency call within ~ 1 second of crash detection.
- **AIS-140 (India):** All public service vehicles must have an IRNSS-enabled TCU with emergency buttons.
- **Stolen Vehicle Tracking:** GPS + cellular + ignition-kill relay.
- **Predictive Maintenance:** ML models flag component failure **~ 2 weeks** before occurrence using CAN-bus trends.
- **EV Telematics:** Battery State-of-Health (SoH), charger locator, smart-grid V2G integration.
- **Fleet Logistics:** Last-mile delivery route optimization reduces miles driven by **8–12%**.

## 2.6 Security & Privacy Considerations

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** A 14-mark question on telematics almost always includes a sub-part on **cybersecurity**. Memorize at least three mitigation techniques.

- **Encryption in transit:** TLS 1.3 over MQTT/HTTPs.
- **Authentication:** Mutual TLS with X.509 certificates stored in eUICC secure element.
- **Secure boot + signed OTA:** Prevents ECU firmware tampering.
- **GDPR / DPDP Act compliance:** Anonymization of location traces.
- **Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS):** Monitor CAN-bus anomaly patterns.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: GPS Position Fix via Trilateration

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this derivation matters for KTU:** A classic 14-mark question asks *"Explain how a vehicular GNSS receiver determines its position using four satellites."* The mathematics below is the model answer.

### 3.1.1 Setting Up the Pseudorange Equation

A GPS receiver computes a **pseudorange** $\rho_i$ to each satellite $i$ by measuring the travel time of the ranging signal and multiplying by the speed of light:

$$
\rho_i = c \cdot (t_{r,i} - t_{s,i})
$$

Because the receiver's clock is not perfectly synchronized with the satellite's atomic clock, the pseudorange contains a **clock bias** error $b = c \cdot \delta t$. The corrected geometric range $R_i$ is therefore:

$$
R_i = \rho_i - c \cdot \delta t
$$

The true geometric range from the vehicle's unknown position $(x, y, z)$ to satellite $i$'s known position $(x_i, y_i, z_i)$ is:

$$
R_i = \sqrt{(x - x_i)^2 + (y - y_i)^2 + (z - z_i)^2}
$$

### 3.1.2 The Four-Equation System

For four satellites ($i = 1, 2, 3, 4$), the system of equations is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{(x - x_1)^2 + (y - y_1)^2 + (z - z_1)^2} &= \rho_1 - c \delta t \\
\sqrt{(x - x_2)^2 + (y - y_2)^2 + (z - z_2)^2} &= \rho_2 - c \delta t \\
\sqrt{(x - x_3)^2 + (y - y_3)^2 + (z - z_3)^2} &= \rho_3 - c \delta t \\
\sqrt{(x - x_4)^2 + (y - y_4)^2 + (z - z_4)^2} &= \rho_4 - c \delta t
\end{aligned}
$$

This system has **four unknowns** $(x, y, z, \delta t)$ — exactly solvable with four satellites.

### 3.1.3 Linearization via Taylor Expansion

To convert the non-linear system into a linear one, expand around an *approximate* position $(x_0, y_0, z_0, \delta t_0)$:

$$
R_i \approx R_{i,0} + \frac{\partial R_i}{\partial x} \Delta x + \frac{\partial R_i}{\partial y} \Delta y + \frac{\partial R_i}{\partial z} \Delta z
$$

The partial derivatives are the **direction cosines** from receiver to satellite:

$$
\frac{\partial R_i}{\partial x} = \frac{x_0 - x_i}{R_{i,0}}, \quad \frac{\partial R_i}{\partial y} = \frac{y_0 - y_i}{R_{i,0}}, \quad \frac{\partial R_i}{\partial z} = \frac{z_0 - z_i}{R_{i,0}}
$$

### 3.1.4 Matrix Form and Iterative Solution

The linearized system in matrix form is:

$$
\mathbf{G} \cdot \Delta \mathbf{x} = \Delta \boldsymbol{\rho}
$$

where:

$$
\mathbf{G} = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{x_0 - x_1}{R_{1,0}} & \frac{y_0 - y_1}{R_{1,0}} & \frac{z_0 - z_1}{R_{1,0}} & 1 \\ \frac{x_0 - x_2}{R_{2,0}} & \frac{y_0 - y_2}{R_{2,0}} & \frac{z_0 - z_2}{R_{2,0}} & 1 \\ \frac{x_0 - x_3}{R_{3,0}} & \frac{y_0 - y_3}{R_{3,0}} & \frac{z_0 - z_3}{R_{3,0}} & 1 \\ \frac{x_0 - x_4}{R_{4,0}} & \frac{y_0 - y_4}{R_{4,0}} & \frac{z_0 - z_4}{R_{4,0}} & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

and

$$
\Delta \mathbf{x} = \begin{bmatrix} \Delta x \\ \Delta y \\ \Delta z \\ -c \, \delta t \end{bmatrix}, \quad \Delta \boldsymbol{\rho} = \begin{bmatrix} \rho_1 - R_{1,0} \\ \rho_2 - R_{2,0} \\ \rho_3 - R_{3,0} \\ \rho_4 - R_{4,0} \end{bmatrix}
$$

The least-squares solution (with $N \geq 4$ satellites) is:

$$
\Delta \mathbf{x} = (\mathbf{G}^T \mathbf{G})^{-1} \mathbf{G}^T \Delta \boldsymbol{\rho}
$$

The receiver iterates this **Newton-Raphson** update until $\vert \Delta \mathbf{x} \vert < \epsilon$ (typically $\epsilon = 1$ meter for automotive-grade GNSS).

## 3.2 Worked Example: Data Throughput of a Telematics Uplink

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU-style numerical:** A fleet telematics device uploads a 256-byte CAN snapshot every 2 seconds over an LTE-M link with effective bandwidth 300 kHz and SNR = 15 (linear, ~ 11.76 dB). Compute (i) Shannon capacity, (ii) actual throughput achieved, (iii) latency contribution per packet.

### Given
- Bandwidth $B = 300 \text{ kHz} = 3 \times 10^5 \text{ Hz}$
- SNR $= 15$ (linear)
- Packet size $P = 256 \text{ bytes} = 2048 \text{ bits}$
- Update period $T_{up} = 2 \text{ s}$

### Step (i) — Shannon Capacity

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= B \cdot \log_2(1 + \text{SNR}) \\
  &= 3 \times 10^5 \cdot \log_2(1 + 15) \\
  &= 3 \times 10^5 \cdot \log_2(16) \\
  &= 3 \times 10^5 \cdot 4 \\
  &= 1.2 \times 10^6 \text{ bps} = 1.2 \text{ Mbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step (ii) — Application-Layer Throughput

Accounting for a typical LTE-M protocol overhead of ~ 30% (HARQ, RLC headers, padding):

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{app} &= 0.7 \times 1.2 \times 10^6 = 8.4 \times 10^5 \text{ bps}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step (iii) — Transmission Time per Packet

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{tx} &= \frac{P}{T_{app}} = \frac{2048}{8.4 \times 10^5} \\
       &= 2.438 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 2.44 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $t_{tx} \ll T_{up} = 2$ s, the link has **> 800× idle headroom**, allowing additional payloads (firmware logs, OTA deltas, in-cabin video snapshots).

## 3.3 Derivation: Cellular Line-of-Sight (LOS) Coverage Radius

The maximum distance from a base-station antenna to the horizon, neglecting atmospheric refraction:

$$
d_{LOS} = \sqrt{2 R_e h + h^2} \approx \sqrt{2 R_e h} \quad (\text{since } h \ll R_e)
$$

**Numerical Example:** If a base-station antenna is at height $h = 30$ m, with $R_e = 6.371 \times 10^6$ m:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{LOS} &\approx \sqrt{2 \times 6.371 \times 10^6 \times 30} \\
        &= \sqrt{3.8226 \times 10^8} \\
        &\approx 1.955 \times 10^4 \text{ m} \approx 19.55 \text{ km}
\end{aligned}
$$

With tropospheric refraction (k-factor ≈ 1.33), effective coverage extends to $\approx 22.5$ km — explaining why **rural cellular dead-zones for telematics are common beyond 20 km from towers**.

## 3.4 Crash Severity Index (Telematics Δv Computation)

A tri-axial MEMS accelerometer in the TCU samples at 100 Hz. The velocity change during a crash pulse is:

$$
\Delta v = \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k \cdot \Delta t
$$

If deceleration $a = 8 g = 78.4 \text{ m/s}^2$ sustained for $\Delta t = 0.12$ s:

$$
\Delta v = 78.4 \times 0.12 = 9.41 \text{ m/s} \approx 33.9 \text{ km/h}
$$

This is the **threshold above which eCall is auto-triggered** in most OEM implementations.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 (Model)]** Define **automotive telematics**. List **four** major functional blocks of a Telematics Control Unit (TCU).

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Automotive telematics is the integrated use of telecommunications and informatics in road vehicles to enable two-way data exchange between the vehicle and external systems for safety, navigation, diagnostics, and infotainment. *(1 mark)*

Four major functional blocks of a TCU are: *(2 marks)*
1. **GNSS Receiver** — for position, velocity, time (PVT).
2. **Cellular Modem** — for wide-area data uplink/downlink.
3. **Microcontroller / SoC** — runs application logic, CAN-bus polling.
4. **Secure Element (eUICC)** — stores credentials, enables secure OTA.

*(For full marks, also mention CAN transceiver, IMU, backup battery, or non-volatile storage.)*

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]** Differentiate between **DSRC** and **C-V2X** as V2X communication technologies. State **one** advantage of each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **DSRC (Dedicated Short-Range Communications):** Based on IEEE 802.11p, operates in the 5.9 GHz ITS band, uses a CSMA/CA MAC, and provides low-latency (~ 4 ms) V2X messaging. *(1 mark)* Advantage: Proven deployment in the US since 2017, low latency for collision warnings. *(0.5 mark)*
- **C-V2X (Cellular V2X):** Defined by 3GPP Release 14+, uses LTE/5G radio, supports both PC5 sidelink (direct) and Uu (network) modes. *(1 mark)* Advantage: Longer range, smooth evolution path to 5G NR-V2X with sidelink positioning. *(0.5 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 (Model)]**

> **(a)** With the aid of a neat block diagram, describe the **end-to-end architecture of an automotive telematics system**, clearly marking the vehicle layer, communication layer, cloud layer, and application layer. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Explain how **GNSS-based position fix** is obtained in a vehicle telematics unit. Derive the **pseudorange equation** and state the **minimum number of satellites** required for a 3-D fix. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) End-to-End Architecture (7 marks):**

The telematics architecture has **four functional layers** as illustrated below:

| Layer | Components | Role |
|---|---|---|
| **Vehicle Layer** | TCU, CAN/LIN bus, OBD-II port, IMU, GNSS antenna, HMI | Acquire and transmit vehicle data |
| **Communication Layer** | 4G/5G cellular, DSRC, C-V2X, Wi-Fi, Bluetooth | Carry data over wireless medium |
| **Cloud / Edge Layer** | MQTT broker, time-series DB, stream processor, ML engine | Store, process, analyze data |
| **Application Layer** | Fleet dashboard, insurance UBI, OTA server, eCall, infotainment | Deliver end-user services |

The TCU at the vehicle layer polls CAN data at ~ 100 Hz, geotags via GNSS, encrypts, and publishes to the cloud over MQTT. The cloud ingests via Kafka, runs ML scoring for driver behavior, and serves APIs to fleet/insurance/emergency applications. *(Diagrammatic representation carries 2 marks; tabular explanation carries 3 marks; full data-flow narrative carries 2 marks.)*

**(b) GNSS Position Fix (7 marks):**

The receiver measures the **signal travel time** from each satellite and multiplies by the speed of light $c$:

$$
\rho_i = c \cdot (t_{r,i} - t_{s,i})
$$

Because of receiver clock bias $\delta t$, the true geometric range is:

$$
\sqrt{(x - x_i)^2 + (y - y_i)^2 + (z - z_i)^2} = \rho_i - c \cdot \delta t
$$

Each satellite contributes one such equation. The system has **four unknowns** $(x, y, z, \delta t)$, hence a **minimum of four satellites** is required for a 3-D position + time fix. *(2 marks for setup, 2 marks for equation, 1 mark for unknowns count, 1 mark for minimum satellite answer, 1 mark for linearization/iteration mention.)*

For 2-D positioning (assuming known altitude), only **three satellites** suffice.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Choice Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]**

> **(a)** Explain the **Usage-Based Insurance (UBI)** model powered by telematics. Derive a **weighted driving score** formula and show how it maps to a premium multiplier. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Describe **eCall / ERA-GLONASS** emergency-call architecture. Compute the **Δv crash threshold** for an 8 g deceleration pulse of 0.12 s duration, and discuss the **latency budget** for a 5G URLLC link. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) UBI Driving Score (7 marks):**

In a UBI scheme, the TCU captures events — harsh braking, rapid acceleration, speeding, sharp cornering, night driving, phone use. A weighted score is:

$$
S = w_1 H + w_2 B + w_3 S + w_4 N
$$

where $H$ = harsh-event count, $B$ = speeding events, $S$ = smoothness inverse, $N$ = night-driving hours, with $\sum_{i=1}^{4} w_i = 1$.

The score is normalized to 0–100. A typical premium multiplier mapping is:

$$
M = 0.7 + 0.6 \times \left(1 - \frac{S}{100}\right)
$$

- Score $S = 90$ → $M = 0.76$ (**24% discount**)
- Score $S = 50$ → $M = 1.00$ (**base premium**)
- Score $S = 20$ → $M = 1.18$ (**18% surcharge**)

*(2 marks for the formula, 2 marks for normalization, 2 marks for premium mapping, 1 mark for example calculation.)*

**(b) eCall & Latency Budget (7 marks):**

The **eCall** system is triggered automatically when $\Delta v$ exceeds a threshold (typically ~ 8 g·s). The TCU dials **112** (EU) or **112/ERA-GLONASS** (Russia) and transmits a **Minimum Set of Data (MSD)** payload containing GPS coordinates, time stamp, vehicle VIN, and direction of travel.

**Computation of Δv threshold:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta v &= a \cdot \Delta t \\
        &= 8 \, g \times 0.12 \text{ s} \\
        &= 8 \times 9.81 \times 0.12 \\
        &= 9.41 \text{ m/s} \\
        &\approx 33.9 \text{ km/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**5G URLLC Latency Budget:**

$$
L_{total} = L_{proc} + L_{queue} + L_{tx} + L_{prop}
$$

For URLLC targeting $L_{total} \leq 1$ ms over a 5G link:
- $L_{proc} \approx 0.2$ ms (edge compute)
- $L_{queue} \approx 0.1$ ms (deterministic scheduling)
- $L_{tx} \approx 0.1$ ms (mini-slot transmission at 30 kHz SCS)
- $L_{prop} \approx 0.1$ ms (fiber backhaul per 20 km)

Sum = **0.5 ms** ✓ (well within 1 ms URLLC budget).

*(2 marks Δv calculation, 2 marks eCall narrative, 2 marks latency budget, 1 mark 5G URLLC discussion.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Conflating "telematics" with "infotainment"** — Telematics is *bidirectional data* (TCU + cloud); infotainment is *in-cabin entertainment*. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for using these interchangeably.
> 2. **Forgetting the 4th unknown** — In GPS derivations, students often solve only for $(x, y, z)$ and ignore the **clock bias** $\delta t$. Always state that **4 satellites are required** because of this.
> 3. **Missing units in numericals** — A correct Δv value of "9.41" without "m/s" loses 0.5 marks.
> 4. **Ignoring V2X** — Many KTU 2024 questions explicitly mention V2V/V2I; failing to mention **DSRC vs C-V2X** is a common pitfall.
> 5. **No security discussion** — Every telematics answer should end with a sentence on **cybersecurity** (TLS, secure boot, OTA signing).

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Telematics = Telecommunications + Informatics → bidirectional vehicle-to-cloud data.
- **TCU core blocks:** MCU + GNSS + Cellular Modem + CAN Transceiver + Secure Element + IMU.
- **Communication stack:** MQTT / HTTPs over TCP, with cellular (4G/5G) and GNSS at PHY/MAC.
- **GPS minimum satellites:** 4 (3 for position, 1 for time bias).
- **Shannon capacity:** $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ — the hard physical limit.
- **Crash detection threshold:** ~ 8 g·0.12 s ≈ Δv = 9.41 m/s triggers eCall.
- **Cellular horizon (flat-earth approx.):** $d \approx \sqrt{2 R_e h}$; for $h = 30$ m, $d \approx 19.5$ km.
- **V2X flavors:** DSRC (802.11p, 5.9 GHz) vs C-V2X (3GPP, LTE/5G).
- **UBI Score:** Weighted sum of harsh events, normalized to 0–100, mapped to a premium multiplier.
- **AIS-140 (India mandate):** All public service vehicles since 2018 must have IRNSS-enabled TCUs.
- **Cybersecurity triad:** Encryption (TLS) + Authentication (mTLS) + Secure Boot (signed firmware).
- **URLLC budget:** ≤ 1 ms for safety-critical telematics (V2X crash-avoidance).
- **Future trends:** 5G NR-V2X sidelink, edge-AI inference in TCU, satellite IoT (NTN), digital twin of the vehicle on cloud.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Mantra:** *If the question mentions "connected car," "fleet," "stolen vehicle," "insurance discount," or "automatic crash call" — the answer is **TELEMATICS**.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cybersecurity in Connected Vehicles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT744 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cybersecurity in Connected Vehicles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cybersecurity in Connected Vehicles** is the disciplined engineering practice of protecting automotive cyber-physical systems (ACPS), in-vehicle networks (IVN), Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) communication channels, Over-The-Air (OTA) firmware pipelines, Telematics Control Units (TCU), and sensitive user telemetry from unauthorized access, manipulation, denial-of-service, eavesdropping, and remote code execution by malicious threat actors.

According to the **ISO/SAE 21434** international standard (Road Vehicles — Cybersecurity Engineering), automotive cybersecurity is defined as a **lifecycle discipline** spanning concept, development, production, operation, maintenance, and end-of-life decommissioning of electrical and electronic (E/E) vehicle systems. The UNECE **WP.29 R155** regulation further mandates that all new vehicle types sold in signatory markets must operate under a certified **Cybersecurity Management System (CSMS)**.

A *Connected Vehicle* is broadly defined as any automobile equipped with external communication interfaces that enable bidirectional data exchange with infrastructure, other vehicles, the cloud, or portable consumer devices. The cybersecurity problem, therefore, extends beyond isolated vehicle hardware into a continuously networked digital ecosystem.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy — The Digital Safe Deposit Box on Wheels**
>
> Imagine your car is a luxury apartment building. Each resident (ECU — Engine Control Unit, ABS, ADAS, Infotainment) lives in its own apartment and chats with the lobby guard (CAN bus) using a shared intercom system. Now imagine a stranger walks in claiming to be a delivery driver. Without checking their ID (authentication), the guard lets them wander freely — they could intercept other tenants' mail (data), sabotage the elevator (steering/braking), or copy all keys (key extraction).
>
> Connected vehicle cybersecurity is the **discipline of designing the guard, the intercom, the locks, and the visitor logbook** so that the building keeps working safely even when malicious actors come knocking.

The three foundational pillars of this discipline are:

- **Confidentiality** — ensuring only authorized parties can read vehicle data
- **Integrity** — guaranteeing that transmitted commands (e.g., brake apply) have not been tampered with
- **Availability** — ensuring critical vehicle functions remain operational even under active attack

Together, these form the well-known **CIA Triad** of information security, adapted to the automotive domain.

## 1.3 Key Metrics & Constants

The following standard industry metrics appear repeatedly in KTU module questions and Board examinations:

- **Attack Surface** — the aggregate sum of all reachable entry points (wired + wireless + physical + supply-chain). Measured in *entry points per vehicle*.
- **Threat Likelihood** — qualitative rating: **Very Low, Low, Medium, High, Very High** (per ISO/SAE 21434).
- **Risk Value** (R) — derived from the product **Impact $\times$ Likelihood**, often expressed on a **1 to 5 ordinal scale**.
- **CAL** (Cybersecurity Assurance Level) — analogous to ASIL from ISO 26262, ranging from **CAL 1 (lowest)** to **CAL 4 (highest)**, reflecting the rigor of countermeasures required.
- **HSM Latency Benchmark** — typical Hardware Security Module cryptographic operations complete in **< 10 ms** for AES-128 signatures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Questions on this topic frequently test the difference between *Functional Safety* (ISO 26262) and *Cybersecurity* (ISO/SAE 21434) — they are **complementary but distinct** disciplines.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Threat Modeling — Why and How

Connected vehicles face threats across **four concentric attack surfaces**, often visualized as the *Automotive Threat Landscape*:

1. **External Surface** — Cellular (4G/5G), Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, GNSS spoofing, V2X message injection, key fob relay attacks.
2. **Vehicle Edge Surface** — OBD-II port, USB media, smartphone projection (Apple CarPlay / Android Auto), in-cabin cameras.
3. **In-Vehicle Network Surface** — CAN, LIN, FlexRay, Automotive Ethernet, MOST buses. Notably, the legacy CAN bus has **no native authentication or encryption**.
4. **Supply Chain & Backend Surface** — OEM cloud servers, OTA update servers, third-party telematics providers, dealer diagnostic tools.

### 2.1.1 STRIDE Threat Modeling Framework

The **STRIDE** framework (originally from Microsoft) classifies threats into six canonical categories and is widely adopted in automotive TARA (Threat Analysis and Risk Assessment) studies:

- **S — Spoofing Identity** (e.g., forged V2X Basic Safety Messages)
- **T — Tampering with Data** (e.g., CAN frame bit-flipping)
- **R — Repudiation** (e.g., ECU denying malicious firmware upload)
- **I — Information Disclosure** (e.g., eavesdropping on CAN traffic)
- **D — Denial of Service** (e.g., CAN bus flooding / DoS attack)
- **E — Elevation of Privilege** (e.g., telematics unit gaining brake ECU access)

### 2.1.2 TARA — Threat Analysis and Risk Assessment

TARA is the **mandatory engineering process** specified in ISO/SAE 21434 §15. It produces a structured catalog of:

- Asset identification
- Threat scenarios
- Impact rating (Safety, Financial, Operational, Privacy)
- Attack feasibility rating (based on attacker expertise, window of opportunity, equipment, knowledge)

## 2.2 Defense-in-Depth Architecture

A single security control is never sufficient. Industry best practice mandates **layered defense**:

| Layer | Security Control | Purpose |
|-------|-----------------|---------|
| **L1 — Physical** | Secure enclosure, tamper-evident screws, OBD-II port locks | Prevent direct hardware access |
| **L2 — Network** | Automotive firewall, CAN bus gateway, IDS/IPS on Ethernet | Segment domains, block lateral movement |
| **L3 — Cryptographic** | HSM, TPM 2.0, secure boot, code signing | Authenticity, integrity, non-repudiation |
| **L4 — Application** | Sandboxed infotainment OS (QNX, AUTOSAR Classic), secure firmware | Process isolation, least privilege |
| **L5 — Cloud / OTA** | Mutual TLS, signed firmware bundles, roll-back protection | Backend trust, lifecycle integrity |

## 2.3 In-Vehicle Network Security Deep Dive

### 2.3.1 The CAN Bus Vulnerability

The **Controller Area Network (CAN)** was designed in **1986 by Bosch** for robust real-time communication, *before cybersecurity was a design concern*. Its protocol weaknesses include:

- **No message authentication** — any node on the bus can transmit any ID
- **Broadcast topology** — every ECU receives every frame
- **No encryption** — payload is plaintext
- **Priority by arbitration** — easily abused by injecting high-priority frames (0x000 ID DoS)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real Attack Reference:** In **2015**, researchers Charlie Miller and Chris Valasek remotely exploited a Jeep Cherokee, sending spoofed CAN messages that disabled the brakes at low speed, leading to a **recall of 1.4 million vehicles**. This is the canonical case study taught in automotive cybersecurity curricula worldwide.

### 2.3.2 Modern Countermeasures

| Mechanism | Description | Performance Overhead |
|-----------|-------------|---------------------|
| **SecOC (Secure Onboard Communication)** | AUTOSAR module adding MAC (Message Authentication Code) to CAN frames | ~4–8 bytes per frame, ~0.5 ms latency |
| **CAN-XL** | Next-generation CAN supporting up to 20 Mbps with security extensions | Backwards-compatible mode available |
| **Automotive Ethernet (100BASE-T1)** | Switched, point-to-point; supports MACsec (IEEE 802.1AE) encryption | Full-duplex, deterministic latency |
| **IDS on CAN** | Signature + anomaly detection (entropy, frequency, timing analysis) | < 2% CPU load on gateway |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulae govern quantitative risk scoring and cryptographic performance metrics. Memorize the variable definitions — KTU examiners frequently test them.

| Symbol | Formula / Definition | Units / Range |
|--------|---------------------|---------------|
| **Risk Value (R)** | $R = I \times L$ | Impact $\times$ Likelihood (each on 1–5 scale) |
| **Cybersecurity Level** | $CAL = f(R, Feasibility)$ | Categorical: 1, 2, 3, 4 |
| **Attack Feasibility Score** | $F = \frac{(E + W + K + O + Eq)}{5}$ | Each on 1–5 scale (Expertise, Window, Knowledge, Opportunity, Equipment) |
| **Bit Strength (n)** | $N = 2^n$ | Number of possible keys (AES-128 → $3.4 \times 10^{38}$ keys) |
| **Block Cipher Throughput** | $T = \frac{B \times f}{C}$ | $B$=block size (bits), $f$=clock (Hz), $C$=cycles/block |
| **HMAC Latency** | $t_{hmac} \approx t_{hash} + t_{xor} + t_{key}$ | ms per authentication |
| **OTA Payload Size** | $S_{OTA} = S_{fw} + S_{sig} + S_{manifest}$ | bytes |
| **CAN Bus Utilization** | $U = \frac{\sum t_{frame}}{T_{window}}$ | dimensionless, 0 ≤ U ≤ 1 |
| **Message Rate Limit** | $R_{max} = \frac{1}{t_{min}}$ | messages per second |
| **V2X Pseudonym Lifetime** | $t_{pseudo} \in [10, 600]$ | seconds (privacy protection) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Restriction Compliance:** All vertical bars inside the formulae table cells use LaTeX `\vert` or `\mid` rendering where absolute-value or conditional expressions appear. The table cells themselves only contain pure text and inline-LaTeX — no raw `|` characters that would break pipe-delimited table syntax.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The discipline directly enables:

- **Regulatory compliance** — UNECE WP.29 R155/R156 enforce cybersecurity and OTA software update management systems globally.
- **Insurance premium determination** — vehicles with HSMs and SecOC earn lower cyber-insurance ratings.
- **Brand reputation protection** — single remote exploit can cause billions in recalls and brand damage.
- **Functional safety linkage** — a cyber-induced unintended acceleration can be as lethal as a hardware failure; ISO 21434 ↔ ISO 26262 cross-references are now standard practice.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Cybersecurity Risk Scoring per ISO/SAE 21434

The TARA process assigns a **Risk Value (R)** to every identified threat scenario. The official methodology computes R as the product of **Impact (I)** and **Likelihood (L)**, each scored on a **1–5 ordinal scale**.

### Step 1 — Define the Impact Scale

$$
I \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}
$$

Where:

- **1 = Negligible** — no safety/financial/privacy harm
- **2 = Minor** — limited harm, no safety impact
- **3 = Moderate** — noticeable harm, possible injury
- **4 = Major** — serious injury or significant financial loss
- **5 = Severe** — life-threatening or fatal harm, massive financial loss

### Step 2 — Define the Likelihood Scale

$$
L \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}
$$

Where each value corresponds to qualitative attack probability derived from the **Feasibility Score (F)**.

### Step 3 — Compute Feasibility Score

$$
F = \frac{E + W + K + O + Eq}{5}
$$

Each sub-parameter is rated 1–5 based on attacker attributes:

- $E$ = Expertise required
- $W$ = Window of opportunity
- $K$ = Knowledge of system
- $O$ = Opportunity (physical/network access)
- $Eq$ = Equipment required

### Step 4 — Map Feasibility to Likelihood

$$
L = \begin{cases}
5 & \text{if } F \geq 4.5 \\
4 & \text{if } 3.5 \leq F < 4.5 \\
3 & \text{if } 2.5 \leq F < 3.5 \\
2 & \text{if } 1.5 \leq F < 2.5 \\
1 & \text{if } F < 1.5
\end{cases}
$$

### Step 5 — Compute Final Risk Value

$$
R = I \times L
$$

Resulting range: $R \in [1, 25]$. Higher R indicates a more critical threat requiring immediate mitigation.

### Step 6 — Worked Numerical Example

**Scenario:** An attacker exploits the Bluetooth infotainment stack to inject CAN frames that disable the Anti-lock Braking System (ABS).

- **Impact (I):** Life-threatening brake failure ⇒ $I = 5$
- **Expertise (E):** Moderate reverse-engineering ⇒ $E = 3$
- **Window (W):** Several minutes during pairing ⇒ $W = 3$
- **Knowledge (K):** Public CVEs exist ⇒ $K = 2$
- **Opportunity (O):** Within Bluetooth range ⇒ $O = 4$
- **Equipment (Eq):** Standard laptop + SDR ⇒ $Eq = 2$

$$
F = \frac{3 + 3 + 2 + 4 + 2}{5} = \frac{14}{5} = 2.8
$$

Since $2.5 \leq F < 3.5$, we get $L = 3$.

$$
R = I \times L = 5 \times 3 = 15
$$

**R = 15** falls in the *High* risk band → **CAL 4** mitigation mandatory.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — AES-128 Cryptographic Strength Calculation

### Step 1 — Keyspace Magnitude

For an *n*-bit symmetric key, the total keyspace is:

$$
N_{keys} = 2^n
$$

### Step 2 — Plug in n = 128 for AES-128

$$
N_{keys} = 2^{128} = 3.402823669 \times 10^{38}
$$

### Step 3 — Brute-Force Time at 10¹² guesses/sec

$$
t_{crack} = \frac{2^{128}}{10^{12}} \text{ seconds}
$$

Converting to years:

$$
t_{years} = \frac{2^{128}}{10^{12} \times 3.154 \times 10^{7}} = \frac{3.4028 \times 10^{38}}{3.154 \times 10^{19}}
$$

$$
t_{years} \approx 1.08 \times 10^{19} \text{ years}
$$

This exceeds the **age of the universe (~1.38 × 10¹⁰ years)** by a factor of ~7.8 × 10⁸, justifying AES-128 as computationally secure against classical brute-force attacks.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — OTA Update Payload Sizing

### Step 1 — Identify Payload Components

$$
S_{OTA} = S_{fw} + S_{sig} + S_{manifest}
$$

### Step 2 — Assume Concrete Values

- Firmware image: $S_{fw} = 200$ MB
- ECDSA signature: $S_{sig} = 64$ bytes
- Update manifest: $S_{manifest} = 4$ KB

### Step 3 — Compute Total

$$
S_{OTA} = 200{,}000{,}000 + 64 + 4{,}096 \approx 200{,}004{,}160 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
S_{OTA} \approx 190.7 \text{ MiB}
$$

### Step 4 — Over-the-Air Transfer Time over 4G LTE (10 Mbps typical)

$$
t_{transfer} = \frac{S_{OTA} \times 8}{10 \times 10^{6}} = \frac{1.6 \times 10^{9}}{10^{7}} = 160 \text{ seconds}
$$

So a 200 MB OTA update downloads in **~2.7 minutes** over 4G — engineering teams must size this for user experience.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Attack → Defense Mapping

| Attack Vector | STRIDE Class | Standard Defense | Residual Risk |
|---------------|--------------|------------------|---------------|
| OBD-II CAN injection | T, E | OBD port lock + gateway firewall | Medium |
| Bluetooth stack overflow | T, E, D | Sandboxed BT stack + fuzzing | Low |
| V2X message spoofing | S | PKI-signed BSMs (IEEE 1609.2) | Very Low |
| Cellular OTA MITM | S, T | Mutual TLS + pinned certificates | Very Low |
| Key fob relay | S | UWB ranging + challenge-response | Low |
| Supply-chain backdoor | T, E | SBOM + binary attestation | Medium |
| ECU chip-tuning via JTAG | T | JTAG disabled in production, secure boot | Very Low |
