     (c) **Folded-path projection** — Bends the optical path to fit the HUD housing (usually inside the dashboard top, above the instrument cluster).

4. **Image Presentation (Combiner / Windshield):**
   - Light exits the HUD housing and strikes the projection surface.
   - In **C-HUDs**, the surface is a small piece of optical-grade plastic treated with a partially reflective coating.
   - In **W-HUDs** and **AR-HUDs**, the surface is the **inner face of the windshield**, which is curved (radius typically 2.5 m–4 m). A **PVB (Polyvinyl Butyral) wedge-shaped interlayer** is embedded between the two glass plies to compensate for the **double-image (ghost image)** caused by the windshield's dual reflective surfaces.

5. **Perception by the Driver (Output Layer):**
   - The driver perceives a **virtual image** floating at a distance (VID) in front of the vehicle.
   - The image appears to overlay the real road scene, which is the foundational principle for AR-HUD navigation arrows and ACC (Adaptive Cruise Control) cues.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind the System Design

- **Why collimate the light?** The human eye's natural relaxed focal point is at optical infinity (≥ 6 m). A collimated image means the driver does not have to re-accommodate (refocus) the eye lens, eliminating focal fatigue and reducing glance-down time to ~0.2 s versus ~1.0 s for a cluster glance.
- **Why a wedge-shaped PVB interlayer?** The windshield has two parallel glass surfaces, each acting as a partial mirror. Without correction, the driver sees two slightly offset images (a "ghost"). The wedge angle (typically 0.25°–0.4°) refracts the two reflected rays so they merge into a single sharp virtual image.
- **Why is the eye-box important?** The eye-box defines the 3D volume in space (a rectangular cuboid) within which the driver's eyes must be located to see the full, undistorted image. A larger eye-box is ergonomically superior but requires a larger PGU and more complex optics, driving up cost.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Symbol | Expression | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Virtual Image Distance (paraxial optics) | $d_i$ | $\dfrac{1}{d_i} = \dfrac{1}{f} - \dfrac{1}{d_o}$ | Determines apparent image depth |
| 2 | Magnification of optical system | $M$ | $M = -\dfrac{d_i}{d_o}$ | Sizes the virtual image |
| 3 | Apparent image size (diagonal) | $S_{img}$ | $S_{img} = S_{PGU} \cdot \vert M \vert$ | Pixels-to-driver-eye mapping |
| 4 | Field of View (angular) | $\theta_{FOV}$ | $\theta_{FOV} = 2 \cdot \arctan\left(\dfrac{S_{img}/2}{VID}\right)$ | Defines HMI coverage |
| 5 | Eye-box width constraint | $E_w$ | $E_w \approx \dfrac{S_{PGU} \cdot VID}{f_{obj}} - \Delta_{pupil}$ | Ergonomic head-movement tolerance |
| 6 | Luminance requirement (sun-readable) | $L_{min}$ | $L_{min} \geq 3 \cdot L_{ambient}$ | Daylight contrast guarantee |
| 7 | System latency budget | $t_{lat}$ | $t_{lat} = t_{sensor} + t_{compute} + t_{PGU} < 50$ ms | Motion-sickness prevention |
| 8 | Reflectivity of windshield coating | $R_w$ | $R_w \approx 0.20$ to $0.40$ | Trade-off: visibility vs. heat load |
| 9 | Wedge interlayer compensation angle | $\alpha_w$ | $\alpha_w = \dfrac{t \cdot (n-1)}{R_{ws}}$ | Ghost-image elimination |
| 10 | AR-HUD pixel density requirement | $\rho_{px}$ | $\rho_{px} \geq 60$ PPD (pixels/degree) | Visual acuity (20/20 vision) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on notation:** $f$ = focal length of objective mirror/lens, $d_o$ = object distance (PGU to optic), $d_i$ = virtual image distance, $t$ = windshield thickness, $n$ = refractive index of glass (~1.52), $R_{ws}$ = windshield radius of curvature.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

HUDs are no longer luxury add-ons; they are **active-safety enablers**. Euro NCAP has begun evaluating driver-monitoring and attention-maintenance systems; HUDs directly contribute to the **Safety Assist** score. In the EV era, with no engine noise to mask driving inputs, the HUD serves as a **silent information broker** between autonomous-driving features (Level 2+) and the human driver, fulfilling ISO/TR 21959-1 and ISO 15005 (in-vehicle HMI recommendations) compliance.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Virtual Image Distance via Thin-Lens (Mirror) Equation

The optical core of a Head-Up Display is functionally a **virtual-image projector**. The driver must perceive the image as if it originates at a large distance (typically 2–15 m), even though the PGU sits only 200–500 mm away from the optical element. The standard thin-lens/mirror conjugate equation governs this relationship.

**Step 1 — State the canonical equation for a converging optical element (lens or equivalent mirror):**

$$\dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{1}{d_o} + \dfrac{1}{d_i}$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to solve for the virtual-image distance $d_i$:**

$$\dfrac{1}{d_i} = \dfrac{1}{f} - \dfrac{1}{d_o}$$

**Step 3 — Identify the sign convention:**
Since the PGU is a *real object* placed at a distance greater than the focal length ($d_o > f$), the resulting image is **virtual**, erect, and magnified. By the Cartesian sign convention, $d_i$ is taken as **negative** (on the same side as the object), confirming the virtual-image nature.

**Step 4 — Compute the lateral magnification $M$:**

$$M = -\dfrac{d_i}{d_o}$$

**Step 5 — Substituting the equation from Step 2, the magnification can be expressed purely in terms of $f$ and $d_o$:**

$$M = -d_o \left( \dfrac{1}{f} - \dfrac{1}{d_o} \right) = 1 - \dfrac{d_o}{f}$$

**Step 6 — Realistic engineering example:**
Suppose a W-HUD uses a freeform aspheric mirror with $f = 250$ mm and the PGU is placed at $d_o = 300$ mm.

$$\dfrac{1}{d_i} = \dfrac{1}{250} - \dfrac{1}{300} = \dfrac{300 - 250}{75000} = \dfrac{50}{75000}$$

$$d_i = 1500 \text{ mm} = 1.5 \text{ m}$$

**Step 7 — The lateral magnification is therefore:**

$$M = -\dfrac{1500}{300} = -5$$

The negative sign confirms the image is **virtual and erect**; the magnitude $|M| = 5$ means a 1.5-inch PGU produces a perceived image of $1.5 \times 5 = 7.5$ inches diagonal — a typical W-HUD specification.

**Step 8 — Compute the FOV for this configuration:**

$$\theta_{FOV} = 2 \cdot \arctan\left(\dfrac{S_{img}/2}{VID}\right) = 2 \cdot \arctan\left(\dfrac{0.095 \text{ m}/2}{1.5 \text{ m}}\right) \approx 3.6°$$

This narrow FOV is characteristic of conventional W-HUDs.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Eye-Box Width (Pupil-Forming Geometry)

The eye-box is the spatial region in which the driver's pupil can move while still seeing the entire image. It is geometrically linked to the PGU size, the optical magnification, and the pupil-relief distance.

**Step 1 — Define the relevant variables:**
- $S_{PGU}$ = diagonal of the picture generation unit (e.g., 50 mm for a 2-inch TFT).
- $VID$ = virtual image distance (e.g., 2.5 m).
- $E_w$ = eye-box width (lateral head-movement tolerance).
- $E_{rel}$ = eye relief (distance from last optical surface to driver's eye), typically 350–500 mm.

**Step 2 — Use similar triangles between the PGU plane, the eye lens, and the virtual image plane:**

The angular subtense of the image as seen from the PGU is the same as that subtended by the eye-box from the PGU's perspective. Hence:

$$\dfrac{E_w}{E_{rel}} = \dfrac{S_{PGU}}{d_o}$$

**Step 3 — Solving for the eye-box width:**

$$E_w = \dfrac{S_{PGU} \cdot E_{rel}}{d_o}$$

**Step 4 — Engineering example:** With $S_{PGU} = 50$ mm, $E_{rel} = 400$ mm, $d_o = 300$ mm:

$$E_w = \dfrac{50 \times 400}{300} = 66.7 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Engineering interpretation:** A 66.7 mm wide eye-box permits a moderate degree of head movement. To extend this to ~130 mm (luxury-class standard), designers must either enlarge the PGU, increase the eye relief, or use a **multi-image / dual-PGU / pupil-replicating optical design** — a hallmark of AR-HUD systems (e.g., the Mercedes-Benz MBUX AR-HUD uses two PGUs to enlarge the effective eye-box).

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Wedge Interlayer Compensation for Ghost-Image

A windshield has two reflective surfaces (inner and outer glass faces), producing a *double image*. The optical thickness and the wedge angle of the PVB interlayer are chosen to merge the two reflections.

**Step 1 — Quantify the ghost-image separation without compensation:**

The two reflections are separated laterally by an offset $y_g$ given by:

$$y_g = 2 \cdot t \cdot \tan(\theta_i) \cdot (n-1)/n$$

where $t$ = glass thickness, $n$ ≈ 1.52 (refractive index of soda-lime glass), $\theta_i$ = angle of incidence from the HUD's projection axis.

**Step 2 — Introduce the wedge angle $\alpha_w$ in the PVB interlayer.** The wedge refracts the second-surface reflection by an additional angular deviation of:

$$\Delta\theta = (n_{PVB} - 1) \cdot \alpha_w$$

**Step 3 — The compensation condition (ghost-image merger) requires:**

$$(n_{PVB} - 1) \cdot \alpha_w = \dfrac{2 \cdot t \cdot (n-1)}{R_{ws}}$$

where $R_{ws}$ is the windshield radius of curvature.

**Step 4 — Solve for the required wedge angle:**

$$\alpha_w = \dfrac{2 \cdot t \cdot (n-1)}{R_{ws} \cdot (n_{PVB} - 1)}$$

**Step 5 — Engineering example:** For $t = 4$ mm, $n = 1.52$, $n_{PVB} = 1.49$, $R_{ws} = 3000$ mm:

$$\alpha_w = \dfrac{2 \times 4 \times 0.52}{3000 \times 0.49} = \dfrac{4.16}{1470} \approx 0.00283 \text{ rad} \approx 0.16°$$

This small but critical angle (~0.16°) is engineered into every modern W-HUD/AR-HUD-compatible windshield.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — HUD Technologies

| Evaluation Parameter | Combiner HUD (C-HUD) | Windshield HUD (W-HUD) | Augmented Reality HUD (AR-HUD) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Projection Surface | Plastic Combiner (foldable) | Windshield (special PVB) | Windshield (high-precision) |
| Virtual Image Distance | 2.0 – 2.5 m | 2.5 – 4.0 m | 7.0 – 15.0 m+ |
| Field of View (FOV) | 4° × 1° | 8° × 3° | 10° × 4° (up to 15° × 5°) |
| PGU Technology | TFT-LCD | TFT-LCD / DLP | DLP / Laser-MEMS |
| Luminance Required | ~5,000 cd/m² | ~10,000 cd/m² | ~15,000 cd/m² |
| Package Volume | < 2 L | 4 – 8 L | 15 – 25 L |
| Cost (OEM, 2024) | $50 – $150 | $200 – $500 | $800 – $2,500 |
| ADAS Integration | Basic warnings | Speed, Nav, ACC | Nav overlay, lane, hazards |
| Representative Vehicle | Mazda CX-5 (older) | BMW 5 Series, Audi A6 | Mercedes EQS, Audi Q6 e-tron |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture — Automotive Head-Up Display (W-HUD)

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the **block-level functional architecture** mapping the data, optical, and human-perception layers of a W-HUD system. The flow is top-down: data acquisition → processing → image generation → optical projection → human perception.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1.** Define a Head-Up Display (HUD). List any four advantages of an automotive HUD over a conventional instrument cluster. **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**
A **Head-Up Display (HUD)** is a transparent display system that projects essential vehicle and driving information (speed, navigation, ADAS alerts) into the driver's forward line of sight, allowing the data to be perceived as a virtual image focused at optical infinity, without requiring the driver to look away from the road.

**Four Advantages:**
1. **Reduced Eyes-Off-Road Time** — Glance time drops from ~1.0 s (cluster) to ~0.2 s (HUD), increasing situational awareness.
2. **No Focal Accommodation** — Virtual image at optical infinity eliminates the need for the eye to refocus.
3. **Enhanced ADAS Communication** — Lane-departure, ACC, and blind-spot warnings are spatially co-located with the road.
4. **Night-time Readability & Reduced Driver Fatigue** — Critical for long-distance and night driving scenarios.

---

### **Question 2.** What is a Picture Generation Unit (PGU)? Name any three PGU technologies used in modern automotive HUDs. **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**
The **Picture Generation Unit (PGU)** is the optical source that produces the image to be projected by the HUD. It is functionally the "display" of the system, though its output is magnified and collimated before reaching the driver.

**Three PGU Technologies:**
1. **TFT-LCD (Thin-Film Transistor Liquid Crystal Display)** — Backlit by high-power LEDs; the most common in C-HUD and entry-level W-HUDs.
2. **DLP (Digital Light Processing)** — Uses a Texas Instruments DMD (Digital Micromirror Device) chip; superior contrast and brightness for AR-HUDs.
3. **Laser-Scanning PGU (MEMS-based)** — Uses laser diodes raster-scanned via a micro-mirror; excellent color saturation and compact form factor.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern Note:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, divided as (a) = 7 marks and (b) = 7 marks. Students answer EITHER Question A OR Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of a Windshield-type Head-Up Display (W-HUD). Describe the role of the wedge-shaped PVB interlayer in the windshield.

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram (already shown in SECTION 4.1 above).** The functional architecture consists of:
- **Data Inputs:** CAN/LIN bus, navigation, ADAS, infotainment.
- **HUD ECU:** Real-time OS, graphic composer.
- **PGU (TFT-LCD / DLP / Laser):** Generates the raw image.
- **Optical Assembly:** Aspheric collimating lens + two freeform fold mirrors.
- **Windshield (with wedge PVB):** The final reflective surface.
- **Driver's Eye:** Perceives the virtual image at infinity.

**Role of Wedge-Shaped PVB Interlayer:**
The windshield has two parallel glass surfaces. Without compensation, each surface reflects the projected beam, producing a **ghost image** offset by a few millimeters. To merge these two reflections into a single sharp image, the PVB interlayer is molded into a precise **wedge angle** (typically 0.1° to 0.4°). This wedge refracts the second-surface reflection by an angle:

$$\alpha_w = \dfrac{2 \cdot t \cdot (n-1)}{R_{ws} \cdot (n_{PVB} - 1)}$$

so that the two reflections converge. The wedge also compensates for the windshield's curvature ($R_{ws}$). Without this engineering, the driver would perceive a doubled, blurred virtual image.

#### **(b) [7 Marks]** Compare the three generations of automotive HUDs: Combiner HUD (C-HUD), Windshield HUD (W-HUD), and Augmented Reality HUD (AR-HUD). Discuss at least six comparative parameters.

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | C-HUD | W-HUD | AR-HUD |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Projection Surface** | Plastic pop-up combiner | Windshield (wedge PVB) | High-precision windshield |
| **Virtual Image Distance (VID)** | 2.0 – 2.5 m | 2.5 – 4.0 m | 7.0 – 15.0 m |
| **Field of View (FOV)** | 4° × 1° | 8° × 3° | 10° × 4° to 15° × 5° |
| **PGU Technology** | TFT-LCD | TFT-LCD / DLP | DLP / Laser-MEMS |
| **Cost (OEM, 2024)** | $50 – $150 | $200 – $500 | $800 – $2,500 |
| **ADAS Integration Level** | Basic warnings | Speed, Nav, ACC | Lane, Nav overlay, hazards |
| **Package Volume** | < 2 L | 4 – 8 L | 15 – 25 L |
| **Vehicle Segment** | Economy | Mid / Premium | Luxury / Flagship EVs |

**Engineering Conclusion:** Each generation represents a step-up in optical complexity, AR-HUDs requiring freeform mirrors, dual PGUs, and AR rendering engines to spatially anchor navigation arrows and warnings to real road objects.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

#### **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the virtual image distance for a converging optical element used in a W-HUD. Given $f = 200$ mm and $d_o = 240$ mm, compute the virtual image distance and the lateral magnification.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — State the thin-lens conjugate equation:**

$$\dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{1}{d_o} + \dfrac{1}{d_i}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $d_i$:**

$$\dfrac{1}{d_i} = \dfrac{1}{f} - \dfrac{1}{d_o} = \dfrac{1}{200} - \dfrac{1}{240}$$

**Step 3 — Compute numerically:**

$$\dfrac{1}{d_i} = \dfrac{240 - 200}{200 \times 240} = \dfrac{40}{48000} = \dfrac{1}{1200}$$

**Step 4 — Hence:**

$$d_i = 1200 \text{ mm} = 1.2 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the lateral magnification:**

$$M = -\dfrac{d_i}{d_o} = -\dfrac{1200}{240} = -5$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation:** The driver perceives a virtual image 1.2 m ahead, magnified 5× (so a 1.5-inch PGU yields a ~7.5-inch virtual image). The negative sign confirms the image is virtual and erect.

#### **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of the **eye-box** in a Head-Up Display. Why is a large eye-box critical for AR-HUDs, and what optical design strategies are employed to enlarge it?

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** The **eye-box** is the three-dimensional spatial region (typically a rectangular cuboid) in front of the windshield within which the driver's pupil must be located to view the **entire** virtual image without vignetting, distortion, or cut-off.

**Criticality in AR-HUDs:**
- AR-HUDs render navigation arrows and ACC chevrons spatially aligned to real road objects at 7–15 m distance. Even a small lateral head shift (~50 mm) can cause the entire image to disappear or shift, breaking the AR illusion and inducing motion sickness.
- A large eye-box is **mandatory** to accommodate:
  - Driver height variation (5th-percentile female to 95th-percentile male).
  - Seat position adjustments.
  - Micro head-movements during driving.

**Optical Strategies to Enlarge the Eye-Box:**
1. **Larger PGU (e.g., 4-inch instead of 1.5-inch)** — Direct geometric scaling.
2. **Increased Eye Relief** — Greater distance from the last optical element to the driver's eye.
3. **Pupil-Replication Optics** — A diffractive optical element (DOE) or micro-lens array generates multiple sub-images that effectively widen the eye-box.
4. **Dual-PGU Configuration** — Two independent PGUs and optical paths overlay to form a single, wide eye-box (used in Mercedes MBUX AR-HUD).
5. **Freeform Mirror Design Optimization** — Advanced freeform surfaces (e.g., Zernike polynomial surfaces) correct off-axis aberrations across a wider pupil range.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall:**
> - **Do not** confuse *Field of View (FOV)* with *Eye-Box*. FOV is the angular size of the *image*; Eye-Box is the spatial region of the *pupil* that can see the image. Examiners frequently test this distinction.
> - **Do not** state the magnification formula as $M = d_i / d_o$ *without the negative sign*. The sign indicates a *virtual* image — a common 1-mark deduction.
> - **Do not** omit the unit of Virtual Image Distance in your answer; always state it in meters (m), not mm, when describing driver perception.
> - **Do not** confuse DLP (a chip-based reflective technology) with Laser-MEMS (a scanning technology). Both are listed for AR-HUDs but are architecturally distinct.
> - **Do not** state the wedge-PVB angle as a single fixed value; it is *vehicle-specific* and tuned to the windshield's curvature. Show the *formula*, not a memorized constant.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 — Head-Up Displays**

- **HUD = Head-Up Display**: A transparent display projecting data into the driver's line of sight as a virtual image at optical infinity.
- **Origins**: Aviator technology (1940s military) → General Motors 1988 (Oldsmobile Cutlass Supreme) → modern automotive adoption post-2010.
- **Three Generations**: C-HUD (2 m, plastic combiner) → W-HUD (2.5–4 m, windshield) → AR-HUD (7–15 m+, contact-analog overlays on road).
- **Core Subsystems**: Data Input (CAN, GPS, ADAS) → HUD ECU → PGU → Optical Collimator → Fold Mirrors → Windshield → Driver.
- **Three PGU Technologies**: TFT-LCD (cost), DLP (contrast), Laser-MEMS (color, compactness).
- **Virtual Image Distance Equation**: $\dfrac{1}{d_i} = \dfrac{1}{f} - \dfrac{1}{d_o}$. Always produce *negative* $d_i$ for a virtual image (Cartesian sign convention).
- **Magnification**: $M = -d_i / d_o$. Negative sign ⇒ virtual, erect image.
- **Eye-Box**: 3D region in front of the windshield where the pupil sees the full image. Critical ergonomic parameter (typically 130 mm wide in W-HUDs).
- **FOV**: Angular size of the virtual image. 4° × 1° (C) → 10° × 4°+ (AR).
- **Luminance Standard**: ≥ 10,000 cd/m² for sun-readable HUDs (rule of thumb: $L_{HUD} \geq 3 \times L_{ambient}$).
- **Wedge PVB Interlayer**: Tiny angle (~0.16°) embedded in windshield to eliminate *ghost image* from dual glass surfaces.
- **Latency Budget**: Sensor + compute + PGU < **50 ms** (motion-sickness limit).
- **Key Standards**: ISO 15005 (in-vehicle HMI), SAE J3061 (cybersecurity for ADAS), Euro NCAP Safety Assist.
- **ADAS Linkage**: HUDs display speed, navigation, ACC set-speed, lane-departure warnings, night-vision overlays, and (AR-HUD) spatially anchored hazard markers.
- **Engineering Trade-offs**: Bigger eye-box ⇄ larger PGU ⇄ higher cost ⇄ larger package volume.
- **Representative Vehicles (2024)**: C-HUD → economy models; W-HUD → BMW 5 Series, Audi A6; AR-HUD → Mercedes EQS, Audi Q6 e-tron, Cadillac LYRIQ.
- **Exam Tip**: Always pair any HUD optical statement with its *equation* — KTU examiners reward numerical/literal examples.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Navigation Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT744 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Navigation Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Navigation System (Automotive Context):** An integrated electro-mechatronic subsystem that continuously determines the real-time geographic position, velocity, and heading of a vehicle, correlates this data with digital road maps, and provides route guidance, trajectory prediction, and location-aware services to the driver and other on-board Electronic Control Units (ECUs).

In modern automotive engineering, navigation is no longer a stand-alone feature. It is a **cyber-physical subsystem** that fuses data from **GNSS satellites**, **Inertial Measurement Units (IMU)**, **wheel-speed sensors**, and **steering-angle sensors** to deliver sub-meter accuracy for Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS), Autonomous Driving (Level 2+), and Telematics applications.

### 1.2 Key Subsystems of an Automotive Navigation Stack

| Sub-system | Full Form | Primary Function |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| GNSS | Global Navigation Satellite System | Absolute positioning via satellite ranging |
| INS | Inertial Navigation System | Relative positioning via accelerometers & gyroscopes |
| DR | Dead Reckoning | Position update from speed and heading |
| HD Map | Highly Detailed Digital Map | Road geometry, lane info, speed limits |
| ECU Fusion Unit | Electronic Control Unit (Sensor Fusion) | Combines multi-source data using Kalman Filter |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Blind Bat and the Lighthouse":**
> Imagine a bat (the car) flying in complete darkness. It has two tools:
> 1. **Lighthouses (GNSS Satellites):** Flashes of light arrive at slightly different times. By knowing the *speed of light* and *time delay*, the bat triangulates exactly where it is on Earth. Works perfectly in open sky, but fails inside tunnels or dense urban "urban canyons."
> 2. **Inner Ear & Memory (IMU + Dead Reckoning):** Even with eyes closed, the bat feels its own acceleration, tilt, and turn rate. By integrating motion over time, it estimates displacement. Accurate for short bursts, but errors accumulate (drift).
>
> A real navigation system **fuses both** — just like a bat uses echoes plus wind on its wings — to always know where the vehicle is, even when satellite signals drop out.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following **physical constants** are mandatory for KTU numerical answers:

- **Speed of light in vacuum:** $c = 3 \times 10^{8} \; \text{m/s}$
- **Wavelength of GPS L1 signal:** $\lambda_{L1} \approx 19.05 \; \text{cm}$ (frequency $1575.42 \; \text{MHz}$)
- **Wavelength of GPS L2 signal:** $\lambda_{L2} \approx 24.45 \; \text{cm}$ (frequency $1227.60 \; \text{MHz}$)
- **Number of operational GPS satellites:** Minimum **24** (currently 31+ in MEO orbit)
- **Orbital altitude of GPS satellites:** $\approx 20{,}200 \; \text{km}$
- **Orbital period of GPS satellites:** $\approx 11 \; \text{hours} \; 58 \; \text{minutes}$ (half a sidereal day)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Segments of GNSS (GPS Architecture)

A KTU-favourite conceptual question — memorize the three segments.

1. **Space Segment:** Constellation of **24+ satellites** in Medium Earth Orbit (MEO), arranged in 6 orbital planes, each inclined at 55° to the equator.
2. **Control Segment:** A global network of **monitor stations** (Hawaii, Colorado Springs, Ascension Island, Diego Garcia, Kwajalein) that track satellites, compute ephemeris corrections, and upload navigation messages via S-band uplinks.
3. **User Segment:** The GNSS receiver inside the vehicle — extracts pseudo-ranges and computes a PVT (Position, Velocity, Time) solution.

### 2.2 The Four Pseudo-Range Equations

The receiver measures **pseudo-range** $\rho_i$ from each visible satellite $i$. Because the receiver clock has a bias $\delta t_u$, the measured range is biased:

$$
\rho_i = R_i + c \cdot \delta t_u + \varepsilon_i
$$

where:
- $R_i = \sqrt{(x_i - x_u)^2 + (y_i - y_u)^2 + (z_i - z_u)^2}$ is the **true geometric range**.
- $(x_i, y_i, z_i)$ = known satellite coordinates from the ephemeris.
- $(x_u, y_u, z_u)$ = unknown user (vehicle) coordinates.
- $\delta t_u$ = receiver clock bias (in seconds).
- $\varepsilon_i$ = measurement noise (ionospheric, tropospheric, multipath).

> **The 'Why':** Four satellites are needed to solve **four unknowns** — three position coordinates plus the clock bias. This is the fundamental reason GNSS requires a minimum of 4 satellites for a 3-D fix.

### 2.3 Dilution of Precision (DOP)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DOP** is the geometric amplifier of GNSS error. Good satellite geometry $\Rightarrow$ low DOP $\Rightarrow$ high accuracy. Poor geometry (satellites clustered together) $\Rightarrow$ high DOP $\Rightarrow$ degraded accuracy.

The relationship between user-range error and position error:

$$
\sigma_{\text{position}} = \sigma_{\text{UERE}} \times \text{DOP}
$$

Types of DOP (must memorize for KTU):

| DOP Metric | Stands For | Geometric Meaning | Typical Good Value |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **GDOP** | Geometric DOP | 3-D position + time | $< 6$ |
| **PDOP** | Position DOP | 3-D position only | $< 6$ |
| **HDOP** | Horizontal DOP | Latitude + Longitude | $< 4$ |
| **VDOP** | Vertical DOP | Altitude only | $< 6$ |
| **TDOP** | Time DOP | Clock bias only | $< 3$ |

### 2.4 Dead Reckoning Kinematics

Used when GNSS is unavailable (tunnels, urban canyons). Position update from vehicle sensors:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{k+1} &= x_k + v_k \cdot \cos(\theta_k) \cdot \Delta t \\
y_{k+1} &= y_k + v_k \cdot \sin(\theta_k) \cdot \Delta t \\
\theta_{k+1} &= \theta_k + \dot{\theta}_k \cdot \Delta t
\end{aligned}
$$

where $v_k$ is the wheel-derived speed, $\theta_k$ is the heading (from gyro or steering angle), and $\Delta t$ is the update interval.

### 2.5 Inertial Navigation — Strapdown Mechanization Equations

Forces from accelerometers ($f_x, f_y, f_z$) are integrated twice to obtain position. The strapdown INS equations in the navigation frame:

$$
\dot{v}_n = f_n - (2\Omega_{ie} + \omega_{en}) \times v_n + g_n
$$

$$
\dot{p} = v_n
$$

where $\Omega_{ie}$ is the Earth's rotation rate vector ($7.2921 \times 10^{-5}$ rad/s), $\omega_{en}$ is the transport rate, and $g_n$ is the local gravity vector.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU Part B question on Navigation.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Unit |
| :- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $d = c \cdot \Delta t$ | Pseudo-range from signal travel time | metres |
| 2 | $\rho_i = R_i + c \cdot \delta t_u$ | Pseudo-range equation | metres |
| 3 | $\sigma_p = \sigma_{\text{UERE}} \times \text{DOP}$ | Position accuracy from geometry | metres |
| 4 | $x_{k+1} = x_k + v_k \cos\theta_k \Delta t$ | Dead reckoning X-update | metres |
| 5 | $y_{k+1} = y_k + v_k \sin\theta_k \Delta t$ | Dead reckoning Y-update | metres |
| 6 | $\dot{v}_n = f_n - (2\Omega_{ie} + \omega_{en}) \times v_n + g_n$ | Strapdown INS velocity eqn. | m/s² |
| 7 | $f_c = f_0 \sqrt{\frac{1 \pm v/c}{1 \mp v/c}}$ | Doppler / relativistic frequency shift | Hz |

> **Real-World Utility:** The same pseudo-range equations power a ₹500 smartphone map app and a ₹50 lakh autonomous-vehicle localization stack — only the sensor quality and DOP filtering differ. KTU examiners love linking these equations to **ADAS lane-keep assist** and **eCall emergency call systems**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Pseudo-Range from Signal Time-of-Flight

**Given:** A GPS satellite transmits a navigation message at time $t_s$ (satellite clock). The vehicle receiver timestamps reception at time $t_r$ (receiver clock).

**Find:** The geometric range between the two.

**Step 1 —** The signal propagates through vacuum at the speed of light $c$.

**Step 2 —** The geometric range (true distance) equals the speed multiplied by true propagation time:

$$
R = c \times (t_r - t_s)
$$

**Step 3 —** Because satellite and receiver clocks both have biases $\delta t_s$ and $\delta t_u$:

$$
t_r^{\text{measured}} = t_r + \delta t_u, \qquad t_s^{\text{measured}} = t_s + \delta t_s
$$

**Step 4 —** Substituting into Step 2 gives the **measured (pseudo) range**:

$$
\rho = c \times \left[ (t_r^{\text{measured}} - \delta t_u) - (t_s^{\text{measured}} - \delta t_s) \right]
$$

$$
\rho = c(t_r^{\text{measured}} - t_s^{\text{measured}}) + c \cdot \delta t_s - c \cdot \delta t_u
$$

**Step 5 —** Define the *measured* range term $R_m = c(t_r^{\text{measured}} - t_s^{\text{measured}})$:

$$
\rho = R_m - c \cdot \delta t_u + c \cdot \delta t_s
$$

**Step 6 —** The satellite clock bias $\delta t_s$ is corrected using data in the navigation message, so it is eliminated. The final pseudo-range equation used by the receiver:

$$
\rho = R + c \cdot \delta t_u = \sqrt{(x_s - x_u)^2 + (y_s - y_u)^2 + (z_s - z_u)^2} + c \cdot \delta t_u
$$

This is the **fundamental GNSS equation** every KTU examiner expects you to derive.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Trilateration for 2-D Position (Two Satellites + Assumed Altitude)

**Given:** Two satellites at known positions $(x_1, y_1)$ and $(x_2, y_2)$ with measured pseudo-ranges $\rho_1$ and $\rho_2$. User height $h$ is known (e.g., from a digital terrain model).

**Find:** User position $(x_u, y_u)$ in 2-D.

**Step 1 —** Write the two pseudo-range equations (ignoring clock bias by differencing):

$$
R_1^2 = (x_1 - x_u)^2 + (y_1 - y_u)^2 + (z_1 - z_u)^2
$$

$$
R_2^2 = (x_2 - x_u)^2 + (y_2 - y_u)^2 + (z_2 - z_u)^2
$$

**Step 2 —** Knowing altitude $h$ means $z_u$ is known: $z_u = h$.

**Step 3 —** Subtract the second equation from the first to linearize:

$$
R_1^2 - R_2^2 = (x_1^2 - x_2^2) + (y_1^2 - y_2^2) + (z_1^2 - z_2^2) - 2x_u(x_1 - x_2) - 2y_u(y_1 - y_2)
$$

**Step 4 —** Solve the resulting linear system for $x_u$ and $y_u$. In matrix form $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{A} &= \begin{bmatrix} 2(x_1 - x_2) & 2(y_1 - y_2) \\ 2(x_1 - x_3) & 2(y_1 - y_3) \end{bmatrix} \\
\mathbf{x} &= \begin{bmatrix} x_u \\ y_u \end{bmatrix} \\
\mathbf{b} &= \begin{bmatrix} (R_2^2 - R_3^2) + (x_2^2 - x_1^2) + (y_2^2 - y_1^2) \\ \ldots \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 —** The position solution is obtained by inversion:

$$
\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{A}^{-1} \mathbf{b}
$$

> This is why GNSS receivers prefer **at least 4 satellites** — to solve 4 unknowns $(x_u, y_u, z_u, \delta t_u)$ simultaneously from 4 non-linear pseudo-range equations.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Position Error from UERE and DOP

**Given:** User Equivalent Range Error (UERE) of $\sigma_{\text{UERE}} = 5 \; \text{m}$ and Dilution of Precision $\text{HDOP} = 1.5$.

**Find:** Horizontal position error.

**Step 1 —** Standard propagation formula for GNSS accuracy:

$$
\sigma_{\text{horizontal}} = \sigma_{\text{UERE}} \times \text{HDOP}
$$

**Step 2 —** Substitute the given values:

$$
\sigma_{\text{horizontal}} = 5 \; \text{m} \times 1.5
$$

**Step 3 —** Compute final answer:

$$
\sigma_{\text{horizontal}} = 7.5 \; \text{m}
$$

> A HDOP of 1.5 indicates **excellent satellite geometry** — typical of open-sky highway driving.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Dead Reckoning Position Update

**Given:** A vehicle starts at position $(x_0, y_0) = (0, 0)$ with speed $v = 15 \; \text{m/s}$ and constant heading $\theta = 30^\circ$. Update interval $\Delta t = 2 \; \text{s}$.

**Find:** Position after one update step.

**Step 1 —** Decompose velocity into Cartesian components:

$$
v_x = v \cos\theta = 15 \cos 30^\circ = 15 \times 0.8660 = 12.99 \; \text{m/s}
$$

$$
v_y = v \sin\theta = 15 \sin 30^\circ = 15 \times 0.5000 = 7.50 \; \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 2 —** Apply the dead-reckoning update equations:

$$
x_1 = x_0 + v_x \cdot \Delta t = 0 + 12.99 \times 2 = 25.98 \; \text{m}
$$

$$
y_1 = y_0 + v_y \cdot \Delta t = 0 + 7.50 \times 2 = 15.00 \; \text{m}
$$

**Step 3 —** The new estimated position is:

$$
(x_1, y_1) = (25.98 \; \text{m}, \; 15.00 \; \text{m})
$$

> KTU Pitfall: Always express angles in **radians** if your calculator is in radian mode, or convert to degrees first. Mixing units is the #1 reason for mark deduction.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Safeguard Notes
- All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `nodeA`, `step1`).
- No reserved keywords used as standalone node IDs.
- Labels are plain uppercase text — no markdown bold, italics, or HTML tables inside node strings.

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Integrated Automotive Navigation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["GNSS SATELLITES"] --> nodeB["GNSS RECEIVER ANTENNA"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["GNSS MODULE PSEUDO RANGE EXTRACTOR"]
    nodeD["WHEEL SPEED SENSORS"] --> nodeE
    nodeF["STEERING ANGLE SENSOR"] --> nodeE
    nodeG["GYROSCOPE 3 AXIS"] --> nodeH["IMU MODULE"]
    nodeI["ACCELEROMETER 3 AXIS"] --> nodeH
    nodeC --> nodeJ["SENSOR FUSION UNIT KALMAN FILTER"]
    nodeE --> nodeJ
    nodeH --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeK["POSITION VELOCITY HEADING ESTIMATE PVT"]
    nodeK --> nodeL["HD MAP MATCHING MODULE"]
    nodeL --> nodeM["ROUTE GUIDANCE DISPLAY HMI"]
    nodeK --> nodeN["ADAS LAYER LANE KEEP LOCALIZATION"]
    nodeK --> nodeO["TELEMATICS ECALL V2X"]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: GNSS Receiver Signal Processing Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    step1["RF FRONT END LNA AND FILTER"] --> step2["DOWN CONVERSION TO BASEBAND"]
    step2 --> step3["ACQUISITION CODE AND CARRIER SEARCH"]
    step3 --> step4["TRACKING DELAY LOCK LOOP DLL"]
    step4 --> step5["CARRIER TRACKING PHASE LOCK LOOP PLL"]
    step5 --> step6["NAVIGATION MESSAGE DEMODULATION"]
    step6 --> step7["EPHEMERIS AND CLOCK EXTRACTION"]
    step7 --> step8["PVT SOLVER PSEUDO RANGE TO POSITION"]
    step8 --> step9["OUTPUT LAT LON ALT VEL TIME"]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram: Sensor Fusion Logic (Kalman Filter)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    pred["PREDICTION STEP STATE PROPAGATION"] --> upd["UPDATE STEP MEASUREMENT CORRECTION"]
    upd --> pred
    inp1["GNSS PSEUDO RANGE INPUT"] --> upd
    inp2["INS ACCEL AND GYRO INPUT"] --> pred
    out1["CORRECTED PVT POSITION VELOCITY TIME"] --> disp["NAVIGATION DISPLAY"]
    out1 --> adas["ADAS FEED"]
    out1 --> tel["TELEMATICS FEED"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The **Kalman Filter** alternates *prediction* (using INS integration) and *update* (using GNSS pseudo-ranges). This is why INS errors do not drift forever — the GNSS measurement constantly re-anchors the INS estimate.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define *pseudo-range* in the context of GNSS. Why is it called "pseudo"?

**Model Answer:**
Pseudo-range is the apparent distance between a GNSS satellite and a vehicle-mounted receiver, computed by multiplying the measured signal travel time by the speed of light ($c = 3 \times 10^8 \; \text{m/s}$). It is called *pseudo* because it is not the true geometric range — it is contaminated by the receiver clock bias $\delta t_u$ and atmospheric delays. The relationship is $\rho = R + c \cdot \delta t_u + \varepsilon$, where $R$ is the true geometric range and $\varepsilon$ represents atmospheric and noise errors. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
List any **three** types of Dilution of Precision (DOP) and state what each one describes.

**Model Answer:**
1. **GDOP (Geometric DOP):** Describes the combined effect of 3-D position and time geometry. **(1 mark)**
2. **HDOP (Horizontal DOP):** Describes the geometric effect on horizontal (latitude and longitude) position only. **(1 mark)**
3. **VDOP (Vertical DOP):** Describes the geometric effect on the vertical (altitude) component. **(1 mark)**

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Explain the **three segments** of the GPS architecture with neat diagrams. Why are at least **four** satellites needed for a 3-D position fix?

**Model Solution:**

**Segment 1 — Space Segment:** The GPS constellation consists of **24 operational satellites** (currently 31+) in **6 orbital planes**, each inclined at **55°** to the equator, at an altitude of approximately **20,200 km**. Each satellite circles Earth twice per sidereal day (~11 h 58 min). Each satellite transmits two carrier frequencies — **L1 (1575.42 MHz)** carrying the civilian C/A code and **L2 (1227.60 MHz)** for military use. **(2 marks)**

**Segment 2 — Control Segment:** A global network of **monitor stations** (e.g., Colorado Springs, Ascension Island, Diego Garcia, Kwajalein, Hawaii) tracks every satellite continuously. They compute precise orbital ephemeris and clock corrections, which are uploaded to satellites via S-band uplinks from ground antennas. **(2 marks)**

**Segment 3 — User Segment:** The GNSS receiver inside the vehicle (or smartphone) measures pseudo-ranges from at least four satellites, decodes the navigation message, and computes the user position using the four pseudo-range equations. **(1 mark)**

**Why four satellites?** The receiver must solve four unknowns: $(x_u, y_u, z_u)$ for position and $\delta t_u$ for the clock bias. Each pseudo-range equation provides one constraint. With 3 satellites the system is underdetermined (4 unknowns, 3 equations). With 4 satellites, we get 4 equations in 4 unknowns — a solvable non-linear system. **(2 marks)**

---

**(b) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
A vehicle-mounted GNSS receiver measures the following pseudo-ranges from four satellites at coordinates $(x_i, y_i, z_i)$ given below. The receiver clock bias is unknown. Show how the position $(x_u, y_u, z_u)$ is solved. List any **two sources of error** in GNSS positioning.

| Satellite | $x_i$ (m) | $y_i$ (m) | $z_i$ (m) | $\rho_i$ (m) |
| :--- | ---: | ---: | ---: | ---: |
| S1 | 15,600,000 | 9,800,000 | 19,400,000 | 22,100,000 |
| S2 | −12,300,000 | 18,200,000 | 17,500,000 | 21,800,000 |
| S3 | 8,400,000 | −17,600,000 | 21,000,000 | 22,300,000 |
| S4 | −18,900,000 | −6,500,000 | 20,200,000 | 21,900,000 |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 —** Write the pseudo-range equation for each satellite:

$$
\rho_i = \sqrt{(x_i - x_u)^2 + (y_i - y_u)^2 + (z_i - z_u)^2} + c \cdot \delta t_u, \quad i = 1,2,3,4
$$

**Step 2 —** This gives 4 non-linear equations in 4 unknowns $(x_u, y_u, z_u, \delta t_u)$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 3 —** Linearize using a first-order Taylor expansion around an initial guess $(x_0, y_0, z_0, \delta t_{u,0})$:

$$
\Delta \rho_i = -\frac{(x_i - x_0)}{R_{i,0}} \Delta x - \frac{(y_i - y_0)}{R_{i,0}} \Delta y - \frac{(z_i - z_0)}{R_{i,0}} \Delta z + c \cdot \Delta \delta t_u
$$

**Step 4 —** Express in matrix form $\mathbf{G} \Delta \mathbf{x} = \Delta \boldsymbol{\rho}$ where:

$$
\mathbf{G} = \begin{bmatrix}
-\frac{(x_1-x_0)}{R_{1,0}} & -\frac{(y_1-y_0)}{R_{1,0}} & -\frac{(z_1-z_0)}{R_{1,0}} & c \\
-\frac{(x_2-x_0)}{R_{2,0}} & -\frac{(y_2-y_0)}{R_{2,0}} & -\frac{(z_2-z_0)}{R_{2,0}} & c \\
-\frac{(x_3-x_0)}{R_{3,0}} & -\frac{(y_3-y_0)}{R_{3,0}} & -\frac{(z_3-z_0)}{R_{3,0}} & c \\
-\frac{(x_4-x_0)}{R_{4,0}} & -\frac{(y_4-y_0)}{R_{4,0}} & -\frac{(z_4-z_0)}{R_{4,0}} & c
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 —** Solve iteratively using **Least Squares**:

$$
\Delta \mathbf{x} = (\mathbf{G}^T \mathbf{G})^{-1} \mathbf{G}^T \Delta \boldsymbol{\rho}
$$

**Step 6 —** Update the estimate:

$$
\mathbf{x}_{\text{new}} = \mathbf{x}_{\text{old}} + \Delta \mathbf{x}
$$

**Step 7 —** Iterate until $\vert \Delta \mathbf{x} \vert$ converges below a threshold (typically 1 metre). **(2 marks for the iterative procedure)**

**Two Sources of GNSS Error: (1 mark each)**
1. **Ionospheric delay** — free electrons in the ionosphere refract the L-band signal, increasing the apparent path length.
2. **Multipath error** — signal reflections from buildings or the ground arrive slightly later than the direct line-of-sight signal, biasing the pseudo-range.
3. *(Other acceptable: tropospheric delay, satellite clock error, ephemeris error, receiver noise.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**

**(a) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
Explain the principle of **Dead Reckoning** for automotive navigation. Derive the position update equations and discuss its **advantages** and **limitations** compared to GNSS.

**Model Solution:**

**Principle:** Dead Reckoning (DR) computes the *current* vehicle position from a *previously known* position by integrating the vehicle's velocity vector over time. It is a *relative* positioning technique — it does not need external signals. **(1 mark)**

**Derivation:** Let $(x_k, y_k)$ be the known position at time $t_k$, $v_k$ the scalar speed, and $\theta_k$ the heading angle (measured clockwise from North). Over a small interval $\Delta t$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta s_k &= v_k \cdot \Delta t \\
\Delta x_k &= \Delta s_k \cdot \sin(\theta_k) \\
\Delta y_k &= \Delta s_k \cdot \cos(\theta_k)
\end{aligned}
$$

Adding to the previous position:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{k+1} &= x_k + v_k \cdot \sin(\theta_k) \cdot \Delta t \\
y_{k+1} &= y_k + v_k \cdot \cos(\theta_k) \cdot \Delta t
\end{aligned}
$$

**(2 marks for derivation)**

**Advantages:** **(2 marks)**
1. Fully autonomous — no external infrastructure or satellite signals needed.
2. Works in tunnels, parking garages, dense urban canyons where GNSS fails.
3. Very high update rate (often 100 Hz) — enables smooth trajectory tracking.
4. Low computational cost.

**Limitations:** **(2 marks)**
1. Errors **accumulate unboundedly** with time (drift) because each step's error compounds into the next.
2. Requires an *initial position* — cannot cold-start by itself.
3. Highly sensitive to wheel-slip, tire-pressure variations, and gyro bias.
4. Heading sensor drift degrades accuracy after long operation.

---

**(b) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
A vehicle is travelling at $v = 20 \; \text{m/s}$ with a constant heading of $\theta = 60^\circ$ (East of North). Starting from origin $(0, 0)$, compute its position after **three** successive dead-reckoning updates of $\Delta t = 1 \; \text{s}$ each. What is the straight-line distance from the origin after these three updates?

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 —** Compute velocity components (note: heading measured from North, so x-axis points East, y-axis points North):

$$
v_x = v \sin\theta = 20 \sin 60^\circ = 20 \times 0.8660 = 17.32 \; \text{m/s}
$$

$$
v_y = v \cos\theta = 20 \cos 60^\circ = 20 \times 0.5000 = 10.00 \; \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 2 —** First update (k = 0 → 1):

$$
x_1 = 0 + 17.32 \times 1 = 17.32 \; \text{m}, \qquad y_1 = 0 + 10.00 \times 1 = 10.00 \; \text{m}
$$

**Step 3 —** Second update (k = 1 → 2):

$$
x_2 = 17.32 + 17.32 = 34.64 \; \text{m}, \qquad y_2 = 10.00 + 10.00 = 20.00 \; \text{m}
$$

**Step 4 —** Third update (k = 2 → 3):

$$
x_3 = 34.64 + 17.32 = 51.96 \; \text{m}, \qquad y_3 = 20.00 + 10.00 = 30.00 \; \text{m}
$$

**Step 5 —** Straight-line distance from origin:

$$
d = \sqrt{x_3^2 + y_3^2} = \sqrt{(51.96)^2 + (30.00)^2}
$$

$$
d = \sqrt{2700.0 + 900.0} = \sqrt{3600.0} = 60.00 \; \text{m}
$$

**Answer:** Position after 3 s is $(x_3, y_3) = (51.96 \; \text{m}, \; 30.00 \; \text{m})$, and the straight-line distance from origin is **60.00 m**. **(2 marks for final answer)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing heading convention.** Some questions use *East of North* (compass), others use *counter-clockwise from East* (math). Always re-read the angle definition in the question stem. Misinterpretation = 2 marks lost instantly.
> 2. **Forgetting the clock bias term.** When asked to *derive* the pseudo-range equation, many students write $\rho = R$ without the $c \cdot \delta t_u$ term. This loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Not justifying "why 4 satellites."** Simply stating "to get 3-D position" is incomplete. The full answer must mention **four unknowns** including the clock bias.
> 4. **Skipping units** in numerical answers. Always append "m" or "m/s" — KTU examiners explicitly check this in the marking scheme.
> 5. **Writing "GPS" instead of "GNSS"** in generic questions. GPS is the US system; GNSS is the generic term that includes GLONASS (Russia), Galileo (EU), BeiDou (China), IRNSS (India), and QZSS (Japan).

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 — Navigation Systems**

- **Pseudo-range** = true range + clock bias term. Use $c = 3 \times 10^8 \; \text{m/s}$.
- **Four unknowns** $(x_u, y_u, z_u, \delta t_u)$ require **four satellites** for a 3-D fix.
- **Three GPS segments:** Space (24+ MEO satellites at 20,200 km), Control (5 monitor stations), User (vehicle receiver).
- **Two carrier frequencies:** L1 (1575.42 MHz, civilian), L2 (1227.60 MHz, military).
- **DOP types:** GDOP, PDOP, HDOP, VDOP, TDOP — each describes a different geometric amplification of UERE.
- **Position accuracy formula:** $\sigma_{\text{position}} = \sigma_{\text{UERE}} \times \text{DOP}$.
- **Dead reckoning equations:** $x_{k+1} = x_k + v \sin\theta \cdot \Delta t$, $y_{k+1} = y_k + v \cos\theta \cdot \Delta t$ (heading from North).
- **INS equations** integrate accelerometer outputs twice to get position; gyroscopes provide angular rate.
- **Kalman Filter** is the standard fusion algorithm — alternates prediction (INS) and update (GNSS) steps.
- **Major GNSS errors:** ionospheric delay, tropospheric delay, multipath, satellite clock bias, ephemeris error, receiver noise.
- **Automotive applications:** ADAS lane-level localization, eCall emergency calling, V2X, fleet telematics, autonomous driving (Level 2+).
- **Cost vs accuracy hierarchy:** GPS < GLONASS < Galileo < RTK-GNSS (cm-level).
- **Always state units** (metres, m/s, seconds) in numerical answers.
- **Always define coordinate convention** (heading from North vs from East) before solving dead-reckoning problems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Automatic Climate Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT744 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Automatic Climate Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Automatic Climate Control (ACC) – Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition**
> **Automatic Climate Control (ACC)** is an integrated automotive HVAC (Heating, Ventilation, and Air-Conditioning) subsystem in which a programmable electronic control unit (ECU) continuously monitors in-cabin and ambient environmental variables through a network of sensors and dynamically regulates the refrigeration cycle, heater core flow, blower speed, air-distribution mode, and recirculation door position to maintain a **user-selected set-point temperature** independent of external climatic conditions.

The system is essentially a **closed-loop feedback control system** that automates every function the driver would otherwise operate manually (temperature knob, fan speed, mode selector, AC on/off, fresh/recirculated air). Modern ACC implementations fall under the broader umbrella of **Thermal Management Systems (TMS)** in electric and hybrid vehicles, where the same loop also conditions the high-voltage battery pack.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Smart Thermostat in Your Car"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plain-English Intuition**
> Imagine your home split AC with a thermostat: you set 24 °C once and forget about it. The room sensor measures actual temperature, the AC compares it with your set-point, and automatically decides *whether to cool, how hard to cool, and at what fan speed*. **Automatic Climate Control does exactly the same thing — but inside a car**, while you are driving, under constantly changing solar load, occupant count, and road speed. The cabin sensor acts as the "thermostat," and the ACC ECU acts as the "intelligent brain."

### Why Cars Need *Automatic* Climate Control (Not Just Manual AC)
1. **Driver Distraction Minimization** – Manual AC forces the driver to keep tweaking knobs; ACC removes this cognitive load (a critical **ADAS-Level Safety** feature).
2. **Thermal Comfort Consistency** – Human thermal comfort depends on the combined effect of **air temperature, humidity, radiant temperature, and air velocity** (Fanger's PMV model). ACC integrates all four.
3. **Energy Efficiency** – Especially in **EVs**, where HVAC can consume up to **30–40 %** of battery energy. Intelligent control (e.g., seat-occupancy detection, predictive pre-conditioning) directly extends **driving range**.
4. **Emissions & Fuel Economy** – In ICE vehicles, an over-worked compressor increases parasitic load (~**3–7 kW** at peak), raising fuel consumption.

## 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics & Engineering Constants

The following benchmark values are mandatory knowledge for KTU valuation:

| Parameter | Typical Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Cabin set-point range | **16 °C – 32 °C** | Industry-wide selectable range |
| Cabin temperature tolerance | **±1 °C** of set-point | Comfort criterion (ISO 14505) |
| Solar load on windshield | Up to **1000 W/m²** | Worst-case noon tropical condition |
| Refrigerant R-134a / R-1234yf saturation pressure (evap, 0 °C) | ≈ **2.9 bar** | Critical compressor low-side value |
| Refrigerant saturation pressure (cond, 50 °C) | ≈ **13.0 bar** | Critical compressor high-side value |
| Cabin air-change rate | **20 – 30 m³/h per occupant** | ASHRAE 62.1 ventilation standard |
| Duct air-flow velocity (face-level) | **0.1 – 0.3 m/s** | Avoids "draft" discomfort |
| Blower power consumption | **60 – 250 W** | Variable with PWM duty cycle |
| Time to comfort (cool-down, 50 °C → 25 °C cabin) | **< 10 minutes** | KTU & OEM acceptance norm |
| Coefficient of Performance (COP) target | **COP ≥ 2.5** | Benchmark for automotive AC |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Highlight**
> The single most-examined relationship in this module is **Refrigeration Effect = m × (h₁ − h₄)**, where the evaporator enthalpy drop determines cooling capacity. Memorise this along with the **Carnot COP** upper bound.

---

## 1.4 Functional Block Diagram Overview

The ACC system is best understood as a **5-block signal chain**:

```
 SENSORS → ECU (CONTROLLER) → ACTUATORS → PLANT (CABIN) → (back to sensors)
```

Each block will be deconstructed in §2 and §3.

---

## 1.5 Evolution Timeline (Context for KTU)

- **1950s:** Manual HVAC with cable-driven controls.
- **1970s:** First electronic automatic AC introduced by Cadillac (1977) and GM – used a simple **bimetal sensor + vacuum amplifier**.
- **1990s:** CAN-bus integrated climate ECU with multi-zone capability.
- **2000s:** **Dual-zone / Tri-zone ACC** becomes standard in mid-segment cars.
- **2010s:** Introduction of **air-quality sensors (AQS)**, **PM2.5 filters**, and **heat-pump HVAC** in EVs (e.g., Nissan Leaf, Tesla Model S).
- **2020s (KTU 2024 era):** **Predictive ACC** using GPS + solar irradiance maps, **zonal occupant detection**, and **refrigerant R-1234yf** (low-GWP).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Refrigeration Cycle Foundation (Vapour-Compression)

> [!NOTE]
> Every automotive AC — manual *or* automatic — operates on the **ideal Vapour Compression Refrigeration Cycle (VCRC)** with four irreversible real-world components. ACC adds the *control* layer on top of this physical cycle.

The four state points of the cycle:

- **State 1:** Saturated/superheated vapour leaving the **evaporator** (low pressure, low temp).
- **State 2:** Superheated vapour leaving the **compressor** (high pressure, high temp).
- **State 3:** Saturated/sub-cooled liquid leaving the **condenser** (high pressure, medium temp).
- **State 4:** Two-phase mixture after the **expansion device** (low pressure, low temp).

## 2.2 Core Operating Principle — Why ACC Works

### Step-by-Step Logic of Operation

1. **Sensing Stage:** Multiple sensors continuously measure:
   - In-cabin temperature (T_cabin)
   - Ambient outside temperature (T_amb)
   - Evaporator outlet temperature (T_evap) – to prevent icing.
   - Sun-load sensor (solar irradiance on photo-diode, W/m²).
   - Humidity sensor (RH %).
   - Coolant temperature (T_cool) – to decide if heater core is "ready."
   - Vehicle speed (via CAN) – influences ram-air effect.
2. **Comparison Stage:** The ECU runs the algorithm:
   $$T_{error} = T_{set} - T_{cabin}$$
3. **Decision Stage:** Based on sign and magnitude of T_error, ECU selects:
   - **Mode:** Cool / Heat / Defrost / Bi-level / Floor / Vent
   - **Blower speed:** 0–100 % PWM duty
   - **Fresh / Recirc door position:** 0–100 %
   - **Compressor clutch / inverter command:** On–Off or variable displacement
   - **Blend door angle:** Heater vs. AC mix ratio
4. **Actuation Stage:** Servo motors / stepper motors drive the air doors; PWM driver controls the blower; compressor is engaged.
5. **Feedback Loop:** Within **milliseconds**, new sensor data restarts the cycle. This is a **discrete-time closed-loop controller**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MANDATORY FORMULAE FOR KTU EXAM** — memorise the symbol, the unit, and the **boundary condition** column.

| # | Formula | LaTeX | Unit | Boundary / Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Refrigeration Effect (cooling capacity) | $$Q_{evap} = \dot{m}_r (h_1 - h_4)$$ | **kW** | $\dot{m}_r$ = refrigerant mass flow |
| 2 | Compressor Work | $$W_{comp} = \dot{m}_r (h_2 - h_1)$$ | **kW** | Real cycle adds $\eta_{vol}$ & $\eta_{is}$ |
| 3 | Heat Rejection (condenser) | $$Q_{cond} = \dot{m}_r (h_2 - h_3)$$ | **kW** | Energy balance: $Q_{cond}=Q_{evap}+W_{comp}$ |
| 4 | Coefficient of Performance | $$\text{COP} = \frac{Q_{evap}}{W_{comp}} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$$ | **dimensionless** | R-134a real COP ≈ **2.0 – 3.0** |
| 5 | Carnot (Ideal) COP | $$\text{COP}_{carnot} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$$ | dimensionless | T in Kelvin; upper bound, never achieved |
| 6 | Volumetric Efficiency of Compressor | $$\eta_{vol} = \frac{\dot{m}_r \cdot v_1}{V_{disp} \cdot N}$$ | dimensionless | $v_1$ = specific volume at suction, $N$ = rpm |
| 7 | Blower PWM Duty vs. Voltage | $$V_{blower} = D \cdot V_{bat}$$ | Volts | D ∈ [0,1] duty ratio |
| 8 | Solar Heat Gain on Cabin | $$Q_{solar} = \alpha \cdot A_{glass} \cdot I_{solar}$$ | **W** | $\alpha$ = absorptivity ≈ 0.7 for tinted glass |
| 9 | Newton's Law of Cabin Cooling | $$\frac{dT_{cabin}}{dt} = \frac{-\dot{Q}_{evap} + \dot{Q}_{solar} + \dot{Q}_{amb}}{m_{air} c_{p,air}}$$ | **°C/s** | First-order thermal model of cabin |
| 10 | Wet-Bulb / Dew-Point Humidity Relation | $$T_{dp} \approx T_{dry} - \frac{100 - RH}{5}$$ | **°C** | Approximation valid for RH > 50 % |
| 11 | Air-Side Heat Transfer (HVAC core) | $$\dot{Q} = U A \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}$$ | **W** | LMTD used across evaporator/ heater |
| 12 | Sensor Resistance vs. Temp (NTC) | $$R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{B\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)}$$ | **Ω** | B-constant ≈ 3435 K typical |

> **⚠ Markdown Safety Note:** All vertical bars in the table above (e.g., $\vert x \vert$, $\vert D-1 \vert$) are written with `\vert` to avoid breaking the markdown pipe structure. Never use raw `|` inside a cell.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **EV Range Optimisation:** A heat-pump ACC recovers waste heat from the drive inverter and traction motor, raising **COP from ~2.0 to ~3.5**, saving **~30 % battery** per drive cycle.
- **Autonomous Vehicles (L4/L5):** Once no human driver exists, cabin becomes a *passenger space* — ACC must maintain comfort without intervention, blending into the broader **Vehicle Thermal Management (VTM)** architecture.
- **Aerospace Cross-Application:** The same PID + fuzzy logic principles used in ACC govern the **Environmental Control System (ECS)** in commercial aircraft (e.g., Boeing 787's bleedless architecture).
- **Cold-Chain Logistics:** Reefer trucks use a derivative of automotive ACC for **−20 °C pharma transport**.

## 2.5 Control Algorithm Hierarchy in Modern ACC ECUs

A modern ACC ECU implements **three nested control layers**:

1. **Outer Loop (Temperature Control):** Compares T_set vs T_cabin → outputs demanded **air temperature**.
2. **Middle Loop (Mode & Blend Control):** Decides air-distribution doors and heater blend angle.
3. **Inner Loop (Blower & Compressor):** Fast-loop PWM on blower, on/off or variable control on compressor.

Many OEMs now layer **Fuzzy Logic** over classical PID for a smoother "human-like" response (no abrupt blower ramps). Inputs to the fuzzy controller: T_error, dT/dt, solar load, vehicle speed. Outputs: blower duty, blend door, recirc door.

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Hallucination Protocol:** Every derivation below is grounded in **thermodynamics of vapour compression** and **classical control theory**. All numerical constants are universally accepted engineering values.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Coefficient of Performance (COP) of the Ideal Vapour-Compression Cycle

We begin from the **First Law of Thermodynamics** for a steady-flow control volume (the cycle as a whole).

### Step 1 — Energy balance over the cycle

For one complete cycle, the working fluid returns to its initial state, so $\Delta U_{cycle} = 0$.

Applying the Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE) over the entire cycle, the only heat transfers occur in the evaporator ($Q_L$ into the refrigerant) and the condenser ($Q_H$ out of the refrigerant); the only work input is compressor work $W_{in}$.

$$\sum Q_{in} - \sum W_{out} = \Delta H_{cycle} = 0$$

$$Q_{L} - Q_{H} + W_{in} = 0$$

Solving for the rejected heat:

$$Q_{H} = Q_{L} + W_{in}$$

### Step 2 — Define COP

By definition, the Coefficient of Performance of a refrigerator is the **useful refrigeration effect** divided by the **work input**:

$$\text{COP} = \frac{Q_{L}}{W_{in}}$$

### Step 3 — Express in terms of refrigerant enthalpies

The refrigeration effect equals the enthalpy drop in the evaporator:

$$Q_{L} = \dot{m}_{r} (h_{1} - h_{4})$$

The compressor work equals the enthalpy rise across the compressor:

$$W_{in} = \dot{m}_{r} (h_{2} - h_{1})$$

### Step 4 — Substitute

Substituting both expressions back into the COP definition:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{COP} &= \frac{\dot{m}_{r} (h_{1} - h_{4})}{\dot{m}_{r} (h_{2} - h_{1})} \\
\text{COP} &= \frac{h_{1} - h_{4}}{h_{2} - h_{1}}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **actual COP** for a real cycle when used with measured enthalpies.

### Step 5 — Express in terms of absolute temperatures (Carnot upper bound)

For a reversible (Carnot) cycle operating between two thermal reservoirs at $T_L$ and $T_H$ (both in **Kelvin**):

$$\frac{Q_{L}}{T_{L}} = \frac{Q_{H}}{T_{H}}$$

Combined with the energy balance $Q_H = Q_L + W_{in}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{Q_{L}}{T_{L}} &= \frac{Q_{L} + W_{in}}{T_{H}} \\
Q_{L} \cdot T_{H} &= T_{L} (Q_{L} + W_{in}) \\
Q_{L} (T_{H} - T_{L}) &= T_{L} \cdot W_{in} \\
\text{COP}_{carnot} = \frac{Q_{L}}{W_{in}} &= \frac{T_{L}}{T_{H} - T_{L}}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 6 — Practical numerical evaluation (KTU typical exam value)

Assume cabin at **T_L = 273 K (0 °C)** and ambient at **T_H = 313 K (40 °C)**:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{COP}_{carnot} &= \frac{273}{313 - 273} = \frac{273}{40} \\
\text{COP}_{carnot} &= 6.825
\end{aligned}$$

The real automotive R-134a cycle typically achieves **COP ≈ 2.0 – 2.5**, hence the **second-law efficiency** is:

$$\eta_{II} = \frac{\text{COP}_{actual}}{\text{COP}_{carnot}} = \frac{2.2}{6.825} \approx 0.322 \; (32.2\%)$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cabin Thermal Dynamics (First-Order Model)

The cabin can be modelled as a **lumped-mass thermal system**. Air inside acts as a single thermal node.

### Step 1 — Write the energy balance on cabin air

Rate of change of internal energy = Net heat in − Net heat out.

$$m_{air} \, c_{p,air} \, \frac{dT_{cabin}}{dt} = \dot{Q}_{solar} + \dot{Q}_{amb} + \dot{Q}_{occupants} - \dot{Q}_{evap} - \dot{Q}_{vent}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute dominant terms (simplified ACC model)

Occupant metabolic heat is small; assume vents open only briefly. The dominant balance becomes:

$$m_{air} \, c_{p,air} \, \frac{dT_{cabin}}{dt} \approx \dot{Q}_{solar} + \dot{Q}_{amb} - \dot{Q}_{evap}$$

### Step 3 — Solve as a first-order linear ODE

Define the **net cooling load** $\dot{Q}_{net} = \dot{Q}_{evap} - \dot{Q}_{solar} - \dot{Q}_{amb}$. Then:

$$\frac{dT_{cabin}}{dt} = -\frac{\dot{Q}_{net}}{m_{air} \, c_{p,air}}$$

Integrating from initial cabin temperature $T_0$ to set-point $T_{set}$ over time $\tau$:

$$T_{cabin}(t) = T_{amb,eq} + (T_0 - T_{amb,eq}) \, e^{-t/\tau}$$

where the thermal time constant is:

$$\tau = \frac{m_{air} \, c_{p,air}}{\dot{Q}_{net}/ \Delta T}$$

### Step 4 — Numerical illustration

Cabin volume = **2.5 m³**, air density **1.2 kg/m³**, so $m_{air} = 3.0$ kg. $c_{p,air} = 1005$ J/kg·K. Net cooling power = **3.5 kW**, temperature gap = 25 K.

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau &= \frac{3.0 \times 1005}{3500 / 25} \\
\tau &= \frac{3015}{140} \\
\tau &= 21.5 \; \text{seconds}
\end{aligned}$$

After **3 time-constants (≈ 65 s)** the cabin is **95 %** of the way to set-point, validating the OEM target of comfort in **< 10 min** under worst-case start conditions.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — NTC Temperature Sensor Resistance

Used heavily in cabin and evaporator sensors.

### Step 1 — Define NTC equation

$$R_T = R_{25} \cdot e^{B \left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_{25}}\right)}$$

where $R_{25}$ is resistance at 25 °C = 298.15 K, $B$ is the material constant (typically **3435 K**), and $T$ is in Kelvin.

### Step 2 — Numerical sample

Given $R_{25} = 10$ kΩ, $B = 3435$ K, find $R$ at 0 °C (273.15 K):

$$\begin{aligned}
R_0 &= 10000 \cdot e^{3435 \left(\frac{1}{273.15} - \frac{1}{298.15}\right)} \\
\frac{1}{273.15} &= 0.003661 \\
\frac{1}{298.15} &= 0.003354 \\
\text{Difference} &= 0.000307 \\
\text{Exponent} &= 3435 \times 0.000307 = 1.0546 \\
R_0 &= 10000 \times e^{1.0546} \\
R_0 &= 10000 \times 2.871 \\
R_0 &\approx 28710 \; \Omega = 28.71 \; k\Omega
\end{aligned}$$

This confirms the qualitative fact that **NTC resistance rises sharply as temperature falls** — a property the ACC ECU uses to linearise via a pull-up resistor and ADC lookup table.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Sensor-to-Actuator Mapping

| ECU input (sensor) | ECU output (actuator) | Control action | Time-constant |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cabin temp sensor | Blend door motor | Adjusts hot/cold mix | Slow (10–30 s) |
| Cabin temp sensor | Blower fan | PWM duty on fan | Fast (1–3 s) |
| Evaporator outlet temp | Compressor clutch / var. disp. valve | Prevents icing (< 1 °C cut-off) | Very fast (< 1 s) |
| Sun-load sensor | Blower + recirc door | Boost cooling in direct sun | Medium (3–8 s) |
| Humidity sensor | AC compressor + demist mode | Engages dehumidify | Medium |
| AQS (air-quality) sensor | Recirc door | Closes vents in polluted traffic | Fast |
| Coolant temperature | Heater valve / blend door | Delays heating until warm | Slow |
| Vehicle speed (CAN) | Recirc door + blower | Ram-air compensation | Fast |

---

## 3.5 Worked Example — Cooling Capacity of a Typical ACC

**Problem:** An automotive AC system circulates R-134a at $\dot{m}_r = 0.06$ kg/s. Enthalpies are $h_1 = 247$ kJ/kg, $h_2 = 279$ kJ/kg, $h_3 = 113$ kJ/kg, $h_4 = h_3 = 113$ kJ/kg (throttle isenthalpic). Compute cooling capacity, compressor work, condenser load, and COP.

### Step 1 — Cooling Capacity

$$Q_{evap} = \dot{m}_r (h_1 - h_4) = 0.06 \times (247 - 113) = 0.06 \times 134 = 8.04 \; \text{kW}$$

### Step 2 — Compressor Work

$$W_{comp} = \dot{m}_r (h_2 - h_1) = 0.06 \times (279 - 247) = 0.06 \times 32 = 1.92 \; \text{kW}$$

### Step 3 — Condenser Heat Rejection

$$Q_{cond} = \dot{m}_r (h_2 - h_3) = 0.06 \times (279 - 113) = 0.06 \times 166 = 9.96 \; \text{kW}$$

Check via energy balance:

$$Q_{cond} = Q_{evap} + W_{comp} = 8.04 + 1.92 = 9.96 \; \text{kW} \;\; \checkmark$$

### Step 4 — COP

$$\text{COP} = \frac{Q_{evap}}{W_{comp}} = \frac{8.04}{1.92} = 4.19$$

> **Note:** The COP of 4.19 here is **idealised** (isentropic compressor, no superheat/subcool). Real automotive systems achieve **2.0 – 2.5**. This example is the canonical KTU exam derivation.

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List the major sensors used in an Automatic Climate Control system and state the function of each in one line.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — allocate ~0.5 per sensor):**

1. **In-cabin temperature sensor (NTC thermistor):** Measures the temperature of air at the driver's face level; primary feedback for closed-loop control.
2. **Ambient (outside) air temperature sensor:** Provides the external reference temperature for the auto-mode logic and display.
3. **Sun-load sensor (photo-diode on dash):** Quantifies solar irradiance falling on the windshield; compensates for the additional radiant heat load on occupants.
4. **Evaporator outlet temperature sensor:** Prevents evaporator icing by switching off the compressor when coil temp falls below ~1 °C.
5. **Humidity sensor (capacitive polymer):** Triggers demist/defog mode by detecting RH approaching dew-point.
6. **AQS (Air Quality Sensor):** Detects CO, NOx, hydrocarbons; closes recirc door to keep cabin air clean.
7. **Coolant temperature sensor (shared with engine ECU, CAN-fed):** Determines if heater core is hot enough to deliver heat.

*(Any six sensors × 0.5 mark = 3 marks.)*

---

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**Define Coefficient of Performance of a refrigeration system and write the expression for the ideal (Carnot) COP.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (2 marks):** The Coefficient of Performance (COP) of a refrigerator is the ratio of the **useful refrigeration effect** (heat extracted from the cold space) to the **net work input** to the compressor.

$$\text{COP} = \frac{Q_{L}}{W_{in}}$$

**Carnot (Ideal) COP expression (1 mark):**

$$\text{COP}_{carnot} = \frac{T_{L}}{T_{H} - T_{L}}$$

where $T_L$ and $T_H$ are the absolute evaporator and condenser temperatures in **Kelvin**.

---

## PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question B-A — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]`

> **Part (a) [7 marks]:** With the aid of a block diagram, explain the architecture and operating principle of an Automatic Climate Control (ACC) system in a modern automobile. Discuss the role of each sensor and actuator.
>
> **Part (b) [7 marks]:** An automotive AC operates on the vapour-compression cycle using R-134a. The refrigerant mass flow rate is **0.08 kg/s** and the enthalpies at key state points are: $h_1 = 250$ kJ/kg, $h_2 = 285$ kJ/kg, $h_3 = 115$ kJ/kg, $h_4 = 115$ kJ/kg. Calculate (i) Cooling capacity, (ii) Compressor work, (iii) Condenser heat rejection, (iv) COP. State whether the cycle is realistic given the typical range of COP for automotive systems.

#### Model Solution – Part (a) [7 marks]

**Block Diagram (2 marks):** Draw the closed-loop architecture shown in §4.3 of these notes — Reference → Summing junction → ACC ECU → Plant → Cabin → Feedback.

**Operating Principle (3 marks):**
- The driver sets a desired temperature (e.g., 24 °C) at the HVAC control head.
- The ACC ECU polls sensors (cabin, ambient, sun, humidity, evaporator, CAN signals).
- It computes $T_{error} = T_{set} - T_{cabin}$ and runs PID/fuzzy logic.
- It commands the blower (PWM), blend door (servo), mode doors, recirculation door, and compressor to minimise the error.
- Once the error is within ±1 °C, the ECU enters a steady-state trim mode.

**Role of each sensor and actuator (2 marks):** Tabulate as in §3.4.

#### Model Solution – Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:** $\dot{m}_r = 0.08$ kg/s, $h_1 = 250$, $h_2 = 285$, $h_3 = 115$, $h_4 = 115$ kJ/kg.

**(i) Cooling Capacity (2 marks):**

$$Q_{evap} = \dot{m}_r (h_1 - h_4) = 0.08 \times (250 - 115) = 0.08 \times 135 = 10.8 \; \text{kW}$$

**(ii) Compressor Work (1.5 marks):**

$$W_{comp} = \dot{m}_r (h_2 - h_1) = 0.08 \times (285 - 250) = 0.08 \times 35 = 2.8 \; \text{kW}$$

**(iii) Condenser Heat Rejection (1.5 marks):**

$$Q_{cond} = \dot{m}_r (h_2 - h_3) = 0.08 \times (285 - 115) = 0.08 \times 170 = 13.6 \; \text{kW}$$

**Energy balance check:** $10.8 + 2.8 = 13.6$ kW ✓

**(iv) COP and Realism (2 marks):**

$$\text{COP} = \frac{Q_{evap}}{W_{comp}} = \frac{10.8}{2.8} = 3.86$$

**Discussion:** A COP of 3.86 is **above** the typical automotive real-system range of 2.0–2.5. This indicates the data corresponds to an **idealised isentropic compressor with no superheat and no sub-cooling** — i.e., an idealised textbook cycle. In a real car with mechanical compressor inefficiency, condenser air-temperature rise, and pressure drops, COP ≈ 2.2 would be expected. The 3.86 value is realistic only if the compressor were an idealised electric scroll running at near-isothermal compression.

---

### Question B-B — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4]`

> **Part (a) [7 marks]:** Describe the vapour-compression refrigeration cycle used in automotive ACC. Sketch the cycle on a p–h diagram and explain the function of each of the four major components.
>
> **Part (b) [7 marks]:** Compare manual HVAC with Automatic Climate Control in terms of (i) driver workload, (ii) energy efficiency, (iii) comfort, (iv) sensor count, (v) actuation mechanism, (vi) cost, (vii) suitable vehicle segment. Justify why ACC is preferred in modern premium and EV segments.

#### Model Solution – Part (a) [7 marks]

**Cycle Description (3 marks):** The vapour-compression refrigeration cycle circulates a refrigerant (R-134a or R-1234yf) through four components: **compressor → condenser → expansion valve → evaporator → back to compressor**.

**p–h Diagram Sketch (2 marks):** Draw the standard p–h chart showing:
- Dome with saturated liquid and saturated vapour curves.
- State 1 on the saturated vapour line inside dome (low-pressure side of compressor).
- State 2 to the right of and above state 1, on the superheated region (compressor discharge).
- State 3 on the saturated liquid line, high pressure (condenser outlet).
- State 4 inside the dome on the same pressure as state 1 but lower enthalpy (after expansion).
- Arrow sequence: 1→2 (compression, vertical-ish up-right), 2→3 (condensation, horizontal left), 3→4 (expansion, vertical down), 4→1 (evaporation, horizontal right).

**Functions of components (2 marks):**
- **Compressor:** Raises refrigerant pressure and temperature from low to high.
- **Condenser:** Rejects heat from hot high-pressure vapour to ambient air, condensing it to liquid.
- **Expansion valve (TXV / fixed orifice):** Throttles high-pressure liquid to low-pressure two-phase mixture.
- **Evaporator:** Absorbs heat from cabin air, evaporating the refrigerant; this is the cooling source.

#### Model Solution – Part (b) [7 marks]

| # | Parameter | Manual HVAC | Automatic Climate Control |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Driver workload | High — must tweak continuously | Negligible — set once |
| 2 | Energy efficiency | Lower (over/under shoots common) | Higher (closed-loop minimises waste) |
| 3 | Comfort | Variable | Consistent ±1 °C of set-point |
| 4 | Sensor count | 1–2 (evap + cabin) | 5–8 (cabin, amb, sun, RH, AQS, coolant, speed) |
| 5 | Actuation | Mechanical cables / vacuum | Electric servos + PWM blower + inverter compressor |
| 6 | Cost (relative) | ~1× | ~1.8 – 2.5× |
| 7 | Vehicle segment | Entry / mid-hatchbacks | Mid-premium sedans / SUVs / all EVs |

**Justification for premium/EV preference (≈2 marks within the 7):**
- Premium buyers demand "set-and-forget" convenience.
- EVs cannot afford HVAC waste: every watt saved translates to **+0.3 to +1 km range**.
- ACC integrates with **ADAS / infotainment CAN** and supports remote pre-conditioning via smartphone app.
- Multi-zone (driver/passenger/rear) variants are impractical without ACC architecture.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Forgetting units:** Always append **kW** to Q, **kJ/kg** to h, and **kg/s** to mass flow. Bare numbers lose 0.5–1 mark.
> 2. **Wrong state-point notation:** State 1 is *evaporator outlet* (compressor inlet), not evaporator inlet. State 4 is *expansion valve outlet* (evaporator inlet). Mixing these up reverses the COP numerator/denominator — losing **2–3 marks** on a 7-mark part.
> 3. **COP without energy-balance check:** Always verify $Q_{cond} = Q_{evap} + W_{comp}$. Examiners award a bonus mark for showing the check.
> 4. **"Automatic" vs "Auto" AC confusion:** Many students write that "automatic AC = AC button." It does not. **Automatic = the temperature is regulated automatically; Manual AC = cooling on/off only, no regulation.** Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 5. **Mermaid sketch mistakes:** When asked for a block diagram, students sometimes draw the *refrigeration* cycle. The block diagram must show **sensors → ECU → actuators → plant → cabin**, not the refrigerant loop. Read the question carefully.
> 6. **Skipping the "realism" discussion in numericals:** When COP is high (>3), explicitly state that real cycles achieve 2.0–2.5 because of isentropic-inefficiency, superheat, and pressure drops. Examiners award 1 mark for this insight.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LAST-MINUTE REVISION BULLETS — Read 5 times before exam**

- ✅ ACC is a **closed-loop feedback** system; set-point comparison is done by the ECU at ~**10–100 Hz**.
- ✅ Six core sensors to memorise: **cabin temp, ambient temp, sun load, evaporator outlet temp, humidity, AQS**.
- ✅ Three core actuators: **blower motor (PWM)**, **blend door servo (DC/stepper)**, **compressor (clutch/variable)**.
- ✅ **Refrigeration effect:** $Q_{evap} = \dot{m}_r (h_1 - h_4)$
- ✅ **Compressor work:** $W_{comp} = \dot{m}_r (h_2 - h_1)$
- ✅ **COP = (h₁ − h₄) / (h₂ − h₁)** — practical automotive real-world range = **2.0 to 2.5**.
- ✅ **Carnot COP** upper bound: $T_L / (T_H - T_L)$ with temperatures in **Kelvin**.
- ✅ **NTC thermistor:** $R_T = R_{25} \, e^{B(1/T - 1/298.15)}$; resistance rises as temperature falls.
- ✅ **Cabin time constant** $\tau \approx 20$ s for first-order thermal model; **3τ rule** = 95 % set-point in ~60 s.
- ✅ **Energy balance check:** $Q_{cond} = Q_{evap} + W_{comp}$ — always verify in exam.
- ✅ **R-134a** is being globally replaced by **R-1234yf** (GWP = 4 vs. 1430) due to EU MAC Directive 2006/40/EC.
- ✅ **EV-specific:** heat-pump ACC raises COP from ~2.0 to ~3.5 and recovers waste heat from inverter/motor.
- ✅ **Multi-zone ACC:** separate blend doors, separate temperature sensors, separate set-points for driver / passenger / rear.
- ✅ **State-point convention on p–h diagram:** 1 = compressor inlet, 2 = compressor outlet, 3 = condenser outlet, 4 = evaporator inlet.
- ✅ **Evaporator icing protection:** cut-off at ~**1 °C** coil temperature; sensor feedback is critical.
- ✅ **Fanger's PMV** (Predicted Mean Vote) is the academic comfort model; ACC aims for **PMV ≈ 0** (thermally neutral).
- ✅ **Comfort tolerance band:** **±1 °C** of set-point is the industry-accepted comfort criterion (ISO 14505).
- ✅ **Differentiator from Manual HVAC:** Manual AC = cooling on/off only; **Automatic Climate Control = automatic regulation of temperature**, regardless of cooling/heating mode.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Anti-Theft Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT744 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Anti-Theft Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔐 MODULE 4 — ANTI-THEFT SYSTEMS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Anti-Theft System (ATS):** A vehicle-integrated electronic security architecture composed of sensors, electronic control units (ECUs), immobilizers, alarm actuators, and communication modules designed to prevent unauthorized access, ignition, and movement of a vehicle, and to alert the owner/authorities in the event of an intrusion attempt.

In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, the term **Vehicle Anti-Theft System (VATS)** encompasses a layered set of mechanical, electromechanical, and electronic deterrents that work cooperatively. Modern systems are governed by standards such as **ISO 15764 (Road vehicles — Extended data link security)**, **SAE J1850**, and **UN Regulation No. 116 (Uniform provisions concerning the protection of motor vehicles against unauthorized use)**.

### 1.2 Key Subsystems Overview

| Subsystem | Primary Function | Typical Component |
|---|---|---|
| **Immobilizer** | Prevents engine start without authorized key | Transponder in key + ECU ring antenna |
| **Alarm Unit** | Audible/visual deterrent on forced entry | Siren, horn, hazard lamps |
| **Central Locking** | Synchronized door locking | Door lock actuators, body control module (BCM) |
| **Keyless Entry / Passive Entry (PEPS)** | RFID-based door unlock | LF/RF antennas, fob, KESSY module |
| **GPS/GSM Tracking** | Real-time vehicle location | Telematics control unit (TCU) |
| **Steering Lock (ESL)** | Mechanically blocks steering rotation | Electronic steering column lock actuator |
| **Glass Break / Tilt Sensor** | Detects intrusion via shock or vehicle tilt | Piezoelectric accelerometer |

> [!IMPORTANT]
