> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Modern anti-theft is no longer a single "car alarm" — it is a **distributed network of ECUs** communicating over **CAN (Controller Area Network)** and **LIN (Local Interconnect Network)** buses, secured through cryptographic challenge–response authentication.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Vault with a Talking Doorman"

Imagine your car is a **bank vault**:

- The **Immobilizer** is the vault's *combination lock* that physically prevents the safe (engine) from being opened even if a thief breaks in.
- The **Transponder Key** is the *secret password* whispered between the key and the lock — a "magic handshake" only the right key can complete.
- The **Alarm** is the *screaming siren* that draws attention the moment the wrong person touches the door.
- The **GPS Tracker** is the *GPS chip hidden in stolen money* that lets police follow the thieves in real time.
- The **Steering Lock** is the *concrete pillar* physically jamming the steering wheel so the car cannot be steered even if hot-wired.
- The **Rolling Code Encryption** is the *password that changes every time* — even if a thief records your password, it is **useless the next time**.

In short: **Detection → Deterrence → Denial → Recovery** — the four pillars of any modern anti-theft system.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Functional Pillars of Vehicle Anti-Theft

#### Pillar 1 — **Detection** (Sensing unauthorized events)
- Door switch / hood switch / trunk switch
- Glass-break acoustic sensor
- Ultrasonic interior motion sensor
- Tilt sensor (vehicle jacking)
- Volumetric / microwave sensor

#### Pillar 2 — **Deterrence** (Audible/visual warning)
- Siren (typically 110–120 dB)
- Horn pulse pattern
- Hazard light blinking
- Headlamp flash

#### Pillar 3 — **Denial** (Prevent operation)
- Engine immobilizer (cuts fuel/injector/spark signals)
- Electronic steering column lock (ESL)
- Transmission shift lock
- Brake system lockout (in some hybrids/EVs)

#### Pillar 4 — **Recovery** (Locate and retrieve)
- GPS + GSM telematics
- Stolen Vehicle Recovery (SVR) services (e.g., OnStar, BMW Connected Drive)
- Remote engine kill via cellular command

### 2.2 Immobilizer — Heart of the Modern System

The **Engine Immobilizer** is a passive RFID-based anti-theft system. The key has a transponder chip; the ignition lock has a ring antenna (LF, typically 125 kHz). When the key is inserted and turned, the Immobilizer Control Unit sends a challenge via the antenna; the transponder responds with a stored secret code.

> [!NOTE]
> **Operating Frequency:** 125 kHz (LF band) is the industry standard for immobilizer challenge.
> **Authentication:** Typically Hitag-2 / Hitag-3 (NXP) or DST-40 / DST-80 (Texas Instruments) proprietary protocols.

#### Authentication Process
1. Insert key into ignition.
2. Ring antenna energizes transponder (passive RFID, no battery needed in key).
3. Immobilizer ECU transmits a **random challenge** $C$.
4. Transponder computes a response $R = f(K, C)$ where $K$ is the secret key stored in EEPROM.
5. Immobilizer ECU verifies $R$ against its expected value.
6. Only if matched, the ECU enables the engine ECU via the CAN bus (typically by clearing a flag in the engine management).
7. If no match (or no transponder), the engine ECU refuses to start, regardless of key bitting.

### 2.3 Rolling Code / Remote Keyless Entry (RKE) Encryption

To prevent *code-grabbing attacks* (where a thief records the unlock signal and replays it), modern RKE/PEPS use a **rolling code** where the encrypted code changes with every button press.

The most common algorithm is **KeeLoq** (by Microchip Technology).

**KeeLoq Algorithm — Encryption:**

$$ C_i = E_{K}(S_i \oplus T_i) $$

where:
- $C_i$ = transmitted codeword
- $E_K(\cdot)$ = block cipher with manufacturer key $K$ (64-bit)
- $S_i$ = 32-bit synchronization counter (incremented every press)
- $T_i$ = 16/20-bit discrimination value
- $\oplus$ = XOR operation

**KeeLoq Algorithm — Decryption (Receiver Side):**

$$ P_i = D_{K}(C_i) $$

$$ S_i = P_i \oplus T_i $$

$$ \text{Accept if } S_i > S_{\text{last\_accepted}} \text{ and } \Delta S_i \le \text{window} $$

**Rolling Window Concept:** The receiver accepts codes only if the synchronization counter $S_i$ lies within a small forward window (typically $\pm 16$ to $\pm 256$ increments) of the last accepted code. This prevents replay attacks while tolerating accidental button presses.

### 2.4 Passive Entry / Passive Start (PEPS) — Hands-Free Key

PEPS uses **bidirectional LF (Low Frequency, 20–125 kHz) challenge and UHF (Ultra High Frequency, 315/433/868 MHz) response**:

- The vehicle continuously emits LF challenge from door-handle antennas.
- The key fob, if within range (~1.5 m), powers up via the LF field, computes a response, and transmits on UHF.
- The vehicle authenticates and unlocks.

**Range Detection Formula (Friis Transmission Equation):**

$$ P_r = P_t G_t G_r \left( \dfrac{\lambda}{4 \pi d} \right)^2 $$

where:
- $P_r$ = received power (W)
- $P_t$ = transmitted power (W)
- $G_t, G_r$ = antenna gains
- $\lambda$ = wavelength (m)
- $d$ = distance (m)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Security Note:** PEPS is vulnerable to **relay attacks** (extending the LF signal via a wire/cable to fool the car into thinking the key is nearby). Modern systems use **UWB (Ultra-Wideband)** with Time-of-Flight ranging to defeat relay attacks. KTU students should be aware of this as a recent industry trend.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Symbols | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| KeeLoq Encryption | $C_i = E_K(S_i \oplus T_i)$ | $C_i$: ciphertext, $K$: 64-bit key, $S_i$: counter, $T_i$: discrimination | bits |
| KeeLoq Decryption | $S_i = D_K(C_i) \oplus T_i$ | $D_K$: inverse cipher | bits |
| Rolling Code Window | $\vert S_i - S_{last} \vert \le W$ | $W$: window size (typ. 16–256) | counts |
| Friis Path Loss | $P_r = P_t G_t G_r \left( \frac{\lambda}{4\pi d} \right)^2$ | $P_r, P_t$: power; $G$: gain; $\lambda$: wavelength; $d$: distance | W, m |
| Path Loss (dB) | $L_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left( \frac{4 \pi d}{\lambda} \right)$ | $L_{dB}$: path loss | dB |
| Alarm Sound Level | $L_p = 20 \log_{10}\left( \frac{p}{p_0} \right)$ | $L_p$: SPL; $p$: pressure; $p_0 = 20 \ \mu \text{Pa}$ | dB SPL |
| Car Alarm Threshold | $L_p \ge 100$ dB SPL @ 1 m | $L_p$: sound pressure level | dB |
| GPS Location Update | $\Delta t = \frac{1}{f_{update}}$ | $f_{update}$: e.g., 1 Hz typical | seconds |
| Engine Disable Time | $t_{\text{disable}} \le 1$ s (KTU spec) | $t$: time to deny crank | seconds |

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation — KeeLoq Block Cipher Round Function

The KeeLoq cipher is a 64-bit block cipher based on a Nonlinear Feedback Shift Register (NLFSR) running 528 rounds. Let us derive the working equations step by step.

**State Definition:**
Let the state register be a 64-bit vector $Y = (y_{63}, y_{62}, \dots, y_0)$.

**Feedback Function (KeeLoq uses 5 feedback taps):**

$$ y_{63}^{\text{new}} = y_0 \oplus y_{16} \oplus y_{32} \oplus y_{48} \oplus y_{63} \oplus f(K, Y) $$

where $f(K, Y)$ is the **nonlinear function** involving 5 key bits and 5 state bits.

**Step-by-Step Encryption Process:**

**Step 1 — Key Setup**
The 64-bit manufacturer key $K = (k_{63}, k_{62}, \dots, k_0)$ is loaded from the receiver's secure EEPROM.

$$ K = \{k_0, k_1, k_2, \dots, k_{63}\} $$

**Step 2 — Plaintext Load**
The 32-bit counter $S_i$ and 16/20-bit discrimination $T_i$ are XORed to form the 32-bit plaintext block:

$$ P = S_i \oplus T_i $$

**Step 3 — State Initialization**
The plaintext $P$ is prepended with zeros to fill the 64-bit state register:

$$ Y_0 = \{P_{31}, P_{30}, \dots, P_0, 0, 0, \dots, 0\} $$

**Step 4 — NLFSR Clocking (528 rounds)**
For each round $r = 1, 2, \dots, 528$, the state shifts right by 1 bit, and the new MSB is computed by the feedback equation above.

**Step 5 — Nonlinear Function $f(K, Y)$**
The nonlinearity is introduced via the lookup-table function $N(x)$:

$$ f(K, Y) = N(y_0 \oplus y_{16} \oplus k_0) \oplus N(y_1 \oplus y_{17} \oplus k_1) \oplus \dots $$

**Step 6 — Ciphertext Output**
After 528 rounds, the state register is read out as:

$$ C_i = Y_{528} $$

**Step 7 — Transmission**
The 32-bit ciphertext $C_i$ (along with the discrimination $T_i$ and a button identifier) is transmitted via UHF (315/433/868 MHz).

> [!NOTE]
> **Full Decryption:** The receiver runs the *inverse* of the NLFSR using the same key $K$ to recover $S_i$, then checks if $S_i$ lies within the rolling window.

### 3.2 Worked Example — Path Loss Calculation for PEPS

A PEPS key operates at **f = 125 kHz**. Calculate the path loss at $d = 2$ m.

**Given:**
- Frequency: $f = 125$ kHz
- Distance: $d = 2$ m

**Step 1 — Compute wavelength $\lambda$**

$$ \lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8 \ \text{m/s}}{125 \times 10^3 \ \text{Hz}} $$

$$ \lambda = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{1.25 \times 10^5} $$

$$ \lambda = 2400 \ \text{m} $$

**Step 2 — Apply Free-Space Path Loss Equation**

$$ L_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left( \frac{4 \pi d}{\lambda} \right) $$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values**

$$ L_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left( \frac{4 \pi \times 2}{2400} \right) $$

$$ L_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left( \frac{8 \pi}{2400} \right) $$

**Step 4 — Simplify the ratio**

$$ 8 \pi \approx 25.133 $$

$$ \frac{25.133}{2400} \approx 0.01047 $$

**Step 5 — Compute the logarithm**

$$ \log_{10}(0.01047) = \log_{10}(1.047 \times 10^{-2}) $$

$$ \log_{10}(0.01047) = -1.98 $$

**Step 6 — Final answer**

$$ L_{dB} = 20 \times (-1.98) $$

$$ \boxed{L_{dB} \approx -39.6 \ \text{dB} } $$

(Note: The path loss is large at LF due to long wavelength — this is precisely why LF is used for short-range wake-up challenges in PEPS systems.)

### 3.3 Comparative Analysis Matrix of Anti-Theft Technologies

| Feature | Mechanical Lock | Basic Alarm | Immobilizer | RKE (Rolling Code) | PEPS | GPS Tracking |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Anti-Hotwire** | ❌ Weak | ❌ No | ✅ Strong | ✅ Strong | ✅ Strong | ❌ No |
| **Anti-Replay** | N/A | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (cryptographic) | ✅ Yes (rolling) | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| **Anti-Relay** | N/A | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ❌ Vulnerable | ⚠️ UWB solves | ❌ No |
| **Anti-Tow/Anti-Jack** | ❌ No | ⚠️ Tilt sensor | ❌ No | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Geofence |
| **Owner Convenience** | Manual key | Manual arm | Auto | Remote | Hands-free | Remote query |
| **Recovery Capability** | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ Strong |
| **Cost** | Low | Low | Medium | Medium | High | High |
| **Standard Reference** | ISO 11154 | UN R116 | UN R116 | SAE J2452 | ISO 14229 | ISO 15764 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** A question worth 7 marks often asks for a **comparative table** of at least 3 anti-theft technologies. Memorize the columns above — *Anti-Hotwire, Anti-Replay, Anti-Relay, Recovery* are the four key discriminators examiners look for.

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 System-Level Block Diagram of a Modern Anti-Theft Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    KeyFob["REMOTE KEY FOB"] --> RKE["RKE RECEIVER BCM"]
    KeyFob --> RFID["RFID TRANSPONDER 125 KHZ"]
    RFE["LF CHALLENGE ANTENNA 125 KHZ"] --> KeyFob
    RFID --> ImmECU["IMMOBILIZER ECU"]
    RKE --> ImmECU
    ImmECU --> Bus["CAN BUS HIGH SPEED 500 KBIT S"]
    Bus --> EngECU["ENGINE ECU"]
    Bus --> BCM["BODY CONTROL MODULE"]
    Bus --> TCU["TELEMATICS CONTROL UNIT TCU"]
    BCM --> Siren["SIREN 110 DB"]
    BCM --> LockAct["DOOR LOCK ACTUATORS"]
    BCM --> ESL["ELECTRONIC STEERING LOCK"]
    TCU --> GPS["GPS MODULE L1 1575 MHZ"]
    TCU --> GSM["GSM 4G LTE MODULE"]
    GPS --> Cloud["CLOUD SERVER"]
    GSM --> Cloud
    Cloud --> Phone["OWNER MOBILE APP"]
    EngECU --> Inj["FUEL INJECTORS DISABLED"]
    EngECU --> Spark["IGNITION COIL DISABLED"]
    ESL --> Steer["STEERING SHAFT LOCKED"]
```

### 4.2 Authentication Flow — Immobilizer Challenge-Response

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Driver
    participant Key as Transponder Key
    participant Ant as Ring Antenna
    participant Imm as Immobilizer ECU
    participant Eng as Engine ECU
    Driver->>Key: Insert key, turn to ON
    Ant->>Key: LF field 125 kHz energize
    Key->>Ant: Return ID and secret
    Ant->>Imm: Forward ID + Secret
    Imm->>Imm: Verify against EEPROM
    alt Authentication SUCCESS
        Imm->>Eng: Enable flag via CAN
        Eng->>Driver: Engine cranks and starts
    else Authentication FAIL
        Imm->>Eng: No enable signal
        Eng->>Driver: Engine remains locked, alarm triggers
    end
```

### 4.3 Rolling Code Authentication — Anti-Replay Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Press["USER PRESSES UNLOCK"] --> Gen["GENERATE COUNTER S_i"]
    Gen --> Enc["ENCRYPT C_i = E_K S_i XOR T_i"]
    Enc --> TX["TRANSMIT C_i via UHF 433 MHZ"]
    TX --> RX["RECEIVER DECRYPTS D_K C_i"]
    RX --> Comp{"S_i WITHIN ROLLING WINDOW?"}
    Comp -- YES --> Accept["ACCEPT COMMAND, S_last = S_i"]
    Comp -- NO --> Reject["REJECT REPLAY ATTACK"]
    Accept --> Action["DOOR UNLOCKS"]
    Reject --> Log["LOG EVENT IN BCM"]
```

### 4.4 Anti-Theft Functional Layered Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L4["LAYER 4 RECOVERY GPS GSM TCU"] --> L3["LAYER 3 DENIAL IMMOBILIZER ESL"]
    L3 --> L2["LAYER 2 DETERRENCE SIREN HORN LIGHTS"]
    L2 --> L1["LAYER 1 DETECTION DOOR SWITCH TILT GLASS BREAK"]
    L1 --> Trigger["INTRUSION EVENT TRIGGERED"]
    Trigger --> AllLayers["ALL 4 LAYERS ACTIVATE SIMULTANEOUSLY"]
```

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define the term "Immobilizer" in the context of automotive anti-theft systems. Mention the operating frequency used.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> An **immobilizer** is an electronic security device fitted in the ignition key and engine control unit that prevents the engine from starting unless a correctly coded transponder key is present. The ignition ring antenna operates at **125 kHz (LF band)**, energizing the passive transponder, which responds with a unique secret code. The immobilizer ECU verifies this code and only then releases the engine start enable signal via the CAN bus. (1 mark — definition, 1 mark — 125 kHz, 1 mark — operation)

#### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is a "rolling code" in RKE systems? Why is it used?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A **rolling code** is a cryptographic technique in which the transmitted unlock code changes with every button press using a counter that increments and is encrypted via a block cipher (e.g., **KeeLoq**) with a 64-bit manufacturer key. (2 marks) It is used to defeat **replay attacks**, where an attacker records a valid unlock signal and replays it later. Since the code changes every time, a recorded code becomes invalid the next time. (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice:** Attempt **Question A OR Question B**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **passive entry / passive start (PEPS)** anti-theft system. Mention the frequencies used and the security threat it is designed to counter. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **KeeLoq block cipher** used in rolling-code remote keyless entry systems. Show the encryption and decryption equations, and state the role of the **discrimination value** and **rolling window**. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**(a) PEPS System Working — 7 marks:**

1. **Concept:** PEPS enables the driver to unlock and start the car without pressing any button on the fob — the car detects the fob automatically when it is within range. (1 mark)

2. **Block Diagram:**

   ```
   LF Antennas (door handles)  →  Key Fob (transponder)  →
   UHF Response (433 MHz)  →  KESSY/BCM Module  →  CAN Bus  →
   Engine ECU + Door Lock Actuators
   ```

3. **Working Steps:**
   - The car continuously (or at low duty cycle) emits an LF challenge at **125 kHz** from antennas in door handles. (1 mark)
   - When the fob is within **~1.5 m**, the LF field powers up the fob's passive RFID chip. (1 mark)
   - The fob computes a cryptographic response and transmits it on **UHF (315/433/868 MHz)**. (1 mark)
   - The KESSY/BCM module authenticates the response. (1 mark)
   - On successful authentication, the door unlocks and the engine is enabled for start. (1 mark)
   - **Security threat countered:** Replay attacks, code grabbing, and basic theft. (1 mark)

4. **Frequency Summary:** LF 125 kHz (challenge) + UHF 433 MHz (response). Modern UWB (3.1–10.6 GHz) is added to defeat **relay attacks**. (Bonus, 0.5 mark)

**(b) KeeLoq Block Cipher — 7 marks:**

1. **Definition:** KeeLoq is a 64-bit block cipher designed by Microchip Technology, using a Nonlinear Feedback Shift Register (NLFSR) with **528 clocking rounds**. (1 mark)

2. **Encryption Equation:**

   $$ C_i = E_{K}(S_i \oplus T_i) $$

   where $E_K$ is encryption with 64-bit manufacturer key $K$, $S_i$ is the 32-bit synchronization counter, $T_i$ is the discrimination value, and $\oplus$ is XOR. (2 marks)

3. **Decryption Equation:**

   $$ S_i = D_K(C_i) \oplus T_i $$

   The receiver uses the same 64-bit key to recover the counter value. (1 mark)

4. **Role of Discrimination Value ($T_i$):**
   $T_i$ is a 16/20-bit fixed value (sometimes serial-related) that is XORed with the counter to form the plaintext. It adds a device-specific signature, so that even if two devices use the same key, their transmitted codes differ — preventing cross-device replay. (1.5 marks)

5. **Role of Rolling Window:**
   The receiver maintains the last-accepted counter $S_{last}$ and accepts a new code only if $S_i > S_{last}$ and $S_i - S_{last} \le W$ where $W$ is the window size (typically 16–256). This allows tolerance for accidental button presses while rejecting replay attacks. (1.5 marks)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]

**(a)** List and explain the **four functional pillars** of a modern vehicle anti-theft system. Give one example sensor/actuator for each pillar. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the following anti-theft technologies in a tabular form: (i) Mechanical steering lock, (ii) Basic audible alarm, (iii) Engine immobilizer, (iv) GPS/GSM tracking. Compare across the criteria of *anti-hotwire, anti-replay, recovery capability, cost, and standard reference.* **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**(a) Four Functional Pillars — 7 marks:**

| Pillar | Function | Example Sensor/Actuator |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Detection** | Senses unauthorized access attempts | Door switch / glass-break acoustic sensor / tilt sensor (1.5 marks) |
| **2. Deterrence** | Audibly/visibly warns the thief and surroundings | 110 dB siren / hazard light flasher / horn (1.5 marks) |
| **3. Denial** | Physically/electronically prevents vehicle operation | Engine immobilizer (cuts injector) / electronic steering column lock (1.5 marks) |
| **4. Recovery** | Helps locate and retrieve the stolen vehicle | GPS + GSM telematics with remote engine kill (1.5 marks) |
| **Conclusion** | Modern systems integrate all 4 pillars via CAN bus | (1 mark) |

**(b) Comparative Table — 7 marks:**

| Criterion | Mechanical Steering Lock | Basic Audible Alarm | Engine Immobilizer | GPS/GSM Tracking |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Anti-Hotwire** | Weak (defeated by cutting wheel) | None | **Strong** (ECU denies start) | None |
| **Anti-Replay** | N/A | None | **Strong** (cryptographic) | None |
| **Recovery Capability** | None | None | None | **Strong** (real-time tracking) |
| **Cost** | Low | Low | Medium | High (subscription) |
| **Standard Reference** | — | UN R116 | UN R116, ISO 15764 | ISO 15764 |

(Marking: 1 mark per technology × 5 criteria = 5 marks, plus 2 marks for overall clarity and conclusion.)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls (Examiner's Eye-View):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to mention the operating frequency (125 kHz).** Examiners specifically test whether you know the LF band used for immobilizers. *Loss: 1 mark.*
> 2. **Confusing "rolling code" with "fixed code."** Many students write "the code changes every time" but forget to mention the **synchronization counter** and **rolling window**. *Loss: 1–2 marks.*
> 3. **Drawing PEPS architecture without LF and UHF labels.** Examiners want to see that you understand it is a *bidirectional* multi-frequency system. *Loss: 1 mark.*
> 4. **Not mentioning UN R116 / ISO 15764 standards.** Standards are a KTU 2024 syllabus emphasis under NEP 2020 outcome-based education. *Loss: 0.5–1 mark.*
> 5. **In KeeLoq derivations, students often forget the XOR between $S_i$ and $T_i$** before encryption. *Loss: 1 mark.*
> 6. **No mention of relay attacks in PEPS answers** — this is a hot topic in 2024 board papers. *Loss: 1 mark.*

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📋 **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — ANTI-THEFT SYSTEMS**

- ✅ **Definition:** Anti-theft = Detection + Deterrence + Denial + Recovery.
- ✅ **Immobilizer frequency:** **125 kHz (LF)**; uses passive RFID transponder in key + ring antenna.
- ✅ **Authentication:** Challenge–response via cryptographic algorithm (Hitag / DST / KeeLoq).
- ✅ **RKE Rolling Code (KeeLoq):** $C_i = E_K(S_i \oplus T_i)$; 64-bit key, 528 NLFSR rounds, rolling window 16–256.
- ✅ **PEPS Frequencies:** LF 125 kHz challenge + UHF 315/433/868 MHz response.
- ✅ **Relay attack threat:** Mitigated by **UWB Time-of-Flight** ranging.
- ✅ **Alarm threshold:** ≥ 110 dB SPL @ 1 m.
- ✅ **Standards:** UN R116 (anti-theft), ISO 15764 (data link security), SAE J2452 (RKE).
- ✅ **GPS Tracking:** L1 band 1575.42 MHz, update rate 1 Hz typical; remote engine kill via GSM.
- ✅ **Four key discriminators** for comparison: *Anti-Hotwire, Anti-Replay, Anti-Relay, Recovery.*
- ✅ **Steering lock is mechanical denial, immobilizer is electronic denial** — both are "Denial" pillar.
- ✅ **Modern architecture:** Distributed ECUs over **CAN (500 kbit/s)** + **LIN (19.2 kbit/s)** buses.
- ✅ **Cytro / KESSY / PEPS** = same concept, different OEM brand names (VW/Audi/Toyota/Ford).

> 🎯 **End of Module 4 — Anti-Theft Systems Notes** 🎯
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Automatic Door Locks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT744 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Automatic Door Locks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Automatic Door Locking System (ADLS):** An electrically controlled, electromechanical subsystem of an automotive body electronics architecture that uses a centralized Body Control Module (BCM), door-mounted actuators, switchgear, and (optionally) radio-frequency (RF) transceivers to simultaneously engage or disengage the latch mechanism of all vehicle doors, either on driver command, remote command, or autonomously based on vehicle speed, gear position, or crash-event triggers.

In the KTU 2024 scheme nomenclature, this system falls under the broader umbrella of **Body Electronics & Convenience Systems**, and is treated as a distributed mechatronic network rather than a stand-alone accessory. The BCM is mandated to be the single point of arbitration.

### 1.2 Sub-Categories Recognized by the KTU Module

| Sub-System | Operating Trigger | Primary Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Power Door Locks (PDL)** | Manual switch / Key cylinder | Base convenience feature |
| **Central Door Locking (CDL)** | Single master command | Driver-side override |
| **Remote Keyless Entry (RKE)** | RF handheld fob | Stand-alone locking at distance |
| **Passive Entry / Passive Start (PEPS)** | Proximity-based LF/RF polling | Smart key / hands-free |
| **Speed-Activated Auto-Lock** | VSS signal > 15 km/h | Anti-theft safety |
| **Crash Unlock (Pyro-fuse based)** | Airbag ECU crash pulse | Post-collision egress |

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — "The Hotel Doorman with a Walkie-Talkie":**
> Imagine a hotel doorman who stands at the main entrance. When a guest pushes a button at *any* door in the corridor, a single radio message reaches the doorman, who simultaneously swings closed every door in the hotel. The doorman is your **BCM**, the button is the **door switch**, the radio is the **RF receiver**, and the door-swinging action is the **actuator**. If a fire alarm rings (the airbag crash pulse), the doorman *automatically* swings them open — that is your **crash-unlock** logic.

## 1.4 Standard Industry Metrics (Bolded for Recall)

- **Operating Voltage:** **12 V DC** (nominal automotive battery rail, operating range **9 V – 16 V**).
- **Lock/Unlock Current Draw per Actuator:** **3 A – 5 A** (peak, inrush).
- **Locking Stroke Time:** **0.2 s – 0.4 s** per actuator.
- **RF Carrier Frequency (RKE):** **315 MHz** (US/Japan) or **433.92 MHz** (Europe/India).
- **LF Polling Frequency (PEPS):** **125 kHz**.
- **Maximum Reliable RKE Range:** **30 m – 100 m** (line of sight).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Decomposition — The Five Logical Layers

The system is best understood as five interacting layers. Each layer communicates with the one above and below it through well-defined electrical interfaces.

### Layer 1 — Input / Switch Layer
- Driver-door master switch (rocker SPDT)
- Passenger-door slave switches
- Key cylinder mechanical-to-electrical switches
- Door ajar switches (microswitch / Hall-Effect)
- RF receiver (for fob commands)
- LF antennas (for PEPS polling)

### Layer 2 — Communication / Bus Layer
- **LIN Bus** (Local Interconnect Network) — sub-network between each door module and the BCM. Speed: **19.2 kbps**.
- **CAN Bus** (Controller Area Network) — backbone between BCM, instrument cluster, and airbag ECU. Speed: **500 kbps** typical.
- **Single-wire LIN** for low-cost mirror/lock control.

### Layer 3 — Control Layer (The BCM)
The BCM is a 32-bit automotive-grade microcontroller (e.g., Infineon Aurix, NXP S32K) running an RTOS. It executes the *Arbitration State Machine* which resolves conflicting commands (e.g., driver presses "Lock" while passenger presses "Unlock").

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Arbitration Rule (KTU Board Exam Favourite):** When simultaneous Lock and Unlock commands are received, the **last-pressed-wins** logic is followed, with a **debounce delay of 50 ms – 200 ms** to suppress switch bounce.

### Layer 4 — Driver Layer
- **N-channel MOSFET H-bridge** per actuator — allows bidirectional current flow to drive the motor in both directions.
- **Flyback / Free-wheeling diodes** (Schottky, rated 30 V reverse, 3 A continuous) across each MOSFET to suppress back-EMF.
- **PTC self-resetting fuse** for overcurrent protection.

### Layer 5 — Actuator Layer
Two dominant topologies exist:

1. **Solenoid (Pull-type) Actuator** — A copper coil wound on a ferrous plunger creates a magnetic field that pulls a latch lever. Used in legacy systems; high inrush current (~**15 A** for 50 ms).
2. **Brushed DC Geared Motor Actuator** — A small **12 V DC motor** with a **planetary gear reduction (ratio ~ 60:1)** drives a rack-and-pinion or cam to rotate the latch fork. Modern standard; current ~**3 A** continuous.

## 2.2 Operating Principle — Motor-Driven Latch

1. BCM receives a "Lock" command (from switch OR RF).
2. BCM energizes the H-bridge: current flows through the motor in the **Lock polarity**.
3. The motor's armature rotates, gear-train reduces speed and amplifies torque.
4. The output cam rotates the **latch fork** by **~30° – 45°**, pushing the **locking pawl** into the **striker** recess.
5. A **Hall-effect position sensor** confirms "Locked" state and feeds it back to BCM → indicator LED flashes.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **mandatory recall items** for KTU University Examination (ESE). Memorize the units carefully.

$$
\begin{array}{|l|l|l|}
\hline
\textbf{Equation} & \textbf{Physical Meaning} & \textbf{Standard Units} \\
\hline
P = V \cdot I & \text{Actuator electrical power} & \text{W (Watts)} \\
\hline
\tau = \dfrac{P \cdot 60}{2\pi \cdot n} & \text{Motor output torque} & \text{N·m} \\
\hline
F = \dfrac{\mu_0 \cdot N^2 \cdot A}{2 \, g^2} \cdot I^2 & \text{Solenoid magnetic pull force} & \text{N (Newtons)} \\
\hline
P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - L_{path} & \text{RF link budget (Friis)} & \text{dBm} \\
\hline
L_{path} = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) & \text{Free-space path loss} & \text{dB, with d in km, f in MHz} \\
\hline
T_{lock} = \dfrac{\theta}{\omega} & \text{Time to rotate latch fork by } \theta & \text{seconds} \\
\hline
E_{bat} = V \cdot I_{avg} \cdot t_{lock} & \text{Energy drawn per lock event} & \text{Joules (W·s)} \\
\hline
f_{rolling} = f_{carrier} \pm f_{dev} & \text{FSK rolling-code deviation} & \text{Hz} \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **LaTeX Substitution Rule:** In the table above, the absolute-value / magnitude symbol in $\mu_0$ and the per-unit gaps were deliberately written without vertical pipes to avoid breaking markdown parsing. Use $\vert x \vert$ in inline math if you need absolute value outside tables.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Anti-theft integration:** Auto-lock at >**15 km/h** prevents forced entry at traffic stops (a common carjacking scenario in metropolitan cities).
- **Crash Safety:** The pyrotechnic battery cutoff (commonly called **pyro-fuse** or **battery disconnect unit**) is fired within **5 ms** of airbag deployment to unlock doors and isolate the HV battery in EVs.
- **Parenting Convenience:** Selective unlock — driver presses Unlock once → only driver door opens; twice within **3 seconds** → all doors open.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following derivations are exhaustive — **every algebraic transition is shown**. No steps are skipped. This is KTU University Exam-grade rigor.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Motor Torque Required for Door Lock Actuation

A typical brushed DC geared motor runs at **n = 6000 rpm** and is geared down by a **60:1 planetary gearbox**. The output shaft must deliver enough torque to rotate the latch fork against a spring preload.

**Given:**
- Motor rated voltage V = **12 V DC**
- Motor no-load speed n₀ = **6000 rpm**
- Motor rated current I_rated = **2 A**
- Gear ratio N = **60**
- Latch fork preload spring force F_s = **20 N**
- Effective lever arm r = **0.025 m**

**Step 1 — Convert no-load speed to rad/s.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_0 &= \frac{2\pi \cdot n_0}{60} \\
         &= \frac{2\pi \cdot 6000}{60} \\
         &= 200\pi \;\text{rad/s} \\
         &\approx 628.32 \;\text{rad/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the output (gearbox) speed.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_{out} &= \frac{\omega_0}{N} = \frac{628.32}{60} \\
             &\approx 10.47 \;\text{rad/s} \quad (\text{i.e., } \approx 100 \;\text{rpm})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute torque required at the latch fork (mechanical side).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{fork} &= F_s \cdot r \\
            &= 20 \;\text{N} \times 0.025 \;\text{m} \\
            &= 0.5 \;\text{N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Account for gearbox efficiency η_gb = 0.7.** Therefore, motor-output torque must be:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{motor,out} &= \frac{\tau_{fork}}{\eta_{gb} \cdot N} \\
                 &= \frac{0.5}{0.7 \times 60} \\
                 &\approx 0.0119 \;\text{N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert electrical power to torque.** Using $\tau = \dfrac{P \cdot 60}{2\pi \cdot n}$, and assuming P = V · I = 12 · 2 = 24 W at rated load:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{electrical} &= \frac{24 \times 60}{2\pi \times 6000} \\
                  &= \frac{1440}{37699.1} \\
                  &\approx 0.0382 \;\text{N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Motor rated torque (0.038 N·m) ≫ required motor-output torque (0.012 N·m) ⇒ **factor-of-3 safety margin**, confirming that the 12 V/2 A motor comfortably actuates the lock.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Free-Space Path Loss for the RKE Receiver

**Given:**
- Transmit power P_t = **10 dBm** (10 mW)
- Transmit antenna gain G_t = **0 dBi** (chip antenna)
- Receive antenna gain G_r = **2 dBi**
- Distance d = **50 m**
- Carrier frequency f = **433.92 MHz**

**Step 1 — Apply the Friis transmission equation in dB form.**

$$
P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - L_{path}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute free-space path loss at d = 50 m, f = 433.92 MHz.** Convert to km: d = 0.05 km.

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{path} &= 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) \\
         &= 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(0.05) + 20\log_{10}(433.92)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate each logarithmic term.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
20\log_{10}(0.05) &= 20 \times (-1.301) = -26.02 \\
20\log_{10}(433.92) &= 20 \times 2.6375 = 52.75
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Sum.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{path} &= 32.44 - 26.02 + 52.75 \\
         &= 59.17 \;\text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute received power.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_r &= 10 + 0 + 2 - 59.17 \\
    &= -47.17 \;\text{dBm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Compare against receiver sensitivity.** Typical RKE receiver sensitivity = **−100 dBm**.

**Margin check:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Link Margin} &= -47.17 - (-100) = 52.83 \;\text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** A **+52.83 dB link margin** means the system can tolerate >**300×** attenuation due to vehicle body obstruction, interference, or battery aging — this is exactly why RKE works reliably when the key fob is in a pocket.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Energy Drawn per Single Lock Event

**Given:** Four doors locked simultaneously. Each actuator draws I_avg = **3 A** for t = **0.3 s** at V = **12 V**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{single} &= V \cdot I_{avg} \cdot t \\
            &= 12 \times 3 \times 0.3 \\
            &= 10.8 \;\text{J (per door)}
\end{aligned}
$$

For four doors (assuming sequential, since current is limited by BCM driver):

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{total} &= 4 \times 10.8 = 43.2 \;\text{J}
\end{aligned}
$$

Compared with the energy stored in a **60 Ah × 12 V** battery (≈ **2.59 MJ**):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Battery drain per event} &= \frac{43.2}{2.59 \times 10^6} \approx 1.67 \times 10^{-5} \;\text{(i.e., 0.00167 \%)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Implication:** Even **30,000 lock events** would drain only ~**5 %** of battery — confirming that automatic door locks have **negligible** parasitic impact on battery state-of-charge.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix (KTU Board Exam Style)

| Parameter | Solenoid Actuator | Brushed DC Motor Actuator | PEPS / Smart Actuator |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Current Inrush** | Very High (~15 A, 50 ms) | Moderate (~5 A peak) | Low (~1 A) |
| **Hold Current** | None (latched mechanically) | ~0.5 A | ~0.2 A |
| **Audible Noise** | Loud "clack" | Soft "whirr" | Silent (motor only on demand) |
| **Failure Mode** | Coil burnout | Brush wear | Electronics failure |
| **Cost (INR, 2024)** | ₹ 180 | ₹ 350 | ₹ 1200 |
| **Cycle Life** | ~50,000 | ~100,000 | ~150,000 |
| **Use Case Today** | Legacy / budget | Mass-market | Premium / EV |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System-Level Block Diagram (Mermaid)

> The following diagram maps the **functional architecture** of an Automatic Door Lock system, showing all signal flows between input devices, the BCM, and actuators.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph inputLayer["INPUT LAYER"]
        keyFob["RF Key Fob 433.92 MHz"]
        drvSw["Driver Master Switch"]
        psgSw["Passenger Switches"]
        keyCyl["Key Cylinder Switch"]
        doorAjar["Door Ajar Microswitch"]
        speedSig["VSS Speed Signal"]
        crashSig["Airbag ECU Crash Pulse"]
    end

    subgraph busLayer["COMMUNICATION LAYER"]
        rfRx["RF Receiver Module"]
        linBus["LIN Bus 19.2 kbps"]
        canBus["CAN Bus 500 kbps"]
    end

    subgraph ctrlLayer["CONTROL LAYER - BCM"]
        bcmArb["Arbitration State Machine"]
        bmsLock["Autolock Logic V greater than 15 km/h"]
        crashLogic["Crash Unlock Logic"]
    end

    subgraph drvLayer["DRIVER LAYER"]
        hBridge["H-Bridge MOSFET Drivers"]
        ptcFuse["PTC Resettable Fuse"]
        flyback["Flyback Diodes"]
    end

    subgraph actLayer["ACTUATOR LAYER"]
        drvAct["Driver Door Motor"]
        psgAct["Passenger Motor"]
        rlAct["Rear Left Motor"]
        rrAct["Rear Right Motor"]
        latchFb["Latch Position Hall Sensor"]
    end

    keyFob --> rfRx
    rfRx --> bcmArb
    drvSw --> bcmArb
    psgSw --> bcmArb
    keyCyl --> bcmArb
    doorAjar --> bcmArb
    speedSig --> canBus
    crashSig --> canBus
    canBus --> bmsLock
    canBus --> crashLogic
    bcmArb --> hBridge
    bmsLock --> bcmArb
    crashLogic --> bcmArb
    hBridge --> ptcFuse
    ptcFuse --> drvAct
    ptcFuse --> psgAct
    ptcFuse --> rlAct
    ptcFuse --> rrAct
    flyback --> hBridge
    drvAct --> latchFb
    psgAct --> latchFb
    rlAct --> latchFb
    rrAct --> latchFb
    latchFb --> bcmArb
```

## 4.2 Lock Command State Machine (Mermaid)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> UNLOCKED
    UNLOCKED --> LOCKING : switch press OR fob OR speed greater than 15 km/h
    LOCKING --> LOCKED : hall sensor confirms full fork rotation
    LOCKING --> FAULT : timeout exceeds 0.5 s
    FAULT --> LOCKING : retry 2 times then log DTC
    LOCKED --> UNLOCKING : switch OR fob OR crash signal
    UNLOCKING --> UNLOCKED : hall sensor confirms release
    LOCKED --> UNLOCKED : crash unlock override bypasses timer
```

## 4.3 H-Bridge Driver Topology (Functional Schematic)

The H-bridge is the heart of the bidirectional motor driver. The BCM controls four MOSFETs in an "H" arrangement so current can flow either way through the motor armature.

```
        +12 V (Battery)
            |
          [Q1]  High-side (Lock polarity)
            |
            +------- MOTOR ARMATURE -------+
            |                              |
          [Q2]                            [Q3]
            |                              |
          GND                            GND
            |
          [Q4]  High-side (Unlock polarity)
            |
        +12 V
```

- **Lock Action:** Q1 + Q3 ON → current flows top-left to bottom-right.
- **Unlock Action:** Q4 + Q2 ON → current flows bottom-left to top-right.
- **Idle / Brake:** Q2 + Q3 ON (low-side slow decay) → motor terminals shorted → dynamic braking.
- **All OFF:** Motor free-wheels; flyback diodes absorb inductive kick.

> [!WARNING]
> **Shoot-Through Risk:** Q1 & Q2 must NEVER be ON simultaneously — this would short the battery. BCM firmware enforces a **10 µs dead-time** between high-side and low-side transitions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**List any three advantages of a motor-driven door lock actuator over a solenoid-type actuator.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Lower continuous current draw** (≈ 3 A vs 15 A inrush), reducing wiring harness gauge and battery stress. **(1 mark)**
2. **Self-latching capability** — the worm gear in the planetary reduction prevents back-drive, so no holding current is needed. **(1 mark)**
3. **Quieter operation** — soft "whirr" rather than the loud "clack" of solenoid, meeting NVH targets. **(1 mark)**

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define the term "rolling code" as used in Remote Keyless Entry systems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A rolling code is a cryptographic security scheme in which the transmitter and receiver share a synchronized counter and a secret key; every button press transmits a **fresh pseudo-random code** generated by an algorithm (e.g., KeeLoq) such that the receiver accepts codes within a forward window of ~**256** increments but rejects replays of older codes, preventing code-grabbing attacks. **(3 marks)**

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A — **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Set A]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **functional architecture** of a Central Door Locking system driven by a Body Control Module. Describe the role of each subsystem. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **free-space path loss** for an RKE link operating at **433.92 MHz** over a distance of **75 m**, assuming isotropic antennas. Comment on whether the received power is sufficient for a receiver with **−95 dBm sensitivity** and a transmit power of **+8 dBm**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks
1. **Block diagram (3 marks):** Draw the 5-layer architecture — Input (switches, fob, sensors) → Communication (LIN/CAN, RF receiver) → Control (BCM with arbitration state machine) → Driver (H-bridge with flyback diodes and PTC fuse) → Actuator (DC geared motor + Hall position sensor). Label all signal flows.
2. **Subsystem roles (4 marks):**
   - *Input layer* — converts human/mechanical intent into electrical signals. **(1)**
   - *Communication layer* — multiplexes multiple door switches onto LIN and carries crash/speed signals via CAN. **(1)**
   - *BCM* — runs arbitration with 50 ms debounce, implements auto-lock at >15 km/h, controls crash unlock. **(1)**
   - *Driver layer* — provides bidirectional current and protects against back-EMF and overcurrent. **(1)**

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks
**Step 1 — Convert distance to km:** d = 0.075 km. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2 — Compute path loss using Friis equation:** **(3 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{path} &= 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(0.075) + 20\log_{10}(433.92) \\
         &= 32.44 + 20 \times (-1.1249) + 52.75 \\
         &= 32.44 - 22.50 + 52.75 \\
         &= 62.69 \;\text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute received power:** P_t = +8 dBm, G_t = G_r = 0 dBi. **(1 mark)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_r &= P_t - L_{path} = 8 - 62.69 = -54.69 \;\text{dBm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Link margin calculation:** **(2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Margin} &= -54.69 - (-95) = 40.31 \;\text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since the margin is positive (≈ 40 dB, i.e., ~**10,000×** power ratio), the link is **comfortably sufficient** under free-space conditions. Margin will be reduced by ~10–20 dB in real urban obstruction scenarios, but remains positive. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question B — **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Set B]**

**(a)** Explain the **operating principle of a brushed DC geared motor actuator** for an automatic door lock, with reference to the role of the planetary gearbox and the Hall-effect position sensor. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A vehicle's automatic door lock system activates **four actuators** simultaneously. Each actuator draws an average current of **2.5 A** at **12 V** for a stroke time of **0.25 s**. Calculate the **total energy consumed** per lock event and the equivalent **percentage of a 60 Ah × 12 V** battery that this represents. Comment on whether this affects the vehicle's standby battery life. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks
1. **DC Motor (1.5 marks):** 12 V brushed DC motor produces rotational motion; speed ~6000 rpm no-load.
2. **Planetary Gearbox (2 marks):** Reduces speed by ratio 60:1; amplifies torque; provides **irreversibility** (worm or planetary stage prevents back-drive, eliminating the need for holding current).
3. **Cam/Latch Fork Conversion (1.5 marks):** Output cam rotates ~30°, moving the locking pawl into the striker recess.
4. **Hall-Effect Feedback (2 marks):** A magnet on the output shaft triggers a Hall sensor at the fully-locked position; the BCM uses this signal to terminate current (preventing stall damage) and to drive the dashboard lock-indicator LED.

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks
**Step 1 — Energy per actuator:** **(2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{actuator} &= V \cdot I \cdot t \\
              &= 12 \times 2.5 \times 0.25 \\
              &= 7.5 \;\text{J}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Total energy for 4 doors:** **(2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{total} &= 4 \times 7.5 = 30 \;\text{J}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Battery stored energy:** **(1 mark)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{batt} &= V \cdot Ah \times 3600 = 12 \times 60 \times 3600 = 2.592 \;\text{MJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Percentage drain:** **(1 mark)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ drain} &= \frac{30}{2.592 \times 10^6} \times 100 \approx 1.157 \times 10^{-3} \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Comment (1 mark):** This drain is **negligible** — even **100,000** lock events would consume only ~**1.16 %** of the battery. Therefore, the door-lock system has **no measurable impact** on standby battery life.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in this Topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** in derived answers — examiners allocate **0.5 mark** specifically for correct units (e.g., dBm vs dB, rad/s vs rpm). Failing to convert rpm → rad/s costs a full mark.
> 2. **Confusing path-loss formula constants** — the constant **32.44** is valid ONLY when distance is in **km** and frequency is in **MHz**. Mixing units (e.g., distance in metres) yields a wrong answer of ~**40 dB error**.
> 3. **Missing the "Comment / Conclusion" step** — KTU 2024 scheme mandates a concluding remark or engineering interpretation. Skipping it forfeits up to **2 marks** in a 14-mark question.
> 4. **Wrong direction of current in H-bridge explanation** — examiners expect you to clearly state which MOSFET pair turns ON for "Lock" vs "Unlock". Generic H-bridge descriptions score zero.
> 5. **Ignoring the debounce delay** — arbitration state machines in BCMs always include a **50–200 ms debounce**; mentioning this in long answers elevates your answer to full-marks territory.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Automatic Door Lock = BCM-controlled, electromechanically actuated latch engagement for all doors.
- **Architecture:** 5 layers — Input → Communication (LIN/CAN) → Control (BCM) → Driver (H-bridge) → Actuator (DC motor + Hall sensor).
- **Operating voltage:** **12 V DC** (range 9–16 V).
- **Actuator type today:** Brushed DC geared motor (planetary ratio ~60:1), NOT solenoid.
- **Auto-lock threshold:** Vehicle speed > **15 km/h** triggers automatic lock.
- **Crash unlock:** Airbag ECU pulse → BCM unlocks within **5 ms** to enable post-collision egress.
- **RKE frequencies:** **315 MHz** (US/Japan) and **433.92 MHz** (Europe/India).
- **PEPS polling:** **125 kHz** LF; smart key fob response on **433.92 MHz** UHF.
- **H-bridge dead-time:** **10 µs** minimum to prevent shoot-through.
- **Flyback diodes:** Mandatory across each MOSFET for back-EMF protection.
- **PTC resettable fuse:** Series element for short-circuit protection.
- **Hall-effect position sensor:** Confirms full lock/unlock; prevents motor stall damage.
- **Rolling code:** Security algorithm (KeeLoq); ~256-code forward window.
- **Friis path loss constant:** **32.44** — valid only with d in km, f in MHz.
- **Link margin:** Real systems target **≥ 30 dB** margin for urban-obstructed environments.
- **Energy per lock event:** ~**30 J** for 4 doors → negligible drain on 60 Ah battery.
- **Examiner favourites:** Block diagrams, RF link budget calculations, H-bridge working, auto-lock/crash-unlock logic, NVH comparison of solenoid vs motor.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tyre Pressure Sensing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT744 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Tyre Pressure Sensing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tyre Pressure Sensing**, commonly implemented through a **Tyre Pressure Monitoring System (TPMS)**, is an active safety subsystem mandated in modern passenger and commercial vehicles that continuously measures the inflation pressure (and in advanced variants, the temperature) of each pneumatic tyre and alerts the driver when the pressure deviates beyond a pre-calibrated safety threshold, typically a drop of more than **25 %** below the manufacturer's recommended cold inflation pressure (as per **FMVSS 138 / ECE R64 / AIS-145** automotive safety standards).

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** terminology used for *PEMUT744 – Modern Automotive Technologies*, Tyre Pressure Sensing is classified as an **active chassis/safety sensor cluster** that integrates **MEMS (Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems)** sensing, **low-power RF telemetry**, and **CAN/LIN bus communication** to deliver real-time tyre health telemetry to the vehicle's central Body Control Module (BCM) or Instrument Cluster.

The two principal architectural families recognised by KTU syllabus are:

| Taxonomy | Full Form | Measurement Basis |
|----------|-----------|-------------------|
| **dTPMS** | Direct Tyre Pressure Monitoring System | In-tyre MEMS pressure transducer |
| **iTPMS** | Indirect Tyre Pressure Monitoring System | ABS wheel-speed sensor signal processing |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Student Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Blood Pressure Cuff" of the Car**
> Think of a TPMS as the **blood-pressure cuff of the automobile**. Just as a doctor wraps a cuff around your arm to measure how hard your heart is pushing blood, the car wraps an electronic "cuff" (a MEMS sensor) inside each tyre to measure how hard the air is pushing the rubber outward. If the "blood pressure" of the tyre drops (under-inflation) or spikes (over-inflation), a "beeper" goes off on the dashboard — exactly like the doctor announcing an abnormal reading. The four tyres are like four "limbs" of the car, and the dashboard warning lamp is the "doctor's diagnosis."

A second analogy that resonates with engineering students is the **"personal trainer on your wrist"** model — a wearable heart-rate strap reports biometric data wirelessly to a display; the TPMS in-wheel sensor reports pressure/temperature data wirelessly to the instrument cluster.

## 1.3 Why Tyre Pressure Sensing is a Safety-Critical Subsystem

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Highlighting:** Examiners frequently award bonus credit when students explicitly mention *legislative compulsion* — TPMS became mandatory in India under **AIS-145** (Automotive Industry Standards) for M1 category vehicles from **1st April 2021**, mirroring the global **TREAD Act (USA, 2000)** and **EU Regulation 661/2009**.

Key engineering motivations for accurate tyre pressure monitoring:

1. **Fuel efficiency** — Under-inflation by **0.2 bar** raises rolling resistance by ~**6–8 %**, increasing fuel consumption.
2. **Tyre life** — A **20 %** under-inflation reduces tread life by roughly **30 %**.
3. **Braking distance** — Wet-road stopping distance at **100 km/h** can extend by up to **4 m** with severely under-inflated tyres.
4. **Aquaplaning risk** — Lower profile height reduces water-displacement volume, elevating hydroplaning onset speed.
5. **CO₂ emissions** — Indirect reduction in exhaust emissions due to lower rolling resistance.
6. **Blowout prevention** — Under-inflated tyres run hotter, accelerating belt separation and catastrophic failure.

## 1.4 Typical Operating Pressure & Temperature Ranges

| Parameter | Passenger Car | Heavy Commercial Vehicle |
|-----------|---------------|--------------------------|
| Nominal cold pressure | **2.2 – 2.5 bar** (220 – 250 kPa) | **7.0 – 9.0 bar** |
| Warning threshold | ≤ **1.7 bar** (~25 % drop) | ≤ **6.0 bar** |
| Operating temperature | **−40 °C to +125 °C** | **−40 °C to +150 °C** |
| Sensor update rate | **every 30 – 60 s** (parked mode slower) | every **15 – 30 s** |
| RF carrier frequency | **315 MHz** (USA/Japan) / **433.92 MHz** (EU/India) | Same |

## 1.5 Core Components of a TPMS Sensor Module

A modern in-tyre TPMS sensor is a System-in-Package (SiP) containing:

1. **MEMS piezoresistive pressure sensor** — converts pressure to resistance change.
2. **MEMS temperature sensor** (bandgap reference) — compensates pressure reading.
3. **MEMS accelerometer** — detects wheel rotation and triggers wake-up from sleep.
4. **ASIC microcontroller** (8-bit/16-bit low-power core, e.g., **Freescale FXTH87**, **Infineon SP37**, **NXP PX3**) — runs the application firmware.
5. **RF transmitter** with integrated loop antenna on the PCB.
6. **Li-SOCl₂ primary cell** (3.6 V, ~550 mAh) — provides **7 – 10 year** lifetime.
7. **Valve-stem mechanical housing** (clamp-in or snap-in variants).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Direct TPMS (dTPMS) — Operating Principle

The **direct TPMS** physically measures the absolute gas pressure inside the tyre cavity using a MEMS diaphragm. The operational sequence is:

1. The **MEMS pressure cell** contains a silicon diaphragm with implanted piezoresistors arranged in a **Wheatstone bridge** configuration.
2. Inflation pressure deflects the diaphragm; piezoresistors change resistance proportionally (the **piezoresistive effect**).
3. The bridge output voltage is amplified, digitised by a **Sigma-Delta ADC**, and temperature-compensated in firmware.
4. The **accelerometer** detects centrifugal force (≥ ~**8 g**) confirming the wheel is rotating; the sensor exits low-power sleep and begins periodic transmission.
5. A unique **32-bit sensor ID** is transmitted (typically **Manchester-encoded**) along with pressure, temperature, and battery-voltage status.
6. The vehicle's **RF receiver module** decodes the message and forwards it to the **BCM/ECU** over **CAN (500 kbps)** or **LIN** bus.
7. The instrument cluster illuminates the **amber TPMS warning lamp** (ISO 7000 symbol **W023**) if any reading crosses the safety threshold.

## 2.2 Indirect TPMS (iTPMS) — Operating Principle

The **indirect TPMS** does **not** measure pressure directly. Instead, it exploits the relationship between **inflation pressure** and **dynamic rolling radius**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Physics:** An under-inflated tyre has a *smaller* vertical stiffness, so the contact patch lengthens and the effective rolling radius **r_eff** decreases. With the same wheel angular velocity, a smaller radius means a *lower* linear road speed, which the **ABS wheel-speed sensor** can detect as a relative slip difference between diagonally paired wheels.

The ECU continuously monitors the **4-wheel speed signals** and applies a spectral analysis algorithm (typically an **FFT-based** or **wheel-speed resonance frequency** method). Under-inflation alters the **spectral signature** of the wheel-speed signal — specifically the **tyre-cavity resonance frequency** and the **rolling-radius-induced speed differential**.

### 2.2.1 Tyre Cavity Resonance Frequency (Key Concept)

When a tyre rolls on a road, the air–rubber system exhibits a fundamental acoustic resonance. The resonance frequency is:

$$f_{res} \;=\; \frac{c_{eff}}{2\,\pi\,r_{eff}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{A_{cavity}}{V_{cavity}}}$$

Where:
- $f_{res}$ = tyre-cavity resonance frequency, in **Hz**
- $c_{eff}$ = effective speed of sound in the tyre air–rubber mixture, typically **200 – 230 m/s**
- $r_{eff}$ = effective rolling radius in **m**
- $A_{cavity}$ = cross-sectional cavity area in **m²**
- $V_{cavity}$ = internal cavity volume in **m³**

A drop in pressure reduces $V_{cavity}$ effective stiffness and **lowers $f_{res}$**, which the algorithm flags as under-inflation.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these eight equations — they cover ~90 % of numerical problems asked by KTU on Tyre Pressure Sensing.**

| # | Formula (LaTeX) | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Pressure = Force / Area | Pa, bar, psi | Fundamental pressure |
| 2 | $P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$ | Absolute = Gauge + Atmospheric | bar or kPa | Sensor calibration |
| 3 | $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = G \cdot \pi_l \cdot \sigma$ | Piezoresistive constitutive law | dimensionless | MEMS pressure sensing |
| 4 | $V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{4R}$ | Wheatstone bridge output | Volts | Bridge transduction |
| 5 | $r_{eff} = r_0 \cdot \left[1 - k \left(1 - \dfrac{P}{P_0}\right)\right]$ | Effective rolling radius vs pressure | metres | iTPMS inference |
| 6 | $\Delta f = f_{ref\,fl} - f_{ref\,fr}$ | Wheel-speed frequency delta | Hz | iTPMS slip detection |
| 7 | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f_{carrier}}$ | RF wavelength | metres | Antenna design |
| 8 | $T_{frame} = N_{bits} \cdot t_{bit}$ | Telemetry frame duration | seconds | Data-rate sizing |

Where:
- $G$ = Gauge factor of piezoresistor (typically **50 – 170** for silicon)
- $\pi_l$ = longitudinal piezoresistive coefficient (Pa⁻¹)
- $\sigma$ = mechanical stress on diaphragm (Pa)
- $V_{ex}$ = bridge excitation voltage (V)
- $k$ = empirical compliance coefficient (≈ 0.05 – 0.15)
- $P_0$ = nominal cold inflation pressure
- $c$ = speed of light = **3 × 10⁸ m/s**
- $f_{carrier}$ = **315 MHz** or **433.92 MHz**
- $N_{bits}$ = bits per frame, typically **96 – 128**
- $t_{bit}$ = bit duration, typically **100 µs** (10 kbps Manchester)

## 2.4 Sensor-Side Signal Conditioning

The raw piezoresistive bridge output is in the millivolt range; therefore the ASIC integrates:

1. **PGA (Programmable Gain Amplifier)** — gain of **16 – 128**.
2. **Sigma-Delta (Σ-Δ) ADC** — resolution of **12 – 16 bits**, oversampling ratio (OSR) of **64 – 256**.
3. **Digital temperature compensation** — multi-point calibration stored in non-volatile memory (NVM) with coefficients $a_0, a_1, a_2$ for the second-order correction:

$$P_{comp} = a_0 + a_1 \cdot V_{ADC} + a_2 \cdot T + a_3 \cdot V_{ADC} \cdot T$$

4. **Low-power duty cycling** — average current draw is targeted below **1 µA** in sleep mode to achieve the 10-year battery life requirement.

## 2.5 Vehicle-Side Receiver Subsystem

The receiver module comprises:

- An **external antenna** (typically roof-mounted or behind-glass shark-fin).
- A **superheterodyne or low-IF RF receiver** with SAW bandpass filtering at **±150 kHz** around the carrier.
- A **microcontroller** that decodes the Manchester bit stream, validates CRC, and forwards the result over **CAN 2.0B (11-bit ID)** at **500 kbps** to the BCM.
- A **re-learning / ID pairing procedure** run via diagnostic tool (OBD-II) or auto-learn sequence (some vehicles drive straight for 10 – 20 minutes at >25 km/h to pair new sensors).

## 2.6 Engineering Trade-Off Summary

| Parameter | dTPMS | iTPMS |
|---|---|---|
| Measurement type | Direct absolute pressure | Inferred (rolling radius) |
| Accuracy | ± **0.05 bar** | ± **0.15 – 0.25 bar** |
| Spare-tyre coverage | Yes | No (spare not monitored) |
| Simultaneous 4-wheel deflation | Detected | **NOT** detected (limitation) |
| Cost per wheel | Higher (₹ 800 – 1500) | Marginal (uses existing ABS sensors) |
| Re-initialisation after tyre rotation | Required | Not required |
| Battery replacement | Not possible — replace whole sensor | N/A |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Note:** All derivations below are presented with the level of algebraic detail expected in a 14-mark KTU End-Semester Examination answer. Each row contains both the mathematical transition and the engineering interpretation.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Wheatstone Bridge Output of a MEMS Pressure Sensor

**Starting Point:** A square silicon diaphragm of side $a$ and thickness $h$ is clamped at all four edges. The diaphragm is integrated with four piezoresistors ($R_1, R_2, R_3, R_4$) arranged as a full Wheatstone bridge, two of which experience longitudinal stress and two transverse stress.

**Step 1 — Apply uniform differential pressure $\Delta P$ to the diaphragm.**

The maximum centre deflection $w_0$ for a clamped square plate is given by the small-deflection plate-bending solution:

$$w_0 \;=\; \dfrac{0.00126 \cdot \Delta P \cdot a^{4}}{E \cdot h^{3}}$$

where $E$ is Young's modulus of silicon (≈ **170 GPa** along ⟨110⟩ direction).

**Step 2 — Compute the maximum bending stress at the diaphragm edge.**

For a clamped plate, the maximum radial stress occurs at the midpoint of the clamped edge:

$$\sigma_{max} \;=\; \dfrac{0.308 \cdot \Delta P \cdot a^{2}}{h^{2}}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the piezoresistive constitutive relation.**

For a single piezoresistor aligned with the principal stress direction:

$$\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} \;=\; G \cdot \pi_l \cdot \sigma_{max}$$

Substituting $\sigma_{max}$ from Step 2:

$$\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} \;=\; G \cdot \pi_l \cdot \dfrac{0.308 \cdot \Delta P \cdot a^{2}}{h^{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Convert bridge arm resistance change to differential output voltage.**

In a fully active Wheatstone bridge, when $R_1$ and $R_3$ increase by $\Delta R$ while $R_2$ and $R_4$ decrease by $\Delta R$ (push-pull arrangement), the differential output voltage is:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{out} &= V_{ex} \cdot \left( \dfrac{R_1 + \Delta R}{R_1 + \Delta R + R_2 - \Delta R} - \dfrac{R_2 - \Delta R}{R_2 - \Delta R + R_1 + \Delta R} \right) \\
&= V_{ex} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{R}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Combine the results to obtain the final transfer function.**

$$\boxed{\;V_{out} \;=\; V_{ex} \cdot G \cdot \pi_l \cdot \dfrac{0.308 \cdot \Delta P \cdot a^{2}}{h^{2}}\;}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** The output voltage is **linear** in applied differential pressure $\Delta P$, scales with the **square of the diaphragm side** $a$, and **inversely with the cube** when the thickness is varied (because $h$ appears as $h^{2}$ in stress and additionally through $w_0$). Designers therefore choose $a/h \approx 20$–$40$ to balance sensitivity against fracture strength.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — iTPMS Effective Rolling Radius Inference

**Step 1 — Express the linear road speed of each wheel.**

For wheel $i$ with rolling radius $r_{eff,i}$ rotating at angular velocity $\omega_i$:

$$v_i \;=\; \omega_i \cdot r_{eff,i}$$

**Step 2 — Relate the wheel angular velocity to ABS sensor frequency.**

The ABS toothed ring has $N$ teeth (typically $N = 48$ on a passenger car). The wheel-speed sensor produces a square wave of frequency:

$$f_i \;=\; \dfrac{N \cdot \omega_i}{2\pi}$$

Hence:

$$\omega_i \;=\; \dfrac{2\pi \cdot f_i}{N}$$

**Step 3 — Eliminate angular velocity to express radius as a function of frequency.**

$$r_{eff,i} \;=\; \dfrac{v_i}{\omega_i} \;=\; \dfrac{N \cdot v_i}{2\pi \cdot f_i}$$

**Step 4 — For straight-line driving without slip, $v_i = v_{vehicle}$.**

$$r_{eff,i} \;=\; \dfrac{N \cdot v_{vehicle}}{2\pi \cdot f_i}$$

**Step 5 — Express pressure dependence using empirical compliance relation.**

A common empirical model is:

$$r_{eff,i} \;=\; r_0 \cdot \left[1 - k \left(1 - \dfrac{P_i}{P_0}\right)\right]$$

where $P_0$ is the nominal cold inflation pressure, $P_i$ is the actual pressure, $r_0$ is the nominal rolling radius, and $k$ is a dimensionless compliance coefficient typically in the range **0.05 – 0.15**.

**Step 6 — Combine to obtain the iTPMS pressure inference equation.**

$$P_i \;=\; P_0 \cdot \left[1 - \dfrac{1}{k} \left(\dfrac{r_{eff,i}}{r_0} - 1\right)\right]$$

Substituting $r_{eff,i} = \dfrac{N \cdot v_{vehicle}}{2\pi \cdot f_i}$:

$$\boxed{\;P_i \;=\; P_0 \cdot \left[1 - \dfrac{1}{k} \left(\dfrac{N \cdot v_{vehicle}}{2\pi \cdot r_0 \cdot f_i} - 1\right)\right]\;}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** The ECU measures $f_i$ for each wheel; if the inferred $P_i$ drops below the safety threshold (typically $0.75 P_0$), the warning lamp is triggered. The algorithm requires the vehicle to drive **above 25 km/h** for at least **10 minutes** to suppress transient signals (cornering, braking, pothole impacts).

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — RF Wavelength and Antenna Length for TPMS Telemetry

**Step 1 — Carrier frequency for the Indian/European market.**

$$f_{carrier} \;=\; 433.92 \;MHz$$

**Step 2 — Compute the free-space wavelength.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\lambda &= \dfrac{c}{f_{carrier}} \\
&= \dfrac{3 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}}{433.92 \times 10^{6} \text{ Hz}} \\
&= 0.691 \text{ m} \\
&\approx 69.1 \text{ cm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Optimal monopole antenna length (quarter-wave).**

For a quarter-wave whip antenna:

$$L_{ant} \;=\; \dfrac{\lambda}{4} \;=\; \dfrac{0.691}{4} \;=\; 0.173 \text{ m} \;=\; 17.3 \text{ cm}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** A 17.3 cm antenna cannot fit inside a wheel rim; therefore TPMS sensors use a **loop antenna** etched directly onto the sensor PCB. The small loop is *electrically short* and is compensated by a high-Q matching network and impedance-matching stripline, trading bandwidth for size.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Frame Duration of a TPMS Telemetry Packet

A typical dTPMS frame structure is:

| Field | Length (bits) | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Preamble | 16 | Synchronisation |
| Sensor ID | 32 | Unique ID per wheel |
| Pressure | 8 | 0 – 5 bar mapped to 0 – 255 |
| Temperature | 8 | −40 °C to +125 °C mapped |
| Battery status | 8 | Voltage / state-of-health |
| CRC | 8 | Error detection |
| **Total** | **80** | — |

With **Manchester encoding** at a bit rate of **9.6 kbps** (typical):

$$t_{bit} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{9600} \;=\; 104.17 \; \mu s$$

$$T_{frame} \;=\; 80 \times 104.17 \; \mu s \;=\; 8.33 \; ms$$

**Duty cycle check:** If a sensor transmits one frame every 60 s, the effective duty cycle is:

$$D \;=\; \dfrac{8.33 \; ms}{60 \; s} \;=\; 1.39 \times 10^{-4} \;=\; 0.014 \;\%$$

This ultra-low duty cycle is what enables a single primary Li-SOCl₂ cell to power the sensor for **7 – 10 years**.

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style 7-Mark Problem)

> **Problem (KTU Pattern):** A passenger-car TPMS sensor uses a square silicon diaphragm of side $a = 1 \; mm$ and thickness $h = 25 \; \mu m$. The piezoresistors have gauge factor $G = 120$ and longitudinal piezoresistive coefficient $\pi_l = 70 \times 10^{-11} \; Pa^{-1}$. The bridge is excited at $V_{ex} = 3.0 \; V$. Compute the differential output voltage when the tyre pressure rises by $\Delta P = 0.5 \; bar$ above atmospheric. ($1 \; bar = 10^{5} \; Pa$.)

**Step 1 — Convert pressure to SI units.**

$$\Delta P \;=\; 0.5 \times 10^{5} \; Pa \;=\; 5 \times 10^{4} \; Pa$$

**Step 2 — Compute the maximum stress.**

$$\sigma_{max} \;=\; \dfrac{0.308 \cdot \Delta P \cdot a^{2}}{h^{2}} \;=\; \dfrac{0.308 \times 5 \times 10^{4} \times (10^{-3})^{2}}{(25 \times 10^{-6})^{2}}$$

$$\sigma_{max} \;=\; \dfrac{0.308 \times 5 \times 10^{4} \times 10^{-6}}{6.25 \times 10^{-10}} \;=\; \dfrac{1.54 \times 10^{-2}}{6.25 \times 10^{-10}} \;=\; 2.464 \times 10^{7} \; Pa \;=\; 24.64 \; MPa$$

**Step 3 — Compute fractional resistance change.**

$$\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} \;=\; G \cdot \pi_l \cdot \sigma_{max} \;=\; 120 \times 70 \times 10^{-11} \times 2.464 \times 10^{7}$$

$$\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} \;=\; 120 \times 70 \times 2.464 \times 10^{-4} \;=\; 2.07 \times 10^{-2} \;=\; 2.07 \;\%$$

**Step 4 — Compute bridge output voltage.**

$$V_{out} \;=\; V_{ex} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{R} \;=\; 3.0 \times 2.07 \times 10^{-2} \;=\; 62.1 \; mV$$

**Step 5 — Verify linearity range.**

A 62 mV output is comfortably within the linear range of a PGA with gain 16, producing a final ADC input of **0.99 V** — well below the 3 V supply. The design is robust.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** $V_{out} \approx 62 \; mV$ for a 0.5 bar pressure increase.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System-Level Architecture of a dTPMS (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[PressureSource<br>Tyre Cavity] --> B[MEMS Pressure<br>Sensor Diaphragm]
    B --> C[Wheatstone<br>Piezoresistive Bridge]
    C --> D[PGA + Sigma Delta<br>ADC + Temp Comp]
    E[MEMS Temperature<br>Sensor] --> D
    F[MEMS Accelerometer<br>Centrifugal Wake Up] --> G[ASIC Microcontroller<br>8 16 bit Core]
    D --> G
    H[Li SOCl2 Battery<br>3.6 V 550 mAh] --> G
    G --> I[Manchester Encoder<br>96 kbps]
    I --> J[RF Transmitter<br>433.92 MHz OOK]
    J --> K[Loop Antenna<br>on PCB]
    K -.->|Wireless RF Link| L[Vehicle Roof<br>Shark Fin Antenna]
    L --> M[Superheterodyne<br>RF Receiver]
    M --> N[Manchester Decoder<br>CRC Check]
    N --> O[CAN 2.0B Bus<br>500 kbps]
    O --> P[BCM ECU<br>Pressure Decision Logic]
    P --> Q[Instrument Cluster<br>TPMS Warning Lamp<br>ISO W023]
    P --> R[Diagnostic Port<br>OBD II PIDs]
```

## 4.2 Signal-Conditioning Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[MEMS Bridge<br>mV range] --> S2[PGA<br>Gain 16 to 128]
    S2 --> S3[Low Pass Filter<br>fc 1 kHz]
    S3 --> S4[Sigma Delta ADC<br>16 bit OSR 128]
    S4 --> S5[Digital Temp Comp<br>Polynomial 2nd order]
    S5 --> S6[Calibration LUT<br>NVM Stored]
    S6 --> S7[Pressure Decision<br>Threshold Compare]
    S7 --> S8[Manchester Encode<br>with CRC 8]
    S8 --> S9[OOK Modulator<br>on 433.92 MHz]
```

## 4.3 iTPMS Algorithm Flowchart (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Ignition ON] --> A1[Read 4 Wheel Speed<br>ABS Sensors]
    A1 --> A2{Vehicle Speed<br>greater than 25 km h}
    A2 -- No --> A3[Hold Last State<br>No Decision]
    A2 -- Yes --> A4[Apply Moving<br>Average Filter]
    A4 --> A5[Compute Diagonal<br>Wheel Speed Ratio]
    A5 --> A6{Ratio Deviation<br>greater than 0.15}
    A6 -- No --> A7[All Tyres OK<br>Lamp OFF]
    A6 -- Yes --> A8[Run FFT on Wheel<br>Speed Signal]
    A8 --> A9{Resonance Peak<br>Shifted Downward}
    A9 -- No --> A7
    A9 -- Yes --> A10[Locate Deflated<br>Wheel Position]
    A10 --> A11[Illuminate TPMS<br>Warning Lamp]
    A11 --> A12[Store DTC in<br>Non Volatile Memory]
```

## 4.4 Component Pin-Out Reference (NXP FXTH87 — Representative dTPMS IC)

| Pin | Function | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | VCC | Battery supply input (3.6 V) |
| 2 | GND | Ground return |
| 3 | RF_OUT | Differential RF output to loop antenna |
| 4 | RF_OUTN | Complementary RF output |
| 5 | PRESSURE_SENSE | Bridge excitation + |
| 6 | PRESSURE_REF | Bridge excitation − |
| 7 | TEMP_SENSE | Bandgap temperature analogue output |
| 8 | ACCEL_X | Centrifugal accelerometer input |
| 9 | LF_IN | Optional low-frequency wake-up (125 kHz) |
| 10 | TEST | Factory test mode |

## 4.5 Pressure-vs-Resistance Characteristic (Schematic Plot Description)

For a typical N-type silicon piezoresistor at room temperature:

| Differential Pressure (bar) | ΔR/R (%) | Bridge Output (mV at 3 V) |
|---|---|---|
| 0.0 | 0.00 | 0.0 |
| 0.2 | 0.83 | 24.8 |
| 0.4 | 1.66 | 49.6 |
| 0.6 | 2.49 | 74.4 |
| 0.8 | 3.32 | 99.2 |
| 1.0 | 4.14 | 124.0 |

This linear characteristic is the engineering basis for using a simple multiplier in the ASIC firmware.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]** Define Tyre Pressure Monitoring System (TPMS). Distinguish between direct and indirect TPMS in two points each.

**Model Answer:**

> A **Tyre Pressure Monitoring System (TPMS)** is an active safety subsystem that continuously monitors the inflation pressure of each pneumatic tyre and warns the driver when the pressure falls below a pre-set safety threshold, typically **25 %** below the manufacturer's recommended cold inflation pressure, in compliance with **AIS-145 / FMVSS 138** standards.

**Distinction Table:**

| Aspect | Direct TPMS (dTPMS) | Indirect TPMS (iTPMS) |
|---|---|---|
| Measurement | Uses in-tyre MEMS pressure sensor | Infers from ABS wheel-speed data |
| Accuracy | Higher (± 0.05 bar) | Lower (± 0.15 – 0.25 bar) |
| Spare tyre | Monitors spare | Cannot monitor spare |
| Re-init after rotation | Required | Not required |

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** List any four advantages of TPMS in modern automobiles.

**Model Answer:**

1. **Improved fuel efficiency** by **6 – 8 %** through reduced rolling resistance.
2. **Extended tyre life** — a properly inflated tyre lasts ~**30 %** longer.
3. **Reduced CO₂ emissions** contributing to environmental compliance.
4. **Enhanced safety** — shorter braking distance on wet roads and lower aquaplaning risk.
5. *(Bonus point)* Legislative compliance with **AIS-145 / ECE R64**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### **Question A** — Direct TPMS Architecture (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) [7 Marks] — Explain the construction and working of a direct TPMS with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the MEMS accelerometer in the sensor module.

**Model Answer:**

**Construction:** A direct TPMS sensor module consists of (i) a **MEMS piezoresistive pressure sensor** built on a silicon diaphragm, (ii) a **MEMS temperature sensor**, (iii) a **MEMS accelerometer** for motion detection, (iv) an **ASIC microcontroller** (e.g., NXP FXTH87) with embedded ADC and Manchester encoder, (v) a **433.92 MHz RF transmitter** with PCB loop antenna, and (vi) a **3.6 V Li-SOCl₂ primary cell**. The assembly is housed in a polymer or metal **clamp-in / snap-in valve-stem** and rubber-sealed against moisture and centrifugal force.

**Working Principle:**

1. Air pressure inside the tyre deflects the MEMS diaphragm, producing a resistance change in the piezoresistive bridge.
2. The bridge output (mV range) is amplified, digitised, and temperature-compensated.
3. The accelerometer detects wheel rotation (centrifugal acceleration ≥ **8 g**) and triggers the ASIC to exit sleep mode.
4. The ASIC Manchester-encodes the pressure, temperature, and unique **32-bit sensor ID** and transmits via OOK modulation on **433.92 MHz**.
5. The vehicle-mounted receiver decodes the message, verifies CRC, and forwards the data over **CAN 500 kbps** to the BCM.
6. The BCM compares the received pressure against the calibrated threshold; if low, the **amber TPMS lamp** is illuminated.

**Role of the Accelerometer:**

- **Wake-up from sleep** — when the vehicle begins moving, the centrifugal force rises sharply; the accelerometer's threshold crossing wakes the ASIC, saving battery life while the car is parked.
- **Motion detection** — required by **FMVSS 138 §S4.2** for re-detection logic: if no motion is sensed for a programmable interval, the sensor reverts to deep sleep.
- **Centrifugal-force-based pressure correction** — in some advanced implementations, the accelerometer reading is used to compensate for the apparent pressure increase due to dynamic tyre bulging at high speed (centrifugal force can add **~0.05 bar** at 200 km/h).

#### Part (b) [7 Marks] — A square silicon diaphragm TPMS sensor has side 1 mm, thickness 25 µm, gauge factor 120, and piezoresistive coefficient $70 \times 10^{-11} \; Pa^{-1}$. The bridge is excited at 3.0 V. Compute the bridge output voltage for a differential pressure of 0.5 bar. Comment on the result.

**Model Solution (as derived in §3.5):**

$$\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{0.308 \cdot \Delta P \cdot a^{2}}{h^{2}} = \dfrac{0.308 \times 5 \times 10^{4} \times 10^{-6}}{6.25 \times 10^{-10}} = 24.64 \; MPa$$

$$\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = G \cdot \pi_l \cdot \sigma_{max} = 120 \times 70 \times 10^{-11} \times 2.464 \times 10^{7} = 0.0207$$

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = 3.0 \times 0.0207 = 62.1 \; mV$$

**Comment:** The output of ~62 mV is in the millivolt range, easily amplified by the on-chip PGA (gain 16 → ~1 V) before ADC conversion. The design exhibits good linearity, robust signal-to-noise ratio, and the sensitivity is sufficient to resolve pressure changes smaller than **0.05 bar** required for **AIS-145** compliance.

---

### **Question B** — Indirect TPMS & Comparative Analysis (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

#### Part (a) [7 Marks] — Describe the working principle of an indirect TPMS. Derive the expression relating inflation pressure to ABS wheel-speed sensor frequency.

**Model Answer:**

**Principle:** The indirect TPMS exploits the physical relationship that **under-inflation reduces the effective rolling radius** $r_{eff}$ of a tyre, which in turn reduces the linear road speed of that wheel for a given angular velocity. The ABS wheel-speed sensor, which already exists in the vehicle, detects this anomaly.

**Working Steps:**

1. With vehicle moving straight above **25 km/h**, the ECU reads the four wheel-speed sensor frequencies $f_{FL}, f_{FR}, f_{RL}, f_{RR}$.
2. Diagonally paired wheels are compared. Under-inflation in one wheel lowers its $r_{eff}$ and hence its $f_i$.
3. Spectral analysis (FFT) is applied; the **tyre-cavity resonance peak** shifts to a lower frequency when pressure is reduced.
4. The algorithm decides which wheel is deflated and triggers the TPMS warning lamp.

**Derivation (as in §3.2):**

For a wheel with effective rolling radius $r_{eff,i}$ and angular velocity $\omega_i$:

$$v_i = \omega_i \cdot r_{eff,i}$$

ABS toothed ring with $N$ teeth produces:

$$f_i = \dfrac{N \cdot \omega_i}{2 \pi} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; r_{eff,i} = \dfrac{N \cdot v_{vehicle}}{2 \pi f_i}$$

Empirical compliance relation:

$$r_{eff,i} = r_0 \left[1 - k \left(1 - \dfrac{P_i}{P_0}\right)\right]$$

Combining and solving for $P_i$:

$$\boxed{\;P_i = P_0 \left[1 - \dfrac{1}{k}\left(\dfrac{N \cdot v_{vehicle}}{2 \pi r_0 f_i} - 1\right)\right]\;}$$

#### Part (b) [7 Marks] — Compare dTPMS and iTPMS across eight parameters. Why is dTPMS preferred in India despite higher cost?

**Model Answer:**

| # | Parameter | dTPMS | iTPMS |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Measurement type | Direct (MEMS) | Inferred (ABS) |
| 2 | Absolute pressure reading | Yes | No — relative only |
| 3 | Accuracy | ± 0.05 bar | ± 0.15 – 0.25 bar |
| 4 | Spare tyre monitoring | Yes | No |
| 5 | Simultaneous 4-wheel deflation | Detected | **Not** detected |
| 6 | Cost per wheel | ₹ 800 – 1500 | Marginal |
| 7 | Re-init after rotation | Required | Not required |
| 8 | Cold-vs-hot pressure | Distinguishable | Confused by temperature |

**Why dTPMS is preferred in India:**

1. **AIS-145 mandates** absolute pressure reporting; only dTPMS can satisfy this.
2. Indian road conditions (heat, dust, mixed speeds) demand higher accuracy and temperature compensation — direct sensing provides both.
3. **Simultaneous 4-wheel deflation** can occur due to natural air loss; iTPMS fails to detect it (no relative speed differential), creating a safety gap.
4. **Spare-tyre monitoring** is a customer-experience differentiator valued in the Indian market.
5. **Aftermarket re-tyre rotation** is frequent in India; dTPMS with auto-relearn is now available at acceptable cost.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls — Read Before Writing Your Exam**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse** *gauge pressure* with *absolute pressure* — TPMS sensors output absolute pressure. Examiners deduct **2 marks** if units are wrongly stated.
> 2. **Do NOT state** that iTPMS can detect *all four* tyres deflating simultaneously — this is its **major limitation**.
> 3. **Do NOT** attribute TPMS to only luxury vehicles — it is **legally mandated** in India for M1 category vehicles since **1st April 2021 (AIS-145)**.
