## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students commonly **lose 2–3 marks** for writing only *block diagrams* without describing the **working principle** in words.
> - For systems like ABS/EPS, **always state the role of the ECU and the sensors** — a common omission.
> - Do **not confuse** "traction control" with "ABS" — they operate on different wheels and different conditions.
> - For HVAC, **never skip the expansion valve** step — it is a favourite 2-mark sub-question in KTU exams.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

#### (a) **[7 Marks]** Explain the working of an automotive Air Conditioning system with a neat block diagram. Mention the role of the expansion valve.

**Model Answer:**

The automotive HVAC system operates on the **Vapour Compression Refrigeration Cycle**, comprising four main components — **compressor, condenser, expansion valve, evaporator** — connected in a closed loop.

**Step 1 — Compression:**
The compressor, driven by the engine (or electric motor in EVs), compresses the low-pressure refrigerant vapour into a high-pressure, high-temperature superheated vapour.

**Step 2 — Condensation:**
This hot vapour flows through the condenser mounted at the front of the vehicle. The condenser fan and the vehicle's motion force ambient air through the condenser fins, which **rejects heat** from the refrigerant. The vapour condenses into a high-pressure liquid.

**Step 3 — Expansion (Role of Expansion Valve):**
The high-pressure liquid passes through the **thermostatic expansion valve (TXV)**. The TXV performs two critical functions:
- It causes a **sudden pressure drop** (Joule–Thomson effect).
- It **atomises** the refrigerant into a fine cold mist, which can readily absorb heat.
It also acts as a **flow regulator**, maintaining the correct superheat at the evaporator outlet.

**Step 4 — Evaporation:**
The cold low-pressure mixture enters the **evaporator coil** inside the HVAC box. The blower fan pushes cabin air across the cold coil. The refrigerant absorbs heat (latent heat of vaporisation) and evaporates, while the cabin air is cooled and dehumidified.

**Step 5 — Return:**
The low-pressure vapour returns to the compressor suction port, completing the cycle.

**Mark Distribution:**
- Correct identification of 4 components — 2 marks
- Working of each component explained — 3 marks
- Role of expansion valve — 1 mark
- Block diagram / neat sketch — 1 mark

#### (b) **[7 Marks]** With the help of a block diagram, explain the working of the Anti-lock Braking System (ABS). How does it improve vehicle safety?

**Model Answer:**

**Components of ABS:**
1. **Wheel speed sensors** (one per wheel) — tone ring + magnetic pickup.
2. **Electronic Control Unit (ABS ECU)** — microcomputer with slip calculation algorithm.
3. **Hydraulic Modulator Unit** — solenoid valves + electric pump.
4. **Master cylinder and brake lines** — conventional brake circuit.

**Working:**

**Step 1 — Sensing:**
Each wheel speed sensor generates an AC signal whose **frequency is proportional to wheel speed**. The ECU continuously monitors all four signals.

**Step 2 — Slip Calculation:**
The ECU calculates **slip ratio** $\lambda$ for each wheel:
$$\lambda = \dfrac{v_{vehicle} - v_{wheel}}{v_{vehicle}}$$

If $\lambda \to 1$, the wheel is about to lock.

**Step 3 — Pressure Modulation (3-phase cycle):**
When impending lock-up is detected, the ECU activates the hydraulic modulator in a high-frequency cycle:
- **Pressure-Increase phase:** Master-cylinder pressure is passed to the brake.
- **Pressure-Hold phase:** Inlet solenoid closes, isolating the wheel.
- **Pressure-Decrease phase:** Outlet solenoid opens, the pump returns fluid to the reservoir, releasing the brake.

This cycle repeats **15–20 times per second** — much faster than any human foot.

**Step 4 — Reapplication:**
Once wheel speed recovers, full brake pressure is restored.

**Safety Benefits:**
- **Steering control retained** during emergency braking.
- **Shorter stopping distance** on loose or wet surfaces.
- **Prevention of tyre flat-spotting** and aquaplaning.
- **Improved vehicle stability** during panic stops.

**Mark Distribution:**
- Components listed — 2 marks
- Step-by-step working — 3 marks
- Safety benefits — 1 mark
- Diagram — 1 mark

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)**

#### (a) **[7 Marks]** Describe the Electric Power Steering (EPS) system. Compare it with Hydraulic Power Steering (HPS).

**Model Answer:**

**Electric Power Steering (EPS) — Working:**

**Components:**
1. **Steering column with torque sensor** (measures driver-applied torque and direction).
2. **EPS ECU** (microcontroller with speed-dependent assist map).
3. **Electric motor** (BLDC, mounted on column, pinion, or rack).
4. **Reduction gear** (worm or planetary).
5. **Vehicle Speed Sensor** (input from CAN bus).

**Step 1 — Driver Input:**
When the driver rotates the steering wheel, the **torque sensor** detects the magnitude and direction of the applied torque.

**Step 2 — ECU Processing:**
The EPS ECU receives:
- Driver torque $T_d$ from the torque sensor.
- Vehicle speed $V$ from the CAN bus.

It then looks up an **assist curve** stored in its memory:
- At low speeds → high motor torque (easy parking).
- At high speeds → low motor torque (stable high-speed feel).

**Step 3 — Motor Actuation:**
The ECU commands the motor driver to supply current to the **BLDC motor**, which produces a **boost torque** $T_m$ on the steering column/rack.

Total assist torque = $T_d + T_m$.

**Step 4 — Self-Centring and Damping:**
The ECU also monitors return-to-centre and applies **active damping torque** to prevent steering oscillation.

**Comparison with HPS:**

| Feature | HPS | EPS |
|:--------|:----|:----|
| Energy source | Engine belt-driven pump | Battery + BLDC motor |
| Fuel economy | Reduced by 2–3 % | Minimal effect |
| Maintenance | High | Very low |
| ADAS compatibility | No | Yes (lane-keep, auto-park) |
| Cost | Lower | Slightly higher |
| Feel | Fixed curve | Software-tunable |

**Mark Distribution:**
- Components and their functions — 2 marks
- Step-by-step operation — 3 marks
- Comparison table — 2 marks

#### (b) **[7 Marks]** Explain the different types of headlamp technologies used in modern automobiles. Compare them on the basis of luminous efficacy, lifespan, and cost.

**Model Answer:**

Modern automobiles use four main headlamp technologies:

**1. Halogen Lamps:**
- **Principle:** A tungsten filament is heated inside a halogen-gas-filled quartz bulb.
- **Colour Temperature:** ≈ **3200 K** (warm yellow).
- **Luminous Efficacy:** **15–20 lm/W**.
- **Lifespan:** **500–1000 hours**.
- **Cost:** **Lowest**.

**2. HID / Xenon Lamps:**
- **Principle:** An electric arc is struck between two electrodes in a quartz chamber filled with xenon gas and metal halide salts.
- **Colour Temperature:** **4200–6000 K** (cool white / bluish).
- **Luminous Efficacy:** **80–100 lm/W**.
- **Lifespan:** **2000–3000 hours**.
- **Cost:** **High** (ballast required).

**3. LED (Light Emitting Diode):**
- **Principle:** Electroluminescence in a semiconductor p-n junction.
- **Colour Temperature:** **5000–6500 K** (crisp white).
- **Luminous Efficacy:** **100–150 lm/W**.
- **Lifespan:** **10,000+ hours**.
- **Cost:** **Medium–High**.

**4. Laser Headlamps:**
- **Principle:** Laser diodes excite a phosphor to produce intense white light.
- **Luminous Efficacy:** Highest among all.
- **Throw distance:** Up to **600 m**.
- **Cost:** **Very High** (used in premium cars like BMW, Audi).

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Halogen | HID | LED | Laser |
|:----------|:--------|:----|:----|:------|
| Efficacy | 15–20 lm/W | 80–100 lm/W | 100–150 lm/W | >150 lm/W |
| Lifespan | 500–1000 h | 2000–3000 h | 10,000+ h | 15,000+ h |
| Cost | Low | High | Medium-High | Very High |
| Warm-up | Instant | 5–10 s | Instant | Instant |

**Mark Distribution:**
- Description of each technology — 4 marks
- Comparison table — 2 marks
- Conclusion / current trend — 1 mark

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Auxiliary Systems (Basic Descriptions):**
> - **Auxiliary systems** = non-propulsion subsystems providing **comfort, safety, and convenience**.
> - **HVAC** works on the **Vapour Compression Refrigeration Cycle** with four key components: Compressor, Condenser, Expansion Valve, Evaporator.
> - **ABS** modulates brake pressure in a **Pressure-Increase → Hold → Decrease** cycle, **15–20 times/second**, to prevent wheel lock.
> - **EPS** is the modern replacement for HPS — **fuel-efficient, ADAS-compatible, low-maintenance**.
> - **Modern braking suite = ABS + EBD + BA + ESP + TCS** — all networked via CAN bus.
> - **Headlamps evolution:** **Halogen → HID → LED → Laser**, with **luminous efficacy** rising from **15 lm/W** to **>150 lm/W**.
> - **Suspension types:** MacPherson (simple), Double Wishbone (performance), Multi-link (luxury), Air (adjustable), Active (fully variable).
> - **Cruise → Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC)** uses **radar/lidar** to maintain vehicle-to-vehicle distance.
> - **CAN bus** is the central nervous system — it allows all ECUs (HVAC, ABS, EPS, BCM) to share data.
> - Always remember the **key sensor-to-actuator signal flow** for each system — examiners love flow-style answers.
> - **Operating voltage** of auxiliary systems: **12 V (ICE), 48 V (mild hybrids), 400 V (BEVs)**.
> - For descriptive answers, **always include a block diagram** — it earns at least 1 mark even if wording is incomplete.
